Breast.patients: DCIS s/p lumpectomy
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis of SweDCIS, EORTC, UK/ANZ, NSABP B17 ➜ 🏆 WBRT vs. ➜ obs
Breast.results: RT improved IBTR, but not BCM, all cause mortality, or other mortality ⬇️ 10-yr IBTR 28% vs. 13% BCM: 4%, not different Benefit with RT in all groups (age, size, grade, margin, TAM, etc) on subanalysis.
Breast.interpretation: WBRT added to lumpectomy improves rates of breast conservation. IBTR is reduced. BCM is unchanged.
Breast.commentary: No therapy has been shown to provide an OS benefit in DCIS in randomized trials. Benefit in all subgroups is later reproduced with dose escalation with boost in TROG BIG.
Breast.references: 1. Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group (EBCTCG), Correa C, McGale P, et al. Overview of the randomized trials of radiotherapy in ductal carcinoma in situ of the breast. J Natl Cancer Inst Monogr. 2010;2010(41):162–177. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/jncimonographs/lgq039

Breast.citation: Holberg et al, JCO, 2008 Wärnberg et al, JCO, 2014
Breast.patients: DCIS <1/4 of breast s/p WLE sector resection
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 50 Gy WBRT (no boost) vs. ➜ obs
Breast.results: ⬇️ IBTR 27% vs. 12% RT On unplanned subanalysis, low RT effect if age <50, but more protection if >60. Update 2014: ARR in DCIS 10% and in IDC 2%, giving 12% total reduction
Breast.interpretation: WBRT added to lumpectomy improves rates of breast conservation. IBTR is reduced. There is less effect in younger age. IBTR continues to rise with f/u.
Breast.commentary: goal >1cm margins, but negative margins not required
Breast.references: 1. Holmberg L, Garmo H, Granstrand B, et al. Breast.citation: Fisher et al, NEJM, 1993 Fisher et al, JCO, 1998 Fisher et al, Semin Oncol, 2001
Breast.patients: DCIS s/p resection with negative margin
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 50 Gy WBRT vs. ➜ obs 9% received boost
Breast.results: ⬇️ 12-yr IBTR 32% vs. 16% ⬇️ 12-yr non-invasive IBTR 15% vs. 8% ⬇️ 12-yr invasive IBTR 17% vs. 8% All cohorts benefitted from RT.
Breast.interpretation: WBRT added to lumpectomy improves rates of breast conservation. IBTR is reduced.

Breast.patients: any DCIS, 1998-2014
Breast.methods: Evaluated those treated with lumpectomy, with and without RT, and mastectomy. 1:1 matching
Breast.results: 15-yr BCM 2.33% with lumpectomy, 1.74% lumpectomy+RT, and 2.26% with mastectomy [NNT: 170] On subanalysis, clear benefit with grade 3 (HR 0.59), benefit in ER negative and ER positive, and benefit in tumors 1-1.9 cm and >5.0 cm. Tumors 2-2.9 cm were p=0.24, tumors 3.0-4.9 were p=0.07, and tumors <1.0 cm were p=0.58.
Breast.interpretation: Lumpectomy and RT was associated with reduced BCM compared to mastectomy. Randomized trials are required for confirmation as adjustments for imbalances in database studies cannot remove all confounding variables.
Breast.commentary: Importantly, BCM is the endpoint used. The subanalysis of pathologic factors strengthens the findings. A trial large enough to show survival benefit in DCIS will likely not be performed. Do exercise caution using large population observational studies: A review found that the conclusions of these were not better than chance at matching randomized trials, and no factors were found to improve conclusions (Soni 2019). This does not necessarily mean the reports worthless - they still contain more data than a single insitution retrospective report for example - but that the eras and parameters shou…
Breast.notes: DCIS summary -Four trials showed the benefit of WBRT in preventing DCIS LR (NSABP B-17, SweDCIS, EORTC, UK/ANZ). There is no OS benefit -EBCTCG meta-analysis confirmed findings. No OS benefit -Benefit of endocrine therapy is found (UKCCR, NSABP B-24). No OS benefit -Trials investigate no RT in low risk DCIS (ECOG, Harvard, RTOG 9804). RTOG 9804 finds that IBTR can still recur later even in low risk. Consider risk vs. benefit when rec treatment -RTOG 9804 establishes hypofx RT as an option for DCIS (although not randomized) and also reinforces option of obs vs RT for low risk -TROG BIG and DBCG…
Breast.references: 1. Giannakeas V, Sopik V, Narod SA. Association of Radiotherapy With Survival in Women Treated for Ductal Carcinoma In Situ With Lumpectomy or Mastectomy. JAMA Netw Open 20181:e181100. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2018.1100
Breast.commentary_refs: Soni PD, Hartman HE, Dess RT, et al. Comparison of Population-Based Observational Studies With Randomized Trials in Oncology. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1209-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.01074

Breast.patients: DCIS s/p BCT, 1998-2007
Breast.methods: Evaluated those treated with and without RT. Propensity matching
Breast.results: 10-yr BCM 1.8% with RT vs. 2.1% High nuclear grade, young age, large tumor size showed a 4% OS benefit at 10 years RT had improved HRs for BCM in all age brackets and grades, and all sizes except that for size <16 mm, p=0.05
Breast.interpretation: DCIS prognostic factors are associated with the magnitude of improvement in BCM with WBRT after lumpectomy. Randomized trials are required for confirmation as adjustments for imbalances in database studies cannot remove all confounding variables.
Breast.commentary: See comment on Giannakeas 2018.
Breast.references: 1. Sagara Y, Freedman RA, Vaz-Luis I, et al. Patient Prognostic Score and Associations With Survival Improvement Offered by Radiotherapy After Breast-Conserving Surgery for Ductal Carcinoma In Situ: A Population-Based Longitudinal Cohort Study. J Clin Oncol 2016;34:1190–1196. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.65.1869

Breast.citation: McCormick et al, JCO, 2015 McCormick et al, JCO, 2021
Breast.patients: Mammogram detected, grade 1-2, size <2.5 cm, margins ≥3mm
Breast.methods: Lumpectomy ➜ 50 or 42.5 Gy WBRT vs. ➜ obs TAM optional Choice of RT to 50 Gy or 42.5 Gy/16 fx Boost not allowed
Breast.results: 7-yr IBTR 6.7% vs. 0.9% 10-yr IBTR 9.2% vs. 1.5% 15-yr IBTR 15.1% vs. 7.1% (10-yr invasive IBTR 4.3% vs. 0.4%) (15-yr invasive IBTR 9.5% vs. 5.4%) In obs, 76% of IBTR were in same quadrant 10-yr DM ~1%, not different 15-yr DM ~3%, not different 15-yr contralat event 10% vs. 7%, p=0.27 10-yr OS 92%, not different 15-yr OS 82%, not different Grade 3 effects 1% ~70% intended to use tam in both arms Tam actual adherence 58% vs. 66% 10% had hypofractionation No recurrences in those who had hypofx Trial closed early due to poor accrual
Breast.interpretation: WBRT for low risk DCIS improves IBTR, but observation can be an appropriate option as well in this benign disease. Decision making should be individualized. IBTR continues to rise with f/u. OS is unchanged.
Breast.commentary: 73% of tumors were <1 cm. Note that if the recurrence rate is 1% per year, a patient will have a 15% chance of recurrence in 15 years and so on. There was no subanalysis of effectiveness hypofractionation vs. WBRT. The trial was in the 2D era and thus no dose constraints were included. Note that RT is first choice per NCCN. It is more effective than TAM, is completed in a shorter time period, and has higher compliance, especially with hypofractionation. TAM causes cognitive decline (TAILORx, Wagner 2020). TAM adherence is unreliable: 20-60% will discontinue (Shinn 2019). In other trials such a…

Breast.patients: Postmenopausal DCIS ER+ s/p resection and radiotherapy, negative margins
Breast.methods: ➜ anastrozole vs. ➜ TAM x 5 yrs
Breast.results: Median f/u 7.2 years. No difference in overall recurrences or death. Tamoxifen: more muscle spasm, gyn cancers, gyn symptoms, vasomotor symptoms, DVTs Anastrozole: more fractures, MSK events, hypercholesterolemia, strokes
Breast.interpretation: There is no difference in efficacy between anastrozole and TAM in DCIS. The side effect profiles differ.
Breast.references: 1. Forbes JF, Sestak I, Howell A, et al. Anastrozole versus tamoxifen for the prevention of locoregional and contralateral breast cancer in postmenopausal women with locally excised ductal carcinoma in situ (IBIS-II DCIS): a double-blind, randomised controlled trial. Lancet 2016;387:866–873. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(15)01129-0

Breast.patients: Postmenopausal DCIS ER+ s/p resection and radiotherapy, negative margins
Breast.methods: ➜ anastrozole vs. ➜ TAM x 5 yrs
Breast.results: 10-yr BCFI 93.1% vs. 89.1% BCFI Benefit of anastrozole in age <60 10-yr DFS 82.7% vs. 77.9% 10-yr OS 92.5% vs. 92.1% More thromboembolic events with Tam
Breast.interpretation: Anastrozole improves BCFI over TAM in DCIS age <60.

Breast.citation: Houghton et al, Lancet, 2003 Cuzick et al, Lancet Oncol, 2010
Breast.patients: DCIS s/p resection with negative margin
Breast.methods: ➜ obs vs. ➜ TAM x 5 yrs vs. ➜ RT 50 Gy (no boost) vs. ➜ RT + TAM
Breast.results: IBTR 22% obs, 18% T, 8% RT, 6% RT+T 10-yr IBTR 17% obs, 13% T, 2.6% RT, 2.4% RT+T RT reduced both ipsi invasive and ipsi DCIS When RT is given, TAM had no benefit on ipsi invasive or ipsi DCIS, or even contralateral tumors RT and TAM do not interact
Breast.interpretation: WBRT for DCIS improves IBTR. Long term, tamoxifen does not reduce ipsilateral or contralateral tumor recurrence when RT is given. However this study was performed prior to the receptor era, and endocrine results may not be applicable in that setting.
Breast.commentary: Trial was done prior to era of receptor analysis. ER+ not required for enrollment. Older patients in this trail compared to NSABP-24.
Breast.references: 1. Houghton J. Radiotherapy and tamoxifen in women with completely excised ductal carcinoma in situ of the breast in the UK, Australia, and New Zealand: randomized controlled trial. Lancet 2003;362:95-102. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/s0140-6736(03)13859-7 2. Cuzick J, Sestak I, Pinder SE, et al. Breast.citation: Fisher et al, Lancet, 1999 Allred et al, Lancet, 2012
Breast.patients: S/p WLE for DCIS (including + margin) ER± allowed at time of trial
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 50 Gy + TAM x 5 yrs vs. ➜ 50 Gy alone boost given in 38.5% Post-hoc analysis of ER+
Breast.results: First publication results (not evaluating ER+/-): 5-yr BCA events 8% vs. 13%, IBTR 6% vs. 9.5%, CBTR 2% vs. 3% 5-yr invasive 4% vs. 7%, inv IBTR 2% vs. 4%, inv CBTR (NS) 2% vs. 2% -Post-hoc analysis of ER+ (n=732): -Decrease in total breast cancer, total IBC, and any contralateral BC at 10-yrs but not ipsilateral recurrence of any kind ER+ 10-yr any recurrence 20% TAM vs. 31% (SS) ER+ 10-yr B/L IBC 12% vs. 19% (SS) ER+ 10-yr B/L DCIS 9% vs. 12% (NS) ER+ 10-yr contra IBC/DCIS 6% vs.11% -On MVA of those ER+, individual benefits were small and not significant in DCIS recurrence alone, or ipsilate…
Breast.interpretation: TAM added to RT reduces invasive breast cancer recurrence (among all patients before ER was assessed) in DCIS. When testing ER, ER+ tumors had reduced total invasive breast cancer events with TAM, although isolated effects were small.
Breast.commentary: This better LC benefit with RT compared to TAM is why RT for DCIS is category 1 on NCCN. TAM functions to reduce total bilateral and contralateral breast recurrences overall. NS interactions at individual sites including ipsilateral breast for both or either IBC and DCIS. Furthermore, TAM may be merely tumorostatic, not tumoricidal. 16% had positive margins
Breast.references: 1. Fisher B, Land S, Mamounas E, et al. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2010.34.0141

Breast.patients: DCIS s/p BCS Age <50 or Age ≥50 with at least one of: margin <10 mm, palpable tumor, multifocal, size ≥1.5 cm, grade 2-3, necrosis, comedo
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 16 Gy boost vs. ➜ no boost ➜ 🏆 hypofx 42.5 Gy/16 fx vs. ➜ conventional 50 Gy/25 fx CTV 10 mm, PTV 5-10 mm Endocrine therapy given in 13%
Breast.results: 🔀 boost vs. no boost ⬇️ 5-yr IBTR 3% vs. 7% Regional recurrence ≤1% in both arms DM also ≤1% in both arms No variation in any subgroup by grade/necrosis, margin, age, endocrine therapy, conventional vs. hypofx, or size grade ≥2 breast pain 14% vs. 10% grade ≥2 induration 14% vs. 6% grade 4 adverse effects rare 🔀 hypofx vs. conventional ✅ 5-yr IBTR 6% in both arms, noninferior
Breast.interpretation: Boost improves LR in all types of DCIS. On subanalysis the benefit is present in all subgroups, with none favored over the other. Grade ≥2 adverse effects are moderately increased. Hypofx should become standard of care in DCIS. LC is maintained.
Breast.commentary: Although the trial's stated purpose was to enroll "non-low-risk" DCIS, low risk DCIS were in reality included as well due to eligibility criterion of any DCIS with margins <10 mm in ages ≥50. 25-29% were grade 1-2 without comedonecrosis (specific numbers for grade 1 or 2 were not shown). Tumor size was ≤10 mm in 26-32% and only 17-18% had close (≤2 mm) margins. Benefit remained present in all factors on subanalysis. Thus the trial can support boost for any kind of DCIS including low risk. Only 13% had endocrine therapy. Favorable LC can be achieved with RT plus boost in the absence of ET in DC…
Breast.references: 1. Chua BH, Link M, Kunkler I, et al. Radiation doses and fractionation schedules in non-low-risk ductal carcinoma in situ in the breast (BIG 3–07/TROG 07.01): a randomised, factorial, multicentre, open-label, phase 3 study. Lancet 2022;400:431-440. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(22)01246-6

Breast.citation: Offersen et al, JCO, 2020 Thomsen et al, Radiother Oncol, 2024
Breast.patients: Invasive N0 or DCIS, age >40 (13% were DCIS)
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 40 Gy/ 15 fx vs. ➜ 50 Gy/ 25 fx boost allowed
Breast.results: 9-yr LRR 3% both arms -LRR results maintained in DCIS alone and invasive alone 3-yr induration 9.0% vs. 11.8% -in large breast no worsening of induration with 40 vs. 50 Gy. These also tended to have hotspots -Hypofx also had more favorable cosmetic outcome ratings, less dyspigmentation, edema 9-yr OS 93% both arms heart and lung adverse effects rare -Boost gave worse induration with large volume or smoking
Breast.interpretation: Hypofractionation and conventional RT had no difference in LRR in invasive breast and DCIS Hypofx did not lead to more induration than conventional RT and appeared to have better cosmetic results Large breast patients, including those with hotspots, did not have worse induration with 40 Gy.
Breast.commentary: These patients were generally medium risk; lowest risk were enrolled in a Danish PBI trial. About 50% were T1c. Smoking and large breast did increase risk of induration overall, but no worsening in 40 Gy vs. 50 Gy. Boost actually didn't lead to worse induration, perhaps because these tended to have more chemo, providing more time to heal after surgery. 13% were DCIS. Boost in 23% (~85% had 10 Gy). Endocrine therapy in 35% of DCIS, chemo in ~35-40% of invasive. Heart V35 <5%, V17 <10% Dose constraints protocol pdf, page 11: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.20.01363/suppl_file/Protocol…

Breast.citation: Solin et al, JNCI, 2013 Rakovitch et al, Breast Cancer Res Treat, 2015 Rakovitch et al, ASCO, 2017
Breast.patients: DCIS s/p surgery without RT from ECOG trial
Breast.methods: Recurrence score of DCIS patients without RT. Developed based on DCIS samples from ECOG cohorts External validation in Ontario cohort 2nd version of DCIS score made with ECOG + Ontario cohorts also with age and tumor size
Breast.results: 10 yr risk IBE: low risk 11%, int risk 27%, high risk 26% 10-yr risk IBE invasive: low risk 4%, int risk 12%, high risk 19% DCIS score distribution by grade -Grade 1: 91% low, 7% int, 2% high -Grade 2: 73% low, 17% int, 11% high -Grade 3: 35% low, 19% int, 46% high Externally validated in Ontario cohort The test predictability was later improved in 2017 using both the ECOG and Ontario cohorts, also taking into account age and tumor size.
Breast.interpretation: The DCIS score quantifies recurrence risk and provides a new tool for individualized treatment.
Breast.commentary: When considering RT vs. TAM or both, RT is first line (NCCN 2019). It is more effective than TAM, is completed in a shorter time period, and has higher compliance, especially with hypofractionation. ET causes cognitive decline (TAILORx, Wagner 2020). Discontinuation rates for endocrine therapy are 20-60% (Shinn 2019). Following that premise, Oncotype could generally be used in patients who either are considering no therapy, or who prefer TAM but have personal prohibitions against radiation. If through shared decision making the patient wishes to have adjuvant therapy for LC, RT is first line o…

Breast.commentary: Early stage overview -Landmark study NSABP B06, and the Milan I study and others, showed that lumpectomy plus WBRT was not different than total mastectomy -Other randomized trials also confirmed the benefit of WBRT, and the landmark EBCTCG meta-analysis of these trials was the first to show a BCM benefit with WBRT. Since then treatment is more nuanced based on subtypes, age, and new developments in chemotherapy and sequencning -NSABP B21 and PMH, and other studies not listed here, established the benefit of TAM -EORTC 22881 and Lyon, as well as some other studies, showed LC benefit with boost.…

Breast.patients: 78 randomized trials involving RT and BSC
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis of 78 randomized trials BCS → ➜ obs vs. ➜ 🏆 WBRT
Breast.results: node negative ⬇️ 5-yr LRR 23% vs. 7% ⬇️ 10-yr LRR 29% vs. 10% ⬇️ 5-yr BCM 6% vs. 5% ⬇️ 10-yr BCM 13% vs. 11% ⬇️ 15-yr BCM 21% vs. 17% pN+ ⬇️ 5-yr LRR 41% vs. 11% ⬇️ 10-yr LRR 47% vs. 13% ⬇️ 5-yr BCM 22% vs. 20% ⬇️ 10-yr BCM 43% vs. 34% ⬇️ 15-yr BCM 51% vs. 43% All patients •5-yr OS 89% vs. 90% •10-yr OS 75% vs. 77% •15-yr OS 62% vs. 65% -If 5-yr risk LC>10%, then 15-yr BCM 50% vs. 45%. If LR risk <10%, very small OS benefit with RT -ER negative and patients who do not receive Tam have less benefit from RT. -Higher grade has more benefit from RT. -On subanalysis, recurrence rates decrease as ag…
Breast.interpretation: RT remains standard of care in BCS. This meta-analysis is the first study to reveal benefit in BCM and OS with RT. RT halves rates of any recurrence (LRR or DM) and reduces mortality by about 1/6 in all populations. One breast cancer death is avoided for every four LRRs avoided.
Breast.commentary: Some distinguish this study from modern era. Endocrine therapy, surgical techniques, and radiation techniques have all advanced, and breast cancer treatment now differs by subtype. And perhaps more importantly, screening has improved, detecting new breast tumors at even smaller sizes (Kaplan 2015). Adverse effects Rate Ratios Non breast mortality: 1.12 Cardiac: 1.27 Lung cancer: 1.78 2nd cancers: 1.6-2 Major BCS trials: NSABP-06, Milan I, EORTC, Danish, NCI, Gustave-Roussy
Breast.notes: Early stage overview -Landmark study NSABP B06, and the Milan I study and others, showed that lumpectomy plus WBRT was not different than total mastectomy -Other randomized trials also confirmed the benefit of WBRT, and the landmark EBCTCG meta-analysis of these trials was the first to show a BCM benefit with WBRT. Since then treatment is more nuanced based on subtypes, age, and new developments in chemotherapy and sequencning -NSABP B21 and PMH, and other studies not listed here, established the benefit of TAM -EORTC 22881 and Lyon, as well as some other studies, showed LC benefit with boost.…

Breast.methods: Analysis of prospective Swedish database OS in BCS+RT, Mx, Mx+PMRT stepwise adjusted by tumor, chemo, endocrine therapy, age, year, region, prognostic group, Nottingham grade, subtype, socioeconomic factors
Breast.results: OS and BCSS worse with Mx and Mx+PMRT than with BCS+RT M+RT had increased mortality in T1N0, T1N1, T2N0, but not T1N2. For T2N1 and T2N2 there was nonsignificant increase
Breast.interpretation: In this largescale database analysis with adjustment for confounders, mastectomy and mastectomy with PMRT were associated with worse OS and BCSS compared to BCS+RT. This is contradictory to the randomized trials that show no difference in OS between mastectomy and BCS.
Breast.commentary: The benefit in BCS could be due to stage nuances that are not captured by the staging system. It seems that patients were adjusted by T stage and N stage, not by exact size of tumor and # nodes. If this is the case, the mastectomy group likely consisted of "bigger" T2s (and possibly T1s) and N1s and N2s with more nodes. It is obvious that there is a difference between a T2 that is 2.1 cm and one that is 4.9 cm. The same holds for N stage. Other explanations for the benefit of RT may be due to RT generating an immune response or targeting occult disease that is missed by mastectomy. There is a …

Breast.citation: Sjӧstrӧm et al, JCO, 2017 Kovács et al, JCO, 2019
Breast.patients: Stage I-II breast cancer
Breast.methods: Lumpectomy ➜ WBRT vs. ➜ obs Subtyping performed TILs assessed
Breast.results: Systemic adjuvant treatment in only 8% 10-yr IBTR results: RT improved IBTR in Luminal A-B and triple negative -Luminal A, 9% vs. 19% -Luminal B, 8% vs. 24% -triple negative, 6% vs. 21% -Her2 19% vs. 15%, p=0.6 (but not powered) -Overall IBTR 6% vs. 20% -Overall difference in RT effect between subtypes was weak, p=.21 Better benefit with RT in low TILs
Breast.interpretation: Subtype was not predictive of response to radiation, although in this study, Her2+ seemed most radioresistant and triple negative the most sensitive. The trial however was not powered for subtype comparison. Those with low tumor infiltrating lymphocytes have a better benefit with RT than those with high TILs.
Breast.commentary: Although the results are interesting, recognize that the trial was not powered for subtype comparison. Luminal A-like: ER+, PR+, Her2-, Ki-67 low Luminal B-like: ER+, PR- or Ki-67 high, Her2- Her2+: Her2+, any ER/PR and any Ki-67 Triple negative Luminal and nonluminal Her2 were combined to one group due to group sizes

Breast.patients: Stage I/II, <4cm
Breast.methods: ➜ Total mastectomy vs. ➜ lumpectomy vs. ➜ 🏆 lumpectomy + 50 Gy (no boost) Negative margins required. ALND in all arms. If (+) nodes, then melphalan and flourouracil.
Breast.results: At 20 years, no difference in OS (46%), CSS (60%), or distant disease-free survival. IBTR 14% RT lumpectomy vs 39%
Breast.interpretation: Long-term OS, CSS, and DM are identical with BCT vs. mastectomy. LC is improved when adding RT to lumpectomy vs lumpectomy alone.
Breast.commentary: Bernie Fisher revolutionized breast cancer care. He was a surgeon advocating against surgery, for chemo and endocrine therapy, and for women's autonomy. These were some of the first ever randomized trials. At the time there was fierce oppositon to his ideas, and the trial was run mainly in community centers. Surgeons were so opposed to the findings that practice took years to change even after the trial, and the axillary portion was repeated in multiple other trials. He was advised by some not to attend conferences in order to avoid physical altercations. Later in his career he became a politi…
Breast.references: 1. Fisher B, Bryant J, Dignam JJ, et al. Tamoxifen, Radiation Therapy, or Both for Prevention of Ipsilateral Breast Tumor Recurrence After Lumpectomy in Women With Invasive Breast Cancers of One Centimeter or Less. J Clin Oncol 2002;20:4141-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa022152

Breast.patients: cT1N0, <70yo
Breast.methods: ➜ Radical mastectomy vs. ➜ quadrantectomy with ALND then 50 Gy + 10 Gy boost. CMF for N+
Breast.results: At 20 years, no difference in OS (58%) or cancer-specific survival (75%). LR 8.8% with RT vs. 2.3%
Breast.interpretation: Long-term OS and CSS are identical with BCT vs. mastectomy. LC is reduced with breast conservation.

Breast.results: 8-yr IBTR 17% TAM vs. 3% TAM RT vs. 9% RT 14-yr IBTR 29% TAM vs. 10% TAM RT vs. 11% RT 14-yr OS 78-82%, not different

Breast.methods: ➜ TAM with RT 45 Gy + 14 Gy boost vs. ➜ TAM alone
Breast.results: RT improves LRR, but no change in OS or DM 5-yr LRR 4% without RT vs. 1% 10-yr LRR 10% vs. 2% 10-yr DM 5% in both arms 10-yr OS ~67% in both arms Tam compliance was not reported
Breast.interpretation: WBRT in elderly patients with low risk tumors improves IBTR. IBTR continues to rise with f/u. There is no benefit in OS or DM. Observation may be reasonable in some populations. Treatment should be individualized.
Breast.commentary: Results may not apply to grade 3. The distribution of grades enrolled was not revealed in the CALGB study, and in PRIME II there were only 2-3% grade 3. Should there be a standard age or subtype cutoff for RT for breast cancer? A personalized approach calls practioners to assess goals, comorbidities, life expectancy, and competing risk with a discussion with the patient. Regarding the lack of OS benefit, it should be emphasized that many women with breast cancer "don't want to go through this again" and value the benefit of LC. One in ten women at 10 years is not a small number for all. A mini…

Breast.patients: age ≥55, cT1N0 luminal A subtype (ER ≥1%, PR >20%, Ki67 ≤13.25%), grade 1-2, s/p lumpectomy with ≥1 mm margins Median age was 67
Breast.methods: Single arm prospective: ET alone
Breast.results: 5-yr LF 2.3% (met the prespecified boundary) 5-yr contralateral breast cancers 1.9% Median size 1.1 cm, median age 67
Breast.interpretation: Luminal A subtype have low risk of recurrence at 5 years with endocrine therapy without RT.
Breast.commentary: Although age ≥55 was permitted, the median IQR age was 67. 32% were age 70 or older and 59% were age 65 or older. They report that 83% were still receiving endocrine therapy if their last follow-up visit was "at 5 years or earlier." Subanalyses of any kind were not performed. If the recurrence rate in luminal A is so low, can we also omit all adjuvant therapy altogether, or treat with radiation alone without endocrine therapy? Radiation is better tolerated and completed in a shorter time period than ET. This idea will be tested in EUROPA. Another attractive approach is evaluation of treatment …

Breast.citation: Blamey et al, Eur J Cancer, 2013
Breast.patients: T1N0, grade 1 (or tubular, cribriform, tubular/cribriform, papillary or mucinous), negative margins, s/p WLE (all enrolled were age <70)
Breast.methods: ➜ RT vs. ➜ no RT ➜ TAM vs. ➜ no TAM
Breast.results: All were age <70 TAM vs. no TAM did not meet accrual LR per year at 10-yrs: 1.9% obs vs. 0.7% RT vs. 0.8% TAM vs. 0% TAM+RT Only 7 DM among all (arms not reported) RT and TAM had nonsignificant improvements in OS
Breast.interpretation: Observation in these good prognosis patients of age <70 leads to excess LR. TAM and RT both individually reduce the rate, and do so even more when combined together. TAM alone or RT alone may be reasonable therapy for some.
Breast.commentary: The TAM vs. no TAM randomization did not meet accrual and may be underpowered. It is unclear from the manuscript if the groups were balanced. Median age was 57, range 33-69. ER/PR status was not assessed at that time of the trial. The authors point out that >90% of grade 1 have been shown to be hormone positive. These trials emerged from an era when radiotherapy was 6 weeks. Noninferior regimens of 5 or 15 fractions have since been developed. A provocative essay entitled "Endocrine Treatment for 5 Years or Radiation for 5 Days" (Naoum 2022) makes a succinct point regarding the durations of the…

Breast.patients: age ≥70, cT1N0, Luminal A. Any grade if size ≤10 mm, grade 1-2 if size 11-19 mm, s/p lumpectomy
Breast.methods: ➜ PBI or WBRT in 5-15 fx vs. ➜ Endocrine therapy (letrozole, anastrozole, exemestane, tamoxifen)
Breast.results: 2-yr global health score change -3.40 vs. -9.79 Adverse effects 67% vs. 85% Serious adverse events 15% both arms Primary endpoints: EORTC QLQ-C30 global health and noninferiority of LC Seconday endpoints: Other QOL, DM, BCM, OS, adverse effects
Breast.interpretation: At this two year interim analysis, radiation therapy has improved QOL and less adverse effects than endocrine therapy. Additional endpoints will be reported with longer follow-up.

Breast.patients: Oncotype RS ≤18 age 50-69, pT1N0, ER/PR+, Her2-, margins ≥2 mm, s/p lumpectomy
Breast.methods: Prospective Oncotype RS ≤18 and agree to 5 years endocrine therapy ➜ omit radiation therapy
Breast.results: 5-yr BCM 0% 5-yr OS 100% age 50-59 crude IBTR 3% age 60-69 crude IBTR 4%
Breast.interpretation: Omission of radiation therapy in those with low Oncotype score who take endocrine therapy leads to low rates of local recurrence at 5 years.
Breast.commentary: Like the TAILORX and RXPONDER trials before it, IDEA seeks to identify what is overtreatment and spare patients from unneeded therapy. The follow up might be considered short, but may be appropriate for some situations. The Oncotype score required here of ≤18 is lower than that of TAILORX and RXPONDER, which have Oncotype ≤25 as indication. The DEBRA trial is a phase III trial to randomize this question. Future directions perhaps should include evaluating omission of endocrine therapy in those with low Oncotype.

Breast.citation: Bartelink et al, JCO, 2007 Jones et al, JCO, 2009 Bartelink et al, Lancet Oncol, 2015 Vrieling et al, JAMA Oncol, 2017
Breast.patients: Microscopically complete excision and ax dissection (T1-2, N0-1)
Breast.methods: 50 Gy WBRT ➜ no boost vs. ➜ 🏆 16 Gy boost
Breast.results: ⬇️ 5-yr LR 7.3% vs. 4.3% ⬇️ 10-yr LR 10% vs. 6% ⬇️ 20-yr LR 16% vs. 12% ⬇️ 10-yr LR age <40, 24% vs. 14% ⬇️ 10-yr LR age 41-50, 13% vs. 9% ⬇️ 10-yr LR age 51-60, 8% vs. 5% ⬇️ 10-yr LR age >60, 7% vs. 4% ⬇️ 20-yr LR if adjacent DCIS, 18% vs. 9% ⬇️ 20-yr LR if adjacent DCIS+age ≤50, 31% vs. 15% Mastectomies increased 41% ⬆️ 10-yr fibrosis 1.6% vs. 4.4% ⬆️ 20-yr severe fibrosis 1.8% vs. 5.2%, moderate-severe 15% vs. 30% OS not different. Initial subanalysis: Age <50 and grade 3 are risk factors for IBTR 20-yr f/u MVA: young age and adjacent DCIS are risk factors for IBTR. Risk with grade 3 dimini…
Breast.interpretation: 16 Gy boost improves local control after whole breast radiation for lumpectomy. Fibrosis is increased. OS is unchanged. On exploratory subanalysis, 16 Gy boost reduces LR in all age groups. Younger age benefits the most. Grade 3 benefits in earlier f/u, and those with adjacent DCIS benefit at long term f/u.
Breast.commentary: 10 Gy or 16 Gy. Since the innovation of SIB boost, the choice between 10 and 16 Gy is less of a discussion, but may still be relevant for some centers and some regimens. The 48 Gy SIB dose is equivalent to a 13-16 Gy sequential boost. Interestingly, 10 Gy boost seems to have become commonplace, but 16 Gy has overwhelmingly better phase III evidence. The phase III trial that evaluated 10 Gy, Lyon, was single institution and found only a 1% benefit at 5 years. Some hold concerns for fibrosis with 16 Gy: the EORTC study shows 5% vs. 2% severe risk at 20 years, and 15% vs. 30% risk of moderate-sev…

Breast.patients: ≤3cm tumor s/p WLE and ALND
Breast.methods: 50 Gy WBRT ➜ no boost vs. ➜ 🏆 10 Gy boost
Breast.results: 5-yr LC 4.5% vs. 3.6% (p=0.044) More telangiectasia, but no difference in self-assessed cosmesis.
Breast.interpretation: 10 Gy boost reduces LF by 1% at 5 years. Cosmesis was not worsened.
Breast.commentary: Some experience Mandela effect for the trial, but long term follow-up was never reported. The study is single instituion and shows a 1% benefit at 5 years, less of a benefit than in EORTC, yet probably with less fibrosis. For those who have concerns with fibrosis with 16 Gy, 10 Gy offers a middle ground, although with less evidence. Regardless, the advent of SIB boost regimens now allow for shorter regimens that are more cost effective and convenient, with 48 Gy in 15 fractions SIB. See commentary on EORTC.
Breast.references: 1. Romestaing P, Lehingue Y, Carrie C, et al. Role of a 10-Gy boost in the conservative treatment of early breast cancer: results of a randomized clinical trial in Lyon, France. J Clin Oncol 1997;15:963-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1997.15.3.963

Breast.methods: ➜ conventional or hypofrac with sequential boost vs. ➜ 🏆 48 Gy boost + 40 Gy WBRT/ 16 fx SIB
Breast.results: 5-yr IBTR 2% both arms 5-yr OS 98% both arms Grade ≥2 fibrosis 8% vs. 7% Grade ≥2 Telangiectasia 1.5% both arms
Breast.interpretation: 48 Gy SIB boost is noninferior to sequential hypofractionation plus boost. Treatment duration is reduced and IBTR is noninferior.
Breast.commentary: 48 Gy boost is equivalent to a 13-16 Gy sequential boost. Those preferring a 10 Gy sequential boost might consider 46-47 Gy simultaneously, but this would not be specially supported by a trial. 16 Gy is supported by the multi institutional EORTC trial, while 10 Gy has moderate support from a smaller trial, Lyon, and may lead to less fibrosis.
Breast.references: 1. Krug D, Dellas K, Schreiber A, et al. Hypofractionated Whole-Breast Irradiation with Simultaneous Integrated Boost for Breast Cancer: Primary Analysis of the HYPOSIB-Trial (ARO 2013-05). Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2024;120:s13-14.

Breast.patients: pT1-3, N0-3a, M0 BCS
Breast.methods: Noninferiority ➜ 40 Gy/15 fx WBRT + seq 16 Gy boost vs. ➜ 🏆 48/40/36 Gy/15 fx SIB to cavity/PBI/WBRT vs. ➜ 53/40/36 Gy/15 fx SIB to cavity/PBI/WBRT IMRT was not required
Breast.results: ✅ 5-yr IBTR 2% vs. 2% vs. 3%, noninferior 5-yr DM 8% in all arms 5-yr LRR ~5% 5-yr induration/hardness: Less with 48 Gy Clinician moderate to marked breast induration 12% vs. 11% vs. 16% 5-yr Any breast adverse event 33% vs. 30% vs. 35% Patient rated 28% vs. 15% vs. 24%
Breast.interpretation: 48 Gy SIB boost is noninferior to sequential hypofractionation plus boost. Treatment duration is reduced and IBTR is noninferior. 53 Gy SIB and 16 Gy sequential boost worsened cosmesis.
Breast.commentary: IMRT was not required. About 50% had inverse planning. The boost volume in UK IMPORT HIGH is smaller than in RTOG 1005. Some who are opposed to partial breast may wish to use 4005 cGy to cover the remainder of the breast. Note the inclusion criteria: the post-hoc EORTC indications for boost were not required (such as young age, grade 3, etc) (Bartelink 2015). However characteristics suggest that they were probably mostly met: median age was 49 (IQR 44-56), 53% grade 3, 9% grade 1, 38% grade 2. 30% were N+. About 10% had RT to lymph nodes (~90% to SCV). 48 Gy boost is equivalent to a 13-16 Gy s…

Breast.methods: Noninferiority ➜ 50 Gy/25 fx or 42.7 Gy/ 16 fx WBRT + sequential 12-14 Gy boost vs. ➜ 🏆 48 Gy boost + 40 Gy WBRT/ 15 fx SIB
Breast.results: ✅ 5-yr IBTR 2.1% vs. 1.9% (noninferior) ✅ 7-yr IBTR 2.2% vs. 2.6% (noninferior) ✅ No difference in grade ≥3 effects ✅ 3-yr excellent/good cosmesis not different QA per protocol in 81% vs. 88% IMRT used in 19%

Breast.commentary: 48 Gy boost is equivalent to a 13-16 Gy sequential boost. Those preferring a 10 Gy sequential boost might consider 46-47 Gy simultaneously, but this would not be specially supported by a trial. 16 Gy is supported by the multi institutional EORTC trial, while 10 Gy has some support by a smaller single institution study, Lyon, with the possible benefit of less fibrosis. RTOG chose a 12-14 Gy boost as the control - a regimen not well studied - as a middle ground in an apparent attempt to avoid strong opinions on either side and as a compromise for enrollment and consistency. 48 Gy tumor bed + 1 c…

Breast.citation: Brunt et al, Lancet, 2020 Brunt et al, Lancet Oncol, 2026
Breast.patients: pT1-3, N0-1, M0 BCS or mastectomy (93% had BCS)
Breast.methods: Noninferiority ➜ 40 Gy/ 15 fx vs. ➜ 27 Gy/ 5 fx daily vs. ➜ 🏆 26 Gy/ 5 fx daily Field: breast or CW (Substudy: inclusion of RNI) Boost of 10 or 16 Gy in ~25%
Breast.results: recurrence and survival were noninferior ↔️ 5-yr IBTR 2.1% vs. 1.7% vs. 1.4% 10-yr IBTR 3.6% vs. 2.9% vs. 2.1% ↔️ 5-yr LRR 3.2% vs. 2.6% vs. 2.1% ↔️ 5-yr DM 4.3% vs. 5.0% vs. 5.6% 10-yr DM not different ↔️ Any breast ca event 8.7% vs. 8.2% vs. 8.3% ↔️ All cause mortality 6.8% vs. 7.7% vs. 6.6% 10-yr mortality not different cosmetic effects worse in 27 Gy 10-yr marked/moderate effects 13% vs. 19% vs. 14% 10-yr breast appearance changed 37% vs. 44% vs. 38% RNI results pending
Breast.interpretation: 26 Gy in 5 daily fractions to the whole breast/CW is noninferior to 40 Gy in 15 fractions. Results are maintained at long term 10-yr follow-up. 27 Gy was noninferior in LC but cosmetic effects were worse. Results for the RNI cohort are pending.
Breast.commentary: Why were 26 Gy and 27 Gy both evaluated? BED calculations predicted a falloff at this point and potential for interpolation of dosing based on results (see the protocol appendix). 93% had breast conservation surgery, 7% mastectomy. A subanalysis to evaluate effects in breast vs. chest wall was not performed and likely would not have had adequate power. Extrapolation from UK IMPORT LOW would suggest the regimen is also appropriate for partial breast. 25% had boost (of these, 75-80% had 10 Gy and 20-25% had 16 Gy). ~95% had ET. ~25% had chemo. heart V1.5 <30%, V7 <5% ipsilateral lung V8 <15%

Breast.results: LR 1% with all doses (at 9.9 yrs f/u) 10-yr cosmetic effects were worse with 30 Gy, and not different between 50 Gy and 28.5 Gy
Breast.interpretation: At 10 year f/u, there is no difference in cosmetic effects between 50 Gy and 28.5 Gy/ 5 fx in once weekly fractions. Cosmesis with 30 Gy was worse. Local recurrence was very low in all arms.

Breast.citation: Offersen et al, JCO, 2020 Thomsen et al, Radiother Oncol, 2024
Breast.patients: Invasive N0 or DCIS, age >40 (13% were DCIS)
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 40 Gy/ 15 fx vs. ➜ 50 Gy/ 25 fx boost allowed
Breast.results: 9-yr LRR 3% both arms -LRR results maintained in DCIS alone and invasive alone 3-yr induration 9.0% vs. 11.8% -in large breast no worsening of induration with 40 vs. 50 Gy. These also tended to have hotspots -Hypofx also had more favorable cosmetic outcome ratings, less dyspigmentation, edema 9-yr OS 93% both arms heart and lung adverse effects rare -Boost gave worse induration with large volume or smoking
Breast.interpretation: Hypofractionation and conventional RT had no difference in LRR in invasive breast and DCIS Hypofx did not lead to more induration than conventional RT and appeared to have better cosmetic results Large breast patients, including those with hotspots, did not have worse induration with 40 Gy.
Breast.commentary: These patients were generally medium risk; lowest risk were enrolled in a Danish PBI trial. About 50% were T1c. Smoking and large breast did increase risk of induration overall, but no worsening in 40 Gy vs. 50 Gy. Boost actually didn't lead to worse induration, perhaps because these tended to have more chemo, providing more time to heal after surgery. 13% were DCIS. Boost in 23% (~85% had 10 Gy). Endocrine therapy in 35% of DCIS, chemo in ~35-40% of invasive. Heart V35 <5%, V17 <10% Dose constraints protocol pdf, page 11: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.20.01363/suppl_file/Protocol…

Breast.citation: Korzets et al, Breast Cancer Res Treat, 2019
Breast.patients: invasive breast cancer suitable for BCT
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis of 9 randomized trials ➜ PBI vs. ➜ WBRT
Breast.results: PBI had: -increased LR, OR 1.69, p<0.001 -less death without BC recurrence, OR 0.55, p<0.001 -trend to improved OS, OR 0.84, p=0.06 -EBRT was best modality for PBI -More LR in node+ PBI -Larger tumors had lesser improvement in OS and death without BC recurrence -PBI had worse fat necrosis, and trend to worse cosmesis p=0.06
Breast.interpretation: In this meta-analysis PBI had increased LR but less death without breast cancer recurrence and trend to improved OS. Restricting use of PBI to smaller node negative tumors with EBRT led to more favorable outcomes.
Breast.references: 1. Korzets Y, Fyles A, Shepshelovich D, et al. Toxicity and clinical outcomes of partial breast irradiation compared to whole breast irradiation for early-stage breast cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Breast Cancer Res Treat 2019;175:531-545. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10549-019-05209-9

Breast.patients: invasive breast cancer or DCIS suitable for BCT
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis of IBTR rates and adverse effects in 15 randomized trials ➜ PBI vs. ➜ WBRT
Breast.results: ⛔ Overall IBTR 5.0% PBI vs. 2.8% WBRT Heterogeneity was present due to 2DCRT and IORT. Excluding 2D planning and IORT: ✅ IBTR 3.3% vs. 2.6% ✅ No differences by subgroups (size, ER/PR, histology, nodes, or PBI suitability) Adverse effects: ✅ Acute adverse effects 12% vs. 33% (no heterogeneity) Slightly worse cosmesis with PBI (RR 1.14, p=0.05). Mostly due to RAPID ✅ Late effects similar, 28% vs. 29%. Less in NSABP with PBI, and worse in RAPID and Manchester BCM and OS not analyzed in this study
Breast.interpretation: Recurrence rates between PBI and WBRT are similar, with <1% difference, when excluding 2DCRT and IORT from analysis. PBI leads to less adverse effects. 2DCRT and IORT showed slightly worse recurrence rates. OS and BCM were not re-evaluated in this analysis.
Breast.commentary: This study answers questions many have regarding any differences in technique in PBI. The lack of difference in subgroups, including PBI suitability, is interesting and could suggest the existence of a wider range of suitable criteria for PBI.
Breast.references: 1. Goldberg M, Bridhikitti J, Khan AJ, McGale P, Whelan TJ. Meta-Analysis of Trials of Partial Breast Irradiation [published online ahead of print, 2022 Sep 22]. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2022;S0360-3016(22)03386-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2022.09.062

Breast.citation: Coles et al, Lancet, 2017 Bhattacharya et al, JCO, 2018 Kirby et al, Lancet Oncol, 2025
Breast.patients: age ≥50, unifocal IDC, grade 1-3, size ≤3 cm, 0-3 nodes, margins ≥2 mm
Breast.methods: ➜ 40 Gy/ 15 fx WBRT vs. ➜ 36 Gy WBRT + SIB PBI 40 Gy/15 fx vs. ➜🏆 40 Gy/ 15 fx EBRT PBI no boost
Breast.results: WBRT vs. WBRT SIB PBI vs. PBI Results noninferior ✅ 5-yr IBTR 1.1% vs. 0.2% vs. 0.5% 10-yr IBTR 2.8% vs. 1.9% vs. 3.0% 5-yr LRR 1.1% vs. 0.2% vs. 0.8% 10-yr DM 3.8% vs. 4.1% vs. 2.8% not different 5-yr BC-EFS 3.7% vs. 3.4% vs. 4.0% 10-yr OS 88% vs. 87% vs. 91% Only 2-4% were node positive Adverse events were lower in partial breast and reduced dose vs. WBRT at 5 and 10 years 10-yr breast shrinkage 9% vs. 9% vs. 7%
Breast.interpretation: WBRT, WBRT + EBRT PBI SIB, and EBRT PBI are noninferior.
Breast.commentary: Note very low recurrence rates. Although not a primary endpoint, OS is higher with partial breast. N+ 3%. ET in 91-92% Grade 3 in 10%, Her 2+ in 4% IDC 85% 4-6% had chemotherapy All arms used forward planning FIF IMRT tangents. Not all institutions used 3DCRT, so dose constraints were institutional and optional. Third beams were not utilized. For node positive patients, the axilla or other nodes were not specifically targeted.

Breast.citation: Livi et al, Eur J Cancer, 2015 Meattini et al, Eur J Cancer, 2017 Meattini et al, JCO, 2020
Breast.patients: Age >40, size <2.5cm, IDC or DCIS
Breast.methods: ➜ 30 Gy/ 5 fx QOD IMRT PBI vs. ➜ WBRT 50 Gy/ 25 fx + 10 Gy boost
Breast.results: 5-yr IBTR 1.5% both arms 10-yr IBTR 3.7% vs. 2.5%, not different 10-yr OS ~92%, not different 10-yr BCSS 98% both arms ABPI had improved acute & late adverse effects, and patient and physician assessed cosmesis
Breast.interpretation: IMRT PBI is equivalent to WBRT in LC. Adverse effects, cosmesis, and HRQOL are improved with IMRT PBI. The trial may be underpowered.
Breast.commentary: The authors acknowledge that the study may be underpowered, and that IBTRs were lower than expected. The CI was wide. On the other hand, the results seem similar to other PBI studies, and there was no heterogeneity on meta-analysis. CTV was 1 cm from clips and PTV also 1 cm. Lung V10 ≤20%, contralateral lung V5 ≤10%, heart V3 ≤10%. Viewing these results in combination with UK FAST FORWARD and UK IMPORT LOW, 26 Gy/5 fx daily PBI also seems rational. The treatment could be considered SBRT, especially if respiratory guidance is used. Similar dosing is used in intracranial SRS and lung oligometast…

Breast.citation: Olivotto et al, JCO, 2013 Whelan et al, Lancet Oncol, 2019
Breast.patients: Age >40, size <3cm, IDC or DCIS
Breast.methods: ➜ 38.5 Gy/ 10 fx BID EBRT PBI vs. ➜ WBRT hypofrac or conventional, boost optional
Breast.results: 8-yr IBTR 3.0% EBRT ABPI vs. 2.8% WBRT Late adverse effects worse with EBRT PBI ⛔ Grade ≥2 induration or telangiectasia 32% vs. 13% Grade 3, 4.5% vs. 1.0% 7-yr fair/poor cosmesis 18% worse with ABPI
Breast.interpretation: EBRT PBI is noninferior to WBRT in LC. However late skin effects is worse with EBRT PBI. Authors propose investigation of other regimens.
Breast.commentary: ET in ~68% Grade 3 in 17%, Her2 not reported IDC in 82% chemo in ~15%

Breast.citation: Julian et al, ASCO, 2011 White et al, ASTRO, 2019 Vicini et al, Lancet Oncol, 2019
Breast.patients: Stage 0-II who underwent lumpectomy, tumor <3 cm, ≤3 nodes
Breast.methods: ➜ conventional RT, 50 or 50.4 Gy, optional 10-16 Gy boost vs. ➜ APBI, 34 Gy/ 3.4 BID with multicatheter interstitial or mammosite OR ➜ 38.5 Gy/ 3.85 BID with EBRT
Breast.results: ⛔ IBTR rates not equivalent 10-yr IBTR 4.6% PBI vs. 3.9% WBRT No difference in DM, DFS, or OS Grade 3 effects 10% PBI vs. 7% WBRT Grade 2 effects 44% vs. 59% -Patient assessed cosmesis not different -MD assessed cosmesis blinded to treatment arm was equivalent -73% were EBRT. No subanalysis of EBRT vs. HDR
Breast.interpretation: PBI (with EBRT or mammosite) did not meet criteria for equivalence to WBRT. However absolute differences are small. PBI had slightly worse grade 3 adverse effects. Cosmetic outcomes were not different on patient or MD rated assessments.
Breast.commentary: RAPID showed worse cosmesis with EBRT PBI, and RTOG 0413/B39 showed slightly worse adverse effects. UK IMPORT LOW however showed no difference in adverse effects with PBI compared to WBRT. That trial used EBRT PBI to 15 daily fractions. Grade 3 in 26%, Her2+ not recorded DCIS in 24%, ER+ in 81%
Breast.references: 1. Julian TB, Costantino JP, Vicini FA, et al. Breast.citation: Strnad et al, Lancet, 2016 Polgár et al, Lancet Oncol, 2017
Breast.patients: age ≥40, size ≤3 cm or DCIS with Van Nuys score ≤8, pN0-pNmi, negative margins
Breast.methods: ➜ ABPI with interstitial multicatheter brachy (32.0 Gy/8 fx or 30.3 Gy/ 7 fx BID) vs. ➜ WBRT 50 Gy + 10 Gy boost
Breast.results: No difference in 5-yr LR or DFS LR 1.4% vs. 0.5% 5-yr grade 2-3 skin 12.0% vs. 9.7%, p=0.28 5-yr grade 2-3 breast pain 8.4% vs. 11.9%, p=0.074 No grade 4 skin Pt assessed excellent/good cosmesis 91-92%, not different MD assessed excellent/good cosmesis 93% vs. 90%, p=0.12
Breast.interpretation: Interstitial APBI and WBRT are noninferior. Adverse effects and cosmetic outcomes are not different.
Breast.commentary: Grade 3 in 8% IDC in 74% Her2 not reported ET in 87% chemo in ~10% pNmi in 1%
Breast.references: 1. Breast.citation: Polgár et al, IJROBP, 2007 Polgár et al, IJROBP, 2020
Breast.patients: breast cancer T1N0-N1mi, Grade 1-2, nonlobular, no EIC, negative margins
Breast.methods: ➜ ABPI (either HDR 36.4 Gy/ 7 fx at 5.2 Gy BID or PBI electrons 50 Gy/ 25 fx) vs. ➜ WBRT 50 Gy
Breast.results: 5-yr LR with PBI 4.7% vs. 3.4% (NS) 10-yr LR 5.9% vs. 5.1% (NS) 20-yr LR 9.6 vs. 7.9% Excellent/good cosmesis: 81% vs. 63%. (HDR 81%, EBRT-ABPI 70%) 20-yr cosmesis 79% vs. 60% OS, CSS, and DFS not different 5-yr OS 992-95%, 5-yr CFF 96-98%
Breast.interpretation: ABPI and WBRT have similar LC, CSS, DFS, and OS. HDR ABPI has better cosmesis. Results are maintained long term. The trial may be underpowered.
Breast.commentary: Observe the sample size: the trial is probably underpowered. Long-term follow-up of 10 years in this study. In the discussion the authors acknowledge that the trial may be underpowered. 70-72% had ET or chemo Grade 3 in 0%, Nmi in 4% Her2 not reported, IDC 82%
Breast.references: 1. Polgár C, Fodor J, Major T, et al. Breast.citation: Vaidya et al, Lancet, 2014 Vaidya et al, JAMA Oncol, 2020 Vaidya et al, BMJ, 2020 Vaidya et al, IJROBP, 2023
Breast.patients: early stage breast suitable for WLE, age ≥45, unifocal Characteristics: Grade 3 in 15%, IDC 100%, Her2 12%, ER+ 93%, N+ 16%
Breast.methods: Noninferiority for 5-yr LR ➜ TARGIT IORT 20 Gy/1 fx vs. ➜ WBRT 45-56Gy Tandem cohorts, each stratified: 📌 Immediate: during lumpectomy (previously called "pre-pathology") 📌Delayed: second procedure IORT after final path results ("post-pathology") If adverse features seen on path, those in TARGIT group received supplemental WBRT without boost. Boost was optional in WBRT arm.
Breast.results: 📌 Immediate IORT (pre-pathology) Noninferiority confirmed 5-yr LR 2.11% vs. 0.95% 10-yr LR not different, p=0.28 Supplemental WBRT used in 20% 12-yr BCM 4.4% less with IORT, p=0.005 10-yr non BCM 4.03% vs. 7.50%, p=0.005 12-yr KM HRs: no change in LRFS, OS (p=0.13 favoring IORT), DM, or mastectomy-FS 📌 Results by treatment received: IORT only (no added WBRT) vs. WBRT: LRFS HR 1.22 (CI: 0.95-1.57) p=0.11 (favors WBRT but CI crosses 1) IORT + WBRT vs. WBRT: LRFS HR 1.25 (CI: 0.87-1.80) p=0.24 Grade 3-4 skin less with TARGIT 📌 Delayed IORT after lumpectomy (post-pathology) 5-yr LR 3.96% vs. 1.05%…
Breast.interpretation: 📌 Immediate IORT (pre-pathology) TARGIT IORT with lumpectomy (with WBRT added for high risk features) is noninferior to WBRT. There is a late benefit detected in non BCM. There is no change in LRFS, OS, DM, or mastectomy-FS. 📌 Delayed second-procedure IORT (post-pathology) Delayed IORT was not noninferior in LR compared to WBRT. There was no difference in OS, DMFS, or mastectomy-FS.
Breast.commentary: TARGIT is a potential disruptor in oncology, and there have been questions related to the design of its arms, statistics, benefit, and mechanism of action. TARGIT is applied using a spherical applicator of 50 kV RT. This rapidly attenuates to 5 Gy at 1cm, leading some to question how it could provide adequate coverage (Polgár 2021). TARGIT authors have proposed biologic mechanisms related to avoiding seroma that may lead to increased effectiveness at lower depth (Belletti 2008). Pre-path. 20% who had IORT crossed-over to receive WBRT for high risk features, which some say biases in favor of IO…
Breast.references: 1. Vaidya JS, Wenz F, Bulsara M, et al. Breast.citation: Veronesi et al, Lancet Oncol, 2013 Orecchia et al, Lancet Oncol, 2021
Breast.patients: early stage breast suitable for lumpectomy, size <2.5 cm
Breast.methods: Equivalency trial. ➜ IORT 21 Gy/1 electrons vs. ➜ WBRT 50 Gy + 10 Gy boost If ≥4 nodes, WBRT indicated (but not given to nodes)
Breast.results: 5-yr IBTR 4% vs. 0.5% 10-yr IBTR 8% vs. 1.1% 15-yr IBTR 13% vs. 2.4% no difference in deaths from any cause 15-yr OS 83.4% vs. 82.4% Adverse effects not recorded in protocol 5% of patients in IORT group had risk features that required EBRT Factors related to increased IBTR: ≥4 nodes, grade 3, triple negative
Breast.interpretation: Although designed as an equivalency trial, IBTR seems worse with IORT compared to WBRT, especially long term. [This protocol did not record adverse effects.]
Breast.commentary: Not all of these patients would qualify for ASTRO ABPI appropriateness criteria. 40% of recurrences with IORT were inside the field and 60% outside Grade 3 in 21% N+ 27%, 4% had ≥4 nodes, 21% had 1-3 nodes Her2 in 3% IDC in 80% ET in ~7%, ER+ 81% chemo in 7-8%
Breast.references: 1. Veronesi U, Orecchia R, Maisonneuve P, et al. Intraoperative radiotherapy versus external radiotherapy for early breast cancer (ELIOT): a randomised controlled equivalence trial. Lancet Oncol 2013;14:1269-77. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(13)70497-2 2. Orecchia R, Veronesi U, Maisonneuve P, et al. Breast.patients: Early stage breast tumor <4 cm, negative margins, no ALND
Breast.methods: ➜ PBI to 40-42.5 Gy/8 fx electrons vs. ➜ WBRT 40 Gy/ 15 fx No adjuvant chemo or hormonal therapy
Breast.results: 8-yr LR tumor bed 25% vs. 13% WBRT. Most recurrences in treated quadrant LR invasive ductal 22% vs. 12% Lobular 43% vs. 17% Axillary recurrence 24% vs. 12% Supraclavicular recurrence 6% vs. 5% ILC 34% vs. 8% Extensive DCIS 21% vs. 14% On MVA, grade and LVI predicted LR
Breast.interpretation: WBRT results in improved LC compared to PBI.
Breast.commentary: Note that the subanalysis in this trial and others was important in creating the ASTRO ABPI appropriateness criteria guidelines.
Breast.references: 1. Ribeiro GG, Magee B, Swindell R, et al. The christie hospital breast conservation trial: An update at 8 years from inception. Clin Oncol 1993;5:278-83. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/s0936-6555(05)80900-8

Breast.patients: ER+ postmenopausal, trials of AI vs. TAM
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis of trials. Varying durations and combinations of: ➜ 🏆 AI vs. ➜ TAM
Breast.results: 5 years AI vs 5 years TAM: 10-yr BCM 12% vs. 14% 10-yr recurrence 19% vs. 23% 10-yr DM as first event 14% vs. 16% 10-yr DM any time 17% vs. 21% 10-yr overall mortality 21% vs. 24% Less endometrial cancers and more bone fractures with AIs 10-yr endometrial cancers 0.4% vs. 1.2% 10-yr bone fractures 8.2% vs. 5.5%
Breast.interpretation: AIs reduce recurrence rates and improve BCM compared to TAM in ER+ postmenopausal women.
Breast.references: 1. Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group (EBCTCG). Aromatase inhibitors versus tamoxifen in early breast cancer: patient-level meta-analysis of the randomised trials. Lancet 2015;386:1341-1352. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(15)61074-1

Breast.citation: ATAC Group, Lancet, 2005 Cuzick et al, Lancet Oncol, 2010
Breast.patients: Postmenopausal, any ER status, s/p tx for early-stage breast cancer
Breast.methods: ➜ TAM x 5 yrs vs. ➜ anastrozole vs. ➜ TAM + anastrazole
Breast.results: 10-yr results: In hormone+, anastrozole improved recurrence. No difference in OS. Fractures were more with anastrozole, serious events worse with TAM. Anastrozole had more colorectal and lung cancer, TAM had more endometrial, melanoma, and ovarian cancer
Breast.interpretation: Anastrozole is more effective and has better adherence than TAM in early-stage postmenopausal.
Breast.references: 1. Howell A1, Cuzick J, Baum M, et al. Results of the ATAC (Arimidex, Tamoxifen, Alone or in Combination) trial after completion of 5 years' adjuvant treatment for breast cancer. Lancet 2005;365:60-2. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(04)17666-6 2. Cuzick J, Sestak I, Baum M, et al. Effect of anastrozole and tamoxifen as adjuvant treatment for early-stage breast cancer: 10-year analysis of the ATAC trial. Lancet Oncol 2010;11:1135-41. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(10)70257-6

Breast.patients: Postmenopausal, any ER status, s/p tx for early-stage breast cancer
Breast.methods: ➜ anastrozole vs. ➜ placebo
Breast.results: 49% reduction in breast cancer, 54% reduction in ER+ tumors, and 59% reduction in DCIS (most ER+) No benefit in OS or BCM Anastrozole decreased non breast cancers (most non-melanoma skin cancer) No difference in fractures or CV events
Breast.interpretation: Anastrozole reduced incidence of new breast cancers in postmenopausal patients of any ER status. There was no benefit in OS or BCM.
Breast.references: 1. Cuzick J, Sestak I, Forbes JF, et al. Use of anastrozole for breast cancer prevention (IBIS-II): long-term results of a randomised controlled trial. Lancet 2020;395:117-122. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(19)32955-1

Breast.patients: Premenopausal primary breast cancer
Breast.methods: ➜ TAM x 2 yrs vs. ➜ none
Breast.results: In ER+ patients: >15-yr cumulative mortality HR 0.84 >15-yr BCM HR 0.72
Breast.interpretation: Two years of tamoxifen results in benefit in OS at long term f/u in premenopausal breast cancer.
Breast.commentary: A unique trial in that a survival benefit was detected at long term follow-up
Breast.references: 1. Ekholm M, Bendahl P-O, Fernö M, et al. Two Years of Adjuvant Tamoxifen Provides a Survival Benefit Compared With No Systemic Treatment in Premenopausal Patients With Primary Breast Cancer: Long-Term Follow-Up (> 25 years) of the Phase III SBII:2pre Trial. J Clin Oncol 2016;34:2232-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.65.6272

Breast.subtopic: AI vs TAM in ovarian supression/ ablation in premenopausal
Breast.patients: Premenopausal ER+ with ovarian suppression (goserelin or triptorelin) or ablation
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis of randomized trials (ABCSG XII, SOFT, TEXT, and HOBOE) in premenopausal ER+ with ovarian suppression/ablation ➜ 🏆 AI vs. ➜ Tam
Breast.results: BCR and DM less with AI. No difference in BCM, death without recurrence, or all cause mortality (but median f/u only 8 years). Fractures & 2nd cancer more with AI 5-yr BCR 7% vs. 10% 10-yr BCR 14.7% vs. 17.5% Benefit seen in years 0-4 DM less with AI, RR 0.83 Fractures 6.4% vs. 5.1% 2nd cancers n=22 vs. 10 Endometrial cancer 0.2% vs. 0.3%, not different Non breast cancer deaths rare: 0.9% vs. 0.7%, p=0.36, no difference
Breast.interpretation: AIs reduce the risk of breast cancer recurrence better than tamoxifen in premenopausal, ER+ patients receiving ovarian supression or ablation. There is no difference in BCM or non breast cancer mortality as of yet. The benefit is in years 0-4.
Breast.commentary: Subanalysis: No benefit with AI if ≥4 nodes+ The second cancers were several different types. Median f/u was 8 years. BCM could be improved with longer f/u. Endometrial cancer patients were likely not different due to young age. In the EBCTCG 2015 analysis of AI vs TAM, endometrial cancers were less with AIs.
Breast.references: 1. Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group (EBCTCG). Aromatase inhibitors versus tamoxifen in premenopausal women with oestrogen receptor-positive early-stage breast cancer treated with ovarian suppression: a patient-level meta-analysis of 7030 women from four randomised trials [published online ahead of print, 2022 Feb 3]. Lancet Oncol 2022;S1470-2045(21)00758-0. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(21)00758-0

Breast.citation: Francis et al, NEJM, 2018 Pagani et al, JCO, 2019
Breast.patients: Premenopausal primary breast cancer
Breast.methods: SOFT: ➜ TAM vs. ➜ TAM+ovarian suppression vs. ➜ exemestane+OS (TEXT was similar but without the TAM only arm)
Breast.results: SOFT 8-yr DFS 79% vs. 83% vs. 86% 8-yr OS 91.5% vs. 93.3% vs. 92.1% (p=0.01 for TAM vs. TAM+OS) if still premenopausal, 85% vs. 89% vs. 87% If receiving chemo 8-yr DM benefit 5.1% (range 1-15%) in E+OS vs. T+OS 8-yr DM benefit 5.2% (max 10%) in E+OS vs T 8-yr DM benefit 3.5% in T+OS vs. T If not receiving chemo 8-yr FFDM 97%, with 1-4% benefit in E+OS Grade ≥3 effects 25% vs. 31% vs. 32%
Breast.interpretation: In premenopausal breast cancer, adding ovarian suppression to TAM improved DFS and OS. Adding exemestane leads to even better FFR. The highest risk patients may receive up to 10-15% reduction in DM. However ovarian suppression has notable adverse effects.
Breast.commentary: Results remained on subanalysis with ±node involvement, ±chemo before endocrine therapy, and any premenopausal age. The therapy had high withdrawl due to adverse effects: over 1/4 in ovarian suppression arms withdrew from study due to adverse effects. But bone strengthening agents were not used in study. Ovarian suppression was achieved by triptorelin, oophorectomy, or RT
Breast.references: 1. Francis PA, Pagani O, Fleming GF, et al. Tailoring Adjuvant Endocrine Therapy for Premenopausal Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med 2018;379:122–137. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1803164 2. Pagani O, Francis PA, Fleming GF, et al. Absolute Improvements in Freedom From Distant Recurrence to Tailor Adjuvant Endocrine Therapies for Premenopausal Women: Results From TEXT and SOFT. J Clin Oncol 2019. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.01967

Breast.patients: Early stage breast
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis of 12 trials ➜ 🏆 ET x 5 years plus additional AI vs. ➜ ET x 5 yrs
Breast.results: RR improved 27% More improvement in prior TAM over prior AI, and 5 years additional over 2-3 years additional With +5 years AI: 5-15-yr Recurrence 12% vs. 15% BCM 4.4% vs. 5.0%, p=0.40 DM 6.6% vs. 8.6%, p=0.0010 Node+ 10- vs 5-years AI Rec 16% vs. 20% Node- 10- vs 5-yerars AI Rec 9% vs. 12% 5-yr bone fracture with +5 yrs AI 4.6% vs. 3.4%, p=0.0009
Breast.interpretation: Five years of AI reduced DM. Thus far there is no effect on mortality.
Breast.references: 1. Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group (EBCTCG). Electronic address: bc.overview@ndph.ox.ac.uk; Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group (EBCTCG). Extending the duration of endocrine treatment for early breast cancer: patient-level meta-analysis of 12 randomised trials of aromatase inhibitors in 22 031 postmenopausal women already treated with at least 5 years of endocrine therapy. Lancet 2025;406(10503):603-614. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(25)01013-X

Breast.patients: Early stage breast
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 TAM x 10 yrs vs. ➜ TAM x 5 yrs
Breast.results: 5-yr DFS 91% vs. 86%, p<0.001 5-yr LR 0.8% vs. 1% 2nd primaries 3.4% vs. 6.5%, p=0.0074 No differences in DM or OS
Breast.interpretation: Additional TAM to 10 years improves DFS. There was no benefit in DM or OS.
Breast.commentary: F/u may not be long enough to detect an OS benefit. How were second primaries reduced, including non-breast? The authors explain that lung cancers express aromatase and colon cancers express estrogen receptor β. The IBIS-II study showed similar findings.
Breast.references: Study protocol: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.22.00577/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.22.00577.pdf 1. Iwase T, Saji S, Iijima K, et al. J Clin Oncol 2023;JCO2200577. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.00577

Breast.patients: Postmenopausal ER/PR+ breast cancer (~31% were N1, 66% N0)
Breast.methods: 5 yrs endocrine therapy→ ➜ +5 yrs anastrozole vs. ➜ +2 yrs 🏆
Breast.results: No difference in disease progression or death (HR 0.99, p=0.9) Bone fractures worse with 5 years No differences in secondary endpoints or subgroups
Breast.interpretation: There is no benefit in an additional 5 years of hormone therapy over an additional 2 years (beyond an initial 5 years). Those with an additional 5 years experienced more bone fractures.
Breast.commentary: ABCSG = Austrian Breast and Colorectal Cancer Study Group About 50% had tamoxifen as previous endocrine therapy.

Breast.methods: 5 yrs endocrine therapy→ ➜ 5 yrs letrozole vs. ➜ 🏆 obs
Breast.results: No improvement in DFS or OS.
Breast.interpretation: Additional 5 years of letrozole after TAM/AI did not improve DFS or OS.

Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 TAM x 10 yrs vs. ➜ TAM x 5 yrs
Breast.results: BCM in years 5-14: 12.2% vs. 15% Risk of recurrence 21% vs. 15%
Breast.interpretation: Additional TAM to 10 years improves BCM and reduces recurrence rates.

Breast.trial: Cross Cancer Institute and University of Alberta, Edmonton, Canada
Breast.patients: T1-2N0, Triple negative breast cancer
Breast.methods: Retrospective: BCT vs. MRM vs. MRM+RT
Breast.results: In T1-T2 tumors, LRR free survival better with lumpectomy+RT vs. MRM without RT, 96% vs. 90%
Breast.interpretation: In this retrospective study, BCT led to improved LRR over masectomy in triple negative early stage breast cancer.
Breast.references: 1. Abdulkarim BS, Cuartero J, Hanson J, et al. Increased risk of locoregional recurrence for women with T1-2N0 triple-negative breast cancer treated with modified radical mastectomy without adjuvant radiation therapy compared with breast-conserving therapy. Breast.patients: cT1-T2N0 breast cancer (90% were T1) receiving lumpectomy, age ≥18
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 omission of SLNB vs. ➜ SLNB Adjuvant WBRT in all. PBI not permitted. High tangents or RNI not permitted except for ≥4 nodes positive
Breast.results: T1 SLNB positive in 14% T2 SLNB positive in 21% 5-yr DFS 92% both arms, noninferior Any primary outcome event 10.3% vs. 10.9% Axillary recurrence 1.0% vs. 0.3% DM 2.7% both arms Death 1.4% vs. 2.4% Second cancer 3.3% vs. 3.9% SLNB more lymphedema and pain and worse arm mobility
Breast.interpretation: Omission of SLNB in T1-T2 breast cancer (90% were T1) was noninferior to SLNB. However SLNB was still positive in 14% in T1 and 20% in T2, and RNI was not allowed in the study.
Breast.commentary: Overview. Given that the EBCTCG 2023 RNI meta-analysis showed benefit of RNI in 1-3 nodes, INSEMA and SOUND are perplexing clinical trials. Prior to release of the meta-analysis, when INSEMA and SOUND were designed, some doubted the existence of benefit of RNI, though some studies offered support. INSEMA did not permit RNI for 1-3 nodes positive, and in SOUND there were no instructions for RT or record of RNI use, nearly eliminating any chance of benefit from SLNB. A rationale for the study is that for many oncologists an elevated Oncotype is what indicates chemotherapy for 1-3 nodes, not node…

Breast.patients: Clinically (-) axilla and clinically (+) axilla
Breast.methods: cN- axilla: ➜ Rad mastectomy vs. ➜ 🏆 TM+RT to axilla vs. ➜ TM cN+ axilla: ➜ Rad mastectomy vs. ➜ TM+RT to axilla 🏆
Breast.results: no significant difference in LF, DFS, or OS between any of the subgroups On 25 year f/u, still no difference
Breast.interpretation: There is no benefit to radical mastectomy compared to total mastectomy.
Breast.commentary: This trial was prior to the era of ultrasound, SLNB, and chemotherapy. Once ultrasound was developed, many used it as cause to once again doubt the findings, but the ALND portion of the trial continues to be reproduced even in the ultrasound era (ACOSOG Z0011 Giulano 2017, AMAROS Donker 2014). Bernie Fisher revolutionized breast cancer care. He was a surgeon advocating against surgery, for chemo and endocrine therapy, and for women's autonomy. These were some of the first ever randomized trials. At the time there was fierce oppositon to his ideas, and the trial was run mainly in community cent…
Breast.references: 1. Fisher B, Anderson S, Bryant J, et al. Twenty-Year Follow-up of a Randomized Trial Comparing Total Mastectomy, Lumpectomy, and Lumpectomy plus Irradiation for the Treatment of Invasive Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med 2002;347:1233-41. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa020128

Breast.citation: Boughey et al, JAMA, 2013 Haffty et al, IJROBP, 2019
Breast.patients: T0-4, cN1-2 confirmed by biopsy, M0 who received neoadjuvant chemo
Breast.methods: Phase II: s/p neoadjuvant chemo ➜ SLN → ALND BCS or mastectomy RT use and field at discretion of physician
Breast.results: -For patients with cN1 disease with ≥2 SLNs removed the FNR was 12.6%, exceeding prespecified 10% limit. Better results if dual technique used and ≥3 LNs taken -FNR 11% with dual tracer vs. 20% with single tracer -CR in 40%. Of those with positive SLN, this was only disease in 40% -Of 40 patients with false negative SLN, # of +N at ALND was 1 in 50%, 2 in 25%, 3 in 10%, and 4-9 in 15%. -Those with pCR had better LRC, BCS, OS, and less DM. -OS 94% with pCR vs. 81 without pCR -5-yr LRR 97% vs. 92% -In pCR, on MVA PMRT and RNI did not improve LRR, DFS, OS, or BSC
Breast.interpretation: SLNB after neoadjuvant chemo did not meet the prespecified FNR threshhold for reliability. Dual tracer technique and ≥3 nodes taken improved the accuracy but still did not meet the threshhold.
Breast.commentary: Terminated early with limited patient numbers and events.
Breast.references: 1. Boughey JC, Suman VJ, Mittendorf EA, et al. Sentinel lymph node surgery after neoadjuvant chemotherapy in patients with node-positive breast cancer: the ACOSOG Z1071 (Alliance) clinical trial. JAMA. 2013;310(14):1455–1461. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2013.278932 2. Haffty BG, McCall LM, Ballman KV, Buchholz TA, Hunt KK, Boughey JC. Impact of Radiation on Locoregional Control in Women with Node-Positive Breast Cancer Treated with Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy and Axillary Lymph Node Dissection: Results from ACOSOG Z1071 Clinical Trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2019;105:174-182. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2019.04.038

Breast.patients: Breast cancer patients undergoing neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Breast.methods: Phase II cN0: ➜ Arm A: SLNB before neoadj chemo. ➜ Arm B: If SLN positive, another SLNB done after chemo cN+: Neoadjuvant chemo, then ➜ Arm C: If ycN0, SLNB and ALND ➜ Arm D: If ycN1, ALND with no SLNB
Breast.results: Arms A and B SLN detection rate before neoadj chemo: 99%. Arm B FNR 52% Arm C (cN+, neoadj chemo to ycN0) detection rate 80% and FNR 14% FNR if one node removed 24% and 14% if two nodes removed. If 3 or more nodes removed, FNR <10%. FNR 8.6% with dual tracer
Breast.interpretation: SLNB is reliable if performed prior to neoadjuvant chemo. However the FNR is higher after neoadjuvant chemo.
Breast.commentary: Arm C results consistent with ACOSOG Z0171 (Boughey 2013). Note high FNR of arm B (Although keep in mind also the methodology: a positive SLN was removed, then another SLN mapping was done after chemo. False negative may not be the ideal term after the positive node was removed then SLN done a second time. The trial is not randomized).
Breast.references: 1. Kuehn T, Bauerfeind I, Fehm T, et al. Sentinel-lymph-node biopsy in patients with breast cancer before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy (SENTINA): a prospective, multicentre cohort study. Lancet Oncol 2013;14:609-18. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(13)70166-9

Breast.citation: Donker et al, Lancet Oncol, 2014 Bartels et al, JCO, 2022
Breast.patients: cT1-2N0 BCS or mastectomy. Lobular allowed.
Breast.methods: Noninferiority. BCS or mastectomy. If SLNB+ → ➜ ALND vs. ➜ 🏆 RNI to axilla levels I-III and medial SCV In all, WBRT for BCS. Optional RT to CW for mastectomy. If in ALND group and ≥4 N+, same RNI was allowed. Dose 50 Gy. For noninferiority, RT group must be ≤4% and ALND group 2%.
Breast.results: Axillary recurrences rare in both arms. Lymphedema worse with ALND. 5-yr axilla recurrence 0.41% vs. 1.04% RT 10-yr axilla recurrence 0.93% vs. 1.82% 10-yr LRR 3.4% vs. 3.8% 1-yr lymphedema 40% vs. 22% 5-yr lymphedema 25% vs. 12% 10-yr 2nd primary 8.3% vs. 12.1% but CIs overlap Contralateral BTR 8% vs. 11%, p=0.035 10-yr OS 85% vs. 81%, p=0.26 DM 12% vs. 15%
Breast.interpretation: ALND is not required in T1-2 SLNB+ when RT is directed to the axilla. Secondary endpoints of OS and DM are similar. Although the trial statistically did not reach noninferiority, overall axillary recurrences are low at 1% or less in each group.
Breast.commentary: ~14% lobular and ~18% mastectomy. 5% had ≥3 nodes positive. T3 was allowed but only one was included. RT was given to axilla in ALND group if ≥4 nodes positive. This trial also shows that there is no obvious OS benefit to limited RNI (without IMN) in early stage node negative breast cancer.
Breast.references: Study protocol: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.22.01565/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.22.01565.pdf 1. Donker M, van Tienhoven G, Straver ME, et al. Lancet Oncol. 2014;15(12):1303–1310. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(14)70460-7 2. Bartels SAL, Donker M, Poncet C, et al. Radiotherapy or Surgery of the Axilla After a Positive Sentinel Node in Breast Cancer: 10-Year Results of the Randomized Controlled EORTC 10981-22023 AMAROS Trial [published online ahead of print, 2022 Nov 16]. J Clin Oncol 2022;JCO2201565. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.01565

Breast.citation: Galimberti et al, Lancet, 2013 Galimberti et al, Lancet, 2018
Breast.patients: 1-2 micromets (N1mi, ≤2mm) on SLNB, T1-T2
Breast.methods: SLNB micromets → ➜ ALND vs. ➜ 🏆 obs
Breast.results: No differnce in LRR, DFS, or OS 10-yr DFS ~76% Lymphedema 13% vs. 4% Sensory neuropathy 19% vs. 13% Motor neuropathy 9% vs. 3%
Breast.interpretation: Obs is nonferior to ALND in nodal micrometastasis found on SLNB.

Breast.patients: T1-3N0, female or male, ENE allowed 6% were T3
Breast.methods: 1-2 SLNB+ → ➜ ALND vs ➜ 🏆 no ALND RNI per each country's national guidelines
Breast.results: 5-yr RFS 89% vs. 90%, noninferior RNI in 88% vs. 89%
Breast.interpretation: This trial reconfirms that ALND can be omitted after 1-2 SLN+ in breast cancer, and now also supports omission in T3. Omission of ALND for 1-2 SLN+ is noninferior to ALND.
Breast.commentary: 6% were T3, about 1/3 had ENE, and 0.4% were male. The trialists's goal was to repeat an ALND study with more consistent radiation therapy delivery and expanded eligibity criteria including males, T3, and ENE. The protocol instructed that each country use its national guidelines for RNI coverage. 88-89%% had RNI in each arm. Details concerning radiation delivery and field are to be published separately.
Breast.references: Study protocol: https://senomac.se/onewebmedia/SENOMAC%20trial%20study%20protocol%208.1%20200626.pdf#page=19 1. Boniface J, Tvedskov TF, Rydén L, et al. Omitting Axillary Dissection in Breast Cancer with Sentinel-Node Metastases. N Eng J Med 2024;390:1163-1175. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2313487

Breast.citation: Giuliano et al, Ann Surg, 2010 Giuliano et al, JAMA, 2017
Breast.patients: T1-T2, clinical N0, lumpectomy, 1-2 SLNB+ Exclusion: mastectomy, gross ECE
Breast.methods: 1-2 SLNB+ → ➜ ALND vs. ➜ 🏆 no ALND WBRT tangents (RT to SCV or targeted axilla prohibited) 97% had adjuvant systemic therapy (chemo or endocrine)
Breast.results: OS was noninferior. At 10 years OS with ALND was worse 10-yr OS 83.6% vs. 86.3%, p=0.02 5-yr DFS 82% vs. 84% (NS) 10-yr DFS 78.2% vs. 80.2% (NS) 5-yr LRR 3.1% vs. 1.6% (NS) Only 1 LRR between year 5-10. Median 17 nodes removed with ALND. On ALND, 21% had ≥3 positive nodes.
Breast.interpretation: ALND is not required after positive SLNB in T1-2 breast cancer with 1-2 SLN+ receiving WBRT and adjuvant systemic tx. At longer f/u, OS was noninferior and favored the SLNB only group.
Breast.commentary: With ALND, suprisingly 21% had ≥3 positive nodes, which suggests that RT and possibly ET can control nodal disease in the RT arm. Nonetheless the regimens were noninferior and OS even appears to be in favor of no ALND. Although the trial was not originally powered for OS, there is a possibility of unknown mechanisms ocurring. Although mastectomy was excluded, there is no reason the results shouldn't apply to mastectomies of similar tumor characteristics. The trial did not complete accrual. There is some question as to whether the trial is powered appropriately. Z0011 had n=856 and AMAROS had n…

Breast.patients: Mastectomy, axillary dissection to at least level II, N+ 22 trials from 1964–1986
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis ➜ obs vs. ➜ 🏆 RT RT was to chest wall, SCV and/or axilla, and IMN (IMNs in 20/22 trials)
Breast.results: pN0 •no benefit with RT for recurrence or BCM 1-3 nodes •5-yr LRR 17% vs. 3% •10-yr LRR: 20% vs. 4% •5-yr BCM 22% vs. 18% •10-yr BCM 37% vs. 31% •20-yr BCM 50% vs. 42% 4+ nodes •5-yr LRR 26% vs. 11% •10-yr LRR: 32% vs. 13% •5-yr BCM 48% vs. 46% •10-yr BCM 67% vs. 61% •20-yr BCM 80% vs. 71% Any N+ •5-yr LRR 21% vs. 7% •10-yr LRR 26% vs. 8% •5-yr BCM 37% vs. 34% •10-yr BCM 54% vs. 48% •20-yr BCM 66% vs. 58% •RT also reduces DM •Results consistent in those who received chemotherapy or ET (CMF or TAM)
Breast.interpretation: PMRT improves both LRR and BCM in those with 1-3 nodes and ≥4 nodes.
Breast.commentary: The trials were prior to the era of Her2. The authors comment in the discussion that since these trials techniques have improved including in targeted systemic therapy, nodal dissection and sectioning, endocrine therapy, and also RT techniques. All therapies have improved, including RT, especially in techniques to avoid the heart. This meta-analysis is the highest level of evidence and remains so until more specific trials are undertaken. For this analysis the authors limited evaluation to those with full lymph node dissections to address concerns that benefit of RT may have been due to inadeq…
Breast.notes: Advanced overview -Landmark trial NSABP B04 shwed that RT can treat the axilla, and patients can undergo total mastectomy instead of the morbid radical mastectomoy -Many doubted the study, and the findings have been reproduced and furthered in varioous trials such as NSABP B32, AMAROS, and ACOSOG Z0011, showing that ALND is not needed in 1-2 SLNB+ -LC and OS benefit of PMRT was established in Danish 82bc and British Columbia studies, as well as others -EBCTCG PMRT meta-analysis showed benefit with PMRT in 1-3 nodes and ≥4 nodes. Some doubt the benefit in 1-3 nodes given advancementt in chemoth…

Effect of radiotherapy after mastectomy and axillary surgery on 10-year recurrence and 20-year breast cancer mortality: meta-analysis of individual patient data for 8135 women in 22 randomised trials [published correction appears in Lancet. 2014 Nov 22;384(9957):1848]. Breast.patients: postmastectomy intermediate risk: T1-2 and 1-3 nodes, T2N0 grade 3, or T2N0 with LVSI About 70% were N1
Breast.methods: ➜ PMRT without RNI. Axillary RT not allowed. SCV and IMN optional vs. ➜ observation RT choice of 40 Gy/15 fx, 45 Gy/20 fx, or 50 Gy/25 fx
Breast.results: 10-yr OS 81-82%, not different 10-yr LR 1.1% vs. 2.5% 10-yr DMFS 78-79% 10-yr DFS 76% both arms Grade ≥2 lung adverse effects n=13 vs. 2 No difference in cardiac or bone adverse effects
Breast.interpretation: PMRT without RNI did not improve outcomes in intermediate risk patients after mastectomy. Adverse effects were very low with radiation.
Breast.commentary: About 70% were N1. RNI was not routine in the study, which may explain the lack of benefit. Axillary RT was completely prohibited. SCV and IMN RT were optional and performed in 12% and 1.5%. A prior EBCTCG PMRT meta-analysis (2014) showed benefit in BCM with PMRT in 1-3 nodes positive. The EBCTCG RNI meta-analysis (2023) showed benefit wth RNI in 1-3 nodes. There were fewer than expected events. An update to the EBCTCG meta-analysis may be warranted. It should also be recognized that the trials within the 2014 EBCTCG meta-analysis may not apply in the modern era. Consistent with this, radiatio…

Breast.citation: Overgaard et al, NEJM, 1997 Overgaard et al, Lancet, 1999 Overgaard et al, Radiother Oncol, 2007 Overgaard et al, Radiother Oncol, 2022
Breast.patients: s/p TM+ALND, high-risk: axillary N+, size >5 cm, or invasion of skin or pectoralis fascia Danish 82b: premenopausal Danish 82c: postmenopausal
Breast.methods: Danish 82b: premenopausal ➜ 🏆 CMF concurrent + 48-50 Gy to CW and RNI vs. ➜ CMF alone Danish 82c: postmenopausal ➜ 🏆 TAM + 48-50 Gy to CW and RNI vs. ➜ TAM alone RT details: RT to CW with RNI to axilla, supraclavicle, and IMNs. Dose 50 Gy/25 fx or 48 Gy/22 fx
Breast.results: •Combined 82bc: 5-yr OS 76% vs. 62% 15-yr OS 41% vs. 32% 30-yr OS 19% vs. 14% 5-yr BCM 32% vs. 36% 15-yr BCM 49% vs. 59% 30-yr BCM 56% vs. 67% 5-yr DM 37% vs. 43% 10-yr DM 47% vs. 57% 30-yr DM 49% vs. 60% 5-yr LRR 7% vs. 30% 10-yr LRR 9% vs. 35% 30-yr LRR 9% vs. 37% 2nd primary non-lung 8.2% vs. 6.6% 2nd primary lung 4% vs. 2% No increase in ischemic heart disease •1-3 nodes 30-yr BCM 48% vs. 60% 30-yr LRR 8% vs. 34% •4+ nodes 30-yr BCM 74% vs. 88% 30-yr LRR 13% vs. 46% On subanalysis, no difference between pre- and postmenopausal except slightly more nonbreast cancer deaths with age
Breast.interpretation: PMRT with chemo in pre-menopausal, or with TAM in postmenopausal, reduces LRR, DM, and BCM and improves OS in high-risk breast cancer. Results are maintainted at up to 30 years follow-up. On subanalysis, no difference between pre- and post-menopausal.
Breast.commentary: The values shown here are mostly from the later 2022 publication. CMF and RT given concurrently. A older, now somewhat dated, criticism of this trial was that only a median of 7 LN were removed. In modern practice it is known that ALND is of no benefit in 1-2 SLNB+ (ACOSOG Z0011, AMAROS). In a subanalysis of 82c in those with ≥8 nodes removed, RT still had benefit in LC and OS. In 82c, about half of the patients were tested for ER/PR.
