Breast.citation: Ragaz et al, NEJM, 1997 Ragaz et al, JNCI, 2005
Breast.patients: Premenopausal s/p MRM with LN+.
Breast.methods: ➜ CMF alone vs. ➜ CMF with 🏆 37.5 Gy/16 fx PMRT RNI to CW, SCV, axilla, and bilateral IMNs (35 Gy/16 fx to IMNs)
Breast.results: 20-yr LRF 26% vs. 10% PMRT 20-yr OS improved 37% to 47% PMRT Late effects low in both arms Cardiac death worse with RT 0.6% vs. 1.8%
Breast.interpretation: Hypofx PMRT with chemo improves OS for N+ breast cancer compared to chemo alone. The benefit was still present in 1-3 nodes Since hypofx was used in both arms, the trial can be considered a phase II for hypofx. Adverse effects were low in both arms.
Breast.commentary: An early example of a trial that used hypofractionation.

Breast.trial: Fourth Military Medical University, Xi'an, China
Breast.patients: Stage I-II triple negative s/p mastectomy +chemo
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 PMRT vs. ➜ obs
Breast.results: 5-yr RFS 88.3% PMRT vs. 74.6% 5-yr OS 90.4% vs. 78.7% 80% patients node negative
Breast.interpretation: PMRT improves OS and RFS in Stage I-II triple negative breast cancer.
Breast.references: 1. Wang J, Shi M, Ling R, et al. Adjuvant chemotherapy and radiotherapy in triple-negative breast carcinoma: A prospective randomized controlled multi-center trial. Radiother Oncol 2011;100:200-4. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2011.07.007

Breast.citation: Canadian Cancer Trials Group, ongoing protocol
Breast.patients: Oncotype <18, ER+ Her2- 1-3 LNs after LND, or 1-2 LNs after SLNB + BCS, or 1 LN after SLNB + mastectomy
Breast.methods: ➜ no RT (except WBRT for BCS) vs. ➜ WBRT+RNI or PMRT+RNI (no neoadj chemo) 16 fx hypofx or 25 fx conventional permitted
Breast.results: Ongoing trial Primary endpoint: BCRFI Secondary endpoints: DFS, BCM, OS, LRRFI, adverse effects, PROs, QOL, cost effectiveness
Breast.commentary: The Oncotype score required here of ≤18 is lower than that of TAILORX and RXPONDER, which have Oncotype ≤25 as indication. Future directions perhaps should include evaluating omission of endocrine therapy in those with low Oncotype. For the RNI arm: Heart, left sided mean ≤3 Gy Heart, left sided V25 ≤10% (variation acc Mean ≤5 Gy, V30 <10%) Ipsi lung V20 ≤35%
Breast.references: 1. Canadian Cancer Trials Group. Regional Radiotherapy in Biomarker Low Risk Node Positive Breast Cancer (TAILOR RT). Available at: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03488693. NLM identifier: NCT03488693. Accessed August 26, 2020.

Breast.patients: Mastectomy
Breast.methods: Retrospective review of PMRT patients. All received PMRT to 50.4 Gy. ➜ 10 Gy boost to CW vs. ➜ 🏆 obs Boost field either full scar + 2-3 cm or majority of CW. Majority had 3-5 mm bolus QOD.
Breast.results: absolute failure (TE or implant removal, failure to replace, no salvage) 10% vs. 4% overall implant failure (permanent removal with or without salvage recon) 35% vs. 19% 5-yr LRR 3.7%, 10-yr LRR ~5%, not different skin necrosis 8% vs. 3% skin infection 13% vs. 6% implant exposure 8% vs. 2% capsular contracture 8% vs. 13%, p=0.059 implant rupture not different, 2% On subgroup analysis, no benefit with in boost with any factors including LVI, close or positive margin, PR or progression with NAC Results are maintained on MVA, except for capsular contracture
Breast.interpretation: Chest wall boost led to more implant failures and skin adverse effects without improvement in LR in this large retrospective study.
Breast.commentary: Boost field was either full scar + 2-3 cm or majority of CW. Majority had 3-5 mm bolus QOD. Groups were mostly balanced. More LVI in boost group but LR still not different on MVA. Slightly more autologous reconstruction in boost group (p=0.06), but this would favor the boost group. More delayed recon in boost group (86% of these were autologous) which would not favor the group (Manyan 2018). Randomized trials are warranted.
Breast.references: 1. Naoum GE, Salama L, Ho A, et al. The Impact of Chest Wall Boost on Reconstruction Complications and Local Control in Patients Treated for Breast Cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2019;105:155-164. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2019.04.027
Breast.commentary_refs: Manyam B, Shah CS, Woody NM, et al. Comparing 10-Year Outcomes in Radiated Patients with Breast Autologous Reconstruction (AR) or Tissue Expander/Implant Based Reconstruction (TE/I). Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2018;102:S45-S6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2018.06.090

Breast.commentary: Hypofx with PMRT summary *The Beijing study provides the first randomized evidence that hypofx RT is noninferior in PMRT, in nonreconstructed patients *Hypofx has also been used in PMRT in the SUPREMO, British Colombia, and START A/B studies *ALLIANCE RT CHARM showed noninferiority with PMRT in reconstructed patients *Hypofrac PMRT did not meet the pre-specific endpoint in FABREC, but did not appear to be different. Some adverse effects was lower with hypofx *Unexpectedly, DBCG SKAGEN, the largest trial, showed 2% worse BCM with hypfx PMRT in the abstract

Breast.patients: Breast cancer with indication for RNI required. T1-4, N0-3, M0, any histology or grade BCS or mastectomy permitted Neoadjuvant and adjuvant chemo permitted Breast implants and connective tissue disease permitted
Breast.methods: ➜ 40 Gy/ 15-18 fx with RNI vs. ➜ 🏆 50 Gy/ 25-28 fx with RNI Boost optional in both arms. SIB required 57 & 50 Gy/25 fx 63 & 51.52 Gy/28 fx 45.75 & 40 Gy/15 fx 52.2 & 42.3 Gy/18 fx
Breast.results: 5-yr LRR 2.3% vs. 2.8% ⛔ 5-yr BCM 7.2% vs. 5.1% 5-yr non BCM 2.7% vs. 2.8% 5-yr DM 11-12%, not different 3-yr lymphedema 8.5%, not different
Breast.interpretation: Breast cancer mortality was unexpectedly worsened with hypofx RNI compared to 25 fractions RNI in this large trial. LRR was noninferior.
Breast.commentary: An explanation for increase in breast cancer mortality with hypofx is unclear. Perhaps hypofx dose and fractionation is not optimal radiobiologically for breast cancer cells, hypofx RNI is harmful to the lymphatics or immune system, this is a statistical anomaly, or perhaps hypofx doesn't interact well with chemo. Measuring cancer mortality vs. non cancer mortality also can be subjective. The full publication is awaited. FABREC and RT CHARM also evaluated hypofx RNI, but both were smaller trials and did not report BCM as an endpoint. The boost doses allowed seem low, although the authors do in…
Breast.references: Study protocol: https://www.dbcg.dk/PDF%20Filer/SKAGEN%20Trial%201_%20protokol.pdf#page=20 1. Offerson BV, Alsner J, Neilson HM, et al, 2025. Phase III randomised trial of hypo- vs standard fractionated loco-regional radiotherapy in node-positive breast cancer patients. Radiother Oncol 2025;206:s4430-4431. https://user-swndwmf.cld.bz/ESTRO-2025-Abstract-Book/4438/

Breast.patients: Stage IIa-IIIa with planned reconstruction Excluded: T4, N3, positive IMN nodes
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 42.56 Gy/ 16 fx RNI vs. ➜ 50 Gy/ 25 fx RNI
Breast.results: 2-yr reconstruction complications 14% vs. 12% Complications less with autologous vs. implant only, OR 0.5 acute and late effects not different 3-yr LRR 1.5% vs. 2.3%
Breast.interpretation: Hypofx PMRT is noninferior in adverse effets to conventional RT in reconstructed patients.
Breast.commentary: After this study, the DBCG SKAGEN large randomized trial showed that breast cancer mortality worsened by 2% with hypofractionated RNI compared to conventional. Heart, left sided mean ≤3 Gy Heart, left sided V25 ≤10% (variation acc Mean ≤5 Gy, V30 <10%) Ipsi lung V20 ≤35% Protocol dose constraints, p46: https://med.uc.edu/docs/default-source/cancercenter/all-protocols/breast/a011202_protocol-vd-12-30-2-july5.pdf?sfvrsn=86f22df0_4#page=46

Some adverse effects was lower with hypofx *Unexpectedly, DBCG SKAGEN, the largest trial, showed 2% worse BCM with hypfx PMRT in the abstract

Breast.patients: Breast cancer s/p mastectomy and immediate expander or implant reconstruction Stage I-III
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 42.56 Gy/ 16 fx vs. ➜ 50 Gy/ 25 fx -Optional RNI allowed to SCV and IMN (given in ~90%) -Bolus allowed at discretion over scar or entire CW -Boost not permitted Primary endpoint: Superiority of physical well being domain of FACT-B Secondary endpoint: recurrence and grade ≥3 chest wall adverse effects (infection, delayed wound healing, TE or implant removal, unplanned surgical intervention). Capsular contraction not included
Breast.results: Median f/u 40 mos Physical well-being QOL was not superior with hypofx, but appeared not different Grade ≥3 chest wall effects 10.0% vs. 9.5% (further specifics not outlined) Recurrence rates were similar Patient characteristics and treatment received: ~90% had RNI, 42% IMRT. 59% were prepectoral, ~20% implants and ~80% expanders. All had immediate reconstruction
Breast.interpretation: Regarding the primary endpoint of the study, hypofractionation did not result in improved physical well-being in mastectomy patients with reconstruction. But there appeared to be no difference. About 90% received RNI. A secondary endpoint, chest wall adverse effects, was lower than expected and similar between arms.
Breast.commentary: After this study, the DBCG SKAGEN large randomized trial showed that breast cancer mortality worsened by 2% with hypofractionated RNI compared to conventional. The FABREC trial did not meet its endpoints per the prespecified design. Although one may wish for a different design of the study, including higher power or other endpoints, the trial nicely illustrates that both conventional and hypofractionated radiation can be performed in those with reconstruction with low adverse effects. Grade ≥3 chest wall effects was only 10% and lower than expected. Capsular contraction was not included in che…
Breast.references: 1. Wong JS, Uno H, Tramontano AC, et al. Hypofractionated vs Conventionally Fractionated Postmastectomy Radiation After Implant-Based Reconstruction: A Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol. Published online August 8, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2024.2652
Breast.commentary_refs: 1. Kaidar-Person O, Dahn HM, Nichol AM, et al. A Delphi study and International Consensus Recommendations: The use of bolus in the setting of postmastectomy radiation therapy for early breast cancer. Radiother Oncol 2021;164:115-121. doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2021.09.012

Breast.trial: Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, China
Breast.citation: Wang et al, Lancet Oncol, 2019 Sun et al, IJROBP, 2020
Breast.patients: Breast cancer s/p mastectomy non-reconstructed T3-T4 or ≥4 nodes
Breast.results: No differences in LRR, DFS, OS, or late skin adverse effects ↔️ 5-yr LRR 8%, ↔️ DFS 74%, ↔️ OS 84% both arms Late skin not different. Late grade 1-2 pneumonitis trended worse with hypofx 15% vs. 10%, p=0.081. No grade ≥3 acute or late pneumonitis. Hypofx had less grade 3 acute skin, 3% vs. 8%. Similar grade 1-2 acute pneumonitits (18%), lymphedema (grade 1-2 in 20%), heart ischemia (1%), and shoulder adverse effects. Less lymphopenia in hypofx arm. Associated with improved DFS, 72% vs. 83%, but not OS
Breast.interpretation: Hypofx PMRT is noninferior to conventional RT in non-reconstructed patients for LRR. There are similar adverse effects.
Breast.commentary: Reconstruction patients were not eligible. An NRG trial will evaluate hypofx in reconstructed patients. 43.5 Gy is bioequivalent to 50 Gy when using α/β of 4.0 Gy. Almost all had 6 MeV to chest wall. To SCV, either 6 MV or 6 MV/12-15 MeV mix. 5 mm bolus was used up to median 40% of dose. Historical trivia: Peking=Beijing. Peking is a variant spelling.
Breast.references: 1. Wang S-L, Fang H, Song Y-W, et al. Hypofractionated versus conventional fractionated postmastectomy radiotherapy for patients with high-risk breast cancer: a randomised, non-inferiority, open-label, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2019;20:352-60. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(18)30813-1 2. Sun G-Y, Wang S-L, Song Y-W, et al. Radiation-Induced Lymphopenia Predicts Poorer Prognosis in Patients with Breast Cancer: A Post-Hoc Analysis of a Randomized Controlled Trial of Postmastectomy Hypofractionated Radiotherapy. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2020. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2020.02.633

Breast.commentary: RNI overview -Historical trials showed OS detriment with IMN RNI -Later more modern trials EORTC 22922, MA.20, AMAROS, DBCG IMN, KROG 0806 showed various small benefits with RNI in DM and OS -An eventual EBCTCG meta-analysis on RNI showed a BCM benefit in 1-3 nodes and even in N0 (possibly limited to medial and central tumors, orr other high rirsk tumors -NSABP B51 showed no benefit with RNI after CR in nodes after neoadjuvant chemo, but the trial may be underpowered

Breast.patients: Localized breast cancer, both node negative and node positive
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis of 16 randomized or pseudorandomized trials evaluating RNI
Breast.results: ➡️ 8 newer trials 1989-2008: RNI vs. no RNI ⬇️ N0 15-yr BCM 10.9% vs. 12.5% ⬇️ 1-3 nodes 15-yr BCM 20.3% vs. 23% ⬇️ ≥4 nodes 15-yr BCM 40.5% vs. 45% Non-breast cancer mortality was not worsened with RNI ⬇️ N0 15-yr any recurrence 19% vs. 21% ⬇️ 1-3 nodes 15-yr any recurrence 26% vs. 29% ⬇️ ≥4 nodes 15-yr any recurrence 47% vs. 51% ⬇️ 5-yr DM 14% vs. 16% ⬇️ 10-yr DM 21% vs. 25% ⬇️ 15-yr DM 26% vs. 30% ⬇️ Overall 15-yr BCM 22% vs. 25% ⬇️ 15-yr OM 32% vs. 35% Results were better with medial or central tumors (any recurrence p=0.021, DM p=0.052, BCM p=0.069, OS p=0.40) ➡️ Older trials 1961-1978: 🚫…
Breast.interpretation: In modern studies, RNI reduces BC recurrence, BCM, DM, and overall mortality in both node positive and node negative patients. The effects may be greater in those with medial or central tumors. Non BCM is not increased.
Breast.commentary: Different types of RNI were used between the studies. Those that included IMNs would be expected to provide more benefit (DBCG-IMN, MA.20). It is uncertain which node negative tumors benefit from RNI. Additional subanalysis was not undertaken. One could use the inclusion criteria from the EORTC and MA.20 trials: EORTC 22922/10925 allowed node negative tumors that were medially or centrally located. These comprised 44% of patients in the study. On subanalysis at 15 years there was no heterogeneity within treatment groups. The stages were not reported, but 60% were T1, 40% were medial, 44% were …
Breast.notes: RNI overview -Historical trials showed OS detriment with IMN RNI -Later more modern trials EORTC 22922, MA.20, AMAROS, DBCG IMN, KROG 0806 showed various small benefits with RNI in DM and OS -An eventual EBCTCG meta-analysis on RNI showed a BCM benefit in 1-3 nodes and even in N0 (possibly limited to medial and central tumors, orr other high rirsk tumors -NSABP B51 showed no benefit with RNI after CR in nodes after neoadjuvant chemo, but the trial may be underpowered
Breast.references: 1. Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group (EBCTCG). Radiotherapy to regional nodes in early breast cancer: an individual patient data meta-analysis of 14 324 women in 16 trials. Lancet 2023. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(23)01082-6
Breast.commentary_refs: 1. Recht A. Internal Mammary Node Irradiation Debate: Case Closed? Not Yet, and Maybe Never. J Clin Oncol 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.23.02480 2. Pan H, Gray R, Braybrooke J, et al. 20-Year Risks of Breast-Cancer Recurrence after Stopping Endocrine Therapy at 5 Years. N Engl J Med. 2017;377(19):1836–1846. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1701830 3. Thorsen LBJ, Thomsen MS, Berg M, et al. CT-planned internal mammary node radiotherapy in the DBCG-IMN study: Benefit versus potentially harmful effects. Acta Oncol 2014;53:1027-34. DOI: https://doi.org/10.3109/0284186X.2014.925579

Breast.subtopic: What is the benefit of RNI if there is pCR in nodes from neoadj chemo?
Breast.patients: node positive cT1-3N1→ neoadj chemo → Surgery (BCS or mastectomy) and SLNB → pCR in nodes Characteristcs: Her2+ 57% Triple negative 23% ER/PR+ Her2-20%
Breast.methods: Phase III superiority Lumpectomy: ➜ WBRT vs. ➜ WBRT + RNI Mastectomy: ➜ obs vs. ➜ PMRT + RNI RNI included IMN 1-3rd intercostal spaces, SCV, and axillary levels 1-3 (levels 1-2 were omitted if ALND done)
Breast.results: Characteristics: 57% were Her2+ and 23% triple negative 5-yr RFS 92% vs. 93%, HR 0.88 (CI 0.60-1.28) 5-yr LR RFS 98% vs. 99% 5-yr DM RFS 93% both arms 5-yr DFS ~85% both groups 5-yr OS 94% both groups Specific outcomes by receptor subtype were not powered and not provided On subgroup analysis, there is benefit in ER/PR+ Her2-, and no benefit in triple negative. There also seems to be benefit in ages 50-59. Due to low power there are wide CIs. grade 3 adverse events 6.5% vs. 10% grade 4 adverse events 0.1% vs. 0.5%
Breast.interpretation: Overall there was no benefit with RNI for those with pCR in nodes after neoadjuvant chemo for cN1 disease. The study may be underpowered. The CIs are wide and nearly all cross 1, and results vary by subtype. RNI should continued to be administered to those ER/PR+ Her2-. On subgroup analysis there was benefit in ER/PR+ Her2-. For lumpectomy, WBRT without RNI is otherwise still recommended in this setting.
Breast.commentary: NSABP B51 highlights advancements in chemotherapy and shows that some patients might be able to safely avoid radiation therapy. LR RFS is outstanding at 99%. However due to low events the trial is underpowered in some aspects, and probably should have been separate trials for each subtype. 57% were Her2+. Statistical design. There are concerns that the trial is underpowered. The study was powered for superiority, not noninferiority, there were less than expected events for analysis, and specific results were not reported by subtype. Some have commented that the analysis should have been event …

Omitting Regional Nodal Irradiation after Response to Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy. N Engl J Med 2025;392:2113-2124. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2414859

Available from: https://www.themednet.org/question/18347 Accessed June 5, 2025.

Breast.subtopic: What is the benefit of ALND vs. axillary RNI if incomplete response in nodes after neoadj chemo?
Breast.citation: Alliance for Clinical Trials in Oncology, ongoing protocol
Breast.patients: node positive cT1-3N1 → neoadj chemo → Surgery (BCS or mastectomy) and SLNB → incomplete path response in nodes
Breast.methods: ➜ ALND + RNI vs. ➜ axillary RT and RNI RT to breast or CW
Breast.results: ongoing protocol Primary endpoint: noninferiority of BCRFI Secondary endpoint: LRRFI, OS
Breast.commentary: Similar to AMAROS study, but with neoadjuvant chemo (Donker 2014). Z0171 shows that we should exercise caution when performing SLN after neoadjuvant in cN+, as is done in this protocol (Boughey 2013).
Breast.references: 1. Alliance for Clinical Trials in Oncology. Comparison of Axillary Lymph Node Dissection With Axillary Radiation for Patients With Node-Positive Breast Cancer Treated With Chemotherapy. Available from: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01901094. NLM identifier: NCT01901094. Accessed June 19, 2019.

Breast.citation: Thorsen et al, JCO, 2015 Thorsen et al, JCO, 2022
Breast.patients: Operable breast cancer with ≥1 N+
Breast.methods: Prospective pseudorandomized. ➜ Left sided: no IMN RNI ➜ Right sided: 🏆 IMN RNI 48 Gy/ 24 fx to CW/breast, axilla levels II-III, and SCV. If ≥6 nodes, then axillary level I added. Adjuvant chemo or endocrine therapy allowed. Boost optional for breast. No boost for CW.
Breast.results: ⬆️ 8-yr OS 72% vs. 76% (p=0.005) 15-yr OS 55% vs. 60% (p=0.007) ⬇️ 8-yr BCM 23% vs. 21% (p=0.03) 15-yr BCM 34% vs. 32% (p=0.05) ↘️ 8-yr DM 30% vs. 27% (p=0.07) 15-yr DM 39% vs. 36% (p=0.04) On subanalysis, benefit with ≥4 nodes or medial tumors. No difference in ischemic heart deaths Supplemental data: First event LR alone 1.4% vs. 1.9% First event RR alone 0.9% vs. 0.7%
Breast.interpretation: RNI to IMN improves OS and BCM in N+ breast cancer. There is trend to benefit in DM.
Breast.commentary: About 60% had 1-3 nodes, 25% had 4-9, and 15% ≥10 nodes. Prospective design allowed greater power for this trial. The rates of LR and DM are supplied in the supplementary data. A secondary publication concluded that IMN RT to left sided patients outweighs risk to the heart (Thorsen 2014). IMN RT was only performed on right sided patients in the study. A secondary publication from the authors concluded that IMN RT to left sided patients outweighs risk to the heart (Thorsen 2014).
Breast.references: 1. Thorsen LBJ, Offersen BV, Danø H, et al. DBCG-IMN: A Population-Based Cohort Study on the Effect of Internal Mammary Node Irradiation in Early Node-Positive Breast Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2016;34:314-20. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.63.6456 2. Thorsen LBJ, Overgaard J, Matthiessen LW, et al. Internal Mammary Node Irradiation in Patients With Node-Positive Early Breast Cancer: Fifteen-Year Results From the Danish Breast Cancer Group Internal Mammary Node Study [published online ahead of print, 2022 Apr 8]. J Clin Oncol 2022;JCO2200044. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.00044
Breast.commentary_refs: Thorsen LBJ, Thomsen MS, Berg M, et al. CT-planned internal mammary node radiotherapy in the DBCG-IMN study: Benefit versus potentially harmful effects. Acta Oncol 2014;53:1027-34. DOI: https://doi.org/10.3109/0284186X.2014.925579

Breast.patients: N+ breast cancer after BSC or mastectomy
Breast.methods: ➜ no IMN RNI vs. ➜ 🏆 IMN RNI 45-54 Gy to CW/breast, axilla, and SCV
Breast.results: improved DFS in medio-central tumors 7-yr DFS 82% vs. 85%, not different subgroup analysis of medial-central only 7-yr DFS 82% vs. 92%, p=0.008 7-yr BCM 10% vs. 5%, p=0.04 pneumonitis 3% vs. 6%, p=0.06
Breast.interpretation: RT to IMNs improves DFS and BCM in those with medial-central tumors. Outcomes are not improved however in overall patients.
Breast.commentary: This trial had greater number of nodes positive than other RNI studies: 41% were N1, 37% N2, 22% N3. The trial was designed to show a 10% improvement in DFS. In other words, it was underpowered to show a possibility of lesser benefit. Observe that the DBCG-IMN study had n >3000 and this study has n-747.
Breast.references: Study protocol in supplements [radiation planning p12]: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2021.6036 1. Kim YB, Byun HK, Kim DY, et al. Effect of Elective Internal Mammary Node Irradiation on Disease-Free Survival in Women With Node-Positive Breast Cancer: A Randomized Phase 3 Clinical Trial [published online ahead of print, 2021 Oct 25]. JAMA Oncol 2021;10.1001/jamaoncol.2021.6036. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2021.6036

Breast.citation: Poortmans et al, NEJM, 2015 Poortmans et al, Lancet Oncol, 2020 Poortmans et al, JNCI, 2021 Kaidar-Person et al, Radiother Oncol, 2022 Kaidar-Person et al, CA Cancer J Clin, 2026
Breast.patients: any medial or central tumor, or any N+. BCS or masectomy allowed 44% were N0
Breast.methods: ➜ no RNI vs. ➜🏆 RNI to IMN, medial SCV 50 Gy breast/CW
Breast.results: ↗️ 10-yr OS 80.7% vs. 82.3% (p=0.06) 15-yr OS 71% vs. 73% (p=0.36) 20-yr OS 61% vs. 62% NS ⬇️ 10-yr BCM 14.4% vs. 12.5% (p=0.02) ⬇️ 15-yr BCM 20% vs. 16% (p=0.0055) 20-yr BCM 22% vs. 19% (p=0.006) 20-yr non BCM 16% vs. 20% (p=0.002) ⬆️ 10-yr DFS 69% vs. 72% (p=0.04) 15-yr DFS 60% vs. 61% (p=0.18) 20-yr DFS 49% vs. 48% NS ⬇️ 10-yr DMFS 75% vs. 78% (p=0.02) 15-yr DMFS 68% vs. 70% (p=0.18) 20-yr DMFS 60% vs. 59% Subgroup analysis did not favor any particular characteristics Better improvements in DFS, OS, and BCM with individualized RT planning techniques •15-yr grade ≥2 effects lung 0.1% vs. 0.8…
Breast.interpretation: RNI to IMN and medial SCV results in small benefits in OS and improves BCM, DFS, and DM in node negative medial/central tumors or node positive breast cancer at 10 years. On 15 year follow-up there remains a benefit in BCM. OS, DFS, and DM become nonsignificant. At 20-yr follow-up, there is no longer a benefit in OS due to increase in non BCM. Individualized RT planning techniques led to better DFS, OS, and BCM.
Breast.commentary: At 20 years the OS benefit is diminished due to increase in non BCM in the IMN RT arm. This trial was prior to the era of arc IMRT. IMRT or protons could reduce long term morbidity. In the EBCTCG RNI meta-analysis (2023) there was a 15-yr benefit in OS. 76% had BCS. On subanalysis at 15 years there was no heterogeneity within treatment groups, so there is no evidence for example that only medial benefits or T1N0 does not benefit. The stages were not reported, but 60% were T1, 40% were medial, 44% were N0, and 43% were N1. Groups were well balanced. A compelling question is, is there a benefit …
