Breast.commentary_refs: 1. Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group (EBCTCG). Radiotherapy to regional nodes in early breast cancer: an individual patient data meta-analysis of 14 324 women in 16 trials. Lancet 2023. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(23)01082-6

Breast.patients: s/p BCS and SLNB/ALND and adjuvant chemo or ET with: any N+, or high risk N- including: tumor >5 cm or size>2 cm with < 10 axillary nodes removed with either ER-, Grade 3, or LVI
Breast.methods: ➜ no RNI vs. ➜ 🏆 RNI to IMN, SCV, axilla level III (plus levels I-II if <10 nodes removed or >3 nodes+) 50 Gy to breast
Breast.results: ↔️ 10-yr OS 82% vs. 83% (p=0.38) ↘️ 10-yr BCM 12% vs. 10% (p=0.11) ⬆️ 10-yr DFS 77% vs. 82% WBRT+RNI (p=0.01) Increased radiation pneumonitis 0.2% vs. 1.2% (p=0.01) and lymphedema 4.5% vs. 8.4% (p=0.001). No increase in brachial plexopathy. On interaction analysis, ER/PR negative had improved DFS with RNI but not ER/PR+. ER negative had trend to improved OS 83% vs. 74%, p=0.05. There were no interactions for # nodes. But note that the trial is not specifically powered for this.
Breast.interpretation: RNI improved DFS but not OS in node positive and high risk node negative breast cancer. There is a nonsignificant benefit in BCM.
Breast.commentary: Only ~5% patients had ≥4 nodes positive. 10% had negative nodes. ~12% had 3 nodes positive. A later EBCTCG meta-analysis incorporating EORTC 22922's and MA.20's results showed a benefit with RNI for N0 of 15-yr BCM of 10.9% vs. 12.5%. Note the low rates of pneumonitis and lymphedema: addition of IMNs gives benefit and adds few adverse effects.
Breast.references: Study protocol [radiation planning p22]: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa1415340/suppl_file/nejmoa1415340_protocol.pdf#page=22 Whelan TJ, Olivotto IA, Parulekar WR, et al. Regional Nodal Irradiation in Early-Stage Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med. 2015;373(4):307–316. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1415340
Breast.commentary_refs: 1. Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group (EBCTCG). Radiotherapy to regional nodes in early breast cancer: an individual patient data meta-analysis of 14 324 women in 16 trials. Lancet 2023. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(23)01082-6

Breast.citation: Donker et al, Lancet Oncol, 2014 Bartels et al, JCO, 2022
Breast.patients: cT1-2N0 BCS or mastectomy. Lobular allowed.
Breast.methods: Noninferiority. BCS or mastectomy. If SLNB+ → ➜ ALND vs. ➜ 🏆 RNI to axilla levels I-III and medial SCV In all, WBRT for BCS. Optional RT to CW for mastectomy. If in ALND group and ≥4 N+, same RNI was allowed. Dose 50 Gy. For noninferiority, RT group must be ≤4% and ALND group 2%.
Breast.results: Axillary recurrences rare in both arms. Lymphedema worse with ALND. 5-yr axilla recurrence 0.41% vs. 1.04% RT 10-yr axilla recurrence 0.93% vs. 1.82% 10-yr LRR 3.4% vs. 3.8% 1-yr lymphedema 40% vs. 22% 5-yr lymphedema 25% vs. 12% 10-yr 2nd primary 8.3% vs. 12.1% but CIs overlap Contralateral BTR 8% vs. 11%, p=0.035 10-yr OS 85% vs. 81%, p=0.26 DM 12% vs. 15%
Breast.interpretation: ALND is not required in T1-2 SLNB+ when RT is directed to the axilla. Secondary endpoints of OS and DM are similar. Although the trial statistically did not reach noninferiority, overall axillary recurrences are low at 1% or less in each group.
Breast.commentary: ~14% lobular and ~18% mastectomy. 5% had ≥3 nodes positive. T3 was allowed but only one was included. RT was given to axilla in ALND group if ≥4 nodes positive. This trial also shows that there is no obvious OS benefit to limited RNI (without IMN) in early stage node negative breast cancer.
Breast.references: Study protocol [radiation planning p18]: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.22.01565/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.22.01565.pdf#page=18 1. Donker M, van Tienhoven G, Straver ME, et al. Radiotherapy or surgery of the axilla after a positive sentinel node in breast cancer (EORTC 10981-22023 AMAROS): a randomised, multicentre, open-label, phase 3 non-inferiority trial. Lancet Oncol 2014;15:1303-1310. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(14)70460-7 2. Bartels SAL, Donker M, Poncet C, et al. Radiotherapy or Surgery of the Axilla After a Positive Sentinel Node in Breast Cancer: 10-Year Results of the Randomized Controlled EORTC 10981-22023 AMAROS Trial. J Clin Oncol. 2023;41(12):2159-2165. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.01565

Breast.patients: Positive axillary LN or medial tumors after mastectomy
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 no IMN RNI vs. ➜ IMN RNI 50 Gy to CW and SCV
Breast.results: At 8.6 year f/u, no difference in DFS or OS 10-yr OS 59% vs. 63%, p=0.8 No benefit seen in any subgroup
Breast.interpretation: RNI to IMN did not improve OS or DFS.
Breast.commentary: Was powered to detect 10% improvement. Note higher number of patients in other studies. MA.20 detects 3% improvement (Whelan 2015). IMN volume was first 5 intercostal spaces. 2 dimensional techniques.
Breast.references: 1. Hennequin C, Bossard N, Servagi-Vernat S, et al. Ten-Year Survival Results of a Randomized Trial of Irradiation of Internal Mammary Nodes After Mastectomy. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2013;86:860-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2013.03.021

Breast.patients: Node+ s/p mastectomy and chemo, no PMRT
Breast.methods: meta-analysis of 5 randomized NSABP trials
Breast.results: Failure patterns: 10-yr LRF (with or without DM) 20%, 10-yr isolate LRF 12%, 10-yr DM alone 43% Predictors for LRF as first event: age, tumor size, premenopausal status, # of LN+, # dissected LNs
Breast.interpretation: Large tumors and ≥4 nodes positive predict a high risk of LRF that could benefit from PMRT. 1-3 nodes positive also elevates LRF risk but less so.
Breast.references: 1. Taghian A, Jeong J-H, Mamounas E, et al. Patterns of Locoregional Failure in Patients With Operable Breast Cancer Treated by Mastectomy and Adjuvant Chemotherapy With or Without Tamoxifen and Without Radiotherapy: Results From Five National Surgical Adjuvant Breast and Bowel Project Randomized Clinical Trials. J Clin Oncol 2004;22:4247-54. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2004.01.042

Breast.patients: Breast cancer patients from 4 ECOG trials
Breast.methods: meta-analysis of 4 ECOG randomized trials
Breast.results: Predictors for LRR include tumor size, numbers of involved nodes, ER-, and decreasing # nodes examined
Breast.references: 1. Recht A, Gray R, Davidson NE, et al. Locoregional Failure 10 Years After Mastectomy and Adjuvant Chemotherapy With or Without Tamoxifen Without Irradiation: Experience of the Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group. J Clin Oncol 1999;17:1689-. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1999.17.6.1689

Breast.patients: T1-2N1 s/p mastectomy
Breast.methods: Retrospective development of nomogram to predict LRR, DM, and BCM
Breast.results: Nomogram was developed to predict LRR and internally validated. In high risk group, PMRT improved 5-yr LRR by 13%, 5-yr DM by 9%, and 5-yr BCM by 7% No benefit in low and int risk groups.
Breast.interpretation: d
Breast.commentary: A node negative T2 patient with LVI grade 3 would benefit from PMRT here. Nomogram factors: T1-T2, N+, ER, grade, LVI. LVI and grade 3 are the poorest factors. The nomogram is not externally validated.
Breast.references: 1. Luo C, Zhong X, Deng L, et al. Nomogram Predicting Locoregional Recurrence to Assist Decision-Making of Postmastectomy Radiation Therapy in Patients With T1-2N1 Breast Cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2019;103:905-12. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2018.11.005

Breast.patients: T1-T2 s/p mastectomy
Breast.methods: Retrospective review to evaluate risk of LRR in high risk factors after mastectomy (age<40, multifocal/centric, LVI, medial or central tumor, grade 3
Breast.results: NS increase in LRR from 3.8% to 9.4% for 1 vs. ≥4 risk factors (p=0.54) Increasing tumor size and ommision of chemo were predictive of LR
Breast.interpretation: Increasing tumor size in the setting of no chemotherapy has increased risk of LRR and may benefit from PMRT. Other factors, isolated or combined, do not seem to elevate LRR risk enough to benefit from RT.
Breast.notes: This and the below studies provide useful stats and reference tables to determine recurrence risk. But note that none of these are externally validated. SUPREMO was based on this data. The MA.20, EORTIC, EBCTCG meta-analysis, and DBCG studies provide randomized data to support for RT in 1-3 nodes. MA.20 and EORTC support RNI PMRT in some node negative patients, but EBCTCG does not. Note that the studies here provide info on mostly only LRR risk, but that MA.20, DBCG, EORTC, and EBCTCG give randomized results on DM and OS as well.
Breast.references: 1. Mamtani A, Patil S, Stempel MM, et al. Are there patients with T1 to T2, lymph node-negative breast cancer who are "high-risk" for locoregional disease recurrence?. Cancer. 2017;123(14):2626–2633. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.30658

Breast.patients: T3N0 s/p mastectomy and chemo
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis (one phase III and 4 case control studies)
Breast.results: LRR 12% obs vs. 3% with PMRT
Breast.interpretation: In this meta-analysis, PMRT improves LC in T3N0 breast cancer.
Breast.references: 1. Shaikh MP, Alite F, Wu MJ, et al. Postmastectomy Radiation Therapy for Node-Negative Breast Cancer with Tumor Size of Five Centimeter or More: A Meta-analysis. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2017;99:s5. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2017.06.028

Breast.patients: Breast cancer node negative, ER+, TAM treated
Breast.methods: Retrospective review of LRR per RS in TAM, placebo, and TAM+chemo
Breast.results: 10-yr LRR for low RS 4%, int RS 7.2%, and high RS 16%
Breast.interpretation: Higher recurrence scores predicted higher LR.
Breast.references: 1. Mamounas EP, Tang G, Fisher B, et al. Association between the 21-gene recurrence score assay and risk of locoregional recurrence in node-negative, estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer: results from NSABP B-14 and NSABP B-20. J Clin Oncol. 2010;28(10):1677–1683. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2009.23.7610

Breast.patients: T1-2, s/p mastectomy and negative margins
Breast.methods: Retrospective
Breast.results: Predictors for LRR include grade, LVI, T stage, and systemic therapy use Grade 3 plus LVI LRR of 21%
Breast.references: 1. Truong PT, Lesperance M, Culhaci A, et al. Patient subsets with T1-T2, node-negative breast cancer at high locoregional recurrence risk after mastectomy. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2005;62:175-82. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2004.09.013

Breast.patients: Node negative patients with high recurrence risk
Breast.methods: Retrospective analysis of node negative patients s/p mastectomy
Breast.results: Predictors for LRR include size >2cm, margins <2 mm, premenopausal, LVSI. 10-yr LRR: 10% if 1 factor, 18% if 2 factors, 41% if 3 factors
Breast.interpretation: Node negative patients with poor prognositic factors may benefit from PMRT
Breast.references: 1. Jagsi R, Raad RA, Goldberg S, et al. Locoregional recurrence rates and prognostic factors for failure in node-negative patients treated with mastectomy: Implications for postmastectomy radiation. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2005;62:1035-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2004.12.014

Breast.patients: Mastectomy and doxorubicin based chemo without RT
Breast.methods: Retrospective analysis of five prospective trials
Breast.results: LRR 4% for 0 nodes+, 10% if 1-3 nodes, 21% if 4-9 nodes, 22% if ≥10 nodes+. Sites of LRR: chest wall (68%), SCV (41%). If T1-T2, risk factors are <10 nodes on dissection, size greater 4 cm, or ECE
Breast.references: 1. Katz A, Strom EA, Buchholz TA, et al. Locoregional Recurrence Patterns After Mastectomy and Doxorubicin-Based Chemotherapy: Implications for Postoperative Irradiation. J Clin Oncol 2000;18:2817-27. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2000.18.15.2817

Breast.patients: Early stage breast cancer without neoadjuvant treatment
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 0.5% lidocaine peritumoral injection 7-10 mins prior to surgery vs. ➜ no lidocaine
Breast.interpretation: Peritumoral lidocaine injection prior to surgery improves DFS and OS and should be standard of care.
Breast.commentary: The old wive's tale: "surgery lets the air in." Were the conspiracy theorists in the right this whole time? This landmark trial asserts yes. In the background of the paper, the authors provide a number of sources for their rationale. Lidocaine modulates receptor tyrosine protein (Src) kinases and sodium channels. This in turn can inhibit cell proliferation, motility, invasion, and promote focal adhesion, which could reduce metastatic potential. Possible critcisms: a 4% benefit in both DFS and OS is unusual, and an OS benefit is not normally seen so early. Regardless lidocaine is a simple and c…
Breast.references: 1. Badwe RA, Parmar V, Nair N, et al. Effect of Peritumoral Infiltration of Local Anesthetic Before Surgery on Survival in Early Breast Cancer [published online ahead of print, 2023 Apr 6]. J Clin Oncol 2023;JCO2201966. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.01966

Breast.patients: ER/PR+ Her2- Stage II-III breast cancer
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 ribociclib + ET vs. ➜ ET With chemotherapy and RT as indicated first
Breast.results: 3-yr invasive DFS 90% vs. 87%
Breast.interpretation: Ribociclib improves IDFS in ER+ Her2- Stage II-III breast cancer.
Breast.commentary: Tannock et al (2025) comment on why they do not use CDK4/6 inhibitors at their institution. They write that the design should have been crossover to evaluate if delay of CDK4/6 inhibitor is just as effective as using upfront, that high censoring makes the trials unable to be interpretted, that adverse effects are high and there is no convincing evidence that the duration should be so long, and that there is evidence that the drugs promote endocrine therapy resistant clonogens.
Breast.references: 1. Slamon D, Lipatov O, Nowecki Z, et al. Ribociclib plus Endocrine Therapy in Early Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med 2024;390:1080-1091. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2305488
Breast.commentary_refs: 1. Tannock IF, Khan QJ, Fojo T. Why We Do Not Recommend That Women With Breast Cancer Receive Adjuvant Treatment With a CDK4/6 Inhibitor. J Clin Oncol. Published online April 14, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-24-02683

Breast.citation: Johnston et al, JCO, 2020 Johnston et al, Lancet, 2023
Breast.patients: ER+ Her2- s/p surgery with either ≥4 N+, or 1-3 N+ plus tumor size ≥5 cm, or grade 3, or Ki-67 ≥20%
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 abemaciclib + ET vs. ➜ ET With chemotherapy and RT as indicated first
Breast.results: 2-yr invasive DFS 92% vs. 89% 4-yr invasive DFS 86% vs. 79% No change in OS, 94% Serious adverse events 16% vs. 9% Most common grade 3-4 events were neutropenia (20%), leukopenia (11%), and diarrhea (8%) Adverse effects, any grade: diarrhea 82%, neutropenia 45%, fatigue, leukopenia 37%, abdominal pain 34%, nausea 28%, anemia 23%, arthralgia 21%
Breast.interpretation: Abemaciclib improves IDFS in ER+ Her2- node positive breast cancer.
Breast.commentary: Tannock et al (2025) comment on why they do not use CDK4/6 inhibitors at their institution. They write that the design should have been crossover to evaluate if delay of CDK4/6 inhibitor is just as effective as using upfront, that high censoring makes the trials unable to be interpretted, that adverse effects are high and there is no convincing evidence that the duration should be so long, and that there is evidence that the drugs promote endocrine therapy resistant clonogens.
Breast.references: 1. Johnston SRD, Harbeck N, Hegg R, et al. Abemaciclib Combined With Endocrine Therapy for the Adjuvant Treatment of HR+, HER2-, Node-Positive, High-Risk, Early Breast Cancer (monarchE). J Clin Oncol 2020;38:3987-3998. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.20.02514 2. Johnston SRD, Toi M, O'Shaughnessy J, et al. Abemaciclib plus endocrine therapy for hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative, node-positive, high-risk early breast cancer (monarchE): results from a preplanned interim analysis of a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2023;24:77-90. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(22)00694-5
Breast.commentary_refs: 1. Tannock IF, Khan QJ, Fojo T. Why We Do Not Recommend That Women With Breast Cancer Receive Adjuvant Treatment With a CDK4/6 Inhibitor. J Clin Oncol. Published online April 14, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-24-02683

Breast.patients: early breast cancer trials eval neoadjuvant vs. adjuvant chemo
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis of trials prior to 2005. 10 trials ➜ neoadjuvant chemo vs. ➜ 🏆 adjuvant chemo
Breast.results: ⬇️ 15-yr LRR 16% vs. 21% ↔️ 15-yr BCM 34% both arms ↔️ 15-yr DM 38%, not different ↔️ 15-yr OM 41%, not different
Breast.interpretation: NAC maintains OS, BCM, or DM outcomes compared to adjuvant chemo. However NAC led to higher LR.
Breast.commentary: 81% of chemo was anthracycline based No difference in DM, BCM, or OS on subanalyses based on type of chemotherapy used.
Breast.references: 1. Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group (EBCTCG). Long-term outcomes for neoadjuvant versus adjuvant chemotherapy in early breast cancer: meta-analysis of individual patient data from ten randomised trials. Lancet Oncol. 2018;19(1):27–39. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30777-5

Breast.patients: T1c-T3 N0-1 or T1-3 N1. Must be palpable.
Breast.methods: Lumpecomty + ALND or masectomy, TAM in all ➜ neoadjuvant AC vs. ➜ neoadj ACT, surg vs. ➜ neoadj AC, surg, T
Breast.results: Neoadj ACT led to best pCR AC vs. ACT vs. AC-Surg-T: pCR 13% vs. 26% vs. 13% negative nodes 51% vs. 58% (AC vs AC-T)
Breast.interpretation: NAC with ACT gives best pCR.
Breast.references: 1. Bear HD, Anderson S, Brown A, et al. The Effect on Tumor Response of Adding Sequential Preoperative Docetaxel to Preoperative Doxorubicin and Cyclophosphamide: Preliminary Results From National Surgical Adjuvant Breast and Bowel Project Protocol B-27. J Clin Oncol 2003;21:4165-74. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2003.12.005

Breast.citation: Fisher et al, JCO, 1998 Wolmark et al, JNCI Monogr, 2001
Breast.patients: T1-3N0-1 operable, palpable, and non-fixed
Breast.methods: ➜ neoadjuvant AC vs. ➜ adjuvant AC
Breast.results: -Ability to undergo BCS 68% vs 60% -9-yr OS 69% vs. 70% (NS) -LR increased if planned mastectomy converted to BCS: 16% vs. 10% -Having pCR predicts OS and DFS -pCR only 9% -OS with CR 78%, PR 67%, no response 65%
Breast.interpretation: There is no OS difference between NAC and adjuvant chemo. NAC allowed more breast conservation, however, LR was increased if planned mastectomy was converted to BCS. Response predicts OS.
Breast.references: 1. Fisher B, Bryant J, Wolmark N, et al. Effect of preoperative chemotherapy on the outcome of women with operable breast cancer. J Clin Oncol 1998;16:2672-85. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1998.16.8.2672 2. Wolmark N, Wang J, Mamounas E, et al. Preoperative Chemotherapy in Patients With Operable Breast Cancer: Nine-Year Results From National Surgical Adjuvant Breast and Bowel Project B-18. J Natl Cancer Inst Monogr 2001;2001:96-102. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/oxfordjournals.jncimonographs.a003469

Breast.trial: Henri Becquerel Cancer Center, Rouen, France
Breast.patients: inflammatory
Breast.methods: Retrospective mutlticenter French study to identify those who benefit from dose escalation or bolus
Breast.results: Standard vs. boost or bolus 5-yr LRR 12% vs. 10%, not different 5-yr DM 29% vs. 21%, not different
Breast.interpretation: In this retrospective series there was no benefit to boost or bolus in inflammatory breast cancer.
Breast.references: 1. Rogé M, Kirova Y, Lévêque E, et al. Impact of Radiation Therapy Modalities on Loco-regional Control in Inflammatory Breast Cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2024;120:496-507. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2024.04.010

Breast.patients: inflammatory
Breast.methods: Retrospective study to identify those who benefit from dose escalation. ➜ Various doses used, but generally 51 Gy/1.5 Gy BID then boost 9-15 Gy
Breast.results: 5-yr LRC 84% full course vs. 51% if not completing full therapy 5-yr DMFS 47% vs. 20% 5-yr OS 51% vs. 24% -Dose escalation up to 66 Gy from 60 gave LRC benefit in: less than partial response to neoadjvant chemo, close, positive, or unknown margins, or <45 yrs old grade 3-4 effects 29% with 66 Gy, 15% with 60 Gy. Lymphedema 9% vs 2%. Fibrosis 6% vs 4%. Brachial plexopathy 2% vs 0%.
Breast.interpretation: In inflammatory breast cancer, completion of mastectomy, RT, and chemo leads to high LC. Dose escalation to 66 Gy appears to mitigate failure in partial responders, young age, or positive or close margins.
Breast.commentary: This study is among the largest series for inflammatory breast cancer. At the same time, don't view in isolation. Also note that it and others are retrospective. Other series show other conclusions or no differences. In a editorial review of IBC series, Woodword (2015) infers that the data is mixed, but either acceleration, bolus, or dose escalation is called for in all IBC. Randomized trials seem unlikely to be undertaken. Consider also the difficulty in classifying outcomes. IBC often recurs just outside the radiation field. Is this a local, regional, or distant recurrence?
Breast.references: 1. Bristol IJ, Woodward WA, Strom EA, et al. Locoregional treatment outcomes after multimodality management of inflammatory breast cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys. 2008;72:474–484. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2008.01.039
Breast.commentary_refs: Woodward WA. Postmastectomy Radiation Therapy for Inflammatory Breast Cancer: Is More Better? Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2014;89:1004-5. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2014.05.004

Breast.patients: Metastatic breast cancer at presentation
Breast.methods: ➜ locoregional treatment to primary and axilla nodes vs. ➜ without Induction chemo given if unresectable initially
Breast.results: -Median OS for locoregional treatment 19.2 mos vs 20.5 mos -2-yr OS 42% vs 43% -No difference in OS on adjustment for stratifications factors, pre/post menopausal, or Her2
Breast.interpretation: Local treatment of the primary tumor in metastatic breast cancer did not improve OS.
Breast.commentary: Only 14 patients had resectable disease at presentation. Low use of taxane and Her2 therapies.
Breast.references: 1. Badwe R, Hawaldar R, Nair N, et al. Locoregional treatment versus no treatment of the primary tumour in metastatic breast cancer: an open-label randomised controlled trial. Lancet Oncol 2015;16:1380-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(15)00135-7

Breast.patients: 1-5 oligoprogressive lesions after at least one type of chemo NSCLC or breast (1/3 of breast were triple negative)
Breast.methods: →chemo→1-5 oligoprogressive lesions→ Phase II ➜ 🏆 SBRT to oligoprogressive sites vs. ➜ no SBRT Most received 27-30 Gy/3 fractions or 30-50 Gy/5 fractions
Breast.results: ⬆️ Median PFS 7.2 vs. 3.2 mos ⬆️ NSCLC median PFS 10 vs. 2.2 mos ⛔ Breast median PFS ~4.3 mos, not different (1/3 breast were triple negative) No improvement in OS overall or in specific groups Median f/u was 12 mos In NSCLC, recurrences were mainly in existing lesions. In breast, recurrences were mainly in new lesions Improvement in ctDNA variant allelle frequences in NSCLC but not breast Grade 2+ adverse effects in n=8 with SBRT
Breast.interpretation: SBRT to oligoprogressive lesions improves PFS in NSCLC. In this analysis there is no benefit in breast cancer, 1/3 of which were triple negative. There was no change in OS. Findings to be further evaluated in Phase III trials.
Breast.commentary: The trial is phase II. Results should be eventually confirmed in phase III trials. SBRT improves PFS at median f/u of 12 months, which makes sense if RT improves LC in 1-5 lesions. OS was not improved, but follow up is short at one year at this time.
Breast.references: 1. Tsai CJ, Yang JT, Shaverdian N, et al. Standard-of-care systemic therapy with or without stereotactic body radiotherapy in patients with oligoprogressive breast cancer or non-small-cell lung cancer (Consolidative Use of Radiotherapy to Block [CURB] oligoprogression): an open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 2 study. Lancet 2023. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(23)01857-3

Breast.patients: ≤4 oligometastases On 1st line systemic therapy for ≤12 mos without progression size <5 cm, amenable to surgery or SBRT Excludes brain mets
Breast.methods: Phase II/III ➜ standard of care chemo vs. ➜ SBRT or surgery
Breast.results: median PFS 23 mos vs. 19.5 mos, p=0.36 2-yr PFS 47% both arms 3-yr PFS 33% vs. 38% 3-yr OS ~70% both arms
Breast.interpretation: The use of local therapy to metastasis in oligometastatic breast cancer did not improve outcomes. Additional results are to be reported with further f/u.
Breast.commentary: There is a possibility that results may change in longer f/u.
Breast.references: 1. Chmura SJ, Winter KA, Borges VF, et al. NRG-BR002: A phase IIR/III trial of standard of care systemic therapy with or without stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) and/or surgical resection (SR) for newly oligometastatic breast cancer (NCT02364557). J Clin Oncol 2022;40:s16.1007. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2022.40.16_suppl.1007

Breast.patients: pT1-T2N0M0 breast cancer
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 IMRT 57.4 Gy/ 28 fx with SIB vs. ➜ 3DCRT 59.4 Gy/33 fx
Breast.results: -Less grade ≥2 effects with IMRT -No difference in IBTR -Better coverage and lower lung doses with IMRT
Breast.interpretation: IMRT for breast cancer leads to lower skin adverse effects. Dose coverage was superior with IMRT.
Breast.references: 1. Choi KH, Ahn SH, Jeong JU, et al. Postoperative radiotherapy with intensity-modulated radiation therapy versus 3-dimensional conformal radiotherapy in early breast cancer: A randomized clinical trial of KROG 15-03. Radiother Oncol 2020. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2020.09.043

Breast.trial: Erasmus MC Cancer Institute, Rotterdam, The Netherlands
Breast.citation: Pignol et al, JCO, 2008 Pignol et al, Radiother Oncol, 2016
Breast.patients: Early stage breast
Breast.methods: ➜ 2D vs. ➜ 🏆 IMRT 50 Gy/ 2 Gy per fx + 16 Gy electron boost
Breast.results: -69% had reconstructions, 89% stage III -Moist desquamation IMRT 31% vs 2D 48% -No significant difference in chronic pain. Young age and pain during RT associated with chronic pain. -Acute moist desquamation associated with late subcutaneous fibrosis and telangiectasia -Pain is associated with poorer self-assessed outcome and reduced QOL -No differences in OS, LRFS, or DFS
Breast.interpretation: IMRT reduced moist desquamation. Late adverse effects correlated with acute side effects, which are increased with poor dose distribution.
Breast.commentary: IMRT may also be appropriate if dose constraints are not met, especially for the heart in left sided patients.
Breast.references: 1. Pignol J-P, Olivotto I, Rakovitch E, et al. A Multicenter Randomized Trial of Breast Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy to Reduce Acute Radiation Dermatitis. J Clin Oncol 2008;26:2085-92. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2007.15.2488 2. Pignol J-P, Truong P, Rakovitch E, et al. Ten years results of the Canadian breast intensity modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) randomized controlled trial. Radiother Oncol 2016;121:414-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2016.08.021

Breast.patients: Early stage breast
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 IMRT vs. ➜ 2D 50 Gy/ 2 Gy per fx and 11.1 Gy/5 fx boost
Breast.results: Change in breast 40% vs. 58% IMRT resulted in less palpable induration
Breast.interpretation: IMRT minimisation of dose inhomogeneity led to reduced late effects.
Breast.references: 1. Donovan E, Bleakley N, Denholm E, et al. Randomised trial of standard 2D radiotherapy (RT) versus intensity modulated radiotherapy (IMRT) in patients prescribed breast radiotherapy. Radiother Oncol 2007;82:254-64. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2006.12.008

Breast.patients: Hyperthermia for locally recurrent breast cancer
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis of single arm and two arm hyperthermia studies 31 articles: 8 randomized, 26 single arm. 779 previously irradiated.
Breast.results: -Techniques: Median 7 HT sessions, 2x per week, average temperature 42.5°C, mean duration 54 min, median interval to tx 55 mos, mean RT dose 38.2 Gy (range 26, 60 Gy, dose per fx mean 2.8 Gy, range 1.8-4 Gy/fx). Hyperthermia usually given after RT. Outcomes: In 2-arm studies, CR of 60.2% with RT+HT vs. 38.1% with RT alone. CR of 66.6% in previously irradiated with HT+RT. Late grade 3-4 adverse effects 5.2% with RT+HT
Breast.interpretation: Hyperthermia added to RT improves CR rates in locally recurrent breast cancer and has low adverse effects.
Breast.commentary: Size of tumors was not well described in most publications. Some tumors were breast and not all were superficial. One study showed improved CR with size ≤6 cm2: CR 65% vs. 26% for larger size. Hyperthermia was given by microwaves or radiofrequency to 8-2450 Mhz.
Breast.references: 1. Datta NR, Puric E, Klingbiel D, et al. Hyperthermia and Radiation Therapy in Locoregional Recurrent Breast Cancers: Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2016;94:1073-87. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2015.12.361

Breast.patients: Local recurrence occurring >1 yr from previous single tumor <3cm. Seroma size <30% of breast. N0. DCIS allowed
Breast.methods: 45 Gy/30 fx BID with PBI EBRT
Breast.results: grade 3 effects 7% 0 grade 4-5 effects 5-yr IBTR 5% 5-yr mastectomy 10% DM and OS both 95%
Breast.interpretation: Treatment of ipsilateral breast tumor recurrence with lumpectomy followed by PBI EBRT results in high control rates, low adverse effects, and likelihood of avoidance of mastectomy.
Breast.commentary: 23 patients were DCIS, 32 invasive. All except one patient were <2cm.
Breast.references: 1. Arthur DW, Winter KA, Kuerer HM, et al. Effectiveness of Breast-Conserving Surgery and 3-Dimensional Conformal Partial Breast Reirradiation for Recurrence of Breast Cancer in the Ipsilateral BreastThe NRG Oncology/RTOG 1014 Phase 2 Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2019. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2019.4320

Breast.citation: van den Bogaard et al, JCO, 2017
Breast.patients: breast conservation survery and 3D conformal radiation, 2005-2008
Breast.methods: Retrospective analysis of heart dosimetry and 9-yr cumulative incidence of acute cardiac events
Breast.results: -Cumulative incidence of ACE increased 16.5% per each Gy, with no threshold -Refer to figures for excess risk of acute cardiac events (ACEs) per age, cardiac risk factors (diabetes, hypertension, ischemic cardiac events), and heart dose -Generally, a mean heart dose of less than 4-8 Gy is needed to achieve ACE risk <5% (see figures) -V5 of left ventricle is most important factor
Breast.interpretation: The relationship between heart dose and excess risk of acute cardiac events is validated in this study.
Breast.commentary: The use of 3D conformal radiation in this study helps to assuage reservations toward the Darby study (2013), which included many patients treated with 2D techniques.
Breast.references: 1. van den Bogaard VA, Ta BD, van der Schaaf A, et al. Validation and Modification of a Prediction Model for Acute Cardiac Events in Patients With Breast Cancer Treated With Radiotherapy Based on Three-Dimensional Dose Distributions to Cardiac Substructures [published correction appears in J Clin Oncol. 2017 Nov 10;35(32):3736]. J Clin Oncol. 2017;35(11):1171–1178. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2016.69.8480

Breast.patients: radiation for breast cancer, 1958-2001
Breast.methods: Case control analysis of major coronary events and estimated dose to mean heart and LAD
Breast.results: Risk of major coronary events increased 7.4% per each Gy, with no threshold Proportional risk was similar with or without cardiac risk factors. Absolute risk was higher with risk factors
Breast.interpretation: Increasing radiation dose to heart is correlated with increased risk of major cardiac events.
Breast.commentary: Many of the patients in this series were treated prior to the 3D era. The investigators used the 2D films to reconstruct virtual 3D simulations to calculate the putative doses to heart. The validative study by van den Bogaard (2017) in 3DCRT patients provides reassurance that dose to the heart is as important to consider as Darby suggests.
Breast.references: 1. Darby SC, Ewertz M, McGale P, et al. Risk of ischemic heart disease in women after radiotherapy for breast cancer. N Engl J Med 2013;368:987-98. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1209825

Breast.patients: early breast cancer chemo trials with anthracyclines and taxanes
Breast.methods: ➜ anthracycline vs. ➜ anthracycline with taxanes (sequential or 🏆 together)
Breast.results: Anthracyclines reduced recurrence by 14% But +1/700 pts secondary AMLs Best decrease in recurrence when adding anthracycline to docetaxel plus C together, not sequential 10-yr rec 12% vs. 21% 10-yr BCM decreased by 4% No benefit in recurrence with sequential taxanes and anthracycline Higher cumulative and dose-intense regimens were better
Breast.interpretation: Best results are achieved with concurrent anthracyclines and taxanes, and with higher cumulative and dose intense schedules. Common practices of sequential AC → T and use of TC without anthracycline are challenged.
Breast.commentary: Them term "concurrent" is used in the study to describe giving anthracycline plus taxanes simultaneously rather then sequentially; "concurrent" here does not refer to administering together with radiation.
Breast.references: 1. Early Breast Cancer Trialists’ Collaborative Group (EBCTCG). Electronic address: bc.overview@ctsu.ox.ac.uk. Anthracycline-containing and taxane-containing chemotherapy for early-stage operable breast cancer: a patient-level meta-analysis of 100 000 women from 86 randomised trials. Lancet 2023;401:1277-1292. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(23)00285-4

Breast.subtopic: Chemo in int risk Oncotope and 1-3 nodes
Breast.citation: Kalinsky et al, NEJM, 2021 Kang et al, JAMA Oncol, 2025
Breast.patients: breast cancer, ER/PR+, Her2-, 1-3 nodes positive
Breast.methods: Oncotype score ≤25 ➜ chemo+endocrine therapy vs. ➜ 🏆 endocrine therapy
Breast.results: •Post-menopausal 5-yr IDFS 91-92%, not different •Premenopausal 5-yr IDFS 94% vs. 89% OS data is maturing No subgroup had any benefit on subanalysis (age, 1 vs 2-3 nodes, grade, size, score) In premenopausal, ovarian surpression in 16% vs. 4% •Cognitive effects - ET deceased cognitive function in premenopausal, which returned to baseline by 36 months - Cognitive scores were stable with ET in postmenopausal patients - Chemo ET decreased cognitive scores in both pre- and postmenopausal patients, which did not return to baseline
Breast.interpretation: Chemotherapy in ER/PR+ postmenopausal women with 1-3 nodes positive provides no IDFS or OS benefit and can be avoided. Chemotherapy leads to IDFS benefit in premenopausal women. Chemotherapy+ET causes cognitive decline. ET alone caused cognitive decline in premenopausal patients, which later returned to baseline.
Breast.commentary: Thus far just 54% of events have occurred. 15 year follow-up is planned. ET also caused cognitive decline in the TAILORx study.
Breast.references: Study protocol: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa2108873/suppl_file/nejmoa2108873_protocol.pdf 1. Kalinsky K, Barlow WE, Gralow JR, et al. 21-Gene Assay to Inform Chemotherapy Benefit in Node-Positive Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med. 2021;385(25):2336-2347. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2108873 2. Kang IM, Forschmiedt JK, Loch MM, et al. Cognitive Impairment and Chemoendocrine vs Endocrine Therapy in Pre- and Postmenopausal Women: A Secondary Analysis of the RxPONDER Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol. Published online December 11, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2025.5220

Breast.citation: Sparano et al, NEJM, 2018 Sparano et al, NEJM, 2019 Sparano et al, JAMA Oncol, 2019 Wagner et al, JCO, 2020
Breast.patients: breast cancer, ER/PR+, Her2-, node negative
Breast.methods: Oncotype Intermediate risk (score 11-25) ➜ chemo+endocrine therapy vs. ➜ 🏆 endocrine therapy High risk (score >25) ➜ chemo+endocrine therapy
Breast.results: •Intermediate risk (score 11-25) -No change in 9-yr OS, DM, DFS, FFDM, FF LRR/DM -9-yr OS 94%, DFS ~83%, FFDM 95%, FF LRR/DM ~92% -Benefit in age <50 in DFS, LRR+DM, but not DM or OS •High risk (score >25) 5-yr DM 7%, FF LRR/DM 91%, OS 96% •Cognitive QOL -Chemo+ET causes cognitive decline. Endocrine therapy alone also causes cognitive decline though less so. The decline from ET is the same in all age groups, indicating it is not age related •Post-hoc subanalysis of "Lower" vs. "higher" risk (low risk: grade 1 size ≤3 cm, grade 2 ≤2 cm, grade 3 ≤1 cm) 9-yr DM in age <50, lower risk: <5% 9-yr DM…
Breast.interpretation: Chemotherapy does not improve outcomes when added to endocrine therapy in intermediate risk Oncotype scores with negative nodes. Outcomes in high risk exceeded expectations. Chemotherapy plus endocrine therapy and endocrine alone each cause cognitive decline. On post-hoc subanalysis, chemotherapy may provide benefit in those that are age <50 with higher risk tumors. The risk of DM in those age <50 is low in lower risk tumors (i.e grade 1 ≤3 cm, grade 2 ≤2 cm, grade 3 ≤1 cm).
Breast.commentary: It is odd that the investigators reanalyzed the data based on low and high risk clinical features and found different outcomes. Is this not what Oncotype is supposed to replace? This implies that Oncotype is deficient in some areas of prognosis; or, if one runs enough subanalyses, something significant will be found. The subanalysis of clinical features was post-hoc and should be regarded as hypothesis generating. There was no adjustment for multiple comparisons. Similar LF of 1-1.5% in low, intermediate, and high risk scores. Mean tumor size 1.7 cm, range 1.2-2.0
Breast.references: 1. Sparano JA, Gray RJ, Makower DF, et al. Adjuvant Chemotherapy Guided by a 21-Gene Expression Assay in Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med. 2018;379(2):111–121. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1804710 2. Sparano JA, Gray RJ, Ravdin PM, et al. Clinical and Genomic Risk to Guide the Use of Adjuvant Therapy for Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med;380(25):2395-2405. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1904819 3. Sparano JA, Gray RJ, Makower DF et al. Clinical Outcomes in Early Breast Cancer With a High 21-Gene Recurrence Score of 26 to 100 Assigned to Adjuvant Chemotherapy Plus Endocrine Therapy: A Secondary Analysis of the TAILORx Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2019. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2019.4794 4. Wagner LI, Gray RJ, Sparano JA, et al. Patient-Reported Cognitive Impairment Among Women With Early Breast Cancer Randomly Assigned to Endocrine Therapy Alone Versus Chemoendocrine Therapy: Results From TAILORx. J Clin Oncol 2020. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.19.01866

Breast.trial: EBCTCG late DM risk after ET
Breast.patients: 88 trials of women disease free after 5 years endocrine therapy
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis. Assessed associations of tumor diameter, nodal status, grade, and other factors on outcomes during years 5-20 after 5 yrs endocrine therapy
Breast.results: Risk of distant mets: T1N0: 13%, T1, N(#1-3 nodes): 20%, T1, N(#4-9 nodes): 34% T2N0: 19%, T2N(#1-3): 26%, T2N(#4-9): 41% T1N0 grade 1: 10%, grade 2: 13%, grade 3: 17% Grade and Ki-67 were moderately predictive of DM PR and Her2 status were not predictive of DM Risk of any recurrence or contralateral cancer: T1N0 grade 1: 17%, grade 2: 22%, grade 3: 26%
Breast.interpretation: Risk of distant metastasis in breast cancer continues to rise even after 5 years of endocrine therapy.
Breast.commentary: Best resource for chance of distant recurrence. Takeaway point: in all breast cancer there is a reasonable risk of failure over time. Does this mean patients should be on endocrine therapy for longer periods or that better chemo is needed? Outcomes may be improved with use of current chemotherapy and radiation regimens such as taxol, more conformal radiation, and regional node radiation. Info on how many had RT was not provided in this study.
Breast.references: 1. Pan H, Gray R, Braybrooke J, et al. 20-Year Risks of Breast-Cancer Recurrence after Stopping Endocrine Therapy at 5 Years. N Engl J Med. 2017;377(19):1836–1846. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1701830

Breast.patients: 194 randomized trials of adjuvant chemo or hormone therapy
Breast.methods: Meta-analysis
Breast.results: Anthracycline chemo is superior to CMF. Better benefit in age <50, regardless of TAM, nodes, tumor features BCM relative reduction of 38% for age <50 and 20% for age 50-59 10-yr OS for N- 71% vs. 78% 10-yr OS for N+ 42% vs. 53% TAM reduces relative BCM by 31%.
Breast.interpretation: Anthracycline-based chemo and tamoxifen improve BCM at long term f/u.
Breast.commentary: Years later, better benefit in age <50 was validated in TAILORx (Sparano 2018). Individual trials typically have not shown an OS or BCM benefit with ET. This EBCTCG reveals that there is a BCM benefit long term.
Breast.references: 1. Early Breast Cancer Trialists' Collaborative Group (EBCTCG). Effects of chemotherapy and hormonal therapy for early breast cancer on recurrence and 15-year survival: an overview of the randomised trials. Lancet 2005;365:1687-717. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(05)66544-0

Breast.patients: Breast cancer, Her2 negative, who had PR after neoadjuvant chemo and surgery
Breast.methods: ➜ capecitabine vs. ➜ none
Breast.results: Capecitabine improved DFS 5-yr DFS 74% vs. 68% 5-yr OS 89% vs. 84% Among triple negative: 5-yr DFS 70% vs. 56% 5-yr OS 79% vs. 70%
Breast.interpretation: Capecitabine improves DFS and OS in Her2- breast cancer with PR after neoadjuvant chemo. The effect is primarily in triple negative patients.
Breast.commentary: Varshavsky-Yanovsky (2020) writes an excellent editorial in review of use of capecitabine in breast cancer. In review of studies, including some meta-analyses, many trials evaluating capecitabine have been negative. Guided by those studies, CREATE-X asked a more specific question in a high risk population. Thus capecitabine does seem to be indicated in the specific circumstance of triple negative without pCR after neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
Breast.references: 1. Masuda N, Lee S-J, Ohtani S, et al. Adjuvant Capecitabine for Breast Cancer after Preoperative Chemotherapy. N Engl J Med 2017;376:2147-59. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1612645
Breast.commentary_refs: Varshavsky-Yanovsky AN, Goldstein LJ. J Clin Oncol 2020:38;172-189. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.19.02946

Breast.citation: Robertson et al, Lancet, 2016 Robertson et al, JCO, 2025
Breast.patients: Locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer
Breast.methods: ➜ fulvestrant vs. ➜ anastrozole
Breast.results: PFS 16.6 mos vs. 13.8 mos No benefit in OS on final analysis, but on post-hoc subanalysis there is decreased RR of death in nonvisceral disease 44.8 vs. 42.7 mos arthrlagia 17% vs. 10% hot flashes 11% vs. 10% Discontinued 7% vs. 5%
Breast.interpretation: Fulvestrant has improved PFS over anastrazole.
Breast.references: 1. Robertson JFR, Bondarenko IM, Trishkina E, et al. Fulvestrant 500 mg versus anastrozole 1 mg for hormone receptor-positive advanced breast cancer (FALCON): an international, randomised, double-blind, phase 3 trial. Lancet 2016;388:2997-3005. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(16)32389-3 2. Robertson JFR, Shao Z, Noguchi S, et al. Fulvestrant Versus Anastrozole in Endocrine Therapy-Naïve Women With Hormone Receptor-Positive Advanced Breast Cancer: Final Overall Survival in the Phase III FALCON Trial. J Clin Oncol. Published online January 7, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.24.00994

Breast.citation: Aebi et al, Lancet, 2015 Wapnir et al, JCO, 2018
Breast.patients: Recurrent breast cancer, radical resection, RT recommended
Breast.methods: chemo vs. no chemo Endocrine therapy given for HR+. Her2 therapy optional
Breast.results: 5-yr OS 88% chemo vs. 76% without 5-yr DFS 69% vs. 57% At final 9-yr follow-up, there was benefit for ER- but not for ER+
Breast.interpretation: Chemo improves OS in ER negative locally recurrent breast cancer. ER positive patients may be managed with endocrine therapy alone.
Breast.references: 1. Aebi S, Gelber S, Anderson SJ, et al. Chemotherapy for isolated locoregional recurrence of breast cancer (CALOR): a randomised trial. Lancet Oncol. 2014;15(2):156–163. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(13)70589-8 2. Wapnir IL, Price KN, Anderson SJ, et al. Efficacy of Chemotherapy for ER-Negative and ER-Positive Isolated Locoregional Recurrence of Breast Cancer: Final Analysis of the CALOR Trial. J Clin Oncol. 2018;36(11):1073–1079. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2017.76.5719

Breast.patients: Early stage breast cancer treated with lumpectomy
Breast.methods: adjuvant chemo before vs. after RT 12 weeks cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, methotrexate, fluorouracil, and prednisone
Breast.results: No differences in time to any event, DM, or OS. On an exploratory subanalysis, for those with close margins the crude LR was 32% for chemo before RT vs. 4% (p value not provided)
Breast.interpretation: Sequencing of adjuvant chemotherapy and radiation does not effect outcomes.
Breast.commentary: For some, an advantage to adjuvant chemotherapy prior to adjuvant RT is that it addresses DM, which is hypothetically the main concern after LC has been reduced with lumpectomy. A high Oncotype, implemented after this study, may create an elevated sense of risk and warrant chemotherapy sooner (Sparano 2018).
Breast.references: 1. Bellon JR, Come SE, Gelman RS, et al. Sequencing of Chemotherapy and Radiation Therapy in Early-Stage Breast Cancer: Updated Results of a Prospective Randomized Trial. J Clin Oncol 2005;23:1934-40. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2005.04.032

Breast.patients: Breast cancer nonresponsive to TAM
Breast.methods: ➜ AC vs. ➜ CMF
Breast.results: No change in DFS, DM, or OS AC completed sooner and with less nausea. More alopecia was CMF
Breast.interpretation: There is difference in DFS, DM, or OS with AC compared to CMF, but AC is more convenient and has less adverse effects.
Breast.references: 1. Fisher B, Brown AM, Dimitrov NV, et al. Two months of doxorubicin-cyclophosphamide with and without interval reinduction therapy compared with 6 months of cyclophosphamide, methotrexate, and fluorouracil in positive-node breast cancer patients with tamoxifen-nonresponsive tumors: results from the National Surgical Adjuvant Breast and Bowel Project B-15. J Clin Oncol 1990;8:1483-96. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1990.8.9.1483

Breast.subtopic: pembrolizumab with NAC in triple negative
Breast.citation: Schmid et al, NEJM, 2020 Schmid et al, NEJM, 2022 Schmid et al, NEJM, 2024
Breast.patients: triple negative breast cancer T1cN1-2 or T2-4N0-2 (Stage II-III)
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 neoadjuvant pembrolizumab + chemo, then adjuvant pembro after surgery vs. ➜ chemo alone Chemo: carboplatin/taxol x4 followed by AC or EC x4
Breast.results: 3-yr EFS 85% vs. 77% pCR 65% vs. 51% 60-mos OS 87% vs. 82% DM 8% vs. 13% LR 4% vs. 4% On subanalysis, improved pCR in PDL1 + or - PDL1+ had better pCR rates in both arms Those without pCR still benefitted
Breast.interpretation: Adding pembrolizumab to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in triple negative breast cancer improves EFS and pCR.
Breast.references: Study protocol: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa2409932/suppl_file/nejmoa2409932_protocol.pdf 1. Schmid P, Cortes J, Pusztai L, et al. Pembrolizumab for Early Triple-Negative Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med 2020;382:810–821. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1910549 2. Schmid P, Cortes J, Dent R, et al. Event-free Survival with Pembrolizumab in Early Triple-Negative Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med 2022;386:556-567. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2112651 3. Schmid P, Cortes J, Dent R, et al. Overall Survival with Pembrolizumab in Early-Stage Triple-Negative Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med 2024. Published online September 15, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2409932

Breast.patients: metastatic Her2 breast cancer (brain mets allowed if treated - in 47%) s/p trastuzumab, pertuzumab, and TDM1 prior WBRT or SRS not required in brain mets but allowed
Breast.methods: ➜ trastuzumab and capecitabine, plus: tucatininb vs. ➜ placebo Primary endpoint: PFS
Breast.results: All patients: 1-yr PFS 33% vs. 12% Median PFS 7.8 vs. 5.6 mos 2-yr OS 45% vs. 27% Median OS 21.9 vs. 17.4 mos Among those with brain mets: 1-yr PFS 25% vs. 0% Median PFS 7.6 vs. 5.4 mos Brain specific control not provided
Breast.interpretation: Tucatinib in Her2+ metastasis, without brain mets, improves PFS and OS. However, some patients with brain metastasis in the control arm did not receive radiation therapy of any kind.
Breast.commentary: One of few systemic therapy trials to allow enrollment of those with brain metastasis. Tucatinib is a small Her2 inhibitor that should cross the blood brain barrier. OS should have been the primary endpoint, not PFS. Vinary Prasad (2020) comments that some patients in the trial did not receive standard of care for progressing brain metastasis, radiation thearpy. About 25% in the control arm had no therapy for potentially progressing brain metastasis. And the 1-yr PFS in that arm was 0%! Intracranial specific LC was not provided. The publication was written by an unidentified medical writer.
Breast.references: 1. Murthy RK, Loi S, Okines A, et al. Tucatinib, Trastuzumab, and Capecitabine for HER2-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med 2020;382:597-609. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1914609

Breast.patients: Breast cancer, Her2 positive, who had PR after neoadjuvant taxane-based chemo & trastuzumab and surgery
Breast.methods: ➜ trastuzumab-emtansine (TDM-1) vs. ➜ trastuzumab
Breast.results: 3-yr Invasive LC 88% vs. 77% Invasive DFS improved, HR 0.50 3-yr DM 11% vs. 16% Pneumonitis increased to 1.5% vs. 0.7%
Breast.interpretation: Trastuzumab-emtansine improves invasive LC and DFS in partial responders to neoadjuvant chemo in Her2+.
Breast.commentary: What is emtansine? A maytansine derivative and microtubule inhibitor. Maytansine is a macrolide from the tree genus Maytenus (Peddi et al, Future Oncol, 2014)
Breast.references: 1. Minckwitz G, Huang C-S, Mano MS, et al. Trastuzumab Emtansine for Residual Invasive HER2-Positive Breast Cancer. N Engl J Med 2019;380:617-28. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1814017
Breast.commentary_refs: 1. Peddi PF, Hurvitz SA. Trastuzumab emtansine: the first targeted chemotherapy for treatment of breast cancer. Future Oncol 2013;9:319-326. DOI: https://doi.org/10.2217/fon.13.7

Breast.patients: Locally advanced, inflammatory, or early stage breast cancer treated with neoadjuvant chemo
Breast.methods: 4 arms, all neoadjuvant x4: ➜ trastuzumab/ docetaxel vs. ➜ 🏆 pertuzumab/ trastuzumab/ docetaxel vs. ➜ pretuzumab/ trastuzumab vs. ➜ pertuzumab/ docetaxel After surgery, all patients get 3 cycles FEC with trastuzumab
Breast.results: 5-yr PFS 81% vs. 86% vs. 73% vs. 73% 5-yr DFS 81% vs. 84% vs. 80% vs. 75% 5-yr PFS 85% if CR vs. 76% if no CR
Breast.interpretation: Pertuzumab, herceptin, and docetaxol NAC improves PFS, DFS, and pCR compared to trastuzumab based therapy.
Breast.references: 1. Gianni L, Pienkowski T, Im Y-H, et al. 5-year analysis of neoadjuvant pertuzumab and trastuzumab in patients with locally advanced, inflammatory, or early-stage HER2-positive breast cancer (NeoSphere): a multicentre, open-label, phase 2 randomised trial. Lancet Oncol 2016;17:791-800. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(16)00163-7

Breast.patients: Advanced breast cancer overexpressing Her2 who completed local regional therapy
Breast.methods: ➜ 🏆 trastuzumab x1 yr vs. ➜ trastuzumab x2 yrs vs. ➜ obs
Breast.results: 2-yr DFS 86% 1 year T vs. 77% 10-yr DFS 69% vs. 69% vs. 63% 52% in obs group crossed over to trastuzumab Cardiac endpoints 1% vs. 4% vs. 7%
Breast.interpretation: Trastuzumab improves long term DFS over observation. 2 years had no additional benefit over 1 year.
Breast.commentary: Hera is the Greek goddess of women, marriage and childbirth. She is sister and wife to Zeus.
Breast.references: 1. Cameron D, Piccart-Gebhart MJ, Gelber RD, et al. 11 years' follow-up of trastuzumab after adjuvant chemotherapy in HER2-positive early breast cancer: final analysis of the HERceptin Adjuvant (HERA) trial [published correction appears in Lancet. 2019 Mar 16;393(10176):1100]. Lancet 2017;389:1195–1205. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(16)32616-2

Breast.patients: early stage Her2+
Breast.methods: Joint analysis of NSABP 31 and NCCTG evaluating trastuzumab
Breast.results: Addition of Trastuzumab to chemo gives 37% increase in relative survival 10-yr OS 75% vs. 84% DFS improved 40% 10-yr DFS 62% vs. 74%
Breast.interpretation: Adding trastuzumab to paclitaxel after AC improves OS.
Breast.references: 1. Perez EA, Romond EH, Suman VJ, et al. Trastuzumab plus adjuvant chemotherapy for human epidermal growth factor receptor 2-positive breast cancer: planned joint analysis of overall survival from NSABP B-31 and NCCTG N9831. J Clin Oncol. 2014;32(33):3744–3752. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2014.55.5730

CNS.methods: ➜ WBRT 30 Gy/10 fx vs. ➜🏆 hippocampal avoidance IMRT WBRT 30 Gy/10 fx memantine in both arms
CNS.results: ✅ hippocampal sparing RT led to less deterioration in executive function and learning and memory also improved fatigue, difficulty speaking, remembering, interference in daily activities, and less cognitive symptoms ✅ Alopecia grade 2 <10% hippocampus D100% <9-10 Gy was best dosimetric factor correlated with outcome
CNS.interpretation: Hippocampal avoidance RT leads to improved cognitive function. Alopecia is markedly reduced with IMRT.
CNS.commentary: Another groundbreaking benefit of whole brain IMRT, not so much emphasized in the trial, is the preservation of hair for the patient. Prior to IMRT, 2D WBRT caused permanent alopecia. The numerical value was provided in the precursor trial, RTOG 0933, referenced here to emphasize the finding. PTV 0 mm Hippocampi D100% <9 (variation acceptable <10) Hippocampi max <16 (VA 17) Optics max <30 Gy (VA <37.5)
CNS.references: 1. Brown PD, Gondi V, Pugh S, et al. Hippocampal Avoidance During Whole-Brain Radiotherapy Plus Memantine for Patients With Brain Metastases: Phase III Trial NRG Oncology CC001. J Clin Oncol 2020. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.19.02767 Protocol pdf [dose constraints p23]: https://classic.clinicaltrials.gov/ProvidedDocs/15/NCT02360215/Prot_SAP_000.pdf#page=23 2. Gondi V, Pugh SL, Tome WA, et al. Preservation of memory with conformal avoidance of the hippocampal neural stem-cell compartment during whole-brain radiotherapy for brain metastases (RTOG 0933): a phase II multi-institutional trial. J Clin Oncol. 2014;32(34):3810-3816. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2014.57.2909

CNS.patients: Mutated NSCLC with asymptomatic intracranial progression
CNS.methods: ➜ upfront cranial RT vs. ➜ delayed cranial RT ~85% EGFR and ~15% ALK
CNS.results: 1-yr intracranial progression 9% vs. 26% 2-yr intracranial progression 22% vs. 50% No change in PFS or OS In the delayed arm, 39/47 who had ICP received RT (or about 38% of that arm) Grade 1-2 radiation necrosis 25% Grade 3 radiation necrosis 6% No grade 4-5 radiation necrosis
CNS.interpretation: Upfront cranial RT delayed next intracranial progression. There was no overall change in PFS or OS. Results could change with additional follow-up. There was crossover in the study.
CNS.commentary: Crossover was ethical but does dilute the power of the study. It's unclear to me from the abstract what type of RT was allowed, but from the wording it seems that it was per discretion.
CNS.references: 1. Tibdewal T, Agarwal JP, Maheshwari G, et al. Wait or treat? managing asymptomatic brain metastases in oncogene-mutated NSCLC: Results of a phase-III randomized controlled trial. J Clin Oncol 2026;44:s16.8624. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2026.44.16_suppl.8624

CNS.patients: NSCLC brain metastasis previously treated with SRS
CNS.methods: NSCLC only. Prior SRS→ ➜ obs vs. ➜ 🏆 tumor treating fields
CNS.results: Median time to intracranial progression 21.9 vs. 11.3 mos TTF also improved deterioration-free survival, physical functioning, and fatigue
CNS.interpretation: TTF after SRS for NSCLC brain metastasis prolongs time to intracranial progression.
CNS.references: 1. Mehta M, Gondi V, Ahluwalia MS, et al. Results from METIS (EF-25), an international, multicenter phase III randomized study evaluating the efficacy and safety of tumor treating fields (TTFields) therapy in NSCLC patients with brain metastases. J Clin Oncol 2024;42:s16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2024.42.16_suppl.2008

CNS.patients: NSCLC with brain metastasis "unsuitable" for surgery or SRS Characteristics: ≥5 mets in ~33% 4 in <10% 3 in ~10% 2 in 20% 1 in 30%
CNS.methods: Noninferiority ➜ WBRT 20 Gy/5 fx vs. ➜ no RT Primary endpoint: QALYs Supportive care and dex in both arms
CNS.results: ↔️ QALYs noninferior ↔️ Median OS ~2 mos, not different ↔️ No difference in QOL or dex use ⬆️ On subanalysis ≥5 metastasis did show OS benefit from WBRT There was some trend to OS benefit if primary controlled, no extracranial mets, or higher GPA.
CNS.interpretation: WBRT for palliation of brain mets is noninferior to observation in QALY outcomes in these poor prognosis patients. Most had ≤4 lesions. On subanalysis OS favors WBRT for ≥5 mets or age <60.
CNS.commentary: On subanalysis, ≥5 mets did favor WBRT for OS. WBRT seems to be more of use in those with greater numbers of mets, while SRS could help preserve QOL in those with fewer lesions. QOL metrics can be unreliable (Amdur 2019), and may especially tend to be negative in poor prognosis patients regardless of the therapy. This is imporant to note, but it should not be suggested that QOL results are to be ignored. Physician assessed symptoms might have been a useful endpoint. There is a better possibility that QALYs would be improved if SRS was used (RTOG 1270, Brown 2017). Over 50% of patients had only…
CNS.references: 1. Mulvenna P, Nankivell M, Barton R, et al. Dexamethasone and supportive care with or without whole brain radiotherapy in treating patients with non-small cell lung cancer with brain metastases unsuitable for resection or stereotactic radiotherapy (QUARTZ): results from a phase 3, non-inferiority, randomised trial. Lancet 2016;388:2004-14. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(16)30825-X
CNS.commentary_refs: Amdur RJ and Chera BS. Misuse of Quality of Life Evaluation in Oncology Studies: Reification, Adaptation, and the U-shaped Curve. Pract Radiat Oncol 2019;9:191-192. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.prro.2018.05.002

CNS.patients: Single brain mets s/p complete resection
CNS.methods: s/p complete resection→ ➜ 🏆 WBRT 50.4 Gy/28 fx vs. ➜ observation
CNS.results: -WBRT after surgery reduces recurrence and neurologic death, but no change in OS ↔️ Median OS 48 wks vs. 43 wks (NS) ⬇️ neurologic death 14% vs. 44% ⬆️ LC 90% vs. 54% ⬆️ Total brain control 82% vs. 30%
CNS.interpretation: WBRT for solitary mets results in improved LC and reduced neurologic death.
CNS.commentary: There was no OS difference with WBRT, but there was a benefit in neurologic death. Dose here is higher than modern dose. Recognize that at the time of WBRT, no gross disease is present, except possibly in the resection cavity. The benefit in total brain recurrence is intriguing. Is there truly that much microscopic disease present at the time of RT, and it is assumed not to arrive later? Or does RT change the tissue in a way such that mets have impaired ability to settle? For a trial evaluating WBRT in multiple brain mets, see QUARTZ (Mulvenna 2016).
CNS.references: 1. Patchell RA, Tibbs PA, Regine WF, et al. Postoperative Radiotherapy in the Treatment of Single Metastases to the Brain: A Randomized Trial. JAMA 1998;280:1485-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.280.17.1485

CNS.methods: ➜🏆 resection + WBRT 36 Gy vs. ➜biopsy + WBRT 36 Gy
CNS.results: Surgery added to WBRT improved OS and LC ⬆️ Median OS 40 wks vs. 15 wks ⬆️ LC 80% vs. 48% (crude)
CNS.interpretation: Addition of surgery to WBRT for solitary mets results in improved OS, reduced LR, and improved QOL.
CNS.commentary: 11% that were randomized were found to not have metastatic tumors. These were not included in analysis. The original result was phrased as LR (20% vs. 52%), but is phrased here as LC to make parallel to other trials and to avoid framing bias. LC of 48% with WBRT seems low; compare to 71% in RTOG 9508 (Andrews 2004). This trial was prior to the era of RECIST criteria. LR was defined as "enlargement of the lesion." Regardless, OS is the more important endpoint. The sizes of the lesions were not reported but would have been enlightening. A current practice is to treat small asymptomatic lesions w…
CNS.references: 1. Patchell RA, Tibbs PA, Walsh JW, et al. A Randomized Trial of Surgery in the Treatment of Single Metastases to the Brain. N Engl J Med 1990;322:494-500. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199002223220802
CNS.commentary_refs: National Comprehensive Cancer Network. Central Nervous System Cancers (Version 1.2022). https://www.nccn.org/professionals/physician_gls/pdf/nscl.pdf. Accessed February 4, 2022.

CNS.citation: Borgelt et al, IJROBP, 1980 Borgelt et al, IJROBP, 1981
CNS.results: -No difference in OS (18 weeks for first study, 15 weeks for 2nd) -OS with 40/15 trended to better in ambulatory lung, p=0.07 and p=0.02 for each study -Better OS with 40/15 in lung brain mets only -No difference in symptom improvement
CNS.interpretation: There is no difference in symptom response or OS between these RT schedules for WBRT. In some populations, 40/15 seemed to trend to better OS.
CNS.commentary: Based on this study some regions such as UK, Europe, Asia, Canada, and Australia routinely treat whole brain with 20 Gy/ 5 fractions (Tsao 2014). For example, the QUARTZ study used 20 Gy/ 5 fractions (Mulvenna 2012). A trend to OS benefit with 15 fx had led some to favor this regimen; however 15 fx in RTOG 1270 led to worse adverse effects compared to 10 fx (Brown 2017).
CNS.references: 1. Borgelt B, Gelber R, Kramer S, et al. The palliation of brain metastases: Final results of the first two studies by the radiation therapy oncology group. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1980;6:1-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/0360-3016(80)90195-9 2. Borgelt B, Gelber R, Larson M, et al. Ultra-rapid high dose irradiation schedules for the palliation of brain metastases: Final results of the first two studies by the radiation therapy oncology group. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1981;7:1633-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/0360-3016(81)90184-x
CNS.commentary_refs: Tsao MN, Rades D, Wirth A, et al. International practice survey on the management of brain metastases: Third International Consensus Workshop on Palliative Radiotherapy and Symptom Control. Clin Oncol (R Coll Radiol) 2012;24:e81–92. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clon.2012.03.008

CNS.patients: Newly diagnosed brain metastasis, with one resected Up to five additional brain mets allowed
CNS.methods: ➜ SRS to post op cavity vs. ➜ 🏆 Cs131 tile brachytherapy to cavity ≤5 other lesions allowed in both arms and treated with SRS
CNS.results: Median cavity RFS 11 mos vs. not reached 2-yr OS 36% vs. 62% Leptomeningeal disease 3% vs. 10% Radiation necrosis 7% vs. 8% Grade ≥3 adverse effects 19% vs. 18%
CNS.interpretation: Cs131 tile brachytherapy to cavity improves LC and OS compared to SRS to cavity.
CNS.commentary: GammaTile® is a bioresorbable collagen implant embedded with four cesium-131 seeds. Half life 9.7 days. It's difficult to understand how the survival rate improved so dramatically. The arms couldn't be imbalanced. It's possible that improved local control and less radiation necrosis did improve survival though the magnitude of benefit still seems to be an outlier. OS was a secondary endpoint and was evaluated because the study met predetermined thresholds to analyze it. At this point I'm unclear if there was central or blinded review. Distinguishing between LC, LR, and radiation necrosis is di…
CNS.references: 1. Weinberg JS, Lin HS, McAleer MF, et al. Final results of a randomized, controlled, phase 3 trial comparing resection and post-operative stereotactic radiation versus resection and cesium-131 tile-based radiation for treatment of newly diagnosed brain metastases. J Clin Oncol 44;2026:s17.LBA2000. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2026.44.17_suppl.LBA2000

CNS.methods: ➜🏆 SRS vs. ➜ SRS+WBRT 30 Gy/12 fx SRS dose 18-22 Gy in SRS+WBRT and SRS dose 20-24 Gy for SRS alone
CNS.results: Median OS: 10.4 mos vs. 7.4 mos (p=.92) 12-mo LC 73% vs. 90% with WBRT 12-mo distant brain control 70% vs. 92% 12-mo total brain control 51% vs. 85% Time to recurrence shorter with SRS No effect of DS-GPA on OS •3-mo cognitive deterioration improved with SRS alone 64% vs. 92% •Long term survivors had improved cognitive deterioration at 12-mos and 3-mos 3-mo QOL higher for SRS alone 3-mo functional independence unchanged
CNS.interpretation: SRS without WBRT in limited mets results in less neuro decline. Tumor reccurence is increased somewhat without WBRT but without change in OS.
CNS.commentary: Although cognitive deterioration was the primary endpoint in this study, it seems well-powered for tumor control as well.
CNS.references: 1. Brown PD, Jaeckle K, Ballman KV, et al. Effect of Radiosurgery Alone vs Radiosurgery With Whole Brain Radiation Therapy on Cognitive Function in Patients With 1 to 3 Brain Metastases: A Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA 2016;316:401-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2016.9839

CNS.citation: Kocher et al, JCO, 2011 Soffietti et al, JCO, 2013
CNS.patients: 1-3 brain mets <3.5 cm, controlled extracranial disease, PS 0-2
CNS.methods: Complete surgery or SRS → ➜ obs vs. ➜🏆 WBRT 30 Gy
CNS.results: HRQOL worse with WBRT ↔️ Time to worse performance status unchanged, 11.7 mos vs. 9.5 mos ↔️ OS similar, ~10.8 mos mos (NS) ⬇️ Neuro death with WBRT: 44% vs. 28% WBRT ⬆️ 2-yr LC surgery: 41% surg vs. 72% WBRT ⬆️ 2-yr LC SRS: 69% SRS vs. 81% WBRT+SRS outfield control surgery: 58% vs. 77% outfield control SRS: 52% vs. 67% intracranial progression 44% vs. 28% Subanalysis shows that surgery and SRS are similar in outcomes.
CNS.interpretation: After surgery or SRS in limited mets, WBRT reduces LR. There is no change in QOL or OS.
CNS.commentary: Interestingly, LC does not appear to be improved with surgery over SRS, though this is non-randomized. The lesions resected were likely of larger size.
CNS.notes: SRS and WBRT in limited mets summary: -Patchell I and II validate use of WBRT and surgery in single met. Surgery results in OS benefit, WBRT improves LC & neuro death but no OS benefit -RTOG 9005 & 9508 dose escalation studies establish doses for SRS after WBRT -Multiple studies (EORTC, MDACC, JSROG, Alliance NCI) look at adding WBRT to SRS alone. Without WBRT there is better cognitive function and OS mostly not different. WBRT does improve total brain control. -N107C and MDACC studies validate use of SRS alone over WBRT alone or obs. Unexpectedly, lower LC with SRS alone than thought. SRS sur…
CNS.references: 1. Kocher M, Soffietti R, Abacioglu U, et al. Adjuvant Whole-Brain Radiotherapy Versus Observation After Radiosurgery or Surgical Resection of One to Three Cerebral Metastases: Results of the EORTC 22952-26001 Study. J Clin Oncol 2011;29:134-41. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2010.30.1655 2. Soffietti R, Kocher M, Abacioglu UM, et al. A European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer Phase III Trial of Adjuvant Whole-Brain Radiotherapy Versus Observation in Patients With One to Three Brain Metastases From Solid Tumors After Surgical Resection or Radiosurgery: Quality-of-Life Results. J Clin Oncol 2013;31:65-72. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2011.41.0639

CNS.methods: ➜🏆 SRS vs. ➜ SRS+WBRT 30 Gy/12 fx Primary endpoint: neuro function per HVLT-R
CNS.results: •Terminated early: SRS+WBRT showed worse neuro decline at 4 months. LC improved with WBRT, but not OS •Neuro decline: SRS alone 24% vs. 52% SRS+WBRT •OS worse with WBRT, 15 mos vs. 6 mos 1-yr LC: 67% SRS vs. 100% SRS+WBRT 1-yr distant brain control: 45% vs. 73%
CNS.interpretation: Adding WBRT to SRS in limited mets results in worse neuro decline and worse OS.
CNS.references: 1. Chang EL, Wefel JS, Hess KR, et al. Neurocognition in patients with brain metastases treated with radiosurgery or radiosurgery plus whole-brain irradiation: a randomised controlled trial. Lancet Oncol 2009;10:1037-44. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(09)70263-3

CNS.citation: Aoyama et al, JAMA, 2006 Aoyama et al, JAMA Oncol 2015
CNS.methods: ➜ SRS vs. ➜🏆 SRS+WBRT 30 Gy/10 fx SRS dose 18-25 Gy for SRS alone. Dose reduced 30% if with WBRT MMSE used to measure cognitive function
CNS.results: •WBRT decreased recurrence but no improvement in OS or neuro death. •Median OS 8.0 mos vs. 7.5 WBRT+SRS (NS) •1-yr OS 28% vs. 39% (NS) •Neuro death 19% vs. 23% (NS) •No diff in adverse effects, neuro function 1-yr brain control 24% vs. 53% (SS) -Secondary analysis of NSCLC patients with 1-4 mets: -If DS-GPA of 2.5-4.0, Median OS 10.6 mos vs. 16.7 mos WBRT+SRS -No OS difference if DS-GPA 0.5-2.0 -No difference in MMSE testing
CNS.interpretation: SRS alone without WBRT in limited mets resulted in increased brain tumor recurrence but no change in OS or neurologic death.
CNS.references: 1. Aoyama H, Shirato H, Tago M, et al. Stereotactic Radiosurgery Plus Whole-Brain Radiation Therapy vs Stereotactic Radiosurgery Alone for Treatment of Brain Metastases: A Randomized Controlled Trial. JAMA 2006;295:2483-91. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.295.21.2483 2. Aoyama H, Tago M, Shirato H, et al. JROSG-. Stereotactic Radiosurgery With or Without Whole-Brain Radiotherapy for Brain Metastases: Secondary Analysis of the JROSG 99-1 Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2015;1:457-64. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2015.1145

CNS.subtopic: Addition of SRS boost to WBRT
CNS.citation: Andrews et al, Lancet, 2004 Sperduto et al, IJROBP, 2014
CNS.methods: ➜ WBRT 37.5 Gy vs. ➜🏆 SRS+WBRT SRS dose per RTOG 9005: 24 Gy size ≤2.0 cm 18 Gy size >2 to ≤3cm 15 Gy size >3 cm
CNS.results: Addition of SRS to WBRT improved LC and KPS but no change in OS ⬆️ 1-yr LC 71% WBRT vs. 82% SRS+WBRT •On univariate analysis, SRS increased survival for single met, 4.9 vs. 6.5 mos. This result was not maintained on MVA. •On MVA of all patients in both arms, RPA 1 and NSCLC histology had better OS (regardless of treatment arm) •Secondary analysis, multiple Cox regression: OS benefit with SRS in GPA 3.5-4, median OS 21 vs. 10.3 mos •6-mo stable/improved KPS 27% vs. 43% •Median OS 5.7 mos SRS vs. 6.5 mos (NS)
CNS.interpretation: Adding an SRS boost to WBRT in limited mets improves LC.
CNS.commentary: Note the subanalaysis of this study - it is commonly misrepresented. Superior prognositc systems are now available from other studies looking at GPA, histology, size, and number of mets - in particular see the MDACC study (Mahajan 2017).
CNS.references: 1. Andrews DW, Scott CB, Sperduto PW, et al. Whole brain radiation therapy with or without stereotactic radiosurgery boost for patients with one to three brain metastases: phase III results of the RTOG 9508 randomised trial. Lancet 2004;363:1665-72. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(04)16250-8 2. Sperduto PW, Shanley R, Luo X, et al. Secondary analysis of RTOG 9508, a phase 3 randomized trial of whole-brain radiation therapy versus WBRT plus stereotactic radiosurgery in patients with 1-3 brain metastases; poststratified by the graded prognostic assessment (GPA). Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2014;90:526-531. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2014.07.002

CNS.patients: Recurrent primary or recurrent brain tumors s/p prior WBRT
CNS.methods: Phase I Dose escalation in 3 Gy increments, beginning at: 18 Gy for ≤2 cm 15 Gy for 2-3 cm 12 Gy for 3-4 cm Grade 3-5 toxicity defined as limiting. All had previous intracranial (range 30-60 Gy). LINAC and GammaKnife allowed. Dose to 50-90% isodose line.
CNS.results: Max tolerated doses: 18 Gy for 21-30 mm and 15 Gy for 31-40 mm. 24 Gy for ≤20 mm was the highest evaluable dose but may not be MTD; Investigators declined to escalate to 27 Gy in ≤2 cm. Diameter associated with neuroadverse effects.
CNS.interpretation: Max tolerated doses in SRS for brain mets: 24 Gy for ≤20 mm, 18 Gy for 21-30 mm, and 15 Gy for 31-40 mm.
CNS.commentary: Prior intracranial in all ranging from 30-60 Gy. Established dosing for later trials. This is a phase I adverse effects study and was not powered for LC. On a MVA dose was not associated with LC: lower doses may have just as effective LC. (The authors did a rudimentary LC analysis looking at GK and Linac, but arms are too imbalanced to draw conclusions.) 36% were primary tumors, 64% were mets.
CNS.references: 1. Shaw E, Scott C, Souhami L, et al. Single dose radiosurgical treatment of recurrent previously irradiated primary brain tumors and brain metastases: final report of RTOG protocol 90-05. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2000;47:291-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0360-3016(99)00507-6

CNS.methods: Phase III ➜🏆 SRS vs. ➜ WBRT
CNS.results: Leptomeningeal disease 8% vs. 3% New lesions 45% vs. 23% Median OS and neurologic death not different
CNS.interpretation: SRS for 4-20 brain metastasis has unchanged OS and neurologic death compared to WBRT.
CNS.references: 1. Aizer AA, Shin KY, Catalano PJ, et al. Treatment for Brain Metastases With Stereotactic Radiation vs Hippocampal-Avoidance Whole Brain Radiation: A Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA 2026;335:1127-1136. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2026.0076

CNS.methods: ➜🏆 SRS vs. ➜ WBRT (62% had memantine) Neurocognitive testing
CNS.results: Cognitive function is better with SRS Median OS 8-10 mos, p=0.45 distant brain failure 4.2 mos vs. 18.1 mos 4-mo LC 100% vs. 96%
CNS.interpretation: SRS for 4-15 brain metastasis has unchanged OS compared to WBRT. Cognitive function is superior with SRS.
CNS.references: 1. Li J, Ludmir EB, Wang Y, et al. Stereotactic Radiosurgery versus Whole-brain Radiation Therapy for Patients with 4-15 Brain Metastases: A Phase III Randomized Controlled Trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2020;108:s21-22. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2020.07.2108

CNS.methods: Meta-analysis of 24 studies of single and multi fx SRS to brain mets. Evaluates radiation necrosis and LC Various doses and fractionation regimens used
CNS.results: LC in size 4-14 cc (2-3 cm) definitive •single fx 78% vs. multi 93%, p=0.18 LC in size >14 cc (>3cm) definitive •single fx 78% vs. multi 79%, p=.76 LC in size >14 cc (>3cm), post op •single fx 62% vs. multi 86%, p=0.13 Rad necrosis in 4-14 cc (2-3 cm), definitive •single fx 23% vs. multi 7%, p=0.003 Rad necrosis in >14 cc (>3cm), definitive •single fx 12% vs. multi 7%, p=0.29 Rad necrosis in >14 cc (>3cm), post op •single fx 7% vs. multi 8%, p=.85
CNS.interpretation: In tumors size 4-14 cc, about 2-3 cm diameter, there is a reduction in radiation necrosis with multi fx RT. There are changes in LC that did not reach statistical significance. Various techniques and assessments would have been used across studies. Randomized and prospective trials are warranted.
CNS.commentary: Observe that only one of the comparisons was statisitically significant. Otherwise there are some trends. There is heterogeneity in techniques and methods used. There is no consensus as to how to differentiate LR from radiation necrosis. The retrospective nature of the study may lead to confirmation bias. The authors acknowledge that the information is hypothesis generating and that randomized trials are warranted. In multi fx definitive and post-op groups, the most common dose was 27 Gy/3 fx. For single fx definitive: 18 Gy. Single fx post-op: 15 Gy. BED ranges: 33-65 Gy for single fx unresec…
CNS.references: 1. Lehrer EJ, Peterson JL, Zaorsky NG, et al. Single versus Multifraction Stereotactic Radiosurgery for Large Brain Metastases: An International Meta-analysis of 24 Trials. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2019;103:618-30. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2018.10.038

CNS.methods: ➜ cavity SRS 12-20 Gy (dose per RTOG 1270) vs. ➜ cavity SRT 27 Gy/3 fx or 25 Gy/ 5 fx (for <30 cc or ≥ 30 cc respectively) SRS for all unresected lesions
CNS.results: Ongoing trial Primary endpoint: cavity LC
CNS.references: 1. Alliance for Clinical Trials in Oncology. Single Fraction Stereotactic Radiosurgery Compared With Fractionated Stereotactic Radiosurgery in Treating Patients With Resected Metastatic Brain Disease. Available at: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04114981. NLM identifier: NCT04114981. Accessed August 21, 2020.

CNS.subtopic: SRS for post op cavity vs. WBRT
CNS.citation: Brown et al, Lancet Oncol, 2017 Trifiletti et al, ASTRO, 2019 Breen et al, ASTRO 2022
CNS.patients: 1-4 brain metastases and resection of 1 lesion. STR allowed (77% had single met, ~10% STR) Resection cavity <5 cm and unresected lesions ≤3.0 cm
CNS.methods: ➜ SRS to cavity vs. ➜ WBRT to 30 or 37.5 Gy Unresected lesions treated with SRS in both arms •Post-op cavity dose: 20 Gy for <4.2 cc 18 Gy for 4.2-8.0 cc 17 Gy for 8.0-14.4 cc 15 Gy for 14.4-20 cc 14 Gy for 20-30 cc 12 Gy for >30 cc or <5 cm max •SRS dose to unresected, without WBRT: 24 Gy for <1 cm 22 Gy for 1-2 cm 20 Gy for 2-3 cm •SRS dose to unresected, with WBRT: 22 Gy for <1 cm 20 Gy for 1-2 cm 18 Gy for 2-3 cm
CNS.results: After post-hoc central review of LC: 12-mo cavity LC 79% vs. 87%, p=0.099 Size >3 cm, after central review: ⛔ 12-mo cavity LC 76% vs. 87%, p=0.010 Initial report (no central review): ⛔ 12-mo cavity LC 61% vs. 81% (supp data) 12-mo distant brain control 65% vs. 89% 12-mo total cranial control 37% vs. 72% ✅ Median OS ~12 mos, not different Leptomeningeal disease in 2-5% Radiation necrosis 4% vs. 0 ✅ Better cognitive deterioration free survival with SRS, median 3.7 mos vs. 3.0 mos ✅ 6-mo cognitive deterioration 52% SRS vs. 85% WBRT 🔀 30 Gy and 37.5 Gy compared: -More grade ≥3 adverse effects with…
CNS.interpretation: With SRS, OS is maintained compared to WBRT alone. QOL is improved with SRS. On post-hoc central review, there was no difference in LC between SRS and WBRT. However, SRS continued to show worse LC in lesions >3 cm. On the initial report, LC was worse with SRS When comparing 37.5 Gy to 30 Gy, 37.5 Gy led to more adverse effects. There was some trend to benefit in LC, cranial control, and OS, but not significant.
CNS.commentary: The defined primary endpoints of the study were QOL and OS, but LC is shown here first due to its interest to practitioners and to make parallel to other similar studies here. If the study is powered for OS, it should also be powered for LC as a primary endpoint. The initial report showed inferior LC with SRS. This finding was contrary to the conventional wisdom that SRS should result in superior LC due to its higher BED (But note similar LC of 70% in MDACC post-op study, as well as EORTC and Alliance looking at SRS without surgery [Chang 2009, Kocher 2011, Brown 2016]). Are these failures or …
CNS.references: 1. Brown PD, Ballman KV, Cerhan JH, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2022:114;063-1064. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2022.09.012
