CNS.subtopic: SRS for post op cavity STR vs. WBRT
CNS.patients: 1-4 brain metastasis and resection of all, GTR or STR (73% had single met) One lesion >3 cm allowed
CNS.methods: Resection of all brain mets → ➜🏆 SRS to cavity for STRs & observation of GTRs (termed "salvage SRS") vs. ➜ WBRT 37.5 Gy 40% in SRS arm had STR and SRS; 60% had GTR and obs SRS doses: 24 Gy for ≤4 cc, 18 Gy for >4 cc
CNS.results: -cavity LC 51% vs. 78% (crude, calculated using table A2) -LF as sole first recurrence 51% vs. 45% -LF as any first recurrence 64% vs. 48% -Median intracranial PFS 4.0 vs. 10.4 mos -Median OS 15.6 mos, not different -Neurologic death ~22%, not different -Leptomeningeal mets 13%, not different -Radiation necrosis 2.6% vs. 1.6% Grade 2-4 cognitive adverse effects 7.7% vs. 16.4%
CNS.interpretation: SRS for STR only in resected brain mets is noninferior compared to WBRT in OS. However LR is increased.
CNS.commentary: Crude LC is hand calculated by myself using supplemental table A2. Note the high rate of LF and low PFS in the SRS arm. Despite the poor control, there is still no change in OS or neurologic death. This study requires resection of all brain mets. SRS was given only when resection was subtotal (40%), but not for GTR (60%), which may explain the poor LC in this study. WBRT was 15 fractions (higher BED than 30 Gy in 10 fx). Protocol dose constraints pdf, page 35: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.2018.78.6186/suppl_file/protocol_2018.786186.pdf#page=35
CNS.references: 1. Kayama T, Sato S, Sakurada K, et al. Effects of Surgery With Salvage Stereotactic Radiosurgery Versus Surgery With Whole-Brain Radiation Therapy in Patients With One to Four Brain Metastases (JCOG0504): A Phase III, Noninferiority, Randomized Controlled Trial. J Clin Oncol 2018;36:3282-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2018.78.6186 Protocol pdf [dose constraints p35]: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.2018.78.6186/suppl_file/protocol_2018.786186.pdf#page=35

CNS.patients: 1-3 brain metastases and GTR of ≥1 lesion (~62% had single met) Resection cavity ≤4 cm and unresected lesions ≤3 cm
CNS.methods: ➜🏆 SRS to cavity vs. ➜ Obs to cavity Unresected lesions treated with SRS in both arms SRS doses: 16 Gy for ≤10 cc, 14 Gy for >10.1-15 cc, 12 Gy for >15 cc
CNS.results: LC improved with SRS over obs 12-mo cavity LC 72% vs. 43% 12-mo distant brain control 58% vs. 67% (NS) Time to WBRT 15 mos (NS) Median OS 17 mos, not different Neurologic death 64% vs. 48%, p=0.13 12-mo leptomeningeal 28% vs. 16% (NS) No radiation necrosis events with SRS -Larger tumor size was associated with LR (in obs and SRS arms). SRS improved LC even in small resected lesions over obs. -Factors not associated with LR: histology (lung, breast, melanoma, other), extracranial disease status, GPA score, # brain mets
CNS.interpretation: SRS improves LC compared to observation in resected brain mets. OS and neurologic death are unchanged.
CNS.commentary: An unexpected finding was that LC after post-op SRS was only 72%; was assumed to be higher. See commentary on RTOG 1270 (Brown 2017). All follow-up MRIs were reviewed centrally by the study neuroradiologist. Still, there is no consensus on how to diagnose rad necrosis by imaging. Similar finding in RTOG 1270, LC 61%. Note similar LC of 70% in EORTC and Alliance looking at SRS without surgery (Kocher 2011, Brown 2016). Does surgery add benefit in LC when SRS is given? Larger tumors had worse LC in both arms. Investigation may be warranted toward higher dose or multi-fractionation in these tumor…
CNS.references: 1. Mahajan A, Ahmed S, McAleer MF, et al. Post-operative stereotactic radiosurgery versus observation for completely resected brain metastases: a single-centre, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2017;18:1040-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30414-X

CNS.citation: Sperduto et al, IJROBP, 2008 Sperduto et al, JCO, 2012
CNS.methods: Evaluation of patients from 11 institutions
CNS.results: Refer to paper for full prognostic index. Lung cancer prognostic factors: KPS, age, extra-cranial mets, # brain mets Melanoma and renal cell prognostic factors: KPS and number of mets Breast cancer prognostic factors: tumor subtype, KPS, age GI cancer prognostic factors: only KPS
CNS.interpretation: Prognostic factors for brain metastasis vary by diagnosis.
CNS.notes: WBRT for multiple brain mets: -RTOG 6901 & 7361 dose escalation studies established a few standard dose regimens for radiation therapy (30 Gy/10 fx, 20 Gy/5 fx) -QUARTZ compared WBRT to supportive care. The approaches were noninferior. On subanalysis, ≥5 mets had OS benefit. -RTOG 0614 showed cognitive benefit with memantine -Phase III NRG-CC001 showed cognitive benefit with IMRT hippocampal sparing
CNS.references: 1. Sperduto PW, Berkey B, Gaspar LE, et al. A New Prognostic Index and Comparison to Three Other Indices for Patients With Brain Metastases: An Analysis of 1,960 Patients in the RTOG Database. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2008;70:510-4. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2007.06.074 2. Sperduto PW, Kased N, Roberge D, et al. Summary Report on the Graded Prognostic Assessment: An Accurate and Facile Diagnosis-Specific Tool to Estimate Survival for Patients With Brain Metastases. J Clin Oncol 2012;30:419-25. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2011.38.0527

CNS.methods: Recursive partitioning analysis
CNS.results: Class 1: KPS≥70, <65yo, brain is only site of mets, controlled primary. 7.1 mos Class 2: all others 4.2 mos Class 3: KPS<70. 2.3 mos
CNS.interpretation: This RPA system classifies patients into distinct groups that may help guide therapeutic decisions.
CNS.commentary: Other more advanced prognostic systems such as GPA have been developed; however, RPA remains a simple method.
CNS.references: 1. Gaspar L, Scott C, Rotman M, et al. Recursive partitioning analysis (RPA) of prognostic factors in three radiation therapy oncology group (RTOG) brain metastases trials. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1997;37:745-51. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0360-3016(96)00619-0

Accessed September 19, 2020.

CNS.citation: Guo et al, JAMA Netw Open, 2023
CNS.methods: ➜🏆 adj TMZ + IFN-α vs. ➜ adj TMZ Maximum 12 cycles of TMZ was allowed, but median 6.0 was given in both arms
CNS.results: Overall (grade 3 & 4) ⬆️ Median OS 26.7 mos vs. 18.8 mos ⬆️ 2-yr OS 57% vs. 37% ⬆️ 5-yr OS 18% vs. 9% WHO Grade 4 ⬆️ Median OS 20.5 mos vs. 17.7 mos WHO Grade 3 ⬆️ Median OS 39.6 mos vs. 20.5 mos Unmethylated MGMT ⬆️ Median OS 24.7 mos vs. 17.4 mos Methylated MGMT No improvement in OS No overall difference in PFS. On subanalysis PFS is improved in grade 3, and trend to improvement in unmethylated, p=0.06. No PFS improvement in grade 4 or methylated.
CNS.interpretation: Adjuvant IFN-α improves OS in high grade glioma. On subanalysis the effect seems to be present only in unmethylated.
CNS.commentary: The rationale for the study was that research has shown that IFN-α downregulates MGMT. 54% were grade 4. It is unknown how many grade 4 were GBM, but epidemiologically, it would be expected to be a large majority. The OS for grade 3, 20.5 mos in the control arm, suggests these may have actually been grade 4 and were miscategorized; OS for grade 3 in CATNON was 82 mos. This may mean that the trial results apply more to grade 4. Detailed molecular studies are not yet available. A post-hoc eval of even simply IDH status would be enlightening. Some may wish to see findings reproduced in their home…
CNS.references: 1. Guo C, Yang Q, Xu P, et al. Adjuvant Temozolomide Chemotherapy With or Without Interferon Alfa Among Patients With Newly Diagnosed High-grade Gliomas: A Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Netw Open 2023;6:e2253285. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2022.53285 Protocol pdf available at: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2022.53285
CNS.commentary_refs: Wikipedia. Interferon alfa. Available from: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interferon_alfa Accessed 29 January, 2023.

CNS.methods: ➜ 60 Gy + conc & adj TMZ x6 cycles vs. ➜ 60 Gy + TMZ + lomustine
CNS.results: Median OS 31 vs. 48 mos (ITT) Median OS 30 vs. 40 mos per protocol Stratified by center and RPA: log rank, p=0.0492, & Cox regression, p=0.0641 Median PFS 16.7 mos, not different (ITT) Grade ≥3 adverse effects 51% vs. 59%
CNS.interpretation: Adding lomustine to TMZ and RT in GB with methylated MGMT improves OS. Some critics question if these results will be reproducible.
CNS.commentary: The trial may be underpowered. The modifed ITT analysis leads to patients being excluded and even lower power. Some call for a multi-institutional trial with larger numbers (Stupp 2019). It is unclear how there can be a change in OS without a change in PFS. Perhaps lomustine has a later effect, or lomustine creates more pseudoprogression that was called progression. There were some small differences in 2nd line therapies but not major. Nevertheless, GBM is a deadly disease, and some may be willing to accept treatments from a possibly underpowered trial for a chance of benefit.
CNS.references: 1. Herrlinger U, Tzaridis T, Mack F, et al. Lomustine-temozolomide combination therapy versus standard temozolomide therapy in patients with newly diagnosed glioblastoma with methylated MGMTpromoter (CeTeG/NOA:09): a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial. Lancet 2019;393:678-88. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(18)31791-4 Full text: https://www.zora.uzh.ch/id/eprint/176124/
CNS.commentary_refs: Louis DN, Perry A, Wesseling P, et al. The 2021 WHO Classification of Tumors of the Central Nervous System: a summary [published online ahead of print, 2021 Jun 29]. Neuro Oncol 2021;noab106. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/neuonc/noab106

CNS.methods: ➜ 54 Gy + 16 Gy boost + conc & adj TMZ x6 cycles vs. ➜ 54 Gy + 16 Gy boost + TMZ + lomustine
CNS.results: Ongoing study Primary endpoint: OS Secondary endpoints: PFS, adverse effects, QOL
CNS.commentary: brainstem and optics max ≤55 Gy (variation acceptable ≤60 Gy)
CNS.references: 1. NRG Oncology. Testing the Addition of the Chemotherapy Drug Lomustine (Gleostine®) to the Usual Treatment (Temozolomide and Radiation Therapy) for Newly Diagnosed MGMT Methylated Glioblastoma. Accessed May 18, 2023. Available at: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT05095376 NLM identifier: NCT05095376.

CNS.subtopic: Optune tumor treating fields in newly diagnosed
CNS.citation: Stupp et al, JAMA, 2015 Kesari et al, SNO, 2015 Stupp et al, JAMA, 2017
CNS.methods: RT + conc TMZ → ➜ 🏆 tumor treating fields (until 2nd progression or 2 years) + adjuvant TMZ x6-12 cycles vs. ➜ adjuvant TMZ x6-12 cycles
CNS.results: OS improved with TTF ⬆️ Median OS 20.9 mos vs. 16.0 mos (24.5 vs. 19.8 mos from diagnosis) ⬆️ 2-yr OS 43% vs. 31% ⬆️ 5-yr OS 13% vs. 5% ⬆️ Median PFS 6.7 vs. 4.0 mos On subanalysis, benefit present in any MGMT status, any age -Adverse effects: 52% grade 1-2 skin, 2% grade 3. Best benefit when wearing 90% of the time. Need to wear >50% of the time to have any benefit. -OS benefit still present with chemo or bev (11.8 mos vs. 9.0 mos)
CNS.interpretation: TTF improves OS and PFS
CNS.commentary: TTF may be able to be approved in some cases of anaplastic astrocytoma if coded as glioblastoma. This is because at the time of the trial, molecular coding was not yet in use, and many anaplastic astrocytomas would have previously been designed as GBM and included in the TTF trial. Tumor treating fields (TTF) mechanism: low amplitude alternating electric fields that disrupt cell division and organelles. Required to wear ≥18 hrs in protocol. Sharp dropoff of treatment effect if device worn <50% of time. At 75% of time (18 hours), a greater benefit occurs.
CNS.references: 1. Stupp R, Taillibert S, Kanner AA, et al. Maintenance Therapy With Tumor-Treating Fields Plus Temozolomide vs Temozolomide Alone for Glioblastoma: A Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA 2015;314:2535-43. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2015.16669 Protocol pdf in supplements: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2015.16669 2. Kesari S, Ram Z, on Behalf of EF-14 Trial Investigators. ATNT-19TUMOR TREATING FIELDS WITH CHEMOTHERAPY COMPARED TO CHEMOTHERAPY ALONE IN GLIOBLASTOMA PATIENTS AT FIRST RECURRENCE: A POST-HOC ANALYSIS OF THE EF-14 TRIAL. Neuro Oncol 2015;17:v14-v. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/neuonc/nov205.19 3. Stupp R, Taillibert S, Kanner A, et al. Effect of Tumor-Treating Fields Plus Maintenance Temozolomide vs Maintenance Temozolomide Alone on Survival in Patients With Glioblastoma: A Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA 2017;318:2306-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2017.18718

CNS.citation: Stupp et al, NEJM, 2005 Hegi et al, NEJM, 2005 Stupp et al, Lancet Oncol, 2009
CNS.patients: Glioblastoma age ≤70, PS≤2 MGMT was methylated in 45%
CNS.methods: ➜ 🏆 60 Gy + conc TMZ & adj TMZ x6 cycles vs. ➜ 60 Gy Later analysis evaluated effect of MGMT methylation
CNS.results: Improved OS with TMZ. ⬆️ Median OS 14.6 mos vs. 12.1 mos ⬆️ 2-yr OS 26.5% vs. 10.4%. ⬆️ 5-yr OS 10% vs. 2% Methylated MGMT: ⬆️ Median OS 22 mos TMZ vs. 15 mos ⬆️ 2-yr OS 46% vs. 23% ⬆️ 5-yr OS 14% vs. 5% Unmethylated MGMT: ↗️ Median OS 12.7 vs. 11.8 mos (p=0.06) ↗️ 2-yr OS 14% vs. 2% ↗️ 5-yr OS 8% vs. 0
CNS.interpretation: Adding TMZ to RT results in OS benefit. On unplanned subanalysis, those with a methylated MGMT promoter benefitted, though there was trend to benefit without methylation.
CNS.commentary: While the p value for unmethylated MGMT did not meet significance, the subanalysis was unplanned, and even if planned, subanalysis are rarely specifically powered. P=0.06, although not meeting typical medical statistical standards, would still be wildly amazing odds at a casino, and for a person who has a GBM.
CNS.references: 1. Stupp R, Mason WP, van den Bent MJ, et al. Radiotherapy plus Concomitant and Adjuvant Temozolomide for Glioblastoma. N Engl J Med 2005;352:987-96. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa043330 2. Hegi ME, Diserens A-C, Gorlia T, et al. MGMT Gene Silencing and Benefit from Temozolomide in Glioblastoma. N Engl J Med 2005;352:997-1003. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa043331 3. Stupp R, Hegi ME, Mason WP, et al. Effects of radiotherapy with concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide versus radiotherapy alone on survival in glioblastoma in a randomised phase III study: 5-year analysis of the EORTC-NCIC trial. Lancet Oncol 2009;10:459-66. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(09)70025-7

CNS.patients: Glioblastoma. All patients received radiation + carmustine
CNS.methods: Update to previous RTOG RPA from 1993 This RPA excludes AA and includes more variables
CNS.results: III: •<50 yo and KPS ≥90, •MS 17.1 mos, 1-yr OS 70%, 2-yr OS 20%, 5-yr OS 14% IV: •<50 yo and KPS <90, OR •≥50 yo, KPS ≥70, resection, and working •MS 11.2 mos, 1-yr OS 46%, 2-yr OS 7%, 3-yr OS 4% "V+VI": •≥50 yo, KPS ≥70, resection, not working OR •≥50 yo, KPS ≥70, biopsy only OR •≥50 yo, KPS <70 •MS 7.5 mos, 1-yr OS 28%, 2-yr OS 1%, 3-yr OS 0%
CNS.interpretation: This RPA system classifies patients into distinct groups that may help guide therapeutic decisions.
CNS.notes: Glioblastoma overview -BTSG dose escalation study establishes 60 Gy dose -EORTC Stupp study shows OS benefit with temozolomide. Effect best if MGMT methylated -Multiple studies (EORTC/NCIC, NOA, Malmstom, Roa, IAEA, French) outline strategies for low KPS or elderly: 5, 10, or 15 fx RT ±TMZ, or TMZ alone -EF14 Stupp study shows OS benefit with Optune tumor treating fields
CNS.references: 1. Li J, Wang M, Won M, et al. Validation and Simplification of the Radiation Therapy Oncology Group Recursive Partitioning Analysis Classification for Glioblastoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2011;81:623-30. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2010.06.012

CNS.patients: 3 BTSG trials for malignant glioma (86% GB)
CNS.methods: Analyzed outcomes according to RT dose
CNS.results: Median OS: 18 wks for no RT, 28 wks for 50 Gy, 36 wks for 55 Gy, and 42 wks for 60 Gy KPS was biased against the ≤45 Gy, group - more died in that group before completing therapy
CNS.interpretation: 60 Gy RT resulted in the best OS compared to lower doses and observation.
CNS.references: 1. Walker MD, Strike TA, Sheline GE, et al. An analysis of dose-effect relationship in the radiotherapy of malignant gliomas. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1979;5:1725-31. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/0360-3016(79)90553-4

CNS.methods: 60 Gy + TMZ → ➜ bevacizumab and TMZ x6-12 cycles vs. ➜ adjuvant TMZ x6-12 cycles Bev started at week 4 during RT and continued as maintenance Crossover allowed if disease progression Median 6 cycles TMZ given in the bev group, and median 3 cycles in the control group
CNS.results: No change in OS: 15.7 vs. 16.1 mos PFS longer with bev: 10.7 mos vs. 7.3 mos •Adverse effects higher in bev group: hypertension, thromboembolism, intenstinal perf, neutropenia, decline in QOL and cognitive function •Those with Pro-B type (indicative of proneural) had improved PFS, but not OS. But non-Pro B type had worse OS
CNS.interpretation: Bev improves only PFS without benefit in OS while increasing adverse effects, including cognitive. PFS may be a result of vascular changes and not tumoricidal effects
CNS.commentary: Amount of vascular events are what is expected in other bevacizumab trials. Some say lack of OS benefit was due to crossover, but note that median OS without bev is nearly identical to EORTC Stupp trial outcome and a direct comparison is not possible (Stupp 2009). Bevacizumab also failed to show OS benefit in a randomized trial in recurrent GB (Wick 2017). Dose constraints: optic chiasm max 56 Gy, optic nerves max 55 Gy, brainstem max 60 Gy
CNS.references: 1. Gilbert MR, Dignam JJ, Armstrong TS, et al. A Randomized Trial of Bevacizumab for Newly Diagnosed Glioblastoma. N Engl J Med 2014;370:699-708. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1308573 Protocol pdf [radiation planning p26]: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa1308573/suppl_file/nejmoa1308573_protocol.pdf#page=26

CNS.methods: 60 Gy + TMZ → ➜ bev concurrent and maintenance and adjuvant TMZ x6 vs. ➜ adjuvant TMZ x6 cycles
CNS.results: No change in OS: 16.8 vs. 16.7 mos 2-yr OS 34% vs. 30%, not different PFS longer with bev: 10.6 mos vs. 6.2 mos •In bev group, QOL and PS maintained longer, and dexamethasone requirement lower. •Grade 3 events higher with bev: 67% vs. 51%
CNS.interpretation: Bev improves only PFS without benefit in OS while increasing adverse effects. PFS may be a result of vascular changes and not tumoricidal effects. In this study QOL did not differ.
CNS.commentary: QOL results are similar to controls in this study, differing from RTOG 0825 results showing worse QOL. This study is sponsored by the pharmaceutical company, leading some to suspect potential bias.
CNS.references: 1. Chinot OL, Wick W, Mason W, et al. Bevacizumab plus Radiotherapy–Temozolomide for Newly Diagnosed Glioblastoma. N Engl J Med 2014;370:709-22. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1308345 Protocol pdf [radiation planning p92]: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa1308345/suppl_file/nejmoa1308345_protocol.pdf#page=92

CNS.patients: Glioblastoma age ≤70, ECOG ≤2 (this study not restricted to elderly)
CNS.methods: Noninferiority ➜ 60 Gy/30 fx + conc/adj TMZ vs. ➜ 60 Gy/20 fx + conc/adj TMZ 6 cycles adjuvant TMZ
CNS.results: Short course non-inf to conventional ↔️ Median OS 11.0 mos vs. 14.4 mos (p<0.01 for noninferiority, p=0.751 for superiority) ↔️ QOL not diff at 3 or 4 mo post-RT No radiation necrosis in hypofx
CNS.interpretation: Short course chemoRT was noninferior to conventional chemoRT & not associated with either increased RN or decreased QOL
CNS.commentary: Characteristics: MGMT+ (54%), IDH1m (10%)
CNS.references: 1. Patel SI, Amanie J, Murtha AD, et al. A Randomized Trial of Short-Course Versus Conventional Radiotherapy With Concomitant and Adjuvant Temozolomide in Patients 18 to 70 Years of Age With Glioblastoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2021;111:s24. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2021.07.083

CNS.subtopic: Elderly: addition of TMZ to RT
CNS.methods: Superiority ➜ 40 Gy/15 fx + conc and adjuvant TMZ vs. ➜ RT alone 40 Gy/15 fx Up to 12 cycles of TMZ were allowed, median 5 given
CNS.results: Median OS and PFS improved with TMZ Median OS 9.3 mos vs. 7.6 mos Median PFS 5.3 mos vs. 3.9 mos Methylated MGMT median OS 13.5 mos vs. 7.7 mos Un-methylated MGMT median OS 10.0 mos vs 7.9 mos
CNS.interpretation: Addition of TMZ to short course RT in elderly GB resulted in longer OS compared to short course RT alone.
CNS.commentary: CTV 1.5 cm. Dose constraints: OARs max ≤100% and lens max ≤4 Gy
CNS.references: 1. Perry JR, Laperriere N, O'Callaghan CJ, et al. Short-Course Radiation plus Temozolomide in Elderly Patients with Glioblastoma. N Engl J Med 2017;376:1027-1037. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1611977 Study protocol: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa1611977/suppl_file/nejmoa1611977_protocol.pdf#page=23

CNS.patients: High grade glioma (95% GB), age >65, KPS≥60
CNS.methods: Noninferiority, margin 0.25 ➜ 54-60 Gy vs. ➜ dose dense TMZ (every other week)
CNS.results: RT and TMZ noninferior: Median OS 9.6 mos vs. 8 mos Median EFS 4.7 vs. 3.3 mos -With MGMT methylation, longer EFS with TMZ: 8.4 vs. 4.6 mos -With no MGMT methylation, outcomes poor but RT better: 4.6 vs. 3.3 mos
CNS.interpretation: OS with TMZ was noninferior to RT in elderly or poor KPS GBM. TMZ is ideal for methylated MGMT and RT is ideal for unmethylated MGMT.
CNS.commentary: The noninferiority margin was 25%, which is generous for a noninferiority trial. The HR & CI for OS was HR=1.09 (0.84-1.42). EFS curves appear in favor of radiation, and the CI only slightly crossed 1, HR=1.15 (0.92-1.43). It seems possible that a differently designed trial, one with higher power, and especially one with EFS as the promised endpoint could have shown superiority with radiation or inconclusiveness.
CNS.references: 1. Wick W, Platten M, Meisner C, et al. Temozolomide chemotherapy alone versus radiotherapy alone for malignant astrocytoma in the elderly: the NOA-08 randomised, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2012;13:707-15. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(12)70164-X

CNS.methods: Noninferiority ➜ 60 Gy/30 fx vs. ➜ 34 Gy/10 fx vs. ➜ TMZ alone
CNS.results: Median OS 6 mos vs. 7.5 mos vs. 8.3 mos 34 Gy and TMZ are not different In age >70, OS better with TMZ and hypofx
CNS.interpretation: 34 Gy and TMZ are noninferior to standard fractionation in elderly GB.
CNS.references: 1. Malmström A, Grønberg BH, Marosi C, et al. Temozolomide versus standard 6-week radiotherapy versus hypofractionated radiotherapy in patients older than 60 years with glioblastoma: the Nordic randomised, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2012;13:916-26. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(12)70265-6

CNS.trial: Cross Cancer Institute, Edmonton, Alberta, Canada
CNS.methods: Noninferiority ➜ 60 Gy/30 fx vs. ➜ 40 Gy/15 fx
CNS.results: Median OS 5.1 vs 5.6 mos (NS)
CNS.interpretation: Hypofx is noninferior to conventional fx in elderly GB.
CNS.references: 1. Roa W, Brasher PMA, Bauman G, et al. Abbreviated Course of Radiation Therapy in Older Patients With Glioblastoma Multiforme: A Prospective Randomized Clinical Trial. J Clin Oncol 2004;22:1583-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2004.06.082

CNS.methods: Noninferiority ➜ 25 Gy/5 fx vs. ➜ 40 Gy/15 fx
CNS.results: -Median OS 7.9 mos vs. 6.4 mos (NS) -Median PFS 4.2 mos vs. 4.2 mos -QOL not different
CNS.interpretation: One week hypofx RT is noninferior to 15 fx in elderly or poor KPS GB.
CNS.commentary: GTV + 2.0 CTV + PTV Dose constraints: optics, retina, brainstem Max <40 Gy BED
CNS.references: 1. Roa W, Kepka L, Kumar N, et al. International Atomic Energy Agency Randomized Phase III Study of Radiation Therapy in Elderly and/or Frail Patients With Newly Diagnosed Glioblastoma Multiforme. J Clin Oncol 2015;33:4145-50. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.62.6606 Protocol pdf [radiation planning p7]: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/jco.2015.62.6606/suppl_file/Protocol_2015.626606.pdf#page=7

CNS.patients: age ≥70 with AA or GB and KPS ≥70
CNS.methods: Superiority ➜ Supportive care with RT 50 Gy/28 fx vs. ➜ supportive care without RT
CNS.results: Median OS 7.3 mos RT vs. 4.3 mos No diff in QOL or cognition
CNS.interpretation: RT improves OS compared to supportive care only in elderly or poor KPS GB.
CNS.references: 1. Keime-Guibert F, Chinot O, Taillandier L, et al. Radiotherapy for Glioblastoma in the Elderly. N Engl J Med 2007;356:1527-35. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa065901

CNS.patients: Recurrent glioblastoma Eligible: up to 3rd relapse, KPS ≥60, size ≤6 cm, and multifocal recurrences of total size ≤6 cm. In and out of field recurrences permitted Any interval after RT allowed. <6 months permitted if out of field, or biopsy proven in field, or conclusive on imaging
CNS.methods: Phase II: ➜ Bev q2 weeks vs. ➜ 🏆 35 Gy/10 fx with concurrent bev q2 weeks 3DCRT, IMRT (86%), and protons allowed
CNS.results: Median PFS 3.8 vs. 7.1 mos 6-mo PFS 29% vs. 54% CR/PR by RANO criteria 18% vs. 29% Median OS ~10 mos both arms 13% in bev arm received salvage RT On subanalysis, there was OS improvement with RT in KPS score 90-100, 1st vs. 2-3rd relapses, and methylated MGMT. There was trend to improvement in age <50 (p=0.65) •no difference in adverse effects •RT plans per protocol in only 61%
CNS.interpretation: Hypofractionated RT in recurrent GBM with bev improves PFS. There is no added adverse effects. On subanalysis, there was OS improvement with RT in KPS score 90-100, 1st vs. 2-3rd relapse, and methylated MGMT.
CNS.commentary: A primary endpoint of OS was too ambitious, but this trial should be considered positive based on its improvement in PFS. On subanalysis there was OS improvement with RT in KPS score 90-100, 1st vs. 2-3rd relapses, and methylated MGMT. Imaging assessment prior to 6 weeks was recommended against for pseudoprogression. No therapies at this time have shown OS benefit in recurrent GBM. There is no OS benefit with bev in the phase III EORTC 26101, but it did benefit PFS. Drugs are often FDA approved based on phase II trials or for showing improvement in PFS. If PFS was the primary endpoint, this tr…
CNS.references: 1. Tsien CI, Pugh SL, Dicker AP, et al. NRG Oncology/RTOG1205: A Randomized Phase II Trial of Concurrent Bevacizumab and Reirradiation Versus Bevacizumab Alone as Treatment for Recurrent Glioblastoma [published online ahead of print, 2022 Oct 19]. J Clin Oncol 2022;JCO2200164. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.00164 Protocol pdf [dose constraints p25]: https://classic.clinicaltrials.gov/ProvidedDocs/50/NCT01730950/Prot_SAP_000.pdf#page=25

CNS.patients: Recurrent glioblastoma or anaplastic astrocytoma, size ≤40cc
CNS.methods: Phase I dose escalation study. Bev given q2 weeks. RT starts after two bev doses. RT dosing starts at 9 Gy x 3 fx, given every other day
CNS.results: 11 Gy x 3 fx was maximum tolerated dose ITT median OS 13 mos •No symptomatic RT necrosis observed •Median time from initial diagnosis to enrollment 14.3 mos
CNS.interpretation: 11 Gy x 3 fx was the maximum tolerated dose for single site small size recurrent GBM. Phase II/III trials are warranted.
CNS.commentary: SRT delivered to GTV plus 2-5 mm PTV margin
CNS.references: 1. Clarke J, Neil E, Terziev R, et al. Multicenter, Phase 1, Dose Escalation Study of Hypofractionated Stereotactic Radiation Therapy With Bevacizumab for Recurrent Glioblastoma and Anaplastic Astrocytoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2017;99:797-804. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2017.06.2466

CNS.citation: Stupp et al, Eur J Cancer, 2012
CNS.methods: ➜ Tumor treating fields (20-24hr/day) vs. ➜ physician's choice chemotherarpy Any prior therapy allowed
CNS.results: Median PFS 2.2 mo vs. 2.1 mo, p=0.13 6-mo PFS 21% vs. 15% Responses 14% vs. 9.6%, p=0.19 3% CR & 9% PR with TTF, 0 CR & 6% PR chemo Median OS 6.6 mo vs. 6.0 mo, p=0.27 1-yr OS 20% vs. 20% 2-yr OS 8% vs. 4% OS wearing ≥18 hrs vs. <18 hrs, 7.7 mos vs. 4.5 AE: mild skin rash in 14%, moderate skin rash 2%. QOL favored TTF in most domains
CNS.interpretation: PFS with TTF, without chemo, is maintained compared chemo and seems to trend in favor of TTF. adverse effects and QOL are better with TTF. OS is not different.
CNS.commentary: Mean number of prior treatments was 2, range 1-6.
CNS.references: 1. Stupp R, Wong ET, Kanner AA, et al. NovoTTF-100A versus physician’s choice chemotherapy in recurrent glioblastoma: A randomised phase III trial of a novel treatment modality. Eur J Cancer 2012;48:2192-202. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejca.2012.04.011

CNS.methods: ➜ lomustine plus bevacizumab vs. ➜ lomustine alone
CNS.results: No OS benefit with bevacizumab Median OS 9.1 mos vs. 8.6 mos (NS) PFS 4.2 mos vs. 1.5 mos (SS) No difference in dexamethasone use Grade 3-5 adverse events 64% vs. 38%
CNS.interpretation: Adding bevazicumab to lomustine improves PFS but gives no benefit in OS.
CNS.commentary: This is the first phase III trial evaluating bevacizumab in recurrent GB; others were phase II. Previously, bevacizumab was used in recurrent glioblastoma based on historical data claiming that it led to a survival benefit. This trial challenges those assumptions. There may be a role for bevacizumab for PFS or symptom control. RT and TTF both also show benefit in PFS, but not OS.
CNS.references: 1. Wick W, Gorlia T, Bendszus M, et al. Lomustine and Bevacizumab in Progressive Glioblastoma. N Engl J Med 2017;377:1954-63. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1707358

CNS.citation: van den Bent et al, Lancet Oncol, 2021 van den Bent et al, Lancet Oncol, 2026
CNS.methods: ➜ RT alone 59.4 Gy vs. ➜ RT with conc TMZ vs. ➜ 🏆 RT with adj TMZ vs. ➜ RT with conc & adj TMZ Adjuvant TMZ was for 12 mos
CNS.results: •IDH mutant, adj TMZ vs. RT alone Median OS 12.5 vs. 6.0 yrs, SS •IDH mutant, conc TMZ vs. RT alone Median OS 9.7 vs 7.2 yrs, NS •IDH wt, adj or conc TMZ No benefit in OS, median OS ~20 mos
CNS.interpretation: There is OS benefit with adjuvant TMZ in IDHmutant. At final analysis there is no benefit to concurrent TMZ with adjuvant TMZ. TMZ in any sequence did not benefit IDHwt tumors.
CNS.commentary: Many of the IDHwt tumors would have now been diagnosed as glioblastoma. Per WHO 2021 IDHwt is indicative of either diffuse astrocytoma or glioblastoma. Glioblastoma is defined as IDHwt tumor with any of EGFR, TERT, gain of chromosome 7, or loss of chromosome 10. The findings here regarding effectiveness of chemo in IDH are similar to that of RTOG 9802. Although there was no benefit to TMZ in IDHwt in CATNON, the subanalysis may be underpowered. A meta-analysis of IDHwt tumors in six phase II/III trials of low grade gliomas showed that alkylating chemotherapy improved PFS and not OS (Kinslow 20…

CNS.topic: Grade III glioma with 1p19q codel and high risk grade II
CNS.patients: grade III with 1p19q codel glioma and grade II oligo with age≥40 or subtotal resection
CNS.methods: ➜ TMZ alone vs. ➜ RT 59.4 Gy then PCV vs. ➜ RT + conc and adj TMZ for 6-12 cycles
CNS.results: 7.5-yr progression 83% TMZ alone vs. 38% in arms with RT -Death from progression 25% vs. 17% -PFS results maintained with IDH -As a result the TMZ alone arm closed -RT+PCV vs. RT+TMZ results pending

CNS.subtopic: Addition of neoadjuvant PCV to RT
CNS.citation: Cairncross et al, JCO, 2014 Lassman et al, JCO, 2022
CNS.patients: AO and AOA ["AOA" diagnosis is now strongly discouraged since WHO 2016]
CNS.methods: ➜ 🏆 neoadj dose escalated PCV x4 (procarbaine, CCNU, vincristine) → RT 59.4 Gy vs. ➜ RT 59.4 Gy
CNS.results: •Codel: ⬆️ Median OS 13.2 yrs vs. 7.3 yrs 10-yr OS 57% vs. 32% 20-yr OS 37% vs. 15% Median PFS 9.8 vs. 2.9 yrs 10-yr PFS 50% vs. 18% 20-yr PFS 36% vs. 8% •Noncodel IDHmut: ⬆️ Median OS 5.5 vs. 3.3 yrs 10-yr OS 38% vs. 10% 20-yr OS not evaluable vs. 10% Median PFS 2.8 vs. 1.9 yrs 10-yr PFS 28% vs. 7% 20-yr PFS not evaluable vs. 7% •IDH-wt: ↔️ Median OS 1.3-1.8 yrs, not different 10-yr OS 4-6% •Overall: ↔️ Median OS 4.8 yrs, not different Median PFS 2.5 yrs vs. 1.7 yrs
CNS.interpretation: Neoadjuvant PCV results in improved OS and PFS in codeleted 1p19q and in noncodeleted IDHmut AOs.
CNS.commentary: The results displayed here are mainly from the final publication, which classifies the tumors molecularly according to updated WHO criteria (time to forget the old numbers!). The 20 year values are estimated. Many of the IDHwt tumors would have now been diagnosed as glioblastoma. Per WHO 2021, IDHwt is indicative of either diffuse astrocytoma or glioblastoma. Glioblastoma is defined as IDHwt tumor with any of EGFR, TERT, gain of chromosome 7, or loss of chromosome 10. A meta-analysis of IDHwt tumors in six phase II/III trials of low grade gliomas showed that alkylating chemotherapy improved PF…
CNS.notes: WHO Grade III glioma overview: -RTOG 9402 and EORTC show PFS and OS benefit with PCV in 1p19q codel tumors. IDH1 important for prognosis. -On NOA, sequencing of RT and PCV or TMZ may not matter -WHO 2016 classifies these tumors molecularly. Oligoastrocytoma diagnosis stongly discouraged -CATNON shows OS benefit with adjuvant TMZ in intact 1p19q -CODEL to compare TMZ vs. PCV in 1p19q codel

CNS.subtopic: Addition of adjuvant PCV to RT
CNS.patients: AO and AOA ["AOA" diagnosis is now strongly discouraged since WHO 2016]
CNS.methods: ➜ 🏆 RT 59.4 Gy → adj PCV x6 vs. ➜ RT alone (PCV allowed at progression)
CNS.results: •Codel: ⬆️ Median OS 14.2 vs. 9.3 yrs 10-yr OS 57% vs. 43% 20-yr OS 37% vs. 14% Median PFS 13.1 vs. 4.2 yrs 10-yr PFS 57% vs. 24% 20-yr PFS 31% vs. 11% •Noncodel IDHmut: ⬆️ Median OS 8.4 vs. 3.0 yrs 10-yr OS 44% vs. 15% 20-yr OS 11% vs. not evaluable Median PFS 4.2 vs. 1.6 yrs 10-yr PFS 26% vs. 15% 20-yr PFS 13.0 vs. not evaluable •Overall: ⬆️ Median OS 3.5 yrs vs. 2.6 yrs Median PFS 2.0 vs. 1.1 yrs
CNS.interpretation: Adjuvant PCV results in improved OS and PFS in codeleted 1p19q and in noncodeleted IDHmut AOs.
CNS.commentary: The results displayed here are from the final publication, which classifies the tumors molecularly according to updated WHO criteria (time to forget the old numbers!). The 20 year values are estimated. 1p19q codeletion seen in 25%.

CNS.subtopic: Sequencing of post-op chemo and RT
CNS.citation: Wick et al, JCO, 2009 Wick et al, Neuro Oncol 2016
CNS.patients: AA (some AO, AOA), KPS≥70 ["AOA" diagnosis is now strongly discouraged in WHO 2016]
CNS.methods: ➜ 60 Gy (Arm A) vs. ➜ 🏆 PCV/TMZ (Arm B1/B2) Crossover at adverse effects or progression: •Arm A→PCV or tem •Arm B→RT Primary endpoint: TTF
CNS.results: •Median TTF, PFS, and OS were similar for arms A and B1/2 •Median TTF ~4.5 yrs, PFS ~2.6 yrs, OS 8 yrs vs. 6.5 yrs (NS) •IDH1 mutation is strongest prognostic factor, even more than 1p19q codel •methylated-MGMT and oligo histology also associated with better PFS (methylated-MGMT gave same prognosis with chemo and RT)
CNS.interpretation: Sequencing order of RT and chemo does not change outcomes in AA and AO. Molecular diagnosis is superior to histology to convey prognosis. No differential activity of chemo vs. RT in any subgroup.
CNS.commentary: One of few studies to include anaplastic astrocytoma. See also CATNON study (van den Bent 2019). IDH1 mutant incidence 66%, codel in 41% At 54 months follow-up, 78% patients completed salvage RT, and 48% completed salvage chemo.
CNS.references: 1. Wick W, Hartmann C, Engel C, et al. CNS.citation: Mellinghoff et al, NEJM, 2023 Cloughesy et al, Lancet Oncol, 2025
CNS.patients: WHO grade II IDH mutant s/p surgery or biopsy 1-5 yrs prior, no other prior therapy including RT, had measurable nonenhancing tumor (residual or progressive), and appropriate for surveillance
CNS.methods: ➜ 🏆 vorasidenib vs. ➜ none Radiation was not permitted as crossover or at disease progression
CNS.results: Median radiographic PFS 2.3 yrs vs. 0.93 yrs Median PFS NR vs. 11.4 mos Median time to next intervention NR vs. 20.1 mos 2-yr alive and not received next intervention 83% vs. 27% OS not reported Tumor growth -1.3% vs. 15.9% Seizures 18.2 vs. 51.2 per person-year No improvement in neurocognitive function with vorasidenib Serious adverse effects 12% vs. 6%
CNS.interpretation: Vorasidenib improves PFS in grade II IDH mutant glioma. There was no improvement in neurocognitive function. OS is not yet reported.
CNS.commentary: Vorasidenib is a mutant IDH1/2 inhibitor. The drug is an option for those who are opposed to or have contraindications for chemoradiation, but not necessarily preferred. These patients had indications for chemoradiation including subtotal resection, progressive tumor, and many of age >40, but were not permitted to receive it. Reporting is unclear: one publication reported radiographic PFS as 2.3 years, and another reported median PFS as not reached. The radiogaphic PFS here is substantially worse than in RTOG 9802, 12 yrs vs. 2.3. Only median PFS was reported. Those in the placebo arm were all…

CNS.patients: LGG WHO grade II with at least one of: age ≥40, progressive mass on imaging, new or worsening neuro symptoms, intractable seizures
CNS.methods: ➜ 50.4 Gy vs. ➜ dose dense TMZ (75 mg/m2 daily for 3 weeks, for 12 mos)
CNS.results: PFS 46 mos with RT vs. 39 mos TMZ, p=0.22 73% had tissue sufficient for post hoc molecular analysis Astrocytoma Median OS IDHmut/noncodel 6.6-6.7 yrs Oligodendroglioma Median OS IDHmut/codel 12.9 vs 14.9 yrs, p=0.63 IDHwt Median OS IDHwt 2.5 vs 4.7 yrs, p=0.0068 Age over 40 had better prognosis •No differences in HRQOL or global cognitive function between groups
CNS.interpretation: There is no difference in OS between radiation and TMZ. HRQOL does not differ and does not support the use of TMZ over RT. Age over 40 had better prognosis, reinforcing that molecular prognosis matters most.
CNS.commentary: In the TMZ arm 68% had salvage with either radiation or chemotherapy. Criteria used in this study are similar to the so called SATAN criteria from the EORTC Believers' study but modified (Karim 1996). HRQOL was not different and could be further improved with IMRT. 80% had 3DCRT. HRQOL was associated with tumor progression, not radiation treatment volume. Although IDHwt had better OS with TMZ, this subanalysis is likely underpowered. IDHwt tumors would have now been diagnosed as glioblastoma or diffuse astrocytoma per WHO 2021. Glioblastoma is defined as IDHwt tumor with any of EGFR, TERT, gai…

CNS.citation: Fisher et al, IJROBP, 2015 Bell et al, JAMA Oncol, 2018 Fisher et al, IJROBP, 2020 Fleming et al, JCO Precis Oncol, 2021
CNS.patients: low grade glioma with ≥3 risk factors (age ≥40, astrocytoma, crosses midline, size 6 cm, pre-op neuro function status >1)
CNS.methods: Phase II ➜ 54 Gy + conc and adj TMZ for 12 mos
CNS.results: 3-yr OS 74% [vs. historical control 54%] 5-yr OS 61%, 10-yr OS 35% Median OS 8.2 yrs 3/5/10-yr PFS 59%/47%/26% median PFS 4.5 yrs Median OS IDHmut/codel 9.4 yrs Median OS IDHmut/noncodel 8.8 yrs Median OS IDHwt 2.3 yrs MGMT in IDHwt tumors: Median OS IDHwt MGMTmeth 3.8 yrs Median OS IDHwt MGMTunmeth 2.6 yrs 44% grade 3 adverse effects, 10% grade 4, grade 5 in n=1
CNS.interpretation: Adding TMZ to RT results in improved OS compared to historical controls.
CNS.commentary: IDH and 1p19q were much more prognostic than MGMT, except MGMT did give some prognostic value in IDHwt. Many of the IDHwt tumors would have now been diagnosed as glioblastoma. Per WHO 2021, IDHwt is indicative of either diffuse astrocytoma or glioblastoma. Glioblastoma is defined as IDHwt tumor with any of EGFR, TERT, gain of chromosome 7, or loss of chromosome 10. A meta-analysis of IDHwt tumors in six phase II/III trials of low grade gliomas showed that alkylating chemotherapy improved PFS not OS (Kinslow 2024). Phase II. A phase III trial is not planned. On subanalysis, TMZ seemed to work b…

CNS.subtopic: Addition of adjuvant PCV to RT
CNS.citation: Shaw et al, JCO, 2012 Buckner et al, NEJM, 2016 Bell et al, JCO, 2020
CNS.patients: WHO Grade II astrocytoma, oligoastrocytoma, or oligodendroglioma
CNS.methods: High risk (age ≥40 or STR): ➜ 🏆 54 Gy RT → PCV x6 vs. ➜ 54 Gy RT Low risk: obs PCV= procarbazine, CCNU, vincristine
CNS.results: PFS and OS improved with PCV ⬆️ Median OS 13.3 yrs vs. 7.8 ⬆️ Median PFS 10.4 yrs vs. 4.0 ⬆️ 5-yr OS 72% vs. 63%, p=0.13 ⬆️ 10-yr OS 60% vs. 40% ⬆️ 10-yr PFS 51% vs. 21% Median OS by type: ⬆️ IDHmut/codel not reached vs. 13.9 yrs ⬆️ IDHmut/noncodel 11.4 yrs vs. 4.3 ↔️ IDHwt 0.7-1.9 yrs, not different •On subanalysis, benefit only in IDH-mutant (non-codeleted or codeleted). There is no benefit to chemo in IDH-wt
CNS.interpretation: The addition of PCV to radiation improves OS and PFS in high risk low grade glioma. On subanalysis, benefit only in IDH-mutant (non-codeleted or codeleted). There is no benefit to chemo in IDH-wt
CNS.commentary: When taking into account IDH, there was no difference in outcomes with MGMT. Many of the IDHwt tumors would have now been diagnosed as glioblastoma. Per WHO 2021, IDHwt is indicative of either diffuse astrocytoma or glioblastoma. Glioblastoma is defined as IDHwt tumor with any of EGFR, TERT, gain of chromosome 7, or loss of chromosome 10. A meta-analysis of IDHwt tumors in six phase II/III trials of low grade gliomas showed that alkylating chemotherapy improved PFS not OS (Kinslow 2024). The findings here regarding effectiveness of chemo in IDH are similar to that of CATNON. Radiation planning…
CNS.notes: Low grade glioma overview: -EORTC Nonbelievers shows PFS benefit with RT over obs. High risk "SATAN" criteria outlined in Believer's trial -Believers and INT/NCCTG show that 45-50.4 Gy is adequate over higher dose -RTOG 9802. New era. PCV gives large OS benefit in high risk (STR or age≥40), especially for oligo and IDH mutant -RTOG 0424 phase II shows TMZ is also effective. TMZ has never been compared to PCV, but many prefer TMZ for its ease of use -The INDIGO study shows a modest PFS with vorasidenib, but subpar compared to adjuvant radiation
CNS.references: 1. Shaw EG, Wang M, Coons SW, et al. CNS.interpretation: There is no OS benefit to dose escalation in low grade glioma. Necrosis is increased.

CNS.citation: Shaw et al, IJROBP, 2002 van den Bent et al, Lancet, 2005
CNS.methods: ➜ 🏆 54 Gy vs. ➜ obs (RT allowed at recurrence)
CNS.results: 5-yr PFS improved RT 55% vs. 35% 5-yr OS unchanged at 66-68% Median OS ~7.3 yrs, not different 1-yr seizures improved 25% RT vs 41% Median PFS 5.3 yrs vs. 3.4 yrs •Rate of malignant transformation 72% vs. 66% (NS) •No changes in cognitive deficits after treatment •RT helped to control seizures
CNS.interpretation: RT improves PFS but not OS in low grade glioma compared to observation.
CNS.commentary: See RTOG 9802 for specific indications for treatment (Shaw 2012). Should a high risk glioma (age ≥40 or STR) be observed when the addition of chemo to RT leads to such a large survival benefit as in RTOG 9802? 25% were high grade gliomas (balanced between arms)

CNS.patients: High risk per RTOG 0539: Grade II recurrent or STR, or grade III any
CNS.methods: Retrospective comparison of dose escalation to ≥79.2 Gy (equivalent to 66 Gy/ 33 fx) and standard dose Mixed carbon photons were used in some cases
CNS.results: Dose escalated vs. standard 3-yr PFS 86% vs. 56% 5-yr PFS 66% vs. 39% Radiation necrosis 34% vs. 13% RN carbon photon vs. photons only 45% vs. 19% There was higher radiation necrosis with mixed carbon photons. Carbon did not improve local control.
CNS.interpretation: In grade III, grade II recurrent, and grade II STR meningiomas dose escalated radiation resulted in better PFS, but radiation necrosis was increased in this retrospective study. Most of the increase in radiation necrosis was due to mixed carbon photons.
CNS.commentary: Caution in interpretation of these results. Distinguishing between radiation necrosis and local recurrence is difficult without confirmation with biopsy and could have swayed results.
CNS.references: 1. Singh R, Koempel A, French B, et al. Improved Progression-Free Survival Following Dose-Escalated Versus Standard-Dose Postoperative Radiation Therapy for High-Risk Meningiomas: An International Multicenter Individual Patient-Level Meta-Analysis (FIRESTORM). Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2026;125:696-705. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2026.03.036

CNS.citation: Rogers et al, J Neurosurg, 2018 Rogers et al, IJROBP, 2019 Rogers et al, Neuro Oncol, 2023 Kotecha et al, JCO, 2026
CNS.patients: Low risk: Grade I GTR and STR Int risk: Grade I recurrent or grade II GTR High risk: Grade II recurrent or STR, or grade III any
CNS.methods: Phase II ➜ Low risk: obs ➜ Int risk: 54 Gy (with CTV 1.0 cm, 0.5 cm CTV at barriers) ➜ High risk: 54 Gy (2.0 cm CTV) and 60 Gy (1.0 cm CTV) Edema and dural tail are not included in volumes
CNS.results: •Low risk (grade I GTR and STR) GTR 3/5/10 yr LC 98% for all STR 3/5/10 yr LC 83/73/83% GTR 3/5/10-yr PFS 94/94/88% STR 3/5/10-yr PFS 83/73/73% Median PFS, OS not reached 10-yr PFS 85%, 10-yr OS 94% •Intermediate risk (grade II GTR, grade I recurrent) 3-yr PFS 94%, 3-yr LC 96% 5-yr PFS 86% 10-yr PFS 72%, 10-yr OS 85% Median PFS, OS not reached Similar results with grade I and II •High risk (grade II recurrent or STR, grade III) 3-yr PFS 59%, 3-yr LC 69%, 3-yr OS 79% 5-yr PFS 45% 10-yr PFS 43%, 10-yr OS 51% Median PFS not reached Median OS 11 years •recurrent grade II vs. grade II STR 5-yr PFS …
CNS.interpretation: Low risk meningioma: Outcomes with STR were worse than expected, but observation may still be appropriate until RT is required. Intermediate and high risk meningioma: Results support RT for any grade II (GTR, STR, or recurrent), any grade III, and grade I recurrent. Recurrent grade II and grade III have poor outcomes. Clinical trial enrollment is preferred.
CNS.commentary: •Int risk: 54 Gy (with CTV 1.0 cm, 0.5 cm CTV at barriers) •High risk: 54 Gy (2.0 cm CTV) and 60 Gy (1.0 cm CTV) For grade II there is literature to support a CTV up to 2.0 cm (Ehret 2026) as well as as low as 0.5 cm, as recommended by French ANOCEF guidelines (Martz 2023). PET dotatate may be useful also for radiation planning. When treating with 60 Gy, max doses allowed were: Brainstem max <60 Gy Optic chiasm max <56 Gy Optic nerves max <55 Gy Study protocol [dose constraints p24] DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-25-01441
