CNS.methods: Retrospective. Gamma Knife.
CNS.results: 5-yr PFS 95% 10-yr PFS 87% Tumor control higher if image defined vs. resected grade 1, female, single, and skull base
CNS.interpretation: SRS for meningioma results in favorable PFS in long term f/u with low adverse effects.
CNS.commentary: Median dose to margin 14 Gy
CNS.references: 1. Santacroce A, Walier M, Régis J, et al. Long-term Tumor Control of Benign Intracranial Meningiomas After Radiosurgery in a Series of 4565 Patients. Neurosurgery 2011;70:32-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1227/NEU.0b013e31822d408a

CNS.methods: Retrospective. Gamma Knife.
CNS.results: •grade 1 10-yr LC 91%, •image defined only 10-yr LC 95% •crude LC of grade I 93%, image defined 97%, grade II 50%, grade III 17%
CNS.interpretation: SRS results in high LC in low risk meningiomas.
CNS.references: 1. Kondziolka D, Mathieu D, Lunsford LD, et al. RADIOSURGERY AS DEFINITIVE MANAGEMENT OF INTRACRANIAL MENINGIOMAS. Neurosurgery 2008;62:53-60. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1227/01.NEU.0000311061.72626.0D

CNS.patients: Spinal myxopapillary ependymoma mainly grade 1 (3.8% grade 2 were included)
CNS.methods: Retrospective review of MDACC and 11 institutions from Rare Cancer Network
CNS.results: Treatment failure in 32% LF 27%, distal spine relapse 9%, brain failure 6% 10-yr PFS 61% 10-yr PFS ~40% for age <35 and 85% for age> 35 Adjuvant RT improved 10-yr PFS <40% to 70% 10-yr OS 92% Prognostic factors: age < or > 36, surgery alone vs surgery+RT, extent of surgery Substantial adverse effects with RT not seen 2nd malignancy 3.4% (not necessarily due to RT)
CNS.interpretation: Treatment failure in ependymoma occurred in 1/3 and was primarily local. Tumor recurrence is more common with younger age, ommision of RT, or lack of GTR.
CNS.commentary: Results justify RT in STS, piecemeal resection, or questionable GTR. Note that GTR is uncommon in ependymoma, in only about 50%, and that with time tumor progression and dissemination will occur.
CNS.references: 1. Weber DC, Wang Y, Miller R, et al. Long-term outcome of patients with spinal myxopapillary ependymoma: treatment results from the MD Anderson Cancer Center and institutions from the Rare Cancer Network. Neuro Oncol 2014;17:588-95. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/neuonc/nou293

CNS.patients: Unruptured AVM (Characteristics: Spetzler-Martin grade I-III in ~30% each, and grade IV in ~10%)
CNS.methods: ➜ 🏆 medical management vs. ➜ medical management + intervention (can include SRS, embolization, neurosurgery, or combination)
CNS.results: -Mean f/u of 50 mos -Death or stroke 3.39 vs. 12.32 per 100 pt-years -Deaths: n=2 vs. 4 -Adverse events 59% vs. 79% •Neurosurgery in 21%. 95% of these had eradication, 41% had an event •SRS in 54%. 21% had eradication, 37% had an event •Embolization in 62%. 52% had eradication, 50% had an event
CNS.interpretation: Medical management alone results in less death, stroke, and adverse effects compared to interventional therapy (SRS, surgery, or embolization) in unruptured AVM.
CNS.commentary: This is the first randomized trial evaluating intervention in unruptured AVM. Follow-up may be too short - closure can occur later. It should still give some caution toward interventional therapy. SRS, a less invasive option, might possibly be preferred. The trial seems to show that events are fewer with SRS than other modalities. However, the rate of eradication is lower. Recognize that outcome data by modality is non-randomized and non-stratified. Deep and larger tumors would be more likely to undergo SRS. This would favor the surgical and embolization groups. Even so, SRS showed less advers…
CNS.references: 1. Mohr JP, Overbey JR, Hartmann A, et al. Medical management with interventional therapy versus medical management alone for unruptured brain arteriovenous malformations (ARUBA): final follow-up of a multicentre, non-blinded, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Neurol 2020. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1474-4422(20)30181-2

CNS.methods: Retrospective. AVM treated with gamma knife. Rate of hemorrhage assessed pre-surgery, during latency, and after obliteration
CNS.results: Risk of hemorrhage decreased 54% in latency period and 88% after obliteration 5-yr obliteration 91% Risk of reduction greater in those who presented with hemorrhage
CNS.interpretation: The risk of hemorrhage is reduced with GK in AVM. The effect begins before obliteration.
CNS.commentary: Mean dose 21 Gy
CNS.references: 1. Maruyama K, Kawahara N, Shin M, et al. The Risk of Hemorrhage after Radiosurgery for Cerebral Arteriovenous Malformations. N Engl J Med 2005;352:146-53. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa040907

CNS.methods: Systematic review. 65 studies
CNS.results: -Mean freedom from pain 80-87% -(no difference in GK, LINAC, or CK) -Mean time to relief 15-81 days -Max time to relief considered as 180 days -Hypoesthesia ranges 0-17% -Mean recurrence 25-32% -(For recurrence, LINAC was better than GK. No difference between GK and CK) 7-yr pain relief 22-60% 10-yr pain relief 30-45%
CNS.interpretation: SRS for trigmeminal neuralgia leads to very good pain control and is an effective treatment option. Over time efficacy can wane in some and repeat therapy may be beneficial.
CNS.commentary: Selected data is displayed here. See the paper for full details and also guidelines for practice.
CNS.references: 1. Tuleasca C, Régis J, Sahgal A, et al. Stereotactic radiosurgery for trigeminal neuralgia: a systematic review. J Neurosurg 2018;130:733-757. DOI: https://doi.org/10.3171/2017.9.JNS17545

CNS.methods: Phase III ➜ SRS 12 Gy (most commonly) vs. ➜ SRS 18 Gy/3 fx
CNS.results: There was no benefit in hearing peservation with fractionated SRS LC 92% overall
CNS.interpretation: There was no benefit in hearing peservation with fractionated SRS.
CNS.references: 1. Marchetti M, Pinzi V, Gemma M, et al. Hypofractionated Versus Single-Session Radiosurgery to Preserve Hearing in Patients Affected by Sporadic Vestibular Schwannoma: The ACOUNEU Randomized Clinical Trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys. Published online April 11, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2025.03.081

CNS.methods: Prospective review. ➜ Surgery vs. ➜ GKS
CNS.results: •surgery vs. GKS •CN VII disturbance: 37% vs. 0% •V disturbancs: 29% vs. 4% •Hearing preserved: 37.5% vs. 70% •Hospital stay: 23 days vs. 3 days
CNS.references: 1. Régis J, Pellet W, Delsanti C, et al. Functional outcome after gamma knife surgery or microsurgery for vestibular schwannomas. J Neurosurg 2002;97:1091. DOI: https://doi.org/10.3171/jns.2002.97.5.1091

CNS.citation: Tsao et al, J Radiosurg SBRT, 2017
CNS.methods: Meta-analysis of studies
CNS.results: 5-yr tumor control similar between GK SRS, linac SRS, and FSRT to 81-100% isodose line GK SRS 12-14 Gy •5-yr tumor control of 90-99%, hearing preservation 41-79%, CN VII preservation 95-100%, CN V preservation 79-99% SRS vs. FSRT (non randomized) •No difference in tumor control, CN VII, CN V, hearing preservation 9 QOL studies had conflicting results
CNS.interpretation: Outcomes with GK and FSRT in acoustic neuroma are outlined. There are no differences in LC or adverse effects. No randomized trials exist.
CNS.references: 1. Tsao MN, Sahgal A, Xu W, et al. Stereotactic radiosurgery for vestibular schwannoma: International Stereotactic Radiosurgery Society (ISRS) Practice Guideline. J Radiosurg SBRT 2017;5:5-24. PMCID: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5675503/

CNS.methods: Retrospective review. ➜ SRS 12 Gy vs. ➜ FSRT 50 Gy/ 25 fx
CNS.results: •Tumor control 98% SRS vs. 97% FSRT •CNV preservation 95% vs. 93% •CNVII preservation 98% vs. 98% •Hearing preservation 33% vs. 81%
CNS.interpretation: FSRT and SRS in acoustic neuroma both lead to excellent LC and low adverse effects. FSRT may result in better hearing preservation.
CNS.references: 1. Andrews DW, Suarez O, Goldman HW, et al. Stereotactic radiosurgery and fractionated stereotactic radiotherapy for the treatment of acoustic schwannomas: comparative observations of 125 patients treated at one institution. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2001;50:1265-78. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/s0360-3016(01)01559-0

CNS.patients: Uveal melanoma, selected T2 tumors (medium); 2.5-10mm ht, <16mm diameter
CNS.methods: ➜ I-125 brachy 85 Gy vs. ➜ enucleation
CNS.results: •12-yr all-cause mortality 41-43%, not different •LF <10% •But, 3-yr blindness 43%
CNS.interpretation: There is no OS difference between brachy vs. enucleation in T2 (medium) uveal melanoma.
CNS.references: 1. The COMS Randomized Trial of Iodine 125 Brachytherapy for Choroidal Melanoma: V. Twelve-Year Mortality Rates and Prognostic Factors: COMS Report No. 28. JAMA Ophthalmol 2006;124:1684-93. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/archopht.124.12.1684

Cutaneous.citation: Burmeister et al, Lancet, 2012 Henderson et al, Lancet, 2015
Cutaneous.patients: s/p LN dissection for clinically palpable nodes [SLN detected only not eligible] Must be node positive in ≥1 LN parotid, ≥2 LNs cervical or axilla , ≥3 groin, ECE, ≥3 cm LN cervical, or ≥4 cm LN axilla/groin
Cutaneous.methods: ➜ 48 Gy/20 fx vs. ➜ obs Primary endpoint: LN field relapse as first relapse and late adverse effects Secondary endpoints: adjacent relapse, DM, OS, PFS, time to infield progression
Cutaneous.results: RT improved LN relapse 6-yr LN relapse 21% vs. 36% RFS and OS unchanged, 5-yr OS 35% 22% with RT had grade 3-4 adverse effects, 20% grade 3 (skin in 10% and subcutaneous tissue in 7%). 7.7% with increased limb volume in RT group
Cutaneous.interpretation: Adjuvant radiation therapy for clinically detected LNs in melanoma improves LN relapse but has no confirmed benefit in RFS or OS. Immunotherapy, clinical trials, or observation are alternative treatment options that may be more ideal.
Cutaneous.commentary: The trial may be underpowered to detect OS benefit, or better eligibility criteria may be needed. A major past concern with RT after ALND was the potential of profound lymphedema, but now ALND is performed less frequently since studies showed no benefit with ALND after SLNB+. Now that there is less risk for lymphedema, a stronger case can perhaps be made for radiation. In the S1801 trial evaluating neoadjuvant pembrolizumab, pCR was only 21%. In the EORTC study evaluating adjuvant immunotherapy, DMFS was still only about 50%, indicating that additional therapy of some kind is warranted. Radiat…
Cutaneous.references: 1. Henderson MA, Burmeister BH, Ainslie J, et al. Adjuvant lymph-node field radiotherapy versus observation only in patients with melanoma at high risk of further lymph-node field relapse after lymphadenectomy (ANZMTG 01.02/TROG 02.01): 6-year follow-up of a phase 3, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Oncol 2015;16:1049-60. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(15)00187-4 2. Burmeister BH, Henderson MA, Ainslie J, et al. Adjuvant radiotherapy versus observation alone for patients at risk of lymph-node field relapse after therapeutic lymphadenectomy for melanoma: a randomised trial. Lancet Oncol 2012;13:589-97. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(12)70138-9
Cutaneous.commentary_refs: 1. Durgham RA, Nassar SI, Gun R, Nguyen SA, Asarkar AA, Nathan CO. The Prognostic Value of the 31-Gene Expression Profile Test in Cutaneous Melanoma: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Cancers (Basel) 2024;16:3714. DOI: https://doi.org/10.3390/cancers16213714

Cutaneous.patients: High-risk melanoma of H&N: 1) size ≥1.5 mm or Clark's level >IV, N0 2) palpable nodes 3) nodal relapse
Cutaneous.methods: Phase II: ➜ 30 Gy/5 fx biweekly 95% dose to 95% volume
Cutaneous.results: 5-yr LRC 88%, 5-yr OS 47%. Late adverse effects in 3 pts: neck fibrosis, mild hearing loss, exposure of EAC cartilage
Cutaneous.interpretation: 5 fraction adjuvant RT led to favorable LRC and low adverse effects.
Cutaneous.commentary: Note the fairly low 5-year OS. This is due to distant disease.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Ang KK, Peters LJ, Weber RS, et al. Postoperative radiotherapy for cutaneous melanoma of the head and neck region. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1994;30:795-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/0360-3016(94)90351-4

Cutaneous.methods: ➜ 32 Gy/ 4 fx (1 fx weekly) vs. ➜ 50 Gy/ 20 fx (5 days per week)
Cutaneous.results: CR 24%, PR 35%. No difference between arms.
Cutaneous.interpretation: The two regimens each showed favorable outcomes with no difference between arms.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Sause WT, Cooper JS, Rush S, et al. Fraction size in external beam radiation therapy in the treatment of melanoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1991;20:429-32. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/0360-3016(91)90053-7

Cutaneous.patients: Melanoma patients, pathologically node+, s/p surgery to regional nodes, Stage IIIA-C, 2003-2011
Cutaneous.methods: NCDB retrospective review. Compared OS in RT to regional nodes vs. no RT. Propensity score matching used.
Cutaneous.results: No difference in OS with regional node RT (HR 1.09, p=0.640)
Cutaneous.interpretation: Adjuvant RT to regional nodes in node positive melanoma did not lead to improvement in OS in this NCDB analysis. Randomized trials are required for confirmation as adjustments for imbalances in database studies cannot remove all confounding variables.
Cutaneous.commentary: NCDB does not provide recurrence data and has limited data on receptor status or RT fields. The eras are arbitrary. Caution using large population observational studies: A review found that the conclusions of these were not better than chance at matching randomized trials, and no factors were found to improve conclusions (Soni 2019).
Cutaneous.references: 1. Danish HH, Patel KR, Switchenko JM, et al. The influence of postoperative lymph node radiation therapy on overall survival of patients with stage III melanoma, a National Cancer Database analysis. Melanoma Res 2016;26:595-603. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/CMR.0000000000000292
Cutaneous.commentary_refs: Soni PD, Hartman HE, Dess RT, et al. Comparison of Population-Based Observational Studies With Randomized Trials in Oncology. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1209-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.01074

Cutaneous.patients: Node+ melanoma s/p LND and RT
Cutaneous.methods: Retrospective review of outcomes
Cutaneous.results: 5-yr rates: DSS 49%, DFS 42%, DMFS 44%, RC 89% 10-yr lymphedema 11% overall, 27% in groin RT recommended for: Most patients: ≥4 LN, LN size ≥3 cm, ECE, recurrence Groin: consider if Groin: 2 high risk features ≥2 high risk features, esp ECE Cervical: smaller of less LNs, ECE
Cutaneous.interpretation: Surgery and adjuvant RT did lead to good regional control, but the risk of DM remains high.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Ballo MT, Ross MI, Cormier JN, et al. Combined-modality therapy for patients with regional nodal metastases from melanoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2006;64:106-13. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2005.06.030

Cutaneous.methods: WLE and nodal observation with dissection for nodal relapse vs. WLE and SLNB with immediate node dissection if nodes detected
Cutaneous.results: No difference in CSS overall Int depth 10-yr DFS 65% vs. 71%, p=0.01 Thick depth 10-yr DFS 41% vs. 51% Int depth 10-yr DMFS and CSS improved
Cutaneous.interpretation: SLN management improves DFS in all melanoma patients. DMFS and CSS are also improved in intermediate thickness.
Cutaneous.commentary: Intermediate depth defined as 1.20-3.50 mm Thick depth >3.50 mm
Cutaneous.references: 1. Morton DL, Thompson JF, Cochran AJ, et al. Final Trial Report of Sentinel-Node Biopsy versus Nodal Observation in Melanoma. N Engl J Med 2014;370:599-609. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1310460

Cutaneous.patients: intermediate thickness melanoma (1.2-3.5 mm) with positive SLN biopsy
Cutaneous.methods: ➜ Completion LND vs. ➜ obs with US Primary endpoint: melanoma specific survival
Cutaneous.results: Per protocol analysis: 3-yr CSS 86%, not different -Subgroup analysis showed no survival benefit in any group including by depth, # nodes, node size, site, ulceration 3-yr DM no difference 3-yr DFS 68% dissection vs. 63%, p=0.05 3-yr node control 92% vs. 77%, SS -Non-sentinal nodes predicted recurrence, HR 1.78 -"Adjuvant therapy" given in 8.1% vs. 6.5% (no details on how many had radiation)
Cutaneous.interpretation: LND improved regional control and provided prognostic information but had no benefit in CSS.
Cutaneous.commentary: Some may assert that longer follow-up is needed. DeCOG has longer f/u (though less patients) (Leiter 2018). MSLT tells who received "adjuvant therapy" but no specifics. More adjuvant therapy in LND arm, which would favor it. Results in this study are parallel to the TROG radiation study (Henderson 2015). Note that in the ipilimumab trial for N+ melanoma, a surivival benefit is seen at 5 years (Eggermont 2016). MSLT-I had 10 year follow-up.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Faries MB, Thompson JF, Cochran AJ, et al. Completion Dissection or Observation for Sentinel-Node Metastasis in Melanoma. N Engl J Med 2017;376:2211-22. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1613210

Cutaneous.methods: ➜ Completion LND vs. ➜ obs Primary endpoint: DMFS, RFS, OS
Cutaneous.results: No difference in DMFS, RFS, OS 5-yr DMFS ~67%, p=0.87 5-yr DMFS was worse with increased tumor load but still did not differ between arms
Cutaneous.interpretation: LND had no benefit in DM or OS.
Cutaneous.commentary: Note no benefit even with higher tumor burdern in the SLN. Some may say that LND does upstage. But it does not do so very meaningfully: all that is needed for adjuvant therapy is the presence of N+ disease.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Leiter UM, Stadler R, Mauch C, et al. Final Analysis of DeCOG-SLT Trial: No Survival Benefit for Complete Lymph Node Dissection in Patients With Melanoma With Positive Sentinel Node. J Clin Oncol 2019. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.02306

Cutaneous.subtopic: Ipilimumab plus nivolumab in brain mets
Cutaneous.methods: Phase II ➜ Ipilimumab plus nivolumab vs. ➜ nivolumab Cohort C (nonrandomized, prior brain-directed therapy, neuro symptoms, or leptomeningeal) →nivolumab
Cutaneous.results: Intracranial ORR 51% vs. 20% vs. 6% 7-yr intracranial PFS 42% vs. 15% vs. 6% 7-yr OS 48% vs. 26% vs. 13%
Cutaneous.interpretation: Ipilimumab plus nivolumab, without radiation, offers some disease control in melanoma brain metastasis.
Cutaneous.commentary: The trial was phase II and did not use radiation. Results here with immunotherapy seem less effective than WBRT and SRS in various clinical trals, though no brain radiation trial has specifically evaluated melanoma. Immunotherapy might be able to be used to safely delay radiation therapy in those without neurologic symptoms. However until a randomized trial is peformed, radiation remains standard of care. (Alliance N0574, EORTC 22952-26001, Patchell 1998, Chang 2009)
Cutaneous.references: 1. Long GV, Atkinson V, Lo SN, et al. Ipilimumab plus nivolumab versus nivolumab alone in patients with melanoma brain metastases (ABC): 7-year follow-up of a multicentre, open-label, randomised, phase 2 study. Lancet Oncol. Published online February 17, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(24)00735-6

Cutaneous.methods: Phase III ➜ 🏆 neoadjuvant ipilimumab plus nivolumab x2 cycles vs. ➜ adjuvant nivolumab x 12 cycles
Cutaneous.results: 12-mos EFS 84% vs. 57% With neoadjuvant: Major path response 59%, 8% PR, 26% non response, 2% progression 12-mos RFS was 95% in major path response, 76% in PR, 57% in nonresponse 4% in the neoadjuvant group did not undergo surgery
Cutaneous.interpretation: Neoadjuvant ipilimumab plus nivolumab improves EFS compared to adjuvant only nivolumab.
Cutaneous.commentary: It's unclear why ipilimumab was not also included in the adjuvant arm. However in phase II SWOG 1801 the same drug and number of cycles was used in each arm with pembrolizumab, and neoadjuvant showed improved EFS.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Blank CU, Lucas MW, Scolyer RA, et al. Neoadjuvant Nivolumab and Ipilimumab in Resectable Stage III Melanoma. N Engl J Med 2024;391:1696-1708. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2402604

Cutaneous.methods: Phase II randomized ➜ 🏆 neoadjuvant pembrolizumab x3 cycles → surgery → adjuvant pembrolizumab x15 cycles vs. ➜ adjuvant pembrolizumab x 18 cycles
Cutaneous.results: 2-yr EFS 72% vs. 49 21% pCR in neoadjuvant
Cutaneous.interpretation: Neoadjuvant & adjuvant pembrolizumab resulted in improved EFS compared to adjuvant only pembrolizumab.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Patel SP, Othus M, Chen Y, et al. Neoadjuvant-Adjuvant or Adjuvant-Only Pembrolizumab in Advanced Melanoma. N Engl J Med 2023;388:813-823. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2211437

Cutaneous.methods: ➜ 🏆 adjuvant ipilimumab vs. ➜ placebo
Cutaneous.results: 5-yr RFS 40.8% vs 30.3% 5-yr OS 65.4% vs 54.4% 5-yr DMFS 48.3% vs 38.9% Grade 3-4 AE 54.1% vs 26.2% Immune related AE Grade 3-4 41.6% vs 2.7% In ipilimumab group 1.1% died due to immune related AE
Cutaneous.interpretation: Ipilimumab results in improved OS, RFS, and DMFS in resected Stage III melanoma.
Cutaneous.commentary: Note that ipilimumab provides more benefit in Stage III melanoma in RFS than RT did in the TROG study. The TROG study was not powered to detect change in OS with RT (Henderson 2015).
Cutaneous.references: 1. Eggermont AMM, Chiarion-Sileni V, Grob J-J, et al. Prolonged Survival in Stage III Melanoma with Ipilimumab Adjuvant Therapy. N Engl J Med 2016;375:1845-55. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1611299

Cutaneous.citation: Eggermont et al, NEJM, 2018 Eggermont et al, NEJM, 2021
Cutaneous.methods: ➜ 🏆 adjuvant pembrolizumab vs. ➜ placebo
Cutaneous.results: 3.5-yr DMFS 65% vs. 49% 3.5-yr RFS 60% vs. 41% If, PDL1+, 3.5-yr RFS 61% vs. 44% Grade 3-5 adverse effects 15% vs. 3%
Cutaneous.interpretation: Pembrolizumab improved DMFS and RFS in resected Stage III melanoma.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Eggermont AMM, Blank CU, Mandala M, et al. Adjuvant Pembrolizumab versus Placebo in Resected Stage III Melanoma. N Engl J Med 2018;378:1789-801. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1802357 2. Eggermont AMM, Blank CU, Mandalà M, et al. Adjuvant pembrolizumab versus placebo in resected stage III melanoma (EORTC 1325-MG/KEYNOTE-054): distant metastasis-free survival results from a double-blind, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol. 2021;22(5):643-654. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(21)00065-6

Cutaneous.patients: resected Stage IIIB, IIIC, or IV melanoma
Cutaneous.methods: ➜ 🏆 nivolumab vs. ➜ ipilimumab
Cutaneous.results: 1-yr RFS 71% nivo vs. 61% ipi Grade 3-4 adverse events 14% vs. 46% D/c due to adverse effects 10% vs. 43% Deaths due to adverse effects within 100 days: n=0 vs. n=2
Cutaneous.interpretation: Nivolumab gave longer RFS and less grade 3-4 effects than ipilimumab for resected Stage IIIB-IV melanoma.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Weber J, Mandala M, Del Vecchio M, et al. Adjuvant Nivolumab versus Ipilimumab in Resected Stage III or IV Melanoma. N Engl J Med 2017;377:1824-35. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1709030

Cutaneous.citation: Long et al, NEJM, 2017 Dummer et al, NEJM, 2020
Cutaneous.patients: resected Stage III melanoma with BRAF V600E or V600K mutations
Cutaneous.methods: ➜ 🏆 adjuvant dabrafenib + trametinib vs. ➜ placebos
Cutaneous.results: Improved RFS with combo pill 3-yr RFS 58% vs. 38% 5-yr RFS 52% vs. 36% 3-yr OS 86% vs. 77% 5-yr DMFS 65% vs. 54%
Cutaneous.interpretation: Adjuvant dabrafenib plus trametinib improved RFS in BRAF mutated Stage III melanoma.
Cutaneous.commentary: dabrafenib = anti BRAF trametinib = anti MEK
Cutaneous.references: 1. Long GV, Hauschild A, Santinami M, et al. Adjuvant Dabrafenib plus Trametinib in Stage III BRAF-Mutated Melanoma. N Engl J Med 2017;377:1813-23. 2. Dummer R, Hauschild A, Santinami M, et al. Five-Year Analysis of Adjuvant Dabrafenib plus Trametinib in Stage III Melanoma. N Engl J Med 2020;10.1056/NEJMoa2005493. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2005493

Cutaneous.patients: At least one of: •ENE with size ≥2 cm •≥3 nodes •in-transit mets •bone invasion •PNI •locally recurrent with ≥1 risk feature
Cutaneous.methods: resection→ adjuvant RT or chemoRT → ➜🏆 adjuvant cemiplimab vs. ➜ placebo
Cutaneous.results: Median DFS 24 vs. 65 events 2-yr est DFS 87% vs. 64% LRR 9 events vs. 40 DM 10 events vs. 26
Cutaneous.interpretation: Adjuvant cemiplimab improves DFS in high risk post-op SCC after adjuvant RT.
Cutaneous.commentary: Cemiplimab is anti PD-1. This report was the first interim analysis. Because the data crossed the pre-specified efficacy threshhold, it became a primary analysis. All patients received radiation before enrollment, thus there were no specific radiotherapy guidelines in the protocol.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Rischin D, Porceddu S, Day F, et al. Adjuvant Cemiplimab or Placebo in High-Risk Cutaneous Squamous-Cell Carcinoma. N Engl J Med 2025;393:774-785. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2502449

Cutaneous.citation: Avril et al, Br J Cancer, 1997
Cutaneous.methods: Randomized: surgery vs. RT In RT group, 55% had interstitial brachy, 33% contact therapy, and 12% conventional
Cutaneous.results: 4-yr failure 0.7% surgery and 7.5% RT, p=0.003 Good cosmetic results per patient 87% surgery vs 69% RT
Cutaneous.interpretation: In BCC <4 cm, surgery has improved LC compared to radiation. Radiation also shows favorable results and is a reasonable treatment alternative.
Cutaneous.commentary: Perhaps the only large randomized trial of surgery vs. RT in primary non-melanoma skin cancer. Note the excellent LC with RT in BCC.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Avril MF, Auperin A, Margulis A, et al. Basal cell carcinoma of the face: surgery or radiotherapy? Results of a randomized study. Br J Cancer 1997;76:100-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1038/bjc.1997.343

Cutaneous.patients: SCC High risk nodal: ECE, intraparotid node, >2 cervical nodes or size >3 cm High risk primary: T3-T4 (excluding nose, EAC, lip); intransit mets
Cutaneous.methods: ➜ 60-66 Gy RT vs. ➜ RT with concurrent weekly carbo x6
Cutaneous.results: No benefit in LRR, DFS, or OS with carboplatin 5-yr FFLRR ~85%, p=.58 5-yr DFS ~70% 5-yr OS ~78% DM as first failure 7%, not different LRF was most common site of failure
Cutaneous.interpretation: No benefit with adjuvant carboplatin in high risk post-op cutaneous SCC. There is excellent FFLRR in both arms.
Cutaneous.commentary: Carboplatin used, not cisplatin. ~50% had ECE, and 5-10% had positive margin. PNI was not an inclusion criteria. 77% were high risk nodal and 19% were high risk primary, 4% both.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Porceddu SV, Bressel M, Poulsen MG, et al. Postoperative Concurrent Chemoradiotherapy Versus Postoperative Radiotherapy in High-Risk Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Head and Neck: The Randomized Phase III TROG 05.01 Trial. J Clin Oncol 2018;36:1275-83. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2017.77.0941

Cutaneous.methods: Prospective. Univariate and multivariate analysis of prognostic factors
Cutaneous.results: At 43 month follow-up, crude rates: 3% LR Tumor size <2mm had no metastases Sizes 2.1-6.0 mm thick had 4% mets Sizes >6.0 mm had 16% mets On MVA, prognostic factors for metastasis were tumor thickness, immunosupression, ear, horizontal size. LR depended on tumor thickness and desmoplasia
Cutaneous.interpretation: Risk factors for DM are identified and include tumor thickness, immunosupression, ear, horizontal size.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Brantsch KD, Meisner C, Schönfisch B, et al. Analysis of risk factors determining prognosis of cutaneous squamous-cell carcinoma: a prospective study. Lancet Oncol 2008;9:713-20. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(08)70178-5

Cutaneous.trial: Fox Chase Cancer Center, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Cutaneous.methods: Meta-analysis of reports of outcomes of brachytherapy, Mohs, EBRT, and conventional excision
Cutaneous.results: good cosmesis in 98% BT, 96% Mohs (1 study only), 75% EBRT, 81%, CE 1-yr LR 0% BT, 0.2% Mohs, 2% EBRT, 0.8% CE
Cutaneous.interpretation: There is excellent LC and good cosmesis with both BT and Mohs. Selection bias may be present in these studies.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Lee CT, Lehrer EJ, Aphale A, et al. Surgical excision, Mohs micrographic surgery, external-beam radiotherapy, or brachytherapy for indolent skin cancer: An international meta-analysis of 58 studies with 21,000 patients. Cancer 2019;125:3582-3594. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.32371

Cutaneous.citation: Cognetta et al, J Am Acad Dermatol, 2012
Cutaneous.patients: 712 BCC and 994 SCC (861 in situ and 133 invasive SCC)
Cutaneous.methods: Retrospective. Superficial xray
Cutaneous.results: 5-yr control 96% BCC and 94% SCC Predictors of recurrence: size ≥2 cm, male
Cutaneous.interpretation: Superficial RT for BCC/SCC leads to favorable LC.
Cutaneous.commentary: Most of the SCC were in situ
Cutaneous.references: 1. Cognetta AB, Howard BM, Heaton HP, Stoddard ER, Hong HG, Green WH. Superficial x-ray in the treatment of basal and squamous cell carcinomas: A viable option in select patients. J Am Acad Dermatol 2012;67:1235-41. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaad.2012.06.001

Cutaneous.trial: Universidad de Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, Spain
Cutaneous.citation: Hernández-Machin et al, Int J Dermatolol, 2007
Cutaneous.methods: Retrospective. Superficial xray
Cutaneous.results: 5-yr LC 94% BCC and 93% SCC 15-yr cure 85% BCC and 78% SCC Predictors of BCC recurrence: nose and size ≥10 mm
Cutaneous.interpretation: Superficial RT for BCC/SCC leads to favorable LC.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Hernández-Machin B, Borrego L, Gil-García M, Hernández BH. Office-based radiation therapy for cutaneous carcinoma: Evaluation of 710 treatments. Int J Dermatol 2007;46:453-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-4632.2006.03108.x

Cutaneous.methods: Retrospective: Orthovoltage, electrons, MV photons and fractionation at oncologist discretion
Cutaneous.results: 4-yr LRC 86% for BCC and 58% for SCC.
Cutaneous.interpretation: Orthovoltage RT for BCC/SCC leads to favorable LC. BCC have better outcome than SCC.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Kwan W, Wilson D, Moravan V. Radiotherapy for locally advanced basal cell and squamous cell carcinomas of the skin. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2004;60:406-11. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2004.03.006

Cutaneous.methods: Retrospective: Mostly electrons, superficial X-rays, or both. <2% MV photons.
Cutaneous.results: BCC: 92% overall control. SCC: 80% overall control. Larger size, smaller fraction size, recurrent tumors correlated with worse outcomes.
Cutaneous.interpretation: RT for BCC/SCC leads to favorable LC. BCC have better outcome than SCC.
Cutaneous.commentary: Overall 6% complication rate, mostly soft-tissue necrosis.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Locke J, Karimpour S, Young G, Lockett MA, Perez CA. Radiotherapy for epithelial skin cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2001;51:748-55. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/s0360-3016(01)01656-x

Cutaneous.citation: Poulsen et al, JCO, 2003 Poulsen et al, IJROBP, 2006
Cutaneous.patients: Phase II: Non metastatic disease, plus ≥1: recurrence, nodes size >1cm, gross disease, occult primary
Cutaneous.methods: Phase II ➜ 50 Gy/25 fx with carboplatin and etoposide 2nd study compared a historical control of 62 patients with RT and no chemo vs. 40 from trial
Cutaneous.results: 3-yr OS 76%, LRC 75%, DM control 76% On MVA comparison to a historical control without chemo, there was no benefit to chemo, but not powered adequately
Cutaneous.interpretation: Chemothearpy added to RT in high risk merkel cell patients results in high local control But MVA suggests chemo has no effect on survival
Cutaneous.references: 1. Poulsen M, Rischin D, Walpole E, et al. High-Risk Merkel Cell Carcinoma of the Skin Treated With Synchronous Carboplatin/Etoposide and Radiation: A Trans-Tasman Radiation Oncology Group Study—TROG 96:07. J Clin Oncol 2003;21:4371-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2003.03.154 2. Poulsen MG, Rischin D, Porter I, et al. Does chemotherapy improve survival in high-risk stage I and II Merkel cell carcinoma of the skin? Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2006;64:114-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2005.04.042

Cutaneous.trial: Nova Southeastern Univerity, Fort Lauderdale, FL
Cutaneous.patients: Merkel cell s/p surgery then obs, RT, or CRT
Cutaneous.methods: Meta-analysis of cases evaluating outcomes of obs, RT, and CRT
Cutaneous.results: obs vs. RT vs. CRT 3-yr LC 20% vs. 65% vs. 67% 3-yr OS 56% vs. 70% vs. 73% recurrence 38% obs vs. 23% post-op RT No difference with any parameters assessed
Cutaneous.interpretation: This case meta-analysis suggests a benefit in OS and LC with RT. Chemotherapy added to RT did not seem to improve results.
Cutaneous.references: 1. Hasan S, Liu L, Triplet J, Mansur D. The Role of Postoperative Radiation and Chemoradiation in Merkel Cell Carcinoma: A Systematic Review of the Literature. Front Oncol 2013;3. DOI: https://doi.org/10.3389/fonc.2013.00276

Cutaneous.citation: Chen et al, JAMA Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg, 2015
Cutaneous.methods: Retrospective analysis of NCDB patients to evaluate outcomes with chemo and RT
Cutaneous.results: CRT and RT provided OS benefit Adjuvant CRT was associated with OS benefit over RT in positive margins, tumor size ≥3 cm, males
Cutaneous.interpretation: Chemo added to RT may improve OS in high risk patients, such as in positive margins, tumor size ≥3 cm, and males. Randomized trials are required for confirmation as adjustments for imbalances in database studies cannot remove all confounding variables.
Cutaneous.commentary: NCDB does not provide recurrence data. The eras are hand selected. Caution using large population observational studies: A review found that the conclusions of these were not better than chance at matching randomized trials, and no factors were found to improve conclusions (Soni et al, JCO, 2019).
Cutaneous.references: 1. Chen MM, Roman SA, Sosa JA, Judson BL. The Role of Adjuvant Therapy in the Management of Head and Neck Merkel Cell Carcinoma: An Analysis of 4815 Patients. JAMA Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2015;141:137-41. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoto.2014.3052
Cutaneous.commentary_refs: Soni PD, Hartman HE, Dess RT, et al. Comparison of Population-Based Observational Studies With Randomized Trials in Oncology. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1209-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.01074

GI.citation: James et al, Lancet, 2013 Glynne-Jones et al, Ann Oncol, 2014
GI.patients: anal squamous cell carcinoma, all stages
GI.methods: Superiority. 50.4 Gy RT with 5FU with concurrent ➜ 🏆 MMC (1 cycle) vs. ➜ cisplatin (60 mg/m2 x2) ➜ maintenance 5FU/cis vs. ➜ 🏆 no maintenance
GI.results: No difference in CR, CFS, or PFS. CR is best at week 26 3-yr CR 90%, CFS 72-74%, PFS 74%. Grade 3/4 effects increased in heme, no changes in others: Heme 26% vs. 16%, skin 48%, pain 27%, GI 17%. Only 1.7% needed colostomy due to late effects.
GI.interpretation: There is no difference in efficacy between MMC and cisplatin. Heme adverse effects is slightly increased with MMC. Thus due to its ease of use, MMC remains standard of care.
GI.commentary: MMC is more convenient to adminster than cisplatin. MMC is a 10 minute injection. Cisplatin is given over hours with hydration and with risk of renal and oto adverse effects. American trials use two cycles of MMC, while European trials and open protocols use one cycle. No trial has randomized one vs two cycles. RT: 30.6 Gy with boost to 50.4 Gy total
GI.references: 1. James RD, Glynne-Jones R, Meadows HM, et al. Mitomycin or cisplatin chemoradiation with or without maintenance chemotherapy for treatment of squamous-cell carcinoma of the anus (ACT II): a randomised, phase 3, open-label, 2x2 factorial trial. Lancet Oncol 2013;14:516-24. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(13)70086-X 2. Glynne-Jones R, Kadalayil L, Meadows HM, et al. Tumour- and treatment-related colostomy rates following mitomycin C or cisplatin chemoradiation with or without maintenance chemotherapy in squamous cell carcinoma of the anus in the ACT II trial. Ann Oncol 2014;25:1616-1622. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/annonc/mdu188

GI.citation: Gilbert et al, Lancet Oncol, 2025 Hawkins et al, GI ASCO, 2026
GI.patients: •ACT3: T1N0 anal margin •ACT4: T1-2N0 (size <4 cm) •ACT5: T3-4N1-3
GI.methods: • ACT3 T1N0 anal margin ➜ Phase II: local excision then observation 41.4 Gy/23 fx for close ≤1 mm margin • ACT4 T1-2N0 size <4 cm Phase II (2:1) ➜ 41.4/34.5 Gy SIB /23 fx vs. ➜ 50.4/40 Gy SIB /28 fx • ACT5 T3-4N1-3 Phase II/III ➜ 53.2/50.4 nodes (or 53 if > 3 cm)/40.0 Gy /28 fx vs. ➜ 53.2 (boost to 58.5)/53.2 nodes /40.0 vs. ➜ 53.2 (boost to 61.6 Gy)/53.2 nodes/40.0 fx SIB All arms with concurrent MMC x1 cycle and cape/5FU
GI.results: • ACT3 Ongoing study • ACT4 6-mo cCR 92% vs. 87% Grade ≥3 adverse effects 35% vs. 46% Grade ≥4 adverse effects 10% vs. 13% • ACT5 6-mo cCR 65% vs. 67% vs. 66% Primary endpoint: LRF Secondary endpoints: DFS, OS, CFS, CR, adverse effects QOL
GI.interpretation: • ACT4 In T1-2N0 size <4 cm, reduced dose IMRT to 41.4 Gy had excellent CR. LRC results are awaited. • ACT5 Dose escalation did not improve CR.
GI.commentary: There has never been a confirmatory trial to prove a benefit of dose escalation in anal cancer over the Nigro regimen of 30 Gy in 10-15 fractions (1981). Trials are ongoing (PLATO ACT IV, DECREASE). A lower dose of radiation and chemotherapy could profoundly reduce skin and heme adverse effects and improve treatment tolerance. RTOG uses two cycles of MMC, but most all other trials only use one cycle, reducing adverse effects. Radiation planning CTVp = 1 cm, covering anal canal, anal verge, internal and external anal sphincters CTVn incluces internal and external iliac, mesorectal, and inguinal…
GI.references: 1. Gilbert A, Adams R, Webster J, et al. Standard versus reduced-dose chemoradiotherapy in anal cancer (PLATO-ACT4): short-term results of a phase 2 randomised controlled trial. Lancet Oncol. Published online May 2, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(25)00213-X Supplement with radiation planning instructions: https://www.thelancet.com/cms/10.1016/S1470-2045(25)00213-X/attachment/7d244b71-8d42-4a33-a080-ee39a51c195c/mmc1.pdf 2. GI.results: Ongoing study Primary endpoints: DFS, QOL Secondary endpoints: QOL, LC, LRC, DM, in and out of field recurrences, eContour
GI.commentary: There has never been a confirmatory trial to confirm a benefit of dose escalation in anal cancer over the Nigro regimen of 30 Gy in 10-15 fractions (1981). Trials are ongoing (PLATO ACT IV, DECREASE). A lower dose of radiation and chemotherapy could profoundly reduce skin and heme adverse effects and improve treatment tolerance.
GI.references: 1. ECOG-ACRIN Cancer Research Group. Lower-Dose Chemoradiation in Treating Patients With Early-Stage Anal Cancer, the DECREASE Study. Available at: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04166318 Accessed May 22, 2023. NLM identifier: NCT04166318.

GI.citation: Kachnic et al, IJROBP, 2013 Olsen et al, IJROBP, 2017 Kachnic et al, IJROBP, 2021
GI.methods: Phase II ➜ 🏆 50-54 Gy IMRT + MMC x2 / 5FU Compared to historical control.
GI.results: IMRT improved skin grade 3+, heme grade 2+, and GI grade 3+ adverse effects over historical control. Less tx breaks with IMRT: 49% vs 61% in 9811 RTOG 0529 vs. 9811: grade 2+ heme: 73% vs. 85% grade 3+ skin 23% vs. 49% grade 3+ GI: 21% vs. 36% Late any adverse effects grade 2 27%, grade 3 16%, grade 4 none, grade 5 4% 5-yr LRF 16%, CF 10%, DM 16%, OS 76%, DFS 70%, CFS 74% 8-yr LRF 16%, CF 12%, DM 22%, OS 68%, DFS 62%, CFS 66%
GI.interpretation: IMRT improves skin and GI grade 3, and heme grade 2 adverse effects compared to historical control. Efficacy is also comparable to historical controls.
GI.commentary: At the time of its publication, the trial was considered conclusive, and a phase III was not designed. 3DCRT planning for anal cancer was tedious, which certainly generated enthusiasm for the transition. Today expectations are that randomized trials are performed. In this case, results have been reproduced in phase III gyn trials (TIME C, PARCER, INTERTECC). The other view is the "just so" perspective, that IMRT is better just because it is, and this can be seen plainly on the DVH, and a randomized trial isn't always needed. There was never a trial to confirm a benefit of dose escalation in an…
GI.references: 1. Kachnic LA, Winter K, Myerson RJ, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2021;S0360-3016(21)02647-X. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2021.08.008

GI.methods: ➜ induction cisplatin/5FU x2 vs. ➜ no induction → chemoRT 45 Gy with conc cis x2/5FU → ➜ 15 Gy boost vs. ➜ 20-25 Gy boost with EBRT or brachy
GI.results: Unchanged 5-yr CFS with induction chemo (~75%) Unchanged 5-yr CFS with boost, 74% with 15 Gy vs. 78%, p=0.065 Overall, the 5-yr CFS was highest with induction chemo plus high dose boost of 82% (though this did not remain significant on arm comparisons)
GI.interpretation: There is no improvement in CFS with induction chemo or escalated boost radiation dose. However the high CFS with induction chemo and high dose seems favorable and warrants additional study.
GI.references: 1. Peiffert D, Tournier-Rangeard L, Gérard J-P, et al. Induction Chemotherapy and Dose Intensification of the Radiation Boost in Locally Advanced Anal Canal Carcinoma: Final Analysis of the Randomized UNICANCER ACCORD 03 Trial. J Clin Oncol 2012;30:1941-8. https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2011.35.4837

GI.citation: Ajani et al, JAMA, 2008 Gunderson et al, JCO, 2012
GI.methods: Equivalency. ➜ 🏆 MMC x2 + 5FU + RT 45-59 Gy vs. ➜ neoadj 5FU/cisplatin (75 mg/m2 two cycles) → RT 45-59 Gy concurrent cis/5FU
GI.results: 5-yr colostomy rate 12% vs. 17%, p=0.074 Trend for improved CFS (72% vs. 65%, p=0.05) and LRF 5-yr DFS 68% vs. 58% (SS) 5-yr OS 78% vs. 71% (SS)
GI.interpretation: MMC improves OS, DFS, and colostomy rate compared to sequential cis/5FU.
GI.commentary: The initial report showed no difference in 5-yr OS and DFS, but the final report showed improved 5-yr OS and DFS with MMC due to more follow-up and power. This was designed to be an equivalency trial, but MMC significantly outperformed neoadjuvant and concurrent cisplatin. Note that in ACT II, there was no difference. Does this mean that the neoadjuvant chemo impaired OS in 9811? MMC 10mg/m2 days 1 and 29, and 5FU 1000 mg/m2 given on days 1 and 29 for four days each. 5-yr LRF by stage: T2N0 17%, T3N0 18%, T4N0 37% T2N1-3 26%, T3N1-3 44%, T4N1-3 60%
GI.references: 1. Ajani JA, Winter KA, Gunderson LL, et al. Fluorouracil, Mitomycin, and Radiotherapy vs Fluorouracil, Cisplatin, and Radiotherapy for Carcinoma of the Anal Canal: A Randomized Controlled Trial. JAMA 2008;299:1914-21. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.299.16.1914 2. Gunderson LL, Winter KA, Ajani JA, et al. Long-term update of US GI intergroup RTOG 98-11 phase III trial for anal carcinoma: survival, relapse, and colostomy failure with concurrent chemoradiation involving fluorouracil/mitomycin versus fluorouracil/cisplatin. J Clin Oncol 2012;30:4344-51. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2012.43.8085

GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 RT concurrent 5FU/MMC x1 vs. ➜ RT alone RT 45 Gy/1.8 with 15-20 Gy boost after 6 week break. Surgery for residual disease
GI.results: CR 80% vs. 54% 5-yr LC 68% vs. 50% colostomy-free survival 72% vs. 40% PFS also improved No diff in OS (65% vs. 72%). No significant diff in severe side effects.
GI.interpretation: The addition of concurrent 5FU/MMC to RT improves outcomes and should be standard of care.
GI.references: 1. Bartelink H, Roelofsen F, Eschwege F, et al. Concomitant radiotherapy and chemotherapy is superior to radiotherapy alone in the treatment of locally advanced anal cancer: results of a phase III randomized trial of the European Organization for Research and Treatment of Cancer Radiotherapy and Gastrointestinal Cooperative Groups. J Clin Oncol 1997;15:2040-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1997.15.5.2040

GI.citation: UKCCCR, Lancet, 1996 Northover et al, J of Cancer, 2010
GI.patients: Anal canal or anal margin SCC, incl. metastatic. Excl. T1N0, prior tx
GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 RT concurrent 5FU/MMC x1 vs. ➜ RT alone RT 45 Gy/20-25 fx. 6 weeks after RT, boost given if >50% response with 15 Gy photons or 25 Gy brachy. RT given AP/PA to anus and inguinal LN
GI.results: 3-yr LC 66% CMT vs. 41%. Risk of anal CA-related death improved though no OS advantage. CFS also improved. 12-yr LRC 66% vs. 42% 12-yr CFS 30% vs. 20% 12-yr OS 33% vs. 27% (NS) In first 5 years of CRT, 9.1% increase in non-anal cancer deaths, disappearing in 10 years
GI.interpretation: The addition of concurrent 5FU/MMC to RT improves outcomes and should be standard of care.
GI.references: 1. Epidermoid anal cancer: results from the UKCCCR randomised trial of radiotherapy alone versus radiotherapy, 5-fluorouracil, and mitomycin. Lancet 1996;348:1049-54. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1038/sj.bjc.6605605

GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 RT concurrent 5FU/MMC x2 vs. ➜ RT concurrent 5FU RT 45-50.4 Gy. Additional 9 Gy, 5-FU, and cisplatin for residual tumor on posttreatment bx
GI.results: CR 92% vs. 85% 4-yr LC 84% vs. 66% 4-yr colostomy 9% vs. 22% DFS 73% vs. 51% No OS difference (76% vs. 67%). Greater adverse effects in MMC arm.
GI.interpretation: The addition of RT and MMC to 5FU improves outcomes and should be standard of care.
GI.commentary: There has never been a confirmatory trial to confirm a benefit of dose escalation in anal cancer over the Nigro regimen of 30 Gy in 10-15 fractions (1981). Trials are ongoing (PLATO ACT IV, DECREASE). A lower dose of radiation and chemotherapy could profoundly reduce skin and heme adverse effects and improve treatment tolerance.

GI.subtopic: Avoidance of RT in good responders
GI.citation: Schrag et al, NEJM, 2023 Basch et al, JCO, 2023
GI.patients: Rectal T2N1, T3N0, T3N1, >5 cm from anal verge, >3 mm radial margin, who are candidates for LAR
GI.methods: Noninferioriy ➜ Standard: pre-op long course chemoRT → TME → suggested FOLFOX x8 vs. ➜ 🏆 Selective arm: FOLFOX x6 → response assessment with MRI → if response ≥20%, omit RT → TME → suggested FOLFOX x6 if response <20% or <5 cycles completed → long course chemoRT → TME → FOLFOX x2 •post-op chemoRT given for +margin
GI.results: 93% in FOLFOX arm had ≥20% response ✅ 5-yr DFS 81% vs. 79%, noninferior 5-yr LR 1.8% vs. 1.6%, not different pCR 24% vs. 22% R0 97% vs. 99% 5-yr OS 90% both arms In the FOLFOX selective arm, 9% had pre-op RT and 1% had post-op RT 📌Adverse effects During neoadjuvant: grade ≥3 adverse effects 23% vs. 41% (neuropathy worse with FOLFOX, 17%, vs. 83%) During any adjuvant treatment: Grade ≥3 adverse effects 26% vs. 33% 📌QOL •During neoadjuvant treatment: chemoRT: worse diarrhea and bowel function FOLFOX: worse anxiety, appetite loss, constipation, depression, dysphagia, dyspnea, edema, fatigue, muco…
GI.interpretation: The two approaches are noninferior in those that meet PROSPECT eligibility criteria.
GI.commentary: Overview. The trial supports chemotherapy alone in those that meet PROSPECT eligibility criteria. LR rates were very low in both arms. ChemoRT is still an option for some circumstances. Nearly double the amount of chemotherapy was permitted in the selective arm. Radiation may be useful for those who wish to undergo less chemotherapy. According to the authors however, similar amounts of chemotherapy were actually given, though this is yet to be published (Schrag D, email communication, Sept 2024). Adverse effects. Neoadjuvant QOL was reported as worse overall with FOLFOX. ChemoRT QOL showed wor…

GI.citation: Swedish Rectal Cancer Trial, NEJM, 1997 Folkesson et al, JCO, 2005
GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 pre-op 25 Gy/5fx (no chemo) then surgery vs. ➜ surgery alone
GI.results: OS benefit with pre-op RT 5-yr LR 11% vs. 27% 5-yr OS 58% vs. 48%. 13-yr OS 38% vs 30% 13-yr LR 9% vs 27% 12-yr CSS 72% vs 62%
GI.interpretation: Pre-op RT reduces LR and improves OS.
GI.commentary: Only trial to show survival benefit with RT. Note that blunt resection was used in this study, not TME. TME is believed to have negated the survival benefit seen here in later studies (Kapitleijn 2001).
GI.references: 1. Improved Survival with Preoperative Radiotherapy in Resectable Rectal Cancer. N Engl J Med 1997;336:980-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199704033361402 2. Folkesson J, Birgisson H, Pahlman L, Cedermark B, Glimelius B, Gunnarsson U. Swedish Rectal Cancer Trial: Long Lasting Benefits From Radiotherapy on Survival and Local Recurrence Rate. J Clin Oncol 2005;23:5644-50. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2005.08.144

GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 pre-op chemo RT vs. ➜ post-op chemoRT 5FU LV and 45 Gy RT plus 5.4 Gy boost
GI.results: pre-op improved DFS 5-yr DFS 65% pre-op vs 53% 5-yr LC 11% vs 11% (NS) 5-yr OS 75% vs 66%, p=.065 pCR 15%
GI.interpretation: Pre-op chemoRT improves DFS and has trend to improved OS over post-op treatment.
GI.references: 1. Roh MS, Colangelo LH, O'Connell MJ, et al. Preoperative multimodality therapy improves disease-free survival in patients with carcinoma of the rectum: NSABP R-03. J Clin Oncol 2009;27:5124-30. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2009.22.0467

GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 pre-op 25Gy/5 fx vs. ➜ post-op 45 Gy/25 fx + 5-FU for +margin only (12%)
GI.results: 3-yr RR of LR 4% vs. 11% 3-yr DFS 78% vs. 72% OS not different
GI.interpretation: Pre-op RT results in improved LC and DFS over post-op RT for positive margin.
GI.references: 1. Sebag-Montefiore D, Stephens RJ, Steele R, et al. Preoperative radiotherapy versus selective postoperative chemoradiotherapy in patients with rectal cancer (MRC CR07 and NCIC-CTG C016): a multicentre, randomised trial. Lancet 2009;373:811-20. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(09)60484-0

GI.citation: Sauer et al, NEJM, 2004 Sauer et al, JCO, 2012
GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 pre-op 50.4 Gy with 5-FU vs. ➜ post-op 50.4 Gy+5.4 Gy boost with 5FU
GI.results: 5-yr LR 6% pre-op vs. 13% 1/5 in post op had been overstaged initially (meaning pre-op may overtreat to some extent) Better compliance pre-op pCR 8% conversion from planned APR to sphincter preservation 39% vs. 19% acute grade 3-4 adverse effects 27% vs. 40% late grade 3-4 adverse effects 14% vs 24% 5-yr OS 76% vs. 74% (NS) 10-yr OS 60% both arms 10-yr LR 7% vs. 10% 10-yr DM 30% both arms LR risk by distance from anal verge of those not undergoing RT: 0 to <5, 4.5%, 5 to <10, 18.7%, 10 to 16, 10.4%
GI.interpretation: Pre-op chemoRT improves LC and early and late adverse effects over post-op treatment.
GI.commentary: Modern pCR can be found in NSABP R-04 trial using capecitabine: pCR 21% (O'Connell 2014). Note that long term, the risk of distant metastasis remains elevated.
GI.references: 1. Sauer R, Becker H, Hohenberger W, et al. Preoperative versus Postoperative Chemoradiotherapy for Rectal Cancer. N Engl J Med 2004;351:1731-40. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa040694 2. Sauer R, Liersch T, Merkel S, et al. Preoperative Versus Postoperative Chemoradiotherapy for Locally Advanced Rectal Cancer: Results of the German CAO/ARO/AIO-94 Randomized Phase III Trial After a Median Follow-Up of 11 Years. J Clin Oncol 2012;30:1926-33. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2011.40.1836

GI.patients: Patients treated with pre-opRT, post-op RT, or no RT
GI.methods: Meta-analysis. 22 randomized trials of pre- and post-operative RT or no RT.
GI.results: Trend to OS benefit with RT Overall Death 62% RT vs. 63% surg alone, p=0.06 Rates of apparent curative resection not improved with pre-op RT 85% vs. 86% Yearly risk LR 46% with pre-op RT vs. 37% post-op, improved also vs. surgery alone (p=0.00001) Death due to rectal cancer improved with RT, 45% pre-op RT vs. 50% surgery alone Deaths within 1 yr 8% pre-op RT vs. 4%
GI.interpretation: Pre-op RT at BED ≥30 Gy reduces risk of LR and cancer specific mortality.
GI.references: 1. Colorectal Cancer Collaborative Group. Adjuvant radiotherapy for rectal cancer: a systematic overview of 8507 patients from 22 randomised trials. Lancet 2001;358:1291-304. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(01)06409-1

GI.subtopic: Sequencing; pre-op short course TNT vs pre-op long course CRT
GI.methods: Noninferiority ➜ TNT: pre-op 25 Gy/5 fx → CAPOX x 4 → TME → CAPOX x 2 vs. ➜ CRT: pre-op 50 Gy/25 fx + cape → TME → CAPOX x 6 Surgery at 6-8 weeks Primary endpoint: DFS IMRT or VMAT required
GI.results: 3-yr DFS 65% (TNT) vs. 62%, non-inferior p<0.001 3-yr MFS 77% vs. 75%, NS 3-yr OS 87% vs. 75% (p=0.033) 3-yr LRR 8% vs. 11%, NS total pCR and sustained cCR (>12 months) 22% vs. 12% (p=0.002) Full completion rates 75% vs. 93% grade 3-4 adverse effects worse with TNT, 27% vs. 13%
GI.interpretation: Short course RT followed by chemo (TNT) DFS is non-inferior to long course preop CRT and adjuvant chemo There was higher G3+ adverse effects in TNT arm, mostly hematologic
GI.commentary: TNT = total neoadjuvant therapy. Could outcomes have been improved even more with a TNT arm of pre-op 50 Gy/25 fx + cape → CAPOX x4? Another interpretation of the trial could be that less CAPOX cycles are needed. Interestingly IMRT was required in the study. Although similar numbers of patients did not undergo surgery in both groups, there were more that refused surgery in the CRT arm, n=17 vs. 36; reasons why were not reported. A word of caution when comparing this to the RAPIDO study: the RAPIDO population was more advanced and adjuvant chemo was optional in the CRT arm. RAPIDO was a superio…
GI.references: 1. Jin J, Tang Y, Hu C, et al. Multicenter, Randomized, Phase III Trial of Short-Term Radiotherapy Plus Chemotherapy Versus Long-Term Chemoradiotherapy in Locally Advanced Rectal Cancer (STELLAR) [published online ahead of print, 2022 Mar 9]. J Clin Oncol. 2022;JCO2101667. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.21.01667 Protocol pdf [radiation planning p10]: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.21.01667/suppl_file/protocol%20JCO.21.01667.pdf#page=10

GI.subtopic: Sequencing; pre-op short course vs pre-op long course
GI.citation: van der Valk et al, Radiother Oncol, 2020 Bahadoer et al, Lancet Oncol, 2020 Dijkstra et al, Ann Surg, 2023
GI.patients: rectal cT4a-b, N2, extramural vascular invasion, involved mesorectal fascia, or enlarged lateral nodes
GI.methods: ➜ TNT: pre-op 25 Gy/5 fx → CAPOX/FOLFOX → TME vs. ➜ pre-op 50-50.4 Gy + cape → TME → optional CAPOX/FOLFOX (given in 59%) Surgery at 6-10 weeks Primary endpoint: disease failure
GI.results: 3-yr disease failure 24% vs. 30% 3-yr DM 20% vs. 27% 3-yr OS 89% in both arms 5-yr LRF 12% vs. 8%, p=0.07 5-yr LRR (LRFs minus those resected with R0/R1) 10% vs. 6%, p=0.027 pCR 28% vs. 14% 75% completed chemo in 84% vs. 58% serious adverse effects 38% vs. 34% (34% with chemo and 34% without)
GI.interpretation: There was less disease failure and DM, better pCR, but worse LRC with [short course RT → chemo → TME] compared to [long course RT → TME → optional chemotherapy]. There was higher tolerance of chemotherapy when delivering chemo pre-op.
GI.commentary: Local failure was worse with short course RT → chemo pre-op (vs. long course chemoRT → surgery → optional chemo) even though pCR was higher. Apparently pCR is not a surrogate for LRR. The optionality of systemic chemotherapy in the long course arm (given in 59%) limits the interpretation of this trial. (Optional chemo was given per predefined hospital policy.) It's unsurprising that mandating systemic chemo in the short course arm gives better DFS. Causation in the trial is unclear: are the results due to addition of chemo, compliance, short vs. long course RT, or sequencing? Chemo compliance …
GI.references: 1. van der Valk MJM, Marijnen CAM, van Etten B, et al. Compliance and tolerability of short-course radiotherapy followed by preoperative chemotherapy and surgery for high-risk rectal cancer - Results of the international randomized RAPIDO-trial [published correction appears in Radiother Oncol. 2020 Jun;147:e1]. Radiother Oncol 2020;147:75-83. doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2020.03.011 Protocol pdf [radiation planning p25]: https://dccg.nl/files/RAPIDO_protocol_3.1_signed_20160318_0.pdf#page=25 2. Bahadoer RR, Dijkstra EA, van Etten B, et al. Short-course radiotherapy followed by chemotherapy before total mesorectal excision (TME) versus preoperative chemoradiotherapy, TME, and optional adjuvant chemotherapy in locally advanced rectal cancer (RAPIDO): a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2020. doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30555-6 3. Dijkstra EA, Nilsson PJ, Hospers GAP, et al. Locoregional Failure During and After Short-course Radiotherapy followed by Chemotherapy and Surgery Compared to Long-course Chemoradiotherapy and Surgery - A Five-year Follow-up of the RAPIDO Trial [published online ahead of print, 2023 Jan 20]. Ann Surg 2023;10.1097/SLA.0000000000005799. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/SLA.0000000000005799

GI.subtopic: pre-op short course vs pre-op long course, timing of surgery
GI.citation: Erlandsson et al, Lancet Oncol, 2017 Erlandsson et al, Radiother Oncol, 2019 Erlandsson et al, BJS Open, 2021
GI.patients: resectable rectal adenocarcinoma Per authors, corresponds to: T3c-d in mid/upper rectum, T2-3b in lower rectum, T4 of only peritoneum or vaginal wall, N1-2, no compromise of mesorectal fascia
GI.methods: ➜ 25 Gy/5 fx then surgery in 1 week vs. ➜ 25 Gy/5 fx then surgery in 4-8 weeks vs. ➜ 50 Gy/25 fx with surgery in 4-8 wks No chemo in any arm
GI.results: Median time to LR 33 mos short course vs. 19 mos short course+delay vs. 33 mos long course+delay pCR 0.3% vs. 10.4% vs. 2.2% Post-op complications: 50% vs. 38% vs. 39%, p=0.075 Admissions due to RT adverse effects: 1% in short course vs. 7% short couse w delay In pooled analysis of short course regimens, post-op complications were less in shortRT with delay compared to short RT+1 week surgery Long-term QOL was similar in all arms
GI.interpretation: Time to LR and pCR were best with short course plus delay. There is some dispute regarding how to interpret the adverse effects results.
GI.commentary: Chemo was not included in the long course arm and might limit the interpretation of the study. Per authors, the T4s enrolled corresponded to invasion of peritoneum or vaginal wall. Results do not apply to T4b with compromise of mesorectal fascia or positive lateral lymph nodes. (Erlandsson and Martling 2017). Some have doubts regarding the adverse effects results. There is speculation that surgical complications were included as radiation adverse effects in the 1 week arm, but this is completely speculative. Observe also that RT admissions were worse in short course with delay. A French trial …
GI.references: 1. Erlandsson J, Holm T, Pettersson D, et al. Optimal fractionation of preoperative radiotherapy and timing to surgery for rectal cancer (Stockholm III): a multicentre, randomised, non-blinded, phase 3, non-inferiority trial. Lancet Oncol 2017;18:336-46. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30086-4 2. Erlandsson J, Lörinc E, Ahlberg M, et al. Tumour regression after radiotherapy for rectal cancer: Results from the randomised Stockholm III trial. Radiother Oncol 2019;135:178-86. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2019.03.016 3. Erlandsson J, Fuentes S, Radu C, et al. Radiotherapy regimens for rectal cancer: long-term outcomes and health-related quality of life in the Stockholm III trial. BJS Open 2021;5:zrab137. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/bjsopen/zrab137
GI.commentary_refs: Bujko K, Sopyło R. Short-course radiotherapy with delayed surgery for rectal cancer: a third option. Lancet Oncol 2017;18:275–276. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30075-X Erlandsson J, Martling A. Short-course radiotherapy with delayed surgery for rectal cancer - Authors' reply. Lancet Oncol 2017;18:e295. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30323-6

GI.subtopic: pre-op short course vs pre-op long course
GI.citation: Bujko et al, Ann Oncol, 2016 Cisel et al, Ann Oncol, 2019
GI.patients: Locally advanced rectal. cT4 or fixed cT3 Allowed low lying tumors
GI.methods: ➜ pre-op 25 Gy/5 fx and FOLFOX4 x3 vs. ➜ pre-op 50.4 Gy RT conc bolus 5FU/leukovorin/oxaliplatin Protocol later amended to allow elimination of oxaliplatin concurrently
GI.results: 3-yr DFS 52-53%, not different DM 30% vs 27% (NS) 3-yr OS 73% vs 65%, p=0.046 No difference in 8-yr OS, DFS, LF, or DM R0 77% vs 71%, p=0.07 pCR 16% vs 12%, p=0.17 LF 22% vs 21% (NS) Pelvic recurrences 13% vs 7% Any adverse effects 75% vs 83% Grade III-IV adverse effects 23% vs 21% No difference in post-operative or late complications toxic deaths 1% vs 3%
GI.interpretation: At 3-yr f/u, short course RT with consolidation chemo results in improved OS and lower acute adverse effects compared to long course with concurrent chemo. There is no difference in LC. At 8-yr f/u, there is no difference in OS, DFS, LF, or DM.
GI.commentary: The inclusion of concurrent oxaliplatin with long course RT, and its subsequent removal by protocol amendment, limits the interpretation of the trial. An alternative trial design could have been short course RT → FOLFOX vs long course RT + conc 5FU/cape → FOLFOX. Oxaliplatin was shown to increase adverse effects with RT in other trials. Only about ~75% were R0. OS benefit but no change in DFS. May be due to adverse effects from chemo. Oxaliplatin not paired with 5FU as it should be for efficacy. Bolus 5FU is no longer used in US (Tepper 2016). This trial is unique in that it is a short course …
GI.references: 1. Bujko K, Group ftPCS, Wyrwicz L, et al. Long-course oxaliplatin-based preoperative chemoradiation versus 5 × 5 Gy and consolidation chemotherapy for cT4 or fixed cT3 rectal cancer: results of a randomized phase III study. Ann Oncol 2016;27:834-42. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/annonc/mdw062 2. Ciseł B, Pietrzak L, Michalski W, et al. Long-course preoperative chemoradiation versus 5 × 5 Gy and consolidation chemotherapy for clinical T4 and fixed clinical T3 rectal cancer: long-term results of the randomized Polish II study. Ann Oncol 2019;30:1298-1303. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/annonc/mdz186
GI.commentary_refs: Tepper, JE. ACRJournal Advisor: Editor's selection. October 2016. Available from: https://www.acrjournaladvisor.com/User/ECRelatedEditions?HasEditionID=448&HasEditionDate=10/1/2016&id=1#t-2884. Accessed June 22, 2019.

GI.subtopic: pre-op short course vs pre-op long course
GI.patients: T3N0-3 rectal cancer staged with US or MRI
GI.methods: Superiority. Primary endpoint: LC ➜ pre-op 25 Gy/5 fx vs. ➜ pre-op 50.4 Gy with CI 5FU. All received consolidation 4 cycles 5FU
GI.results: 3-yr LR 4.4% vs 7.5% (NS) 5-yr LR 5.7% vs 7.5% (NS) On subanalysis, distal tumor LR 12.5% vs 0% long course No diff in late adverse effects, OS, or DM
GI.interpretation: Long course pre-op CRT did not improve LC, OS, or DM. Long course pre-op CRT had a trend in improved LC, especially in distal tumors.
GI.commentary: Had 80% power to detect LR of 15% for short course and 5% long course. Wide confidence intervals.
GI.references: 1. Ngan SY, Burmeister B, Fisher RJ, et al. Randomized Trial of Short-Course Radiotherapy Versus Long-Course Chemoradiation Comparing Rates of Local Recurrence in Patients With T3 Rectal Cancer: Trans-Tasman Radiation Oncology Group Trial 01.04. J Clin Oncol 2012;30:3827-33. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2012.42.9597

GI.subtopic: pre-op short course vs pre-op long course
GI.trial: Maria Sklodowska-Curie Memorial Cancer Center, Warsaw, Poland
GI.citation: Bujko et al, Br J Surg, 2006
GI.methods: ➜ pre-op 25 Gy/5 fx RT vs. ➜ pre-op chemoRT 50.4 Gy/ 28 fx with 5FU LV Superiority trial. Primary endpoint: 15% improvement in sphincter preservation
GI.results: Crude LR 14% vs. 9%, p=.170 Positive margins 13% vs. 4%, p=.017 4-yr DFS unchanged, 56-58% 4-yr OS unchanged, 66-67% Acute adverse effects increased with long course chemoRT: 3% vs. 18% Severe late adverse effects 7% vs. 10%, p=.360
GI.interpretation: Long course pre-op CRT did not improve the primary endpoint of sphincter control. There was also no improvement in OS, LC, or late adverse effects. Long course pre-op CRT had a trend in improved LC and negative margins.
GI.commentary: LC was not a primary endpoint. Primary endpoint: 15% improvement in sphincter preservation
GI.notes: Short course rectal RT summary: -Polish (2006): Long course chemo RT has more acute adverse effects. No difference in survival, LC, or late adverse effects -TROG: Possibly better LC with long course especially in distal tumors. No difference in OS, DM, or late adverse effects. -Chinese: Long course with improved pCR and worse acute adverse effects. No difference in OS, DFS, LRR, or DM -Stockholm III: Lowest adverse effects with short course with 4-8 weeks delay to surgery. LR noninferior. -Polish (2016): Complex, see study. Only for T4 and fixed T3. Favors 5x5 plus FOLFOX. -RAPIDO evaluates sh…
GI.references: 1. Bujko K, Nowacki MP, Nasierowska-Guttmejer A, Michalski W, Bebenek M, Kryj M. Long-term results of a randomized trial comparing preoperative short-course radiotherapy with preoperative conventionally fractionated chemoradiation for rectal cancer. Br J Surg 2006;93:1215-23. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/bjs.5506

GI.methods: Phase II IMRT. Comparison to RTOG 0247. ➜ IMRT 45 Gy with 3DCRT boost to 5.4 Gy + concurrent cape/ox → surgery → FOLFOX
GI.results: Endpoint was adverse effects Grade 2+ GI adverse effects: 51.5% vs. 57.7% on RTOG 0247 historical control (NS)
GI.interpretation: This phase II study showed no benefit to IMRT in rectal cancer in GI adverse effects compared to a historical control.
GI.commentary: The trial used concurrent oxaliplatin, which is now not in use. A limitation of the trial is that PROs were not collected, which at the time were not common in studies. This trial was negative, but the gyn TIME-C post-op IMRT trial was positive (Yeung 2020, 2022). Is this because TIME-C was post-op or because the trial had higher power and was better designed with PROs? Both studies had concurrent platinum chemo. PROSPECT showed increased sexual adverse effects with RT, but this was not observed in RTOG 0822. There may be value in repeating a study evaluating IMRT in rectal cancer with higher …
GI.references: 1. Hong TS, Moughan J, Garofalo MC, et al. NRG Oncology Radiation Therapy Oncology Group 0822: A Phase 2 Study of Preoperative Chemoradiation Therapy Using Intensity Modulated Radiation Therapy in Combination With Capecitabine and Oxaliplatin for Patients With Locally Advanced Rectal Cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2015;93:29-36. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2015.05.005
GI.commentary_refs: 1. Yeung AR, Pugh S, Klopp A, et al. Improvement in Patient-Reported Outcomes With Intensity-Modulated Radiotherapy (RT) Compared With Standard RT: A Report From the NRG Oncology RTOG 1203 Study. J Clin Oncol 2020. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.19.02381 2. Yeung AR, Deshmukh S, Klopp AH, et al. Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy Reduces Patient-Reported Chronic Toxicity Compared With Conventional Pelvic Radiation Therapy: Updated Results of a Phase III Trial [published online ahead of print, 2022 Aug 12]. J Clin Oncol. 2022;JCO2102831. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.21.02831 3. Basch E, Dueck AC, Mitchell SA, et al. Patient-Reported Outcomes During and After Treatment for Locally Advanced Rectal Cancer in the PROSPECT Trial (Alliance N1048). DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.23.00903

GI.methods: Retrospective database comparison ➜ watch and wait with gross LR after CR (IWWD database) vs. ➜ TME with ≤10% viable cells (VIKINGO database)
GI.results: DM 23% vs. 10% 3-yr DMFS 75% vs. 87%
GI.interpretation: In this retropspective database analysis, those with LR after watch and wait have worse outcomes and more DM than those with ≤10% viable cells after TME. Randomized trials and careful patient counseling prior to organ preservation are warranted.
GI.commentary: The comparison is reasonable but may not be equal: one cannot say that those with gross LR after WW are the same group as those that would have had ≤10% viable cells after TME. Some with ≤10% viable cells may have had cells that were still undergoing mitotic death, inactive, or that would have had later CR if observed for longer. Although adjustment can correct for some imbalances, this also cannot replace random assortment in a randomized trial. It is also possible however that leaving those cells present for longer periods of time in WW provides them more opportunity to spread, or that chemo…
GI.references: 1. Fernandez LM, São Julião GP, Santacruz CC, et al. Risks of Organ Preservation in Rectal Cancer: Data From Two International Registries on Rectal Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2024. Published online October 28, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.24.00405
GI.commentary_refs: 1. National Comprehensive Cancer Center Network. NCCN Guidelines: Rectal Cancer. Version 6.2023. Available at: https://www.nccn.org/professionals/physician_gls/pdf/rectal.pdf Accessed January 22 2024. 2. Scott AJ, Kennedy EB, Berlin J, et al. Management of Locally Advanced Rectal Cancer: ASCO Guideline. J Clin Oncol. Published online August 8, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.24.01160

GI.citation: Gerard et al, Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol, 2023 Dia et al, ASTRO, 2025
GI.patients: cT2-3b, grade 1-2, circumference <1/2, N0-N1, low to mid rectum of distance <10 cm, primary size ≤5 cm, node size ≤0.8 cm
GI.methods: Organ preservation. TEM allowed for near complete response →long course chemoRT to 45 Gy/25 fx with capecitabine, then: ➜ 9 Gy EBRT boost vs. ➜ 🏆 contact 50 kV x-ray brachytherapy boost 90 Gy/3 fx
GI.results: 3-yr organ preservation 59% vs. 81% 5-yr organ preservation 56% vs. 79% For size ≤3 cm 3-yr organ preservation 63% vs. 97% 5-yr organ preservation 54% vs. 93% For size >3 cm, 55% vs. 68% grade 1-2 rectal bleeding 63% vs. 12% and subsided after 3 years
GI.interpretation: Contact brachytherapy boost improves organ preservation rates in rectal cancer. Results were best in those with tumor size ≤3 cm. Adverse effect of rectal bleeding was increased.
GI.commentary: Only 6 patients received adjuvant chemo. The contact brachy boost was given over a period of 4 weeks using the Papillon 50 system, produced by Ariane Medical Systems in UK. If the tumor size was ≤3 cm, the contact brachy boost was given first, followed by EBRT after 1-2 weeks. Otherwise EBRT was first followed by brachy boost after 2-3 weeks. The first response assessment was at week 14 then week 20 or 24.
GI.references: 1. Gerard J, Barbet N, Schiappa R, et al. Neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy with radiation dose escalation with contact x-ray brachytherapy boost or external beam radiotherapy boost for organ preservation in early cT2–cT3 rectal adenocarcinoma (OPERA): a phase 3, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol 2023. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S2468-1253(22)00392-2 2. Dhia S, Doyen J, Pace-Loscos T, et al. 107 - Quality of Life and 5-Year Long-Term Organ Preservation in Early Rectal Adenocarcinoma Final Results from the OPERA Trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2025;123:S37-S38. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2025.06.1017

GI.patients: Stage II-III rectal cancer pMMR of low to mid rectum
GI.methods: Phase II organ preservation ➜ CAPOX → 50-50.4 Gy/25 fx RT with capecitabine → MRI, Endoscopy, DRE at 8 weeks → if clinical CR, nonoperative managment
GI.results: CR in 26% 30-mos DMRFS 95% if CR 30-mos DMRFS 74% overall
GI.interpretation: 26% were able to avoid surgery in this protocol assessing for CR at 8 weeks.
GI.commentary: OPRA allowed for assessment of CR at 8-12 weeks, and NO-CUT required 8 weeks. This could explain why results were better in OPRA.
GI.references: 1. Amatu A, Patelli G, Zampino MG, et al. Total neoadjuvant therapy followed by non-operative management or surgery in stage II-III rectal cancer (NO-CUT): a multicentre, single-arm, phase 2 trial. Lancet Oncol 2025;26:1612-1625. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(25)00542-X

GI.citation: Garcia-Aguilar et al, JCO, 2022 Verheij et al, JCO, 2023 Verheij et al, IJROBP, 2023
GI.patients: Stage II-III rectal cancer 77% T3, 10-15% T4, 71% N+
GI.methods: Phase II organ preservation Arm A: ➜ induction FOLFOX/CAPEOX → long course chemoRT 54 Gy Arm B: ➜ 🏆 long course chemoRT 54 Gy → consolidation FOLFOX/CAPEOX →Response assessment at 8-12 weeks with EUS, MRI, and DRE. If CR or nearCR → watchful waiting Follow-up with Procto- or sigmoidoscopy, MRI, etc, with lengthening intervals each year
GI.results: 3-yr organ preservation 41% vs. 53% 5-yr organ preservation 39% vs. 54% 94% of recurrences were within 2 years, and 99% within 3 years 3-yr regrowth 40% vs. 27% 3-yr DFS 76% in both arms [vs. 75% historical control] 5-yr DFS ~70%, not different 5-yr LRFS 94% vs. 90%, p=0.27 No difference in DMFS (~80%), OS, or R0 No difference in adverse effects
GI.interpretation: Chemoradiation first followed by systemic chemotherapy leads to better organ preservation rates than systemic therapy first. There is no difference in DFS between arms. DFS is unchanged compared to historical control. Most patients were T3 and/or N+.
GI.commentary: The trial is phase II and compared to historical control. Most were T3 and N+, and 10-15% were T4, a wide range of eligibility. Some may wish to see a randomized trial of TME vs. organ preservation. Both ASCO (Scott 2024) and NCCN guidelines allow for rectal preservation. ASCO's consensus is that the procedure has moderate evidence, strength conditional. NCCN also allows rectal sparing for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 ultra-hypermutated treated with immunotherapy based on phase II data. It should be acknowledged that if considered a toxicity, colostomy from APR is grade 4 RTOG. Framing colostomy a…

GI.results: Organ preservation achieved in 70% 30% had CR, 30% converted to TME Organ preservation had superior QOL Serious adverse events in 15% vs. 39%
GI.interpretation: Organ preservation has very good rates of success and superior QOL and adverse effects over TME.

GI.results: pCR similar 36-38% tumor regression 69% vs. 45% but wide CI Grade ≥3 adverse effects 9.4% vs. 7.8%
GI.interpretation: Boost prior to chemoradaition did not increase pCR or tumor regression in watchful waiting.

GI.methods: Database analysis of rectal cancer treated with neoadjuvant chemoRT that underwent watch and wait
GI.results: 87% had CR If CR for 1 yr, then 2-yr LRC 88%, 3-yr 97%, 5-yr 99% If CR and no DM for 1-yr, the risk of later DM is 5% or less 2-yr LR 25% 88% of recurrences were within 2 years DM 8%, 5-yr OS 85%, 5-yr DSS 94%
GI.interpretation: Most recurrences after watchful waiting occur within 2 years. Failure of salvage is rare. Outcomes stabilize at 3 years, suggesting active surveillance can be discontinued at that time.
GI.commentary: No information provided on why these patients did not undergo surgery. n=793 in the second study evaluating outcomes after CR

GI.citation: Rullier et al, Lancet, 2017 Rullier et al, Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol, 2020
GI.patients: T2-3, <8 cm from anal verge, size <4cm, with PR to chemoRT of size ≤2 cm
GI.methods: Superiority design: PR to chemoRT of size ≤2 cm → ➜ local excision vs. ➜ TME In the LE group, TME is required if ypT2-3
GI.results: events (death, recurrence, morbidity, and side-effects): 56% vs. 48% (NS) 5-yr LF 7% both arms 5-yr DM 18-19%, not different 5-yr CSS 7-10%, not different 5-yr OS ~83%, not different Worse morbidity with TME Node positive in only 8% in TME (0 in ypT0, 0 in T1, 8% in T2, 40% in T3) In the LE group, 26 pts required TME. Major morbidity 78% in TME after LE
GI.interpretation: Local exicsion did not show superiority to TME. There are no differences in outcomes. The low lymph node rates suggest TME may not be required for all patients.
GI.commentary: A noninferiority design seems better suited for the study than superiority. The authors suggest that TME may not be required in ypT0-T2 or cN0.
GI.references: 1. Rullier E, Rouanet P, Tuech J-J, et al. Organ preservation for rectal cancer (GRECCAR 2): a prospective, randomised, open-label, multicentre, phase 3 trial. Lancet 2017;390:469-79. GI.methods: Phase II organ preservation ➜ long course chemoRT → FOLFOX/CAPOX vs. ➜ long course chemoRT → FOLFIRINOX
GI.results: Ongoing study Primary endpoint: cCR Secondary endpoints: DFS, time to organ preservation failure, DM, OS, adverse events
GI.commentary: Since the design of this protocol, PRODIGE 23 showed improved OS and DM with FOLFIRINOX. It's now likely unethical to randomize to the FOLFOX arm, especially for higher stages, but there is a question of how tolerable FOLFIRINOX is after chemoRT. The FOLFOX arm could be closed or changed to FOLFIRINOX first followed by radiation.

GI.citation: Schrag et al, NEJM, 2023 Basch et al, JCO, 2023
GI.patients: Rectal T2N1, T3N0, T3N1, >5 cm from anal verge, >3 mm radial margin, who are candidates for LAR
GI.methods: Noninferioriy ➜ Standard: pre-op long course chemoRT → TME → suggested FOLFOX x8 vs. ➜ 🏆 Selective arm: FOLFOX x6 → response assessment with MRI → if response ≥20%, omit RT → TME → suggested FOLFOX x6 if response <20% or <5 cycles completed → long course chemoRT → TME → FOLFOX x2 •post-op chemoRT given for +margin
GI.results: 93% in FOLFOX arm had ≥20% response ✅ 5-yr DFS 81% vs. 79%, noninferior 5-yr LR 1.8% vs. 1.6%, not different pCR 24% vs. 22% R0 97% vs. 99% 5-yr OS 90% both arms In the FOLFOX selective arm, 9% had pre-op RT and 1% had post-op RT 📌Adverse effects During neoadjuvant: grade ≥3 adverse effects 23% vs. 41% (neuropathy worse with FOLFOX, 17%, vs. 83%) During any adjuvant treatment: Grade ≥3 adverse effects 26% vs. 33% 📌QOL •During neoadjuvant treatment: chemoRT: worse diarrhea and bowel function FOLFOX: worse anxiety, appetite loss, constipation, depression, dysphagia, dyspnea, edema, fatigue, muco…
GI.interpretation: The two approaches are noninferior in those that meet PROSPECT eligibility criteria.
GI.commentary: Overview. The trial supports chemotherapy alone in those that meet PROSPECT eligibility criteria. LR rates were very low in both arms. ChemoRT is still an option for some circumstances. Nearly double the amount of chemotherapy was permitted in the selective arm. Radiation may be useful for those who wish to undergo less chemotherapy. According to the authors however, similar amounts of chemotherapy were actually given, though this is yet to be published (Schrag D, email communication, Sept 2024). Adverse effects. Neoadjuvant QOL was reported as worse overall with FOLFOX. ChemoRT QOL showed wor…

GI.citation: Deng et al, JCO, 2019 Zhang et al, JCO, 2024
GI.methods: ➜ 46-50.4 Gy + 5FU CI → TME → FOLFOX x7 vs. ➜ FOLFOX x6 plus 46-50.4 Gy concurrent at cycle 2 → TME → FOLFOX x7 vs. ➜ FOLFOX x4-6 → TME → FOLFOX x6-8
GI.results: pCR 14% vs. 28% vs. 7% 10-yr LRR 11% vs. 8% vs. 10%, not different 10-yr DFS 3% vs. 63% vs. 61% not dfferent 10-yr OS 63% vs. 72% vs. 73%, not different
GI.interpretation: RT can be omitted if extended FOLFOX is given.
GI.commentary: A multi-institutional study from China. See also the PROSPECT trial, which had narrower inclusion criteria, and more detailed QOL reporting. Results differ. In that trial, DFS was not different between arms similar to 1 and 3. In FOWARC, pCR is improved with radiation, but not LRR, possibly supporting its role, and especially for non operative management. A variety of substages were included in FOWARC, and a subanalysis was not done. Some of the lower stages may be overtreated and could have chemoRT alone, less FOLFOX, or even surgery alone for favorable T3. About 65% were T3, 25% T4a, and 9% …
