GI.citation: Mei W et al, Ann Surg, 2023
GI.patients: cT2N+ or cT3-4a Nany rectal cancer Excluded: adjacent to mesorectal fascia
GI.methods: ➜ CAPOX x4 → if progression, chemoRT → surgery → CAPOX x4 vs. ➜ chemoRT 50 Gy/ 25 fx → surgery → CAPOX x6
GI.results: Report of secondary endpoints: pCR 11% vs. 13%, p-0.33 Downstaging 41% vs. 46%, p=0.27 DM 1% vs. 3%, p=0.03 sphincter preservation 95% vs. 94% preventative ileostomy 52% vs. 64% post-op complications 19% vs. 26%, p=0.05 Nonoperative management after pCR in n=2 & n=5
GI.interpretation: CAPOX shows favorable results in secondary endpoints thus far compared to chemoRT. Further f/u is awaited.

GI.citation: Conroy et al, Lancet Oncol 2021 Conroy et al, Ann Oncol, 2024
GI.methods: ➜ 50 Gy/ 25 fx + cape → TME → 6 mos FOLFOX or cape vs. ➜ →🏆 TNT: FOLFIRINOX x6 cycles → 50 Gy/ 25 fx + cape → TME → 3 mos FOLFOX or cape
GI.results: pCR 12% vs. 28% 3-yr DFS 69% vs. 76% 7-yr DFS 63% vs. 68% 7-yr MFS 72% vs. 79% 7-yr DM 28% vs. 21% 7-yr OS 76% vs. 82% 7-yr LR 8.1% vs. 5.3% both arms well tolerated
GI.interpretation: Neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX with 3 months adjuvant FOLFOX improves OS, pCR, DFS, and MFS compared to 6 months adjuvant FOLFOX.
GI.commentary: After years without innovation, PRODIGE 23 finally established a new standard of care in advanced rectal cancer for improvement of OS and MFS. LR of 5% is low, though not as low as in PROSPECT, indicating perhaps a continuing role for radiation therapy in this population. PRODIGE allowed entry of patients of higher stage than PROSPECT, but also anything that was eligible for PROSPECT. Because of a higher risk patient population, and use of different chemotherapy, it is unclear if radiation can be omitted with FOLFIRNOX. In PRODIGE 92% completed all cycles of FOLFIRINOX, and 81% in that arm com…

GI.citation: Garcia-Aguilar et al, JCO, 2022 Verheij et al, JCO, 2023 Verheij et al, IJROBP, 2023
GI.patients: Stage II-III rectal cancer 77% T3, 10-15% T4, 71% N+
GI.methods: Phase II organ preservation Arm A: ➜ induction FOLFOX/CAPEOX → long course chemoRT 54 Gy Arm B: ➜ 🏆 long course chemoRT 54 Gy → consolidation FOLFOX/CAPEOX →Response assessment at 8-12 weeks with EUS, MRI, and DRE. If CR or nearCR → watchful waiting Follow-up with Procto- or sigmoidoscopy, MRI, etc, with lengthening intervals each year
GI.results: 3-yr organ preservation 41% vs. 53% 5-yr organ preservation 39% vs. 54% 94% of recurrences were within 2 years, and 99% within 3 years 3-yr regrowth 40% vs. 27% 3-yr DFS 76% in both arms [vs. 75% historical control] 5-yr DFS ~70%, not different 5-yr LRFS 94% vs. 90%, p=0.27 No difference in DMFS (~80%), OS, or R0 No difference in adverse effects
GI.interpretation: Chemoradiation first followed by systemic chemotherapy leads to better organ preservation rates than systemic therapy first. There is no difference in DFS between arms. DFS is unchanged compared to historical control. Most patients were T3 and/or N+.
GI.commentary: The trial is phase II and compared to historical control. Most were T3 and N+, and 10-15% were T4, a wide range of eligibility. Some may wish to see a randomized trial of TME vs. organ preservation. Both ASCO (Scott 2024) and NCCN guidelines allow for rectal preservation. ASCO's consensus is that the procedure has moderate evidence, strength conditional. NCCN also allows rectal sparing for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 ultra-hypermutated treated with immunotherapy based on phase II data. It should be acknowledged that if considered a toxicity, colostomy from APR is grade 4 RTOG. Framing colostomy a…

GI.citation: Fokas et al, JCO, 2019 Diefenhardt et al, Radiother Oncol, 2023
GI.methods: Phase II/III. Endpoint: pCR Group A: ➜ pre-op FOLFOX x3 → 50.4 Gy + 5FU/oxali → surgery Group B: ➜ 🏆 pre-op 50.4 Gy + 5FU/oxali → FOLFOX x3 → surgery
GI.results: pCR 17% vs. 25%, p=0.04 APR 26% vs. 22% R0 86% chemo completion 92% vs. 85% Other efficacy outcomes not different 3-yr DFS 73%, LRR 5%, DM 17%, OS 92%
GI.interpretation: In the phase II portion of the trial, chemoRT first followed by consolidation chemo then surgery led to better pCR rates than with chemo → chemoRT → surgery.
GI.commentary: Although arm A, the chemotherapy first arm, did lead to better chemo compliance, 92% vs. 85%, there was no suggestion of benefit in the p values of DM, DFS, or OS. There is also the option that more chemotherapy could be given after surgery, if preferred. ChemoRT may remove the need for additional cycles of FOLFOX prior to surgery. The TIMING trial did show improved pcR with 6 cycles of FOLFOX; however, the trial was nonrandomized, and other outcomes including DM, DFS, and OS were not reported.
GI.references: 1. Fokas E, Allgäuer M, Polat B, et al. Randomized Phase II Trial of Chemoradiotherapy Plus Induction or Consolidation Chemotherapy as Total Neoadjuvant Therapy for Locally Advanced Rectal Cancer: CAO/ARO/AIO-12. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:3212-3222. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.19.00308 2. Diefenhardt M, Fleischmann M, Martin D, et al. Clinical outcome after total neoadjuvant treatment (CAO/ARO/AIO-12) versus intensified neoadjuvant and adjuvant treatment (CAO/ARO/AIO-04) a comparison between two multicenter randomized phase II/III trials [published online ahead of print, 2022 Dec 23]. Radiother Oncol. 2022;179:109455. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2022.109455

GI.patients: Stage II-III locally advanced rectal cancer
GI.methods: Phase II, nonrandomized. TNT then TME: ➜ CI 5FU+RT 45+5.4 Gy RT vs. ➜ CI 5FU+RT --> mFOLFOX6 x2 vs. ➜ CI 5FU+RT --> mFOLFOX6 x4 vs. ➜ CI 5FU+RT --> mFOLFOX6 x6
GI.results: pCR best with 6 cycles of FOLFOX 18% vs. 25% vs. 30% vs. 38% In group 4, 28% grade 3 adverse events (mostly lymphopenia and neutropenia) and 8% grade 4 More surgery related complications in group 4, most common pelvic abscesses and anastomotic leaks
GI.interpretation: Delivery of mFOLFOXx6 after chemoRT and before TME results in favorable pCR and may help lead to future deescalation of therapy in phase III trials. However the trial was nonrandomized, complicating the interpretation of the findings.
GI.references: 1. Garcia-Aguilar J, Chow OS, Smith DD, et al. Effect of adding mFOLFOX6 after neoadjuvant chemoradiation in locally advanced rectal cancer: a multicentre, phase 2 trial. Lancet Oncol 2015;16:957-66. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(15)00004-2

GI.patients: ypT3-4N0 or any ypN1-2 after 5FU chemoRT and TME
GI.methods: 5FU chemo RT and TME → ➜ 5FU+LV vs. ➜ FOLFOX Primary endpoint: DFS
GI.results: 6-yr DFS 57% vs. 68% 6-yr OS 76% vs. 78%, p=0.21 In subanalysis, benefit with FOLFOX in ypN2 and minimally regressed tumor
GI.interpretation: The addition of oxaliplatin to 5FU+LV improves DFS in ypT3-4N0 and any ypN1-2 rectal cancer. The trial was not powered for OS.
GI.commentary: Should the trial have been powered for OS? One view is that PFS/DFS is a surrogate for OS, but this is met with critcism. If DFS leads to OS benefit later, confirmation of causation is difficult. And second, DFS/PFS themselves are composed of heterogeneous factors, and one trial's PFS may be very different from another trial's. Third, the mechanisms of the interventions leading to PFS in each trial may be very different. There is OS benefit with oxaliplatin in the MOSAIC trial in colorectal cancer (André 2015). Nonetheless, the US FDA does approve drugs based on PFS/DFS.
GI.references: 1. Hong YS, Kim SY, Lee JS, et al. Oxaliplatin-Based Adjuvant Chemotherapy for Rectal Cancer After Preoperative Chemoradiotherapy (ADORE): Long-Term Results of a Randomized Controlled Trial. J Clin Oncol 2019;37(:3111-3123. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.19.00016

GI.methods: Pre-op 50.4-55.8 Gy with conc: ➜ 5-FU CVI vs. ➜ cape/ox vs. 5-FU/ox
GI.results: pCR with cape 21%. No significant difference in pCR, sphincter saving surgery, or downstaging
GI.interpretation: Adding oxaliplatin to long course pre-op RT + 5FU worsens adverse effects and does not improve pCR.
GI.commentary: Some practicioners may treat post op cases to 55.8 Gy based on this study.
GI.references: 1. O'Connell MJ, Colangelo LH, Beart RW, et al. Capecitabine and oxaliplatin in the preoperative multimodality treatment of rectal cancer: surgical end points from National Surgical Adjuvant Breast and Bowel Project trial R-04. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:1927-1934. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2013.53.7753

GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 40 Gy + 10 Gy boost + carbo/taxol vs. ➜ 40 Gy + 26 Gy boost + carbo/taxol
GI.results: Median LRPFS 16.2 vs. 18.4 mos 2-yr LRPFS 43-44% No difference in OS Median OS ~24 mos 2-yr OS ~49%, 3-yr OS ~40% 3DCRT vs. IMRT/VMAT Median OS 19 mos vs. 25 mos 2-yr OS 43% vs. 50% 3-yr OS 26% vs. 44%
GI.interpretation: There was no improvement in LRPFS or OS with dose escalation. Chemoradiation alone results in reasonable rates of local control when surgery is not an option.
GI.commentary: The trial was designed to evaluate dose escalation, but also provides useful statistics for patients who wish to avoid or who are unable to undergo surgical resection.
GI.references: 1. Crehange G, M'vondo C, Bertaut A, et al. Exclusive Chemoradiotherapy With or Without Radiation Dose Escalation in Esophageal Cancer: Multicenter Phase 2/3 Randomized Trial CONCORDE (PRODIGE-26). Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2021;111:s5. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2021.07.045

GI.patients: T2-4, N0-3 esophageal cancer, inoperable (either medically or anatomically). M1 supraclavicle nodes allowed
GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 50.4 Gy + carbo/taxol vs. ➜ 61.6/50.4 Gy SIB + carbo/taxol
GI.results: 3-yr LPFS 70% vs. 73% (NS) 1-yr LPFS SCC 75% vs. 79% (NS) 1-yr LPFS adeno 61% both arms 3-yr LRPFS 52% vs. 59%, p=0.08 No change in OS
GI.interpretation: Dose escalation with SIB of 61.6/50.4 Gy did not improve local control. Chemoradiation alone results in reasonable rates of local control when surgery is not an option.
GI.commentary: The trial was designed to evaluate dose escalation, but also provides useful statistics for patients who wish to avoid or who are unable to undergo surgical resection.
GI.references: 1. Hulshof MCCM, Geijsen ED, Rozema T, et al. GI.trial: National Cancer Center Hospital East, Chiba, Japan
GI.methods: Prospective: 60 Gy and concurrent cis/5FU then adj cis/5FU x2
GI.results: ORR 73% 3-yr OS 66.5%, 3-yr laryngectomy free survival 52.5% For T4, 3-yr OS 58.3% and 3-yr LFS 38.5%
GI.interpretation: Definitive chemoRT for cervical esophageal cancer has favorable outcomes.
GI.commentary: The only prospective study for cervical esophagus.
GI.references: 1. Zenda S, Kojima T, Kato K, et al. Multicenter Phase 2 Study of Cisplatin and 5-Fluorouracil With Concurrent Radiation Therapy as an Organ Preservation Approach in Patients With Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Cervical Esophagus. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2016;96:976-84. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2016.08.045

GI.methods: ➜🏆 50.4 Gy + 5FU/cisplatin vs. ➜ 64.8 Gy + 5FU/cisplatin
GI.results: Closed and reached futility early due to deaths in high dose arm, but prior to recieving 50.4 Gy No difference in any outcomes OS 13 vs. 18 mos (NS) 2-yr OS 31% vs. 40% (NS) LRF 56% vs. 52% (NS)
GI.interpretation: Dose escalation to 64.8 Gy from 50.4 Gy did not improve OS or LF. Chemoradiation alone results in reasonable rates of local control when surgery is not an option.
GI.commentary: The study was previously questioned for its closure for futility, but now that ARTDECO and PRODIGE 26 show no benefit to dose escalation, the case seems to be closed. At the time some criticized the study for terminating "early," but the term futility is a statistical statement, and means that statistically continuing the trial would have been futile and a waste of funding. The trial was designed to evaluate dose escalation, but also provides useful data for patients who wish to avoid or who are unable to undergo surgical resection.
GI.references: 1. Minsky BD, Pajak TF, Ginsberg RJ, et al. GI.citation: Herskovic et al, NEJM, 1992 Al-Sarraf et al, JCO, 1997 Cooper et al, JAMA, 1999
GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 50 Gy/25 fx + 5FU/cisplatin vs. ➜ 64 Gy alone
GI.results: 5-yr OS 26% CRT vs. 0% RT alone Median OS 14.1 vs. 9.3 mos crude DM 12% vs. 26% crude infield LR 44% vs. 65% Only 4 patients eventually underwent surgery for clinical recurrence (one had no evidence of tumor, the others died of cancer)
GI.interpretation: Adding chemo to RT leads to improved OS in unresectable esophageal cancer compared to RT alone. Chemoradiation alone results in reasonable rates of local control when surgery is not an option.
GI.commentary: The crude recurrences listed are derived from Table 2 of Herskovic 1992. The trial was designed to evaluate dose escalation, but also provides useful statistics for patients who wish to avoid or who are unable to undergo surgical resection.

GI.citation: Hoeppner et al, NEJM, 2025 Hoeppner et al, JCO, 2025
GI.patients: T2-4a or any N+, M0, adenocarcinoma only
GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 perioperative docetaxel, oxaliplatin, 5FU, LV (FLOT4) vs. ➜ pre-op chemoRT to 41.4 Gy + carbo/paclitaxel weekly (CROSS)
GI.results: FLOT vs. chemoRT Median OS 66 vs. 37 mos 3-yr OS 57% vs. 51% 3-yr PFS 52% vs. 35% 3-yr DM 32% vs. 47% 3-yr LRR 20% vs. 17%, not different pCR 17% vs. 8%
GI.interpretation: Peri-operative FLOT improves OS and DM compared to pre-op chemoRT for locally advanced esophageal adenocarcinoma. LRR was not improved with chemoRT.
GI.commentary: ESOPEC and NeoAEGIS were pre-op trials for adenocarcinoma only. Results in ESOPEC show an impressive OS benefit with FLOT in esophageal cancer, an entity with few advancements over years other than nivolumab for those with partial response. ChemoRT did not improve LRR or pCR in ESOPEC. ChemoRT did have better pCR in NeoAEGIS. Neither trial investigated radiation with FLOT or nivolumab (Checkmate 577, Kelly 2021). Fojo et al (2025) explain a statistical bias called skimming the median that overestimates survival gains by not taking into account the full dataset, and provide ESOPEC as one of the…

GI.methods: Noninferiority ➜ perioperative chemo (ECF/ECX/EOF/EOX or FLOT) vs. ➜ 🏆pre-op chemoRT to 41.4 Gy + carbo/paclitaxel weekly (CROSS)
GI.results: 3-yr OS 56-57%, noninferior MAGIC/FLOT vs. chemoRT R0 82% vs. 96% LRR not different, 20-25% pCR 4% vs. 12% Neutropenia, sepsis slightly worse with MAGIC/FLOT
GI.interpretation: The CROSS regimen leads to superior pathologic response and R0 resections over MAGIC/FLOT. In regards to OS, CROSS and MAGIC/FLOT are noninferior.
GI.commentary: ESOPEC and NeoAEGIS were pre-op trials for adenocarcinoma only. Results in ESOPEC show an impressive OS benefit with FLOT in esophageal cancer, an entity with few advancements over years other than nivolumab for those with partial response. ChemoRT did not improve LRR or pCR in ESOPEC. ChemoRT did have better pCR in NeoAEGIS. Neither trial investigated radiation with FLOT or nivolumab (Checkmate 577, Kelly 2021). Fojo et al (2025) explain a statistical bias called skimming the median that overestimates survival gains by not taking into account the full dataset, and provide ESOPEC as one of the…
GI.references: 1. Reynolds JV, Preston SR, O'Neill B, et al. GI.citation: Kelly et al, NEJM, 2021 Kelly et al, ASCO, 2025
GI.patients: Stage II-III esophageal or GE junction s/p neoadjuvant chemoRT and surgery with incomplete response
GI.methods: s/p neoadjuvant chemoRT and surgery with incomplete response ➜ 🏆 nivolumab vs. ➜ placebo
GI.results: 5-yr OS 50% vs. 41% ⬆️ Median OS 51.7 vs. 35.3 mos ⬆️ Median DMFS 27.3 vs. 14.6 mos ⬆️ Median DFS 21.8 vs. 10.8 mos grade 3-4 adverse events 13% vs. 6% grade 3-4 pneumonitis in <1% with nivo
GI.interpretation: Adjuvant nivolumab improves OS in resected esophageal cancer with incomplete response after neoadjuvant chemoRT.
GI.commentary: For future directions, nivolumab should be investigated for those with any response. Radiation details: 64% had 41.4-50.4 Gy. About 20% had >50.4 Gy and about 15% had <41.4. No details provided on modality.
GI.references: 1. Kelly RJ, Ajani JA, Kuzdzal J, et al. Adjuvant Nivolumab in Resected Esophageal or Gastroesophageal Junction Cancer. N Engl J Med 2021;384:1191-1203. GI.patients: Resectable esophageal cancer T1-4N1M0 or T4N0M0
GI.methods: ➜ surgery alone vs. ➜ pre-op RT to 40 Gy/2 Gy fx + cisplatin vinorelbine q3 wks
GI.results: pCR 43% R0 91% vs. 98% Median OS 67 mos vs. 100 mos Median DFS 42 mos vs. 100 mos
GI.interpretation: Pre-op chemoRT improves OS compared to surgery alone.
GI.references: 1. Yang H, Liu H, Chen Y, et al. Neoadjuvant Chemoradiotherapy Followed by Surgery Versus Surgery Alone for Locally Advanced Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Esophagus (NEOCRTEC5010): A Phase III Multicenter, Randomized, Open-Label Clinical Trial. GI.citation: Hagan et al, NEJM, 2012 Shapiro et al, Lancet, 2015 Eyck et al, JCO, 2021
GI.patients: Resectable T1N1 and T2/3 N0/1 adeno (75%) or squamous (25%) of esophagus (75%) or GEJ (25%)
GI.methods: ➜ surgery alone vs. ➜ 🏆pre-op chemoRT to 41.4 Gy + carbo/paclitaxel weekly RT did not include SCV or celiac
GI.results: Improved OS with chemoRT Median OS 49 vs. 24 mos Median OS SCC 82 vs. 21 mos Median OS adeno 43 vs. 27 mos 5-yr OS 47% vs. 34% 10-yr OS 38% vs. 25% R0 92% vs. 69% pCR 29% (23% in adeno, 49% in SCC)
GI.interpretation: Adding concurrent carbo/paclitaxel to pre-op RT improves OS over surgery alone with favorable pCR and low adverse effects.
GI.commentary: Most patients were adenocarinoma. This trial can support the use of 41.4 Gy dose, especially in the pre-op setting.
GI.references: 1. van Hagen P, Hulshof MCCM, van Lanschot JJB, et al. Preoperative Chemoradiotherapy for Esophageal or Junctional Cancer. N Engl J Med 2012;366:2074-84. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1112088 Protocol pdf [radiation planning p16]: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.20.03614/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.20.03614.pdf 2. Shapiro J, van Lanschot JJB, Hulshof MCCM, et al. Neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy plus surgery versus surgery alone for oesophageal or junctional cancer (CROSS): long-term results of a randomised controlled trial. Lancet Oncol 2015;16:1090-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(15)00040-6 3. Eyck BM, van Lanschot JJB, Hulfshof MCCM, et al. Ten-Year Outcome of Neoadjuvant Chemoradiotherapy Plus Surgery for Esophageal Cancer: The Randomized Controlled CROSS Trial. J Clin Oncol 2021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.20.03614

GI.patients: CROSS Phase I, II, and III trials
GI.methods: Recurrence outcomes collected
GI.results: chemoRT gave improvement in RFS, Median OS, peritoneal carcinomatosis, and hemotogenous spread Trend at celiac and para-aortic. No diff at SCV (but only 8 proximal patients) LRR 14% vs. 34% Distant only 21% vs. 24% LRR plus distant 10.8% vs. 24% Only 2 borderline field LRR Of pCR patients, only one with solitary LRR. But no LN dissection in this patient
GI.interpretation: Pre-op chemoRT reduced LRR and peritoneal carcinomatosis Most LRR were concominant with outfield recurrences.
GI.commentary: Very few upper esophageal patients were included.
GI.references: 1. Oppedijk V, van der Gaast A, van Lanschot JJ, et al. Patterns of Recurrence After Surgery Alone Versus Preoperative Chemoradiotherapy and Surgery in the CROSS Trials. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:385-91. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2013.51.2186 Protocol pdf [radiation planning p16]: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.20.03614/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.20.03614.pdf

GI.methods: neoadj chemoRT: ➜ pre-op 40 Gy/20 fx 3DCRT concurrent with cisplatin (25mg/m2) + paclitaxel (50mg/m2) → esophagectomy vs. neoadj chemo: ➜ pre-op cisplatin (75mg/m2) + paclitaxel (175mg/m2) x2→ esophagectomy Surgery at 4-8 weeks after neoadjuvant tx Primary endpoint: 3-yr OS
GI.results: pCR 28% vs. 3%, p=0.004 downstage to Stage I, 53% vs. 20% 3-yr OS 64% vs. 55%, NS 3-yr PFS 54% vs. 50%, NS R0 resection rate not different: 97%
GI.interpretation: Neoadjuvant CRT leads to better pCR rate and downstaging in patients with locally advanced ESCC with a trend toward improved OS
GI.commentary: A CROSS-like regimen of neoadjuvant CRT still reigns supreme. This now provides evidence in squamous histology esophageal cancer that neoadjuvant CRT is superior to CT (POET trial provides evidence of CRT superiority for adenos). The dose in the CRT arm was 40 Gy and possibly a higher dose of up to 50.4 could improve outcomes. This study was prior to Checkmate 577 which made nivolumab the standard of care for patients with incomplete response to surgery so it is unclear how this subset of patients would compare in the immunotherapy era. There were more grade 3 postoperative complications in th…
GI.references: 1. Tang H, Wang H, Fang Y, et al. Neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy versus neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by minimally invasive esophagectomy for locally advanced esophageal squamous cell carcinoma: a prospective multicenter randomized clinical trial. Ann Oncol. 2023;34(2):163-172. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.annonc.2022.10.508

GI.patients: Esophageal SCC (70%) or adeno (29%), T1-T2N0-1 or T3N0. Celiac and SCV LN excluded
GI.methods: ➜ Transthoracic surgery vs. ➜ pre-op RT 45 Gy + cis/5FU
GI.results: No difference in OS Median OS 41.2 vs. 31.8 mos (NS) Post-op death worse with CRT-S: 11% vs 3% pCR 33% No difference in R0: 92-94%
GI.interpretation: Pre-op chemoRT did not improve OS compared to surgery alone.
GI.commentary: Study terminated early due to futility.
GI.references: 1. Mariette C, Dahan L, Mornex F, et al. Surgery Alone Versus Chemoradiotherapy Followed by Surgery for Stage I and II Esophageal Cancer: Final Analysis of Randomized Controlled Phase III Trial FFCD 9901. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:2416-22. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2013.53.6532

GI.patients: adeno or SCC thoracic esophagus to GEJ with less than 2 cm to cardia T1-3, N0-1. Nodes <1.5 cm
GI.methods: ➜ sugery alone vs ➜ pre-op RT to 50.4 Gy + cis/5FU Optional inclusion of SCV or celiac Surgery in 3-8 weeks, Ivor-Lewis
GI.results: Median OS 4.5 yrs vs. 1.8 5-yr OS 39% vs. 16% PFS 3.5 yrs vs. 1
GI.interpretation: Pre-op chemoRT improves OS compared to surgery alone.
GI.commentary: Study closed early due to poor accrual.
GI.references: 1. Tepper J, Krasna MJ, Niedzwiecki D, et al. Phase III trial of trimodality therapy with cisplatin, fluorouracil, radiotherapy, and surgery compared with surgery alone for esophageal cancer: CALGB 9781. J Clin Oncol 2008;26:1086–1092. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2007.12.9593

GI.methods: ➜ surgery alone vs. ➜ pre-op 45 Gy 1.5 BID + cisplatin/vinblastine/5FU
GI.interpretation: Pre-op chemoRT trends to improved OS compared to surgery alone.
GI.references: 1. Urba SG, Orringer MB, Turrisi A, Iannettoni M, Forastiere A, Strawderman M. Randomized Trial of Preoperative Chemoradiation Versus Surgery Alone in Patients With Locoregional Esophageal Carcinoma. J Clin Oncol 2001;19:305-13. https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2001.19.2.305

GI.methods: ➜ surgery alone vs. ➜ pre-op chemoRT to 40 Gy/15 fx + cis/5FU
GI.results: ChemoRT improved 1-yr OS 52% vs. 32% 3-yr OS 6% vs. 32% Median OS 16 vs. 11 mos
GI.interpretation: Pre-op chemoRT improves OS compared to surgery alone.
GI.references: 1. Walsh TN, Noonan N, Hollywood D, Kelly A, Keeling N, Hennessy TPJ. A Comparison of Multimodal Therapy and Surgery for Esophageal Adenocarcinoma. N Engl J Med 1996;335:462-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199608153350702

GI.subtopic: pre-op chemo vs. pre-op chemoRT SCC only
GI.methods: Superiority ➜ neoadj cisplatin 5FU vs. ➜ 🏆 neoadj cisplatin 5FU docetaxel vs. ➜ neoadj cisplatin 5FU → 41.4 Gy radiation Followed by surgery
GI.results: Cis 5FU docetaxel vs. Cis 5 FU 3-yr OS 63% vs. 72% Cis 5FU docetaxel vs. Cis 5 FU → RT 3-yr OS 68% vs. 72%, p=0.12 DM 57% CFD vs. 77% CRT pCR 2% vs. 20% vs. 39% R0 89% vs. 93% vs. 98% LRR 43% vs. 38% vs. 23%
GI.interpretation: Cisplatin 5FU docetaxel has improved OS over cisplatin 5FU. Cis 5FU taxol was not significantly better than cisplatin 5FU followed by radiation. Radiation therapy had better pCR, LC, and R0.
GI.commentary: Concurrent chemoradiation was not tested in the study. Since the trial was superiority, not a noninferiority trial, we cannot conclude that radiatioin is noninferior to chemotherapy alone. Improved LC with radiation therapy suggests that combination therapy may be useful.
GI.references: 1. Kato K, Machida R, Ito Y, et al. Doublet chemotherapy, triplet chemotherapy, or doublet chemotherapy combined with radiotherapy as neoadjuvant treatment for locally advanced oesophageal cancer (JCOG1109 NExT): a randomised, controlled, open-label, phase 3 trial. Lancet 2024;404(10447):55-66. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(24)00745-1

GI.citation: Stahl et al, JCO, 2009 Stahl et al, Eur J Cancer, 2017
GI.patients: T3-4Nx GE junction adenocarcinoma Type I-III
GI.methods: ➜ pre-op cisplatin/5FU/leucovorin then 30 Gy concurrent cis/etop vs. ➜ pre-op chemo alone RT to cardiac, gastric, celiac, splenic, hepatic nodes
GI.results: Poor accrual. Terminated early. pCR 16% vs. 2% 3-yr OS 47% vs. 26%, p=0.07 5-yr OS 40% vs. 24%, p=0.055 3-yr LR 41% vs. 24%, p=0.06 5-yr LR 38% vs. 21%, p=0.03
GI.interpretation: Pre-op chemoRT improves pCR and LC, with trend to improvement in OS, compared to pre-op chemo alone.
GI.references: 1. Stahl M, Walz MK, Stuschke M, et al. Phase III Comparison of Preoperative Chemotherapy Compared With Chemoradiotherapy in Patients With Locally Advanced Adenocarcinoma of the Esophagogastric Junction. J Clin Oncol 2009;27:851-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2008.17.0506 2. Stahl M, Walz MK, Riera-Knorrenschild J, et al. Preoperative chemotherapy versus chemoradiotherapy in locally advanced adenocarcinomas of the oesophagogastric junction (POET): Long-term results of a controlled randomised trial. Eur J Cancer 2017;81:183-190. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejca.2017.04.027

GI.patients: operable T3N0-1 thoracic esophageal Ca. 90% SCC. 94% transthoracic surgery
GI.methods: ➜ Pre-op RT 46 Gy (or split course) + 5FU/cis x2 → surgery vs. ➜ chemoRT (total dose 66 Gy)
GI.results: Median OS 18 vs. 19 mos (NS) 3-mo death 9% vs. 1% 2-yr LC 65% vs. 57% FFLP 64% vs. 41% stents 5% vs. 32%
GI.interpretation: Pre-op chemoRT improves LC and FFLP compared to definitive chemoRT. ChemoRT reduced short term mortallity.
GI.commentary: Most of these patients were squamous cell carcinoma.
GI.references: 1. Bedenne L, Michel P, Bouché O, et al. Chemoradiation Followed by Surgery Compared With Chemoradiation Alone in Squamous Cancer of the Esophagus: FFCD 9102. J Clin Oncol 2007;25:1160-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2005.04.7118

GI.methods: ➜ Induction chemo → pre-op chemoRT 40 Gy → surgery vs. ➜ Induction chemo → definitive chemoRT to ≥65 Gy
GI.results: NS difference in MS (16 mos vs. 15 mos) and 3-yr OS (31% vs. 24%) 2-yr FFLP 64% surgery vs. 41% chemoRT. Treatment mortality 13% vs. 4%
GI.interpretation: Pre-op induction chemo and chemoRT followed by surgery improves LC but not OS compared to inductioon chemotherapy followed by definitive chemoRT. ChemoRT has reduced treatment related deaths.
GI.commentary: Surgery improves LC but not OS.
GI.references: 1. Stahl M, Stuschke M, Lehmann N, et al. Chemoradiation With and Without Surgery in Patients With Locally Advanced Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Esophagus. J Clin Oncol 2005;23:2310-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2005.00.034

GI.citation: van der Wilk et al, Lancet Oncol, 2025
GI.patients: Locally advanced esophageal cancer with CR after neoadjuvant chemoRT
GI.methods: Phase III noninferiority CR after preop chemoRT to 41.4 Gy + carbo/paclitaxel then→ ➜ active surveillance vs. ➜ surgery Surveillance: Endoscopy 6 weeks after surgery with no viable tumor cells on ≥4 bite on bite biopses, and 3 month endoscopy with no viable tumor cells on ≥4 bite on bite biopsies, and negative PET and EUS, and q3 months in year 1
GI.results: 2-yr OS 75% vs. 70%, noninferior 30-mos DM 43% vs. 30% During surveillance, 35% had persistant CR, 48% had isolated LR, and 17% had DM Post-op complications similar between immediate and delayed surgery
GI.interpretation: Active surveillance after chemoRT for esophageal cancer is noninferior to immediate surgery.
GI.commentary: 74% were adenocarcinoma. Additional follow up is awaited, but results at two years are promising. For many patients a major surgery is not an easy decision, and active surveillance with the option of salvage surgery provides a safe alternative.
GI.references: 1. van der Wilk BJ, Eyck BM, Wijnhoven BPL, et al. Neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy followed by active surveillance versus standard surgery for oesophageal cancer (SANO trial): a multicentre, stepped-wedge, cluster-randomised, non-inferiority, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol. Published online March 17, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(25)00027-0

GI.trial: Tianjin Medical University Cancer Institute and Hospital, Tianjin, China
GI.patients: T1-4a, N0-N3, only squamous cell histology
GI.methods: Phase II → neoadjuvant CRT 40 Gy/20 fx + docetaxel/cisplatin x4 → If cCR in 1 week, randomized: ➜ definitive CRT to total 50-60 Gy + 4-6 cycles of chemo vs. ➜ Esophagectomy and LND 4-8 weeks after nCRT
GI.results: 27% of patients achieved a cCR and were randomized 3-year DFS 55% CRT vs, 56% surgery, not different 3-year OS 2% vs. 70%, NS pCR 91% for patients who achieved a cCR
GI.interpretation: In esophageal squamous cell cancer patients who achieved a cCR to intial chemoRT, OS and DFS did not differ in those who received definitive chemoradiation alone vs. surgery
GI.commentary: Criteria for judging a cCR in this study involved contrasted CT, upper gasteroenterography, and endoscopy with at least 3 locations biopsied at the primary tumor site. Note that PET scan was not used for restaging. Assessing CR at 1 week is intriguing, and, given no change in DFS, might be as long an interval as needed. The authors stopped at a dose of 40 Gy to assess for cCR and then randomized to surgery or definitive CRT. Would the outcomes in both arms be improved if the authors assessed cCR at 50-50.4 Gy and avoided split course radiation? The FFCD 9102 trial (Bedenne 2007) randomized pat…
GI.references: 1. Qian D, Chen X, Shang X, et al. Definitive chemoradiotherapy versus neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy followed by surgery in patients with locally advanced esophageal squamous cell carcinoma who achieved clinical complete response when induction chemoradiation finished: A phase II random. Radiother Oncol 2022;174:1-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2022.06.015
GI.commentary_refs: 1. Tianjin Medical University Cancer Institute and Hospital. A Study to Compare Efficacy and Safety of DRT VS CRT Plus Surgery in Patients Who Achieved CCR for Esophageal Cancer. Available from: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03708042. Accessed October 13, 2022.

GI.citation: Herskovic et al, NEJM, 1992 Al-Sarraf et al, JCO, 1997 Cooper et al, JAMA, 1999
GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 50 Gy/25 fx + 5FU/cisplatin vs. ➜ 64 Gy alone
GI.results: 5-yr OS 26% CRT vs. 0% RT alone Median OS 14.1 vs. 9.3 mos crude DM 12% vs. 26% crude infield LR 44% vs. 65% Only 4 patients eventually underwent surgery for clinical recurrence (one had no evidence of tumor, the others died of cancer)
GI.interpretation: Adding chemo to RT leads to improved OS in unresectable esophageal cancer compared to RT alone. Chemoradiation alone results in reasonable rates of local control when surgery is not an option.
GI.commentary: The crude recurrences listed are derived from Table 2 of Herskovic 1992. The trial was designed to evaluate dose escalation, but also provides useful statistics for patients who wish to avoid or who are unable to undergo surgical resection.
GI.references: 1. Herskovic A, Martz K, Al-Sarraf M, et al. Combined Chemotherapy and Radiotherapy Compared with Radiotherapy Alone in Patients with Cancer of the Esophagus. N Engl J Med 1992;326:1593-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199206113262403 2. al-Sarraf M, Martz K, Herskovic A, et al. Progress report of combined chemoradiotherapy versus radiotherapy alone in patients with esophageal cancer: an intergroup study. J Clin Oncol 1997;15:277-84. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1997.15.1.277 3. Cooper JS, Guo MD, Herskovic A, et al. Chemoradiotherapy of Locally Advanced Esophageal Cancer: Long-term Follow-up of a Prospective Randomized Trial (RTOG 85-01). JAMA 1999;281:1623-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.281.17.1623

GI.methods: ➜🏆 50.4 Gy + 5FU/cisplatin vs. ➜ 64.8 Gy + 5FU/cisplatin
GI.results: Closed and reached futility early due to deaths in high dose arm, but prior to recieving 50.4 Gy No difference in any outcomes OS 13 vs. 18 mos (NS) 2-yr OS 31% vs. 40% (NS) LRF 56% vs. 52% (NS)
GI.interpretation: Dose escalation to 64.8 Gy from 50.4 Gy did not improve OS or LF. Chemoradiation alone results in reasonable rates of local control when surgery is not an option.
GI.commentary: The study was previously questioned for its closure for futility, but now that ARTDECO and PRODIGE 26 show no benefit to dose escalation, the case seems to be closed. At the time some criticized the study for terminating "early," but the term futility is a statistical statement, and means that statistically continuing the trial would have been futile and a waste of funding. The trial was designed to evaluate dose escalation, but also provides useful data for patients who wish to avoid or who are unable to undergo surgical resection.
GI.references: 1. Minsky BD, Pajak TF, Ginsberg RJ, et al. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2002.20.5.1167

GI.patients: T2-4, N0-3 esophageal cancer, inoperable (either medically or anatomically). M1 supraclavicle nodes allowed

GI.interpretation: Dose escalation with SIB of 61.6/50.4 Gy did not improve local control. Chemoradiation alone results in reasonable rates of local control when surgery is not an option.
GI.commentary: The trial was designed to evaluate dose escalation, but also provides useful statistics for patients who wish to avoid or who are unable to undergo surgical resection.

GI.results: Median LRPFS 16.2 vs. 18.4 mos 2-yr LRPFS 43-44% No difference in OS Median OS ~24 mos 2-yr OS ~49%, 3-yr OS ~40% 3DCRT vs. IMRT/VMAT Median OS 19 mos vs. 25 mos 2-yr OS 43% vs. 50% 3-yr OS 26% vs. 44%

GI.methods: Phase IIB ➜ IMRT 50.4 Gy vs. ➜ protons 50.4 Gy Primary endpoints: PFS and total adverse effects burden (TTB, a sum of 11 adverse effects values)
GI.results: -Mean TTB 2.3x more with IMRT -Post-op complications 7.6x more with IMRT 3-yr PFS 51%, not different 3-yr OS 45%, not different
GI.interpretation: Proton radiation therapy reduces adverse effects compared to IMRT.
GI.commentary: Arcs were not required. There is a possibility there would be no difference if IMRT arcs were used. Some have commented that for the Bayesian analysis, a highly skeptical prior should have been used instead of weakly uniform prior. Some have also commented that the endpoint of total adverse effects burden should be validated first (similar to the controversy surrounding PFS as an endpoint). On the other hand, the trial here is higher level than that used to support IMRT in many cases. Many may use the "just so" rationale for protons (it simply meets constraints when IMRT doesn't, and is more c…

Possibly different biology -CRITICS and TOPGEAR show no benefit with ad…

GI.patients: Resectable adenocarcinoma of stomach or GEJ
GI.methods: ➜ periop ECF/FLOT vs. ➜ ECF/FLOT → pre-op chemoRT 45 Gy → surgery → ECF/FLOT
GI.results: pCR 8% vs. 17% downstaged to pT1-2 in 25% vs 32% Median OS 49 vs. 46 mos, not different 3-yr OS 58% vs. 55% 5-yr OS 46% vs. 44% Median PFS 32 vs. 31 mos On subanalysis no benefit with chemoRT in any subgroup and no difference by type of chemo No difference in adverse effects
GI.interpretation: Although pCR and downstaging were improved, the addition of pre-op radiation to peri-op chemo did not improve OS.
GI.commentary: The trial originally planned to evaluate ECF, then was later rightly addended to also allow FLOT to continue to keep the trial relevant and ethical. 33% had FLOT. On subanalysis there was no benefit with chemoRT with either type of chemo.
GI.references: 1. Leong T, Smithers BM, Michael M, et al. Preoperative Chemoradiotherapy for Resectable Gastric Cancer. N Engl J Med 2024. Published online September 14, 2024. GI.patients: Stage IB-IVA gastric or gastric esophageal adenocarcinoma AJCC 6th
GI.methods: ➜ ECC or EOC (epirubicin/(cis or ox)/cape) → D1 surg → ECC vs. ➜ →ECC → D1 surg → post-op 45 Gy RT cape/cis
GI.results: 5-yr OS ~41% both arms Median OS 43 mos vs. 37 mos (p=0.9) No difference in EFS or LRC Post-op nonfebrile neutropenia 34% vs. 4% ~60% each arm started post-op therapy
GI.interpretation: There is no difference in OS with peri-op chemo compared to adjuvant chemoRT in gastric cancer. There were poor completion rates in both groups.
GI.commentary: The trial was prior to FLOT era. There was poor compliance in both arms, suggesting future trials should focus on pre-op therapy. Phase III TOPGEAR later evaluated pre-op chemoRT and found benefit in pCR and downstaging but not OS benefit. D1 dissection used in 87% Note heme adverse effects is lower in RT arm. Performed in Netherlands, Sweden, Denmark
GI.references: 1. Cats A, Jansen EPM, van Grieken NCT, et al. GI.results: Trial stopped early due to futility No difference in SOX vs. SOXRT (p=0.057) DFS is worse in S1 arm 3-yr DFS 65% vs. 78% vs. 73%

GI.results: Trend to improved DFS ~75% (p=0.09) 5-yr OS ~75% (NS) DFS benefit in node positive and intestinal subtype
GI.interpretation: There was no significant difference between adjuvant chemo and chemoRT. On subanalysis chemoRT led to DFS benefit in N+ and intestinal subtype.

GI.citation: Abrams et al, JCO, 2020 Abrams et al, ASCO, 2024
GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 50.4 Gy + 5FU/cape vs. ➜ no RT ➜ erlotinib vs. ➜ 🏆 no erlotinib
GI.results: RT randomization Median OS 2.6 vs. 2.3 yrs, not significant 2-yr OS 58% vs. 59% 5-yr OS 28% vs. 23%, NS ⬆️ Median DFS 16 vs. 12 mos ⬆️ 2-yr DFS 36% vs. 31% ⬆️ 5-yr DFS 21% vs. 15% LC not reported 14% refused chemoRT, but results were similar on ITT analysis Node negative ⬆️ 2-yr OS 76% vs. 71% ⬆️ 5-yr OS 48% vs. 29% ⬆️ 2-yr DFS 56% vs. 45% ⬆️ 5-yr DFS 47% vs. 19% Grade 3 adverse effects 38% vs. 19% Erlotinib: No difference in 3-yr OS (39%) Summary: LF NR, MS 37 mos, +M 17%
GI.interpretation: Adjuvant chemoradiation did not improve outcomes overall. In node negative patients both OS and DFS are improved. Adjuvant erlotinib did not improve OS.
GI.commentary: Impressively OS is improved with adjuvant radiation in node negative patients. The trial took years to accrue and mature however, and since its inception new systemic therapies have been developed including FOLFIRINOX and nab-paclitaxel. If the patient still has good performance status after chemotherapy, it seems reasonable to offer chemoradiation. Dose constraints: Stomach and small bowel max ≤56 Gy, D15% <50% OS is much improved in both arms from prior trials evaluating post-operative radiotherapy. The authors suspect this may be due to better imaging criteria to determine those who are res…

GI.subtopic: Addition of RT to gem? No
GI.methods: Phase II: ➜ 50.4 Gy conc Gem vs. ➜ Gem alone
GI.results: Median OS 24 mos in both arms LF 11% vs. 24%, improved with RT Summary: LF 11%, MS 24 mos, +M 0%
GI.interpretation: ChemoRT is feasible and well tolerated. There is LC benefit with RT but no OS benefit.
GI.commentary: Small study. Phase II and likely underpowered. No survival benefit to RT but 13% LC benefit. Modern techniques are used. Concurrent gem has higher adverse effects. Low rates of positive margins.
GI.references: 1. van Laethem JL, Hammel P, Mornex F, et al. GI.subtopic: Adjuvant chemo, RT, chemoRT, or obs? Chemo, not RT
GI.methods: ➜ 40 Gy split conc 5FU (EORTC) vs. ➜ 5FU alone vs. ➜ chemoRT → chemo (GITSG) vs. ➜ obs
GI.results: 5-yr OS 10% chemoRT vs. 20% without 5-yr OS 21% chemo vs. 8% without Summary: MS 20 mos, +M 19%
GI.interpretation: Adjuvant chemo improved OS, and adjuvant chemoRT worsened OS.
GI.commentary: In original design, not all patients were randomized. No RT quality assurance. Option of up to 60 Gy instead of 40. Only 70% had full dose. Optional "background therapy".
GI.references: 1. Neoptolemos JP, Stocken DD, Friess H, et al. A Randomized Trial of Chemoradiotherapy and Chemotherapy after Resection of Pancreatic Cancer. N Engl J Med 2004;350:1200-10. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa032295

GI.methods: ➜ Split-course 40 Gy conc 5-FU vs. ➜ obs
GI.results: No significant difference in 2-yr PFS or OS. Trend to benefit with RT. Median OS 17 mos vs. 13 mos 2-yr OS 37% vs. 23%, p=0.09 10-yr OS 18% (NS) (8% in head, 29% in periampullary) Exploratory analysis: OS benefit in pancreatic head tumors, one sided p=0.049 LR 20% both arms Summary: LF 20%, MS 17 mos, +M 19%
GI.interpretation: Adjuvant CRT led to no OS or PFS benefit over obs. On exploratory analysis there is possible benefit in pancreatic head tumors.
GI.commentary: Since periampullary tumors are better prognosis, an exploratory subanalysis was performed based on tumor location. A two-sided t test showed no OS benefit with RT in pancreatic head tumors, p=0.099. However in a letter to the editor, Garofalo et al asserted that a one-sided t test is more appropriate: this was significant for OS benefit of RT with p=0.049.

GI.notes: Pancreas summary: -GITSG shows benefit with chemoRT over obs, but no chemoRT benefit in EORTC. -ESPAC-1 and later EORTC-FFCD-GERCOR (small study) show no OS benefit with RT, but some LC benefit. GI.references: 1. Kaiser MH, Ellenberg SS. Pancreatic Cancer: Adjuvant Combined Radiation and Chemotherapy Following Curative Resection. JAMA Surg 1985;120:899-903. Gastrointestinal Tumor Study Group. Cancer 1987;59:2006-10. PMID: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3567862

GI.methods: ➜ adj gem vs. ➜ 🏆 mFOLFIRINOX
GI.results: mFOLFIRINOX improves DFS, DM, and OS Median DFS 12.8 mos vs. 21.4 mos 5-yr DFS 19% vs. 26% Median OS 35.5 mos vs. 53.5 mos 5-yr OS 31% vs. 43% Median DMFS 17.7 mos vs. 29.4 mos 5-yr DM 54% vs. 37% Median CSS 36.3 mos vs. 54.7 mos Summary: LF NR, MS 54 mos, +M ~43%
GI.interpretation: Adjuvant mFOLFIRINOX improves DFS, DMFS, and OS compared to gemcitabine.
GI.commentary: mFOLFIRINOX is given q14 days x 12 cycles.

GI.subtopic: Adjuvant 5FU vs. gem? gem RT with both
GI.citation: Regine et al, JAMA, 2008 Regine et al, Ann Surg Oncol, 2011
GI.patients: Gross total resection of adenoCA 35% with positive margins (highest among trials)
GI.methods: ➜ pre and post gem vs. ➜ pre and post 5FU 50.4 Gy to elective nodes and post-op bed with concurrent 5FU for all
GI.results: Gem trended to improved OS panc head 3-yr OS 31% vs. 22%, p=0.09 5-yr OS 22% vs. 18% 5FU, p=0.08 Median OS 20.5 mos vs. 17.1 mos First relapse: LR 23% vs. 28%, DM 75 vs. 71% Grade 3+ heme 58% gem vs. 9% 5FU Little difference in non heme grade 3+ (~59%), neutropenia or infection, diarrhea (15 vs. 19%), mucositis (10% vs. 15%), N/V (~10%) Summary: LF 23%, MS 21 mos, +M 35%
GI.interpretation: Adding peri-op gemcitabine to RT with concurrent 5-FU led to a trend in improved OS.
GI.commentary: Subanalysis showed improved survival with per protocol RT Twice as many patients with positive margins than CONKO
GI.references: 1. Regine WF, Winter KA, Abrams RA, et al. Fluorouracil vs Gemcitabine Chemotherapy Before and After Fluorouracil-Based Chemoradiation Following Resection of Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma: A Randomized Controlled Trial. JAMA 2008;299:1019-26. GI.patients: pancreas R0/R1, no patients with CEA >2.5 ULN
GI.methods: ➜ obs vs. ➜ 🏆 gemcitabine 6 cycles
GI.results: Improved Median OS 22 mos gem vs. 20 mos OS 23% vs. 12% 10 yr DFS 14% vs. 6% Summary: MS 22 mos, +M 19%
GI.interpretation: Adjuvant gemicitabine improves OS.
GI.commentary: Very good results in this trial; of note amount of positive margins lower here than in ESPAC and RTOG 9704 (Neoptolemos 2001, Regine 2008).
GI.references: 1. Oettle H, Post S, Neuhaus P, et al. Adjuvant Chemotherapy With Gemcitabine vs Observation in Patients Undergoing Curative-Intent Resection of Pancreatic Cancer: A Randomized Controlled Trial. JAMA 2007;297:267-77. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.297.3.267

GI.methods: Review and pooled analysis of 19 SBRT studies
GI.results: 1-yr LRC 72%, 1-yr OS 52%, Median OS 17 mos (range 5.7-47 mos) Severe adverse effects <10% Borderline resected in 50-56% and unresectable removed in 0-20% LRC correlated to dose and # fractions
GI.interpretation: SBRT seems to show favorable outcomes. Randomized trials are needed.
GI.references: 1. Petrelli F, Comito T, Ghidini A, et al. Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy for Locally Advanced Pancreatic Cancer: A Systematic Review and Pooled Analysis of 19 Trials. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2017;97:313-22. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2016.10.030

GI.subtopic: Addition of RT to chemo? LC benefit, not OS Induction erlotinib? No
GI.methods: ➜ induction gem vs. ➜ induction gem + erlotinib if disease controlled → ➜ further chemo vs. ➜ 54 Gy 3DCRT conc cape (no ENI)
GI.results: • RT randomization No difference in OS Median OS 16.5 mos chemo vs. 15.2 mos chemoRT, p=0.83 RT improved LC, 68% vs. 54%, p=0.03 PFS 9.9 mos RT vs. 8.4 mos, p=0.06 No increase in Grade 3-4 adverse effects with RT except for nausea • Erlotinib randomization No improvement in OS adverse effects was increased
GI.interpretation: No improvement in OS with the addition of RT or erlotinib to gemcitabine alone. RT improved LC.
GI.commentary: 3DCRT used, not IMRT. Similar to EORTC/RTOG currently open trial for resectable panc. 18% major deviations with RT. 9% randomized to radiation received no RT. 3% RT records not available. Some assert that RT would be sensitized better with 5-FU/cape or with FOLFIRINOX. SBRT trials are ongoing. Radiation planning: 3DCRT in all. ENI was not done There was no dose constraint for small bowel
GI.notes: -There is some controversy regarding the role of radiation in unresectable pancreatic cancer -An early trial, GITSG, showed OS benefit with addition of RT. Another trial, FFCD-SFRO, found worse OS with RT -The best powered trial, LAP07, shows no OS difference with addition of RT -LAP07 did show LC benefit with RT -Better advances have been made in chemotherapy than with RT, such as with FOLFIRINOX and nab-paclitaxel -Data supporting the role of SBRT in pancreatic cancer is emerging, with phase II data now available -Potential roles for RT include: borderline tumors, unresectable tumors not rec…
GI.references: 1. Hammel P, Huguet F, van Laethem J-L, et al. Effect of Chemoradiotherapy vs Chemotherapy on Survival in Patients With Locally Advanced Pancreatic Cancer Controlled After 4 Months of Gemcitabine With or Without Erlotinib: The LAP07 Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA 2016;315:1844-53. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2016.4324 Study protocol in supplements, dose constraints p24: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2016.4324

GI.subtopic: Addition of RT to gem? Yes
GI.methods: ➜ gem alone vs. ➜ 50.4 Gy RT + gem then consolidation gem
GI.results: Underpowered, terminated early Median OS 9.2 mos vs. 11 mos CRT (p=0.017) Grade 4-5 toxicity worse with CRT 9% vs. 41%
GI.interpretation: CRT with gem seems to result in improved OS over gem alone. The trial is likely underpowered.
GI.references: 1. Loehrer PJ, Sr., Feng Y, Cardenes H, et al. Gemcitabine alone versus gemcitabine plus radiotherapy in patients with locally advanced pancreatic cancer: an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group trial. J Clin Oncol 2011;29:4105-12. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2011.34.8904

GI.subtopic: Addition of RT to chemo? Unclear
GI.methods: ➜ 60 Gy + 5FU/cis vs. ➜ Gem alone mainteance Gem in both arms
GI.results: • RT randomization No difference in OS Median OS 16.5 mos chemo vs. 15.2 mos chemoRT, p=0.83 RT improved LC, 68% vs. 54%, p=0.03 PFS 9.9 mos RT vs. 8.4 mos, p=0.06 No increase in Grade 3-4 adverse effects with RT except for nausea • Erlotinib randomization No improvement in OS Adverse effects were increased
GI.interpretation: CRT with 5FU/cis results leads to worse OS over gem alone.
GI.commentary: Critisism: Concurrent cisplatin with RT+5FU was too toxic
GI.references: 1. Chauffert B, Mornex F, Bonnetain F, et al. Phase III trial comparing intensive induction chemoradiotherapy (60 Gy, infusional 5-FU and intermittent cisplatin) followed by maintenance gemcitabine with gemcitabine alone for locally advanced unresectable pancreatic cancer. GI.subtopic: Addition of RT to chemo? Yes
GI.methods: ➜ SMF (streptozocin, mitomycin, 5FU) vs. ➜ 54 Gy conc 5FU then SMF
GI.results: 1-yr OS 19% vs. 42% with RT
GI.interpretation: CRT with 5FU->SMF results in improved OS over SMF alone
GI.references: 1. Treatment of locally unresectable carcinoma of the pancreas: comparison of combined-modality therapy (chemotherapy plus radiotherapy) to chemotherapy alone. Gastrointestinal Tumor Study Group. J Natl Cancer Inst 1988;80:751-5. PMID: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/2898536/

GI.methods: 12 wks induction gem+cape then if stable disease or response, more chemo, then randomized: ➜ Gem + 50.4 Gy RT vs. ➜ Cape + 50.4 Gy RT
GI.results: PFS improved with capecitabine 9-mos PFS 63% vs. 51% (primary endpoint) Median PFS 12.0 mos vs. 10.4 mos, p=.11 Median OS 15.2 mos vs. 13.4 mos 1-yr OS 79% vs. 64% No grade 3-4 heme in cape arm vs. 18% with gem Non heme adverse effects 12% vs. 26% RT completed in 74% cape vs. 68% gem
GI.interpretation: There is a trend to PFS benefit in favor of capecitabine added to RT over gemcitabine, though results were nonsignificant.
GI.references: 1. Mukherjee S, Hurt CN, Bridgewater J, et al. Gemcitabine-based or capecitabine-based chemoradiotherapy for locally advanced pancreatic cancer (SCALOP): a multicentre, randomised, phase 2 trial. Lancet Oncol 2013;14:317-26. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(13)70021-4

GI.methods: ➜ 60 Gy vs. 40 Gy + 5FU vs. ➜ 60 Gy + 5FU Split course RT
GI.results: 1-yr OS 40% CRT vs. 10% without chemo. No significant difference b/w dose levels. Median OS: 10 mos.
GI.interpretation: CRT with 5FU results in improved OS over RT alone.
GI.references: 1. Moertel CG, Frytak S, Hahn RG, et al. Therapy of locally unresectable pancreatic carcinoma: a randomized comparison of high dose (6000 rads) radiation alone, moderate dose radiation (4000 rads + 5-fluorouracil), and high dose radiation + 5-fluorouracil: The Gastrointestinal Tumor Study Group. Cancer 1981;48:1705-10. PMID: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/7284971

GI.patients: resectable and borderline resectable pancreatic cancer
GI.methods: ➜ FOLFIRINOX x 8 →Surgery vs. ➜ gem 1000 mg/m2 x3 cycles + 36 Gy/ 15 fx during cycle 2 → surgery → adjuvant gem x4
GI.results: Median OS 21.9 mos vs. 21.3 mos, p=0.28 Resection rates 77% vs. 75% Serious adverse events 49% vs. 43%, p=0.26
GI.interpretation: Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX without radiation did not improve OS or resection rates compared to neoadjuvant gemcitabine with radiation followed by adjuvant gem.
GI.commentary: The benefit of radiation is difficult to interpret given that different chemotherapy regimens were used in each arm. There does not appear to be benefitt with FOLFIRINOX over gem. The trial was superiority, not noninferiority. Per this (not ideal) trial design, gemcitabine plus radiation would remain standard of care. QUANTEC dose constraints were recommended in the protocol, and thus dose constraints for stomach and small bowel were not intended to be met.
GI.references: 1. Janssen QP, van Dam JL, van Bekkum ML, et al. Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX versus neoadjuvant gemcitabine-based chemoradiotherapy in resectable and borderline resectable pancreatic cancer (PREOPANC-2): a multicentre, open-label, phase 3 randomised trial. Lancet Oncol. Published online September 10, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(25)00363-8 Protocol pdf [dose constraints p40]: https://dpcg.nl/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/C1_Onderzoeksprotocol_PREOPANC-2_versie_5_dd_06-09-2019.pdf

GI.methods: Phase II ➜ 🏆induction mFOLFIRINOX x8 → surgery → FOLFOX x4 vs. ➜ induction mFOLFIRINOX x7 → SBRT (33-40 Gy/5 fx) or hypofractioned IGRT (25 Gy/5 fx) → surgery → FOLFOX x4 Interim analysis: termination of an arm if R0 ≤11/30 patients (37%)
GI.results: Interim analysis mandated closure of RT arm due to low R0. The chemo only arm proceeded to full enrollment Overall R0: 57% and 33% Proceeded to surgery: 58% and 51% Of those who underwent surgery: R0: 88% and 74% pCR: 0 and 2% 18-mo OS: 67% and 47% (unpowered) Median OS: 29.8 and 17.1 mos Median EFS: 15.0 and 10.2 mos
GI.interpretation: The RT arm reached futility in R0 and was terminated early. See commentary. The chemo only induction arm showed favorable OS and R0 outcomes.
GI.commentary: Was the trial powered and designed appropriately? The only 2 patients to achieve pCR in the trial were in the RT arm, even after the chemo only arm reached full accrual. The arms were imbalanced, which may be reflected in the higher OS for the no RT arm. Several factors favored the no RT arm including age, CA19-9 at diagnosis, hepatic artery resections, and disease progression before completion of therapy (Chuong 2022). Because the RT arm did not reach full accrual, the outcomes here cannot be directly statistically compared; this is why no p-values are provided in the study. The author's conc…
GI.references: 1. Katz MHG, Shi Q, Meyers J, et al. Efficacy of Preoperative mFOLFIRINOX vs mFOLFIRINOX Plus Hypofractionated Radiotherapy for Borderline Resectable Adenocarcinoma of the Pancreas: The A021501 Phase 2 Randomized Clinical Trial [published online ahead of print, 2022 Jul 14]. JAMA Oncol 2022;10.1001/jamaoncol.2022.2319. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2022.2319 Protocol pdf in supplements: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2022.2319
GI.commentary_refs: 1. Chuong MD, Anker CJ, Buckstein MH, et al. Hits and Misses in Novel Pancreatic and Rectal Cancer Treatment Options. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2023;115:545-552. doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2022.10.022

GI.patients: nonresectable and borderline resectable pancreatic cancer
GI.methods: →induction FOLFIRINOX x6 or gem 1000 mg/m2 x3 cycles [77% had FOLFIRINOX]→ if no progression, randomized: ➜ additional FOLFIRINOX x6 or gem 1000 mg/m2 x3 cycles →surgery if operable vs. ➜ 50.4 Gy RT with concurrent weekly gem 300 mg/m2 →gem 1000 mg/m2 x1 cycle →surgery if operable
GI.results: 36% in both arms proceeded to surgery In those that had surgery: ⬆️ R0 50% vs. 69% ⬆️ pCR also improved with CRT No OS change in overall population If, CA 19-9 levels decreased, OS benefit with chemoRT: 4% vs. 12% R0 rate overall 18% vs. 25%, p=.113
GI.interpretation: In those that underwent surgery, CRT improved R0 and pCR. Overall, R0 was not different but there was trend toward improvement. There is no OS benefit to additional chemotherapy. If CA 19-9 levels respond, there is an OS benefit to radiation.
GI.commentary: The OS benefit with CRT in CA 19-9 response was clarified in an errata. However there was not an OS benefit in the overall population. Hopefully future developments will help identify what tumors will benefit most from radiotherapy.
GI.references: 1. Fietkau R, Ghadimi M, Grützmann R, et al. Benefit of Chemoradiotherapy Versus Chemotherapy After Induction Therapy for Conversion of Unresectable Into Resectable Pancreatic Cancer: The Randomized CONKO-007 Trial. J Clin Oncol. Published online August 13, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-24-01502
GI.commentary_refs: 1. Fietkau R, Grützmann R, Wittel UA, et al. R0 resection following chemo (radio)therapy improves survival of primary inoperable pancreatic cancer patients. Interim results of the German randomized CONKO-007± trial. Strahlenther Onkol. 2021;197(1):8-18. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/s00066-020-01680-2

GI.citation: Versteijne et al, JCO, 2020 Versteijne et al, JCO, 2022
GI.patients: resectable and borderline resectable pancreatic cancer
GI.methods: ➜ Surgery → adjuvant gem x6 vs. ➜ 🏆 gem 1000 mg/m2 x3 cycles + 36 Gy/ 15 fx during cycle 2 → surgery → adjuvant gem x4
GI.results: ⬆️ ITT R0 28% vs. 41% DFS and LRF also improved ⬆️ 5-yr OS 7% vs. 21% ⬆️ Median OS 14.3 vs. 15.7 mos On subgroup analysis, neoadjuvant treatment was favored in all groups, with even greater benefit in borderline resectable
GI.interpretation: Pre-operative gemcitabine followed by RT with concurrent gemcitabine before surgery improves R0 resection rates and OS compared to adjuvant gem only.
GI.commentary: This trial supports neoadjuvant treatment for resectable pancreatic cancer in general, not just borderline. There was much higher chemo compliance in the pre-op arm: Pre-op received median 7 cycles, and adjuvant median only 2 cycles. FOLFIRINOX however is now the preferred regimen. Any radiation technique was allowed, from 3DCRT to stereotactic. CTV 5 mm, 4DCT recommended for PTV 10 mm Dose constraints: PTV D98% > 95% QUANTEC (bowel and stomach not met) Mean kidney <18 Gy Liver mean <30 Gy (higher dose allowed to small volume)
GI.references: 1. Versteijne E, Suker M, Groothuis K, et al. Preoperative Chemoradiotherapy Versus Immediate Surgery for Resectable and Borderline Resectable Pancreatic Cancer: Results of the Dutch Randomized Phase III PREOPANC Trial. J Clin Oncol 2020;JCO1902274. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.19.02274 Protocol pdf: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.19.02274/suppl_file/protocol_jco.19.02274-1.pdf 2. Versteijne E, van Dam JL, Suker M, et al. Neoadjuvant Chemoradiotherapy Versus Upfront Surgery for Resectable and Borderline Resectable Pancreatic Cancer: Long-Term Results of the Dutch Randomized PREOPANC Trial [published online ahead of print, 2022 Jan 27]. J Clin Oncol 2022;JCO2102233. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.21.02233

GI.patients: pancreatic cancer with 1-5 oligometastasis ≤4 prior lines of systemic therapy allowed
GI.methods: Phase II ➜ 🏆 Local treatment to all sites plus systemic therapy vs. ➜ systemic therapy At least one site must be treated with radiation. Only definitive doses were permitted.
GI.results: ⬆️ Median PFS 10.3 vs. 2.5 mos ⬆️ 1-yr PFS 42% vs. 9% Immune activation events were associated with MDT Most common doses were 50 Gy/4 fx and 70 Gy/10 fx. Most common dose to primary was 40 Gy/ 5 fx.
GI.interpretation: Metastasis directed therapy for oligometatic pancreatic cancer improves PFS and is associated with immune systemic activation.
GI.references: 1. Ludmir EB, Sherry AD, Fellman BM, et al. Addition of Metastasis-Directed Therapy to Systemic Therapy for Oligometastatic Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma (EXTEND): A Multicenter, Randomized Phase II Trial. J Clin Oncol. Published online August 5, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.24.00081 Protocol pdf: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.24.00081/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.24.00081.pdf

GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 nab-paclitaxel then gem vs. ➜ gem alone
GI.results: OS benefit with nabpaclitaxel Median OS 8.5 mos vs. 6.7 mos OS 35% vs. 22% PFS 5.5 mos vs. 3.7 mos
GI.interpretation: Nab-paclitaxel improves OS in metastatic pancreatic cancer.
GI.references: 1. Von Hoff DD, Ervin T, Arena FP, et al. Increased Survival in Pancreatic Cancer with nab-Paclitaxel plus Gemcitabine. N Engl J Med 2013;369:1691-703. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1304369

GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 FOLFIRNOX vs. ➜ gem
GI.results: Benefit with FOLFIRINOX in Median OS 11.1 mos vs. 6.8 Objective response 32% vs. 9%
GI.interpretation: FOLFIRINOX improves OS and has lower adverse effects than gemcitabine in metastatic pancreatic cancer.

GI.results: 10-yr OS 67% vs. 72% Stage III OS 59% vs. 67% No benefit in Stage II (OS ~80%) 5-yr DFS 73% vs. 67%

J Clin Oncol 2015;33:4176‐4187. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.63.4238

GI.patients: Resectable liver metastases from colorectal cancer
GI.methods: ➜ perioperative FOLFOX vs. ➜ surgery alone
GI.results: PFS benefit seen on initial publication, but lost on update. Median OS 54 mos vs. 51 mos, trend toward benefit with peri-op
GI.interpretation: There is trend to benefit in OS with FOLFOX and no long term benefit to PFS, although a PFS benefit was seen on earlier f/u.
GI.references: 1. Nordlinger B, Sorbye H, Glimelius B, et al. Perioperative FOLFOX4 chemotherapy and surgery versus surgery alone for resectable liver metastases from colorectal cancer (EORTC 40983): long-term results of a randomised, controlled, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2013;14:1208-15. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(13)70447-9

GI.patients: Resected colon cancer patients who are T4 at any location or T3N1/T3N2 in ascending or descending colon
GI.methods: ➜ 5FU and levamisole alone vs. ➜ with radiation therapy
GI.results: Underpowered, terminated early No change in OS or DFS 5-yr OS 62% vs. 58% (NS) 5-yr DFS 51% in both (NS) Grade ≥3 adverse effects 42% vs. 54% (p=0.04)
GI.interpretation: Adjuvant RT does not benefit OS or DFS in advanced colon cancer. However the trial had low power and was terminated early.
GI.commentary: Trial was terminated due to poor accrual (222 out of planned 700 patients). Therefore power was insufficient. LC not assessed. RT was delivered to PA lymph nodes. T3N1 may be too low risk. Chemotherapy used is no longer current. No pre-op imaging or clips required to locate tumor. Margins often not assessed on pathology. Is there a benefit with radiation therapy with modern technique? One obstacle is that small bowel is often adjacent or adherent to site.
GI.references: 1. Martenson JA Jr, Willett CG, Sargent DJ, et al. Phase III Study of Adjuvant Chemotherapy and Radiation Therapy Compared With Chemotherapy Alone in the Surgical Adjuvant Treatment of Colon Cancer: Results of Intergroup Protocol 0130. J Clin Oncol 2004;22:3277-83. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2004.01.029

GI.patients: T3-4 with liver infiltration (2-5 cm), N1, obstructive jaundice (type I/II biliary obstruction), duodenal or colonic abutment with no mucosal infiltration <180° vascular involvement
GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 neoadjuvant 55-57 Gy/ 25 fractions plus concurrent & adjuvant cis/gemcitabine x2 → surgery vs. ➜ cisplatin plus gemcitabine x4 → surgery Radiation: Arc IMRT required. 55-57 Gy to gross disease and 45 Gy SIB to CTV including lymphatics
GI.results: 5-yr OS 27% vs. 18% Median OS 21.8 vs. 10.1 mos 5-yr EFS 21% vs. 13% R0 52% vs. 30% Grade ≥3 post-op morbidity 28% vs. 18%, p=0.30
GI.interpretation: Neoadjuvant concurrent chemoradiation improves OS and R0 resection rates in locally advanced gallbladder cancer.
GI.commentary: Concurrent chemo was weekly gemicitabine 300 mg/m² Radiation planning CTV = areas of suspected microscopic disease in the liver and lymph nodes at the peri-coledochal, cystic duct, retroportal, along the common hepatic artery, along the hepatoduodenal ligament, pancreaticoduodenal, hilar, periportal, portacaval and retropancreatic regions PTV 0.5-7 cm Liver V30 <70%, mean <25 Gy Kidney each V20 <70%, mean <18 Gy Small bowel V15 <190cc Duodenum V55 <1cc, V50 <4cc PTV V95% ≥95%
GI.references: 1. Engineer R, Goel M, Ostwa V, et al. A phase III randomized clinical trial evaluating perioperative therapy (neoadjuvant chemotherapy versus chemoradiotherapy) in locally advanced gallbladder cancers (POLCAGB). J Clin Oncol 2025;48:s. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2025.43.16_suppl.4007 Study protocol: https://doi.org/10.1136/bmjopen-2018-028147 2. Engineer R, Patkar S, Lewis SC, et al. A phase III randomised clinical trial of perioperative therapy (neoadjuvant chemotherapy versus chemoradiotherapy) in locally advanced gallbladder cancers (POLCAGB): study protocol. BMJ Open 2019;9:e028147. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1136/bmjopen-2018-028147

GI.patients: advanced unresectable gallbladder carcinoma, T3-4 N1-2, liver mets of segment IV or V, or 1-2 oligomets, with PR or stable disease after chemo. 10% had liver metastasis
GI.methods: cisplatin plus gemcitabine x4 → PR or stable disease→ ➜ 🏆 45 Gy/25 fractions plus 9 Gy boost 3DCRT with capecitabine vs. ➜ observation
GI.results: Median OS 10 vs. 4 mos Median OS from accrual 13 vs. 7 mos Grade 3-4 adverse events RILD 13%, GI bleed 5.8%, anemia 9%, nausea 3%
GI.interpretation: Chemoradiation improves OS in unresectable gallbladder cancer.
GI.commentary: The authors explain that PET was not required because the study was not funded. Eligibility was originally for nonmetastatic only, but then expanded due to slow accrual during COVID. GI bleed of 5.8% is high and may be able to be improved with 4DCT and sparing of bowel over PTV. Radiation planning: 45 Gy to tumor (including any liver mets), liver infiltration, periportal, celiac, superior mesenteric and PA lymph-nodes inferiorly to L2 plus 9 Gy boost to tumor. CTV 0.5 cm and PTV 1 cm. Dose constraints: stomach and bowel D50 <4cc, mean liver minus GTV <30 Gy, mean kidney <18 Gy
GI.references: 1. Agrawal S, Kapoor V, Rahul R, Singh A, Mishra P, Saxena R. GI.citation: Ben-Josef et al, JCO, 2015 Gholami et al, Ann Surg Oncol, 2023
GI.patients: extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma or gallbladder carcinoma, pT2-T4, or N+, or R1
GI.methods: Phase II ➜ adjuvant capecitabine+gem → 45 Gy RT LNs and tumor bed plus boost to 54-59 Gy with concurrent capecitabine
GI.results: 2-yr OS 65%, median OS 35 mos 2-yr OS 71% N0, 61% N+ 2-yr DFS 63% N0, 50% N+ LR in 14 pts, DM in 24 pts, combined relapse in 9 pts In N+, 2-yr DFS 50% vs. 30% in historical controls
GI.interpretation: ChemoRT produced favorable results and low adverse effects. In those node positive, results were improved compared to historical controls. Randomized trials are warranted.
GI.references: 1. Ben-Josef E, Guthrie KA, El-Khoueiry AB, et al. GI.patients: Intra- or extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma or gallbladder carcinoma (all stages)
GI.methods: ➜ adj capecitabine x6 mos vs. ➜ obs Primary endpoint: OS
GI.results: ⬆️ OS and RFS in per-protocol analysis ITT Median OS 51.1 vs. 36.4 mos, p=0.097 PP Median OS 53 vs. 36 mos, p=0.028 ITT Median RFS 24.4 vs 17.5 mos Serious AE in 21% vs 10%
GI.interpretation: Adj capecitabine shows OS benefit in this PP analysis, but not in ITT. Adj capecitable following resection could be considered a treatment option in resected biliary tract cancer.
GI.commentary: Results were not significant (p=0.097) in ITT analysis, but this included patients allocated to chemo arm who did not receive tx, hence pre-specified per protocol analysis. There should be some caution regarding the results, which could be biased based on what type of patients dropped out of the chemo arm (perhaps a few with poor performance status decided to drop out of the chemo arm). Authors also note that no diff in RFS b/w 24 & 60 mos, suggesting possibility of delayed recurrence in capecitabine arm. Chemo sched: 1250 mg/m2 BID day 1-14 of 3w cycle for 24wks
GI.references: 1. Primrose JN, Fox RP, Palmer DH, et al. GI.patients: HCC size >1 cm unsuitable or refractory to TACE or drug eluting beads, unsuitable for surgery, transplant, or RFA
GI.methods: ➜ 🏆 SBRT + sorafenib vs. ➜ sorafenib SBRT to 27.5-50 Gy directed by mean liver doses achievable [median dose was 35 Gy]
GI.results: ✅ Median OS 15.8 vs. 12.3 mos, p=0.06, and p=0.04 after adjustment for stratification factors ✅ Median PFS 9.2 vs. 5.5 mos ⬆️ 6-mos improved QOL in 35% vs. 10% No difference in grade 3+ adverse effects, ~45% 74% had macrovascular invasion 82% were BCLC Stage C (=portal invasion, N1, M1, or ECOG 1-2)
GI.interpretation: SBRT for HCC tumors that were not candidates for TACE improved OS, PFS, and TTP. The vast majority had macrovascular invasion and were poor prognosis, BCLC Stage C. Large volume tumors were permitted.
GI.commentary: Large tumor volume was permitted in this study: total tumor volume up to 20 cm, single size 15 cm, and up to 5 mets! SBRT easily can be delivered first, followed by immunotherapy, or the two could potentially be given confurrently. RTOG 1112 was terminated early, possibly due to enthusiasm for immunotherapy at the time. The statistical power of 1112 was modified. In a study evaluating pre-op immuntherapy in HCC, pCR was only 18% (D'Alessio 2024). The IMBrave trial, randomizing atezolumab + bev vs. sorafenib showed OS & PFS benefit with immunotherapy, median OS 19.2 vs. 13.4 mos and median PFS …

GI.patients: untreated HCC meeting Milan or San Francisco transplant criteria
GI.methods: Phase III ➜ 🏆 70.2 Gy/ 15 fx protons vs. ➜ TACE, repeated until CR or max response
GI.results: Median PFS not reached vs. 12 mos Day hospitalized 24 vs. 166 2-yr OS 68%, not different TACE had more grade 3 adverse effects and more abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Protons had more fatigue and erythema
GI.interpretation: Proton radiation used as bridge to transplant has improved PFS over TACE
GI.commentary: CTV 1 cm, PTV 0.5 com Liver V30 <33% If any bowel was within 90% dose, the plan was modified at 8 fx to be within 50% R kidney V20 <50%
GI.references: 1. Bush DA, Volk M, Smith JC, et al. Proton beam radiotherapy versus transarterial chemoembolization for hepatocellular carcinoma: Results of a randomized clinical trial. Cancer 2023;129:3554-3563. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.34965

GI.patients: Any HCC eligible for TACE, bridging permitted but not required
GI.methods: Phase II, multi-institutional ➜ 🏆 SBRT 48-54 Gy/ 6 fx vs. ➜ TACE drug eluting beads
GI.results: Closed early due to slow accrual Median time to LF >40 vs. 12 mos, p=0.075 2-yr LC 100% vs. 44% Median TTP 19 vs. 12 mos, p=0.15 Median OS 44 vs. 37 mos, p=0.36
GI.interpretation: In this small trial, SBRT seemed to result in improved LC over TACE-DEB.
GI.commentary: Although a small trial, it represents the highest level of evidence comparing SBRT and TACE. Liver minus GTV mean <22 Gy, V23.4 Gy < 800 cc, NTCP <5% Bowel and stomach max <39 Gy, V30 Gy <5cc Kidney D2/3 <19.2 Gy
GI.references: 1. Romero AM, van der Holt B, Willemssen FEJA, et al. GI.methods: Phase II ➜ 🏆 SBRT bridge vs. ➜ DEB-TACE bridge
GI.results: 12-mos time to retreatment 0% vs. 39% 2 hospital days 0% vs. 87% Pain 73% vs. 87% Nausea 36% vs. 47% Fatigue 73% vs. 93%
GI.interpretation: SBRT seems to have better control and less adverse effects as bridge to liver transplantation in this small phase II study. Phase III randomized trials are warranted.

GI.interpretation: SBRT results in better LPFS than RFA.

GI.trial: National Cancer Center, Goyang, Republic of Korea
GI.methods: Noninferiority ➜ protons 66 GyE/ 10 fx daily vs. ➜ RFA crossover premitted if technique not feasible
GI.results: Regimens were noninferior Per protocol 2-yr LPFS 95% vs. 84% ITT 2-yr LPFS 93% vs. 83% 3- and 4-yr LPFS also noninferior Adverse effects RFA: abdominal pain 30%, inc ALT 96% protons: Pneumonitis 33%, dec WBCs 24%
GI.interpretation: Protons are noninferior to RFA in HCC.
GI.commentary: PTV 5-7 mm with gating. If the assigned treatment was not feasible, crossover was premitted, which is why both PP and ITT are presented. Six assigned to protons had RFA, and 19 assigned to RFA had protons.

GI.results: 1-yr OS 75% vs. 43% 2-yr OS 27% vs. 9% PR 21%, CR 0% IL-6 was higher in progression 2-yr DFS 13% vs. 3% RT caused grade 3 adverse effects in 2 patients that led to inoperability

GI.patients: embolization eligible unresectable HCC, Child-Pugh A to B7
GI.methods: ➜ durvalumab + bevacizumab + TACE vs. ➜ durvalumab + TACE vs. ➜ TACE TACE could be cTACE or DEB-TACE
GI.results: D+B+TACE vs. D+TACE vs. TACE Median PFS 15.0 vs. 10.0 vs. 8.2 mos (significant for D+B+TACE) 1-yr PFS 55% vs. 44% vs. 40% 2-yr PFS 32% vs. 25% vs. 25% ORR 44% vs. 41% vs. 30% Median TTP 22.1 vs. 14.0 vs. 16.4 mos Any grade 3-4 45% vs. 28% vs. 23% Any study related grade 3-4 27% vs. 6% vs. 6% Any adverse event with outcome of death 10% vs. 9% vs. 6% Treatment related AE with death 0% vs. 1% vs. 2% Any adverse event leading to d/c 28% vs. 13% vs. 8% Treatment related AE leading to d/c 12% vs. 3% vs. 3%
GI.interpretation: Durvalumab + bevacizumab + TACE improves PFS over TACE. However there were high grade 3-5 events in the study, and it was difficult to ascertain which were due to intervention.
GI.commentary: The ORR was only 44%, which seems worse than SBRT (2-yr LPFS 93% in Xi 2024, disease control 75% in RTOG 1112, and 2-yr LC 100% in TRENDY). There is concern that bevacizumab masks true progression. In the HIMALAYA trial tremelimumab + durvalumab, without TACE, shows an OS benefit compared to sorafinib. Refer to the Discussion section of the study for commentary on adverse effects. According to the authors, the investigator determined whether an adverse event was due to treatment or not. For example bevacizumab has risk of hemorrhage, and many liver patients already have esophageal varices, and…
GI.references: 1. Sangro B, Kudo M, Erinjeri JP, et al. GU.patients: cT2-T4N0M0 muscle-invasive bladder cancer s/p maximal TURBT
GU.methods: Phase II randomized ➜ bladder preservation RT with concurrent cisplatin vs. ➜ above with nivolumab starting 2 weeks prior to radiation and continuing concurrent and 6 months after Radiation was 60 Gy to whole bladder plus 10 Gy boost
GU.results: 2-yr LRF 72% vs. 59%, p=0.22 2-yr BCFFS 43% vs. 70% 2-yr OS 61% vs. 87%
GU.interpretation: OS and CSS was improved with nivolumab added to bladder preservation therapy. LRC did trended to improvement. Phase III trials are warranted.
GU.references: 1. Kougioumtzopoulou A, Koutsoukos K, Zakopoulou R, et al. GU.trial: Mount Sinai Hospital, University of Toronto
GU.patients: cT2-T4N0M0 muscle-invasive bladder cancer, solitary tumors, no hydronephrosis, no extensive or multifocal carcinoma in situ
GU.methods: Retrospective meta-analysis from three centers, using propensity score matching and inverse probability treatment weighting radical cystectomy compared to bladder preservation
GU.results: radical cystectomy vs. bladder preservation 5-yr OS 72% vs. 77% 5-yr MFS 74% vs. 75% 5-yr CSS 81% vs. 84% 5-yr DFS 73% vs. 74% (propensity score matching values only are shown here)
GU.interpretation: This meta-analysis shows favorable outcomes for both radical cystectomy and bladder preservation. OS seems to favor bladder preservation. The study is limited by its retrospective nature. Randomized trials are warranted.
GU.commentary: The results are intriguing given that bladder preservation patients would be expected to do worse: these patients often have worse medical issues, have had multiple recurrences, and have larger T2s than those that undergo surgery. An adjusted analysis would not be able to correct all the imbalances; for example, there are many sizes of T2s, and staging accuracy by TURBT is limited. However this analysis has several strengths over the typical large scale database analysis given that these institutions recorded more data points and outcomes.
GU.references: 1. Zlotta AR, Ballas LK, Niemierko A, et al. Radical cystectomy versus trimodality therapy for muscle-invasive bladder cancer: a multi-institutional propensity score matched and weighted analysis. Lancet Oncol 2023. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(23)00170-5

GU.citation: Efstathiou et al, JCO, 2009 Mak et al, JCO, 2014
GU.patients: muscle invasive bladder cancer in six RTOG studies (8802, 8903, 9506, 9706, 9906, 0233), T2-T4a
GU.methods: Adverse effects outcomes: Meta-analysis of 4 RTOG trials. Median f/u 5.4 years. Control outcomes: Meta-analysis of 6 RTOG bladder preservation trials
GU.results: CR 69% 5-yr intact bladder: 80% 5-yr OS 57%, 10-yr OS 36% 5-yr DSS 71%, 10-yr DSS 65% 5-yr bladder intact DFS 56%, 10-yr 55% 5-yr muscle invasive LF 13%, 10-yr 14% 5-yr non-muscle inv LF 31%, 10-yr 36% 5-yr node recurrence 13%, 10-yr 16% 5-yr DM 31%, 10-yr DM 35% CR rates: 74% in T2, 67% in T3-T4a, 73% in complete TURBT, 56% in incomplete TURBT. No difference in response by histology, hydronephrosis, or grade. Late grade 3 effects: 6% GU, 2% GI No grade 4 or grade 5 toxicity
GU.interpretation: Bladder preservation leads to favorable outcomes in long-term rates of intact bladder. There is low late adverse effects. Randomized trials are warranted to compare the approach to cystectomy.
GU.commentary: There is a possibility that adverse effects could be increased with longer follow-up. Patient reported QOL was not assessed in the era of these trials.
GU.references: 1. Mak RH, Hunt D, Shipley WU, et al. Long-Term Outcomes in Patients With Muscle-Invasive Bladder Cancer After Selective Bladder-Preserving Combined-Modality Therapy: A Pooled Analysis of Radiation Therapy Oncology Group Protocols 8802, 8903, 9506, 9706, 9906, and 0233. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:3801-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2014.57.5548 2. Efstathiou JA, Bae K, Shipley WU, et al. GU.methods: Pooled analysis of 5 prospective protocols TURBT then chemoRT to 64-65 Gy if CR after 40 Gy, or those medically unfit for cystectomy.
GU.results: 5-yr OS 54%, 10-yr OS 36% 5-yr DSS 63%, 10-yr DSS 59% 5-yr DSS with intact bladder 46%, 10-yr 45% Pelvic recurrence 8.4% 1/3 of required salvage cystectomy.
GU.interpretation: Bladder preservation results in favorable outcomes.
GU.commentary: 5-yr and 10-yr DSS Stage T2, 74% and 66% T3-T4a 53% and 54% 5-yr and 10-yr DSS with intact bladder Stage T2 46% and 45% T3 and T4a 35% and 34%
GU.references: 1. Shipley WU, Kaufman DS, Zehr E, et al. Selective bladder preservation by combined modality protocol treatment: long-term outcomes of 190 patients with invasive bladder cancer. Urology 2002;60:62-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/s0090-4295(02)01650-3

GU.methods: ➜ MCV x2 then 39.6 Gy pelvic RT + concurrent cisplatin vs. ➜ without induction MCV If CR, boost to 64.8Gy and additional dose cisplatin, otherwise cystectomy
GU.results: 5-yr OS 49% in both arms cCR unchanged, 61% and 55% 5-yr OS with functioning bladder 38% (78% surviving with bladder, 38%/49%) 5-yr DM 33-39% No statistically significant changes

GU.citation: James et al, NEJM, 2012 Huddart et al, IJROBP, 2014 Hall et al, Eur Urol, 2022
GU.methods: ➜ standard volume RT vs. ➜ reduced high dose volume RT ➜ concurrent 5FU/MMC vs. ➜ no chemo RT primary endpoint: 2-yr LRC of RHDVRT at most 10% inferior RT dose: Choice of 55 Gy/20 fx or 64 Gy/32 fx. RHDVRT received the rx dose to the GTV and 80% dose to the whole bladder. Standard volume RT received the dose to the whole bladder.
GU.results: 📌 RT randomization Noninferiority of LRC of standard RT vs. RHDVRT could not be confirmed 2-yr LRC 62% vs. 67% (not noninferior) 5-yr LRC ~55%, 10-yr ~48% inv LRC 2-yr ~81%, 5-yr 76%, 10-yr 69% 2-yr bladder preservation ~89% Grade 3-4 adverse effects 13% in both RT arms 📌 Chemo randomization Chemo improved LRC 5-yr LRC 63% vs. 49% 5-yr inv LRC 79% vs. 64% 5-yr bladder preservation 86% vs. 78% At long term f/u at 9.9 yrs, there were some trends to improvements in DFS, MFS, OS, and CSS with chemo, but no differences, and all CIs crossed 1
GU.interpretation: Reduced volume high dose RT (RHDVRT) was not noninferior for LC and did not reduce late effects. However overall outcomes seemed favorable. Chemotherapy improves LC and bladder preservation rates. There is a trend to improved OS, CSS, DFS, and MFS when adding 5FU and MMC to radiation; however this is not significant long-term.
GU.commentary: Although the radiation randomization addressed a question of volumes, the trial's primary innovation is its use of hypofractionation. Hypofractionation was physician's choice and not randomized. A later combined propensity matched type analysis of BC2001 and BCON showed no difference between hypofx vs. conventional RT. There are limitations since neither trial originally randomized the dose regimens (Choudhury 2021). A propensity or adjusted analysis is not a replacement for a randomized trial and cannot create fully balanced arms. Hypofx shows generally favorable results prospectively and sta…
