GU.methods: Choice of 55 Gy/20 fx or 64 Gy/32 fx in BC2001 and BCON Combined analysis of LC, OS, and adverse effects in these trials to assess noninferiority
GU.results: 📌 RT randomization Noninferiority of LRC of standard RT vs. RHDVRT could not be confirmed 2-yr LRC 62% vs. 67% (not noninferior) 5-yr LRC ~55%, 10-yr ~48% inv LRC 2-yr ~81%, 5-yr 76%, 10-yr 69% 2-yr bladder preservation ~89% Grade 3-4 effects 13% in both RT arms 📌 Chemo randomization Chemo improved LRC 5-yr LRC 63% vs. 49% 5-yr inv LRC 79% vs. 64% 5-yr bladder preservation 86% vs. 78% At long term f/u at 9.9 yrs, there were some trends to improvements in DFS, MFS, OS, and CSS with chemo, but no differences, and all CIs crossed 1
GU.interpretation: In this propensity matched analysis of two trials that allowed hypofractionation as physicians' choice, 55 Gy/20 fx is noninferior to 64 Gy/32 fx in LC, OS, and adverse effects. LC with 55 Gy is superior. Randomized trials are warranted.
GU.commentary: BCON was a randomized trial assessing hypoxia modification with carbogen and nicotinamide. Neither original trial randomized hypofx vs. conventional fractionation; the regimens were investigator's choice. This means that this study is a propensity analysis. Investigators may have tended to prescribe long course for more advanced tumors. Recognize that after incomplete TURBT for biopsy, a wide variation of tumors, including very large ones and indeterminate T3s, can be categorized as T2. That, and other factors, cannot be completely adjusted for post-hoc. Randomized trials are warranted. Hypofx…

GU.citation: Murthy et al, IJROBP, 2024 Murthy et al, JCO, 2026
GU.patients: urothelial bladder cancer s/p radical cystectomy with ≥1 risk factor: pT3-4, pN1-3, nodal yield <10, positive margin, or neoadjuvant chemo for ≥cT3
GU.methods: Phase III ➜ 🏆 adjuvant 50.4 Gy/28 fx to nodes and SIB 45 Gy to cystectomy bed vs. ➜ obs
GU.results: 2-yr LRFS 87% vs. 76%, p=0.04 2-yr LRF 8% vs. 27% 2-yr DM 31%, not different 2-yr DFS 72% vs. 59% 2-yr CSS 80% vs. 65% 2-yr OS 70% vs. 57% On subanalysis, benefit with RT in T3-4 and N+ No isolated LF with RT Acute grade 3 effects 1.6% vs. 4.2% Acute grade 2 effects 17.5% vs. 1.1% Late grade 3-4 effects 8.4% vs. 10.5% Late grade ≥2 effects 23.3% vs. 17.5%
GU.interpretation: Adjuvant radiation therapy in bladder cancer improves LRFS.
GU.commentary: CT sim was performed with contrast in the ileal conduit. The analysis was somewhat ITT: those in the RT arm who did not receive radiation were analyzed in the observation arm. Such methodology is not ideal but may be useful if investigators suspect high noncompliance and deviations from protocol. 49% were N+, 4.5% R1, 28% had variant histology, 90% had neoadjuvant chemo Stoma mean <5 Gy Small bowel V40 <30% Rectum V30 <60% Bladder V45 <35% Femoral head V30 <15%
GU.references: Study protocol DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-25-02093 [radiation planning p9] 1. Murthy V, Maitre P, Pal M, et al. Bladder Adjuvant Radiotherapy: Phase III Multicenter Randomized Controlled Trial of Adjuvant Radiotherapy or Observation for Postcystectomy Muscle-Invasive Bladder Cancer. J Clin Oncol. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-25-02093

GU.citation: Zaghloul et al, JAMA Surg, 2018 Zaghloul et al, ASTRO, 2019
GU.patients: ≥pT3b, N+, or grade 3 with negative margins, age ≤70
GU.methods: Phase II randomized ➜ RT to 45 Gy in 150 cGy BID over 3 wks with 2 cycles gem/cis before and after vs. ➜ RT then adjuvant chemo vs. ➜ gem/cis x4 alone
GU.results: 2-yr LRFS (chemoRT vs. chemo) 96% vs. 69% 2-yr DFS 68% vs. 56%, p=0.07 2-yr OS 71% vs. 60%, p=0.11 SCC subset 2-yr OS (chemoRT vs. chemo) 71% vs. 43% characteristics: 53% urothelial and 47% SCC
GU.interpretation: Adjuvant chemotherapy plus RT resulted in improved LRF and trend to improvement in DFS and OS.
GU.commentary: Trial conducted in Egpyt. Note high proportion of squamous cell compared to US. Tumor size and age favored RT arm.
GU.references: 1. Zaghloul MS, Christodouleas JP, Smith A, et al. Adjuvant Sandwich Chemotherapy Plus Radiotherapy vs Adjuvant Chemotherapy Alone for Locally Advanced Bladder Cancer After Radical Cystectomy: A Randomized Phase 2 Trial. JAMA Surg 2018;153:e174591-e. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamasurg.2017.4591 2. Zaghloul MS,Christodouleas JP, Zaghloul T, et al. Randomized Trial of Adjuvant Therapies after Radical Cystectomy for Locally Advanced Bladder Cancer: Squamous Cell Carcinoma Subgroup Analysis. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2019;105:s138-39. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2019.06.131

GU.patients: s/p radical cystectomy, pT3a-T4b, any N1-2, or any R1. MIBC pure urothelial carcinoma or mixed (>50% urothelial)
GU.methods: Phase III ➜ adjuvant 50.4 Gy WPRT vs. ➜ obs
GU.results: ongoing trial Primary endpoint: pelvic RFS
GU.references: 1. UNICANCER. Adjuvant Radiotherapy in Patients With Pathological High-risk Bladder Cancer (GETUG-AFU 30) (Bladder-ART). Available at: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT03333356. NLM identifier: NCT03333356. Accessed August 21, 2020.

GU.patients: Patients undergoing radical cystectomy or CRT
GU.methods: Databases searched for randomized, prospective, or restrospective trials evaluating radical cystectomy vs. CRT
GU.results: 8 studies were eligible for meta-analysis No difference in 5-yr OS, 10-yr OS, 5-yr DSS, 10-yr DSS, or 10-yr PFS Cystectomy arms had higher rates of early complications and minor complications were similar between arms
GU.interpretation: This meta-analysis showed no difference in OS, DSS, or PFS between cystectomy and bladder preservation. Randomized trials are warranted.
GU.references: 1. Vashistha V, Wang H, Mazzone A, et al. Radical Cystectomy Compared to Combined Modality Treatment for Muscle-Invasive Bladder Cancer: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2017;97:1002-20. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2016.11.056

GU.methods: Phase II ➜ TURBT → chemoRT (taxol/cis vs. 5FU/cis) → check response → more chemoRT if T0, Tcis, or Ta → adjuvant gemzar, taxol, cisplatin RT 40.3 Gy BID plus 24 Gy BID boost if downstaged
GU.results: TC vs. FC: after induction, T0, Ta, Tis: 87% vs. 79% CR 72% vs. 62% 4-yr alive with intact bladder 73% vs. 69% late grade 3-4 RT effects: 11% vs. 6% late grade 3-4 non-RT effects: 24% vs. 34% 67% completed entire taxol and 53% completed entire 5FU chemo
GU.interpretation: Taxol and cisplatin has favorable control and less adverse effects compared to 5FU/cis in this phase II trial.
GU.references: 1. Mitin T, Hunt D, Shipley WU, et al. Transurethral surgery and twice-daily radiation plus paclitaxel-cisplatin or fluorouracil-cisplatin with selective bladder preservation and adjuvant chemotherapy for patients with muscle invasive bladder cancer (RTOG 0233): a randomised multicentre phase 2 trial. Lancet Oncol 2013;14:863-72. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(13)70255-9

GU.methods: Meta-analysis of observational and randomized trials evaluating ➜ SBRT vs. ➜ RFA vs. ➜ microwave ablation vs. ➜ cryoablation
GU.results: SBRT vs. RFA vs. microwave ablation vs. cryo 5-yr LC 95% vs. 92% vs. 86% vs. 90% grade 3-4 adverse effects 2% vs. 2% vs. 1% vs. 3%
GU.interpretation: In this meta-analysis SBRT seems to have better local control compared to RFA, microwave ablation, and cryotherapy. All seem to be effective treatment approaches.
GU.commentary: There is limitation to the data given that much of it is observational, but at the time of publication it's the highest level of evidence comparing the various modalities.
GU.references: 1. Huang RS, Chow R, Benour A, et al. Comparative efficacy and safety of ablative therapies in the management of primary localised renal cell carcinoma: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Lancet Oncol. Published online February 5, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(24)00731-9

GU.methods: Meta-analysis of eGFR in studies evaluating radical nephrectomy, partial nephrectomy, SBRT, ablative therapy
GU.results: Pre vs post eGFR mean 21.8 RN vs. 5.5 PN vs. 8.6 SBRT vs. 5.1 ablatives (worse with radical nephrectomy, not different with others) Grade 3-5 events 4.7 PN vs. 0.2 SBRT vs. 4.3 ablatives
GU.interpretation: SBRT, partial nephrectomy, and ablative therapies have the least reduction in eGFR. SBRT has the fewest grade 3-5 events.
GU.commentary: Obviously there are some limitations to the data given that these were non randomized, but this meta-analysis provides good evidence in the absence of randomized trials. See also the RADSTER randomized trial comparing SBRT and RFA in RCC. RFA had less post-biopsy residual disease, but LC was still 100% in both arms at one year.
GU.references: 1. Rajoulh C, Ali M, Wang M, et al. Renal Function after Definitive Local Therapy for Primary Renal Cell Carcinoma: A Meta-Analysis. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2024;120:s73-74. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2024.07.126

GU.patients: Medically inoperable RCC with single lesion, biopsy confirmed Median tumor size was 4.6 cm
GU.methods: Phase II SBRT 26 Gy single fraction for size ≤4 cm SBRT 42 Gy/3 fx for size >4 cm (67% had three fractions)
GU.results: 1-yr LC 100% CSS 100% 3-yr FFDF 97% 3-yr OS 82% 1-yr eGFR reduction -10.8 mL/min 2-yr eGFR reduction 14.6 mL/min 2-yr split funciton 33% grade 3 adverse effects 10% (none related to renal function) no grade 4-5 adverse effects
GU.interpretation: SBRT is an effective treatment for primary renal cell carcinoma and with low adverse effects.
GU.commentary: Dose constraints Ipsilateral kidney minus ITV V10 Gy <1.5 cc Median tumor size was 4.6 cm. Split renal function is the relative contribution of each of the two kidneys to total renal function. Normal is 45-55%. Dose constraints pdf, p6: https://bmccancer.biomedcentral.com/counter/pdf/10.1186/s12885-018-4916-2.pdf#page=6
GU.references: 1. Siva S, Bressel M, Sidhom M, et al. Stereotactic ablative body radiotherapy for primary kidney cancer (TROG 15.03 FASTRACK II): a non-randomised phase 2 trial. Lancet Oncol 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(24)00020-2

GU.patients: renal masses ≤4 cm who were inoperable or who declined surgery or active surveillance
GU.methods: ➜ RFA vs. ➜ SBRT 1-yr biopsy required (see commentary)
GU.results: 14 had SBRT and 7 had RFA. Crossover occured mostly due to technical inability to perform RFA 1-yr RECIST LC 100% vs. 100% No deaths or DM 1-yr arterial enhancement 23% vs. 83% Mean GFR change ~4 mL/min both groups 1-yr biopsy no residual RCC 100% vs. 31% (plus 15% with minimal residual disease and 54% with evidence of RCC)
GU.interpretation: SBRT and RFA for small renal tumors both lead to excellent outcomes in this small study. RFA had less post-biopsy residual disease, but LC was still 100% in both arms.
GU.commentary: Some have recommended no post-biopsy after SBRT treatment (Siva 2023). Radiation can lead to mitotic death, which can be difficult to interpret on biospy.
GU.references: 1. Swaminath A, Cassim R, Millan B, et al. Final Results from a Prospective Randomized Pilot Trial of Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy vs. Radiofrequency Ablation for the Management of Small Renal Masses (RADSTER). Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2023:117;s82. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2023.06.402
GU.commentary_refs: 1. Correa RJM, Appu S, Siva S. Stereotactic Radiotherapy for Renal Cell Carcinoma: The Fallacy of (False) Positive Post-treatment Biopsy? Eur Urol 2023;84:287-288. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eururo.2023.03.025

GU.citation: Siva et al, Future Oncol, 2016 Siva et al, Cancer, 2018 Correa et al, J Urol, 2019 Siva et al, Lancet Oncol, 2022
GU.patients: SBRT for renal tumors, primary or metastatic
GU.methods: pooled multicenter cohort analysis 30% had solitary kidney
GU.results: 5-yr LC 96% 2/5-yr CSS 96/92% 2/5-yr PFS 72/64% Single fx SBRT had better LC than fractionated 5-yr median GFR decrease of 14.2 mL/min 4% required dialysis n=2 of grade 4 (duodenal ulcer & gastritis) no grade 3 adverse effects Grade 1-2 adverse effects 37%
GU.interpretation: SBRT for kidney cancer leads to favorable outcomes and low adverse effects.
GU.commentary: Wide range of doses used: 1-12 fraction, 25-80 GyE with max size limits of 5-8 cm. Selected collected data is displayed here. According to the IROCK report, there is some mild to moderate decrease in GFR in the case of treatment of solitary kidney. Apparently with bilateral functioning kidneys, the untreated kidney compensates. A later IROCK trial, FASTRACK II, shared a kidney constraint: Ipsilateral kidney minus ITV V10 Gy <1.5 cc Dose constraints pdf, p6: https://bmccancer.biomedcentral.com/counter/pdf/10.1186/s12885-018-4916-2.pdf#page=6
GU.references: 1. Siva S, Ellis RJ, Ponsky L, et al. Consensus statement from the International Radiosurgery Oncology Consortium for Kidney for primary renal cell carcinoma. Future Oncol 2016;12:637-45. DOI: https://doi.org/10.2217/fon.16.2 2. Siva S, Louie AV, Warner A, et al. Pooled analysis of stereotactic ablative radiotherapy for primary renal cell carcinoma: A report from the International Radiosurgery Oncology Consortium for Kidney (IROCK). Cancer 2018;124:934-42. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.31156 3. Correa RJM, Louie AV, Staehler M, et al. Stereotactic Radiotherapy as a Treatment Option for Renal Tumors in the Solitary Kidney: A Multicenter Analysis from the IROCK. J Urol 2019;201:1097-104. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/JU.0000000000000111 4. Siva S, Ali M, Correa RJM, et al. 5-year outcomes after stereotactic ablative body radiotherapy for primary renal cell carcinoma: an individual patient data meta-analysis from IROCK (the International Radiosurgery Consortium of the Kidney) [published online ahead of print, 2022 Nov 15]. Lancet Oncol 2022;S1470-2045(22)00656-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(22)00656-8
GU.commentary_refs: 1. Siva S, Bressel M, Sidhom M, et al. TROG 15.03/ANZUP International Multicenter Phase II Trial of Focal Ablative STereotactic RAdiotherapy for Cancers of the Kidney (FASTRACK II). Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2023;117:s3. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2023.06.208

GU.trial: London Regional Cancer Program, London, Ontario, Canada
GU.citation: Correa et al, Eur Urol Focus, 2019
GU.methods: Meta-analysis 26 studies (11 prospective)
GU.results: LC 97% grade 3-4 adverse effects 1.5% eGFR change -7.7 -Six with pre-exisiting dysfunction needed dialysis -Most common doses: 26 Gy/ 1 fx and 40 Gy/ 5 fx -RCC confirmed in ~80% prior to SBRT
GU.interpretation: SBRT for primary RCC has excellent safety and LC.
GU.commentary: The favorable GFR results might lead one to seek dose constraints for the technique, but no clear dose constraints have been detailed as of yet. According to the IROCK report, there is some mild to moderate decrease in GFR in the case of treatment of solitary kidney. Apparently with bilateral functioning kidneys, the untreated kidney compensates.
GU.references: 1. Correa RJM, Louie AV, Zaorsky NG, et al. The Emerging Role of Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy for Primary Renal Cell Carcinoma: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Eur Urol Focus 2019;5:958-969. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.euf.2019.06.002

GU.patients: ≤5 oligometastases, ≤1 prior systemic therapy, any histology
GU.methods: Phase II: SBRT to all lesions IMRT 60-70 Gy/10 fx or 52.5-67.5 Gy/10 fx allowed if SBRT constraints not met
GU.results: Median PFS 22.7 mos 1-yr PFS 64%
GU.interpretation: SBRT for oligometastatic disease in RCC may allow for deferral of systemic therapy.
GU.references: 1. Tang C, Msaouel P, Hara K, et al. Definitive radiotherapy in lieu of systemic therapy for oligometastatic renal cell carcinoma: a single-arm, single-centre, feasibility, phase 2 trial. Lancet Oncol 2021;22(12):1732-1739. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(21)00528-3

GU.trial: Third Spanish Germ Cell Cancer Group
GU.methods: Prospective: survellance if 0-1 risk factors. Carboplatin x2 cycles if 2 risk factors
GU.results: 10% relapses in surveillance group and 1% relapse in chemo group 3-yr DFS 88% with surveillance, 98% carboplatin 3-yr OS 100% All 14 failures were successfully salvaged.
GU.interpretation: Surveillance and chemotherapy are both appropriate options for seminoma Stage I.
GU.references: 1. Aparicio J, Maroto P, Muro XGd, et al. Risk-Adapted Treatment in Clinical Stage I Testicular Seminoma: The Third Spanish Germ Cell Cancer Group Study. J Clin Oncol 2011;29:4677-81. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2011.36.0503

GU.methods: Retrospective
GU.results: After surveillance in stage I, CSS (salvage) still 100%. 17% of seminomas relapsed, most in first year
GU.interpretation: Observation in stage I seminoma results in low relapses.
GU.references: 1. Daugaard G, Gundgaard MG, Mortensen MS, et al. Surveillance for Stage I Nonseminoma Testicular Cancer: Outcomes and Long-Term Follow-Up in a Population-Based Cohort. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:3817-23. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2013.53.5831

GU.citation: Oliver et al, Lancet, 2005 Olilver et al, JCO, 2011
GU.methods: ➜ 20-30 Gy to perioartics vs. ➜ carbo AUC 7 x 1 (5:3 ratio) If prior surgery, inguinals were added to the radiation field ("dogleg" field)
GU.results: 5-yr RFS rates ~95%, not different Higher PA node relapse with carbo 74% vs. 9% But other relapses more common after RT Fewer secondary germ cell tumors after carbo. Contralateral recurrence n=15 vs. n=2 for chemo
GU.interpretation: Carboplatin is non-inferior to RT.
GU.references: 1. Oliver RTD, Mason MD, Mead GM, et al. Radiotherapy versus single-dose carboplatin in adjuvant treatment of stage I seminoma: a randomised trial. Lancet 2005;366:293-300. 10.1016/S0140-6736(05)66984-X 2. Oliver RTD, Mead GM, Rustin GJS, et al. Randomized Trial of Carboplatin Versus Radiotherapy for Stage I Seminoma: Mature Results on Relapse and Contralateral Testis Cancer Rates in MRC TE19/EORTC 30982 Study (ISRCTN27163214). J Clin Oncol 2011;29:957-62. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2009.26.4655

GU.methods: ➜ 30 Gy vs. ➜ 20 Gy🏆 Field: periaortic (or dogleg if prior inguinal surgery)
GU.results: 5-yr RFS 96-97%, not different Less lethargy and work impairment with lower dose
GU.interpretation: There is no benefit to 30 Gy RT over 20 Gy. 20 Gy should be used.
GU.commentary: This trial established 20 Gy radiation for PA fields.
GU.references: 1. Jones WG, Fossa SD, Mead GM, et al. Randomized trial of 30 versus 20 Gy in the adjuvant treatment of stage I Testicular Seminoma: a report on Medical Research Council Trial TE18, European Organisation for the Research and Treatment of Cancer Trial 30942 (ISRCTN18525328). J Clin Oncol 2005;23:1200-1208. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2005.08.003

GU.methods: ➜ periaortic + inguinals field ("dogleg") vs. ➜ periaortic field, 30 Gy/15 fx PA fields T10/11 to L5/S1 and including ipsilateral renal hilum
GU.results: 3-yr RFS (97% vs. 96%) and OS no difference Four pelvic relapses occurred in PA group vs. none in DL group (SS) Improved rates of diarrhea, leukopenia, nausea, vomiting, azoospermia in PA group.
GU.interpretation: PA field has decreased morbidity compared to dogleg and should be standard.
GU.commentary: Note that modern PA fields are slightly different and do not include renal hilum Sperm counts rose faster with PA 20 mos vs. 13 mos In dogleg arm, 92% normal sperm counts at 3 years
GU.references: 1. Fosså SD, Horwich A, Russell JM, et al. Optimal Planning Target Volume for Stage I Testicular Seminoma: A Medical Research Council Randomized Trial. J Clin Oncol 1999;17:1146-. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1999.17.4.1146
GU.commentary_refs: Wilder RB, Buyyounouski MK, Efstathiou JA, Beard CJ. Radiotherapy Treatment Planning for Testicular Seminoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2012;83:e445-e52. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2012.01.044

GU.patients: 1-yr survivors of testicular cancer from American and European registries
GU.methods: Retrospective review: Analysis of risk of second solid cancers
GU.results: Risk of 2nd cancer remains elevated till at least 35 years after tx. Risk of solid tumors in lung, pleura, esophagus, gastric, colon, bladder and pancreas. RR: RT 2.0, chemo 1.8, both 2.9
GU.interpretation: The risk of 2nd cancer in testicular cancer after treatment continues to rise even after 35 years.
GU.commentary: Also refer to van den Belt-Dusebout 2007.
GU.references: 1. Travis LB, Fosså SD, Schonfeld SJ, et al. Second Cancers Among 40 576 Testicular Cancer Patients: Focus on Long-term Survivors. J Natl Cancer Inst 2005;97:1354-65. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/jnci/dji278
GU.commentary_refs: Belt-Dusebout AWvd, Wit Rd, Gietema JA, et al. Treatment-Specific Risks of Second Malignancies and Cardiovascular Disease in 5-Year Survivors of Testicular Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2007;25:4370-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2006.10.5296

GU.methods: Retrospective review. 7-yr median f/u. Prognostic factor pooled analysis; primary endpoint relapse.
GU.results: 5-yr relapse-free rate 82%. Size, rete testis invasion give increased risk of relapse. 0 factor: 12% relapse risk 1 factors: 16% 2 factors: 30%
GU.interpretation: Size of tumor and rete testis invasion are prognostic for relapse in surveillance.
GU.commentary: These factors are now used for prognosis only and not to guide treatment decisions. Surveillance now recommended in Stage I who will be complaint with f/u (NCCN 2019).
GU.references: 1. Warde P, Specht L, Horwich A, et al. Prognostic Factors for Relapse in Stage I Seminoma Managed by Surveillance: A Pooled Analysis. J Clin Oncol 2002;20:4448-52. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2002.01.038
GU.commentary_refs: National Comprehensive Cancer Network. Testicular Cancer (Version 1.2019). https://www.nccn.org/professionals/physician_gls/pdf/testicular.pdf. Accessed June 20, 2019

GU.methods: Meta-analysis of 19 retrospective studies from 1984-2012 (n=673 in brachytherapy) Median brachy dose 60 Gy Median EBRT dose 51 Gy
GU.results: 5-yr OS 76% surgery vs. 73% brachy 5-yr LC 84% penectomy vs. 79% brachy •Brachy organ preservation 74% •Stage I/II surgery 5-yr OS 80%, 5-yr LC 86% •Chi-squared testing showed no difference for OS or LC for early stage disease RT adverse effects: 33% urethral stenosis with brachy (78% of managed with dilators) 17% urethral stenosis EBRT (lower dose) 8-44% required dilation for meatal stenosis •Brachy necrosis 6-26% •Required debridement/amputation in 0-24% •Sexual dysfunction <20% with brachy but not well reported •If inguinal nodes treated w EBRT, lymphedema 10-35% •Lymphedema after inguinal …
GU.interpretation: This meta-analysis is limited due to retrospective nature of data. Penectomy provided better LC, but has no OS benefit compared to BT, implying most cases of failed brachy can be salvaged.
GU.commentary: LC overall better with surgery, but for Stage I/II tumors equal with surgery vs brachy. Retrospective data.
GU.references: 1. Hasan S, Francis A, Hagenauer A, et al. The role of brachytherapy in organ preservation for penile cancer: A meta-analysis and review of the literature. Brachytherapy; 2015:14(4):517-24. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.brachy.2015.03.008

GU.citation: Institute of Cancer Research, United Kingdom, ongoing protocol
GU.methods: 1st randomization: ➜ Inguinal LND vs. ➜ neoadj TIP (paclitaxel, ifosfamide, and cisplatin) vs. ➜ neoadj 45 Gy with conc cis 2nd randomization (if not given neoadj chemoRT, will be given adjuvant): ➜ prophylactic pelvic LND vs. ➜ no pelvic LND
GU.results: Ongoing trial Primary endpoint: OS
GU.references: 1. Institute of Cancer Research, United Kingdom. Institute of Cancer Research, United Kingdom. Available at: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT02305654. Acessed August 27, 2020.

Gyn.citation: Landoni et al, Lancet, 1997 Landoni et al, J Gynecol Oncol, 2017
Gyn.patients: Stage IB + IIA cervical cancer
Gyn.methods: ➜ Radical hyst + LND + adjuvant RT as indicated vs. ➜ 47 Gy EBRT + LDR to 70-90 Gy (median 76) Adjuvant EBRT to 50.4 Gy for ≥pT2b, ≤3 mm cervical stroma margin, cut-through, or N+ (given in 64%) EFRT 45 Gy given if PA or common iliac N+
Gyn.results: ↔️ No difference in 5-yr OS (83%), 20-yr OS (~75%), DFS (74%), or recurrence (25%). Median time to relapse 13.5 vs. 11.5 mos (p=0.10) 20-yr late complications 32% vs. 23%, worse with surgery. 64% in surgery arm had adjuvant RT Worst morbidity with adjuvant RT plus surgery (hydronephrosis, GU incontinence, LE edema, bowel obstruction) ⬆️ OS benefit trend with RT in size ≥4 cm, and significant benefit in size ≥5 cm ⬆️ OS benefit trend with surgery in adeno, p=0.09 65% received adjuvant RT
Gyn.interpretation: Surgery and RT have similar efficacy. Adverse effects are worse with surgery. RT seems preferable for larger tumors and surgery seems preferable for adenocarcinoma. Tumor diameter, histology, age, and comorbidities should be considered.
Gyn.commentary: This landmark trial established the role of definitive radiation for cervical cancer. It's often quoted to support definitive RT over surgery in early cervical cancer, but its application is more nuanced. Individualized care is called for. Those that would be expected to require post-op RT, especially chemoRT, may be best suited for definitive chemoRT. In the 2017 update there is trend to benefit with surgery in adeno, and trend to benefit with RT in size >4 cm. Adverse effects. The adverse effects from post op RT are excess here compared to modern therapy and the study should be referenced li…
Gyn.notes: Cervix summary -Milan study shows that WPRT+brachy vs surgery have similar OS in early stage cervical cancer. Surgery tends to have more morbidity, so RT becomes standard for many cases -Trials show OS benefit with chemo in addition to RT for definitive treatment of cervical cancer. This is confirmed in meta-analysis -Trials have some mixed results regarding the use of coverage of PA fields (EORTC, RTOG 7920). EFRT is generally then restricted for PA N+ only -The Peters trial shows OS benefit with chemo in post-op cervix with N+, +margin, or +parametria -The Sedlis trials shows PFS benefit wit…
Gyn.references: 1. Landoni F, Maneo A, Colombo A, et al. Randomised study of radical surgery versus radiotherapy for stage Ib-IIa cervical cancer. Lancet 1997;350:535-40. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(97)02250-2 2. Landoni F, Colombo A, Milani R, Placa F, Zanagnolo V, Mangioni C. Randomized study between radical surgery and radiotherapy for the treatment of stage IB-IIA cervical cancer: 20-year update. J Gynecol Oncol 2017;28:e34-e. DOI: https://doi.org/10.3802/jgo.2017.28.e34
Gyn.commentary_refs: 1. Plante M, Kwon JS, Ferguson S, et al. Simple versus Radical Hysterectomy in Women with Low-Risk Cervical Cancer. N Engl J Med. 2024;390(9):819-829. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2308900

Gyn.patients: Cervix SCC Stage IB2, IIA, IIB
Gyn.methods: ➜ neoadjuvant cisplatin based chemo, radical hysterectomy, & post-op chemoRT as indicated for N+, parametrial invasion, or +M (no RT for Sedlis criteria) vs. ➜ 🏆 definitive RT + cisplatin
Gyn.results: 5-yr OS not different, 72-76% 5-yr PFS 57% vs. 66%, p=0.010 Post-op chemoRT was given in 48% late grade ≥3 effects 15% vs. 21%, worse with radiation
Gyn.interpretation: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by surgery is not superior to chemoradiation. PFS may be worse.
Gyn.commentary: Adverse effects. In the EORTC and Tata Memorial studies the neoadjuvant chemo plus surgery arms with post-op RT as indicated did not lead to worse late adverse effects overall compared to definitive radiation, as was shown in the Milan trial. Those trials however did not report additional subanalysis on adverse effects with post-op RT specifically. This could be due to less receiving adjuvant RT (64% post-op RT in Milan study vs. 48% post-op CRT in EORTC, 13% post-op CRT & 10% RT alone in Tata Memorial) and/or improved surgical and post-op radiation techniques such as laparoscopy and IMRT. A m…
Gyn.references: 1. Kenter GG, Greggi S, Vergote I, et al. Randomized Phase III Study Comparing Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy Followed by Surgery Versus Chemoradiation in Stage IB2-IIB Cervical Cancer: EORTC-55994 [published online ahead of print, 2023 Sep 1]. J Clin Oncol 2023;JCO2202852. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.02852 Protocol pdf: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.22.02852/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.22.02852.pdf
Gyn.commentary_refs: 1. Plante M, Kwon JS, Ferguson S, et al. Simple versus Radical Hysterectomy in Women with Low-Risk Cervical Cancer. N Engl J Med. 2024;390(9):819-829. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2308900

Gyn.patients: Cervix SCC Stage IB2, IIA, IIB
Gyn.methods: ➜ neoadjuvant carbo/taxol, radical hysterectomy, & post-op RT or chemo as indicated vs. ➜ 🏆 definitive RT + cisplatin
Gyn.results: No change in OS. Worse DFS with neoadjuvant 5-yr DFS 69% neoadjuvant vs. 78% 5-yr OS 75% both arms delayed adverse effects: rectal 2.2% vs. 3.5%, bladder 1.6% vs. 3.5%, vaginal 12.0% vs. 26%
Gyn.interpretation: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy before hysterectomy should not be employed. DFS was worsened compared to definitive chemoRT.
Gyn.commentary: Adverse effects. In the EORTC and Tata Memorial studies the neoadjuvant chemo plus surgery arms with post-op RT as indicated did not lead to worse late adverse effects overall compared to definitive radiation, as was shown in the Milan trial. Those trials however did not report additional subanalysis on adverse effects with post-op RT specifically. This could be due to less receiving adjuvant RT (64% post-op RT in Milan study vs. 48% post-op CRT in EORTC, 13% post-op CRT & 10% RT alone in Tata Memorial) and/or improved surgical and post-op radiation techniques such as laparoscopy and IMRT. A m…
Gyn.references: 1. Gupta S, Maheshwari A, Parab P, et al. Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy Followed by Radical Surgery Versus Concomitant Chemotherapy and Radiotherapy in Patients With Stage IB2, IIA, or IIB Squamous Cervical Cancer: A Randomized Controlled Trial. J Clin Oncol 2018;36:1548-55. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2017.75.9985 Protocol pdf: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.2017.75.9985/suppl_file/protocol_2017.759985.pdf
Gyn.commentary_refs: 1. Plante M, Kwon JS, Ferguson S, et al. Simple versus Radical Hysterectomy in Women with Low-Risk Cervical Cancer. N Engl J Med. 2024;390(9):819-829. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2308900

Gyn.subtopic: Definitive RT vs Neoadjuvant RT + surgery
Gyn.patients: Bulky IB disease (tumor or cervix ≥4cm)
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 40 Gy EBRT + 40 Gy LDR vs. ➜ 45 Gy EBRT + 30 Gy LDR + extrafascial hysterectomy
Gyn.results: 5-yr LR 27% vs. 14% Hyst 5-yr PFS 53% vs. 62%, p=0.09 Disease progression 46% vs. 37%, p=0.07 Grade 3-4 effects not different On unplanned subanalysis hyst improved control and survival in size 4-6 cm
Gyn.interpretation: There was no overall benefit to hysterectomy after definitive RT. On subanalysis hysterectomy seemed to be favored in larger tumors of size 4-6 cm.
Gyn.commentary: Some surgeons may reference this trial to support surgery for size >4 cm without LVSI or deep stromal invasion that may not require adjuvant radiation. However the subanalysis in the trial is unplanned and exploratory. The authors acknowledged interpretation with caution. Additionally, long term f/u of the Milan study (Landoni 2017) favors definitive RT for larger tumors.
Gyn.references: 1. Keys HM, Bundy BN, Stehman FB, et al. Radiation therapy with and without extrafascial hysterectomy for bulky stage IB cervical carcinoma: a randomized trial of the Gynecologic Oncology Group. Gynecol Oncol 2003;89:343-53. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/s0090-8258(03)00173-2

Gyn.patients: Cervix SCC Stage FIGO IB2-IIB node positive, or III-IVA
Gyn.methods: ➜ Definitive chemoRT vs. ➜ 🏆 Definitive chemoRT + concurrent and adjuvant pembrolizumab 45-50 Gy WPRT plus brachy to 25-30 Gy in 4-6 fractions HDR or LDR
Gyn.results: 2-yr OS 81% vs. 87%, not different 2-yr PFS 57% vs. 68% Median PFS not reached in either arm
Gyn.interpretation: Concurrent and adjuvant pembrolizumab with chemoradiation improves PFS in locally advanced cervical cancer.
Gyn.commentary: Brachytherapy was prescribed to HR-CTV. ICRU/GEC ESTRO report 89 was recommended for radiation planning.
Gyn.references: 1. Lorusso D, Xiang Y, Hasegawa K, et al. Pembrolizumab or placebo with chemoradiotherapy followed by pembrolizumab or placebo for newly diagnosed, high-risk, locally advanced cervical cancer (ENGOT-cx11/GOG-3047/KEYNOTE-A18): a randomised, double-blind, phase 3 clinical trial. Lancet 2024;403:1341-1350. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(24)00317-9
Gyn.commentary_refs: 1. Prescribing, Recording, and Reporting Brachytherapy for Cancer of the Cervix. J ICRU. 2013;13(1-2):. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/jicru/ndw027

Gyn.subtopic: Induction chemo then chemoRT vs chemo RT
Gyn.patients: Cervix SCC Stage FIGO IB1, IB2, II, IIIB, IVA Excluded: periaortic nodes or lower vaginal involvement
Gyn.methods: ➜ definitive RT + cisplatin vs. ➜ 🏆 neoadjuvant carbo/taxol x6 then definitive RT + cisplatin 40-50.4 Gy/ 20-28 fx plus brachy to 78-86 Gy EQD2
Gyn.results: 5-yr OS 72% vs. 80% 3-yr LRR 9% vs. 11% 3-yr DM only 7% vs. 12% 5-yr PFS 64% vs. 72% Grade ≥3 adverse effects 48% vs. 49% (mainly heme)
Gyn.interpretation: Induction chemotherapy prior to definitive chemoradiation improves OS and PFS.
Gyn.commentary: 70% prescribed to point A, and 30% to HRCTV. PET used in only 25%. 40% had IMRT. Lindegaard et al commented that well-conducted brachytherapy replaces the role of neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Image guided brachy yields LC of 90% in all stages and improves OS by 10% in studies. Ideally the trial would have been performed with high quality brachytherapy delivery, but INTERLACE is the trial that was performed with the resources available. Pembrolizumab in KEYNOTE A18 provides a similar OS beneft and required rx to HR-CTV, which may lead some to favor that regimen. The authors state that periaortic n…
Gyn.references: 1. McCormack M, Eminowicz G, Gallardo D, et al. Induction chemotherapy followed by standard chemoradiotherapy versus standard chemoradiotherapy alone in patients with locally advanced cervical cancer (GCIG INTERLACE): an international, multicentre, randomised phase 3 trial. Lancet 2024. Published online October 11, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(24)01438-7 Study protocol: https://www.thelancet.com/cms/10.1016/S0140-6736(24)01438-7/attachment/7886b287-a527-4ba0-b200-268a53b562ed/mmc1.pdf
Gyn.commentary_refs: 1. Lindegaard JC, Petric P, Tan LT, et al. Are we making progress in curing advanced cervical cancer-again?. Int J Gynecol Cancer. Published online August 24, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1136/ijgc-2024-005572

Gyn.citation: Keys et al, NEJM, 1999 Stehman et al, Am J Obstet Gynecol, 2007
Gyn.patients: Cervical cancer ≥4 cm (previously IB2, later staged as IB3)
Gyn.methods: ➜ neoadjuvant definitive WPRT+brachy→simple hyst vs. ➜ 🏆 neoadjuvant definitive WPRT+brachy+weekly cisplatin→hyst
Gyn.results: 3-yr OS 83% vs. 74% 3-yr LR 21% vs. 37% 5-yr PFS 71% vs. 60% 5-yr OS 78% vs. 64% pCR 52% vs. 41%
Gyn.interpretation: The addition of chemotherapy to RT prior to hysterectomy improves OS and LC.
Gyn.references: 1. Keys HM, Bundy BN, Stehman FB, et al. Cisplatin, Radiation, and Adjuvant Hysterectomy Compared with Radiation and Adjuvant Hysterectomy for Bulky Stage IB Cervical Carcinoma. N Engl J Med 1999;340:1154-61. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199904153401503 2.Stehman FB, Ali S, Keys HM, et al. Radiation therapy with or without weekly cisplatin for bulky stage 1B cervical carcinoma: follow-up of a Gynecologic Oncology Group trial. Am J Obstet Gynecol 2007;197:503.e1-.e6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ajog.2007.08.003

Gyn.patients: IB2-IVA of size >5cm or +N
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT 45 Gy + brachy (35 Gy LDR or 24 Gy HDR) alone vs. ➜ RT + weekly cisplatin
Gyn.results: No significant difference in PFS, 3- or 5-yr OS (66-69% and 58-62%)
Gyn.interpretation: The addition of chemotherapy to definitive RT did not improve PFS or OS.
Gyn.commentary: Power may have been too low to detect an OS benefit.
Gyn.references: 1. Pearcey R, Brundage M, Drouin P, et al. Phase III Trial Comparing Radical Radiotherapy With and Without Cisplatin Chemotherapy in Patients With Advanced Squamous Cell Cancer of the Cervix. J Clin Oncol 2002;20:966-72. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2002.20.4.966

Gyn.patients: Meta-analysis of 19 trials of cervival cancer patients treated with or without chemotherapy
Gyn.methods: Meta-analysis of 19 trials. ➜ RT alone vs. ➜ 🏆 RT with concurrent chemo
Gyn.results: PFS and OS improved with chemo PFS 47% RT vs. 63% chemoRT OS 40% vs. 52%, greatest in IB-IIB No increase in late adverse effects with RT
Gyn.interpretation: The addition of chemotherapy to definitive RT improves OS in this meta-analysis.
Gyn.references: 1. Green JA, Kirwan JM, Tierney JF, et al. Survival and recurrence after concomitant chemotherapy and radiotherapy for cancer of the uterine cervix: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Lancet 2001;358:781-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(01)05965-7

Gyn.patients: Stage IIB-IVA, negative PA LN
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT concurent oral hydroxyurea vs. ➜ 🏆 WPRT concurrent 5-FU/cis
Gyn.results: OS and PFS improved with chemo 5FU/cis
Gyn.interpretation: 5-FU cisplatin added to definitive RT improves OS and PFS compared to hydroxyurea.
Gyn.references: 1. Whitney CW, Sause W, Bundy BN, et al. Randomized Comparison of Fluorouracil Plus Cisplatin Versus Hydroxyurea as an Adjunct to Radiation Therapy in Stage IIB-IVA Carcinoma of the Cervix With Negative Para-Aortic Lymph Nodes: A Gynecologic Oncology Group and Southwest Oncology Group Study. J Clin Oncol 1999;17:1339-. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1999.17.5.1339

Gyn.patients: IIB-IVA disease, surgically staged, PA LN negative
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT + brachy 81 Gy alone vs. ➜ RT + weekly cisplatin vs. ➜ RT + cis 5-FU hydroxyurea vs. ➜ RT+ hydroxyurea
Gyn.results: 2-yr PFS 67% for cisplatin alone vs. 64% for combo vs. 47% for hydroxyurea. OS also improved. adverse effects less for cisplatin alone vs. combo.
Gyn.interpretation: The addition of chemotherapy to definitive RT improves OS. Cisplatin alone is less toxic than with 5-FU and should be standard.
Gyn.commentary: This trial established giving weekly cisplatin without 5FU or other chemo
Gyn.references: 1. Rose PG, Bundy BN, Watkins EB, et al. Concurrent Cisplatin-Based Radiotherapy and Chemotherapy for Locally Advanced Cervical Cancer. N Engl J Med 1999;340:1144-53. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199904153401502

Gyn.patients: Stage IIB-IVA, or IB-IIA with tumor ≥ 5cm or + pelvic LN
Gyn.methods: ➜ 45 Gy EFRT + LDR to 85 Gy vs. ➜ 🏆 WPRT + LDR to 85 Gy + cisplatin/5-FU
Gyn.results: 8-yr OS 41% vs. 67% DFS 46% vs. 61% LRF 35% vs. 18% DM 35% vs. 20% Late effects unchanged.
Gyn.interpretation: The addition of chemotherapy to definitive RT improves OS, LR, and DM.
Gyn.references: 1. Morris M, Eifel PJ, Lu J, et al. Pelvic radiation with concurrent chemotherapy compared with pelvic and para-aortic radiation for high-risk cervical cancer. N Engl J Med 1999;340:1137-1143. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199904153401501 2. Eifel PJ, Winter K, Morris M, et al. Pelvic Irradiation With Concurrent Chemotherapy Versus Pelvic and Para-Aortic Irradiation for High-Risk Cervical Cancer: An Update of Radiation Therapy Oncology Group Trial (RTOG) 90-01. J Clin Oncol 2004;22:872-80. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2004.07.197

Gyn.patients: Stage IIB-IVA, or IB-IIA with tumor ≥ 5cm or + pelvic LN
Gyn.methods: →45 Gy EFRT + LDR to 85 Gy vs. →🏆 WPRT + LDR to 85 Gy + cisplatin/5-FU
Gyn.results: 8-yr OS 41% vs. 67% DFS 46% vs. 61% LRF 35% vs. 18% DM 35% vs. 20% Late effects unchanged.
Gyn.interpretation: The addition of chemotherapy to definitive RT improves OS, LR, and DM.
Gyn.references: 1. Eifel PJ, Winter K, Morris M, et al. Pelvic Irradiation With Concurrent Chemotherapy Versus Pelvic and Para-Aortic Irradiation for High-Risk Cervical Cancer: An Update of Radiation Therapy Oncology Group Trial (RTOG) 90-01. J Clin Oncol 2004;22:872-80. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2004.07.197

Gyn.patients: Stage IB-IIB N1 or Stage IIB with distal vaginal or parametrial involvement, or Stage III. PA nodes must be negative.
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT 45 Gy vs. ➜ EFRT 45 Gy
Gyn.results: No change in LC, DFS, or DM. Decreased PA failure noted.
Gyn.interpretation: EFRT does not improve LC, DFS, or DM. There was decreased PA failure.
Gyn.commentary: This trial done prior to IMRT era.
Gyn.references: 1. Haie C, Pejovic MH, Gerbaulet A, et al. Is prophylactic para-aortic irradiation worthwhile in the treatment of advanced cervical carcinoma? Results of a controlled clinical trial of the EORTC radiotherapy group. Radiother Oncol 1988;11:101-12. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/0167-8140(88)90245-9

Gyn.patients: IB-IIA ≥ 4cm, or IIB
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT 40-50 Gy vs. ➜ 🏆 EFRT 44-45 Gy → 30-40 Gy intracavitary brachytherapy to point A
Gyn.results: Benefit in OS and DM with EFRT 10-yr OS 44% vs. 55% EFRT DM 23% vs. 16% DFS ~42%, not different LRF 31-35%, not different Local salvage 8% vs. 25% Grade 4-5 adverse effects 4% vs. 8% (p=0.06)
Gyn.interpretation: EFRT has improved OS over WPRT with less DM and salvage. DFS and LRF are unchanged.
Gyn.commentary: This trial done prior to IMRT era.
Gyn.references: 1. Rotman M, Pajak TF, Choi K, et al. Prophylactic Extended-Field Irradiation of Para-aortic Lymph Nodes in Stages IIB and Bulky IB and IIA Cervical Carcinomas: Ten-Year Treatment Results of RTOG 79-20. JAMA 1995;274:387-93. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.1995.03530050035029

Gyn.trial: AC Camargo Cancer Center, São Paulo, Brazil
Gyn.patients: Meta-analysis to evaluate EFRT
Gyn.methods: 4 studies (RTOG 7920, EORTC, Osaka University, Chang Gung University)
Gyn.results: EFRT improved PA failure (HR 0.35), DM (HR 0.69) LRC and CSM were not different
Gyn.interpretation: EFRT reduces PA failure and DM. CSM was unchanged.
Gyn.commentary: All trials used 45 Gy to PA field
Gyn.references: 1. Sapienza LG, Gomes MJL, Calsavara VF, Leitao MM Jr., Baiocchi G. Does para-aortic irradiation reduce the risk of distant metastasis in advanced cervical cancer? A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials. Gynecol Oncol 2017;144:312-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ygyno.2016.11.044

Gyn.patients: locally advanced cervical cancer (FIGO 2008 stage IB1 and node positive, IB2, II, IIIB or IVA)
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 Definitive chemoRT vs. ➜ Definitive chemoRT with adjuvant carbo/taxol -RT: 45-50.4Gy EBRT + brachy -Adjuvant: q3w x4 carbo (AUC5)/taxol (155) mg/m2 -Concurrent: weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2
Gyn.results: No benefit to adjuvant chemo 5-yr OS 72%, not different 5-yr PFS 62%, not different 5-yr DM 14%, not different No benefit even if treatment completed
Gyn.interpretation: Adjuvant chemo after definitive chemoRT should not be employed. OS or PFS are not improved with the addition of adjuvant chemo compared to chemoRT alone.
Gyn.references: 1. Mileshkin, LR, Moore KN, Barnes E, et al. Adjuvant chemotherapy following chemoradiotherapy as primary treatment for locally advanced cervical cancer versus chemoradiotherapy alone (OUTBACK): an international, open-label, randomised, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2023. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(23)00147-X

Gyn.trial: NCI, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, México City, México
Gyn.patients: Stage IIB-IVA
Gyn.methods: ➜ EBRT 50.4 Gy + brachy 30-35 Gy with weekly cisplatin vs. ➜ Same RT + concurrent cis/gem + "outback" cis/gem
Gyn.results: Improved PFS with chemo 3-yr PFS 74% chemo vs. 65% worse grade 3/4 effects: 87% vs. 46% More hospitalizations 30% vs. 11%
Gyn.interpretation: Concurrent cis/gem with "outback" cis/gem improved PFS compared to standard definitive RT, but adverse effects was markedly increased.
Gyn.commentary: GOG 274 investigates further, removing gemcitabine as a component, which may contribute to the adverse effects in this trial.
Gyn.references: 1. Dueñas-González A, Zarbá JJ, Patel F, et al. Phase III, Open-Label, Randomized Study Comparing Concurrent Gemcitabine Plus Cisplatin and Radiation Followed by Adjuvant Gemcitabine and Cisplatin Versus Concurrent Cisplatin and Radiation in Patients With Stage IIB to IVA Carcinoma of the Cervix. J Clin Oncol 2011;29:1678-85. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2009.25.9663

Gyn.subtopic: Adjuvant chemoRT in int risk after surgery
Gyn.patients: cervical cancer stage I-IIA with: •LVI+ and ≥1 of: deep third stromal involvement, middle 1/3 and size ≥2 cm, superficial 1/3 and size ≥5 cm •LVI- with middle or deep involvement and size ≥4 cm
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT 50.4 Gy vs. ➜ WPRT with concurrent carbo/taxol weekly x6 cycles
Gyn.interpretation: There was no statistically significant improvement in RFS or OS with concurrent chemotherapy in intermediate risk post-op cervical cancer. There was however trend to improvement in RFS and OS, which could support concurrent chemotherapy, especially in those with multiple risk factors.
Gyn.references: 1. Ryu SY, Deng W, Albuquerque K, et al. Randomized Phase III Trial of Adjuvant Radiation versus Chemoradiation in Intermediate-Risk, Early-stage Cervical Cancer following Radical Hysterectomy and Lymphadenectomy: Results from NRG Oncology/GOG-263/KGOG1008. Ann Oncol. Published online September 12, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.annonc.2025.09.003

Gyn.patients: Cervix cancer stage IA2, IB or IIA squamous s/p radical hysterectomy with positive nodes, positive parametrium, positive paraaortic nodes (completely resected and PET negative after surgery)
Gyn.methods: 45-50.4 Gy + cisplatin → ➜ no further treatment vs. ➜ additional carbo/paclitaxel VC brachy allowed
Gyn.results: 4-yr DFS 76-77%, not different 4-yr OS 87%, not different
Gyn.interpretation: There is no benefit to additional chemotherapy after adjuvant concurrernt chemoradiation in post-op cervical cancer with high risk features.
Gyn.references: 1. Jhingran A. Adjuvant chemotherapy following concurrent chemoradiation (CRT) in patients with high-risk early-stage cervical carcinoma following radical hysterectomy: Results of NRG oncology/RTOG 0724/GOG-0724. J Clin Oncol 2024;42:s16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2024.42.16_suppl.5504

Gyn.patients: cervical cancer stage IB-IIA with ≥1 of LVSI, deep stromal invasion, N+, positive margin, or parametrial invasion
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT 45-50 Gy vs. ➜ WPRT with concurrent cisplatin vs. ➜ 🏆 WPRT with sandwich cisplatin and taxol Sequential: 2 cycles pre and 2 cycles post RT, q3w cis 60-75 mg/m2 and paclitaxel 135-175 mg/m2 Concurrent: weekly cisplatin 30-40 mg/m2 Neoadj chemo allowed for size >4 cm
Gyn.results: DFS, cancer death, and OS significantly improved with sequential vs. RT alone. DFS improved with sequential vs. concurrent WPRT vs. WPRT con vs. WPRT seq: 3-yr DFS 82% vs. 85% vs. 90% High risk (+margin, +node) (supp eFig 3) 3-yr DFS 67% vs. 68% vs. 83% 3-yr OS 78% vs. 80% vs. 90% Int risk only (eFig 3) 3-yr DFS 85% vs. 92% vs. 90% 3-yr OS 90% vs. 94% vs. 90% results were maintained across subgroups except for size >4 cm or received neoadjuvant chemo 5-yr cancer death 11% vs. 10% vs. 8% 5-yr OS 88% vs. 89% vs. 92% 5-yr DM 11% vs. 11% vs. 7%
Gyn.interpretation: Sequential chemotherapy and radiation has superior DFS and risk of cancer death than radiation alone. On subanalysis, in the high risk group only sandwich chemo gave benefit in DFS and OS. In the intermediate risk group, concurrent and sandwich chemo both gave benefit in DFS and OS. Sequential also had superior DFS compared to concurrent.
Gyn.commentary: Although a well designed trial, it isn't referenced in NCCN 2024 guidelines. The chemo dose was higher in the sandwich arm, which could explain the benefit. 73% completed sandwich and 62% completed concurrent. This study had n=287 with high risk factors, slightly more than Peters et al. The concurrent chemo here was different that in Peters: Peters had two cycles concurrent plus two adjuvant. Patient characteristics were imbalanced, favoring the single modality RT arm, but RT alone was still inferior to sandwich chemotherapy RT. In the RT arm 18% were node positive, and 30% were node positive …
Gyn.references: 1. Huang H, Feng YL, Wan T, et al. Effectiveness of Sequential Chemoradiation vs Concurrent Chemoradiation or Radiation Alone in Adjuvant Treatment After Hysterectomy for Cervical Cancer: The STARS Phase 3 Randomized Clinical Trial [published online ahead of print, 2021 Jan 14]. JAMA Oncol 2021;e207168. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2020.7168 Protocol pdf in supplements: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2020.7168
Gyn.commentary_refs: 1. NCCN. Cervical Cancer Version 1.2023. Available at: https://www.nccn.org/professionals/physician_gls/pdf/cervical.pdf Accessed June 1, 2023.

Gyn.patients: Stage I cervix s/p surgery and no chemo from GOG 49, 92, and 141
Gyn.methods: Ancillary analysis of GOG 49, 92, and 141 to determine risk of recurrence after surgery
Gyn.results: See publication for nomograms for 3-yr recurrence in SCC and ACC after surgery For SCC, DOI led to the most risk For ACC, tumor size and LVI were the biggest factors
Gyn.interpretation: A nomogram was developed to determine the risk of recurrence after surgery in Stage I cervical cancer.
Gyn.commentary: These nomograms replace the Sedlis criteria for indications for radiation therapy.
Gyn.references: 1. Levinson K, Beavis AL, Purdy C, et al. Beyond Sedlis-A novel histology-specific nomogram for predicting cervical cancer recurrence risk: An NRG/GOG ancillary analysis [published online ahead of print, 2021 Jun 30]. Gynecol Oncol 2021;S0090-8258(21)00511-4. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ygyno.2021.06.017

Gyn.citation: Sedlis et al, Gynecol Oncol, 1999 Rotman et al, IJROBP, 2006
Gyn.patients: Stage IB, N0 s/p radical hysterectomy with ≥2 risk factors of >1/3 stromal invasion, LVI, size ≥4 cm
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 WPRT 46 Gy or 50.4 Gy vs. ➜ obs Primary endpoint: PFS Not powered for OS
Gyn.results: 10-yr LR 14% vs. 21% 10-yr PFS 78% vs. 65% 10-yr OS 80% vs. 71%, p=0.07 12-yr recurrence adenocarcinoma or adenosquamous 8.8% vs. 44% obs acute grade 3 & 4 effects 7% vs. 2% late adverse effects not recorded
Gyn.interpretation: Adjuvant RT improves PFS and LR in int risk IB cervical cancer.
Gyn.commentary: On subanalysis, RT had a significant benefit in those with deep stromal invasion plus size ≥4 cm if no LVSI, and benefited those with LVSI plus deep stromal invasion in any size.
Gyn.references: 1. Sedlis A, Bundy BN, Rotman MZ, Lentz SS, Muderspach LI, Zaino RJ. A Randomized Trial of Pelvic Radiation Therapy versus No Further Therapy in Selected Patients with Stage IB Carcinoma of the Cervix after Radical Hysterectomy and Pelvic Lymphadenectomy: A Gynecologic Oncology Group Study. Gynecol Oncol 1999;73:177-83. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1006/gyno.1999.5387 2. Rotman M, Sedlis A, Piedmonte MR, et al. A phase III randomized trial of postoperative pelvic irradiation in stage IB cervical carcinoma with poor prognostic features: Follow-up of a gynecologic oncology group study. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2006;65:169-76. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2005.10.019

Gyn.patients: Stage IA2, IB, IIA s/p radical hysterectomy with ≥1 of node positive, positive margin or parametrial involvement
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT 49.3 Gy/ 29 fx vs. ➜ 🏆 WPRT + x2 cycles concurrent cis/5-FU and x2 adjuvant cis/5FU x4 If common iliac node+, 45 Gy/ 1.5 Gy daily to PA nodes given
Gyn.results: 4-yr OS 71% vs. 81% 4-yr PFS 63% vs. 80% No difference in recurrence patterns 60% completed all 4 cycles of chemo grade 4 adverse effects in 4% vs. 17% grade 5 in n=1 in CRT arm, but this patient declined chemo
Gyn.interpretation: Adjuvant cisplatin improves OS and PFS in resected cervical cancer with + nodes, margin, or parametrial involvement.
Gyn.commentary: Subanalysis was not planned or performed. Since this study, the STARS trial showed superior results with sandwich chemotherapy; however the concurrent regimen in STARS, weekly cisplatin, was different than the concurrent + adjuvant regimen in Peters.
Gyn.references: 1. Peters WA 3rd, Liu PY, Barrett RJ 2nd, et al. Concurrent Chemotherapy and Pelvic Radiation Therapy Compared With Pelvic Radiation Therapy Alone as Adjuvant Therapy After Radical Surgery in High-Risk Early-Stage Cancer of the Cervix. J Clin Oncol 2000;18:1606-13. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2000.18.8.1606

Gyn.patients: Patients with cervical cancer who underwent pre-op PET
Gyn.methods: Retrospective analysis
Gyn.results: -Periaortic nodes long term 4-5-yr DSS of ~20-40% (see figure) -SCV nodes long term 4-5-yr DSS of ~10-35% (see figure) -(Refer to paper for RFS and DSS plots for pelvic, periaortic, and SCV nodes per FIGO stage [Kaplan-Meier and HRs provided, not absolute values]) Risk of pelvic and PA nodes on PET: Stage I: 9-51% and 0-9% Stage IIA: 50%, 21% Stage IIB: 54%, 17% Stage IIIA 50%, 25% Stage IIIB/IV 55-85%, 13-38% Frequency of lymph node metastases was similar to historic surgical series
Gyn.interpretation: PET detected nodes help predict DSS. Long term DSS is possible in advanced stages, even with supraclavicular metastasis.
Gyn.commentary: A useful paper to show that patients with SCV and PA nodes can have long term DSS with chemoRT. Shows the value of PET in staging. Also an important study showing chance of LN metastasis.

Gyn.commentary: Early endometrial summary: -Aalders Norwegian trial shows benefit of WPRT+VCB in Stage I, especially in IB grade 3 -GOG99 and PORTEC-1 establish that the benefit with WPRT is in high intermediate risk groups. Most recurrences in vaginal cuff -PORTEC 2 shows VC brachy is noninferior to WPRT in high int risk -GOG 249 compared WPRT vs. VC+chemo in high intermediate risk and high risk early stage patients and found that VCB+chemo was not superior and had more pelvic recurrences and worse adverse effects. Thus WPRT remained standard. There is still rationale to treat HIR patients with VCB only per …

Gyn.citation: van den Heerik et al, Lancet Oncol, 2026
Gyn.patients: High intermediate risk IA, grade 3, any age, ± LVSI IB, grade 1-2, age >60 IB, grade 1-2, LVSI IB, grade 3, no LVSI II (microscopic), grade 1 These were then molecularly classified as: -Favorable: POLEmut -Intermediate: dMMR or CTNNB1mut -Unfavorable: p53abn, >10% L1CAMmut, or substantial LVSI
Gyn.methods: Molecularly guided therapy ➜ 🏆 obs for favorable (POLEmut) ➜ 🏆 VCB for intermediate (dMMR or CTNNB1mut) ➜ 🏆 WPRT for unfavorable (p53abn, >10% L1CAMmut, or substantial LVSI) vs. ➜ VCB 7 Gy x 3 for all
Gyn.results: •Favorable (POLEmut) 5-yr VCR 4.1% vs. 0.9% (noninferior) 5-yr pelvic recurrence first 0.6% vs. 3.2% 5-yr LRR 4.7% vs. 4.1% 5-yrr DM first 3.7% vs. 2.8% •Intermediate cohort (dMMR or CTNNB1mut) Results were not specifically reported, though the authors did share results for the combined molecular arm. This group was essentially unrandomized and had VCB in both arms. •Unfavorable (p53abn, >10% L1CAMmut, or substantial LVSI) 5-yr VCR 4.3% vs. 8.7% 5-yr pelvic recurrence first 2.0% vs. 15.7% 5-yr LRR 8% vs. 31%, p=0.05 5-yr DM 22% vs. 42%
Gyn.interpretation: POLEmut patients were able to be observed. Results were noninferior. Unfavorable (p53abn, >10% L1CAMmut, or substantial LVSI) benefited from WPRT. However other studies show that CTNNB1mut and L1CAMmut should perhaps be classified as NSMP. Results for the Intermediate risk cohort (dMMR or CTNNB1mut), which received VCB only in both arms, were not specifically reported.
Gyn.commentary: In their discussion the authors comment that some studies show that CTNNB1mut and L1CAMmut should perhaps be classified as NSMP. NSMP may benefit from further subdivision into ER positive and negative. ER positive have a worse prognosis. Studies have also shown that dMMR may not benefit from radiation or chemotherapy at all, and may be better served with immunotherapy. Further subanalysis would have been very underpowered. For POLEmut 5-yr VCR was 4.1% vs. 0.9% and determined to be noninferior. However numerically there seems to be a difference, suggesting that the prespecified noninferiority …
Gyn.references: 1. van den Heerik ASVM, Horeweg N, Haverkort MAD, et al. Gyn.subtopic: RT vs. VCB/C in HR and HIR
Gyn.patients: Stage I endometrial HIR Stage II endo Stage I-II serous or clear cell with negative cytology [74% stage I, 15% serous, 5% clear cell] HIR defined as: age ≥70 + 1 risk factor, age ≥50 + 2 risk factors, age ≥18 + 3 factors. Risk factors: grade 2 or 3, LVI, >50% myometrium
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 WPRT 45-50 Gy + optional VC for serous, pap or Stage II vs. ➜ VC brachy then carbo/paclitaxel x3 pelvic LND encouraged [used in 89%]
Gyn.results: Similar outcomes but worse acute adverse effects and more pelvic/PA relapse with VCB/C 5-yr OS ~86%, RFS 76%, not different pelvic or PA recurrence 4% vs. 9% VCR 2.5%, not different DM 18%, not different -On interactions analysis there is no favoring of treatment in any subgroup -Worse neuroadverse effects and fatigue with chemo -Acute adverse effects worse with chemo. Late adverse effects similar
Gyn.interpretation: VCB/C not superior to WPRT. Acute adverse effects was worse with VCB/C. Late adverse effects was similar. WPRT is an appropriate treatment option. However, DM remains elevated and investigation of other approaches is warranted.
Gyn.commentary: Molecular analysis is underway (Klopp A, email communication, Mar 2025). Why weren't 6 cycles of chemo used? The investigators had concern for overtreatment and adverse effects with 6 cycles. Results are similar to PORTEC 3, which shows no benefit to chemo added to WPRT in this population. Also compare to PORTEC 2, where VCB alone had more pelvic recurrences but these were simultaneous with DM. Note in GOG 249 that even DM is not reduced with chemo. VCB alone may still be sufficient in HIR per PORTEC 2 (de Boer 2019, Nout 2010). There is a possibility that the serous and clear cell histologies…

Gyn.subtopic: CRT vs. RT in HIR and advanced
Gyn.citation: de Boer et al, Lancet Oncol, 2018 de Boer et al, Lancet Oncol, 2019 León-Castillo et al, JCO, 2020 Wortman et al, IJROBP, 2021
Gyn.patients: IA with invasion, G3, and LVSI IB G3 II IIIA, IIIC, IIIB if parametrial Serous or clear cell histology: IA with invasion, IB, II, or III
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 WPRT + conc cisplatin x2 and adjuvant carbo/taxol x4 vs. ➜ WPRT 48.6 Gy + VCB for cervical invasion Prespecified molecular analysis
Gyn.results: 📌 Post-hoc adjusted analysis with longer f/u Benefit in OS, FFS, and DM ⬆️5-yr OS 81% vs. 76% ⬆️ 10-yr OS 74% vs. 67% ⬆️ 5-yr FFS 77% vs. 69% ⬆️ 10-yr FSS 73% vs. 67% 5-yr isolated DM 21% vs. 29% isolated pelvic (0.3%) and vaginal recurrence (0.9%) not different Benefits were only in Stage III on subanalysis ⬆️ Stage III 5-yr OS 79% vs. 69% ⬆️ Stage III 10-yr OS 70% vs. 56% ⬆️ Stage III 5-yr FFS 71% vs. 58% ⬆️ Stage III 10-yr FFS 67% vs. 56% ↔️ Stage I-II OS ~83%, FFS ~80%, not different 📌 Serous ⬆️ 5-yr OS 71% vs. 53% ⬆️ 10-yr OS 57% vs. 42% ⬆️ 5-yr FFS 60% vs. 48% ⬆️ 10-yr FFS 56% vs. 46% se…
Gyn.interpretation: There is OS and FFS benefit with chemotherapy added to RT on post-hoc analysis with longer f/u. (On initial reporting, there was no OS benefit.) On subanalysis the benefit is only present in Stage III. On histologic analysis the benefit is in serous, and in molecular analysis the benefit is only in p53abn (mainly in serous). There was no benefit in Stage I-II, POLEmut, dMMR, or NSMP. De-escalation and further trials may be warranted for some of the molecular groups. OS and RFS were outstanding in POLEmut in both arms. IMRT had less adverse effects than 3DCRT.
Gyn.commentary: PORTEC-3 is a landmark trial that revealed important information as to who benefits from chemotherapy added to WPRT, and began the transition to the molecular subtype era in post-op endometrial cancer. POLEmut. PORTEC 4a shows that early POLEmut can be observed. PORTEC3 shows no benefit with chemo in Stage I-III. GOG258 did not test POLEmut. NSMP. There is some trend to benefit with chemotherapy in PORTEC 3, 10-yr RFS p=0.13 and OS p=0.21 that warrants further investigation. There was RFS benefit with WPRT in GOG 258 after adjustment, though OS p=0.26. In NSMP in early stage in PORTEC 1&2, VCB…
Gyn.notes: Endometrial advanced summary -GOG 122 found a survival detriment with whole abdominal radiation therapy. But the trial was poorly designed and the RT technique is outdates -For years, RT was an option but generally not prioritized -Results of other studies are mixed, and various sequencing techniques used including chemo alone, sequential chemoRT, "sandwich" chemoRT, RT and adjuvant chemo, or radiation alone -A phase II trial, RTOG 9708, showed promising results with concurrent chemoRT followed by adjuvant chemoRT -Over 10 years after GOG 122, GOG 258 re-evaluates the role of radiation with co…

Outcomes were outstanding in all arms in PORTEC 1-3 in POLEmut, and in PORTEC 4a these HIR patients were able to be observed. RAINBO doesn't randomize this question. Gyn.commentary: This trial set a standard of VC brachytherapy for HIR patients. These patients were also included as eligible for GOG 249, which concluded WPRT to be standard (Randall 2019). However eligibility in the study may have been too broad. A combined analysis (Witlink 2014) showed no increase in 2nd cancers after RT in PORTEC 1, PORTEC 2, and patients who received pelvic RT for rectum after TME. POLEmut. PORTEC 4a shows that early POLEmut can be observed. PORTEC3 shows no benefit with chemo in Stage I-III. GOG258 did not test POLEmut. NSMP. There is some trend to benefit with chemotherapy in PORTEC 3…

Gyn.subtopic: VC brachy in grade 1-2 vs. obs
Gyn.citation: Sorbe et al, Int J Gynecol Cancer, 2009
Gyn.patients: Stage IA, grade 1-2, post-op
Gyn.methods: ➜ VCB 3-8 Gy/ 3-6 fx to 5 mm depth vs. ➜ obs
Gyn.results: Vaginal cuff recurrences 1.2% vs. 3.1%, p=0.114 Recurrence rate 4.0% overall (2.6% LRR and 1.4% DM)
Gyn.interpretation: Omission of VCB is safe in low risk patients.
Gyn.references: 1. Sorbe B, Nordström B, Mäenpää J, et al. Intravaginal brachytherapy in FIGO stage I low-risk endometrial cancer: a controlled randomized study. Int J Gynecol Cancer 2009;19(5):873-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1111/IGC.0b013e3181a6c9df

Gyn.citation: Creutzberg et al, Lancet, 2000 Creutzberg et al, IJROBP, 2001 Creutzberg et al, Gynecol Oncol, 2003 Scholten et al, IJROBP, 2005 Nout et al, JCO, 2011 Creutzberg et al, IJROBP, 2015 Horeweg et al, JCO, 2023
Gyn.patients: Stage I (no IC Gr 3 or IB Gr 1) with TAH/BSO (any histo allowed, but vast majority endo) HIR group (established post-hoc): need 2/3 factors of age >60, invasion >50%, or grade 3, or inv >50% plus grade 3 any age.
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 WPRT 46 Gy vs. ➜ observation Nodes: Sampling of suspicious LN. Routine LN dissection not done
Gyn.results: 5-yr LRR 4% WPRT vs. 14% obs 5-yr LR in vaginal vault 1.6% vs. 6.4% in vagina 0.7% vs. 3.9% in pelvis 2.0% vs. 3.4% in HIR group, LRR 4% vs. 23% 73% of first recurrences at vagina 5-yr LRR as first failure 13% with obs 10-yr LRR results identical Not different: 5-yr DM 7%, 5-yr OS ~82% Nomogram in Creutzberg et al, IJROBP, 2015 📌 Molecular analysis PORTEC 1&2 (2023) -POLEmut LRRFS 100% in all -dMMR LRRFS 90-94%, not different -p53abn ⬆️ LRRFS 97% WPRT vs. 64% VCB vs. 72% obs -NSMP ⬆️ LRRFS 98% WPRT vs. 96% VCB vs. 87% obs If vaginal recurrence, long term control 68% (most treated with radiatio…
Gyn.interpretation: WPRT reduces LR. OS is unchanged. At long term 15-yr f/u, WPRT has long-term GI and GU adverse effects and worse physical function. WPRT should be limited to HIR patients. On later post-hoc molecular analysis, p53abn benefits from WPRT, and VCB is preferred for NSMP. WPRT does not seem to improve outcomes in POLEmut or dMMR.
Gyn.commentary: This trial showed that WPRT resulted in LC benefit in HIR patients. Later PORTEC-2 trial showed that VCB in these patients gives similar benefit with less adverse effects (Nout 2010). POLEmut. PORTEC 4a shows that early POLEmut can be observed. PORTEC3 shows no benefit with chemo in Stage I-III. GOG258 did not test POLEmut. NSMP. There is some trend to benefit with chemotherapy in PORTEC 3, 10-yr RFS p=0.13 and OS p=0.21 that warrants further investigation. There was RFS benefit with WPRT in GOG 258 after adjustment, though OS p=0.26. In NSMP in early stage in PORTEC 1&2, VCB is preferred. dMM…
