Gyn.patients: Stage IA to IIB, Grade 1-3. Pap serous and clear cell allowed. Positive pelvic nodes allowed in ASTEC, but not in EN.5. PA nodes are excluded
Gyn.methods: →WPRT (median 45 Gy) vs. →obs The int and high risk patients from ASTEC LND trial were forwarded to this trial ~30% had pelvic LND VCB allowed if stated before randomization and used in both arms ASTEC: 2x4 Gy HDR, or 15 Gy LDR EN.5: VCB by local practice ~50% brachy given in both arms
Gyn.results: Isolated pelvic or vag recurrence improved: 3.2% WPRT vs. 6.1% No change in OS or DSS adverse effects worse with EBRT 57% WPRT vs. 27% moderate adverse effects 22% vs. 8% late adverse effects 8% vs. 3%
Gyn.interpretation: Adjuvant WPRT improved LC by a small amount compared to observation. adverse effects was increased.
Gyn.commentary: A major criticism of the trial is that if a patient was node+ after pelvic LND, randomization was not impacted. In other words node positive could enter into the no RT arm. 50% VCB 82% EBRT compliance 4% of all had positive nodes Groups are well balanced: median age 65, 83% endometrial histology, 25% LVSI, 29% with LND Note that pelvic recurrences rates are low in GOG 99 (LND allowed) and PORTEC-1 (no LND) both with and without RT. However vaginal recurrences were higher in GOG and PORTEC-1 than in ASTEC (Keys 2004, Creutzberg 2000).
Gyn.references: 1. ASTEC/EN.5 Study Group, Blake P, Swart AM, et al. Adjuvant external beam radiotherapy in the treatment of endometrial cancer (MRC ASTEC and NCIC CTG EN.5 randomised trials): pooled trial results, systematic review, and meta-analysis. Lancet 2009;373:137-146. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(08)61767-5

Gyn.patients: IB, IC, occult IIA,B s/p TAH/BSO Excluded: pap serous/clear cell, cytology Originally included intermediate risk, then amended to include HIR: age ≥70 with 1 risk factor, ≥50 with 2 risk factors, or any age with 3 risk factors. Factors are grade 2-3, LVI, or IC
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 WPRT 50.4 Gy vs. ➜ observation Nodes:Selective LN dissection of pelvis and PA nodes
Gyn.results: 2-yr LR 3% WPRT vs. 12% obs LR in vagina 2% vs. 13% (those in the WPRT group had refused RT) LR in pelvis only 0% vs. 4% 4-yr OS 92% vs. 86% (NS) (not primary endpoint) In HIR subgroup, 2-yr LR 6% vs. 26% Most recurrences occur within 18 mos
Gyn.interpretation: WPRT reduced LR, especially in the HIR subgroup.
Gyn.commentary: This trial showed that HIR patients benefit from adjuvant therapy. The later PORTEC 2 showed that VC brachy is as effective as WPRT and with less adverse effects. Note that pelvic recurrence in GOG 99 (which used LND) with no RT is 4%, same as PORTEC-1 (no LND allowed), which is 3.6% (Creutzberg 2000).
Gyn.references: 1. Keys HM, Roberts JA, Brunetto VL, et al. A phase III trial of surgery with or without adjunctive external pelvic radiation therapy in intermediate risk endometrial adenocarcinoma: a Gynecologic Oncology Group study. Gynecol Oncol 2004;92:744-51. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ygyno.2003.11.048

Gyn.patients: Stage I with at least on risk factor: Grade 3, IB, or DNA aneuploidy
Gyn.methods: 46 Gy plus VCB vs. VCB alone Doses: 3.0 Gy x 6, 5.9 Gy x 3, 20 Gy x 1 LDR to 5 mm, 2/3 length vagina
Gyn.results: 5-yr LRF 1.5% vs. 5% 15 pelvic failures in VCB alone arm vs. 1
Gyn.interpretation: The addition of WPRT to VCB reduces LRF in grade 3, IB, or DNA aneuploidy.
Gyn.commentary: More GI, GU, and vaginal adverse effects with WPRT.
Gyn.references: 1. Sorbe B, Horvath G, Andersson H, Boman K, Lundgren C, Pettersson B. External Pelvic and Vaginal Irradiation Versus Vaginal Irradiation Alone as Postoperative Therapy in Medium-Risk Endometrial Carcinoma: A Prospective Randomized Study. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2012;82:1249-55. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2011.04.014

Gyn.citation: Aalders et al, Obstet Gynecol, 1980 Onsrud et al, JCO, 2013
Gyn.patients: Stage I adeno with TAH/BSO
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 60 Gy VC brachy + WPRT 40 Gy vs. ➜ 60 Gy VC brachy Brachy was 40 Gy LDR to vaginal surface plus about 25 Gy HDR to 0.5 cm. Cobalt and betatron Nodes: LN sampling not allowed
Gyn.results: 9-yr LR 2% WPRT vs. 7% 9-yr DM 10% vs. 5% 9-yr OS 87-90%, not different In IC G3 tumors: LR 5% WPRT vs. 20% LVSI LR: 0% without vs. 20% with LVSI IC Grade 3 9-yr OS 72% vs. 82% Long term update 2013 JCO: After 20-yr followup, OS not changed, except decreased in patients age <60 due to 2nd cancer, HR 1.3. Risk of 2nd cancer increased HR 2.0. Most common 2nd malignancy was skin cancer.
Gyn.interpretation: WPRT improves LC compared to VCB alone. At 20 year f/u, OS was decreased with WPRT in age <60, likely due to second malignancy.
Gyn.commentary: This trial shows WPRT+VCB gives a survival benefit in IC Grade 3. Cobalt and betatron cause extra scatter. An secondary analysis (Wiltink 2014) showed no increase in secondary cancers after RT in PORTEC 1, PORTEC 2, and patients who received pelvic RT for rectum after TME. The Norway Radium Hospital merged with other hospitals and is now Oslo University Hospital.
Gyn.references: 1. Aalders J, Abeler V, Kolstad P, Onsrud M. Postoperative external irradiation and prognostic parameters in stage I endometrial carcinoma: clinical and histopathologic study of 540 patients. Obstetrics and gynecology 1980;56:419-27. PMID: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/6999399/ 2. Onsrud M, Cvancarova M, Hellebust TP, Tropé CG, Kristensen GB, Lindemann K. Long-Term Outcomes After Pelvic Radiation for Early-Stage Endometrial Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2013;31:3951-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2013.48.8023
Gyn.commentary_refs: Wiltink LM, Nout RA, Fiocco M, et al. No Increased Risk of Second Cancer After Radiotherapy in Patients Treated for Rectal or Endometrial Cancer in the Randomized TME, PORTEC-1, and PORTEC-2 Trials. J Clin Oncol 2015;33:1640-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2014.58.6693

Gyn.patients: Stage I endometrial CA who had TAH/BSO, lymphadenectomy, peritoneal cytology
Gyn.methods: Analyzed various factors for prognostication of lymph node positivity
Gyn.results: See selected editorialized info on risk of pelvic and PA nodes in commentary Increased grade and invasion correlates with probability of nodal mets. Low-risk: Gr 1, endometrium only. Mod-risk: Gr2-3 and/or inner-middle invasion. High-risk: deep invasion and/or intraperitoneal disease
Gyn.interpretation: Grade and depth of invasion are related to extrauterine disease
Gyn.commentary: Risk of pelvic nodes Grade 1 2 3 inner 1/3 | <5% | 5% | 10% middle 1/3 | 5% | 10% | 10% outer 1/3 | 10% | 20% | 35% Risk of PA nodes Grade 1 2 3 inner 1/3 |<5% | 5% | 5% middle 1/3 | 5% | 5% | 5% outer 1/3 | 5% | 15% | 25% [nonscientific interpolation shown; for original see paper]
Gyn.notes: Early endometrial summary: -Aalders Norwegian trial shows benefit of WPRT+VCB in Stage I, especially in IB grade 3 -GOG99 and PORTEC-1 establish that the benefit with WPRT is in high intermediate risk groups. Most recurrences in vaginal cuff -PORTEC 2 shows VC brachy is noninferior to WPRT in high int risk -GOG 249 compared WPRT vs. VC+chemo in high intermediate risk and high risk early stage patients and found that VCB+chemo was not superior and had more pelvic recurrences and worse adverse effects. Thus WPRT remained standard. There is still rationale to treat HIR patients with VCB only per …
Gyn.references: 1. Creasman WT, Morrow CP, Bundy BN, Homesley HD, Graham JE, Heller PB. Surgical pathologic spread patterns of endometrial cancer. A Gynecologic Oncology Group Study. Cancer 1987;60:2035-41. PMID: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3652025

Gyn.patients: Clinical Stage I endometrial cancer of all histologies and grades undergoing robotic staging
Gyn.methods: Prospective cohort. ➜ 🏆 SLN mapping with cervical injection of indocyanine green → pelvic LND ± PA LND Negative SLNs were ultrastaged with immunohistochemistry for cytokeratin
Gyn.results: SLN detected nodal mets in 97% of LNDs (in 12% with positive nodes on dissection) Sensitivity 97%, NPV 99.6% PA node dissection in 58% 86% had successful mapping of at least one node Post-op stage: 66% IA, 14% IB, 4% II, 3% IIA, 12% IIIC, 1% IV 82% endometrial, 43% grade 1, 29% grade 2, 11% grade 3, 12% serous, 4% carcinosarcoma, 2% clear cell, 1% other
Gyn.interpretation: SLN mapping has high sensitivity and NPV and can replace LND.
Gyn.commentary: Results might not apply to high grade or higher stage tumors. Note entry criteria is clinical stage I and entry criteria cannot be the post op stage. Few high grade and higher stage patients were diagnosed on pathology.
Gyn.references: 1. Rossi EC, Kowalski LD, Scalici J, et al. A comparison of sentinel lymph node biopsy to lymphadenectomy for endometrial cancer staging (FIRES trial): a multicentre, prospective, cohort study. Lancet Oncol 2017;18:384-92. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30068-2

Gyn.patients: Stage IA to IIB, Grade 1-3. Pap serous and clear cell allowed. Positive pelvic nodes allowed in ASTEC, but not in EN.5. PA nodes are excluded
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT (median 45 Gy) vs. ➜ obs The int and high risk patients from ASTEC LND trial were forwarded to this trial ~30% had pelvic LND VCB allowed if stated before randomization and used in both arms ASTEC: 2x4 Gy HDR, or 15 Gy LDR EN.5: VCB by local practice ~50% brachy given in both arms
Gyn.results: Isolated pelvic or vag recurrence improved: 3.2% WPRT vs. 6.1% No change in OS or DSS adverse effects worse with EBRT 57% WPRT vs. 27% moderate adverse effects 22% vs. 8% late adverse effects 8% vs. 3%
Gyn.interpretation: Adjuvant WPRT improved LC by a small amount compared to observation. adverse effects was increased.
Gyn.commentary: A major criticism of the trial is that if a patient was node+ after pelvic LND, randomization was not impacted. In other words node positive could enter into the no RT arm. 50% VCB 82% EBRT compliance 4% of all had positive nodes Groups are well balanced: median age 65, 83% endometrial histology, 25% LVSI, 29% with LND Note that pelvic recurrences rates are low in GOG 99 (LND allowed) and PORTEC-1 (no LND) both with and without RT. However vaginal recurrences were higher in GOG and PORTEC-1 than in ASTEC (Keys 2004, Creutzberg 2000).
Gyn.references: 1. ASTEC/EN.5 Study Group, Blake P, Swart AM, et al. Adjuvant external beam radiotherapy in the treatment of endometrial cancer (MRC ASTEC and NCIC CTG EN.5 randomised trials): pooled trial results, systematic review, and meta-analysis. Lancet 2009;373:137-146. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(08)61767-5

Gyn.citation: ASTEC study group et al, Lancet, 2009
Gyn.patients: disease limited to uterus body
Gyn.methods: ➜ pelvic LND vs. ➜ no PLND Patients int/high risk (33%) further randomized to ASTEC RT trial
Gyn.results: No change in RFS or OS with lymphadenectomy 9% positive nodes detected 5% crude rate of lymphedema On subanalysis, no difference with depth, histology, grade, age, or performance status
Gyn.interpretation: LND did not improved RFS or OS.
Gyn.commentary: Criticisms: If LND resulted in positive nodes, no adjuvant therapy was required. N dissection does provide staging information and guides therapy that could result in increased OS.
Gyn.references: 1. ASTEC study group, Kitchener H, Swart AM, Qian Q, Amos C, Parmar MK. Efficacy of systematic pelvic lymphadenectomy in endometrial cancer (MRC ASTEC trial): a randomised study [published correction appears in Lancet. 2009 May 23;373(9677):1764]. Lancet 2009;373:125-136. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(08)61766-3

Gyn.patients: early stage endometrial cancer
Gyn.methods: surgery ➜ pelvic LND vs. ➜ no PLND
Gyn.results: LN mets found: 13% with LND vs. 3% without No change in DFS or OS
Gyn.interpretation: Pelvic LND improved surgical staging but not DFS or OS.
Gyn.commentary: Although it's not obvious that LND improves outcomes, perhaps the improved staging may help better outcomes if it guides adjuvant therapies such as WPRT for LN+.
Gyn.references: 1. Panici PB, Basile S, Maneschi F, et al. Systematic Pelvic Lymphadenectomy vs No Lymphadenectomy in Early-Stage Endometrial Carcinoma: Randomized Clinical Trial. J Natl Cancer Inst 2008;100:1707-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/jnci/djn397

Gyn.patients: Clinical (not pathologic) Stage I-II endometrial adenocarcinoma, high operative risk
Gyn.methods: Retrospective eval of definitive endometrial RT. 79% patients treated with HDR alone, 21% treated with EBRT plus HDR. Median EBRT 42 Gy, median HDR 24 Gy in 3 fx
Gyn.results: 15-yr DSS 78% for all stages stage I DSS 90% and stage II DSS 42% If Stage I by MRI and at least 30 Gy HDR, then 10-yr DSS 100%
Gyn.interpretation: Single modality HDR has favorable results in Stage I endometrial cancer.
Gyn.references: 1. Niazi TM, Souhami L, Portelance L, Bahoric B, Gilbert L, Stanimir G. Long-term results of high-dose-rate brachytherapy in the primary treatment of medically inoperable stage I-II endometrial carcinoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2005;63:1108-13. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2005.04.036

Gyn.patients: Stage III, IVA, IVB, or recurrent endometrial cancer
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆pembrolizumab, carboplatin, and paclitaxel then pembrolizumab for 21 months vs. ➜ carboplatin and paclitaxel Prior radiation was permitted
Gyn.results: ddMMR 1-yr PFS 74% vs. 38% dMMR median PFS not reached vs. 7.6 mos pMMR median PFS 13.1 vs. 8.7 mos
Gyn.interpretation: Pembrolizumab added to chemo in Stage III, IV, or recurrent endometrial cancer improves PFS. There is benefit in both pMMR and dMMR, and there seems to be a larger benefit in dMMR.
Gyn.commentary: The distribution of stages was not reported in patient characteristics. It's unknown what stages benefit the most. In the RUBY trial there was no benefit in Stage III on subanalysis. Future trials will evaluate immunotherapy in dMMR in earlier stages. In subanalysis of dMMR tumors in GOG 258 and PORTEC 1-3, there's no indication yet of any benefit with either radiation or chemotherapy.
Gyn.references: 1. Eskander RN, Sill MW, Beffa L, et al. Pembrolizumab plus Chemotherapy in Advanced Endometrial Cancer. N Engl J Med 2023;388:2159-2170. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2302312

Gyn.patients: Stage III, IVA, IVB, or recurrent endometrial cancer 26-31% were stage III
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆dostarlimab, carboplatin, and paclitaxel then dostarlimab for up to 3 years vs. ➜ carboplatin and paclitaxel Prior radiation was permitted
Gyn.results: dMMR-MSI-H 2-yr PFS 61% vs. 15% pMMR 2-yr PFS 28% vs. 19% 2-yr OS 71% vs. 56% On subanalysis there was no benefit in Stage III (Figure S2)
Gyn.interpretation: Dostarlimab added to chemo in Stage III, IV, or recurrent endometrial cancer improves PFS. There is a larger benefit in dMMR-MSI-H.
Gyn.commentary: 26-31% were stage III. On subanalysis there was no benefit in Stage III (Figure S2). Future trials will evaluate immunotherapy in dMMR in earlier stages. In subanalysis of dMMR tumors in GOG 258 and PORTEC 1-3, there's no indication yet of any benefit with either radiation or chemotherapy.
Gyn.references: 1. Mirza MR, Chase DM, Slomovitz BM, et al. Dostarlimab for Primary Advanced or Recurrent Endometrial Cancer. N Engl J Med 2023;388:2145-2158. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2216334 Protocol pdf: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa2216334/suppl_file/nejmoa2216334_protocol.pdf

Gyn.commentary: Endometrial advanced summary -GOG 122 found a survival detriment with whole abdominal radiation therapy. But the trial was poorly designed and the RT technique is outdates -For years, RT was an option but generally not prioritized -Results of other studies are mixed, and various sequencing techniques used including chemo alone, sequential chemoRT, "sandwich" chemoRT, RT and adjuvant chemo, or radiation alone -A phase II trial, RTOG 9708, showed promising results with concurrent chemoRT followed by adjuvant chemoRT -Over 10 years after GOG 122, GOG 258 re-evaluates the role of radiation with co…

Gyn.citation: Leiden University Medical Center, ongoing protocol
Gyn.patients: Stage I-III endometrial cancer s/p resection POLEmut lower risk group: IA grade 3 ±LVSI IB grade 1-2 N0-1 ±LVSI IB grade 3 N0 ±LVSI II grade 1-2 N0 ±LVSI
Gyn.methods: POLEmut Stage I-III ➜ lower risk: no adjuvant therapy for low risk ➜ higher risk: no adjuvant therapy or radiation alone NSMP ER+ Stage II LVSI+ or Stage III ➜ chemoRT vs. ➜ chemoRT and progestin dMMR Stage II LVSI+ or Stage III ➜ WPRT vs. ➜ WPRT plus durvalumab p53abn ➜ chemoRT vs. ➜ chemoRT plus olaparib
Gyn.results: Ongoing study Primary endpoints NSMP, dMMR, p53abn: 3-yr RFS POLEmut: 3-yr LRR Secondary endpoints All trials: 3-yr and 5-yr VCRFS, DMFS, CSS, OS, adverse effects, QOL POLEmut: 5-yr LRR, 3-yr and 5-yr RFS, decisional conflict, fear of recurrence
Gyn.commentary: This study is conducted by PORTEC and available internationally. The trial investigates the benefit of new agents based on molecular profiles, and makes a few changes in chemo and radiation based on prior molecular analysis. However reconsideration of chemo and radiation in some additional subgroups is still warranted. POLEmut. Outcomes were outstanding in all arms in PORTEC 1-3 in POLEmut, and in PORTEC 4a these HIR patients were able to be observed. RAINBO doesn't randomize this question. However many clinics don't have access to POLEmut testing. NSMP. ER is a prognostic factor in NSMP, whic…
Gyn.references: 1. Leiden University Medical Center. Refining Adjuvant Treatment IN Endometrial Cancer Based On Molecular Features (RAINBO). Available from: https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05255653 NLM identifier: NCT05255653. Accessed 4 Oct 2024. 2. RAINBO Research Consortium. Refining adjuvant treatment in endometrial cancer based on molecular features: the RAINBO clinical trial program. Int J Gynecol Cancer. Published online December 20, 2022. Gyn.citation: Matei et al, NEJM, 2019 Clements et al, SGO, 2024 Clements et al, Int J Gynecol Oncol, 2024 Matei et al, JCO, 2024
Gyn.patients: any Stage III-IVA (serous papillary and undifferentiated carcinomas allowed) Stage I-II clear cell or serous carcinoma Lymph node dissection optional
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT 45 Gy + cisplatin + VCB (for cervix or vaginal involvment) then carbo/paclitaxel x4 vs. ➜ carbo/taxol x6
Gyn.results: ⛔ 5-yr RFS 59% RT vs. 58%, p=0.20 ⛔ 5-yr OS not different Median OS not reached in either arm ⬇️ 5-yr VCR 2% RT vs. 7% ⬇️ 5-yr Pelvic/PA recurrence 11% vs. 20% 5-yr DM 27% vs. 21% (p not provided) On subgroup analysis there was RFS benefit in NSMP. No OS benefit in any subgroup including stage, histology, molecular group, age, residual disease, BMI. For NSMP OS was p=0.26 📌 Molecular analysis NSMP 5-yr RFS 77% vs. 60%, p=0.02 p53abn 5-yr RFS 29% both arms dMMR 5-yr RFS 53% vs. 64%, not different POLEmut testing not done NSMP 10-yr OS 76% vs. 71%, p=0.26 p53abn 10-yr OS 23-25%, not different dM…
Gyn.interpretation: RT with concurrent and adjuvant chemo did not improve RFS or OS compared to chemo alone. ChemoRT had less VCR, LN recurrence, and adverse effects. Chemo alone had less DM, possibly due to more cycles being given. On molecular analysis, after adjustment there was RFS improvement in NSMP with WPRT, but otherwise there was no improvement in OS or RFS with radiation in any subgroup.
Gyn.commentary: Characteristics. 49% were NSMP, 24% p53abn, and 27% dMMR. 75% in the CRT arm completed therapy. There was 85% completion for chemo alone. 18% of patients were serous, 3% clear cell. The abstract refers to p53wt and dMMR negative as simply p53wt, which I have renamed NSMP here to be more consisent with PORTEC. Note also that POLEmut was not evaluated. POLEmut. PORTEC 4a shows that early POLEmut can be observed. PORTEC3 shows no benefit with chemo in Stage I-III. GOG258 did not test POLEmut. NSMP. There is some trend to benefit with chemotherapy in PORTEC 3, 10-yr RFS p=0.13 and OS p=0.21 that w…

Gyn.subtopic: CRT vs. RT in HIR and advanced
Gyn.citation: de Boer et al, Lancet Oncol, 2018 de Boer et al, Lancet Oncol, 2019 León-Castillo et al, JCO, 2020 Wortman et al, IJROBP, 2021 Post et al, Lancet Oncol, 2025
Gyn.patients: IA with invasion, G3, and LVSI IB G3 II IIIA, IIIC, IIIB if parametrial Serous or clear cell histology: IA with invasion, IB, II, or III
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 WPRT + conc cisplatin x2 and adjuvant carbo/taxol x4 vs. ➜ WPRT 48.6 Gy + VCB for cervical invasion Prespecified molecular analysis
Gyn.results: 📌 Post-hoc adjusted analysis with longer f/u Benefit in OS, FFS, and DM ⬆️5-yr OS 81% vs. 76% ⬆️ 10-yr OS 74% vs. 67% ⬆️ 5-yr FFS 77% vs. 69% ⬆️ 10-yr FSS 73% vs. 67% 5-yr isolated DM 21% vs. 29% isolated pelvic (0.3%) and vaginal recurrence (0.9%) not different Benefits were only in Stage III on subanalysis ⬆️ Stage III 5-yr OS 79% vs. 69% ⬆️ Stage III 10-yr OS 70% vs. 56% ⬆️ Stage III 5-yr FFS 71% vs. 58% ⬆️ Stage III 10-yr FFS 67% vs. 56% ↔️ Stage I-II OS ~83%, FFS ~80%, not different 📌 Serous ⬆️ 5-yr OS 71% vs. 53% ⬆️ 10-yr OS 57% vs. 42% ⬆️ 5-yr FFS 60% vs. 48% ⬆️ 10-yr FFS 56% vs. 46% se…
Gyn.interpretation: There is OS and FFS benefit with chemotherapy added to RT on post-hoc analysis with longer f/u. (On initial reporting, there was no OS benefit.) On subanalysis the benefit is only present in Stage III. On histologic analysis the benefit is in serous, and in molecular analysis the benefit is only in p53abn (mainly in serous). There was no benefit in Stage I-II, POLEmut, dMMR, or NSMP. De-escalation and further trials may be warranted for some of the molecular groups. OS and RFS were outstanding in POLEmut in both arms. IMRT had less adverse effects than 3DCRT.
Gyn.commentary: PORTEC-3 is a landmark trial that revealed important information as to who benefits from chemotherapy added to WPRT, and began the transition to the molecular subtype era in post-op endometrial cancer. POLEmut. PORTEC 4a shows that early POLEmut can be observed. PORTEC3 shows no benefit with chemo in Stage I-III. GOG258 did not test POLEmut. NSMP. There is some trend to benefit with chemotherapy in PORTEC 3, 10-yr RFS p=0.13 and OS p=0.21 that warrants further investigation. There was RFS benefit with WPRT in GOG 258 after adjustment, though OS p=0.26. In NSMP in early stage in PORTEC 1&2, VCB…
Gyn.notes: Endometrial advanced summary -GOG 122 found a survival detriment with whole abdominal radiation therapy. But the trial was poorly designed and the RT technique is outdates -For years, RT was an option but generally not prioritized -Results of other studies are mixed, and various sequencing techniques used including chemo alone, sequential chemoRT, "sandwich" chemoRT, RT and adjuvant chemo, or radiation alone -A phase II trial, RTOG 9708, showed promising results with concurrent chemoRT followed by adjuvant chemoRT -Over 10 years after GOG 122, GOG 258 re-evaluates the role of radiation with co…

Gyn.citation: Hogberg et al, Eur J Cancer, 2010
Gyn.patients: Operable endometrial Stage I-III with no residual tumor and presence of high risk factors. Serous and clear cell also allowed
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 WPRT 45 Gy → chemo vs. ➜ WPRT alone EFRT and VCB allowed
Gyn.results: RT vs. chemo-RT 5-yr PFS 69% vs. 78% 5-yr OS 75% vs. 82%, p=0.07 Subanalysis: benefit only for serous/clear cell, not endometriod
Gyn.interpretation: The addition of adjuvant chemotherapy after RT improves PFS compared to RT alone.
Gyn.references: 1. Hogberg T, Signorelli M, de Oliveira CF, et al. Sequential adjuvant chemotherapy and radiotherapy in endometrial cancer--results from two randomised studies. Eur J Cancer 2010;46:2422-31. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejca.2010.06.002

Gyn.patients: Stage IC - IIIC s/p surgery
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT vs. ➜ Cyclo/doxo/cis x3
Gyn.results: No difference in OS or PFS 5-yr PFS 83% vs. 82% 5-yr OS 85% vs. 87% On subanalysis, benefit in high risk High risk (IC and age>70, IC G3, II, IIIA from +cytology): PFS 66% vs. 84% chemo High risk OS 74% vs. 90%
Gyn.interpretation: Overall there was no difference in OS or PFS. On subanalysis OS and PFS were improved in high risk tumors.
Gyn.references: 1. Susumu N, Sagae S, Udagawa Y, et al. Randomized phase III trial of pelvic radiotherapy versus cisplatin-based combined chemotherapy in patients with intermediate- and high-risk endometrial cancer: A Japanese Gynecologic Oncology Group study. Gynecol Oncol 2008;108:226-33. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ygyno.2007.09.029

Gyn.trial: Università degli Studi di Milano, Milano, Italy
Gyn.citation: Maggi et al, Br J Cancer, 2006
Gyn.patients: High risk endometrial: IC grade 3, II grade 3 and >50% invasion, stage III
Gyn.methods: ➜ WPRT 45-50 Gy vs. ➜ cis/doxo/cyclo x5
Gyn.results: No differences in OS or PFS
Gyn.interpretation: There was no difference in OS or PFS between chemo and RT.
Gyn.references: 1. Maggi R, Lissoni A, Spina F, et al. Adjuvant chemotherapy vs radiotherapy in high-risk endometrial carcinoma: results of a randomised trial. Br J Cancer 2006;95:266-71. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1038/sj.bjc.6603279

Gyn.patients: Stage III or IV with ≤2cm residual post-op disease
Gyn.methods: ➜ cisplatin/doxorubcin vs. ➜ Whole abdomen RT (30 Gy/ 20 fx with 15 Gy boost)
Gyn.results: 5-yr PFS 38% RT vs. 42% chemo 5-yr OS 42% vs. 53%. P value not provided Arms were imbalanced, so stage adjustment was done: 5-yr PFS 38% vs. 50% 5-yr OS 42% vs. 52% With chemo, more grade 3-4 heme and GI adverse effects 63% completed chemo
Gyn.interpretation: Chemotherapy improves PFS and OS compared with WART.
Gyn.commentary: Residual disease was allowed - the RT dose might not have been sufficient. Relapse rates were high in both arms. Significance came only after adjustment for stage imbalance in the arms. A later modernized trial, GOG 258, was performed to re-evaluate this question.
Gyn.references: 1. Randall ME, Filiaci VL, Muss H, et al. Randomized Phase III Trial of Whole-Abdominal Irradiation Versus Doxorubicin and Cisplatin Chemotherapy in Advanced Endometrial Carcinoma: A Gynecologic Oncology Group Study. J Clin Oncol 2006;24:36-44. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2004.00.7617

Gyn.patients: Locally recurrent endometrial cancer
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 radiation alone vs. ➜ radiation with concurrent chemotherapy
Gyn.results: Median PFS not reached vs. 73 mos 3-yr PFS 73% vs. 62%
Gyn.interpretation: Concurrent chemotherapy with radiation worsened PFS compared to radiation alone in locally recurrent endometrial cancer.
Gyn.commentary: 82% were grade 1-2 and 86% of recurrences were located in the vagina. Hopefully we'll see a posthoc molecular analysis on this study.
Gyn.references: 1. Klopp AH, Enserro D, Powell M, et al. Radiation Therapy With or Without Cisplatin for Local Recurrences of Endometrial Cancer: Results From an NRG Oncology/GOG Prospective Randomized Multicenter Clinical Trial. J Clin Oncol 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.23.01279

Gyn.citation: Reed et al, Eur J Cancer, 2008
Gyn.patients: leiomyosarcoma, carcinosarcoma, ESS s/p surgery
Gyn.methods: ➜ obs vs. ➜ WPRT 50.4 Gy
Gyn.results: LR 40% vs. 21%. On subanalysis, better effect in carcinosarcoma. DM unchanged, 7-15% Not powered to evaluate OS
Gyn.interpretation: RT improves LC in carcinosarcoma.
Gyn.references: 1. Reed NS, Mangioni C, Malmström H, et al. Phase III randomised study to evaluate the role of adjuvant pelvic radiotherapy in the treatment of uterine sarcomas stages I and II: An European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer Gynaecological Cancer Group Study (protocol 55874). Eur J Cancer 2008;44:808-18. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejca.2008.01.019

Gyn.patients: vaginal SCC s/p definitive RT
Gyn.methods: Retrospective review from MDACC
Gyn.results: 5-yr DSS: 85% for Stage I, 78% for II, 58% for III-IVA; 82% for ≤4cm tumors and 60% for >4cm. Relapse mainly locoregional (68% for early stage, 83% for late). Vaginal control: I-II 91%, III-IVA 83% Pelvic control: I 86%, II 84%, II-IVA 71%
Gyn.interpretation: Definitive RT leads to favorable outcomes in vaginal cancer.
Gyn.commentary: This study provides useful outcome data. For modern planning techniques refer to ABS guidelines (Beriwal 2012).
Gyn.references: 1. Frank SJ, Jhingran A, Levenback C, Eifel PJ. Definitive radiation therapy for squamous cell carcinoma of the vagina. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2005;62:138-47. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2004.09.032

Gyn.commentary: Vulva summary -Heaps study finds risk factors for recurrence. These are then used as indications for RT -GOG 37 finds OS benefit with WPRT after LND over pelvic LND alone in palpable N+, ECE, ulcerated, and ≥2 nodes -GOG 88 finds that groin dissection is superior to inguinal RT -A number of trials investigate the role of SLNB and LND (GOG 173, GROINSS V and VII) -GROINSS VII finds that RT can replace groin dissection in SLNB+ of size ≤2 mm -Phase II trials for advanced tumors (GOG 101, GOG 205) show the effectiveness of chemoRT for gross disease, possibly as a tool to downstage for resection

Gyn.citation: University Medical Center Groningen, ongoing protocol
Gyn.patients: Clinically node negative with SLN+ of size >2 mm and/or ECE, or ≥1 SLN of size ≤2 mm depth >1mm, T1, <4 cm, not encroaching urethra, vagina, or anus
Gyn.methods: Phase II →SLN+ of size >2 mm and/or ECE, or ≥1 SLN of size ≤2 mm→ →56 Gy SIB 48-50 Gy to involved groin with concurrent cisplatin
Gyn.results: Ongoing protocol Primary endpoint: 2-yr groin recurrence Secondary endpoints: adverse effects, QOL, and vulvar specific QOL using VU34
Gyn.references: 1. University Medical Center Groningen. Groningen International Study on Sentinel Nodes in Vulvar Cancer-III (GROINSS-VIII). Accessed March 29, 2024. Available from: https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05076942 2. Gien LT, Slomovitz B, Van der Zee A, Oonk M. Phase II activity trial of high-dose radiation and chemosensitization in patients with macrometastatic lymph node spread after sentinel node biopsy in vulvar cancer: GROningen INternational Study on Sentinel nodes in Vulvar cancer III (GROINSS-V III/NRG-GY024). Int J Gynecol Cancer 2023;33:619-622. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1136/ijgc-2022-004122 Full text: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10233466/

Gyn.patients: depth >1mm, T1-T2N0, <4 cm, not encroaching urethra, vagina, or anus
Gyn.methods: Phase II SLNB→ ➜ Adjuvant RT for any SLN+ ➜ Observe if SLN- If unilateral N+, choice of RT to 1 or both sides. Chemo optional Protocol amended to the following after excess failures: •≤2mm nodal micromet, no ECE: ➜ 🏆 50 Gy/25-28 fx to ipsilateral groin & ipsilateral low pelvis •>2mm nodal macromet or ECE: (this arm designed after excess failures in >2mm) ➜ complete LND without RT RT added if ≥2 LN+ or ECE (given in 56%)
Gyn.results: •≤2 mm cohort: per protocol groin failure 1.6% •>2 mm cohort: RT alone 2-yr groin failure 22% LND (±RT if indicated) 2-yr groin failure 7% 56% in LND arm had RT for >1 LN or ECE after LND grade 3 adverse effects in 4% 1-yr lymphedema 4% SLNB, 11% SLNB +RT, 23% LND Trend to better groin control with chemoRT, p=0.91 Contralateral recurrences: -In micromet cohort, only one contralateral recurrence in a SLN negative groin that had RT to both sides -In macromet cohort, no contralateral recurrences in an unirradiated groin
Gyn.interpretation: In micrometastasis of ≤2 mm on SLNB, radiation therapy can replace LND. In macrometastasis of >2 mm on SLNB, LND alone (with RT given for multiple nodes or ECE) had less recurrences than RT alone (without LND) in this trial. However it is difficult to draw conclusions: prior to 2015, nodal volumes were inadequately contoured.
Gyn.commentary: The groin nodes were not contoured fully prior to 2015: only a 5 mm margin around vessels was required. In 2015 the protocol was amended. Furthermore 56% in the LND alone arm (size >2 mm) still received RT for ≥2 nodes positive or ECE. Therefore the trial may be underpowered and it can be difficult to draw conclusions. And since the macromets arm was nonrandomized, there is a possibility for imbalances in the two cohorts even though the stopping rule was met. The vulva was only included in RT field if specifically indicated. Chemotherapy was optional. The trend to better control with chemoRT s…
Gyn.references: 1. Oonk MHM, Slomovitz B, Baldwin PJW, et al. Radiotherapy Versus Inguinofemoral Lymphadenectomy as Treatment for Vulvar Cancer Patients With Micrometastases in the Sentinel Node: Results of GROINSS-V II [published online ahead of print, 2021 Aug 25]. J Clin Oncol 2021;JCO2100006. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.21.00006

Gyn.citation: Te Grootenhuis et al, Gynecol Oncol, 2016
Gyn.patients: T1, <4cm, clinically negative inguinal LNs
Gyn.methods: SLN ➜ if positive, inguinal LND
Gyn.results: •SLN positive 5/10-yr LR 33%/46% 5-yr groin recurrence 8% 10-yr DSS 91% for SLNB- and 65% for SLNB+
Gyn.interpretation: Inguinal LND guided by SNLB leads to low groin failure.
Gyn.references: 1. Te Grootenhuis NC, van der Zee AG, van Doorn HC, et al. Sentinel nodes in vulvar cancer: Long-term follow-up of the GROningen INternational Study on Sentinel nodes in Vulvar cancer (GROINSS-V) I. Gynecol Oncol 2016;140:8-14. doi: 10.1016/j.ygyno.2015.09.077. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ygyno.2015.09.077

Gyn.subtopic: False negative and positive in SLN bx
Gyn.patients: ≥1 mm invasion, size ≥2 cm to <6 cm, clinically node negative
Gyn.methods: Phase II ➜ SLN → inguinal dissection to all (regardless of SLN status)
Gyn.results: 8.3% with false negative nodes FN predictive value 3.7% For tumors <4 cm, FNPV 2.0%
Gyn.interpretation: SLNB has favorable FNR and is a reasonable approach.
Gyn.references: 1. Levenback CF, Ali S, Coleman RL, et al. Lymphatic mapping and sentinel lymph node biopsy in women with squamous cell carcinoma of the vulva: a gynecologic oncology group study. J Clin Oncol 2012;30:3786-91. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2011.41.2528

Gyn.citation: Van der Zee et al, JCO, 2008
Gyn.patients: T1-2, <4cm, DOI >1 mm, clinically negative inguinal LNs
Gyn.methods: SLN ➜ if negative, no LND
Gyn.results: •SLN negative 5/10-yr LR 25%/36% 5-yr groin recurrence 2.5% wound breakdown 12% vs. 34% Lymphedema 2% vs. 25%
Gyn.interpretation: Inguinal LND guided by SNLB leads to low groin failure.
Gyn.commentary: Worsening of DSS after LR supports the practice of treating the vulva with RT.
Gyn.references: 1. van der Zee AGJ, Oonk MH, Hullu JAD, et al. Sentinel Node Dissection Is Safe in the Treatment of Early-Stage Vulvar Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2008;26:884-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2007.14.0566

Gyn.patients: clinically N0
Gyn.methods: Radical vulvectomy → ➜ 50 Gy inguinal RT to 3 cm depth vs. ➜ 🏆 groin dissection + pelvic dissection if positive
Gyn.results: Closed early due to excessive recurrences in RT arm. 19% relapse in RT vs. 0 in surgery. 20% in surgery arm were N+ and treated with RT OS 63% vs. 88%
Gyn.interpretation: Groin dissection has superior OS and LC compared to inguinal RT.
Gyn.commentary: Criticism: Dose Rx was to 3 cm, leading to underdosage. LF of 18% is comparable to historical GOG failure rate of 24%.
Gyn.references: 1. Stehman FB, Bundy BN, Thomas G, et al. Groin dissection versus groin radiation in carcinoma of the vulva: a Gynecologic Oncology Group study. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1992;24:389-96. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/0360-3016(92)90699-i

Gyn.citation: Homesley et al, Obstet Gynecol, 1986 Kunos et al, Obstet Gynecol, 2009
Gyn.patients: Positive groin nodes after vulvectomy and lymphadenectomy
Gyn.methods: ➜ 🏆 RT 45-50 Gy to bilateral pelvic and inguinal nodes (excludes primary) vs. ➜ pelvic node resection
Gyn.results: 2-yr OS 68% RT vs. 54% surgery On subanalysis, effect only in ECE and ≥2 nodes 6-yr groin recurrence 5% vs. 24% 6-yr OS 51% vs. 41% (benefit only in palpable N+, ECE, ulcerated, or ≥2 nodes) Lymphedema 16 vs. 22% (NS)
Gyn.interpretation: Adjuvant RT improved OS in palpable N+, ECE, ulcerated, and ≥2 nodes, compared to pelvic LND after radical vulvectomy and inguinal LND.
Gyn.commentary: This could be viewed as showing the benefit of RT vs obs. Why randomize vs. pelvic nodal dissection? Pelvic nodes in vulva are considered metastatic.
Gyn.references: 1. Homesley HD, Bundy BN, Sedlis A, Adcock L. Radiation therapy versus pelvic node resection for carcinoma of the vulva with positive groin nodes. Obstetrics and gynecology 1986;68:733-40. PMID: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/3785783/ 2. Kunos C, Simpkins F, Gibbons H, Tian C, Homesley H. Radiation therapy compared with pelvic node resection for node-positive vulvar cancer: a randomized controlled trial. Obstetrics and gynecology 2009;114:537-46. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/AOG.0b013e3181b12f99

Gyn.patients: Stage III/IV with +LN treated with RT and midline block
Gyn.methods: Retrospective review of recurrence patterns based on use of midline block. Median dose 45 Gy. 26/27 had midline block.
Gyn.results: 48% recurrence within midline block. 63% recurrences total
Gyn.interpretation: Midline block in pelvic RT leads high recurrence rates within the block.
Gyn.commentary: This study may be used to support inclusion of the primary when treating nodes, but do observe its small sample size and retrospective nature.
Gyn.references: 1. Dusenbery KE, Carlson JW, Laporte RM, et al. Radical vulvectomy with postoperative irradiation for vulvar cancer: Therapeutic implications of a central block. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1994;29:989-98. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/0360-3016(94)90393-x

Gyn.patients: s/p surgical resection
Gyn.methods: Retrospective review. Pathologic features associated with local recurrence
Gyn.results: Local recurrence risk factors in order of significance: Stage, Margin, Depth, Growth pattern, Vascular invasion, Keratin amount, Mitotic activity NOT: Tumor size, Nucleoli, grade Margin <8mm had LR of 48% Margin <5mm had LR of 57%
Gyn.interpretation: Risk factors for LR in vulvar cancer are outlined.
Gyn.commentary: These risk factors are used as indications for treatment with radiation therapy. Ideally risk factors would be evaluated more formally with phase II, randomized, or perhaps external validation studies.
Gyn.notes: Vulva summary -Heaps study finds risk factors for recurrence. These are then used as indications for RT -GOG 37 finds OS benefit with WPRT after LND over pelvic LND alone in palpable N+, ECE, ulcerated, and ≥2 nodes -GOG 88 finds that groin dissection is superior to inguinal RT -A number of trials investigate the role of SLNB and LND (GOG 173, GROINSS V and VII) -GROINSS VII finds that RT can replace groin dissection in SLNB+ of size ≤2 mm -Phase II trials for advanced tumors (GOG 101, GOG 205) show the effectiveness of chemoRT for gross disease, possibly as a tool to downstage for resection

Gyn.subtopic: Neoadjuvant chemoRT in unresectable, dose escalation
Gyn.methods: Phase II: ➜ RT 57.6 Gy/ 32 fx + weekly cis ➜ resection cCR defined by biopsy, pCR defined by surgical resection
Gyn.results: cCR 64% pCR in 50% [compare to 40% in GOG 101] (59% had surgery)
Gyn.interpretation: Dose escalation in neoadjuvant chemoRT further improves pCR compared to historical trial.
Gyn.references: 1. Moore DH, Ali S, Koh W-J, et al. A phase II trial of radiation therapy and weekly cisplatin chemotherapy for the treatment of locally-advanced squamous cell carcinoma of the vulva: A gynecologic oncology group study. Gynecol Oncol 2012;124:529-33. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ygyno.2011.11.003

Gyn.subtopic: Neoadjuvant chemoRT in unresectable, dose escalation, IMRT
Gyn.patients: locally advanced vulva T2-T3, N0-3, not amenable to resection
Gyn.methods: Phase II: ➜ 🏆 IMRT 64 Gy (45-50 Gy to groin and pelvis) + cis/ gem → resection Goal: pCR 65% or greater 60 Gy boost for 3+ LN, ECE, or +margin, boost to 64 Gy for gross disease
Gyn.results: pCR 73% 2-yr PFS 70%
Gyn.interpretation: 64 Gy radiation with concurrent cisplatin and gemcitabine leads to favorable pCR and PFS.
Gyn.references: 1. Horowitz NS, Deng W, Peterson I, Mannel RS, Thompson S, Lokich E, Myers T, Hanjani P, O'Malley DM, Chung KY, Miller DS, Ueland FR, Dizon DS, Miller A, Mayadev JS, Leath CA 3rd, Monk BJ. Phase II Trial of Cisplatin, Gemcitabine, and Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy for Locally Advanced Vulvar Squamous Cell Carcinoma: NRG Oncology/GOG Study 279. J Clin Oncol 2024:JCO2302235. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.23.02235 Study protocol: Refer to appendix 2 https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.23.02235

Gyn.citation: Moore et al, IJROBP, 1998 Montana et al, IJROBP, 2000
Gyn.patients: unresectable T3 or T4, unresectable N2 or N3
Gyn.methods: Phase II: ➜ split course 47.6 Gy RT + cis/5FU Nodes included for N2-N3 disease
Gyn.results: 55% OS. Nodal pCR 40% and primary pCR 30%
Gyn.interpretation: Neoadjuvant chemoRT is effective with high pCR and favorable OS.
Gyn.references: 1. Montana GS, Thomas GM, Moore DH, et al. Preoperative chemo-radiation for carcinoma of the vulva with N2/N3 nodes: a gynecologic oncology group study. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2000;48:1007-13. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0360-3016(00)00762-8 2. Moore DH, Thomas GM, Montana GS, Saxer A, Gallup DG, Olt G. Gyn.trial: National Institute for Cancer Research, Genova, Italy
Gyn.citation: Chiara et al, Am J Clin Oncol, 1994
Gyn.patients: Ovarian cancer high risk FIGO Stage I-II
Gyn.methods: ➜ Cisplatin+cyclophosphamide vs. ➜ whole abdominal RT (43.2 Gy to pelvis & 30.2 Gy to upper abdomen)
Gyn.results: No difference in 5-yr RFS/OS. Trend towards worse outcome with WART. 5-yr OS 71% vs. 53% WART, p=0.16 5-yr RFS 74% vs. 50%, p=0.07 Chemo adverse effects: grade 3 emesis 71% WART adverse effects: grade 2-4 diarrhea 28%, severe enteritis in n=2
Gyn.interpretation: There is trend to worse OS and RFS with WART compared to chemo, though results were not significant.
Gyn.commentary: The RT technique used is now historical. Protocol violations occurred in 8 patients in WART group. Closed due to poor accrual. Nonetheless it can be conceded that results do appear favorable with chemotherapy.
Gyn.references: 1. Chiara S, Conte P, Franzone P, et al. High-risk early-stage ovarian cancer. Randomized clinical trial comparing cisplatin plus cyclophosphamide versus whole abdominal radiotherapy. Am J Clin Oncol 1994;17:72-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/00000421-199402000-00016

H&N.citation: Peters et al, IJROBP, 1993 Rosenthal et al, IJROBP, 2017
H&N.patients: Stage III or IV oral cavity, oropharynx, hypopharynx, larynx who underwent surgery who required post-op RT
H&N.methods: Low risk: ➜ 🏆 57.6 Gy (dose changed to 57.6 after excess failures with 52.2) vs. ➜ 63 Gy High risk: ➜ 🏆 63 Gy vs. ➜ 68.4 Gy Stratified into low and high risk by a point system now not in use. Head and neck were separately scored. Any positive margin was high risk. However ENE could be low or high risk based on extent. Other factors were # of nodes, margin distances, sizes.
H&N.results: 📌 Final report (over 20-yr follow-up): 5-yr to 20-yr LRC not different 5-yr LRC 67% 5/10-yr DM 36/40% 5/10-yr OS 32/20% 2nd cancers 27% Factors impacting LRC & OS: S->RT completion time ≥85 days, +margin, ECE Factor leading to worse OS: age ≥57 📌 Initial report: 2-yr crude LF worse with <54 Gy than with ≥ 57.6 Gy. No difference between 57.6 Gy and ≥63 Gy overall. ENE results seem best with ≥63 Gy based on crude LR On UVA, ENE was the only independent risk factor for recurrence (regardless of dose). Trend to worse recurrence with ≥2 nodes, p=0.08. There was increasing recurrence risk with ≥2 ri…
H&N.interpretation: At early interim analysis, LF was worse with <54 Gy and the arm was changed to 57.6 Gy. ENE seems to benefit from dose escalation to ≥63 Gy based on a crude subanalysis at 2 years. There is no benefit in LRC with dose escalation from 63 Gy → 68.4 Gy in the high risk group or 57.6 → 63 Gy in the low risk group at any point in f/u, up to 20 years. Chemo was not used in this study.
H&N.commentary: This trial laid the foundation for dosing in intermediate and high risk post-op H&N cancer. There are a few important points to note in context of modern statistical approaches and staging. Observe that the trial endorses 63 Gy for ENE, not 66 Gy. Note that ENE was allowed in both groups and not randomized, and the finding that ≥63 Gy is ideal for ENE was determined by subanalysis, which was simply a review of crude recurrence rates by risk factors (Table 9). Today, a subanalysis such as MVA would have been performed with p values. Modern standards in statistical approaches in these situations…
H&N.references: 1. Peters LJ, Goepfert H, Ang KK, et al. Evaluation of the dose for postoperative radiation therapy of head and neck cancer: First report of a prospective randomized trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1993;26:3-11. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/0360-3016(93)90167-t 2. Rosenthal DI, Mohamed ASR, Garden AS, et al. Final Report of a Prospective Randomized Trial to Evaluate the Dose-Response Relationship for Postoperative Radiation Therapy and Pathologic Risk Groups in Patients With Head and Neck Cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2017;98:1002-11. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2017.02.218

H&N.citation: van den Bosch et al, JCO, 2025
H&N.patients: unresected cT2-4N0-2 head and neck any site
H&N.methods: Noninferiority: accelerated RT 68 Gy/34 fx in 5.5 weeks ➜ 🏆 43 Gy ENI vs. ➜ 50 Gy ENI
H&N.results: 2-yr recurrence in ENI areas 4.9% vs. 4.3% Less dysphagia and xerostomia with dose reduction Normalcy of diet score similar
H&N.interpretation: Reduced dose in results in similar recurrence rates with less adverse effects. However indeterminate nodes were allowed to be boosted in the reduced dose arrm.
H&N.commentary: In their commentary Awan and Yao (2026) point out important nuances in the methods: in the 43 Gy arm, nodes with activity ≥2x background received 70 Gy, and indeterminate nodes of summed axis ≥1.7 cm or uptake >1.5x & <2x background received 60 Gy. In other words the dose reduction arm was escalated in some aspects.
H&N.references: 1. van den Bosch S, Doornaert PAH, Hoebers FJP, et al. Clinical Benefit and Safety of Reduced Elective Dose in Definitive Radiotherapy for Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma: The UPGRADE-RT Multicenter Randomized Controlled Trial. J Clin Oncol 2025;43:2583-2594. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-24-02194 Study protocol: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO-24-02194/suppl_file/protocol_JCO-24-02194.pdf
H&N.commentary_refs: 1. Awan MJ, Yao M. Read Between the (Isodose) Lines. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2026;124:96-97. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2025.08.016

H&N.citation: Nevens et al, Radiother Oncol, 2017 Nevens et al, Radiother Oncol, 2017 Deschuymer et al, Radiother Oncol, 2020
H&N.patients: Any stage or site head and neck cancer planned to receive definitive RT with elective neck RT LN dissection was allowed and done in ~20%
H&N.methods: Noninferiority: ➜ 50 Gy to elective nodes vs. ➜ 40 Gy to elective nodes Doses in normalized effective dose in 2 Gy fractions. 70 Gy in NID 2 Gy per fraction to primary tumor. All patients treated with IMRT
H&N.results: Reduction of salivary adverse effects with 40 Gy 2-yr LF 3.9% in 40 Gy arm, similar to 50 Gy 5-yr RR 8% vs. 14%, p=0.10 5-yr DM 24% vs. 13%, p=0.07 5-yr OS ~50-55%, not different Only 2 recurrences in low dose PTV in both arms
H&N.interpretation: At 5 year follow-up, there is no difference RR. Low recurrences in the low dose PTV in both arms suggests 40 Gy may be adequate. There is a possibility the study is underpowered.
H&N.commentary: The trial may be underpowered. However, low recurrences in both arms is not a negative thing. Still, the trial has higher N than the MDACC post-op trial which had two randomizations. Only about 20% had neck dissection. Some believe that post-op neck requires higher dose, but this concept is employed for few to no other post-op disease sites. It is possible however that results do not apply to post-op neck. If so, post-op neck may require 57.6 Gy per MDACC. Both studies were performed prior to the HPV testing era. The trial used iso-effective 2 Gy fractions. reflecting modern practice for many …
H&N.references: 1. Nevens D, Duprez F, Daisne J-F, et al. Recurrence patterns after a decreased dose of 40 Gy to the elective treated neck in head and neck cancer. Radiother Oncol 2017;123:419-23. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2017.03.003 2. Nevens D, Duprez F, Daisne JF, et al. Reduction of the dose of radiotherapy to the elective neck in head and neck squamous cell carcinoma; a randomized clinical trial. Effect on late toxicity and tumor control. Radiother Oncol 2017;122:171-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2016.08.009 3. Sarah D, Daan N, Frederic D, et al. Randomized clinical trial on reduction of radiotherapy dose to the elective neck in head and neck squamous cell carcinoma; update of the long-term tumor outcome. Radiother Oncol 2020;S0167-8140:30017-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2020.01.005

H&N.trial: University of Nijmegan Medical Center, Huispost, Netherlands
H&N.citation: van den Bosch et al, IJROBP, 2016
H&N.patients: H&N cT2-4, N0-2 SCC treated with accelerated RT
H&N.methods: Retrospective. 1166 lymph nodes localized in elective neck fields and sites of recurrence reconstructed and analyzed based on dose received IMRT SIB accelerated to 68 Gy in 2.00 Gy for boost and 50.3 Gy in 1.48 Gy for elective volume. Last 14 treatments delivered BID.
H&N.results: •Elective neck node recurrence 5.1% •LN size of 17 mm sum of long and short diameters is risk factor for recurrence •Recurrence in nodes usually happens with LR •In nodes receiving 60 Gy no recurrences seen
H&N.interpretation: Risk factors are presented to determine borderline nodes. Borderline nodes may be controlled with 60 Gy. 45-50 Gy EQD2 controls elective neck otherwise.
H&N.notes: H&N dose overview: -MDACC Peters initially showed 57.6 Gy needed for post-op low risk neck and 63 Gy for ECE -Concominant boost becomes standard from RTOG 9003 fx study -66 Gy used for ECE and +M with concurrent chemo in EORTC and RTOG. Benefit in OS with chemo for ECE and +M -RTOG 0022 outlines use of IMRT SIB to spare salivary glands, combining MDACC Peters and CCB into an IMRT SIB regimen -Doses as low as 45-50 Gy may be reasonable for untreated neck in Belgium study
H&N.references: 1. van den Bosch S, Dijkema T, Verhoef LCG, et al. Patterns of Recurrence in Electively Irradiated Lymph Node Regions After Definitive Accelerated Intensity Modulated Radiation Therapy for Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2016;94:766-74. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2015.12.002

H&N.patients: Head and neck
H&N.methods: Network meta-analysis of meta-analyses: MARCH and MACH 115 randomized trials
H&N.results: Best OS with hyperfx + concurrent chemo HR 0.63 (vs. without chemo) HR 0.82 (vs. daily RT with concurrent cis) Results maintained with sensitivity analyses
H&N.interpretation: Hyperfractionation with concurrent chemotherapy has the best OS in this large meta-analysis.
H&N.references: 1. Petit C, Lacas B, Pignon JP, et al. Chemotherapy and radiotherapy in locally advanced head and neck cancer: an individual patient data network meta-analysis [published online ahead of print, 2021 Apr 13]. Lancet Oncol 2021;S1470-2045(21)00076-0. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(21)00076-0

H&N.patients: HPV negative Stage III-IV H&N cancer
H&N.methods: Phase I/II ➜ Hyperfx accel RT 76 Gy/ 56 fx BID with cisplatin + nimorazole Primary endpoint: LRF
H&N.results: 3-yr LRF 21% 3-yr OS 74% acute severe dysphagia in 67% acute severe mucositis in 61% late severe dysphagia in n=2, severe xerostomia in n=1, severe fibrosis in n=3, osteoradionecrosis in n=1, permanent PEG in n=3
H&N.interpretation: Hyperfx accel RT wih cisplatin and nimorazole is feasible. Late adverse effects might be considered high by some.
H&N.commentary: This is the first trial to investigate hyperfractionation with chemotherapy with IMRT, and also to ask a fractionation question specifically in HPV negative tumors. Acute severe dysphagia of 67% and permanent PEG in 3/50 is high. The results certainly lessen enthusiasm for hyperfractionation with concurrent chemotherapy, but perhaps results would have been different without nimorazole or with more restrictive dose constraints. A trial from Sun Yat-Sen University did show OS benefit with hyperfractionation compared to daily fractionation, both without concurrent chemotherapy, for recurrent naso…
H&N.references: 1. Saksø M, Jensen K, Andersen M, et al. DAHANCA 28: A phase I/II feasibility study of hyperfractionated, accelerated radiotherapy with concomitant cisplatin and nimorazole (HART-CN) for patients with locally advanced, HPV/p16-negative squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx, hypopharynx, larynx and oral cavity. Radiother Oncol 2020;148:65-72. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2020.03.025
H&N.commentary_refs: 1. You R, Liu YP, Xie YL, et al. Hyperfractionation compared with standard fractionation in intensity-modulated radiotherapy for patients with locally advanced recurrent nasopharyngeal carcinoma: a multicentre, randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial. Lancet 2023;401:917-927. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(23)00269-6

H&N.patients: Head and neck definitive and post-op
H&N.methods: Meta-analysis of 15 trials evaluating the benefit of altered fractionation (defined as either hyperfx, accelerated, or accel with dose reduction) compared to conventional fx Update: Meta-analysis of 33 trials Comparison 1: Primary or post op conv fx vs. altered fx Comparison 2: conv fx chemoRT vs. altered fx RT alone
H&N.results: • Compared to primary or post-op conv fx, altered fx benefit in OS: 5-yr +3.1% and 10-yr +1.2% • OS benefit only in hyperfx group: 5-yr OS +8.1% and 10-yr OS +3.9% • Compared to conv fx chemo RT, OS worse with hyperfx RT alone, 5-yr OS -5.8% and 10-yr OS -5.1%
H&N.interpretation: Hyperfx RT, as well as conv fx chemoRT, are standards of care for H&N. Hyperfx RT with chemo remains to be tested.
H&N.notes: H&N fractionation overview: -RTOG 9003 results initially show CCB and hyperfx both improve LC; since more convenient, accel-CCB was favored. Split course no longer in use -CCB goes on to become standard and have OS benefit with chemo -Long term 9003 follow-up shows hyperfx has best OS, LC and the least late effects -MARCH HN meta-analysis also confirms hyperfx gives best OS -Overgaard DAHANCA and later IAEA show better LC with 6 fx per week, compared to conventional, if no chemo -Transition from 2D to IMRT with RTOG 0222, using SIB technique, like a modified CCB, to spare salivary glands -Whet…
H&N.references: 1. Lacas B, Bourhis J, Overgaard J, et al. Role of radiotherapy fractionation in head and neck cancers (MARCH): an updated meta-analysis. Lancet Oncol 2017;18:1221-37. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30458-8
H&N.commentary_refs: 1. Blanchard, P. @PBlanchardMD. Twitter. March 25, 2021. Available at: https://twitter.com/PBlanchardMD/status/1374985184177704962. Accessed March 25, 2021.

H&N.citation: Fu et al, IJROBP, 2000 Beitler et al, IJROBP, 2014
H&N.patients: Stage III-IV (or II for BOT/hypopharynx), previously untreated, to have primary RT
H&N.methods: ➜ Conventional 70/35 fx in 2Gy vs. ➜ 🏆 Hyperfx 81.6/68 fx in 7 wks in 1.2Gy BID vs. ➜ Accel Split: 67.2/42 fx in 6 wks at 1.6Gy BID with 2-wk break at 38.4Gy vs. vs. ➜ Accel-con boost: 72Gy/42 fx in 6 wks at 1.8Gy and 1.5Gy IMRT not allowed
H&N.results: Initial: Hyperfrac and delayed concominant boost had better LRC (54%), with trend for better DFS. OS not different. Update: Hyperfx increased OS, LC when 5 year patients are censored, and decreased late effects. 97% of LR occurred within 5 years. After five years, more second cancers arose though small amounts. Severe late grade 3+ adverse effects trended worse for Accel.
H&N.interpretation: Hyperfrac and accelerated concominant boost result im improved outcomes and lower adverse effects than standard fractionation
H&N.commentary: Performed before IMRT era. Concurrent chemo was not used. There is criticism that some of the long term results were only significant when data after 5 years was censored. On initial results, accelerated concominant boost seemed more favorable and led to hyperfractionation falling out of favor. Do the long term results warrant a reconsideration of hyperfx radiation? The effectiveness of hyperfx without chemo is unclear; the popular SIB regimens today are used with chemo in the definitive setting. BID scheduling has also been impeded by its inconvenience for some clinics.
H&N.references: 1. Fu KK, Pajak TF, Trotti A, et al. A radiation therapy oncology group (RTOG) phase III randomized study to compare hyperfractionation and two variants of accelerated fractionation to standard fractionation radiotherapy for head and neck squamous cell carcinomas: first report of RTOG 9003. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2000;48:7-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/s0360-3016(00)00663-5 2. Beitler JJ, Zhang Q, Fu KK, et al. Final Results of Local-Regional Control and Late Toxicity of RTOG 9003: A Randomized Trial of Altered Fractionation Radiation for Locally Advanced Head and Neck Cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2014;89:13-20. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2013.12.027

H&N.patients: glottic, supraglottic pharynx, oral cavity not receiving chemo, eligible for curative RT
H&N.methods: ➜ 5 fx per week vs. ➜ 🏆 6 fx per week 66-70 Gy/ 33-35 fx, no chemo [no nimorazole as in DAHANCA 6/7]
H&N.results: 5-yr LRC 42% 6 fx vs. 30% 5 fx 5-yr OS 35% vs. 28%, p=0.07 5-yr DSS 50% vs. 40% More acute skin and mucositis in accel RT. No difference in late adverse effects
H&N.interpretation: For those not receiving chemo, six fractions per week of RT results in better LC and DFS than 5 fractions per week
H&N.commentary: This study repeated the DAHANCA regimen but this time without nimorazole
H&N.references: 1. Overgaard J, Mohanti BK, Begum N, et al. Five versus six fractions of radiotherapy per week for squamous-cell carcinoma of the head and neck (IAEA-ACC study): a randomised, multicentre trial. Lancet Oncol 2010;11:553-60. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(10)70072-3

H&N.citation: Overgaard et al, Lancet, 2003 Lyhne et al, Radiother Oncol, 2015
H&N.patients: glottic, supraglottic pharynx, oral cavity not receiving chemo, treated with primary RT
H&N.methods: ➜ 5 fx per week vs. ➜ 🏆 6 fx per week 66-68 Gy/ 33-34 fx, no chemo Nimorazole for all except for glottic
H&N.results: 5-yr LRC 70% 6 fx vs. 60% 5 fx DAHANCA 6 14.5-yr LRF 22% vs. 29% Larynx preservation 80% vs. 68% 5-yr DSS 73% vs. 66% No change in OS Acute morbidity more frequent with 6 fx but was transient
H&N.interpretation: For those not receiving chemo, six fractions per week of RT with nimorazole results in better LC and DFS than 5 fractions per week
H&N.commentary: Note the use of the hypoxic radiosensitizer nimorazole in this trial in all sites but glottic.
H&N.references: 1. Overgaard J, Hansen HS, Specht L, et al. Lancet 2003;362:933-40. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0140-6736(03)14361-9 2. Lyhne NM, Primdahl H, Kristensen CA, et al. The DAHANCA 6 randomized trial: Effect of 6 vs 5 weekly fractions of radiotherapy in patients with glottic squamous cell carcinoma. Radiother Oncol 2015;117:91-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2015.07.004

H&N.patients: Post-op H&N Low risk: 0 factors int risk: 1 factor, no ECE high risk: ENE or ≥2 factors Factors: oral site, PNI, N+, ENE, +margin
H&N.methods: Low risk ➜ no PORT Intermediate risk ➜ 57.6 Gy High risk - randomized: ➜ 63 Gy/5 wks accel vs. ➜ 63 Gy/7 wks conv + 2 wks CCB
H&N.results: Low Risk: 90% LRC, 83% OS Int risk: 94% LRC, 66% OS High Risk: 68% LRC, 42% OS Trend to higher LRC when RT delivered in 5 vs. 7 weeks for high risk
H&N.interpretation: Low risk is unlikely to benefit from adjuvant RT. Int risk has favorable results with moderate dose adjuvant RT. In high risk there is opportunity for improvement in LRC. Split course was less effective.
H&N.commentary: Time <11 weeks gave better survival and LRC
H&N.references: 1. Ang KK, Trotti A, Brown BW, et al. Randomized trial addressing risk features and time factors of surgery plus radiotherapy in advanced head-and-neck cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2001;51:571-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/s0360-3016(01)01690-x

H&N.patients: locally advanced H&N cancer
H&N.methods: Noninferiority ➜ 66 Gy/33 fx, 6 fx per week, SIB 52.8 Gy vs. ➜ 🏆 55 Gy/20 fx, SIB 44 Gy cisplatin optional Primary endpoints: LRC and grade ≥3 adverse effects
H&N.results: 3-yr LRC 51% both arms (noninferior) 3-yr OS ~55% 3-yr PFS ~45% Late grade 3+ adverse effects ~20% both arms
H&N.interpretation: 55 Gy/20 fx hypofractionation is noninferior to 66 Gy in 33 fractions in head and neck cancer.
H&N.commentary: 66 Gy in 6 fx per week was chosen as the control arm based on the prior study IAEA-ACC (2010), showing that the schedule was more effective than conventional fractionation. IMRT was used in 70%, 3D in 7%, and 2D in 22.5%. This trial was designed in part due to COVID-19. The trial was performed in 10 middle- and low- income countries around the world. An incredible global effort! The trial was sponsored by the IAEA. Some may ask that results be reproduced in modern therapy in high income nations. The high power of the study makes the results compelling.
H&N.references: Study protocol: https://www.redjournal.org/cms/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2025.12.056/attachment/e57a5fb4-2476-47ff-adef-eb7b80c55aab/mmc2.pdf 1. Bentzen SM, Gupta T, Jacinto AA, et al. H&N.patients: post op int risk: close margin ≤5 mm, T3-T4a, N2a-N2b, PNI, LVI, T2 oral cavity >5 mm depth
H&N.methods: ➜ 60 Gy vs. ➜ 60 Gy with cetuximab 56 Gy SIB to low risk areas
H&N.results: Due to lower than expected deaths, an interim analysis was performed early 5-yr OS 69% vs. 77%, p=0.07 5-yr DFS 64% vs. 72%, p=0.02 5-yr LRF 25% vs. 19%, p=0.07
H&N.interpretation: Cetuximab improves DFS when combined with adjuvant radiation therapy for intermediate risk head and neck cancer.

H&N.patients: post op high risk: ENE, positive margin, or close 3 mm margin
H&N.methods: RT 66/60/56 Gy SIB. Randomized: Arm 1: weekly cis, 40mg/m2 Arm 2: weekly docetaxol 15mg/m2 Arm 3: weekly docetaxol 15 mg /m2 plus cetuximab
H&N.results: accruing
