H&N.subtopic: Addition of chemo to post-op RT
H&N.citation: Laskar et al, Eur J Cancer, 2023
H&N.patients: locally advanced, resected oral cavity tumors with ENE, positive margin, Stage III/IV, >2 nodes, PNI with N+, LVI with N+, major reconstruction required (only 0.4% included had positive margins)
H&N.methods: ➜ 56-60 Gy/ 28-30 fx, 5 fx per wk vs. ➜ same RT & concurrent weekly cisplatin vs. ➜ 56-60 Gy, 6 fx per wk, no chemo
H&N.results: No difference in 5-yr LRC or OS, even with high risk features including T stage, N stage, and ENE. There was benefit for T3-T4 plus N2-3 plus ENE 5-yr LRC ~60-65%
H&N.interpretation: The addition of chemo to adjuvant RT or 6 fx RT without chemo did not improve outcomes in post-op oral cavity tumors with high risk or intermediate risk factors.
H&N.commentary: 55% had ENE and only 0.4% had positive margins 90% T3-T4 and 48% N2
H&N.references: 1. Laskar SG, Chaukar D, Deshpande M, et al. Oral cavity adjuvant therapy (OCAT) -a phase III, randomized controlled trial of surgery followed by conventional RT (5 fr/wk) versus concurrent CT-RT versus accelerated RT (6fr/wk) in locally advanced, resectable, squamous cell carcinoma of oral cavity. Eur J Cancer 2023;181:179-187. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejca.2022.12.016

H&N.subtopic: Addition of chemo to post-op RT
H&N.citation: Bernier et al, Head Neck, 2005 Lu et al, IJROBP, 2022
H&N.patients: post-op H&N with risk factors as above
H&N.methods: Combined analysis of EORTC 22931 and RTOG 9501 A later combined analysis also included RTOG 0234 to evaluate findings with rising nodal count
H&N.results: ⬆️ Chemo improved OS in ENE and positive margin ↔️ No OS benefit if int risk factors only (p=0.33 in EORTC and p=0.78 in RTOG) No interaction between # lymph nodes and benefit with chemo (DM p=0.8, OS p=0.161, DFS p=0.45) (this analysis also included RTOG 0234)
H&N.interpretation: The addition of chemo to adjuvant RT improves OS in post-op H&N tumors with ENE or positive margins. There is no improvement in intermediate risk factors including number of nodes.
H&N.commentary: If one considers the combined EORTC 22931 RTOG 9501 analysis as exploratory and limited in its methodology, EORTC 22931 might be used to support an OS benefit with chemotherapy in those with multiple intermediate risk factors. MVA or adjustment for risk factors for example was not performed. Statistical approaches for post-hoc analysis have improved, and plenty of useful ones exist (consider STAMPEDE, EORTC 22881, 9413 & Ottawa, PORTECs, even EBCTCG). One must be cautious of seeing what they want to see in subanalysis, which goes both ways. Nature does not have a clear cutoff for significance,…
H&N.references: 1. Bernier J, Cooper JS, Pajak TF, et al. Defining risk levels in locally advanced head and neck cancers: A comparative analysis of concurrent postoperative radiation plus chemotherapy trials of the EORTC (#22931) and RTOG (# 9501). Head Neck 2005;27:843-50. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/hed.20279 2. Lu DJ, Luu M, Gay C, et al. Nodal Metastasis Count and Oncologic Outcomes in Head and Neck Cancer: A Secondary Analysis of NRG/RTOG 9501, NRG/RTOG 0234, and EORTC 22931. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2022;113:787-795. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2022.03.033

H&N.subtopic: Addition of chemo to post-op RT
H&N.patients: ≥1 of T3-4 (except larynx T3N0), T1-2N2-3, or (T1-2N0-1 with ENE, +M, PNI, or LVI), or (OC or OPX with level IV or V nodes)
H&N.methods: ➜ Post-op RT 66 Gy (54 Gy to low risk) vs. ➜ 🏆 same RT + cisplatin x3
H&N.results: ⬆️ 5-yr PFS 36% vs. 47% ⬆️ 5-yr OS 40% vs. 53% ⬆️ 3-yr OS 49% vs. 65% ⬆️ 5-yr LRC 69% vs. 82% DM not reported
H&N.interpretation: The addition of chemo to adjuvant RT improves OS and LRC in post-op H&N tumors with risk factors.
H&N.commentary: If one considers the combined EORTC 22931 RTOG 9501 analysis as exploratory and limited in its methodology, EORTC 22931 might be used to support an OS benefit with chemotherapy in those with multiple intermediate risk factors. MVA or adjustment for risk factors for example was not performed. Statistical approaches for post-hoc analysis have improved, and plenty of useful ones exist (consider STAMPEDE, EORTC 22881, 9413 & Ottawa, PORTECs, even EBCTCG). One must be cautious of seeing what they want to see in subanalysis, which goes both ways. Nature does not have a clear cutoff for significance,…
H&N.references: 1. Bernier J, Domenge C, Ozsahin M, et al. Postoperative Irradiation with or without Concomitant Chemotherapy for Locally Advanced Head and Neck Cancer. N Engl J Med 2004;350:1945-52. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa032641

H&N.subtopic: Addition of chemo to post-op RT
H&N.citation: Cooper et al, NEJM, 2004 Cooper et al, IJROBP, 2012
H&N.patients: operable H+N with ≥2 N+, ENE, or + margin
H&N.methods: ➜ Post-op RT 60 Gy ± 6 Gy boost vs. ➜ 🏆 same RT + cisplatin x3
H&N.results: ⬆️ 2-yr LRC 72% vs. 82%, p=0.01 ⬆️ 5-yr LRC 74% vs. 84% ⬆️ 10-yr LRC 71% vs. 88% ↗️ 2-yr OS 57% vs. 63%, p=0.19 ⬆️ 5-yr OS 27% vs. 29%, p=0.31 DM ~20% in both arms, not different For positive margins or ENE: ⬆️ 10-yr LRC 67% vs. 79% 10-yr OS 20% vs. 27%, p=0.07
H&N.interpretation: The addition of chemo to adjuvant RT improves LRC in post-op H&N tumors with risk factors. The subset of those with positive margin or ENE had benefit in OS.
H&N.commentary: What doses did the two trials use? EORTC used 66 Gy (Bernier 2004), and although RTOG used a base dose of 60 Gy, a 6 Gy boost was allowed to total 66 Gy Why is LRC rising with longer f/u? LRC does not factor in those who have died. This is why many now endorse the endpoints of LRFFS and DMFS.
H&N.references: 1. Cooper JS, Pajak TF, Forastiere AA, et al. Postoperative Concurrent Radiotherapy and Chemotherapy for High-Risk Squamous-Cell Carcinoma of the Head and Neck. N Engl J Med 2004;350:1937-44. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa032646 2. Cooper JS, Zhang Q, Pajak TF, et al. Long-term Follow-up of the RTOG 9501/Intergroup Phase III Trial: Postoperative Concurrent Radiation Therapy and Chemotherapy in High-Risk Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Head and Neck. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2012;84:1198-205. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2012.05.008

H&N.patients: H&N SCC trials comparing local treatment with chemo or without
H&N.methods: Meta-analysis of 107 randomized trials
H&N.results: concurrent chemo 5-yr OS benefit of +6.5%. Concurrent chemo improves LRF Induction and adjuvant improve DM Efficacy decreases with increasing age and performance status No OS improvement with induction chemo
H&N.interpretation: Meta-analysis confirms an OS benefit with the addition of concurrent chemotherapy to RT in H&N tumors. There is no benefit with induction chemotherapy.
H&N.commentary: This meta-analysis combines all types of chemotherapy together. There is a possibiity that some specific chemotherapy regimens may be individually beneficial while others are not. Many of the trials are historical, prior to 1965. As such information on p16 is not available.
H&N.references: 1. Lacas B, Carmel A, Landais C, et al. Meta-analysis of chemotherapy in head and neck cancer (MACH-NC): An update on 107 randomized trials and 19805 patients, on behalf of MACH-NC group. Radiother Oncol 2021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2021.01.013
H&N.commentary_refs: 1. Blanchard, P. @PBlanchardMD. Twitter. March 25, 2021. Available at: https://twitter.com/PBlanchardMD/status/1374985184177704962. Accessed March 25, 2021.

H&N.subtopic: Definitive CRT vs. RT, split course
H&N.patients: Unresectable oral cavity, oropharynx, larynx, or hypopharynx
H&N.methods: ➜ 70 Gy alone vs. ➜ 70 Gy + concurrent cisplatin x 3 vs. ➜ split-course 70 Gy + cis/5-FU
H&N.results: Continuous CRT improved 3-yr OS (37% vs. RT 23% vs. split-course plus chemo 27%) and DFS, but increased Gr 3-4 adverse effects
H&N.interpretation: Concurrent chemo improves OS with increase in adverse effects. The benefit was lost when chemo was added to split course RT.
H&N.references: 1. Adelstein DJ, Li Y, Adams GL, et al. An Intergroup Phase III Comparison of Standard Radiation Therapy and Two Schedules of Concurrent Chemoradiotherapy in Patients With Unresectable Squamous Cell Head and Neck Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2003;21:92-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2003.01.008

H&N.subtopic: Addition of chemo to split course RT vs hyper fx
H&N.patients: T3-4 cancers or T2N0 BOT
H&N.methods: ➜ 75 Gy hyperfx, 1.2 Gy BID vs. ➜ concurrent cis/5FU (split 1 wk RT break at 40 Gy) → adj cis/5FU
H&N.results: CRT improved 3-yr LC from 44% to 70% RFS 61 vs. 41%. p=0.08 3-yr OS 34% vs. 55%, p=0.07 adverse effects similar
H&N.interpretation: CRT with split course improves outcomes over hyperfrac RT without chemo.
H&N.commentary: Only 57% finished all chemotherapy. Hyperfractionation with concurrent chemotherapy has not been evaluated.
H&N.references: 1. Brizel DM, Albers ME, Fisher SR, et al. Hyperfractionated Irradiation with or without Concurrent Chemotherapy for Locally Advanced Head and Neck Cancer. N Engl J Med 1998;338:1798-804. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199806183382503

H&N.patients: Stage III or IV nonmetastatic SCC of oropharynx, hypopharynx, oral cavity, or larynx
H&N.methods: ➜ RT with concurrent cetuximab vs. ➜ RT with concurrent cisplatin RT conventional 70 Gy, or hyperfrac 72-76.8 Gy/ 1.2 BID, or CCB 72 Gy/ 42 fx
H&N.results: Cumulative incidence of locoregional failure was more than twice as high in cetuximab arm 3-yr LRF 23% vs. 9% 3-yr DM 6% vs. 8%, not different 3-yr EFS 67% vs. 85%
H&N.interpretation: Concurrent cetuximab-based RT is inferior to concurrent cisplatin-based RT regardless of p16 status. No difference was observed in adverse effects burden.
H&N.commentary: This randomized phase III study comparing concurrent cisplatin with concurrent cetuximab in patients with locally advanced HNSCC showed a significantly better locoregional control with concurrent cisplatin, which should remain the standard treatment for patients with p16-positive or p16-negative HNSCC.
H&N.references: 1. Gebre-Medhin M, Brun E, Engström P, et al. ARTSCAN III: A Randomized Phase III Study Comparing Chemoradiotherapy With Cisplatin Versus Cetuximab in Patients With Locoregionally Advanced Head and Neck Squamous Cell Cancer. JCO. 2021;39:38-47. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.20.02072

H&N.subtopic: Addition of concurrent cetuximab to RT
H&N.patients: Stage III or IV nonmetastatic SCC of oropharynx, hypopharynx, or larynx
H&N.methods: ➜ RT with concurrent cetuximab vs. ➜ RT alone RT conventional 70 Gy, or hyperfrac 72-76.8 Gy/ 1.2 BID, or CCB 72 Gy/ 42 fx
H&N.results: Median LC 24 vs. 15 mos 2-yr LC 50% vs 41% median OS 49 vs. 29 mos 3-yr OS 55% vs 45%. Acneiform rash and infusion reactions more common, but otherwise not more toxic. OS for grade 2-4 acneiform rash 69 mos vs 25 without Favorable factors on analysis: oropharynx, EGFR >50%, hyperfx, CCB, node positive, age <65
H&N.interpretation: Adding cetuximab to RT (without chemotherapy) improved LC and OS. The trial was not powered to detect differences in subgroups by primary site or RT fractionation.
H&N.commentary: The control arm did not receive standard of care therapy, cisplatin. The later De-ESCALATE and RTOG 1016 trials showed that cisplatin with RT is superior to cetuximab in HPV oropharynx tumors (Mehanna 2018, Gillison 2019). This trial was not powered to detect differences in subgroups. At the time some questioned whether all subgroups would benefit from cetuximab or only p16+ (Bonner 2006, Bonner 2017). Cetuximab stands as a medical reversal in radiation and chemotherapy history.
H&N.references: 1. Bonner JA, Harari PM, Giralt J, et al. Radiotherapy plus Cetuximab for Squamous-Cell Carcinoma of the Head and Neck. N Engl J Med 2006;354:567-78. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa053422
H&N.commentary_refs: Bonner JA. Author's reply. N Engl J Med 2006;18;354(20):2187. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa053422 Bonner JA, Mesia R, Giralt J, et al. p16, HPV, and Cetuximab: What Is the Evidence? Oncologist 2017;22:811-22. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1634/theoncologist.2016-0433

H&N.patients: locally advanced, Stage III-IV AJCC V, nonmetastatic SCC unresectable or low suitability for surgery
H&N.methods: ➜ induction TPF vs. ➜ 🏆 no induction ➜ conc cis/5FU vs. ➜ conc cetuximab 2x2 randomization
H&N.results: Induction chemo improved OS, PFS, LRC, and CR Median OS induction 55 vs. 32 mos 3-yr OS 58% vs. 47% CR 43% vs. 28% median PFS 31 mos vs. 19 mos DM ~15%, not different -On subanalysis, better effect with conc cetuximab and non-OPX site, but underpowered -More febrile neutropenia with induction -ChemoRT breaks not different
H&N.interpretation: Induction chemo improves OS, PFS, LRC, and CR and maintains compliance with chemoRT. On subanalysis the best effect was with concurrent cetuximab. Given the history of other negative induction trials, results should be interpreted with caution.
H&N.commentary: Why was this induction trial positive and the others negative? Perhaps HPV epidemiology played role; prevalence may be higher in US and lower in this study. The trials had slightly different regimens. Perhaps cetuximab somehow sensitizes better when delivered after induction chemo as the subanalysis suggests (but this is contradictory to the lower HPV prevalence idea), or perhaps the concurrent chemo is overtreatment. It is unclear what population, if any, truly benefits from induction chemo. Refer also to RTOG 9111, where on long term follow-up the induction chemo arm (with no concurrent chem…
H&N.references: 1. Ghi MG, Paccagnella A, Ferrari D, et al. Induction TPF followed by concomitant treatment versus concomitant treatment alone in locally advanced head and neck cancer. A phase II–III trial. Ann Oncol 2017;28:2206-12. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/annonc/mdx299
H&N.commentary_refs: Margalit DN, Lin A. Two Sides of the Same Coin: Head and Neck Cancer Treatment De-Intensification and Intensification with Induction Chemotherapy. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2018;102:1–4. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2018.04.002

H&N.patients: unresectable, low surgical curability T3/T4, N2/3 (but not T1N2), or organ preservation
H&N.methods: ➜ Induction TPF, RT + conc carbo or docetaxol vs. ➜ 🏆 RT + conc cis If poor response to induction: 72 Gy RT in 6 weeks with docetaxel If favorable response: 70 Gy RT in 7 weeks with carboplatin AUC 1.5
H&N.results: No benefit in OS or PFS 3-yr OS ~75% and 3-yr PFS ~68% -Serious adverse events in 52 vs. 22 pts -Grade 3-4 mucositis 47% vs. 16% -More febrile neutropenia with induction -RT breaks similar, 6 vs. 5 pts -Terminated due to slow accrual
H&N.interpretation: No benefit to induction TPF. Trial terminated early. adverse effects is increased.
H&N.commentary: The induction studies often included N2 and T3, and not specifically bulky disease. There may be some situations in which induction chemo is useful, such as a patient who is unable to lie flat due to cough or SOB from the tumor or perhaps very bulky HPV negative tumor.
H&N.references: Haddad R, O'Neill A, Rabinowits G, et al. Induction chemotherapy followed by concurrent chemoradiotherapy (sequential chemoradiotherapy) versus concurrent chemoradiotherapy alone in locally advanced head and neck cancer (PARADIGM): a randomised phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2013;14:257-64. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(13)70011-1

H&N.patients: unresectable, Stage III-IV AJCC V, nonmetastatic SCC
H&N.methods: ➜ Induction TPF, RT + conc cis vs. ➜ induction PF, RT + conc cis vs. ➜ 🏆 RT + conc cis
H&N.results: No change in PFS, TTF, or OS Median PFS ~14 mos, TTF 8 mos, OS 27 mos -More neutropenia, odynophagia, stomatitis with induction chemo -With induction 113/155 completed RT compared to 118/128 in RT alone arm
H&N.interpretation: No benefit to induction chemotherapy. adverse effects is increased.
H&N.commentary: This is the only induction trial that specifically enrolled only purely unresectable tumors. Others also enrolled those with unsuitability or with low curability with surgery.
H&N.references: 1. Hitt R, Grau JJ, López-Pousa A, et al. A randomized phase III trial comparing induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiotherapy versus chemoradiotherapy alone as treatment of unresectable head and neck cancer. Ann Oncol 2013;25:216-25. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/annonc/mdt461

H&N.patients: N2 or N3 SCC of H&N
H&N.methods: ➜ Induction TPF, RT + conc THF vs. ➜ 🏆 RT + conc THF H=hydroxyurea. RT to 74-75 Gy hyperfx
H&N.results: -No benefit in 30-mos OS, RFS, or DMFS -Mortality ~30% in both arms -Serious adverse events more common in induction arm 47% vs. 28% -In the induction group, 122/124 completed RT
H&N.interpretation: No benefit to induction THF. adverse effects is increased.
H&N.references: 1. Cohen EEW, Karrison TG, Kocherginsky M, et al. Phase III Randomized Trial of Induction Chemotherapy in Patients With N2 or N3 Locally Advanced Head and Neck Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:2735-43. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2013.54.6309

H&N.patients: cT2-T4 and N0/N+, M0 oral cavity SCC requiring mandibular resection due to paramandibular disease w/o bone erosion
H&N.methods: Phase II ➜ Upfront surgery (segmental resection) + adjuvant RT±chemo vs. ➜ Neoadjuvant TPF x2c (docetaxel, cisplatin, FU), then surgery (after 3-4 weeks) then adjuvant CRT (76% received chemo per protocol) 1⁰ endpoint: mandibular preservation >30%
H&N.results: Median f/u 3.6 yrs Mandibular Preservation Rate (1⁰ endpoint) 47% (95% CI 31.5-63.2) vs. 0% CR (n=1, 3%), PR (n=12; 35%) No difference in DFS or OS Positive/close margin: 0% vs 8.8% (p=0.50) favoring NACT adverse effects similar, but higher trend for surgery-induced adverse effects in neoadjuvant arm
H&N.interpretation: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy for mandibular preservation shows preliminary evidence as a treatment option in oral cavity SCC. adverse effects is reasonable and OS is not different. Results require confirmation in phase III trials.
H&N.commentary: Surgical complications were observed in two patients in the neoadjuvant chemotherapy group and none in upfront surgery group. After NACT, five patients did not undergo surgery (one due to CR, who had CRT) and one patient had inoperable disease due to progression. There was 38% PR (n=12) or CR (n= 1). For upfront patients, surgery entailed segmental mandibulectomy, ipsilateral neck dissection with appropriate reconstruction and adjuvant radiation+chemo (weekly cis) per standard indications (about half received CRT). Two patients did not receive adjuvant treatment due to no adverse features. All…
H&N.notes: Oral cavity overview -There have been few studies for oral cavity specifically. Refer also to post-op trials -Most randomized trials come from Tata Memorial. They showed than LND improves OS. They also held a phase II study to show that neoadjuvant chemo can help mandibular preservation -However the Senti MERORL trial and a Japanese trial showed that SLNB and LND are noninferior. -Much data in oral cavity centers around management of RT field and lymphatics -Retrospective studies show that ipsilateral RT can be employed for well-lateralized lesions -The ACRIN 6685 study supports omission of RT…
H&N.references: 1. Chaukar D, Prabash K, Rane P, et al. Prospective Phase II Open-Label Randomized Controlled Trial to Compare Mandibular Preservation in Upfront Surgery With Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy Followed by Surgery in Operable Oral Cavity Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2021;JCO2100179. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.21.00179

H&N.citation: Zhang et al, NEJM, 2019 Zhang et al, JCO, 2022
H&N.patients: Stage III-IVB (not T3 or T4N0) nonkeratinizing
H&N.methods: ➜ 🏆 gem/cis induction vs. ➜ no induction prior to chemoRT RT to 66-70 Gy/ 30-33 fx with concurrent cisplatin
H&N.results: 5-yr OS 88% vs. 79% 5-yr FFS 81% vs. 67% 5-yr DMFS 90% vs. 78% 5-yr LRFS 88% vs 83% (p=0.06) Subgroup pretreatment cfEBV >4k/mL 86% vs. 71% No benefit for induction for patients ≤4000 copies cfEBV DNA Prognostic value of response after induction: 5-yr OS 100% (CR) vs. 88% (PR) vs. 62% (SD/PD) acute grade 3-4 adverse effects 76% vs. 56% late grade 3-4 adverse effects 11% both arms
H&N.interpretation: Updated five year results confirm that induction chemo with gemcitabine and cisplatin added to chemoRT improves RFS and OS compared to no induction chemo with similar late G3+ adverse effects. Response after induction chemotherapy was prognostic for OS. Pretreatment cfEBV DNA may also be a putative predictive biomarker for identifying patients who benefit from induction chemotherapy.
H&N.references: 1. Zhang Y, Chen L, Hu G-Q, et al. Gemcitabine and Cisplatin Induction Chemotherapy in Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma. N Engl J Med;381:1124-1135. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1905287 1. Zhang Y, Chen L, Hu G-Q, et al. Final Overall Survival Analysis of Gemcitabine and Cisplatin Induction Chemotherapy in Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma: A Multicenter, Randomized Phase III Trial. J Clin Oncol. 2022 Jun 16;JCO2200327. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.00327

H&N.patients: Stage III-IVB (not T3 or T4N0) nonkeratinizing
H&N.methods: ➜ 🏆 Induction TPF then RT with cisplatin vs. ➜ chemoRT alone
H&N.results: 3-yr FFS 80% vs 72% Grade 3-4 neutropenia 42% vs 7% Grade 3-4 leucopenia 41% vs 17% Grade 3-4 stomatitis 41% vs 35%
H&N.interpretation: Addition of induction TPF to chemoRT improved FFS. Longer term follow-up is needed.
H&N.references: 1. Sun Y, Li W-F, Chen N-Y, et al. Induction chemotherapy plus concurrent chemoradiotherapy versus concurrent chemoradiotherapy alone in locoregionally advanced nasopharyngeal carcinoma: a phase 3, multicentre, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Oncol 2016;17:1509-20. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(16)30410-7

H&N.patients: Nasopharynx WHO II-III
H&N.methods: ➜ 🏆RT with concurrent weekly cisplatin vs. ➜ induction gem/carbo/paclitaxel x2 with chemoRT
H&N.results: No difference in 3-yr OS, 92% GCP vs 94%. No difference in DFS or DMFS After GCP, more dose de-intensification of cis were required
H&N.interpretation: There is no benefit to induction chemotherapy in nasopharynx cancer.
H&N.references: 1. Tan T, Lim W-T, Fong K-W, et al. Concurrent Chemo-Radiation With or Without Induction Gemcitabine, Carboplatin, and Paclitaxel: A Randomized, Phase 2/3 Trial in Locally Advanced Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2015;91:952-60. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2015.01.002

H&N.patients: H&N s/p surgery and neck dissection with a pN0 neck with indications for adjuvant RT. T1-2N0-2b ipsilateral tonsil was excluded.
H&N.methods: Phase II. ➜ Omit RT to dissected N0 neck IMRT SIB to 66/54 Gy or 60/52 Gy. Chemo as indicated.
H&N.results: Control of uninvolved dissected neck 97% Those that failed also failed at primary 5-yr LC 84%, RC 93%, PFS 60%, OS 64% Characteristics: 59% N2-3, 51% T3-4, 71% crossed midline, 34% had chemo
H&N.interpretation: Control of uninvolved neck is 97% with ispilateral RT when omitting RT in the dissected N0 neck.
H&N.commentary: This study was performed in a single institution, Wash U. Is it applicable to all? Wash U typically performs high quality resections and extensive neck dissections. Note that this study included any pN0 neck, not necessarily the contralateral neck. For example, applying this data could allow one to exclude RT to the neck in pT4N0 larynx and treat primary site only. In this study dissection to the N0 neck is required. Another interesting question would be whether RT is required in a cN0 undissected neck. Which is more toxic: dissection or RT to the contralateral neck? If neck dissection can be …
H&N.references: 1. Contreras J, Spencer CR, DeWees T, et al. Eliminating Postoperative Radiation to the Pathologically Node-Negative Neck: Long-Term Results of a Prospective Phase II Study. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:2548-2555. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.19.00186
H&N.commentary_refs: Biau J, Lapeyre M, Troussier I, et al. Selection of lymph node target volumes for definitive head and neck radiation therapy: a 2019 Update. Radiother Oncol 2019;134:1-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2019.01.018

H&N.patients: H&N cancer with at least one cN0 neck that was planned for dissection, PET
H&N.methods: Prospective ➜ PET → negative cN0 neck → dissection of that neck and evaluation of node status
H&N.results: -PET in cN0 neck was true negative in 87% after dissection -If max SUV <3.5 in neck, NPV was 94%, specific to each node level -PET changed decisions in 22%
H&N.interpretation: PET negative cN0 neck can provide confidence that a neck dissection will be negative, especially with lower SUV. Note that the endpoint here is NPV, not LR such as at 2 or 5 years.
H&N.commentary: A negative PET in combination with negative nodal dissection should provide even greater local control. The trial however only shows the NPV at the time of diagnosis and staging, not eventual local control.
H&N.references: 1. Lowe VJ, Duan F, Subramaniam RM, et al. Multicenter Trial of [18F]fluorodeoxyglucose Positron Emission Tomography/Computed Tomography Staging of Head and Neck Cancer and Negative Predictive Value and Surgical Impact in the N0 Neck: Results From ACRIN 6685. J Clin Oncol, 2019;37:1695-1703. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.01182
H&N.commentary_refs: National Comprehensive Cancer Network. Head and Neck Cancers (Version 1.2019). https://www.nccn.org/professionals/physician_gls/pdf/head-and-neck.pdf. Accessed June 19, 2019.

H&N.patients: tonsil HPV+ SCC pT1-pT2 N1-3 s/p TORS
H&N.methods: Omit RT to resected tonsil post-op bed if no indications to treat primary, and RT to neck with indications to treat neck
H&N.results: 2-yr primary LC 98.3% (n=1 recurrence) 2-yr regional recurrence 1.7% 2-yr DM 3.3% Mean RT dose to primary 39.6 Gy
H&N.interpretation: Control of tonsil post-op bed is 98% when omitting RT to this area when indications to treat the primary are not present while treating the neck with RT.
H&N.references: 1. Swisher-McClure S, Lukens JN, Aggarwal C, et al. A Phase II Trial of Alternative Volumes of Oropharyngeal Irradiation for De-intensification (AVOID): Omission of the Resected Primary Tumor Bed Following Transoral Robotic Surgery for Human Papilloma Virus Related Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Oropharynx. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2019. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2019.11.021

H&N.subtopic: Addition of chemo to post-op RT
H&N.citation: Laskar et al, Eur J Cancer, 2023
H&N.patients: locally advanced, resected oral cavity tumors with ENE, positive margin, Stage III/IV, >2 nodes, PNI with N+, LVI with N+, major reconstruction required (only 0.4% included had positive margins)
H&N.methods: ➜ 56-60 Gy/ 28-30 fx, 5 fx per wk vs. ➜ same RT & concurrent weekly cisplatin vs. ➜ 56-60 Gy, 6 fx per wk, no chemo
H&N.results: No difference in 5-yr LRC or OS, even with high risk features including T stage, N stage, and ENE. There was benefit for T3-T4 plus N2-3 plus ENE 5-yr LRC ~60-65%
H&N.interpretation: The addition of chemo to adjuvant RT or 6 fx RT without chemo did not improve outcomes in post-op oral cavity tumors with high risk or intermediate risk factors.
H&N.commentary: 55% had ENE and only 0.4% had positive margins 90% T3-T4 and 48% N2
H&N.references: 1. Laskar SG, Chaukar D, Deshpande M, et al. Oral cavity adjuvant therapy (OCAT) -a phase III, randomized controlled trial of surgery followed by conventional RT (5 fr/wk) versus concurrent CT-RT versus accelerated RT (6fr/wk) in locally advanced, resectable, squamous cell carcinoma of oral cavity. Eur J Cancer 2023;181:179-187. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejca.2022.12.016

H&N.patients: post op high risk: ENE, positive margin, or close 3 mm margin
H&N.methods: RT 66/60/56 Gy SIB. Randomized: Arm 1: weekly cis, 40mg/m2 Arm 2: weekly docetaxol 15mg/m2 Arm 3: weekly docetaxol 15 mg /m2 plus cetuximab
H&N.results: accruing
H&N.references: 1. Radiation Therapy Oncology Group. Radiation Therapy With Cisplatin, Docetaxel, or Cetuximab After Surgery in Treating Patients With Stage III-IV Squamous Cell Head and Neck Cancer. Available from: https://www.clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01810913. NLM identifier: NCT01810913. Accessed June 20, 2019.

H&N.subtopic: Addition of chemo to post-op RT
H&N.citation: Bernier et al, Head Neck, 2005 Lu et al, IJROBP, 2022
H&N.patients: post-op H&N with risk factors as above
H&N.methods: Combined analysis of EORTC 22931 and RTOG 9501 A later combined analysis also included RTOG 0234 to evaluate findings with rising nodal count
H&N.results: ⬆️ Chemo improved OS in ENE and positive margin ↔️ No OS benefit if int risk factors only (p=0.33 in EORTC and p=0.78 in RTOG) No interaction between # lymph nodes and benefit with chemo (DM p=0.8, OS p=0.161, DFS p=0.45) (this analysis also included RTOG 0234)
H&N.interpretation: The addition of chemo to adjuvant RT improves OS in post-op H&N tumors with ENE or positive margins. There is no improvement in intermediate risk factors including number of nodes.
H&N.commentary: If one considers the combined EORTC 22931 RTOG 9501 analysis as exploratory and limited in its methodology, EORTC 22931 might be used to support an OS benefit with chemotherapy in those with multiple intermediate risk factors. MVA or adjustment for risk factors for example was not performed. Statistical approaches for post-hoc analysis have improved, and plenty of useful ones exist (consider STAMPEDE, EORTC 22881, 9413 & Ottawa, PORTECs, even EBCTCG). One must be cautious of seeing what they want to see in subanalysis, which goes both ways. Nature does not have a clear cutoff for significance,…
H&N.references: 1. Bernier J, Cooper JS, Pajak TF, et al. Defining risk levels in locally advanced head and neck cancers: A comparative analysis of concurrent postoperative radiation plus chemotherapy trials of the EORTC (#22931) and RTOG (# 9501). Head Neck 2005;27:843-50. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/hed.20279 2. Lu DJ, Luu M, Gay C, et al. Nodal Metastasis Count and Oncologic Outcomes in Head and Neck Cancer: A Secondary Analysis of NRG/RTOG 9501, NRG/RTOG 0234, and EORTC 22931. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2022;113:787-795. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2022.03.033

H&N.subtopic: Addition of chemo to post-op RT
H&N.patients: ≥1 of T3-4 (except larynx T3N0), T1-2N2-3, or (T1-2N0-1 with ENE, +M, PNI, or LVI), or (OC or OPX with level IV or V nodes)
H&N.methods: ➜ Post-op RT 66 Gy (54 Gy to low risk) vs. ➜ 🏆 same RT + cisplatin x3
H&N.results: ⬆️ 5-yr PFS 36% vs. 47% ⬆️ 5-yr OS 40% vs. 53% ⬆️ 3-yr OS 49% vs. 65% ⬆️ 5-yr LRC 69% vs. 82% DM not reported
H&N.interpretation: The addition of chemo to adjuvant RT improves OS and LRC in post-op H&N tumors with risk factors.
H&N.commentary: If one considers the combined EORTC 22931 RTOG 9501 analysis as exploratory and limited in its methodology, EORTC 22931 might be used to support an OS benefit with chemotherapy in those with multiple intermediate risk factors. MVA or adjustment for risk factors for example was not performed. Statistical approaches for post-hoc analysis have improved, and plenty of useful ones exist (consider STAMPEDE, EORTC 22881, 9413 & Ottawa, PORTECs, even EBCTCG). One must be cautious of seeing what they want to see in subanalysis, which goes both ways. Nature does not have a clear cutoff for significance,…
H&N.references: 1. Bernier J, Domenge C, Ozsahin M, et al. Postoperative Irradiation with or without Concomitant Chemotherapy for Locally Advanced Head and Neck Cancer. N Engl J Med 2004;350:1945-52. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa032641

H&N.subtopic: Addition of chemo to post-op RT
H&N.citation: Cooper et al, NEJM, 2004 Cooper et al, IJROBP, 2012
H&N.patients: operable H+N with ≥2 N+, ENE, or + margin
H&N.methods: ➜ Post-op RT 60 Gy ± 6 Gy boost vs. ➜ 🏆 same RT + cisplatin x3
H&N.results: ⬆️ 2-yr LRC 72% vs. 82%, p=0.01 ⬆️ 5-yr LRC 74% vs. 84% ⬆️ 10-yr LRC 71% vs. 88% ↗️ 2-yr OS 57% vs. 63%, p=0.19 ⬆️ 5-yr OS 27% vs. 29%, p=0.31 DM ~20% in both arms, not different For positive margins or ENE: ⬆️ 10-yr LRC 67% vs. 79% 10-yr OS 20% vs. 27%, p=0.07
H&N.interpretation: The addition of chemo to adjuvant RT improves LRC in post-op H&N tumors with risk factors. The subset of those with positive margin or ENE had benefit in OS.
H&N.commentary: What doses did the two trials use? EORTC used 66 Gy (Bernier 2004), and although RTOG used a base dose of 60 Gy, a 6 Gy boost was allowed to total 66 Gy Why is LRC rising with longer f/u? LRC does not factor in those who have died. This is why many now endorse the endpoints of LRFFS and DMFS.
H&N.references: 1. Cooper JS, Pajak TF, Forastiere AA, et al. Postoperative Concurrent Radiotherapy and Chemotherapy for High-Risk Squamous-Cell Carcinoma of the Head and Neck. N Engl J Med 2004;350:1937-44. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa032646 2. Cooper JS, Zhang Q, Pajak TF, et al. Long-term Follow-up of the RTOG 9501/Intergroup Phase III Trial: Postoperative Concurrent Radiation Therapy and Chemotherapy in High-Risk Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Head and Neck. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2012;84:1198-205. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2012.05.008

H&N.citation: Peters et al, IJROBP, 1993 Rosenthal et al, IJROBP, 2017
H&N.patients: Stage III or IV oral cavity, oropharynx, hypopharynx, larynx who underwent surgery who required post-op RT
H&N.methods: Low risk: ➜ 🏆 57.6 Gy (dose changed to 57.6 after excess failures with 52.2) vs. ➜ 63 Gy High risk: ➜ 🏆 63 Gy vs. ➜ 68.4 Gy Stratified into low and high risk by a point system now not in use. Head and neck were separately scored. Any positive margin was high risk. However ENE could be low or high risk based on extent. Other factors were # of nodes, margin distances, sizes.
H&N.results: 📌 Final report (over 20-yr follow-up): 5-yr to 20-yr LRC not different 5-yr LRC 67% 5/10-yr DM 36/40% 5/10-yr OS 32/20% 2nd cancers 27% Factors impacting LRC & OS: S->RT completion time ≥85 days, +margin, ECE Factor leading to worse OS: age ≥57 📌 Initial report: 2-yr crude LF worse with <54 Gy than with ≥ 57.6 Gy. No difference between 57.6 Gy and ≥63 Gy overall. ENE results seem best with ≥63 Gy based on crude LR On UVA, ENE was the only independent risk factor for recurrence (regardless of dose). Trend to worse recurrence with ≥2 nodes, p=0.08. There was increasing recurrence risk with ≥2 ri…
H&N.interpretation: At early interim analysis, LF was worse with <54 Gy and the arm was changed to 57.6 Gy. ENE seems to benefit from dose escalation to ≥63 Gy based on a crude subanalysis at 2 years. There is no benefit in LRC with dose escalation from 63 Gy → 68.4 Gy in the high risk group or 57.6 → 63 Gy in the low risk group at any point in f/u, up to 20 years. Chemo was not used in this study.
H&N.commentary: This trial laid the foundation for dosing in intermediate and high risk post-op H&N cancer. There are a few important points to note in context of modern statistical approaches and staging. Observe that the trial endorses 63 Gy for ENE, not 66 Gy. Note that ENE was allowed in both groups and not randomized, and the finding that ≥63 Gy is ideal for ENE was determined by subanalysis, which was simply a review of crude recurrence rates by risk factors (Table 9). Today, a subanalysis such as MVA would have been performed with p values. Modern standards in statistical approaches in these situations…
H&N.references: 1. Peters LJ, Goepfert H, Ang KK, et al. Evaluation of the dose for postoperative radiation therapy of head and neck cancer: First report of a prospective randomized trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1993;26:3-11. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/0360-3016(93)90167-t 2. Rosenthal DI, Mohamed ASR, Garden AS, et al. Final Report of a Prospective Randomized Trial to Evaluate the Dose-Response Relationship for Postoperative Radiation Therapy and Pathologic Risk Groups in Patients With Head and Neck Cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2017;98:1002-11. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2017.02.218

H&N.trial: University of Nijmegan Medical Center, Huispost, Netherlands
H&N.citation: van den Bosch et al, IJROBP, 2016
H&N.patients: H&N cT2-4, N0-2 SCC treated with accelerated RT
H&N.methods: Retrospective. 1166 lymph nodes localized in elective neck fields and sites of recurrence reconstructed and analyzed based on dose received IMRT SIB accelerated to 68 Gy in 2.00 Gy for boost and 50.3 Gy in 1.48 Gy for elective volume. Last 14 treatments delivered BID.
H&N.results: •Elective neck node recurrence 5.1% •LN size of 17 mm sum of long and short diameters is risk factor for recurrence •Recurrence in nodes usually happens with LR •In nodes receiving 60 Gy no recurrences seen
H&N.interpretation: Risk factors are presented to determine borderline nodes. Borderline nodes may be controlled with 60 Gy. 45-50 Gy EQD2 controls elective neck otherwise.
H&N.notes: H&N dose overview: -MDACC Peters initially showed 57.6 Gy needed for post-op low risk neck and 63 Gy for ECE -Concominant boost becomes standard from RTOG 9003 fx study -66 Gy used for ECE and +M with concurrent chemo in EORTC and RTOG. Benefit in OS with chemo for ECE and +M -RTOG 0022 outlines use of IMRT SIB to spare salivary glands, combining MDACC Peters and CCB into an IMRT SIB regimen -Doses as low as 45-50 Gy may be reasonable for untreated neck in Belgium study
H&N.references: 1. van den Bosch S, Dijkema T, Verhoef LCG, et al. Patterns of Recurrence in Electively Irradiated Lymph Node Regions After Definitive Accelerated Intensity Modulated Radiation Therapy for Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2016;94:766-74. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2015.12.002

H&N.patients: H&N s/p surgery and neck dissection with a pN0 neck with indications for adjuvant RT. T1-2N0-2b ipsilateral tonsil was excluded.
H&N.methods: Phase II. ➜ Omit RT to dissected N0 neck IMRT SIB to 66/54 Gy or 60/52 Gy. Chemo as indicated.
H&N.results: Control of uninvolved dissected neck 97% Those that failed also failed at primary 5-yr LC 84%, RC 93%, PFS 60%, OS 64% Characteristics: 59% N2-3, 51% T3-4, 71% crossed midline, 34% had chemo
H&N.interpretation: Control of uninvolved neck is 97% with ispilateral RT when omitting RT in the dissected N0 neck.
H&N.commentary: This study was performed in a single institution, Wash U. Is it applicable to all? Wash U typically performs high quality resections and extensive neck dissections. Note that this study included any pN0 neck, not necessarily the contralateral neck. For example, applying this data could allow one to exclude RT to the neck in pT4N0 larynx and treat primary site only. In this study dissection to the N0 neck is required. Another interesting question would be whether RT is required in a cN0 undissected neck. Which is more toxic: dissection or RT to the contralateral neck? If neck dissection can be …
H&N.references: 1. Contreras J, Spencer CR, DeWees T, et al. Eliminating Postoperative Radiation to the Pathologically Node-Negative Neck: Long-Term Results of a Prospective Phase II Study. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:2548-2555. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.19.00186

H&N.patients: N2 or N3 SCC of H&N (75% were known p16+)
H&N.methods: Noninferiority ➜ chemoRT then PET in 3 mos then neck dissection only if incomplete or equivocal response vs. ➜ planned neck dissection
H&N.results: neck dissections in 19% vs. 78% With chemoRT, PET CR in 69% CR in primary & PR/equivocal neck in 17% PR in primary & CR in neck in 6% Incomplete response in both in 7% p16 status did not favor either group Surgical complications 42% vs. 38% 2-yr OS 84.9% vs. 81.5% HR for death slightly favored PET and indicated noninferiority
H&N.interpretation: OS is noninferior with neck dissection guided by PET vs. planned dissection in all.
H&N.commentary: 75% were known p16+ and 79% were tested for p16. A retrospective study has found that 4 months is a better timepoint for PET in HPV+ tumors (Liu 2019). But as to whether if even longer is ideal remains to be determined. One option could be to monitor with CT, and reserve PET for unresponsive or progressive lesions. In anal cancer, 26 weeks was found to be optimal (James 2013).
H&N.references: 1. Mehanna H, Wong W-L, McConkey CC, et al. PET-CT Surveillance versus Neck Dissection in Advanced Head and Neck Cancer. N Engl J Med 2016;374:1444-54. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1514493 Protocol pdf: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa1514493/suppl_file/nejmoa1514493_protocol.pdf
H&N.commentary_refs: 1. Liu HY-h, Milne R, Lock G, et al. Utility of a repeat PET/CT scan in HPV-associated Oropharyngeal Cancer following incomplete nodal response from (chemo)radiotherapy. Oral Oncol 2019;88:153-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oraloncology.2018.11.033 2. James RD, Glynne-Jones R, Meadows HM, et al. Mitomycin or cisplatin chemoradiation with or without maintenance chemotherapy for treatment of squamous-cell carcinoma of the anus (ACT II): a randomised, phase 3, open-label, 2×2 factorial trial. Lancet Oncol 2013;14:516-24. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(13)70086-X

H&N.patients: H&N cancer treated with primary radiation
H&N.methods: Retrospective. 77% IMRT. RP nodes only included for posterior pharyngeal wall involvement. Level IB only included in oral cavity tumors
H&N.interpretation: Recurrences in RP and level I after radiation are rare and should be limited to posterior pharyngeal wall or oral cavity involvement.
H&N.commentary: Excluded: nasopharynx, T1 glottic larynx, and post-op cases
H&N.references: 1. Kjems J, Gothelf AB, Håkansson K, et al Elective Nodal Irradiation and Patterns of Failure in Head and Neck Cancer After Primary Radiation Therapy. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2016;94:775-82. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2015.12.380

H&N.patients: oropharynx Stage I-II
H&N.methods: ➜ 🏆 50 Gy/25 fx then HDR 21 Gy/7 fx BID vs. ➜ 70 Gy/35 fx
H&N.results: 6-mo xerostomia by scintography 14% vs. 44% RTOG grade 2+ xerostomia 7% vs. 30% 3-yr LC 66% vs. 56%
H&N.interpretation: Adding brachytherapy to IMRT improves xerostomia and LC compared to IMRT alone.
H&N.commentary: HPV testing was done retrospectively. 52-59% were HPV negative, 7-11% were positive, and the rest were unknown. The LC results here are worse than would be expected with standard of care surgery. It is unclear from the publication why these patients were not offered surgery. This is the first randomized trial showing the benefit of brachytherapy in H&N cancer, and may be useful for those who are not surgical candidates.
H&N.references: 1. Budrukkar A, Murthy V, Kashid S, et al. Intensity Modulated Radiation Therapy Alone Vs Intensity Modulated Radiation Therapy and Brachytherapy for T1-T2N0M0 Oropharyngeal Cancers: Results from A Randomized Controlled Trial [published online ahead of print, 2023 Sep 1]. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2023;S0360-3016(23)07854-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2023.08.056

H&N.patients: locally advanced H&N SCC
H&N.methods: ➜ 70 Gy/ 35 fx 3DCRT vs. ➜ 75 Gy/ 35 fx IMRT 50 Gy then sequential boost in both arms Concurrent cisplatin in both
H&N.results: 1-yr grade ≥2 xerostomia 63% vs. 23% 3-yr grade ≥2 xerostomia 45% vs. 11% LR and OS not different
H&N.interpretation: IMRT reduced xerostomia. There was no difference in LC with dose escalation.
H&N.references: 1. Tao Y, Auperin A, Blanchard P, et al. Concurrent cisplatin and dose escalation with intensity-modulated radiotherapy (IMRT) versus conventional radiotherapy for locally advanced head and neck squamous cell carcinomas (HNSCC): GORTEC 2004-01 randomized phase III trial. Radiother Oncol 2020;150:18-25. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2020.05.021

H&N.patients: pharynx SCC T1-T4, N0-N3
H&N.methods: ➜ 60 or 65 Gy/ 30 fx parotid sparing IMRT vs. ➜ conventional RT
H&N.results: IMRT improved xerostomia 12-mo xerostomia 38% vs 75% 24-mo xerostomia grade ≥2 29% vs 83% QOL scores
H&N.interpretation: Parotid sparing IMRT reduces xerostomia.
H&N.references: 1. Nutting CM, Morden JP, Harrington KJ, et al. Parotid-sparing intensity modulated versus conventional radiotherapy in head and neck cancer (PARSPORT): a phase 3 multicentre randomised controlled trial. Lancet Oncol 2011;12:127-36. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(10)70290-4

H&N.patients: oropharynx T1-2, N0-1
H&N.methods: IMRT SIB 66/60-54 in 30 fx. No chemo
H&N.results: Xerostomia 6 mos 55%, 1 year 25%, 2 years 16%. Grade 2 reactions: salivary 67%, skin 12%, mucosa 24%, esophagus 19%. Salivary output did not improve over time. 2-yr LRC 91%
H&N.interpretation: IMRT reduces xerostomia. RT alone to 66 Gy with SIB dosing to neck in early stage H&N results in favorable outcomes.
H&N.commentary: This trial established a sometimes used dose regimen for SIB IMRT. Though some advocate caution using 220 cGy per fraction.
H&N.references: 1. Eisbruch A, Harris J, Garden AS, et al. Multi-Institutional Trial of Accelerated Hypofractionated Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy for Early-Stage Oropharyngeal Cancer (RTOG 00-22). Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2010;76:1333-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2009.04.011

H&N.patients: oropharynx Stage III-IV
H&N.methods: ➜ Protons vs. ➜ IMRT Dose, chemo, etc were physician's choice per guidelines
H&N.results: Less severe lymphopenia, dysphagia, xerostomia, and PEG tube dependence with protons 5-yr OS 91% vs 81% 5-yr PFS 83% vs 83%, non inferior 3-yr PFS 81% vs 76% LC, LRR, DM rates similar
H&N.interpretation: Protons have less adverse effects compared to IMRT.
H&N.references: 1. Frank SJ, Busse PM, Lee JJ, et al. Proton versus photon radiotherapy for patients with oropharyngeal cancer in the USA: a multicentre, randomised, open-label, non-inferiority phase 3 trial. Lancet. Published online December 11, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(25)01962-2

H&N.commentary: Larynx summary -VASLG establishes larynx preservation with chemotherapy and RT as an option, with no change in OS vs surgery -RTOG 9111 evaluates the use of chemo in larynx preservation. Induction and concurrent both had favorable results, though results long-term were mixed and perhaps in favor of induction -In early stage glottic larynx, RT is an option. -The Helskinki randomized trial found that RT seemed to have better voice outcomes than CO2 laser

H&N.patients: Post-op H&N with ≥1 of: ENE, positive margin, ≥4 nodes, or multiple PNI
H&N.methods: Surgery→ ➜ 🏆adjuvant nivolumab with 66 Gy and cisplatin, continuing x6 after radiation q3weeks vs. ➜ adjuvant 66 Gy and cisplatin
H&N.results: DFS improved HR 0.76, p=0.034 grade ≥4 adverse events 10% vs. 5%
H&N.interpretation: Adjuvant nivolumab improves DFS in high risk post-op head and neck cancer.
H&N.commentary: The criteria for immunotherapy in this study are stricter than KEYNOTE 689, which allowed any pre-op Stage III-IVA. So a patient that qualified for KEYNOTE, such as a T4aN1, might not qualify for NIVOPOST-OP. It may be reasonable however to expand post-op criteria based on extrapolation from KEYNOTE if a patient did not receive immunotherapy neoadjuvantly.
H&N.references: 1. Bourhis J, Aupérin A, Borel C, et al. Nivolumab added to cisplatin and radiotherapy versus cisplatin and radiotherapy alone after surgery for people with squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck at a high risk of relapse (GORTEC 2018-01 NIVOPOST-OP): a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial. Lancet. Published online December 22, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(25)01850-1

H&N.patients: p16+ T4 N0-2 p16- Stage III-IVA (T3N0-1, T1-3N1, T4a N0-2, T1-4aN2) or Stage III-IVA larynx, hypophyarynx, or oral cavity with any p16
H&N.methods: ➜ 🏆 neoadj pembrolizumab x2 cycles → surgery → adj pembrolizumab x15 cycles plus adjuvant therapy as indicated vs. ➜ surgery→radiation and/or chemo as indicated Immunotherapy q21 days
H&N.results: 3-yr EFS 60% vs. 46% est 3-yr OS 68% vs. 61% (data not mature) 12% stopped pembrolizumab due to progression prior to adjuvant phase (Table S2B. 43/361=12%) pCR ≤5% CPS-10 pCR 4% vs. 0 CPS-1 pCR 3% vs. 0 Grade ≥3 immune-related adverse effects 10% Grade ≥3 adverse effects 45% vs. 43% Grade 5 adverse effects 1.1% vs. 0.3%
H&N.interpretation: Neoadjuvant before surgery followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab improved EFS in locally advanced head and neck cancer. pCR was low at <5% but did occur in some.
H&N.commentary: The trial allowed pre-op tumor growth to be deemed a "tumor flare" and was not counted as an EFS event. Table S2B shows that n=43, or 12%, discontinued pembrolizumab prior to the adjuvant phase due to progressive disease. The true number that had progression would be even higher, as some would have continued despite progression. Another trial IMMUNEBOOST-HPV showed similar findings, that 14% had progression after two cycles of nivolumab prior to chemoradiation (Mirghani 2022). pCR was <5% with immunotherapy but did occur in some after only two cycles, a remarkable finding. CR with chemoradiati…
H&N.references: 1. Uppaluri R, Haddad RI, Tao Y, et al. Neoadjuvant and Adjuvant Pembrolizumab in Locally Advanced Head and Neck Cancer. N Engl J Med 2025;393:37-50. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2415434
H&N.commentary_refs: 1. Mirghani H, Aupérin A, Even C, et al. Induction Nivolumab Before Chemoradiation in High-Risk Human Papillomavirus-Driven Oropharynx Cancers: IMMUNEBOOST-HPV, a Multicenter Randomized Phase II Trial. J Clin Oncol. Published online January 22, 2026. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-25-00835

H&N.subtopic: Concurrent CRT vs. induction vs. RT alone
H&N.citation: Forastierre et al, NEJM, 2003 Forastierre et al, JCO, 2013
H&N.patients: Stage III-IV glottic or supraglottic (ineligible: extension through thyroid cartilage, large volume T4a, and >1 cm BOT involvment. Limited T4 was allowed) who would otherwise had required total laryngectomy 69% of patients were supraglottic
H&N.methods: ➜ 🏆 RT with concurrent cis 100mg/m2 x3 qw3 vs. ➜ induction cis/5FU → RT (VA regimen) vs. ➜ RT alone 70 Gy
H&N.results: Concurrent chemoRT improved larynx preservation. At long term f/u OS trends to better with induction over concurrent. 2-yr larynx sparing 88% vs. 75% vs. 70% 10-yr larynx sparing 82% vs. 68% vs. 64% 2-yr LRC 78% vs. 61% vs. 56% 10-yr LC 69% vs. 54% vs. 50% Long term f/u: Deaths not attributed to larynx cancer worse with concurrent: 31% vs. 21% vs. 17% Trend to better OS with induction (p=0.08 vs. concurrent) 10-yr OS 28% vs. 39% vs. 32% (NS) 10-yr DM 16% vs. 16% vs. 24% <3% with inability to swallow
H&N.interpretation: On long term f/u there is trend to worse OS with concurrent CRT but overall no change in OS. Concurrent chemoRT resulted in improved LRC and larynx preservation.
H&N.commentary: With this trial and GORTEC 2000-01, for some there is still some question regarding the usefulness of induction chemotherapy in larynx despite the results of the PARADIGM, DeCIDE, and Madrid trials (Pointreau 2009, Haddad 2013, Cohen 2014, Hitt, 2014). Unlike in those three trials, there is no concurrent chemo after induction in RTOG 9111. There are late deaths in the concurrrent chemo arm at 10 years possibly due to adverse effects such as aspiration.
H&N.notes: Larynx summary -VASLG establishes larynx preservation with chemotherapy and RT as an option, with no change in OS vs surgery -RTOG 9111 evaluates the use of chemo in larynx preservation. Induction and concurrent both had favorable results, though results long-term were mixed and perhaps in favor of induction -In early stage glottic larynx, RT is an option. -The Helskinki randomized trial found that RT seemed to have better voice outcomes than CO2 laser
H&N.references: 1. Forastiere AA, Goepfert H, Maor M, et al. Concurrent Chemotherapy and Radiotherapy for Organ Preservation in Advanced Laryngeal Cancer. N Engl J Med 2003;349:2091-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa031317 2. Forastiere AA, Zhang Q, Weber RS, et al. Long-term results of RTOG 91-11: a comparison of three nonsurgical treatment strategies to preserve the larynx in patients with locally advanced larynx cancer. J Clin Oncol 2013;31:845–852. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2012.43.6097

H&N.citation: VALSG, NEJM, 1991 Terrell et al, JAMA Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg, 1998
H&N.patients: Stage III-IV (not T1N1)
H&N.methods: ➜ total laryngectomy + post-op RT (50-70 Gy) vs. ➜ induction 3 cycles PF → 70 Gy if CR or PR. If no response: surgery+post-op RT
H&N.results: No difference in 2-yr OS. CRT had worse LF but improved DM. 2-yr larynx preservation 64% 2-yr OS 68%, not different LF 12% vs. 2% RF 8% vs. 5% (NS) DM 11% vs. 17%, p=0.04 -Long term QOL is improved with chemoRT. Better freedom from pain, emotional well being, less depression
H&N.interpretation: Organ preservation feasible and maintains OS outcomes. Long-term QOL is superior with preservation.
H&N.commentary: Salvage laryngectomy 56% in T4 and 29% if <T4
H&N.references: 1. Induction Chemotherapy plus Radiation Compared with Surgery plus Radiation in Patients with Advanced Laryngeal Cancer. N Engl J Med 1991;324:1685-90. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199106133242402 2. Terrell JE, Fisher SG, Wolf GT, et al. Long-term Quality of Life After Treatment of Laryngeal Cancer. JAMA Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 1998;124:964-71. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/archotol.124.9.964

H&N.citation: Aaltonen et al, IJROBP, 2014 Pakkanen et al, IJROBP, 2022
H&N.patients: T1a larynx
H&N.methods: ➜ 66 Gy (6 MeV wedge pair) vs. ➜ CO2 laser (TLS) 1⁰ endpoint: 2-yr voice quality blinded experts rated voice quality on GRBAS scale, patient self-assessed voice quality and ADL impact, and blinded videostrobe
H&N.results: 2-yr voice quality: similar voice quality, CO2 laser had wider glottal gap and breathier voice. RT group had less hoarseness related inconvenience (p=0.007) 5-yr OS 89%, 5-yr DSS 98% 5-yr DFS 84% (3 recurrences in each arm) 5-yr larynx preservation 92% vs 97%, p=.575
H&N.interpretation: RT seems to provide superior voice outcomes compared to surgery for early stage larynx cancer. The prognosis of T1a LSCC is favorable with either treatment modality, with no significant differences in survival or organ preservation.
H&N.commentary: Breathiness was from expert ratings based on GRBAS scale. Glottal closure was detected on videotrobe. Hoarseness related impact on life was self-assessed.
H&N.references: 1. Aaltonen L-M, Rautiainen N, Sellman J, et al. Voice Quality After Treatment of Early Vocal Cord Cancer: A Randomized Trial Comparing Laser Surgery With Radiation Therapy. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2014;90:255-60. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2014.06.032 2. Pakkanen P, Irjala H, Ilmarinen T, et al. Survival and Larynx Preservation in Early Glottic Cancer: A Randomized Trial Comparing Laser Surgery and Radiation Therapy. Int J Radit Oncol Biol Phys 2022:113:96-100. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2022.01.010

H&N.patients: T1N0 glottic
H&N.methods: 2 Gy vs. 2.25 Gy fractions: Minimal tumors (≤2/3 vocal cord) 60Gy vs. 56.25Gy Larger tumors 66Gy vs. 63Gy
H&N.results: 5-yr LC 77% vs. 92% for 2.25 Gy/fx No difference in 5-yr CSS or adverse effects
H&N.interpretation: Hypofractionation with 2.25 Gy per fraction shows superior local control over 2 Gy per fraction.
H&N.references: 1. Yamazaki H, Nishiyama K, Tanaka E, et al. Radiotherapy for early glottic carcinoma (T1N0M0): Results of prospective randomized study of radiation fraction size and overall treatment time. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2006;64:77-82. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2005.06.014

H&N.citation: Pointreau et al, JNCI, 2009 Janoray et al, JCNI, 2015
H&N.patients: larynx or hypopharynx (54% were hypopharynx) T3-T4 primary sites or T2 requiring total laryngectomy
H&N.methods: ➜ Induction TPF x3 vs. ➜ Induction PF →RT 70 Gy for CR or PR →If no response, total laryngectomy and RT 54-66 Gy
H&N.results: Increased laryngeal preservation in TPF arm 3-yr laryngeal preservation 70% vs 58% 10-yr larynx preservation 70% vs 47% 10-yr larynx dysfunction free survival 64% vs. 37% Response rates 80% vs 59% More grade 2 alopecia, grade 4 neutropenia, and febrile neutropenia with TPF, and more stomatitis and thrombocytopenia with PF
H&N.interpretation: TPF resulted in superior response rates compared to PF in larynx sparing in hypoharynx cancer.
H&N.commentary: 54% were hypopharynx. Although the PARADIGM, DECIDE, and Spanish trials showed no benefit to induction chemo in overall H&N, could there still be some utility in larynx? (Haddad 2013, Cohen 2014, Hitt, 2014). In RTOG 9111 long term f/u showed a trend toward reduced survival with concurrent chemo compared to induction (Forastierre 2003).
H&N.references: 1. Pointreau Y, Garaud P, Chapet S, et al. Randomized Trial of Induction Chemotherapy With Cisplatin and 5-Fluorouracil With or Without Docetaxel for Larynx Preservation. J Natl Cancer Inst 2009;101:498-506. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/jnci/djp007 2. Janoray G, Pointreau Y, Garaud P, et al. Long-Term Results of a Multicenter Randomized Phase III Trial of Induction Chemotherapy With Cisplatin, 5-fluorouracil, ± Docetaxel for Larynx Preservation. J Natl Cancer Inst 2015;10. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/jnci/djv368

H&N.citation: Lefebvre et al, JNCI, 1996 Lefebvre et al, Ann Oncol, 2012
H&N.patients: SCC of pyriform sinus or AE fold
H&N.methods: Noninferiority ➜ surgery + post-op RT vs. ➜ 🏆 induction cis/5FU → RT if CR (surgery for PR) [Similar to VA trial]
H&N.results: LF trend to higher with RT. Trend to less DM with chemo 3-yr larynx preservation 42%, 5-yr larynx preservation 35% 5-yr OS 33%, noninferior 10-yr OS ~13.5%, not different 10-yr chemoRT survival with functional larynx 8.7% 10-yr PFS 8.5% vs. 11% 10-yr DM 36% vs. 28%, p=0.14
H&N.interpretation: Larynx preservation with induction chemo for hypopharynx yields outcomes comparable to surgery. OS is noninferior. However, overall outcomes are still poor.
H&N.references: 1. Lefebvre J-L, Chevalier D, Luboinski B, et al. Larynx Preservation in Pyriform Sinus Cancer: Preliminary Results of a European Organization for Research and Treatment of Cancer Phase III Trial. J Natl Cancer Inst 1996;88:890-9. https://doi.org/10.1093/jnci/88.13.890 2. Lefebvre J-L, Andry G, Chevalier D, et al. Laryngeal preservation with induction chemotherapy for hypopharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma: 10-year results of EORTC trial 24891. Ann Oncol 2012;23:2708-14. https://doi.org/10.1093/annonc/mds065

H&N.patients: cT2-T4 and N0/N+, M0 oral cavity SCC requiring mandibular resection due to paramandibular disease w/o bone erosion
H&N.methods: Phase II ➜ Upfront surgery (segmental resection) + adjuvant RT±chemo vs. ➜ Neoadjuvant TPF x2c (docetaxel, cisplatin, FU), then surgery (after 3-4 weeks) then adjuvant CRT (76% received chemo per protocol) 1⁰ endpoint: mandibular preservation >30%
H&N.results: Median f/u 3.6 yrs Mandibular Preservation Rate (1⁰ endpoint) 47% (95% CI 31.5-63.2) vs. 0% CR (n=1, 3%), PR (n=12; 35%) No difference in DFS or OS Positive/close margin: 0% vs 8.8% (p=0.50) favoring NACT adverse effects similar, but higher trend for surgery-induced adverse effects in neoadjuvant arm
H&N.interpretation: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy for mandibular preservation shows preliminary evidence as a treatment option in oral cavity SCC. adverse effects is reasonable and OS is not different. Results require confirmation in phase III trials.
H&N.commentary: Surgical complications were observed in two patients in the neoadjuvant chemotherapy group and none in upfront surgery group. After NACT, five patients did not undergo surgery (one due to CR, who had CRT) and one patient had inoperable disease due to progression. There was 38% PR (n=12) or CR (n= 1). For upfront patients, surgery entailed segmental mandibulectomy, ipsilateral neck dissection with appropriate reconstruction and adjuvant radiation+chemo (weekly cis) per standard indications (about half received CRT). Two patients did not receive adjuvant treatment due to no adverse features. All…
H&N.notes: Oral cavity overview -There have been few studies for oral cavity specifically. Refer also to post-op trials -Most randomized trials come from Tata Memorial. They showed than LND improves OS. They also held a phase II study to show that neoadjuvant chemo can help mandibular preservation -However the Senti MERORL trial and a Japanese trial showed that SLNB and LND are noninferior. -Much data in oral cavity centers around management of RT field and lymphatics -Retrospective studies show that ipsilateral RT can be employed for well-lateralized lesions -The ACRIN 6685 study supports omission of RT…
H&N.references: 1. Chaukar D, Prabash K, Rane P, et al. Prospective Phase II Open-Label Randomized Controlled Trial to Compare Mandibular Preservation in Upfront Surgery With Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy Followed by Surgery in Operable Oral Cavity Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2021;JCO2100179. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.21.00179

H&N.subtopic: Addition of chemo to post-op RT, altered fx
H&N.citation: Laskar et al, Eur J Cancer, 2023
H&N.patients: locally advanced, resected oral cavity tumors with ENE, positive margin, Stage III/IV, >2 nodes, PNI with N+, LVI with N+, major reconstruction required (only 0.4% included had positive margins)
H&N.methods: ➜ 56-60 Gy/ 28-30 fx, 5 fx per wk vs. ➜ same RT & concurrent weekly cisplatin vs. ➜ 56-60 Gy, 6 fx per wk, no chemo
H&N.results: No difference in 5-yr LRC or OS, even with high risk features including T stage, N stage, and ENE. There was benefit for T3-T4 plus N2-3 plus ENE 5-yr LRC ~60-65%
H&N.interpretation: The addition of chemo to adjuvant RT or 6 fx RT without chemo did not improve outcomes in post-op oral cavity tumors with high risk or intermediate risk factors.
H&N.commentary: 55% had ENE and only 0.4% had positive margins 90% T3-T4 and 48% N2
H&N.references: 1. Laskar SG, Chaukar D, Deshpande M, et al. Oral cavity adjuvant therapy (OCAT) -a phase III, randomized controlled trial of surgery followed by conventional RT (5 fr/wk) versus concurrent CT-RT versus accelerated RT (6fr/wk) in locally advanced, resectable, squamous cell carcinoma of oral cavity. Eur J Cancer 2023;181:179-187. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejca.2022.12.016

H&N.patients: well lateralized T1-2 N0-2b oral cavity s/p surgery & unilateral neck dissection ± RT to primary ± RT to unilateral neck Excluded bilateral neck treatment, ENE, +margin, chemo
H&N.methods: Retrospective database review from 6 institutions
H&N.results: 5-yr bilateral or contralateral neck failures in 4.3% 17% had RT, 68% T1, and 55% N0
H&N.interpretation: In well-lateralized early T stage oral cavity, unilateral treatment is associated with low risk of contralateral failures.
H&N.commentary: See also ACRIN 6685 above showing low incidence of positive nodes in dissected PET negative neck (Lowe 2019).
H&N.references: 1. Liu H, Tam L, Woody NM, et al. Risk of Contralateral Nodal Failure in Well Lateralized Early T-stage Oral Cavity Cancer Receiving Unilateral Treatment. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2018;102:S22-S3. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2018.06.142

H&N.patients: T1-2N0 oral cavity SCC
H&N.methods: Resection then ➜ ipsilateral neck dissection vs. ➜ obs (LND for recurrence)
H&N.results: OS 80% vs. 68% DFS 70% vs. 46% Some suggestion of benefit with DOI >3 mm, p=0.12
H&N.interpretation: Elective neck dissection results in improved DFS and OS.
H&N.references: 1. D’Cruz AK, Vaish R, Kapre N, et al. Elective versus Therapeutic Neck Dissection in Node-Negative Oral Cancer. N Engl J Med 2015;373:521-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1506007

H&N.patients: T1-2N0 oral cavity SCC, ≥4 mm depth of invasion
H&N.methods: Noninferiority Resection then ➜ ipsilateral neck dissection vs. ➜ SLNB
H&N.results: 3-yr OS noninferior, 87-88% path+ nodes in 25% vs. 34%, p=0.19 neck function better with SLNB
H&N.interpretation: OS with SLNB is noninferior to neck dissection.
H&N.references: 1. Hasegawa Y, Tsukahara K, Yoshimoto S, et al. Neck Dissections Based on Sentinel Lymph Node Navigation Versus Elective Neck Dissections in Early Oral Cancers: A Randomized, Multicenter, and Noninferiority Trial [published online ahead of print, 2021 Apr 20]. J Clin Oncol. 2021;JCO2003637. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.20.03637

H&N.patients: T1-2N0 oral cavity SCC
H&N.methods: Equivalence Resection then ➜ ipsilateral neck dissection vs. ➜ SLNB and LND if positive
H&N.results: 2-yr neck RFS 90% vs. 91% (equivalent)
H&N.interpretation: SLNB and neck dissection are equivalent in early stage SCC. SLNB is preferred.
H&N.references: 1. Garrel R, Poissonnet G, Moyà Plana A, et al. Equivalence Randomized Trial to Compare Treatment on the Basis of Sentinel Node Biopsy Versus Neck Node Dissection in Operable T1-T2N0 Oral and Oropharyngeal Cancer [published online ahead of print, 2020 Oct 14]. J Clin Oncol. 2020;JCO2001661. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.20.01661

H&N.commentary: Oropharynx summary -HPV was discovered as a prognostic factor for response to treatment and OS, validated in RTOG 0129 -Studies sought to de-escalate therapy in HPV+ tumors by various means, but most of these failed under multi institutional randomized trials -RTOG 1016 and De-Escalate found that chemo cannot be replaced by cetuximab -A surgical technique, TORS, is developed that leads to less morbidity than standard surgery -In HN005, 60 Gy was found inferior and the arm was closed -ECOG 3311 validates TORS and post-op approaches as options in low, int, and high risk HPV -ORATOR2 compared che…

H&N.patients: Oropharynx HPV+ T1-3, N1, and <10 PY (AJCC 8)
H&N.methods: ➜ 70 Gy in 6 weeks (DAHANCA) + cisplatin vs. ➜ ⭕️ 60 Gy/ 30 fx (6 weeks) + cisplatin vs. ➜ 60 Gy/ 30 fx (DAHANCA) + nivolumab
H&N.results: 60 Gy plus cispatin is not noninferior and this arm is now closed. Futility was reached. Interim estimated PFS and OS outcomes 2-yr PFS 98% vs. 89% vs. 90% 2-yr OS 99% vs. 98% vs. 96%
H&N.interpretation: De-escalation to 60 Gy with cisplatin should not be considered a standard option. The trial will continue to evaluate 70 Gy + cisplatin vs. 60 Gy + nivolumab.
H&N.commentary: 2-yr PFS of 98% in the control arm is outstanding. HN-005 is a cautionary tale of adopting phase II data too quickly. Phase II and prospective data may be reasonable to apply in disease states with poor prognosis in which one is willing to take risks, but one should be more cautious in applying experimental therapy in favorable prognosis. In HPV+ H&N, where LRC is 85-90%, such an approach is probably not appropriate. Some had a goal of reducing adverse effects. HN-005 finally resolved the question. An interesting feature of RTOG 1016 and HN-005 is that the control used the DAHANCA regimen with…
H&N.references: 1. Yom SS, Harris J, Caudell JJ, et al. Interim Futility Results of NRG-HN005, A Randomized, Phase II/III Non-Inferiority Trial for Non-Smoking p16+ Oropharyngeal Cancer Patients. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2024;120:s2-3. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2024.08.014

H&N.patients: Resectable oropharynx cancer by TOS T1-T2, N0-N2, p16+
H&N.methods: Phase II ➜ 60 Gy IMRT (54 & 48 Gy SIB) with weekly cis if N2 by AJCC 7th ed vs. ➜ TOS (TLM or TORS) of primary site with neck dissection with adjuvant RT based on risk features int: 50 Gy (LVI, N2, close margin) high: 60 Gy (+margins or ENE)
H&N.results: Initially planned for 2-yr OS Terminated early due to deaths acute grade 5 adverse effects 4% (n=2), all in surgery arm due to post-op hemorrhage Median f/u 17 mos OS 100% vs. 89% PFS 100% vs. 84% (favoring RT) +margin in 8% of TORS 78% of these received adjuvant RT
H&N.interpretation: The trial was terminated early due to deaths in the surgical arm. Primary RT seems to provide favorable outcomes.
H&N.commentary: ENTs have concerns regarding the surgical quality, and rad oncs should have concerns that the RT dose is too low. The trial closed early due to excess rates of G5 post-op hemorrhage. There are concerns regarding surgical quality: 4% mortality in this study (n=2/35). Compare to other studies: In ECOG 3311, there were 1/495 deaths (0.2%) with TORS, and in MC1675, no grade 5 toxicites with n=194. In ECOG there were no further deaths once the protocol was amended to strongly recommend vessel ligation, which was not specified in ORATOR2. Palma comments on Twitter (2022) however that ECOG 3311 outco…
H&N.references: 1. Palma DA, Prisman E, Berthelet E, et al. Assessment of Toxic Effects and Survival in Treatment Deescalation With Radiotherapy vs Transoral Surgery for HPV-Associated Oropharyngeal Squamous Cell Carcinoma: The ORATOR2 Phase 2 Randomized Clinical Trial [published online ahead of print, 2022 Apr 28]. JAMA Oncol 2022;10.1001/jamaoncol.2022.0615. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2022.0615 Protocol pdf in supplements: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2022.0615
H&N.commentary_refs: 1. Palma, D. David A. Palma, MD, PhD, on HPV-Associated Oropharyngeal Squamous Cell Carcinoma: Radiotherapy vs Surgery. Available at: https://ascopost.com/videos/2021-astro-annual-meeting/david-palma-on-hpv-associated-oropharyngeal-squamous-cell-carcinoma-radiotherapy-vs-surgery/ Accessed May 2, 2022. 2. Palma, D. @drdavidpalma. Twitter. May 2, 2022. Available at: https://twitter.com/drdavidpalma/status/1521109707129241602?s=20&t=SK5atwuhqpEFLiInzC8YyQ. Accessed May 2, 2022. 3. Teckie S, Gross ND. The ORATOR Trials Have Spoken-Where Do We Go From Here? [published online ahead of print, 2022 Apr 28]. JAMA Oncol 2022;10.1001/jamaoncol.2022.0461. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2022.0461

H&N.subtopic: HPV+ post-op intermediate risk RT dose
H&N.citation: Ferris et al, JCO, 2021 Burtness et al, JCO, 2025
H&N.patients: Resectable oropharynx cancer stage III-IVA AJCC 7, p16+, who undergo TORS
H&N.methods: Phase II TORS → • Low risk (negative margins, no ECE, 0-1 nodes): ➜ observation • Intermediate risk (close margin <3 mm, 2-4 nodes, ENE ≤1 mm): ➜ 50 Gy vs. ➜ 60 Gy • High risk (>1 mm ENE, positive margins, or ≥5 nodes): ➜ 66 Gy RT weekly cisplatin
H&N.results: Low risk (obs) 2-yr PFS 97% 0% recurrence in N0 15% recurrence in N1 Int risk (50 Gy vs. 60 Gy) 2-yr PFS 95% vs 96% 54-mos PFS 95% vs. 90% High risk (chemoRT) 2-yr PFS 91% 54-mos PFS 86% (no signficant differences between groups in exploratory comparisons) Grade 5 toxicity in n=1 Decline in QOL and FACT-HN for all arms with recovery to baseline in low-int risk groups, however not for high-risk (P=0.005)
H&N.interpretation: TORS and adjuvant 50 Gy RT for intermediate risk (close margin <3 mm, 2-4 nodes, ENE ≤1 mm) warrants comparison to definitive chemoRT in a phase III trial All approaches led to favorable outcomes in HPV+ oropharynx.
H&N.commentary: Surgery. ENTs have concerns regarding the surgical quality. There were no longer any deaths after the protocol was amended to strongly recommend vessel ligation. Palma comments on Twitter (2022) however that ECOG 3311 outcomes are not representative of US outcomes. ENE was the most common reason for allocation to high-risk arm (44%), which typically only can be determined following pathologic assessment, requiring staged surgeries (i.e. upfront neck dissection) to eventually indicate chemoradiotherapy. Radiation. There is concern regarding high-quality and protocol-compliant radiotherapy deliv…
H&N.references: 1. Ferris RL, Flamand Y, Weinstein GS, et al. H&N.subtopic: HPV+ post-op RT dose and chemo
H&N.patients: Oropharynx cancer stage II-III (T1-T3, N0-2b, <10 PY), p16+, who undergo TORS
H&N.methods: Intermediate risk: 50 Gy IMRT vs. 60 Gy High risk: 60 Gy IMRT vs. 60 Gy with weekly cisplatin
H&N.references: 1. Lisette Nixon. Post-operative Adjuvant Treatment for HPV-positive Tumours (PATHOS). Available from: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT02215265 . NLM identifier: NCT02215265. Accessed June 20, 2019.

H&N.patients: Oropharynx Stage I/II HPV+/-
H&N.methods: ➜ TORS and neck dissection vs. ➜ RT
H&N.references: 1. European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer – EORTC. Study Assessing The "Best of" Radiotherapy vs the "Best of" Surgery in Patients With Oropharyngeal Carcinoma (Best Of). Available from: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT02984410. NLM identifier: NCT02984410. Accessed June 20, 2019.

H&N.patients: HPV+ locally advanced oropharynx p16+. T1-2 N2a-3 or T3-4, any N
H&N.methods: ➜ RT and concurrent cisplatin vs. ➜ RT and concurrent cetuximab RT 70 Gy in 6 weeks (DAHANCA) SIB 56 & 52.5 Gy
H&N.results: 5-yr OS 85% vs. 78% 5-yr PFS 78% vs. 67% 5-yr LRF 10% vs. 17% 5-yr DM 9% vs. 12% Severe and acute adverse effects similar
H&N.interpretation: Cetuximab with RT resulted in inferior OS and PFS compared to cisplatin in HPV+ oropharynx. adverse effects is not reduced with cetuximab.
H&N.commentary: An interesting feature of 1016 and HN-005 is that the control used the DAHANCA regimen with concurrent chemotherapy. The original DAHANCA 6&7 and IAEA-ACC trials did not use chemotherapy concurrent with the 6 days per week regimen. Note the high local control with chemoRT in HPV+ tumors without surgery: 90%. Dose constraints protocol pdf, page 48: https://classic.clinicaltrials.gov/ProvidedDocs/34/NCT01302834/Prot_SAP_001.pdf#page=48 Cumulative cisplatin 200 mg/m2.
H&N.references: 1. Gillison ML, Trotti AM, Harris J, et al. Radiotherapy plus cetuximab or cisplatin in human papillomavirus-positive oropharyngeal cancer (NRG Oncology RTOG 1016): a randomised, multicentre, non-inferiority trial. Lancet 2019;393:40-50. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(18)32779-X Protocol pdf [radiation planning p48]: https://classic.clinicaltrials.gov/ProvidedDocs/34/NCT01302834/Prot_SAP_001.pdf#page=48

H&N.patients: Stage III-IV (T3N0, T4N0, T1N1-T2N3) HPV+ oropharynx cancer. Excludes N2b, N2c, >10 PY
H&N.methods: ➜ 70 Gy/35 fx and concurrent cisplatin vs. ➜ 70 Gy/35 fx and concurrent cetuximab
H&N.results: 2-yr OS 98% vs. 89% 2-yr any recurrence 16% vs. 6% Severe and any grade adverse effects similar
H&N.interpretation: Cetuximab with RT resulted in inferior OS and LC compared to cisplatin in HPV+ oropharynx. adverse effects is not reduced with cetuximab.
H&N.commentary: Note excellent disease control with RT cisplatin in HPV+ of 94% at 2 years. Cumulative cisplatin 300 mg/m2.
H&N.references: 1. Mehanna H, Robinson M, Hartley A, et al. Radiotherapy plus cisplatin or cetuximab in low-risk human papillomavirus-positive oropharyngeal cancer (De-ESCALaTE HPV): an open-label randomised controlled phase 3 trial. Lancet 2019;393:51-60. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(18)32752-1

H&N.subtopic: Addition of cetuximab to cisplatin and RT
H&N.patients: oropharynx Stage III or IV
H&N.methods: ➜ concurrent cisplatin+cetuximab vs. ➜ cisplatin AccCB/IMRT
H&N.results: No diff in 3 yr OS (76% vs. 73%) or PFS (59% vs. 61%). With cetuximab, mucositis and skin reactions worse.
H&N.interpretation: Adding cetuximab to RT and cisplatin did not improve outcomes.
H&N.references: 1. Ang KK, Zhang Q, Rosenthal DI, et al. Randomized Phase III Trial of Concurrent Accelerated Radiation Plus Cisplatin With or Without Cetuximab for Stage III to IV Head and Neck Carcinoma: RTOG 0522. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:2940-50. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2013.53.5633

H&N.patients: Stage III or IV SCC
H&N.methods: ➜ RT 70 Gy/7 weeks with carbo/5FU vs. ➜ Acc RT 70 Gy/6 weeks (2 Gy daily for 40 Gy then 1.5 BID) with carbo/5FU/ vs. ➜ Very Acc RT 64.8 Gy/3.5 weeks, no chemo
H&N.results: Slight improvements with conventional RT 3-yr PFS 38% Conv vs. 34% Acc vs. 32% Very Acc 3-yr OS 43% Conv vs. 39% vs. 37% 3-yr LFR 42% vs. 45% vs. 50% 3-yr DM 25% vs. 34% vs. 28% (NS) G3-4 mucosal adverse effects 69% vs. 76% vs. 84% p=0.0001 Feeding tube 60% vs. 64% vs. 70%, p=0.045
H&N.interpretation: Conventional fractionation with chemo results in superior OS and lower adverse effects compared to accelerated RT.
H&N.commentary: Two cycles of chemotherapy given
H&N.references: 1. Bourhis J, Sire C, Graff P, et al. H&N.patients: Oropharynx H&N cancer, HPV positive and negative treated with CRT
H&N.methods: RPA to stratify risk
H&N.results: Median f/u 3.9 years HPV+ vs HPV- LC 94% vs 80% Regional control 95% vs 82% Similar DM 10% vs 14% HPV+ low DM risk (T1-3N0-2c) DM 7% HPV+ high DM risk (T4 or N3) DM 24% HPV- low DM risk (T1-T2N0-2c) DM 7% HPV+ high DM risk (T3-4N3) DM 28% DM in HPV+ low risk N0-2a or >10py N2b patients similar for RT alone and chemoRT, but worse in N2c with RT alone
H&N.interpretation: HPV+ T1-3N0-2c have a low DM risk. N2c have worse DM with RT alone.
H&N.commentary: Refer to publication for useful nomogram to predict 3-yr Distant Control and 3-yr LRC for HPV+ and HPV- patients.
H&N.references: 1. O'Sullivan B, Huang SH, Siu LL, et al. Deintensification Candidate Subgroups in Human Papillomavirus–Related Oropharyngeal Cancer According to Minimal Risk of Distant Metastasis. J Clin Oncol 2013;31:543-50. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2012.44.0164

H&N.citation: Ang et al, NEJM, 2010 Nguyen-Tan et al, JCO, 2014
H&N.patients: Stage III-IV SCC or oral cavity, oropharynx, hypopharyx, or larynx
H&N.methods: ➜ Standard RT 70 Gy with cisplatin vs. ➜ accelerated fractionation with concominant boost (AFX-CB) 72 Gy in 6 weeks concurrent cis
H&N.results: HPV positive vs. negative has different prognosis. 3-yr OS by RPA group (see commentary for classes): Low risk: 93% Int risk: 71% High risk: 46% 3-yr OS HPV+/HPV-: 82% vs. 57% 3-yr DM HPV+/HPV-: 9% vs. 15% 3-yr LRC HPV+/HPV-: 86% vs. 65% "Good risk" HPV+ (T1-2N1-2b or T3N0-N2b, and ≤10 py): 8-yr LRF 86%, OS 81%, DM 8.3% For RT regimens, No difference in 8-yr OS, PFS, LRF, DM, or adverse effects
H&N.interpretation: There is no difference in LRF, PFS, OS, DM, and adverse effects in AFX-CB vs. standard RT.
H&N.commentary: An important reference to show HPV+ tumors can do very well without surgery. This trial is commonly referenced for its useful prognostic algorithms. RPA risk groups: Low:p16+ with <10 py any, or p16+ with >10 py and N0-2a Int: p16+ and >10py and N2b-3 or p16- and <10py and T2-3 High: p16- with >10py, or p16- with <10 py and T4
H&N.notes: Oropharynx summary -HPV was discovered as a prognostic factor for response to treatment and OS, validated in RTOG 0129 -Studies sought to de-escalate therapy in HPV+ tumors by various means, but most of these failed under multi institutional randomized trials -RTOG 1016 and De-Escalate found that chemo cannot be replaced by cetuximab -A surgical technique, TORS, is developed that leads to less morbidity than standard surgery -In HN005, 60 Gy was found inferior and the arm was closed -ECOG 3311 validates TORS and post-op approaches as options in low, int, and high risk HPV -ORATOR2 compared che…
H&N.references: 1. Ang KK, Harris J, Wheeler R, et al. Human Papillomavirus and Survival of Patients with Oropharyngeal Cancer. N Engl J Med 2010;363:24-35. H&N.methods: ➜ 70 Gy with carbo/5-FU x3 vs. ➜ RT alone

H&N.methods: Meta-analysis of 26 trials
H&N.results: Benefit of chemo at 5-yrs +6.1%, at 10-yrs +8.4% Specific benefit: concomitant + adjuvant 5-yr +12.3% induction 5-yr +6% concomitant +5% Chemo improved OS, CSS, LRC, DM
H&N.interpretation: Induction chemotherapy and adjuvant chemotherapy with concurrent improve disease control and OS over concurrent alone.

H&N.methods: Meta-analysis of 28 randomized trials to evaluate benefit of neoadjuvant, concurrent, and adjuvant chemo Trial sequence approach and fixed or random effects model
H&N.results: Induction chemo improves OS, PFS, DM, and LRC Concurrent chemo improves OS, PFS, DM, and LRC Adjuvant chemo did not improve outcomes
