H&N.subtopic: CRT + adjuvant chemo vs. RT alone
H&N.citation: Al-Sarraf et al, JCO, 1998 Al-Sarraf et al, Pro Am Soc Clin Oncol 2001
H&N.patients: Stage III-IV (would include some current T2)
H&N.methods: ➜ 70 Gy vs. ➜ 🏆 70 Gy + concurrent cisplatin x3 & adj cisplatin 5FU x3
H&N.results: 3-yr OS 78% vs. 47%. PFS 69% vs. 24% 5-yr OS 67% vs 37%, PFS 58% vs 29%

H&N.patients: Stage II-IVB with detectable EBV DNA
H&N.methods: RT with concurrent cisplatin → test EBV DNA ➜ if detectable EBV, then consolidation 5-FU/cisplatin x3 vs gem/paclitaxel x4 ➜ if undetectable EBV, then consolidation 5-FU/cisplatin x3 vs obs
H&N.results: accruing Primary endpoints: OS and PFS Secondary endpoints: time to LR, DM, RR, adverse effects, QOL
H&N.references: 1. NRG Oncology. Individualized Treatment in Treating Patients With Stage II-IVB Nasopharyngeal Cancer Based on EBV DNA. Available from: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT02135042. NLM identifier: NCT02135042. Accessed June 19, 2019.

H&N.patients: Stage II or T3N0 without adverse features* *nodes <3 cm, no level IV/Vb nodes, no ENE, EBV DNA <4k copies/mL
H&N.methods: ➜ 🏆 IMRT alone vs. ➜ IMRT with cisplatin (100 mg/m² q3weeks) RT to 68-70 Gy in 2.0-2.2 Gy/fx
H&N.results: 1° endpoint: 3-yr FFS 91% vs. 92%, p< 0.001 for noninferiority 2° endpoints: 3-yr OS 98% vs. 99% 3-yr LRFS 94% both arms 3-yr DMFS 96% vs. 98% QOL (QLQ-C30): IMRT-alone had significant benefit in QOL in global health, social function, fatigue, nausea and vomiting, pain, insomnia, appetite loss, and constipation acute grade 3-4 adverse effects 17% vs. 46% (P <0.001) late grade 2 adverse effects 73% vs. 80%, related to periphal neuropathy and hypothyroidism late grade 3+ 4% vs. 2% (n.s.)
H&N.interpretation: IMRT-alone is noninferior to current standard of care concurrent chemo in low-risk NPC for FFS, OS, and DMFS, with improvement in acute adverse effects and QOL
H&N.commentary: Potentially practice changing given that IMRT alone is currently recommended by NCCN only for a small subset of T2N0 NPC, whereas T3N0 and N1+ mandate chemotherapy. Omission of chemotherapy led to preservation of multiple QOL domains and less acute G3-4 adverse effects, however these findings may not be generalizable to non-EBV endemic areas where NPC etiology is unclear despite non-viral mediated NPC being rare. The authors also note that the threshold of 4000 EBV viral copies/mL is assay dependent and would require normalization in order to be directly applied to assays from other centers.
H&N.references: 1. Tang LL, Guo R, Zhang N, et al. Effect of Radiotherapy Alone vs Radiotherapy With Concurrent Chemoradiotherapy on Survival Without Disease Relapse in Patients With Low-risk Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma. JAMA 2022;328:728-736. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2022.13997

H&N.patients: nasopharynx Stage III-IVB, nonkeratinizing
H&N.methods: 6 arms: ➜ adj PF, conventional RT ➜ ind PF, conventional RT ➜ ind PX, conventional RT ➜ adj PF, 6 fx weekly RT ➜ ind PF, 6 fx weekly RT ➜ ind PX, 6 fx weekly RT
H&N.results: No difference with RT regimens Overall, no difference in induction/adj On exploratory analysis, some difference with induction/adj with conventional RT only: 5-yr PFS 78% vs. 62% 5-yr OS 84% vs. 72% better PFS with PX over PF induction 5-yr PFS 78% vs. 62%
H&N.interpretation: Induction chemotherapy with PX may improve PFS and OS compared to adjuvant chemotherapy with conventionally fractionated RT.
H&N.commentary: A chemo cohort was created for centers that preferd not to participate in the radiation randomization. However this cohort required a higher radiation dose. Nancy Lee comments that the statistical method used in the exploratory analysis, the Bonferroni method, is not appropriate to give conclusive results. At 803 patients, the trial may not be adequately powered (2020). The adjuvant chemo dose was lower than the induction chemo dose (Cavelieri 2020).
H&N.references: 1. Lee AWM, Ngan RKC, Ng WT, et al. NPC-0501 trial on the value of changing chemoradiotherapy sequence, replacing 5-fluorouracil with capecitabine, and altering fractionation for patients with advanced nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Cancer 2020;10.1002/cncr.32972. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.32972
H&N.commentary_refs: 1. Lee NY, Sherman EJ. Nasopharynx cancer: Induction or adjuvant? That is the question. Cancer 2020;10.1002/cncr.32970. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.32970 2. Cavalieri S, Licitra L. Induction chemotherapy is the best timekeeper in nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Cancer 2020;10.1002/cncr.32971. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.32971

H&N.methods: ➜ 68-70 Gy alone vs. ➜ 🏆 68-70 Gy with weekly cisplatin
H&N.results: Chemo RT improved OS, PFS, DMFS, LRC: 5-yr OS 86% vs 95% 5-yr LRC 91% vs 93% 5-yr PFS 78% vs 88% 5-yr DMFS 84% vs 95%
H&N.interpretation: Concurrent chemo added to RT results in improved OS, PFS, DMFS, and LRC.

H&N.interpretation: There is no benefit to adjuvant chemotherapy.

H&N.methods: ➜ 🏆 cis/docetaxel/5FU induction then RT vs. ➜ cis/docetaxel/5FU induction then RT with concurrent cisplatin RT to 66-70 Gy/ 30-33 fx with concurrent cisplatin
H&N.results: Noninferiority 3-yr PFS 76% vs. 78%, noninferior

H&N.citation: Zhang et al, NEJM, 2019 Zhang et al, JCO, 2022
H&N.patients: Stage III-IVB (not T3 or T4N0) nonkeratinizing
H&N.methods: ➜ 🏆 gem/cis induction vs. ➜ no induction prior to chemoRT RT to 66-70 Gy/ 30-33 fx with concurrent cisplatin
H&N.results: 5-yr OS 88% vs. 79% 5-yr FFS 81% vs. 67% 5-yr DMFS 90% vs. 78% 5-yr LRFS 88% vs 83% (p=0.06) Subgroup pretreatment cfEBV >4k/mL 86% vs. 71% No benefit for induction for patients ≤4000 copies cfEBV DNA Prognostic value of response after induction: 5-yr OS 100% (CR) vs. 88% (PR) vs. 62% (SD/PD) acute grade 3-4 adverse effects 76% vs. 56% late grade 3-4 adverse effects 11% both arms
H&N.interpretation: Updated five year results confirm that induction chemo with gemcitabine and cisplatin added to chemoRT improves RFS and OS compared to no induction chemo with similar late G3+ adverse effects. Response after induction chemotherapy was prognostic for OS. Pretreatment cfEBV DNA may also be a putative predictive biomarker for identifying patients who benefit from induction chemotherapy.
H&N.references: 1. Zhang Y, Chen L, Hu G-Q, et al. Gemcitabine and Cisplatin Induction Chemotherapy in Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma. N Engl J Med;381:1124-1135. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1905287 1. Zhang Y, Chen L, Hu G-Q, et al. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.00327

H&N.subtopic: elective RT omission of partial uninvolved lower neck
H&N.citation: Tang et al, Lancet Oncol, 2022 Huang et al, JCO, 2024
H&N.patients: Stage I-IV Nasopharynx, nonkeratinizing (T1-T4,N0-N1) 80% were N1, 78.5% Stage III-IV
H&N.methods: ➜ N0, b/l upper-neck irradiation (II,III, and VA); N1, ipsilateral (II-VB), contralateral (II-III, and VA) vs. ➜ bilateral whole-neck irradiation (II-VB) Stage II-IVA receieved concurrent cis (27% induction) (70 Gy to primary tumor N(rp)+, 66-70 N+, 60-62 high risk, 54-56 low risk)
H&N.results: 3-yr RFS 96% vs 98% 5-yr RFS 95% 3-yr OS 100% both arms 5-yr OS 96% vs. 93% similar acute grade 3-4 adverse effects 9 vs 10% late any grade adverse effects improved with unilateral RT hypothyroidism 34% vs 48%, dysphagia 14% vs 27%, neck tissue damage 29% vs 46%, carotid artery stenosis 15% vs. 26%
H&N.interpretation: Elective omission of unilateral or bilateral (in N0) lower neck (IV, VB) provides similar regional control and results in less radiation adverse effects compared with standard whole neck irradiation in patients with N0–N1 nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
H&N.commentary: Given well documented nodal drainage patterns, outcomes are not surprising. Majority of patients are N1 (80%), and thus only contralateral lower neck was spared. Improvements are primarily seen in G1-G2 adverse effects, although some of the effects maybe due to the slight imbalance in higher stage tumors in the control arm (T4/Stage IV 19% vs 25%) which may result in a larger volume of tissue receiving a higher dose.
H&N.references: 1. Tang L-L, Huang C-L, Zhang N et al. Elective upper-neck versus whole-neck irradiation of the uninvolved neck in patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma: an open-label, non-inferiority, multicentre, randomised phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2022. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(22)00058-4 2. Huang CL, Zhang N, Jiang W, et al. Reduced-Volume Irradiation of Uninvolved Neck in Patients With Nasopharyngeal Cancer: Updated Results From an Open-Label, Noninferiority, Multicenter, Randomized Phase III Trial. J Clin Oncol 2024. Published online March 20, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.23.02086

H&N.patients: Locally recurrent nasopharynx nonkeratinizing s/p prior RT, differentiated or non-differentiated
H&N.methods: ➜ Endoscopic nasopharyngectomy vs. ➜ IMRT reirradiation to 60-70 Gy in 2-2.36 Gy/fx
H&N.results: Improved OS with surgery 3-yr OS 86% vs. 68% Deaths due to radiation 5% vs. 20% Late grade ≥3 mucositis 5% vs. 26%
H&N.interpretation: Endoscopic nasopharyngectomy for recurrent locally advanced nasopharynx cancer leads to superior OS over reirradiation.
H&N.commentary: CTV of 5-10 mm and PTV of 1-3 mm. The death rate attributed to radiation is surprising. Elective nodes were not treated if negative. More details regarding the radiotherapy methods, dosing, and volumes would be useful, such as if higher doses per fraction or higher volumes were associated with higher deaths.
H&N.references: 1. Liu Y-P, Wen Y-H, Tang J, et al. Endoscopic surgery compared with intensity-modulated radiotherapy in resectable locally recurrent nasopharyngeal carcinoma: a multicentre, open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30673-2
H&N.commentary_refs: Liu YP. Author's reply. Lancet Oncol 2021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30673-2

H&N.subtopic: Treatment of primary in Stage IV
H&N.patients: Metastatic nasopharynx cancer with CR or PR after PF x3 (only 1.5% were keratininzing)
H&N.methods: PF x3, then if CR or PR→ ➜ 🏆 PF x6 then 70 Gy IMRT to primary vs. ➜ PF x6 Primary endpoint: OS
H&N.results: 2-yr OS 76% C+RT vs. 55% chemo 2-yr DM 54% vs 68% 2-yr PFS 35% vs. 4% About 70% had ≥3 metastases
H&N.interpretation: Locoregional treatment in metastatic nasopharynx cancer improves OS and reduces DM.
H&N.commentary: There was no concurrent chemo. About 80-90% were T3-T4 or N2-N3. Seven patients progressed at cycle 5-6 and all these progressed within 3 months after treatment. PR or CR to three cycles was required for enrollment. Results might not apply to those that progress with chemotherapy. Note that the metastatic lesions were not treated with RT, and oligometastasis was not required.
H&N.references: 1. You R, Liu Y-P, Huang P-Y, et al. Efficacy and Safety of Locoregional Radiotherapy With Chemotherapy vs Chemotherapy Alone in De Novo Metastatic Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma: A Multicenter Phase 3 Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2020. doi: DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2020.1808

H&N.patients: Nasopharynx Stage I-IVB
H&N.methods: IMRT to 70 Gy/59.4 Gy in 33 fx. For ≥T2b or N+: concurrent cisplatin and adjuvant cis/5FU
H&N.results: 2 yr LC 92%, 2 yr OS 80%, 2-yr regional PFS 91%, 2-yr DMF 86% acute grade 4 mucositis 4%, late grade 3 dysphagia 5%, xerostomia 3%, long term PEG 4%
H&N.interpretation: Outcomes with this IMRT SIB regimen are improved over Intergroup 0099.
H&N.commentary: Level I and submandibular were omitted for T1-T2 tumors
H&N.references: 1. Lee N, Harris J, Garden AS, et al. Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy With or Without Chemotherapy for Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma: Radiation Therapy Oncology Group Phase II Trial 0225. J Clin Oncol 2009;27:3684-90. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2008.19.9109

H&N.patients: Unknown primary patients review of articles
H&N.methods: Review of studies Evaluation of unilateral and bilateral RT
H&N.results: Nodal stage at dx: N1 14%, N2a 25%, N2b 26%, N2c 6%, N3 22%, Nx 6% •With unilateral neck RT mucosal emergence 5-12% (one study 44%), ipsilateral neck failure 0-46%, contralateral neck failure 2-5% (44% In one study), DM 8-31% •With bilateral neck RT mucosal emergence 2-8%, ipsilateral neck failure 0-8% (31% in one study), contralateral neck failure 0-5% (14% in one study), DM 0-30%
H&N.interpretation: Characteristics and outcomes in unknown primary tumors are outlined.
H&N.commentary: Refer to publication for useful review of unknown primary studies and management. The authors had restraint to not perform a meta-analysis on this retrospective data with limited evidence and instead expressed the results as a range.
H&N.references: 1. Galloway TJ, Ridge JA. Management of Squamous Cancer Metastatic to Cervical Nodes With an Unknown Primary Site. J Clin Oncol 2015;33:3328-37. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.61.0063

H&N.commentary: H&N Recurrence summary -More study is needed in this area -Various trials including GORTEC and RTOG 9601 evaluated reirradiation. DFS seemed to be improved but not OS. adverse effects can be severe -A Sun Yat-sen randomized trial compared hyperfractionated RT to daily RT and found that hyperfx reduced late adverse effects and improved OS. Neither regimen used chemo. -The MIRI retrospective study showed hyperfx RT worsened OS (but this was not protocol, as was in the Sun Yat-sen study) . The study also helps guide use of RT. -PET-NECK showed that the neck can be salvaged with surgery 3 mos afte…

H&N.patients: Recurrent or metastatic squamous cell carcinoma 30% were recurrent
H&N.methods: ➜ 🏆 pembrolizumab vs. ➜ 🏆 pembrolizumab-chemo vs. ➜ cetuximab chemo Reirradiation was not permitted (or if it did occur, was considered a PFS event) Chemo was either 6 cycles of cisplatin or carbo/5FU q3 weeks
H&N.results: There was no benefit with pembrolizumab or pembro-chemo in locally recurrent only disease (Fig 3) Pembro vs. cetuximab chemo ≥20% CPS PDL1 4-yr OS 22% vs. 8% ≥1% CPS PDL1 4-yr OS 17% vs. 6% Pembro-chemo vs. cetuximab chemo ≥20% CPS PDL1 4-yr OS 29% vs. 7% >1% CPS PDL1 4-yr OS 22% vs. 4% •CR rates (Table S4-5) PDL1 ≥20% CPS pembro vs. pem-chemo vs. cetux-chemo CR 8% vs. 10% vs. 3% PDL1 ≥1% CPS pembro vs. pem-chemo vs. cetux-chemo CR 6% vs. 7% vs. 3% •ORR rates PDL1 ≥20% CPS pembro vs. pem-chemo vs. cetux-chemo ORR 23% vs. 44% vs. 36-38% PDL1 ≥1% CPS pembro vs. pem-chemo vs. cetux-chemo ORR 19% …
H&N.interpretation: Overall, pembrolizumab and pembrolizumab-chemo improved OS compared to cetuximab-chemo. On subanalysis there was no benefit with pembrolizumab or pembro-chemo in locally recurrent only disease.
H&N.commentary: OS is improved with immunotherapy, but low CR rates show that it is rarely a cure and that further advancements are needed. In addition, on subanalysis there was no benefit in locally recurrent only disease. This comparison could be underpowered or could illustrate that immunotherapy should be combined with local therapy. It is important to emphasize that immunotherapy and chemotherapy are palliative. Definitive reirradiation is an option for local control while carefully taking into consideration its high rate of adverse effects, and the cure rate with radiation also shows room for improvemen…
H&N.notes: H&N Recurrence summary -More study is needed in this area -Various trials including GORTEC and RTOG 9601 evaluated reirradiation. DFS seemed to be improved but not OS. adverse effects can be severe -A Sun Yat-sen randomized trial compared hyperfractionated RT to daily RT and found that hyperfx reduced late adverse effects and improved OS. Neither regimen used chemo. -The MIRI retrospective study showed hyperfx RT worsened OS (but this was not protocol, as was in the Sun Yat-sen study) . The study also helps guide use of RT. -PET-NECK showed that the neck can be salvaged with surgery 3 mos afte…
H&N.references: 1. Harrington KJ, Burtness B, Greil R, et al. Pembrolizumab With or Without Chemotherapy in Recurrent or Metastatic Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma: Updated Results of the Phase III KEYNOTE-048 Study. J Clin Oncol 2023;41:790-802. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.21.02508 Study protocol https://ascopubs.org/action/downloadSupplement?doi=10.1200%2FJCO.21.02508&file=protocol_JCO.21.02508.pdf

H&N.patients: high risk resected locoregional recurrence or second primary in prior RT field, s/p resection with negative margins and ENE or positive margin
H&N.methods: ➜ 60 Gy daily + pembrolizumab vs. ➜ 60 Gy daily + platinum vs. ➜ pembrolizumab
H&N.results: open protocol Primary endpoint: OS Secondary endpoint: DFS, LRC, DM, adverse effects, PDL-1 expression relation to efficacy
H&N.references: 1. NCI. A Phase II Randomized Trial of Adjuvant Therapy with Pembrolizumab after Resection of Recurrent/Second Primary Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma with High Risk Features. Available from: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04671667. NLM identifier: NCT04671667. Accessed January 19, 2021.

H&N.patients: Recurrent and unresectable nasopharyngeal carcinoma in a previously irradiated area
H&N.methods: [about 50% had induction chemo] ➜ 🏆 hyperfx 65 Gy/54 fx BID vs. ➜ 60 Gy/27 fx daily no concurrent chemo
H&N.results: ⬇️ Grade ≥3 adverse effects 34% vs. 57% ⬆️ 3-yr OS 75% vs. 55% ⬇️ Grade 5 deaths 7% vs. 24% LRC 51% vs. 54%, not different
H&N.interpretation: Hyperfractionation is standard for recurrent nasopharyngeal cancer over daily fractionation. Chemotherapy was not given. Hyperfractionated radiotherapy reduces late adverse effects and improves OS compared to daily fractionation.
H&N.commentary: There was no concurrent chemo. About half had induction chemo. Whether hyperfx without chemo is better than daily radiation with concurrent chemo is unknown. Gross disease only was treated. CTV of 0.5 cm added to primary and treated to 54 Gy. Dose constraints: max ≤1% of brainstem, optic nerves, and optic chiasm <50 Gy max ≤1% temporal lobe <54 Gy max ≤1 cc spinal cord <40 Gy
H&N.references: 1. You R, Liu YP, Xie YL, et al. Hyperfractionation compared with standard fractionation in intensity-modulated radiotherapy for patients with locally advanced recurrent nasopharyngeal carcinoma: a multicentre, randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial. Lancet 2023;401:917-927. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(23)00269-6

H&N.patients: Locally recurrent nasopharynx nonkeratinizing s/p prior RT, differentiated or non-differentiated
H&N.methods: ➜ Endoscopic nasopharyngectomy vs. ➜ IMRT reirradiation to 60-70 Gy in 2-2.36 Gy/fx
H&N.results: Improved OS with surgery 3-yr OS 86% vs. 68% Deaths due to radiation 5% vs. 20% Late grade ≥3 mucositis 5% vs. 26%
H&N.interpretation: Endoscopic nasopharyngectomy for recurrent locally advanced nasopharynx cancer leads to superior OS over reirradiation.
H&N.commentary: CTV of 5-10 mm and PTV of 1-3 mm. The death rate attributed to radiation is surprising. Elective nodes were not treated if negative. More details regarding the radiotherapy methods, dosing, and volumes would be useful, such as if higher doses per fraction or higher volumes were associated with higher deaths.
H&N.references: 1. Liu Y-P, Wen Y-H, Tang J, et al. Endoscopic surgery compared with intensity-modulated radiotherapy in resectable locally recurrent nasopharyngeal carcinoma: a multicentre, open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30673-2
H&N.commentary_refs: Liu YP. Author's reply. Lancet Oncol 2021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30673-2

H&N.patients: N2 or N3 SCC of H&N (75% were known p16+)
H&N.methods: Noninferiority ➜ chemoRT then PET in 3 mos then neck dissection only if incomplete or equivocal response vs. ➜ planned neck dissection
H&N.results: neck dissections in 19% vs. 78% With chemoRT, PET CR in 69% CR in primary & PR/equivocal neck in 17% PR in primary & CR in neck in 6% Incomplete response in both in 7% p16 status did not favor either group Surgical complications 42% vs. 38% 2-yr OS 84.9% vs. 81.5% HR for death slightly favored PET and indicated noninferiority
H&N.interpretation: OS is noninferior with neck dissection guided by PET vs. planned dissection in all.
H&N.commentary: 75% were known p16+ and 79% were tested for p16. A retrospective study has found that 4 months is a better timepoint for PET in HPV+ tumors (Liu 2019). But as to whether if even longer is ideal remains to be determined. One option could be to monitor with CT, and reserve PET for unresponsive or progressive lesions. In anal cancer, 26 weeks was found to be optimal (James 2013).
H&N.references: 1. Mehanna H, Wong W-L, McConkey CC, et al. PET-CT Surveillance versus Neck Dissection in Advanced Head and Neck Cancer. N Engl J Med 2016;374:1444-54. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1514493 Protocol pdf: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa1514493/suppl_file/nejmoa1514493_protocol.pdf
H&N.commentary_refs: 1. Liu HY-h, Milne R, Lock G, et al. Utility of a repeat PET/CT scan in HPV-associated Oropharyngeal Cancer following incomplete nodal response from (chemo)radiotherapy. Oral Oncol 2019;88:153-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oraloncology.2018.11.033 2. James RD, Glynne-Jones R, Meadows HM, et al. Mitomycin or cisplatin chemoradiation with or without maintenance chemotherapy for treatment of squamous-cell carcinoma of the anus (ACT II): a randomised, phase 3, open-label, 2×2 factorial trial. Lancet Oncol 2013;14:516-24. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(13)70086-X

H&N.patients: Recurrent or 2nd primary H&N cancer in a previously irradiated area
H&N.methods: Salvage surgery then: ➜ Obs vs. ➜ 60 Gy in 11 weeks with concurrent 5FU and hydroxyurea
H&N.results: DFS significantly improved with RT with HR of 1.68, but OS not significantly different. Grade 3-4 late adverse effects was 39% vs. 10% with obs.
H&N.interpretation: Salvage chemoRT improves DFS but not OS compared to no treatment. adverse effects can be significant.
H&N.commentary: This is the first randomized radiation study for recurrent head and neck cancer.
H&N.references: 1. Janot F, Raucourt Dd, Benhamou E, et al. Randomized Trial of Postoperative Reirradiation Combined With Chemotherapy After Salvage Surgery Compared With Salvage Surgery Alone in Head and Neck Carcinoma. J Clin Oncol 2008;26:5518-23. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2007.15.0102

H&N.patients: Recurrent SCC or "second primary" in prev irradiated field, unresectable. RT finished >6 mos prior
H&N.methods: Phase II. RT to 60 Gy in 1.5 Gy BID per fx 3DCRT allowed. Chemo with 5FU/hydroxyurea.
H&N.results: 2 yr-OS 15%, 5-yr OS 5%. Better survivial if >1 year interval from recurrence. F/u 16 months Acute grade 4 in 18%, grade 5 in 8%. Late grade 3-4 adverse effects 9%
H&N.interpretation: ChemoRT for recurrent H&N cancer is feasible with acceptable adverse effects.
H&N.references: 1. Spencer SA, Harris J, Wheeler RH, et al. Final report of RTOG 9610, a multi-institutional trial of reirradiation and chemotherapy for unresectable recurrent squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck. Head Neck 2008;30:281-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/hed.20697

H&N.patients: Recurrent H&N undergoing reirradiation with IMRT
H&N.methods: Review of patients from 8 institutions
H&N.results: •Elective neck RT and hyperfx did not improve LRF or OS. •≥66 Gy improved OS and LRF. •Dose did not obviously affect outcomes in post-op reirradiation. •Highest rates of late adverse effects in hyperfx and post-op RT.
H&N.interpretation: Hyperfx and elective neck RT did not improve outcomes and may worsen adverse effects. RT to a dose of ≥66 Gy improved OS and LRF.
H&N.references: 1. Caudell JJ, Ward MC, Riaz N, et al. Volume, Dose, and Fractionation Considerations for IMRT-based Reirradiation in Head and Neck Cancer: A Multi-institution Analysis. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2018;100:606-17. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2017.11.036

Lung.citation: Bradley et al, Lancet, 2015 Bradley et al, JCO, 2019
Lung.methods: ➜ 🏆 60 Gy vs. ➜ 74 Gy ➜ cetuximab (concurrent and consolidation) vs. ➜ 🏆 no cetuximab Carbo/taxol concurrent weekly and adjuvant x2 in all
Lung.results: 74 Gy worsened OS ⬇️ Median OS 29 mos vs. 20 mos, p=0.004 2-yr OS 58% vs. 45% 5-yr OS 32% vs. 23% ↗️ 2-yr LF 31% vs. 39%, p=0.13 ↗️ 5-yr LF 38% vs. 46%, p=0.068 ↗️ 5-yr LRF 35.7% vs. 38.4%, p=0.05 ↘️ 5-yr PFS 18% vs. 13%, p=0.055 Median PFS 1.0 vs. 0.8 yrs 5-yr DM 52% vs. 58%, p=0.3 ⬆️ Grade ≥3 esophagitis 5% vs. 17% Grade 5 adverse effects n=3 vs. 9 Grade ≥3 pneumonitis 21% vs. 19% Cetuximab arm had more adverse effects and no OS difference Use of IMRT or 3DCRT not associated with outcome MVA analysis factors associated with OS: rx dose, PTV, heart V5, tumor location, institution accrual, and…
Lung.interpretation: 60 Gy is standard for locally advanced NSCLC. Dose escalation to 74 Gy worsened OS. This also led to acceptance of carboplatin and taxol into common practice rather than cisplatin and etoposide. Concurrent and maintenance cetuximab has no role in locally advanced NSCLC.
Lung.commentary: Overview. Note that 74 Gy didn't even improve LC. In fact, LF, LRF, PFS and DM all trended to better outcome with 60 Gy. Why was 60 Gy superior? Theories include dose to heart, better immune response at 60 Gy, or variations in contouring and RT QA. Regarding RT protocol compliance, although there was slightly worse contouring in the 74 Gy arm, this was not associated with worse outcomes (see the discussion section of the 2019 publication). Per authors, RT protocol compliance was rigorous and every patient was centrally reviewed. It's hard to conceive that a better trial could have been perform…
Lung.references: 1. Bradley JD, Paulus R, Komaki R, et al. Lung.citation: Antonia et al, NEJM, 2017 Antonia et al, NEJM, 2018 Spigel et al, JCO, 2022 Naidoo et al, J Thorac Oncol, 2023
Lung.patients: NSCLC stage III with no progression after at least 2 cycles platinum chemo
Lung.methods: ➜ 🏆 consolidation durvalumab q2 wks for 1 year vs. ➜ placebo RT was investigator's choice 92% received 54-66 Gy
Lung.results: Improved OS, PFS, RR, time to death or DM, new lesions, and brain mets ⬆️ 2-yr OS 66% vs. 56% ⬆️ 5-yr OS 43% vs. 33% ⬆️⬆️ Median OS 47.5 mos vs. 29.1 mos ⬇️ 5-yr new lesions 24% vs. 33% ⬇️ 5-yr brain mets 7% vs. 12% ⬆️ Median DMFS 36.5 mos vs. 17.7 mos LC not reported ORR 30% vs. 18% ⬆️ Median PFS 17.2 mos vs. 5.6 mos 1-yr PFS 56% vs. 35% 5-yr PFS 33% vs. 19% Effective in all PD-L1 tumor% except in <1% No benefit in EGFR+, but low power (n=35) Grade 3-4 adverse effects 30% vs. 26% Improved OS when started <14 days after RT
Lung.interpretation: Durvalumab is a new standard of care for locally advanced NSCLC. OS, PFS, RR, and time to death or DM improved with durvalumab
Lung.commentary: A radiation dose was not specified, but 92% received 54-66 Gy. The 5 year OS is impressive - 43%. Compare to RTOG 0617 5-yr OS of 32%. PACIFIC had more stage IIIB. In RTOG 9410, the 5-yr OS was only 16% and median OS only 17 mos! While an astounding improvement over historical trials, over 50% still die by five years - we still have much space for further improvement. Note that the results from 0617 and the no immunotherapy arm in PACIFIC are nearly identical - even though PACIFIC had more advanced patients than RTOG 0617: 0617 had ~35% Stage IIIB and PACIFIC had 45% Stage IIIB. The protocol d…

Lung.methods: ➜ 🏆 chemoRT then osimertinib vs. ➜ chemoRT then placebo. Osimertinib allowed as crossover
Lung.results: Median PFS 39.1 vs. 5.6 mos 1-yr PFS 74% vs. 22% interim 3-yr OS 84% vs. 74%
Lung.interpretation: Osimertinib after chemoRT for EGFR+ NSCLC markedly improves PFS. Interim OS also appears improved.
Lung.commentary: Osimertinib is tumorostatic rather than tumorocidal: in a pre-op study, there were zero pCRs with osimertinib, and major path responses were only 15% (Blakely 2024).
Lung.references: 1. Lu S, Kato T, Dong X, et al. Osimertinib after Chemoradiotherapy in Stage III EGFR-Mutated NSCLC. N Engl J Med. Published online June 2, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2402614
Lung.commentary_refs: 1. Blakely CM, Urisman A, Gubens MA, et al. Neoadjuvant Osimertinib for the Treatment of Stage I-IIIA Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor-Mutated Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Phase II Multicenter Study. J Clin Oncol 2024;42:3105-3114. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.24.00071

Lung.methods: ➜ 🏆 chemoRT then osimertinib vs. ➜ chemoRT then placebo. Osimertinib allowed as crossover
Lung.results: Median PFS 39.1 vs. 5.6 mos 1-yr PFS 74% vs. 22% interim 3-yr OS 84% vs. 74%
Lung.interpretation: Osimertinib after chemoRT for EGFR+ NSCLC markedly improves PFS. Interim OS also appears improved.
Lung.commentary: Osimertinib is tumorostatic rather than tumorocidal: in a pre-op study, there were zero pCRs with osimertinib, and major path responses were only 15% (Blakely 2024).

Lung.methods: ➜ RT to PET FDG avid sites and 50 Gy ENI vs. ➜ 🏆 RT to PET FDG avid sites only 60-74 Gy as high as feasible, concurrent chemo. ENI was delivered to stations at ≥10% risk based on an algorithm
Lung.results: Per protocol LRR 29% vs. 14% in PET only, p=0.039 (intention to treat was not technically lower but was noninferior, similar to per protocol, 30% vs. 17%, p=0.11) Higher doses in PET only group mean dose 65.3 Gy vs. 67.3 Gy
Lung.interpretation: Targeting FDG avid sites only, compared to doing so with ENI, reduces LRR.
Lung.commentary: LRR was more favorable in the PET only group, even though the mean RT dose was only 2 Gy higher and there were larger GTVs in that group. Can dose escalation solely explain the benefit? Or perhaps there is less injury to the immune system in the group with smaller volume, leading to improved LRC? Results were similar in the Jinan, China trial (Yuan 2007). Should this trial be evaluated per protocol or intention to treat? Intention to treat would presumably disfavor the PET only arm (imagine an investigator sees that the volume is large and not feasible for ENI, and thus treats PET only. This a…
Lung.references: 1. Nestle U, Schimek-Jasch T, Kremp S, et al. Imaging-based target volume reduction in chemoradiotherapy for locally advanced non-small-cell lung cancer (PET-Plan): a multicentre, open-label, randomised, controlled trial. Lancet Oncol 2020. doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30013-9

Lung.trial: Shandong Cancer Hospital & Institute, Jinan, China
Lung.citation: Yuan et al, Am J Clin Oncol, 2007
Lung.methods: Induction chemo x1 -> concurrent chemoRT -> adjuvant chemo ➜ ENI 60-64 Gy vs. ➜ IFRT 68-74 Gy
Lung.results: 2-yr LF 51% ENI vs. 36% IFRT 2-yr OS 26% vs. 39%, p=0.048 5-yr OS 18% vs. 25% (NS) Pneumonitis 29% vs. 17%
Lung.interpretation: Omission of ENI leads to improved OS and LC and seems to allow dose escalation.
Lung.commentary: IFRT volume was prechemotherapy. OS benefit to omitting ENI could be due to heart adverse effects, sparing of the immune system, or dose escalation to tumor. This trial was also a dose escalation trial. How can the differences from RTOG 0617 be reconciled? Observe that in the PET-PLAN study (Nestle 2020), LRR was worse in the ENI group, and in this study there were only small differences in dosing between arms. Perhaps the larger field in ENI injures the immune system.
Lung.references: 1. Yuan S, Sun X, Li M, et al. A Randomized Study of Involved-Field Irradiation Versus Elective Nodal Irradiation in Combination With Concurrent Chemotherapy for Inoperable Stage III Nonsmall Cell Lung Cancer. Am J Clin Oncol 2007;30:239-44. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/01.coc.0000256691.27796.24

Lung.methods: Phase I-II dose escalation Group 1 (lung V20 <25%) 70.9 → 77.4 →83.8 → 90.3 Gy Group 2 (lung V20 of 25%-37%) 70.9 → 77.4 Gy
Lung.results: Maximum tolerated dose was 83.8 Gy in Group 1 and 77.4 Gy in Group 2 90.3 Gy had 2 deaths, 1 late grade 4, and 5 late grade 3 toxicities. 90.3 Gy had 3 acute grade 3. 83.8 Gy had 1 late grade 3. There was 1 acute grade 4 and no acute grade 3 LC and OS were similar in dose levels
Lung.interpretation: 83.8 Gy was the maximum tolerated dose.
Lung.references: 1. Bradley J, Graham MV, Winter K, et al. Toxicity and outcome results of RTOG 9311: a phase I-II dose-escalation study using three-dimensional conformal radiotherapy in patients with inoperable non-small-cell lung carcinoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2005;61:318-328. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2004.06.260

Lung.patients: Stage I-II (28%), Stage IIIA (33%), Stage IIIB (32%), recurrent (6%)
Lung.methods: Phase I: Dose escalated from 70.2→75.6→81→84→90 Gy. Induction chemo in 16%. No concurrent chemo. Protocol later amended to omit ENI.
Lung.results: 84 Gy was maximum tolerated dose. Excess lung adverse effects at 90 Gy. LC, DFS, and OS seem to improve with increasing dose
Lung.interpretation: 84 Gy was the maximum tolerated dose.
Lung.commentary: LC and OS seem to improve with increasing dose. The trial recorded this outcomes, but being a phase I, was not powered for definitive comparison.
Lung.references: 1. Rosenzweig KE, Fox JL, Yorke E, et al. Results of a phase I dose-escalation study using three-dimensional conformal radiotherapy in the treatment of inoperable nonsmall cell lung carcinoma. Cancer 2005;103:2118-27. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.21007

Lung.patients: NSCLC Stage II-III, not chemo candidates Zubrod PS ≥2 >10% weight loss in 6 months
Lung.methods: Superiority ➜ hypofractionated RT 60 Gy/ 15 fx vs. ➜ conventional fractionated RT 60 Gy/ 30 fx
Lung.results: 1-yr OS 38% vs. 45%, p=0.29 Median OS 8.2 mos vs. 10.6 mos, p=0.17 1-yr PFS 6.4 mos vs. 7.3 mos (NS) Grade 2 esophagitis 22% vs. 8.7% p=.09 On MVA, receipt systemic therapy and max dose to esophagus most associated with OS
Lung.interpretation: Accelerated hypofractionated is not superior to conventional fractionation in locally advanced lung cancer, but may represent a viable treatment alternative for those unable to have chemotherapy.
Lung.commentary: The trial was designed to investigate superiority of hypofractionated RT and was not powered to show noninferiority or equivalence (note only 103 patients); given lack of SS difference in endpoints between arms, conventional fractionation is still standard of care. 60 Gy/15 fx was associated with increased Grade 2 AEs, which may be impactful in the more fragile "target" patient population with medical comorbidities and poor KPS who are too deconditioned to tolerate chemotherapy in the first place. The characteristics were imbalanced against the conventional arm. There were significantly more N…
Lung.references: 1. Iyengar P, Zhang-Velten E, Court L, et al. Accelerated Hypofractionated Image-Guided vs Conventional Radiotherapy for Patients With Stage II/III Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer and Poor Performance Status: A Randomized Clinical Trial [published online ahead of print, 2021 Aug 12]. JAMA Oncol. 2021;10.1001/jamaoncol.2021.3186. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2021.3186

Lung.citation: Maguire et al, Eur J Cancer, 2014
Lung.methods: Phase II randomized 55 Gy RT with ➜ sequential cis/vinorelbine vs. ➜ concurrent cis/vinorelbine
Lung.results: Treatment mortality 1.7% and 2.9% 2-yr OS 46% and 50%
Lung.interpretation: Hypofractionated radiation therapy of 55 Gy in Stage III NSCLC is safe and results in reasonable outcomes.
Lung.commentary: Compare the 2-yr OS here to that in RTOG 0617. The 2-yr OS is similar to that with 74 Gy and concurrent chemo. In RTOG 0617, 60 Gy with concurrent chemo had 2-yr OS of 58%. Although the SOCCAR trial is not designed for this comparison, this comparison is the best available.
Lung.references: 1. Maguire J, Khan I, McMenemin R, et al. SOCCAR: A randomised phase II trial comparing sequential versus concurrent chemotherapy and radical hypofractionated radiotherapy in patients with inoperable stage III Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer and good performance status. Eur J Cancer 2014;50:2939–2949. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejca.2014.07.009

Lung.patients: Stage I NSCLC ≤4 cm, poor pulmonary function
Lung.methods: Phase I: 70 Gy RT hypofractionated in various doses (2.42, 2.69, 3.04, 3.5, 4.11 Gy/fx)
Lung.results: MTD not defined. LC 92% (3 failures) No grade 4 or late grade 3 adverse effects Two acute grade 3 toxicities
Lung.interpretation: LC is favorable with hypofractionated RT for lung. The MTD was not reached.
Lung.commentary: This trial may provide useful dose options for palliation.
Lung.references: 1. Bogart JA, Hodgson L, Seagren SL, et al. Phase I study of accelerated conformal radiotherapy for stage I non-small-cell lung cancer in patients with pulmonary dysfunction: CALGB 39904. J Clin Oncol 2010;28:202-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2009.25.0753

Lung.subtopic: RT lung adverse effects with chemo
Lung.patients: NSCLC who underwent chemoRT cis etoposide in 38% carbo taxol in 26% other in 36% neoadjuvant in 56% concurrent in 100%
Lung.methods: Meta-analysis of 12 studies Evaluated rates of lung pneumonitis related to RT dose to lungs. All patients received concurrent chemo. Assessed total lung - GTV Results validated externally. RPA to define risk groups.
Lung.results: -Refer to publication for useful statistics and RPA -Median dose 60 Gy. Overall symptomatic pneumonitis 29%. Fatal pneumonitis in 1.9%. -Increased risk of radiation pneumonitis with carbo/paclitaxel, OR 3.3, and V20≥20. -Predictors of fatal pneumonitis: >2 Gy/fx, V20, lower lobe -If both age >65 and treated with carbo/taxol, then >50% risk of symptomatic pneumonitis V20 <20%: 18% grade 2, 0 grade 5 V20 20-30%: 30% grade 2, 1% grade 5 V20 30-40%: 33% grade 2, 2.9% grade 5 V20 >40%: 36% grade 2, 3.5% grade 5
Lung.interpretation: Factors predictive of radiation pneumonitis are outlined. Elderly patients undergoing concurrent carbo taxol are at highert risk.
Lung.commentary: These numbers would likely be reduced with modern arc IMRT
Lung.references: 1. Palma DA, Senan S, Tsujino K, et al. Predicting radiation pneumonitis after chemoradiation therapy for lung cancer: an international individual patient data meta-analysis. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2013;85:444-50. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2012.04.043

Lung.methods: Retrospective evaluation of DVH parameters compared with incidence and grade of pneumonitis after treatment. 42% had concurrent chemo
Lung.interpretation: V20 values predictive of radiation pneumonitis are outlined.
Lung.commentary: Only 42% of patients had chemo. With chemo, chance of pneumonitis may increase (Palma 2013).
Lung.references: 1. Graham MV, Purdy JA, Emami B, et al. Clinical dose & volume histogram analysis for pneumonitis after 3D treatment for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 1999;45:323-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0360-3016(99)00183-2

Lung.trial: Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC), The Netherlands
Lung.methods: Meta-analysis ➜ PCI vs. ➜ obs
Lung.results: PCI improved PFS (5-yr +4.7%, HR 0.77) and BMFS (5-yr +4.2%, HR 0.82). No benefit in OS PCI grade ≥3 adverse effects 6.4% 2-yr Memory impairment 21% vs. 0% 2-yr fatigue 21% vs. 8% 7 RCTs found, 4 with data available
Lung.interpretation: PCI improves PFS and BMFS, but not OS. There are adverse effects of memory changes and fatigue.
Lung.commentary: One of the RCTs included did require MRI, RTOG 0214. MRI was optional in NVALT and Guangzhou. The doses used were markedly higher than now employed in SCLC PCI: 40-58 Gy, ≥30 Gy, and 45 or 60 or 66 Gy, PCI improved PFS without a benefit in OS. This is not uncommon in oncology. However the absolute benefit here with PCI is minimal. Workup with MRI allows for preservation of QOL until therapy is needed.
Lung.references: 1. Witlox WJA, Ramaekers LT, Lacas B, et al. Individual patient data meta-analysis of prophylactic cranial irradiation in locally advanced non-small cell lung cancer. Radiother Oncol 2021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2021.02.002

Lung.methods: ➜ seq cis/vinbl then 60 Gy RT vs. ➜ 🏆 conc cis/vinbl 60 Gy RT vs. ➜ conc cis/etoposide RT BID
Lung.results: Median OS 14.6 mos vs. 17.0 vs. 15.6 5-yr OS 10% vs. 16% vs. 13% (OS SS for arms 2 vs. 1, but not for 2 vs. 3) (from errata) LF 39% vs. 30% vs. 29% (LC in arms 2 vs. 1 p=0.09, in 3 vs. 1 p=0.053) DM 45-47%, not different
Lung.interpretation: Concurrent chemoRT with QD RT results in improved OS over induction chemo or chemoRT with BID fx.
Lung.commentary: The information on statistical significance in the survival outcomes is sourced from the errata. 42% IIIA and 57% IIIB in this trial. RTOG 0617 had ~66% Stage IIIA. RTOG 0617 treated to 60 Gy as well, but had much greater improved survival outcomes. A number of advancements were applied in 0617 including omission of ENI (elective nodes were treated to 45 Gy in 9410), use of IMRT, VMAT, and carbo/taxol instead of cis/etop.
Lung.references: 1. Curran WJ Jr, Paulus R, Langer CJ, et al. Sequential vs. concurrent chemoradiation for stage III non-small cell lung cancer: randomized phase III trial RTOG 9410 [published correction appears in J Natl Cancer Inst. 2012 Jan 4;104(1):79]. J Natl Cancer Inst 2011;103:1452–1460. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/jnci/djr325

Lung.methods: Meta-analysis of 6 trials evaluating 🏆concurrent vs. sequential chemoRT WJLCG, RTOG 9410, GMMA Ankara, GLOT-GFPC NPC, EORTC 09972, CALGB
Lung.results: 5-yr OS increased, 15.1% vs. 10.6% 5-yr LRF improved, 29% vs. 35% No difference in DM Acute grade 3-4 esophagus adverse effects increased, 18% vs. 4% No difference in pulmonary adverse effects
Lung.interpretation: Concurrent chemoradiation improves OS compared to sequencial chemoRT, with increased esophageal adverse effects.
Lung.references: 1. Aupérin A, Péchoux CL, Rolland E, et al. Meta-Analysis of Concomitant Versus Sequential Radiochemotherapy in Locally Advanced Non–Small-Cell Lung Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2010;28:2181-90. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2009.26.2543

Lung.trial: Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, China
Lung.methods: ➜ concurrent cis/etoposide x2 q4 wks vs. ➜ concurrent carbo/paclitaxel weekly with 60-66 Gy RT
Lung.results: 3-yr OS 41% vs. 26%, p=0.024 (did not meet primary endpoint of 17% difference for superiority) Median OS 23.3 cis/etop vs. 20.7 carbo/taxol, p=0.095 grade ≥2 pneumonitis 19% vs. 33% grade ≥3 esophagitis 20% vs. 6%
Lung.interpretation: Cis/etoposide might be superior to weekly carbo/taxol concurrent with RT for Stage III NSCLC.
Lung.commentary: The STRIPE study showed an increased risk of pneumonitis with carbo/taxol (Palma 2013). Historical trivia: Peking=Beijing. Peking is a variant anglicization.
Lung.references: 1. Liang J, Bi N, Wu S, et al. Etoposide and cisplatin versus paclitaxel and carboplatin with concurrent thoracic radiotherapy in unresectable stage III non-small cell lung cancer: a multicenter randomized phase III trial. Ann Oncol 2017;28:777-83. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/annonc/mdx009

Lung.citation: Vokes et al, JCO, 2007 Stinchcombe et al, J Thorac Oncol, 2009
Lung.methods: ➜ No induction chemo vs. ➜ induction with two upfront cycles of carboplatin AUC 6 and paclitaxel 200 mg/m2 66 Gy RT with concurrent weekly x 7 weeks carboplatin AUC 2 and paclitaxel 50 mg/m2
Lung.results: Median OS not different: 12 vs. 14 mos (p=0.3) 2-yr OS 29% vs. 31% (NS) At median f/u of 88 mos, no benefit in any risk group including age, gender, race, stage IIIB vs. IIIA, hgb, ECOG PS, weight loss
Lung.interpretation: The addition of 2 cycles of induction carboplatin and paclitaxel increased adverse effects and provided no OS benefit.
Lung.commentary: Despite the results of this study, those involved in creation of RTOG 0617 had insisted on inclusion of adjuvant chemo in RTOG 0617. Their rationale was that the chemo in RTOG 0617 is not full strength and therefore two adjuvant cycles are needed (Bradley 2015).
Lung.references: 1. Vokes EE, II JEH, Kelley MJ, et al. Induction Chemotherapy Followed by Chemoradiotherapy Compared With Chemoradiotherapy Alone for Regionally Advanced Unresectable Stage III Non–Small-Cell Lung Cancer: Cancer and Leukemia Group B. J Clin Oncol 2007;25:1698-704. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2006.07.3569 2. Stinchcombe TE, Hodgson L, Herndon JE, 2nd, et al. Treatment outcomes of different prognostic groups of patients on cancer and leukemia group B trial 39801: induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiotherapy compared with chemoradiotherapy alone for unresectable stage III non-small cell lung cancer. J Thorac Oncol 2009;4:1117-25. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/JTO.0b013e3181b27b33

Lung.methods: ➜ 60 Gy RT alone vs. ➜ induction cis/vinblastine then RT
Lung.results: Median OS 9.7 mos vs. 13.8 mos 5-yr OS 6% vs. 17%
Lung.interpretation: Induction chemotherapy improves OS in NSCLC.
Lung.commentary: Trial showed the benefit of chemotherapy. Later trials showed concurrenct chemo to be superior over induction
Lung.references: 1. Dillman RO, Herndon J, Seagren SL, Eaton WL Jr., Green MR. Improved Survival in Stage III Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer: Seven-Year Follow-up of Cancer and Leukemia Group B (CALGB) 8433 Trial. J Natl Cancer Inst 1996;88:1210-5. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/jnci/88.17.1210

Lung.trial: Hoosier Oncology Group and US Oncology
Lung.methods: RT to 59.4 Gy with cis/etop then: ➜ adjuvant docetaxel vs. ➜ no adjuvant chemo
Lung.results: No improvement in OS. Increased adverse effects. Trial terminated early due to futility.
Lung.interpretation: Consolidation docetaxel after RT with concurrent cisplatin and etoposide results in increased adverse effects with no improvement in OS.
Lung.commentary: The study is sometimes incorrectly cited colloquially as justification to support no adjuvant chemotherapy of any kind after chemoRT, but this is too broad of an interpretation. The study evaluates single agent docetaxel only.
Lung.references: 1. Hanna N, Neubauer M, Yiannoutsos C, et al. Phase III Study of Cisplatin, Etoposide, and Concurrent Chest Radiation With or Without Consolidation Docetaxel in Patients With Inoperable Stage III Non–Small-Cell Lung Cancer: The Hoosier Oncology Group and U.S. Oncology. J Clin Oncol 2008;26:5755-60. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2008.17.7840

Lung.patients: size ≤2 cm, N0, in outer 3rd of lung (60% were adenocarcinoma)
Lung.methods: Noninferiority ➜ lobectomy vs. ➜ 🏆 sublobar resection (segmentectomy in 38%, or wedge in 59%) Mediastinal sampling and PET in all ~80% had VATS
Lung.results: ✅ 5-yr DSF 64% both arms, noninferior 5-yr OS ~80% both arms No difference in recurrence patterns 5-yr RFS ~70% both arms 5-yr LRR 13% vs. 10% 5-yr RR ~2% 5-yr DM ~16% Sublobar had 2% benefit in median predicted FEV1 30-day mortality 1.1% vs. 0.6% 90-day mortality 1.7% vs. 1.2%
Lung.interpretation: Sublobar resection for early NSCLC size ≤2cm in outer 3rd of lung is now standard. The procedure was noninferior to lobectomy.
Lung.commentary: CALGB/Alliance 140503 and JCOG0802 dispelled a 30-year-old finding that lobectomy was superior to wedge (LCSG 821 Ginsberg 1995) in Western and Eastern populations respectively. The studies are representative of modern surgical outcomes. CALGB had 0.6-1.7% 30 to 90-day post-op moratlity, and JCOG had 0% post-op deaths. In CALGB 80% had VATS, and all had PET and mediastinal sampling. In JCOG 90% had VATS, all had mediastinal sampling, and PET was not required. There is room for further improvement in RFS/DFS. In CALGB these tumors of size ≤2 cm still have a DM rate of 16%. But caution cross com…
Lung.references: 1. Altorki NK, Wang X, Kozono D, et al. Lobar or Sublobar Resection for Peripheral Stage IA Non–Small-Cell Lung Cancer. N Engl J Med 2023;388:489-498. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2212083 Protocol pdf: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa2212083/suppl_file/nejmoa2212083_protocol.pdf

Lung.patients: size ≤2 cm, N0, in outer 3rd of lung, consolidation to tumor ratio >0.5 (~90% were adenocarcinoma)
Lung.methods: Noninferiority ➜ lobectomy vs. ➜ 🏆 segmentectomy Mediastinal dissection in all. PET not required 90% had VATS
Lung.results: ✅ 5-yr OS 91% vs. 94%, noninferior 5-yr RFS 88% both arms 5-yr LR 5% vs. 11% 1-yr FEV1 reduction 12% vs. 9% 30- and 90-day mortality: none
Lung.interpretation: Segmentectomy for early NSCLC size ≤2 cm in outer 3rd of lung is now standard. The procedure is noninferior to lobectomy. There was no post-operative mortality. Superiority was not powered, but the procedure may possibly lead to superior OS and/or worse LC.
Lung.commentary: CALGB/Alliance 140503 and JCOG0802 dispelled a 30-year-old finding that lobectomy was superior to wedge (LCSG 821 Ginsberg 1995) in Western and Eastern populations respectively. The studies are representative of modern surgical outcomes. CALGB had 0.6-1.7% 30 to 90-day post-op moratlity, and JCOG had 0% post-op deaths. In JCOG 90% had VATS, all had mediastinal sampling, and PET was not required. In CALGB 80% had VATS, and all had PET and mediastinal sampling. RFS in JCOG was more favorable than in CALGB: 88% vs. 70%. In JCOG 90% were adenocarcinoma, which may explain the better prognosis.
Lung.references: Saji H, Okada M, Tsuboi M, et al. Segmentectomy versus lobectomy in small-sized peripheral non-small-cell lung cancer (JCOG0802/WJOG4607L): a multicentre, open-label, phase 3, randomised, controlled, non-inferiority trial. Lancet 2022;399:1607-1617. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(21)02333-3

Lung.citation: Ginsberg et al, Ann Thorac Surg, 1995
Lung.methods: ➜ lobectomy vs. ➜ wedge resection, 2 cm margins
Lung.results: LR 6% lobectomy vs. 18% wedge Trend to increase in overall death and cancer death with wedge resection
Lung.interpretation: LC is worse with wedge resection in Stage I, and wedge resection does not improve OS or reduce adverse effects.
Lung.commentary: A later trial, CALGB/Alliance 140503, showed noninferiority between lobectomy and wedge. The present study is likely underpowered.
Lung.references: 1. Ginsberg RJ, Rubinstein LV. Randomized trial of lobectomy versus limited resection for T1 N0 non-small cell lung cancer. Ann Thorac Surg 1995;60:615-23. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/0003-4975(95)00537-u
Lung.commentary_refs: Okada M, Koike T, Higashiyama M, Yamato Y, Kodama K, Tsubota N. Radical sublobar resection for small-sized non–small cell lung cancer: A multicenter study. J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg 2006;132:769-75. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtcvs.2006.02.063

Lung.citation: Palma et al, JAMA Oncol, 2019 Tan et al, IJROBP, 2024
Lung.methods: SBRT→ VATS lobectomy or sublobar resection after 10 weeks (SBRT doses: 54 Gy/3 fx if peripheral, 55 Gy/5 fx for chest wall contact or size >3 cm, 60 Gy/8 fx if within 2 cm of MS or brachial plexus)
Lung.results: Of those who had surgery: pCR 60%, major pCR 63% 5-yr LC 94% 5-yr RC 80% 5-yr DM 18% 2-yr OS 77%, 5-yr OS 67% No 30- or 90-day post op deaths Grade 3-5 adverse effects in 17%
Lung.interpretation: With SBRT followed by VATS resection, the pCR rate was lower than expected, but higher than in many pre-op chemoimmunotherapy trials. adverse effects and post op deaths were similar to surgical series. Randomized trials are warranted to detect if pre-op SBRT results in any survival or DM benefit.
Lung.commentary: The pCR rate is lower than expected compared to LC seen in SBRT studies, but markedly higher than that in pre-op chemoimmunotherapy trials. On Twitter David Palma (2019) comments that a pCR of 60% may not represent active tumor in all cases; tumor can be alive but not mitotically active to cause LF later, undergoing a slow senescent death. Ten weeks may be early to assess response surgically. This time point was chosen for surgical purposes to avoid fibrosis during resection. Yet in view of other pre-op therapy studies, 60% pCR actually is an achievement, and is among the highest of any trial …
Lung.references: 1. Palma DA, Nguyen TK, Louie AV, et al. Measuring the Integration of Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy Plus Surgery for Early-Stage Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Phase 2 Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2019;5:681-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2018.6993 Protocol pdf in supplements: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2018.6993 2. Tan V, Correa RJM, Nguyen TK, et al. Measuring the Integration of Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy Plus Surgery for Early-Stage Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: Long-Term Clinical Outcomes. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2024.07.2332
Lung.commentary_refs: Palma DA. @drdavidpalma. Twitter. March 1, 2019. Available from: https://twitter.com/drdavidpalma/status/1101603630921248770. Accessed June 23, 2019.

Lung.citation: VA Office of Research and Development, ongoing protocol
Lung.methods: ➜ SBRT vs. ➜ anatomic resection (lobectomy or segmentectomy) Peripheral tumors: 54 Gy/ 3 fx, 48 Gy/ 4 fx, or 57.5 Gy/ 5 fx Central: 50 Gy/ 5 fx
Lung.results: Ongoing study Primary endpoint: OS Secondary endpoints: QOL, CSS, LC, regional control, DM, FEV1
Lung.commentary: Note that size ≤5 cm is larger than the eligibility criteria in CALGB/Alliance 140503 and JCOG0802, the trials that showed noninferiority with sublobar resection in tumors ≤2 cm. Wedge is not permitted in VALOR.
Lung.references: 1. VA Office of Research and Development. Veterans Affairs Lung Cancer Surgery Or Stereotactic Radiotherapy (VALOR). Available from: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT02984761. NLM identifier: NCT02984761. Accessed June 19, 2019.

Lung.methods: ➜ platinum-based chemo alone→ surgery vs. ➜ 🏆 Peri-op immunotherapy: Neoadjuvant nivolumab + platinum-based chemo → surgery → adjuvant nivolumab
Lung.results: Interim analysis pCR 5% vs. 27% If PDL1 ≥50% pCR 6% vs. 52% major path response 12% vs. 35% Underwent surgery 77% vs. 78% (Canceled surgery due to progression 9.5% vs. 5.7%) 1.5-yr EFS 50% vs. 70% Grade 3-4 events 25% vs. 33%
Lung.interpretation: Peri-operative nivolumab for surgical NSCLC improves EFS at interim analysis. Chemotherapy and chemoimmunotherapy do not improve the resection rate, but rather decrease it.
Lung.commentary: In an editorial, Bazarbachi et al points out that results are similar to CheckMate 816, which uses neoadjuvant nivolumab only, and suggests there is no benefit to adjuvant nivolumab.
Lung.references: 1. Cascone T, Awad MM, Spicer JD, et al. Perioperative Nivolumab in Resectable Lung Cancer. N Engl J Med 2024;390:1756-1769. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2311926 Study protocol https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa2311926/suppl_file/nejmoa2311926_protocol.pdf
Lung.commentary_refs: 1. Bazarbachi AH, Magrini N, Aziz Z, Fojo T. Evidence for a reduction in number of cycles of immune checkpoint inhibitors. Lancet Oncol 2025;26:9-11. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(24)00594-1

Lung.citation: Wakelee et al, NEJM, 2023 Spicer et al, Lancet, 2024
Lung.patients: Stage II, IIIA, or IIIB (N2) NSCLC
Lung.methods: ➜ platinum-based chemo alone→ surgery vs. ➜ 🏆 Peri-op immunotherapy: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab + platinum-based chemo → surgery → adjuvant pembrolizumab
Lung.results: pCR 4% vs. 18% major path response 11% vs. 30% Underwent surgery 79% vs. 82% (Did not undergo surgery due to progression 6.5% vs. 3.8%) 3-yr OS 64% vs. 71% Median EFS 2-yr EFS 41% vs. 62% grade ≥3 adverse effects 38% vs. 45%
Lung.interpretation: Peri-operative pembrolizumab for surgical NSCLC improves OS. Chemotherapy and chemoimmunotherapy do not improve the resection rate, but rather decrease it.
Lung.commentary: For eligibility patients were required to be resectable upfront, but note that at the end of the trials evaluating neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy that respectability was decreased. Only 75% to 83% finally underwent surgery in either arm, and about 5-10% were due to progression. Poor response or progression from chemoimmunotherapy in a patient who was resectable upfront or borderline resectable places the patient in a worse position: they then must complete chemoradiation, which will be more difficult with already impaired bone marrow. PET images can be difficult to interpret after immunotherap…
Lung.references: 1. Wakelee H, Liberman M, Kato T, et al. Perioperative Pembrolizumab for Early-Stage Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer [published online ahead of print, 2023 Jun 3]. N Engl J Med 2023;10.1056/NEJMoa2302983. Study protocol https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa2302983/suppl_file/nejmoa2302983_protocol.pdf DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2302983 2. Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy versus chemotherapy alone in patients with advanced non-small cell lung cancer without tumor PD-L1 expression: A pooled analysis of 3 randomized controlled trials. Cancer 2020;126:4867-4877. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.33142

Lung.patients: Stage II, IIIA, or IIIB (N2) NSCLC
Lung.methods: Phase III ➜ platinum-based chemo → surgery vs. ➜ 🏆 Peri-op durvalumab + platinum-based chemo Primary endpoint: pCR & EFS
Lung.results: 1-yr EFS 65% vs. 73% pCR 4% vs. 17% Underrwent surgery 77% vs. 77% (Canceled surgery due to progression 7.5% vs. 6.8%) in PDL1 ≥50%, pCR 23% but CIs overlap Grade 3-4 events similar at 42-43%
Lung.interpretation: Peri-op durvalumab improves EFS and pCR in NSCLC. Chemotherapy and chemoimmunotherapy do not improve the resection rate, but rather decrease it.
Lung.commentary: Durvalumab was given pre-op q3w x4 doses with chemo and post-op q4w x 12 doses.
Lung.references: 1. Heymach JV, Harpole D, Mitsudomi T, Taube JM, Galffy G, Hochmair M, Winder T, Zukov R, Garbaos G, Gao S, Kuroda H, Ostoros G, Tran TV, You J, Lee KY, Antonuzzo L, Papai-Szekely Z, Akamatsu H, Biswas B, Spira A, Crawford J, Le HT, Aperghis M, Doherty GJ, Mann H, Fouad TM, Reck M; AEGEAN Investigators. Perioperative Durvalumab for Resectable Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer. N Engl J Med 2023;389:1672-1684. Lung.subtopic: Neoadjuvant nivo + chemo vs chemo alone
Lung.citation: Forde et al, NEJM, 2022 Awad et al, JCO, 2025 Forde et al, NEJM, 2025
Lung.patients: Stage IB - IIIA NSCLC (64% stage IIIA) (50% had PD-L1 ≥1%)
Lung.methods: ➜ platinum-based chemo alone vs. ➜ 🏆 Nivolumab + platinum-based chemo q3w x3 ➜ Exploratory amended arm, unpowered: 🏆 Nivolumab + ipilimumab platinum-based chemo q3w x3 Then surgery in 6 weeks Primary endpoints: EFS, pCR
Lung.results: pCR 2% vs. 24% nivo in PDL1 ≥50%, pCR 44% with nivo Proceeded to surgery: 75% vs. 83% nivo (Surgery canceled due to progression 6.7% vs. 9.5%) Median EFS 20.8 vs. 31.6 mos nivo If no PCR, median EFS 41 vs. 20 mos 5-yr OS 55% vs. 65% nivo If pCR, 5-yr OS 95% If no pCR, 5-yr OS 56% (no improvement) 2-yr DMFS 76% vs. 57% LR info not provided RT for recurrence 22% vs. 11% 1-yr no disease progression/recurrence 63% vs. 76% Minimally invasive surgery was more common with nivolumab, with no delays in surgery observed grade 3+ adverse effects 37% vs. 34% Nivolumab plus ipilimumab amendment Unpowered f…
Lung.interpretation: Neoadjuvant nivolumab with chemo prior to surgery improves OS and pCR compared to chemo alone in resectable NSCLC. The OS benefit is only in those with pCR. Adverse effects are not increased. Chemotherapy and chemoimmunotherapy do not improve the resection rate, but rather decrease it. Neoadjuvant nivoluman plus ipilimumab also shows improvement in OS and EFS, though this arm was added to the protocol in a late addendum and is unpowered.
Lung.commentary: For eligibility patients were required to be resectable upfront, but note that at the end of the trials evaluating neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy that respectability was decreased. Only 75% to 83% finally underwent surgery in either arm, and about 5-10% were due to progression. Poor response or progression from chemoimmunotherapy in a patient who was resectable upfront or borderline resectable places the patient in a worse position: they then must complete chemoradiation, which will be more difficult with already impaired bone marrow. PET images can be difficult to interpret after immunotherap…

Lung.commentary: For eligibility patients were required to be resectable upfront, but note that at the end of the trials evaluating neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy that respectability was decreased. Lung.citation: Felip et al, Lancet, 2021 Felix et al, JCO, 2025
Lung.methods: Surgery then adjuvant platinum-based chemo→ ➜ best care vs. ➜ 🏆 atezolizumab
Lung.results: Atezolizumab improved DFS 3-yr DFS 49% vs. 56% ITT 5-yr not different PP PDL1 ≥1%, 5-yr OS 79% vs. 82% PP all patients, 5-yr OS not different PP PDL1 ≥50%, 5-yr OS 65% vs. 83% On subgroup analysis, benefit is in Stage II-IIIA, and better in those with PD-L1 ≥1%
Lung.interpretation: Atezolizumab after adjuvant chemotherapy improves DFS in resected Stage II-IIIA, especially in those with PD-L1 ≥1%. OS is improved in those with PDL1 ≥50% who were treated per protocol. There was no OS benefit in ITT.
Lung.commentary: For eligibility patients were required to be resectable upfront, but note that at the end of the trials evaluating neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy that respectability was decreased. Only 75% to 83% finally underwent surgery in either arm, and about 5-10% were due to progression. Poor response or progression from chemoimmunotherapy in a patient who was resectable upfront or borderline resectable places the patient in a worse position: they then must complete chemoradiation, which will be more difficult with already impaired bone marrow. PET images can be difficult to interpret after immunotherap…
