Lung.patients: Stage IIIA N2 NSCLC s/p surgery + platinum chemo x4 cycles
Lung.methods: ➜ post-op ≤50 Gy/ 2 Gy per fx vs. ➜ 🏆 no RT RT given ≤6 weeks after chemo CTV: ipsilateral hilum and MS, subcarina, stump 89% had IMRT
Lung.results: ↗️ ITT 3-yr DFS 41% vs. 33%, p=0.20 PP 3-yr DFS 43% vs. 31% ITT median DFS 22.1 mos vs. 18.6 mos ⬇️ 3-yr LRR 10% vs. 18% ⬆️ 3-yr LRFS 67% vs. 60%, p=0.03 3-yr ITT OS ~80%, not different PP OS also not different In RT arm, 24% did not receive RT 3-yr DM ~40%, not different
Lung.interpretation: Post-op RT for N2 disease in NSCLC should not be routinely recommended. There was no OS benefit. There is some LC benefit, and no significant DFS benefit. There was 24% noncompliance in the RT arm. In the per protocol analysis, post-op RT for N2 disease led to benefit in DFS but not OS, but these results could be biased.
Lung.commentary: Increased deaths in LUNG-ART, which were not seen in PORT-C, might be accounted for by IMRT in only 11% and/or higher RT compliance in LUNG-ART that could have led to more exposure to heart, ENI, or blood pool; less may be more. LRR was improved in both trials. Perhaps more targeted radiation to only the involved stations with IMRT might lead to a better ratio of risk benefit. ENI often shows an OS detriment (Yuan 2007), and that finding seems to be reproduced here. 11% had IMRT in LUNG-ART, and 89% had IMRT in PORT-C. There was poor protocol adherence in the RT group: 24% did not receive any …
Lung.references: 1. Hui Z, Men Y, Hu C, et al. Effect of Postoperative Radiotherapy for Patients With pIIIA-N2 Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer After Complete Resection and Adjuvant Chemotherapy: The Phase 3 PORT-C Randomized Clinical Trial [published online ahead of print, 2021 Jun 24]. JAMA Oncol 2021;10.1001/jamaoncol.2021.1910. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2021.1910 Protocol pdf in supplements [Treatment planning p9]: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2021.1910

Lung.patients: NSCLC s/p complete resection, pN2 (41% were path N2 only, 34% single cN2, 25% multi cN2)
Lung.methods: Chemo neoadjuvant or adjuvant ➜ post-op 54 Gy/ 27-30 fx vs. ➜ 🏆 no RT RT given 2-6 weeks after chemo, or 4-8 weeks after surgery if neoadj chemo given CTV: volumes are complex. See atlas. 11% had IMRT
Lung.results: ↗️ 3-yr DFS 47% vs. 44% (p=0.18) ↗️ Median DFS 30.5 mos vs. 22.8 mos ⬇️ Mediastinal relapse 25% vs. 46% ⬆️ Deaths 15% vs. 5% ↔️ 3-yr OS 67-69%, not different ⬇️ Deaths from recurrence 69% vs. 85% ⬆️ Deaths from cardiopulm 16% vs. 2% 2nd cancer death 5% vs. 1% 2nd cancer 11% vs. 7% Other deaths 7% vs. 12% ⬆️ grade 3-4 cardiopulm 11% vs. 5%
Lung.interpretation: Post-op 3DCRT for N2 disease in NSCLC should not be routinely recommended. There was no benefit in DFS or OS. RT had less mediastinal recurrence and death from progression, but higher deaths from adverse effects.
Lung.commentary: Increased deaths in LUNG-ART, which were not seen in PORT-C, might be accounted for by IMRT in only 11% and/or higher RT compliance in LUNG-ART that could have led to more exposure to heart, ENI, or blood pool; less may be more. LRR was improved in both trials. Perhaps more targeted radiation to only the involved stations with IMRT might lead to a better ratio of risk benefit. ENI often shows an OS detriment (Yuan 2007), and that finding seems to be reproduced here. 11% had IMRT in LUNG-ART, and 89% had IMRT in PORT-C. There were no violations in dose constraints. Note the increased morbidity …
Lung.references: 1. Le Pechoux C, Pourel N, Barlesi F, et al. Postoperative radiotherapy versus no postoperative radiotherapy in patients with completely resected non-small-cell lung cancer and proven mediastinal N2 involvement (Lung ART): an open-label, randomised, phase 3 trial [published online ahead of print, 2021 Dec 14]. Lancet Oncol 2021;S1470-2045(21)00606-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(21)00606-9

Lung.patients: NSCLC Stage IIIA(N2) and select operable Stage IIIB
Lung.methods: Induction cisplatin/paclitaxel → 45 Gy RT (1.5 Gy BID) conc cisplatin/vinorelbine → if resectable → ➜ chemoRT 65-71 Gy vs. ➜ surgery
Lung.results: Terminated early. Slow accrual 65% became resectable and were randomized. Of those undergoing surgery, 81% R0 5-yr OS 44% vs. 40%, p=0.34 5-yr PFS 32% vs. 35%, p=0.75
Lung.interpretation: The trial was terminated early due to slow accrual and thus was not powered to meet its endpoints. Both approaches resulted in excellent outcomes.
Lung.references: 1. Eberhardt WEE, Pöttgen C, Gauler TC, et al. Phase III Study of Surgery Versus Definitive Concurrent Chemoradiotherapy Boost in Patients With Resectable Stage IIIA(N2) and Selected IIIB Non–Small-Cell Lung Cancer After Induction Chemotherapy and Concurrent Chemoradiotherapy (ESPATUE). J Clin Oncol 2015;33:4194-201. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.62.6812

Lung.methods: ➜ cis/docetaxel x3 followed by RT 44 Gy then surgery vs. ➜ same without RT
Lung.results: No change in survival Median EFS ~12 mos, NS Median OS 37.1 mos vs 26.2 mos (p value not given and not powered for OS) No differences on subgroup analysis and per protocol analysis
Lung.interpretation: The addition of RT to chemotherapy prior to surgery did not improve EFS compared to chemo alone.
Lung.commentary: There is much controversy regarding pre-operative therapy for lung cancer. Treatments should be individualized.
Lung.references: 1. Pless M, Stupp R, Ris H-B, et al. Induction chemoradiation in stage IIIA/N2 non-small-cell lung cancer: a phase 3 randomised trial. Lancet 2015;386:1049-56. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(15)60294-X

Lung.subtopic: neoadj CRT + Surgery vs. definitive RT
Lung.methods: 45 Gy with concurrent cis/etoposide → if no PD → ➜ surgery vs. ➜ RT to 61 Gy total (uninterrupted) then adjuvant x2 cis/etoposide in both arms
Lung.results: ⬆️ PFS 12.8 mos surgery vs. 10.5 mos ⬆️ 5-yr PFS 22% surg vs. 11% More tx-related deaths with surgery ⬇️ LR only 22% vs. 10% ⬇️ (Primary recurrence 14% vs. 2%) ↔️ Median OS ~23 mos both arms ↔️ 5-yr OS 27% vs. 20% (NS) In exploratory analysis, lobectomy patients had improved 5-yr OS 18% vs. 36%, but not pneumonectomy patients
Lung.interpretation: The addition of surgery to chemoRT in Stage IIIA NSCLC did not result in OS benefit overall. On exploratory analysis there is an overall survival benefit with radiation in those who had lobectomy.
Lung.commentary: The study was designed to show that surgery was superior, which it did not.
Lung.references: 1. Albain KS, Swann RS, Rusch VW, et al. Radiotherapy plus chemotherapy with or without surgical resection for stage III non-small-cell lung cancer: a phase III randomised controlled trial. Lancet 2009;374:379-86. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(09)60737-6

Lung.methods: Induction platinum-based chemo x3, then if response: ➜ radical resection and optional post-op RT vs. ➜ 60 Gy definitive RT
Lung.results: No difference in median OS, 16.4 mos vs. 17.5 mos In induction chemo->surgery arm, 5% pCR
Lung.interpretation: After response to induction chemo then surgery, surgery doesn't improve OS or PFS compared to chemoRT alone in Stage IIIA N2 NSCLC.
Lung.commentary: RT compliance to prescription 55%. 40% received PORT.
Lung.references: 1. van Meerbeeck JP, Research ObotEOf, Group ToC-LC, et al. Randomized Controlled Trial of Resection Versus Radiotherapy After Induction Chemotherapy in Stage IIIA-N2 Non–Small-Cell Lung Cancer. J Natl Cancer Inst 2007;99:442-50. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/jnci/djk093

Lung.patients: superior sulcus T3-4N0-1 1) apical tumor with Pancoast syndrome with or without inv chest wall or spine 2) superior or sulcus tumors with inv of chest wall, spine, or subclavian vessels
Lung.methods: ➜ 2 cycles of cis/etoposide + 45 Gy → If no progression then surgery in 3-5 weeks → cis/etoposide x2 Surgery was lobectomy/pneumonectomy
Lung.results: 80% had surgery 94% of these were R0 (75% of all) Of all: 55% pCR or minimal microscopic disease (56% in abstract seems to be in error) 5-yr OS 44% Median OS 33 mos No difference in T3 vs T4 If R0: 5-yr OS 54% Median OS 94 mos If pCR Median OS not reached crude DM 41% (calc from Table A5) Of those who had surgery: (calc from Table A4) 69% pCR or minimal microscopic disease 36% pCR 33% minimal microscopic disease 31% gross residual
Lung.interpretation: For superior sulcus tumors, chemoRT followed by surgery and adjuvant chemo is feasible and leads to excellent outcomes.
Lung.commentary: There appear to be some potential errors in the publication. Although the abstract reports 56% pCR/nearCR (apparently of all patients), this does not seem to be correct (61/110 = 55%). Furthermore it seems more rational to provide the pCR rate of those who actually underwent surgery, as is done in CROSS (Eyck 2021), which I have added here, calculated from Table A4. It is unclear from the text if the tumors were intended to be resectable upfront, or if chemoradiation was designed to render some borderline tumors to be potentially resectable. OS is more favorable than typical Stage III NSCLC. R…
Lung.references: 1. Rusch VW, Giroux DJ, Kraut MJ, Crowley J, Hazuka M, Winton T, Johnson DH, Shulman L, Shepherd F, Deschamps C, Livingston RB, Gandara D. Induction chemoradiation and surgical resection for superior sulcus non-small-cell lung carcinomas: long-term results of Southwest Oncology Group Trial 9416 (Intergroup Trial 0160). J Clin Oncol 2007 Jan 20;25:313-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2006.08.2826

Lung.methods: SBRT→ VATS lobectomy or sublobar resection after 10 weeks (SBRT doses: 54 Gy/3 fx if peripheral, 55 Gy/5 fx for chest wall contact or size >3 cm, 60 Gy/8 fx if within 2 cm of MS or brachial plexus)
Lung.results: Of those who had surgery: pCR 60%, major pCR 63% 5-yr LC 94% 5-yr RC 80% 5-yr DM 18% 2-yr OS 77%, 5-yr OS 67% No 30- or 90-day post op deaths Grade 3-5 adverse effects in 17%
Lung.interpretation: With SBRT followed by VATS resection, the pCR rate was lower than expected, but higher than in many pre-op chemoimmunotherapy trials. adverse effects and post op deaths were similar to surgical series. Randomized trials are warranted to detect if pre-op SBRT results in any survival or DM benefit.
Lung.commentary: The pCR rate is lower than expected compared to LC seen in SBRT studies, but markedly higher than that in pre-op chemoimmunotherapy trials. On Twitter David Palma (2019) comments that a pCR of 60% may not represent active tumor in all cases; tumor can be alive but not mitotically active to cause LF later, undergoing a slow senescent death. Ten weeks may be early to assess response surgically. This time point was chosen for surgical purposes to avoid fibrosis during resection. Yet in view of other pre-op therapy studies, 60% pCR actually is an achievement, and is among the highest of any trial …
Lung.references: 1. Palma DA, Nguyen TK, Louie AV, et al. Measuring the Integration of Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy Plus Surgery for Early-Stage Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Phase 2 Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2019;5:681-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2018.6993
Lung.commentary_refs: Palma DA. @drdavidpalma. Twitter. March 1, 2019. Available from: https://twitter.com/drdavidpalma/status/1101603630921248770. Accessed June 23, 2019.

Lung.methods: ➜ 🏆 SBRT 48 Gy/4 fx (or 60 Gy/8 fx for central) vs. ➜ 60 Gy/ 15 fx
Lung.results: ✅ 3-yr LC 88% vs. 82%, p=0.15 No change in DFS or OS grade 3-4 adverse effects in central tumors in 11% vs. 5%, in peripheral tumors in 2% vs. 2% In the SBRT arm 4/23 (17%) had grade ≥3 adverse effects With 60 Gy in 8 fractions, one pt died of fatal hemoptysis
Lung.interpretation: SBRT is a safe and efficacious treatment option in NSCLC. SBRT shows a nonsignificant improvememt in LC compared to hypofractionated RT.
Lung.commentary: On Twitter Joseph Deasy comments that the Kaplan Meyer HR is 0.61, but p is only <0.15, illustrating that this trial is not well powered. 60 Gy in 15 fx was used in the control arm, a regimen that not everyone finds to be standard of care. An alternative way of interpreting the LUSTRE trial is to view the SBRT arm as the control arm, and hypofx as the experimental arm. (See commentary on Iyengar 2021, conventional RT vs. hypofx: the study was not powered to show noninferiority, hypofx was not superior, esophagitis was worse, and outcomes tended to favor conventional.) 60 Gy in 8 fractions was …
Lung.references: 1. Swaminath A, Parpia S, Wierzbicki M, et al. Stereotactic vs Hypofractionated Radiotherapy for Inoperable Stage I Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: The LUSTRE Phase 3 Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2024. Published online September 19, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2024.3089 Study protocol https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11413752/bin/jamaoncol-e243089-s001.pdf
Lung.commentary_refs: 1. Deasy, J. @joe_deasy. Twitter. October 26, 2022. Available at: https://twitter.com/joe_deasy/status/1585257813731069953?s=20&t=s3piZbshD_nzMBgNbkA0uw

Lung.methods: ➜ 🏆 SBRT 54 Gy/3 fx (or 48 Gy/4 fx if <2 cm from chest wall) vs. ➜ 3DCRT to 66 Gy, or 50 Gy/20 fx 2:1 randomization
Lung.results: SBRT had improved LC and OS crude FFLF 14% vs. 31% ⬆️ 2-yr LC 89% vs. 65% ⬆️ Median OS 5 yrs vs. 3 yrs ⬆️ 2-yr OS 77% vs. 59% CSS favored SBRT (HR 0.49, but p=0.092) crude DM 16% vs. 12% (p not provided) adverse effects: SBRT with grade 4 in n=1 and grade 3 in 7. EBRT with grade 3 in 2
Lung.interpretation: SBRT is a safe and efficacious treatment option in NSCLC. SBRT improved LC and OS compared to conventional RT. Similar adverse effects.
Lung.commentary: LF can be challenging to classify in SBRT because of fibrosis. RECIST criteria were first used in the study, but when the Delphi guidelines were released they were implemented. LF was the primary endpoint, but improved OS and CSS (p=0.092) with SBRT is arguably more intriguing. At n=101, the trial seems underpowered. DM are reported in table 2 but not emphasized. The 50 Gy/20 fractionation has lower BED than 66 Gy. But on subanalysis there is no difference between 66 Gy and 50 Gy EBRT. Rx was to PTV. Compare to the SPACE study (Nyman 2016).
Lung.references: 1. Ball D, Mai GT, Vinod S, et al. Stereotactic ablative radiotherapy versus standard radiotherapy in stage 1 non-small-cell lung cancer (TROG 09.02 CHISEL): a phase 3, open-label, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Oncol 2019;20:494-503. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(18)30896-9

Lung.methods: Phase II: ➜ SBRT 66 Gy/3 fx to isocenter [=45 Gy/3 fx to PTV] vs. ➜ 70 Gy/35 fx
Lung.results: ✅LC and OS not different 3-yr PFS 42% in both arms Crude progression in 70% of SBRT vs. 59% 3DCRT (p=0.26) Any pneumonitis 19% vs. 34% Any esophagitis 8% vs. 30% On HRQL, 3DCRT had worse dyspnea, chest pain, and cough
Lung.interpretation: SBRT has less adverse effects than conventional radiation. SBRT has a trend toward better control. There is no change in PFS or OS.SBRT is a safe and efficacious treatment option in NSCLC.
Lung.commentary: Initially designed as a Phase III trial but then amended to Phase II due to low accrual. Although CHISEL (Ball 2019) had similar patient numbers, SPACE was not fully balanced. Some SCLC patients were included. The SBRT arm included more T2 tumors and had better performance status. Dose was rx to isocenter, therefore 45 Gy/3 fx to PTV was given.
Lung.references: 1. Nyman J, Hallqvist A, Lund J-Å, et al. SPACE – A randomized study of SBRT vs conventional fractionated radiotherapy in medically inoperable stage I NSCLC. Radiother Oncol 2016;121:1-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2016.08.015

Lung.citation: Palma et al, JAMA Oncol, 2019 Tan et al, IJROBP, 2024
Lung.methods: SBRT→ VATS lobectomy or sublobar resection after 10 weeks (SBRT doses: 54 Gy/3 fx if peripheral, 55 Gy/5 fx for chest wall contact or size >3 cm, 60 Gy/8 fx if within 2 cm of MS or brachial plexus)
Lung.results: Of those who had surgery: pCR 60%, major pCR 63% 5-yr LC 94% 5-yr RC 80% 5-yr DM 18% 2-yr OS 77%, 5-yr OS 67% No 30- or 90-day post op deaths Grade 3-5 adverse effects in 17%
Lung.interpretation: With SBRT followed by VATS resection, the pCR rate was lower than expected, but higher than in many pre-op chemoimmunotherapy trials. adverse effects and post op deaths were similar to surgical series. Randomized trials are warranted to detect if pre-op SBRT results in any survival or DM benefit.
Lung.commentary: The pCR rate is lower than expected compared to LC seen in SBRT studies, but markedly higher than that in pre-op chemoimmunotherapy trials. On Twitter David Palma (2019) comments that a pCR of 60% may not represent active tumor in all cases; tumor can be alive but not mitotically active to cause LF later, undergoing a slow senescent death. Ten weeks may be early to assess response surgically. This time point was chosen for surgical purposes to avoid fibrosis during resection. Yet in view of other pre-op therapy studies, 60% pCR actually is an achievement, and is among the highest of any trial …
Lung.references: 1. Palma DA, Nguyen TK, Louie AV, et al. Measuring the Integration of Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy Plus Surgery for Early-Stage Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Phase 2 Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2019;5:681-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2018.6993 Protocol pdf in supplements: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2018.6993 2. Tan VA, Correa RJM, Nguyen T, et al. Measuring the Integration of Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy plus Surgery for Early-Stage Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (MISSILE): Long-Term Clinical Outcomes. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2024;120:s104-105.
Lung.commentary_refs: Palma DA. @drdavidpalma. Twitter. March 1, 2019. Available from: https://twitter.com/drdavidpalma/status/1101603630921248770. Accessed June 23, 2019.

Lung.methods: Phase II: ➜ 🏆SBRT 24 Gy/ 3 fx + durvalumab x2 vs. ➜ durvalumab then surgery Primary endpoint: major pathologic response
Lung.results: major path response 53% vs. 7% (p<0.0001) pCR 27% vs. 0 Median time to surgery 5.5 weeks
Lung.interpretation: Neoadjuvant durvalumab prior to surgery results in reasonable pathologic response rates, but not as high as in the MISSILE trial.
Lung.commentary: "Major" pathologic response was defined as ≤10% viable tumor cells. The authors chose this endpoint because it was found to be correlated to OS. However one should be cautious with surrogate endpoints: correlation is not causation, and the total effect of one intervention may be very different from another, even if path response is the same (consider effect of adverse effects on OS, DM, local vs. systemic vs. abscopal effects, etc). The authors chose 24 Gy/3 fx as the dose for the intention of stimulating immune response, not for the purpose of local control; the BED is just 43 Gy. The time to…
Lung.references: 1. Altorki NK, McGraw TE, Borczuk AC, et al. Neoadjuvant durvalumab with or without stereotactic body radiotherapy in patients with early-stage non-small-cell lung cancer: a single-centre, randomised phase 2 trial [published online ahead of print, 2021 May 17]. Lancet Oncol. 2021;S1470-2045(21)00149-2. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(21)00149-2

Lung.citation: Timmerman et al, JAMA, 2010 Timmerman et al, JAMA Oncol, 2018
Lung.patients: NSCLC T1-T2, medically inoperable, peripheral lesions
Lung.methods: Phase II: SBRT to 60 Gy/3 fx (closer to 54Gy/3 fx using heterogeneity corrections) rx to periphery
Lung.results: 3/5-yr tumor + lobe control 91%/80% 3/5-yr primary control 98%/93% 3/5-yr LRC 87%/74% 5-yr RC 89% 3-yr OS 56%, 5-yr 40% 3/5-yr DM 22%/24% (5-yr DM 18% in T1, 46% in T2) DM in squamous 6%, nonsquamous 32% 5-yr DFS 26% Median DFS 3.0 yrs, OS 4.0 yrs 27% grade 3 adverse effects and 4% grade 4
Lung.interpretation: SBRT for peripheral lung lesions is feasible and results in high LC and LRC. Additional recurrences can occur with longer follow-up.
Lung.commentary: Note the high DM rates in T2 and nonsquamous tumors. 5-yr DFS is only 26%. Dose rx to isodose line such that PTV is covered by 95% of 60Gy and 99% receives 54Gy (not using heterogeneity corrections)
Lung.references: 1. Timmerman R, Paulus R, Galvin J, et al. Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy for Inoperable Early Stage Lung Cancer. JAMA 2010;303:1070-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2010.261 2. Timmerman RD, Hu C, Michalski JM, et al. Long-term Results of Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy in Medically Inoperable Stage I Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer. JAMA Oncol 2018;4:1287–1288. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2018.1258

Nagata Y, Hiraoka M, Shibata T, et al. Prospective Trial of Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy for Both Operable and Inoperable T1N0M0 Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: Japan Clinical Oncology Group Study JCOG0403. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2015;93:989-996. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2015.07.2278

Lung.subtopic: SBRT dose for peripheral tumors, operable
Lung.patients: Operable biopsy proven T1-T3 N0M0, peripheral lesions
Lung.methods: Phase II: 54 Gy/3 fx in 1.5-2 weeks
Lung.results: 4-yr primary tumor control and LC 96% 4-yr DFS 57% and OS 56% Median OS 55.2 mos grade 3, 4, and 5 adverse effects in 8%, 0%, 0%
Lung.interpretation: SBRT has high tumor control and low adverse effects.
Lung.commentary: Dose rx to an isodose line so that PTV coverage is 95%
Lung.references: 1. Timmerman RD, Paulus R, Pass HI, et al. Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy for Operable Early-Stage Lung Cancer: Findings From the NRG Oncology RTOG 0618 Trial. JAMA Oncol 2018;4:1263-1266. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2018.1251

Lung.citation: Videtic et al, IJROBP, 2015 Videtic et al, IJROBP, 2018
Lung.patients: medically inoperable T1-T2N0M0 NSCLC, peripheral lesions
Lung.methods: Phase II randomized: ➜ 34 Gy/1 fx vs. ➜ 48 Gy/4 fx, daily fx Primary endpoint: grade ≥3 adverse events <17% and LC >90% at 1 year
Lung.results: 5-yr report: Grade ≥3 adverse effects 2.6% with 34 Gy and 11% with 48 Gy 1-yr primary control 97% and 93% 5-yr primary control 89% and 93% 2-yr OS 61% and 78% 5-yr OS 30% and 41% Median OS 4.1 yrs and 4.6 yrs 1/3 causes of death unknown, 1/3 unrelated to cancer, 1/3 deaths cancer related 2-yr PFS 56% and 71% 5-yr PFS 19% and 33% 5-yr first failure DM 19% and 41% 5-yr 2nd primary 16% and 13%
Lung.interpretation: Both fractionations met the prespecified endpoints and warrant further testing.
Lung.commentary: Not powered to compare LF, PFS, or DM (phase II). Shankar Siva and David Ball (2019) comment on why NRG have not developed future trials building on 0915. They infer that the reason is because outcomes with various schedules are already excellent and would require large power to detect a difference anyway. There is an apparent discrepancy in adverse effects numbers between the initial and 5 year report. This is because the authors found that some adverse effects had been miscategorized (Videtic GM, email communication, Jan 2019). Regardless, in both reports the prespecified endpoint of <17% gr…
Lung.references: 1. Videtic GM, Hu C, Singh AK, et al. A Randomized Phase 2 Study Comparing 2 Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy Schedules for Medically Inoperable Patients With Stage I Peripheral Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: NRG Oncology RTOG 0915 (NCCTG N0927). Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2015;93:757-64. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2015.07.2260 Dose constraints: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4744654/bin/NIHMS742283-supplement-2.docx 2. Videtic GM, Paulus R, Singh AK, et al. Long-term Follow-up on NRG Oncology RTOG 0915 (NCCTG N0927): A Randomized Phase 2 Study Comparing 2 Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy Schedules for Medically Inoperable Patients With Stage I Peripheral Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2019;103:1077-84. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2018.11.051
Lung.commentary_refs: Siva S, Ball DL. Single Fraction SBRT for Early Stage Lung Cancer: Less is More? Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2019;103:1085-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2018.12.041

Lung.patients: medically inoperable T1-T2N0M0 NSCLC, peripheral lesions
Lung.methods: Phase II randomized: ➜ 30 Gy/1 fx vs. ➜ 60 Gy/3 fx Endpoint: grade ≥3 adverse effects
Lung.results: Median f/u 54 mos Grade 3 adverse effects 16% vs. 12% No grade 4-5 adverse effects (median f/u 54 mos) No difference in LC, PFS, or OS
Lung.interpretation: Single fraction lung SBRT is equivalent to 3 fractions for peripheral lesions.
Lung.commentary: Dose constraints were per RTOG 0915 for single fraction and RTOG 0236 for three fractions.
Lung.references: 1. Singh AK, Gomez-Suescun JA, Stephens KL, et al. One versus three fractions of stereotactic body radiation therapy for peripheral stage I-II non-small cell lung cancer: a randomized, multi-institution, phase 2 trial. doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2019.08.019

Lung.patients: centrally located tumors, T1-T2 <5 cm, medically inoperable
Lung.methods: Dose escalation: SBRT 10, 10.5, 11, 11.5, 12 Gy x 5 fx Endpoint: 1-yr grade ≥3 adverse effects <20%
Lung.results: 12 Gy was MTD with 1-yr grade 3+ adverse effects of 7.2% Within one year, total 9% deaths (!): 11.5 Gy had 3/25 die (esophageal ulcer that eroded into vessel and 2 bronchopulmonary hemorrhages). 12 Gy had 1/21 die (bronchopulmonary hemorrhage) 2-yr LC 89%, OS 68%, PFS 52%, DM 15%
Lung.interpretation: The highest dose level allowed, 12 Gy x 5 fx, was tolerated as defined by the predetermined endpoint of grade 3+ adverse effects <20% at short f/u of 1 year. However after 1 year there were deaths with 11.5 and 12 Gy due to hemorrhage and ulceration. The trial was not powered to compare local control. [Editorialization: Consider anticoagulants a contraindication for higher doses, and only treating peripheral lesions with higher dose.]
Lung.commentary: The interpretation here is an editorialization and differs from the original conclusion. Follow-up of one year is too short (such a f/u length would never be accepted in a hypofractionation or omission of RT trial). In the HILUS trial, hemorrhage continued to occur until 2 years later. The primary endpoint was grade ≥3 adverse effects at 1 year. Even at 1-yr f/u, many will find the risk of grade 5 adverse effects unacceptable. The authors replied that more tumors in the higher dose levels were located adjacent to bronchi (Bezjak 2019). The HILUS trial, a phase II evaluting 56 Gy/ 8 fx to ultra…
Lung.references: 1. Bezjak A, Paulus R, Gaspar LE, et al. Safety and Efficacy of a Five-Fraction Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy Schedule for Centrally Located Non–Small-Cell Lung Cancer: NRG Oncology/RTOG 0813 Trial. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1316-25. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.00622 Protocol pdf [Dose constraints p11]: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.18.00622/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.18.00622.pdf#page=11
Lung.commentary_refs: Bezjak A, Paulus R, Gaspar LE, Timmerman RD, Bradley JD. Reply to T. Sio et al. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:2699-2700. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.19.01663

Lung.citation: Lindberg et. al, J Thorac Oncol, 2021
Lung.patients: ultracentral tumors ≤1 cm from proximal bronchial tree (median distance in the study was 0 mm)
Lung.methods: Stratified by Group A (tumors ≤ 1 cm from mainstem bronchus or carina) and Group B (all others) 7 Gy x 8 prescribed to the 67% IDL encompassing PTV, ≤ 4 fx/week. 95% Rx to 95% of PTV
Lung.results: Median f/u 25 months Grade 3+ adverse effects of 34% (22/65 patients) Grade 5 adverse effects of 15% (10/65 patients)! Group A: 8/39 died (7 bronchopulmonary hemorrhage, 1 pneumonitis) Group B: 2/26 died (1 esophageal fistula, 1 bronchopulmonary hemorrhage) Median time to fatal bronchopulmonary hemorrhage 15 mos (2-22 mos) All those with fatal hemorrhage had EQD2 Dmax to mainstem bronchus/trachea > 100 Gy 2-yr LC 83%, OS 58%
Lung.interpretation: 7 Gy × 8, prescribed to the 67% isodose encompassing PTV implicates high risk of high-grade adverse effects, and should not be used for tumors located within 1 cm from the main bronchi and trachea
Lung.commentary: Endobronchial involvement was not assessed at baseline, but is associated with a ~20% risk of grade 5 adverse effects with SBRT Rx to 67% IDL allows for a 150% hot spot within the target, no 4DCT contouring of PBT means that central structures may have received higher doses than planning would suggest
Lung.references: 1. Lindberg K, Grozman V, Karlsson K, et al. The HILUS-Trial-a Prospective Nordic Multicenter Phase 2 Study of Ultracentral Lung Tumors Treated With Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy. J Thorac Oncol 2021;16:1200-1210. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtho.2021.03.019

Lung.patients: ultracentral tumors, T1-T3, ≤6 cm PTV expected to touch or overlaps the central bronchial tree, esophagus, pulmonary vein, or pulmonary artery
Lung.methods: Phase I SBRT 60 Gy/ 8 fractions (de-escalation if excess toxicity was noted) Primary endpoint: MTD, defined as ≤30% 2-yr grade 3-5 adverse effects
Lung.results: 7% had grade 3-5 toxity grade 3 dyspnea n=1 grade 5 pneumonia n=1 3-yr LC 90% 3-yr RC 96% 3-yr DM 14%
Lung.interpretation: 60 Gy/ 8 fractions shows favorable adverse effects and local control for ultracentral lung tumors.
Lung.commentary: Only one patient was T3. Six were T2. The original study was dose escalation or de-escalation, with 60 Gy/8 fractions at start, but then modified to de-escaltion only when escalation was determined not to be safe. 4DCT and ITV generation used in planning PTV D95% = 100% PTV D99% ≥ 90% Rx IDL within 60-90% of normalization dose Hotspot within ITV ≤ 120% Rx Esophagus/stomach/bowel max <45 Gy, 5cc <40 Gy Proximal bronchus/trachea/heart/GV max <64 Gy, 10cc <60 Gy Lungs minus GTV mean <12 Gy Spinal canal max <32 Gy, PRV 3mm max <34 Gy Brachial plexus max <39 Gy
Lung.references: 1. Meredith E, Filion E, Faria S, et al. Stereotactic Radiation for Ultra-Central Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Safety and Efficacy Trial (SUNSET). Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2024. [Published online ahead of print.] DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2024.03.050 Study protocol [radiation planning p19]: https://www.redjournal.org/cms/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2024.03.050/attachment/46e9273b-a137-4b4e-8209-52e03690c656/mmc2.docx

Lung.methods: 1) Prospective SBRT cohort with propensity analysis comparison to 2) VATS database 54 Gy/3 fx SBRT for peripheral 50 Gy/4 fx SBRT for central with SIB to 60 Gy
Lung.results: SBRT cohort vs. VATS from database 3-yr OS 91% vs. 91% 5-yr OS 87% vs. 84%, not different 5-yr LR 6% vs. 1%, p=0.10 5-yr DM 9% vs. 4%, p=0.19 5-yr RR 13% vs. 3%, p=0.017 5-yr any recurrence 18% vs. 8%, p=0.051 3-yr PFS 80% vs. 88% 5-yr PFS 77% vs. 80%, not different 3-yr CSS 95% vs. 97% 5-yr CSS 92% vs. 93%, not different no grade 4-5 adverse effects with SBRT
Lung.interpretation: In this propensity matched analysis, SBRT was noninferior to VATS in OS, but LR, DM, and RR trended to favor surgery. The analysis is likely underpowered. Randomized trials are ongoing. SBRT is a reasonable alternative to surgery.
Lung.commentary: This is a propensity matched analysis, which cannot replace a randomized trial. A strength of the study is that it was guided by protocol, and those who received SBRT would have had consistent workup and treatment. The revised STARS trial seems underpowered at 80 patients. Recurrence rates seem to trend higher for SBRT. The VALOR randomized trial is ongoing, where planned n=670 (NCT02984761). In the VATS group, 60% had EBUS, and in the SBRT group, 90% had EBUS. In the VATS group there was a rate of 10% occult nodal metastasis on LND, followed by less eventual regional recurrences after VATS tr…
Lung.references: 1. Chang JY, Mehran RJ, Feng L, et al. Stereotactic ablative radiotherapy for operable stage I non-small-cell lung cancer (revised STARS): long-term results of a single-arm, prospective trial with prespecified comparison to surgery. Lancet Oncol 2021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(21)00401-0

Lung.methods: Combined analysis two studies, STARS and ROSEL, that were terminated due to slow accrual ➜ SBRT vs. ➜ lobectomy with MS LND or sampling STARS dose: Peripheral 54 Gy/ 3 fx, central 50 Gy/ 4 fx ROSEL dose: 54 Gy/ 3, or 60 Gy/ 5 fx
Lung.results: 3-yr OS 95% SABR vs. 79% (one died SABR vs. six in surgery, p=0.037) 3-yr RFS 86% SABR vs. 80%, p=.54 -(Surgery: one nodal recurrence, two DM) -(SBRT: one LR, four nodal recurrences, one DM) •Surgery complications (note few had VATS) grade 5 in n=1 grade 3-4 in 44% •SBRT adverse effects -no grade 4-5 adverse effects -grade 3 in 10% (chest pain, fatigue, dyspnea, cough, rib fracture)
Lung.interpretation: SBRT seems to yield favorable results in operable NSCLC. The analysis is limited by small numbers of patients, now outdated surgical techniques, and 3 year f/u.
Lung.commentary: Arguably this combined analysis of early terminated trials, at 58 patients, is also underpowered, but there is a possibility of unknown interactions. Few had VATS; VATS would be expected to further improve outcomes in the surgery arm. (In the surgery arm there were 5 VATS lobectomy, 19 lobectomy, 1 VATS biopsy, 1 open wedge, 1 aborted). None had segmental resection. Post-op death with VATS is low but can occur. Compare the results here to MISSILE, in which 100% had VATS, yielding 0% post-op mortality and low adverse effects. In CALGB/Alliance 140503 (2023) comparing lobectomy vs. sublobar rese…
Lung.references: 1. Chang JY, Senan S, Paul MA, et al. Stereotactic ablative radiotherapy versus lobectomy for operable stage I non-small-cell lung cancer: a pooled analysis of two randomised trials. Lancet Oncol 2015;16:630-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(15)70168-3

Lung.citation: VA Office of Research and Development, ongoing protocol
Lung.methods: ➜ SBRT vs. ➜ anatomic resection (lobectomy or segmentectomy) Peripheral tumors: 54 Gy/ 3 fx, 48 Gy/ 4 fx, or 57.5 Gy/ 5 fx Central: 50 Gy/ 5 fx
Lung.results: Ongoing study Primary endpoint: OS Secondary endpoints: QOL, CSS, LC, regional control, DM, FEV1
Lung.commentary: Note that size ≤5 cm is larger than the eligibility criteria in CALGB/Alliance 140503 and JCOG0802, the trials that showed noninferiority with sublobar resection in tumors ≤2 cm. Wedge is not permitted in VALOR.
Lung.references: 1. VA Office of Research and Development. Veterans Affairs Lung Cancer Surgery Or Stereotactic Radiotherapy (VALOR). Available from: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT02984761. NLM identifier: NCT02984761. Accessed June 19, 2019.

Lung.trial: London Health Sciences Centre, London, Ontario, Canada
Lung.methods: Meta-analysis of 16 propensity matched studies comparing SABR and surgery
Lung.results: OS favored surgery CSS was not significantly different Surgery upstaged 16% to LN+
Lung.interpretation: There was better OS with surgery compared to RT in this large database retrospective analysis. CSS was not different.
Lung.commentary: Although the patients in the meta-analysis are propensity matched, it is impossible to account for all biases without a randomized trial.
Lung.references: 1. Chen H, Laba JM, Boldt RG, et al. Stereotactic Ablative Radiation Therapy Versus Surgery in Early Lung Cancer: A Meta-analysis of Propensity Score Studies. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2018;101:186-94. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2018.01.064

Lung.methods: NCDB analysis of wedge resection vs. SBRT short term mortality. Years 2004-2013. Propensity score matched.
Lung.results: Worse short term mortality with surgery 30-day mortality 2.41% vs. 0.79% 90-day mortality 4.23% vs. 2.82% Greater difference with higher age Mortality worse with larger surgery
Lung.interpretation: There can be significant short term mortality with surgery, especially with increasing age. Results may be improved with VATS. Randomized trials are required for confirmation as adjustments for imbalances in database studies cannot remove all confounding variables.
Lung.references: 1. Stokes WA, Bronsert MR, Meguid RA, et al. Post-Treatment Mortality After Surgery and Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy for Early-Stage Non–Small-Cell Lung Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2018;36:642-51. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2017.75.6536

Lung.methods: Meta-analysis of 40 SBRT studies and 23 surgery studies from 2000-2012.
Lung.results: After adjustment for operability and age, SBRT, lobar resection, and limited lobar resection had similar OS and DFS
Lung.interpretation: After adjustment for operability and age, SBRT and surgery had similar OS and DFS in the large database retrospective analysis.
Lung.references: 1. Zheng X, Schipper M, Kidwell K, et al. Survival Outcome After Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy and Surgery for Stage I Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Meta-Analysis. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2014;90:603-11. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2014.05.055

Lung.methods: Phase II: ➜ SBRT 24 Gy/3 fx --> pembrolizumab vs. ➜ pembrolizumab Goal endpoint: 3-mo ORR of 50% vs. 20%, p<0.10
Lung.results: 3-mo ORR 36% vs. 18%, p=0.07 median PFS 6.6 mos vs. 1.9 mos, p=0.19 median OS 15.9 vs. 7.6 mos, p=0.16 subanalysis favored SBRT in PDL1 negative
Lung.interpretation: Outcomes did not meet the predetermined thresholds, however additional studies are warranted of SBRT in PDL1 negative tumors to activate immune response.
Lung.commentary: Although SBRT did not meet the predetermimed threshholds, results still seem to favor SBRT. ORR of 36% with SBRT seems low compared to LC reported in clinical trials, such as 5-yr LC of 93% in RTOG 0915 (Videtic 2018).
Lung.references: 1. Theelan WSNE, Peulen HMU, Lalezari F, et al. Effect of Pembrolizumab After Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy vs Pembrolizumab Alone on Tumor Response in Patients With Advanced Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer: Results of the PEMBRO-RT Phase 2 Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2019. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2019.1478

Lung.citation: Wu et al, NEJM, 2020 Herbst et al, JCO, 2023 Tsuboi, et al, NEJM, 2023
Lung.patients: NSCLC EGFR+ s/p complete resection CT head or MRI brain allowed. PET not required.
Lung.methods: NSCLC EGFR+ s/p complete resection ➜ 🏆 osimertinib x3 years vs. ➜ placebo x3 years (23% crossed-over to osimertinib) post-op RT not permitted
Lung.results: Stage II-IIIA 5-yr OS 85% vs. 73% overall 5-yr OS 88% vs. 78% Median OS not reached in either arm Stage II-IIIA 4-yr DFS 70% vs. 29% overall 4-yr DFS 73% vs. 38% Stage II-IIIA 4-yr CNS recurrence 8% vs. 14% overall 4-yr CNS recurrence 6% vs. 11% Looking at Figs 1 & 3, it seems that DFS eventually begins to merge at about 5-6 years f/u
Lung.interpretation: Osimertinib improves OS, DFS and reduces CNS relapse in completely resected EGFR+ NSCLC vs. control with minimal crossover.
Lung.commentary: Osimertinib is tumorostatic rather than tumorocidal. In a pre-op study, there were zero pCRs with osimertinib, and major path responses were 15% (Blakely 2024). Still osimertinib is a landmark discovery in personalized medicine, a drug that greatly extends outcomes with minimal adverse effects. There is controversy regarding whether all need the drug in the adjuvant setting, for what duration and the design of the control arm. Role of radiation. The findings of later disease failure and lack of pCR may lend for a tandem role of radiation alongside osimertinib for recurrence, especially for bra…
Lung.references: 1. Wu Y-L, Tsuboi M, He J, et al. Osimertinib in Resected EGFR-Mutated Non–Small-Cell Lung Cancer. N Eng J Med 2020. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2027071 Protocol pdf: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.22.02186/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.22.02186.pdf 2. Herbst RS, Wu YL, John T, et al. Adjuvant Osimertinib for Resected EGFR-Mutated Stage IB-IIIA Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer: Updated Results From the Phase III Randomized ADAURA Trial. J Clin Oncol 2023;JCO2202186. Lung.citation: Wu et al, NEJM, 2020 Herbst et al, JCO, 2023 Tsuboi, et al, NEJM, 2023
Lung.patients: NSCLC EGFR+ s/p complete resection CT head or MRI brain allowed. PET not required.
Lung.methods: NSCLC EGFR+ s/p complete resection ➜ 🏆 osimertinib x3 years vs. ➜ placebo x3 years (23% crossed-over to osimertinib) post-op RT not permitted
Lung.results: Stage II-IIIA 5-yr OS 85% vs. 73% overall 5-yr OS 88% vs. 78% Median OS not reached in either arm Stage II-IIIA 4-yr DFS 70% vs. 29% overall 4-yr DFS 73% vs. 38% Stage II-IIIA 4-yr CNS recurrence 8% vs. 14% overall 4-yr CNS recurrence 6% vs. 11% Looking at Figs 1 & 3, it seems that DFS eventually begins to merge at about 5-6 years f/u
Lung.interpretation: Osimertinib improves OS, DFS and reduces CNS relapse in completely resected EGFR+ NSCLC vs. control with minimal crossover.
Lung.commentary: Osimertinib is tumorostatic rather than tumorocidal. In a pre-op study, there were zero pCRs with osimertinib, and major path responses were 15% (Blakely 2024). Still osimertinib is a landmark discovery in personalized medicine, a drug that greatly extends outcomes with minimal adverse effects. There is controversy regarding whether all need the drug in the adjuvant setting, for what duration and the design of the control arm. Role of radiation. The findings of later disease failure and lack of pCR may lend for a tandem role of radiation alongside osimertinib for recurrence, especially for bra…

Lung.methods: Crizotinib 250 mg twice daily
Lung.results: ORR 72%, 3 CR and 33 PR Median response duration 17.6 mos Median PFS 19.2 mos

Lung.patients: NSCLC EGFR+, 1-3 oligo-organs involved (thus multiple lesions permitted: ~1/3 had 1-5, 1/3 had 6-10, and 1/3 had ≥10 mets) ≤2 cycles chemotherapy given Brain metastasis were allowed
Lung.methods: ≤2 cycles chemotherapy given→ 1-3 oligo-organs involved Phase III ➜ 🏆 60 Gy to primary and nodes plus icotinib concurrently, with RT to oligo sites per discretion and maintenance TKI vs. ➜ icotinib Brain metastases: RT recommended for symptomatic and optional for asymptomatic TKI in both arms was continued indefinitely until progression or adverse effects
Lung.results: Median OS 34.4 vs. 26.2 mos 3-yr OS 45% vs. 28% Median PFS 17.1 vs. 10.6 mos grade 3-4 toxcity 12% vs. 5%
Lung.interpretation: Upfront radiation therapy added to icotinib improves OS in metastatic disease involving ≤3 organs in EGFR+ NSCLC.
Lung.commentary: The trial evaluated those with oligo organ metastasis. In other words, >5 lesions were permitted if contained within 1-3 organs. The trial results can also apply to those with multiple metastases. TKIs are tumorostatic rather than tumorocidal. In a pre-op study, there were zero pCRs with osimertinib, and major path responses were 15% (Blakely 2024). Icotinib is a first generation TKI, and the only one permitted in the trial. Concurrent TKI was given with radiation, not concurrent chemotherapy. Palliative radiation was allowed in both arms.
Lung.references: 1. Sun H, Li M, Huang W, et al. Thoracic Radiotherapy Improves the Survival in Patients With EGFR-Mutated Oligo-Organ Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer Treated With Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor-Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors: A Multicenter, Randomized, Controlled, Phase III Trial. J Clin Oncol. Published online October 7, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.23.02075 Study protocol: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.23.02075/suppl_file/protocol_jco.23.02075.pdf#page=9

Lung.patients: 1-5 oligoprogressive lesions Exclusion: Oligoprogression only in brain mets. SRS for brain was allowed in either arm
Lung.methods: →chemo→1-5 oligoprogressive lesions→ Phase II ➜ SBRT to oligoprogressive sites vs. ➜ no SBRT Subablative doses were recommended: 8 Gy/1 fx, 20 Gy/5, or 30 Gy/10
Lung.results: Median PFS 8.4 vs. 4.3 mos, nonsignficant 2-yr PFS 9% vs. 24%, p=0.91 Median OS 31.2 vs. 27.4 mos, p=0.22 Lesion control 70% vs. 38% 35% protocol violations in SBRT arm due to either higher dose RT or no radiation Lung cancers were 44%, GU 23%, breast 13%
Lung.interpretation: Subablative SBRT for oligoprogessive disease improved local control of the treated lesions, but not PFS or OS.
Lung.commentary: Review and future directions. Why was this trial negative when others were positive? The answer could be due to choice of endpoints, sequencing, radiation doses, or that there is no benefit. A strength of LU002 and STOP are their multi institutional reach. Neither LU002 or STOP used ablative doses. The SBRT doses used in LU002 and STOP were BEDs <100. The IAEA study used similar doses as LU002. IAEA was a positive study, although phase II and used different sequencing. The 34 Gy/5 fractions permitted in LU002 is a departure from the 34 Gy/1 fraction determined safe in RTOG 0915. The NSCLC olig…
Lung.references: 1. Schellenberg D, Gabos Z, Duimering A, et al. Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy for Oligoprogressive Cancers: Results of the Randomized Phase II STOP Trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys. Published online August 19, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2024.08.031

Lung.patients: 1-5 oligoprogressive lesions after at least one type of chemo NSCLC or breast (1/3 of breast were triple negative)
Lung.methods: →chemo→1-5 oligoprogressive lesions→ Phase II ➜ 🏆 SBRT to oligoprogressive sites vs. ➜ no SBRT Most received 27-30 Gy/3 fractions or 30-50 Gy/5 fractions
Lung.results: ⬆️ Median PFS 7.2 vs. 3.2 mos ⬆️ NSCLC median PFS 10 vs. 2.2 mos ⛔ Breast median PFS ~4.3 mos, not different (1/3 breast were triple negative) No improvement in OS overall or in specific groups Median f/u was 12 mos In NSCLC, recurrences were mainly in existing lesions. In breast, recurrences were mainly in new lesions Improvement in ctDNA variant allelle frequences in NSCLC but not breast Grade 2+ adverse effects in n=8 with SBRT
Lung.interpretation: SBRT to oligoprogressive lesions improves PFS in NSCLC. In this analysis there is no benefit in breast cancer, 1/3 of which were triple negative. There was no change in OS. Findings to be further evaluated in Phase III trials.
Lung.commentary: The trial is phase II. Results should be eventually confirmed in phase III trials. SBRT improves PFS at median f/u of 12 months, which makes sense if RT improves LC in 1-5 lesions. OS was not improved, but follow up is short at one year at this time.
Lung.references: 1. Tsai CJ, Yang JT, Shaverdian N, et al. Standard-of-care systemic therapy with or without stereotactic body radiotherapy in patients with oligoprogressive breast cancer or non-small-cell lung cancer (Consolidative Use of Radiotherapy to Block [CURB] oligoprogression): an open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 2 study. Lancet 2023. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(23)01857-3

Lung.methods: Phase II/III ➜ Local therapy to all lesions then chemo (SBRT, hypofx, or surgery) vs. ➜ 🏆 chemo Doses: 24 Gy/1 fx, 30 Gy/3 fx, 34 Gy/ 5 fx. 45 Gy/15 fx also allowed Not allowed: 60 Gy chemoradiation
Lung.results: Study closed to accrual early due to failure to meet the endpoint of PFS. This was announced via press release and further info is to be presented at a meeting.
Lung.interpretation: Upfront local therapy (sub-ablative SBRT, hypofx 15 fractions, or surgery) to all lesions followed by chemo for NSCLC oligometastasis did not improve PFS compared to chemo alone. Definitive chemoradiation was not permitted in this study. The phase II portion was closed and will not proceed to phase III.
Lung.commentary: Review and future directions. Why was this trial negative when others were positive? The answer could be due to choice of endpoints, sequencing, radiation doses, or that there is no benefit. A strength of LU002 and STOP are their multi institutional reach. Neither LU002 or STOP used ablative doses. The SBRT doses used in LU002 and STOP were BEDs <100. The IAEA study used similar doses as LU002. IAEA was a positive study, although phase II and used different sequencing. The 34 Gy/5 fractions permitted in LU002 is a departure from the 34 Gy/1 fraction determined safe in RTOG 0915. The NSCLC olig…

Lung.patients: NSCLC EGFR+, 1-5 oligometastasis Excluded: brain metastasis no prior therapy
Lung.methods: Phase III ➜ 🏆 25-40 Gy in 1-5 fx to all sites (primary, nodes, & oligomets) then TKI vs. ➜ TKI (later palliative RT allowed) (VMAT, IMRT, and SBRT were allowed. Most used ~500 MU dose rate)
Lung.results: ⬆️ Median OS 25.5 vs. 17.4 mos, p<0.001 ⬆️ Median PFS 20.2 vs. 12.5 mos, p<0.001 ⬆️ 6-mos PFS 99% vs. 95% ⬆️ LC 91% vs. 55% Grade 3-4 pneumonitis 6% with RT+TKI no grade 5 adverse effects
Lung.interpretation: Upfront RT in 5 or less fractions to primary and oligometastatic disease in EGFR+ oligometastatic NSCLC improves OS and PFS. This approach should be a standard of care treatment option.
Lung.commentary: Radiation is cytotoxic and osimeritiib is tumorostatic. In a pre-op study, there were zero pCRs with osimertinib, and major path responses were only 15% (Blakely 2024). DFS can be extented with osimeritinib, but other treatment is required to eradicate disease. VMAT, IMRT, and SBRT were allowed. 3DCRT was not. Most institutions used ~500 MU dose rate (Zeng M, email communication, Feb 2022). Although 6-mos PFS, the primary endpoint, does not show a large difference, later median PFS and OS show impressive separations. This suggests that the benefit of RT continues to manifest and evolve with ti…

Lung.citation: Palma et al, Lancet, 2019 Palma et al, JCO, 2020 Harrow et al, IJROBP, 2022
Lung.patients: 1-5 oligometastases (93% were 1-3) any histology, controlled primary tumor from definitive treatment (such as chemoRT) (most were lung, breast, colorectal, prostate. Misc was about 1/3)
Lung.methods: →controlled primary tumor from definitive treatment (such as chemoRT)→ Phase II ➜ SBRT and palliative standard of care (+salvage SABR allowed) vs. ➜ palliative standard of care Doses: SBRT to 30-60 Gy/ 3-8 fx or single fx of 16-24 Gy for brain, spine In this phase II trial, p<0.005 was prespecified as definitive. A p of <0.05 requires validation in phase III.
Lung.results: Median OS 50 vs. 28 mos 5-yr OS 42% vs. 18%, p=0.006 8-yr OS 27% vs. 14% grade 5 toxicity 4.5% (n=3) vs. none Median PFS 12 vs. 6.0 mos 8-yr PFS 21% vs. 0, p=0.001 5-yr LC 63% vs. 46% 11/25 patients in the SABR arm alive beyond 5 years had no progression 5/25 had salvage SABR at some point after randomization Grade 4 adverse effects 0 On MVA, histology did not impact findings
Lung.interpretation: SABR in oligometastatic disease of varying histologies improved PFS and OS as per phase II statistical design. Results are maintainted long term. Overall results, including power, applicability by # mets and reproducibility in individual histologies, are to be validated in Phase III trials.
Lung.commentary: Note that PFS was definitive (p<0.005), and OS was nearly definitive per the statistical design. Results should eventually be evaluated in individual histologies. The median PFS of 6 mos and LC of 63% suggests that the improvement in OS was in part a result of continuing salvage SBRT and potentially reduced future spread due to lower tumor burden, and possible immune response. There were more prostate cancer patients in the SBRT arm (21% vs. 6%). A post-hoc sensitivity analysis of other histologies showed consistent results. Although there were perhaps more favorable histologies in the SBRT ar…
