Lung.citation: Gomez et al, Lancet Oncol, 2016 Gomez et al, JCO, 2019
Lung.patients: Stage IV NSCLC, 0-3 oligometastatases as counted after chemo (See commentary. 0-1 nonregional mets in ~66%, and 2-3 in ~33%.) s/p first line systemic therapy and no progression
Lung.methods: →chemo→if 0-3 oligometastatases remaining→ Phase II ➜ 🏆 local consolidative therapy (surgery, RT, SBRT) & optional concurrent or maintenace therapy vs. ➜ maintenance therapy
Lung.results: Terminated early due to favorable results Local therapy improved median PFS and OS OS 41.2 mos vs. 17 mos OS after progression 38 mos vs. 9 mos PFS 14.2 mos vs. 4.4 mos Time to new lesion 14.2 mos vs. 6.0 mos, p=0.11 0 grade 5 adverse effects There were no total CRs from chemo in the local therapy arm. All in that arm received some form of local therapy.
Lung.interpretation: Local therapy in oligometastatic NSCLC improves PFS. Findings to be further evaluated in Phase III trials.
Lung.commentary: local therapies used were: SBRT 48% surgery and RT 24% chemoRT 8% hypofx RT/hypofx chemo RT 12% surgery only 1% In the inclusion critera, any # regional nodes also counted as a metastatic site. So when applying conventional TNM classification, many actually had 0-2 metastatic sites as counted after chemo. 76% of "mets" were regional. Brain mets were allowed. None had total CR and all received some form of local therapy to the primary or mets.
Lung.references: 1. Gomez DR, Blumenschein GR Jr., Lee JJ, et al. Local consolidative therapy versus maintenance therapy or observation for patients with oligometastatic non-small-cell lung cancer without progression after first-line systemic therapy: a multicentre, randomised, controlled, phase 2 study. Lancet Oncol 2016;17:1672-82. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(16)30532-0 Protocol pdf: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.19.00201/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.19.00201.pdf 2. Gomez DR, Tang C, Zhang J, et al. Local Consolidative Therapy Vs. Maintenance Therapy or Observation for Patients With Oligometastatic Non–Small-Cell Lung Cancer: Long-Term Results of a Multi-Institutional, Phase II, Randomized Study. J Clin Oncol;0:JCO.19.00201. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.19.00201

Lung.patients: NSCLC, ≤6 oligometastatic sites (97% with 1-3 lesions, 3% with 4) s/p first line systemic therapy and no progression
Lung.methods: →s/p first line systemic therapy and no progression→ Phase II ➜ 🏆 consolidative SBRT to all sites then chemo vs. ➜ chemo 45 Gy/15 fx allowed to mediastinal nodes
Lung.results: Terminated early due to favorable results Local therapy improved median PFS Median OS not reached vs. 17 mos (OS was adjusted for crossover in two) PFS 9.7 mos vs. 3.5 mos (median f/u 9.6 mos) 0 grade 4-5 toxicity There were no CRs in mets from chemo in the SBRT arm. All in that arm received SBRT.
Lung.interpretation: Local therapy in oligometastatic NSCLC improves PFS. Findings to be further evaluated in Phase III trials.
Lung.commentary: The doses used were not ablative, with BEDs <100. Radiation doses given: 21-27 Gy/ 1 fx 26.5-33 Gy/ 3 fx 30-37.5 Gy/ 5 fx QD or QOD allowed 45 Gy/ 15 fx allowed for nodes Note that untreated brain mets were excluded.
Lung.references: 1. Iyengar P, Wardak Z, Gerber DE, et al. Consolidative Radiotherapy for Limited Metastatic Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer: A Phase 2 Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2018;4:e173501-e. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2017.3501 Protocol pdf in supplements: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2017.3501

Lung.commentary: SCLC dose summary -Early trials show an OS benefit with RT, and this is confirmed on meta-analysis -INT 0096 establishes 45 Gy/ 30 fx BID as standard -Over 15 years later, CONVERT compares 45 Gy BID regimen to 60 Gy and did not show superiority. Trends in results support 45 Gy BID -CALGB is an ongoing trial to also evaluate dose SCLC PCI cognitive benefit summary -There was previously a debate as to whether PCI caused cognitive decline -Early trials were mixed on whether PCI causes neuroadverse effects (INT and UKCCR/EORTC) -SRS trials looking at addition of WBRT (EORTC, MDACC, Alliance NCI), …

Lung.patients: Limited stage SCLC s/p concurrent chemoRT with no progression
Lung.methods: Concurrent chemoRT with optional PCI then: ➜ durvalumab + tremelimumab vs. ➜ 🏆 durvalumab vs. ➜ placebo
Lung.results: Durvalumab vs. placebo Median OS 55.9 vs. 33.4 mos 3-yr OS 57% vs. 48% 2-yr OS 68% vs. 59% Median PFS 16.6 vs. 9.2 mos 2-yr PFS 46% vs. 34% Pneumonitis 3.1% vs. 2.6%
Lung.interpretation: Adjuvant durvalumab after chemoradiation for limited stage SCLC improves survival.
Lung.commentary: Doses of 60-66 Gy daily or 45 Gy BID were allowed. OS in the control arm is very good compared to historical trials, which may be explained by the trial's exclusion of those with progressive disease after chemoRT. LF would be nice to know but was not a secondary endpoint.
Lung.references: 1. Cheng Y, Spigel DR, Cho BC, et al. Durvalumab after Chemoradiotherapy in Limited-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer. N Engl J Med 2024. Published online September 13, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2404873 Study protocol: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa2404873/suppl_file/nejmoa2404873_protocol.pdf#page=232

Lung.citation: Bogart et al, ASCO, 2021 Bogart et al, JCO, 2023
Lung.methods: ➜ 45 Gy BID (BED 52) vs. ➜ 70 Gy (BED 84) vs. ➜ [arm closed] 61.2 Gy in 1.8 concom boost, qday for 16 fx then BID for 9 fx (BED 72) with cis/etoposide 4 cycles, then PCI if CR or near CR
Lung.results: No difference in OS or PFS in 70 Gy vs. 45 Gy BID Median OS ~29 mos 2-yr OS ~58%, 5-yr OS ~30% adverse effects similar The 61.2 Gy arm was closed and not included in the above results
Lung.interpretation: 70 Gy QD did not improve OS compared to 45 Gy BID. Since the trial was designed to show superiority and did not, BID RT remains standard of care per the prespecified design. Daily RT is a reasonable alternative.
Lung.commentary: CONVERT results trended in favor of BID, and there was worse esophageal adverse effects with daily fractionation, so BID is preferred. For those strictly following pre-specified design, CALGB and CONVERT were superiority studies - not noninferiority - so per the prespecifed design the conclusion is that 66-70 Gy is not superior and 45 Gy BID remains standard of care. It is important also to keep in mind that design of superiority and noninferiority, especially selection of noninferiority margins, is somewhat arbitrary (Dunn 2018). Treating SCLC with 60-70 Gy does demonstrate favorable results …
Lung.references: 1. Bogart JA, Wang XF, Masters GA, et al. Phase 3 comparison of high-dose once-daily (QD) thoracic radiotherapy (TRT) with standard twice-daily (BID) TRT in limited stage small cell lung cancer (LSCLC): CALGB 30610 (Alliance)/RTOG 0538. J Clin Oncol 2021:s15;8505. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2021.39.15_suppl.8505 Protocol pdf [dose constraints p33]: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.22.01359/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.22.01359.pdf#page=33 2. Bogart J, Wang X, Masters G, et al. High-Dose Once-Daily Thoracic Radiotherapy in Limited-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer: CALGB 30610 (Alliance)/RTOG 0538 [published online ahead of print, 2023 Jan 9]. J Clin Oncol 2023;JCO2201359. DOI: http://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.01359
Lung.commentary_refs: 1. Dunn DT, Copas AJ, Brocklehurst P. Superiority and non-inferiority: two sides of the same coin?. Trials 2018;19:499. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1186/s13063-018-2885-z

Lung.citation: Faivre-Finn et al, Lancet Oncol, 2017 Salem et al, JAMA Oncol, 2018 Walls et al, IJROBP, 2024
Lung.methods: cis/etoposide x1, then ➜ 45 Gy BID/30 fx vs. ➜ 66 Gy/33 fx 4-6 cycles cis/etoposide IMRT or 3DCRT. RT starts with 2nd cycle chemo No ENI Followed by PCI if indicated.
Lung.results: Median OS 30.0 mos vs. 25.4 mos, p=0.25 2-yr OS 56% vs. 51% 5-yr OS 34% vs. 31% -Median local PFS 20.7 vs. 17.9 mos p=0.20 -Median DM PFS 20.2 vs. 16.6 mos, p=0.24 -More grade 4 neutropenia with BID RT 49% vs. 38% -late grade 3 esophagitis in n=0 vs 7 No difference in febrile neutropenia or grade 3-4 pneumonitis -Stage I-II: median OS 50 mos, 2-yr OS 64%, 5-yr OS 49%, DM 41%, LRF 14% -Stage III: median OS 25 mos, 2-yr OS 51%, 5-yr OS 28%, DM 57%, LRF 17%
Lung.interpretation: Outcomes trended in favor of BID RT but the difference was nonsignificant. Since the trial was designed to show superiority and did not, and once daily shows slightly worse late adverse effects, BID RT remains standard of care. Daily radiation may be a reasonable alternative for those with limited schedules with tumors not close to the esophagus.
Lung.commentary: CONVERT results trended in favor of BID, and there was worse esophageal adverse effects with daily fractionation, so BID is preferred. For those strictly following pre-specified design, CALGB and CONVERT were superiority studies - not noninferiority - so per the prespecifed design the conclusion is that 66-70 Gy is not superior and 45 Gy BID remains standard of care. It is important also to keep in mind that design of superiority and noninferiority, especially selection of noninferiority margins, is somewhat arbitrary (Dunn 2018). Treating SCLC with 60-70 Gy does demonstrate favorable results …
Lung.references: 1. Faivre-Finn C, Snee M, Ashcroft L, et al. Concurrent once-daily versus twice-daily chemoradiotherapy in patients with limited-stage small-cell lung cancer (CONVERT): an open-label, phase 3, randomised, superiority trial. Lancet Oncol 2017;18:1116-25. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30318-2 2. Salem A, Mistry H, Hatton M, et al. Association of Chemoradiotherapy With Outcomes Among Patients With Stage I to II vs Stage III Small Cell Lung Cancer: Secondary Analysis of a Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2019;5:e185335-e. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2018.5335 3. Mir R, Groom N, Mistry HB, Wilson E, Faivre-Finn C. Association between radiotherapy protocol variations and outcome in the CONVERT trial. Clin Transl Radiat Oncol. 2022;39:100560. Published 2022 Dec 13. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ctro.2022.100560 4. Walls GM, Mistry H, Barlesi F, et al. Long-term outcomes after concurrent once- or twice-daily chemoradiation in limited-stage small-cell lung cancer: a brief report from the CONVERT trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2024.02.063
Lung.commentary_refs: 1. Dunn DT, Copas AJ, Brocklehurst P. Superiority and non-inferiority: two sides of the same coin?. Trials 2018;19:499. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1186/s13063-018-2885-z

Lung.patients: Limited stage SCLC: ipsilateral hilum, ipsilateral SCV, and bilateral mediastinum allowed. Exlusions: contralateral supraclavicle or hilum
Lung.methods: ➜ 45 Gy RT in 1.8 Gy daily vs. ➜ 45 Gy in 1.5 Gy BID fractions with concurrent cisplatin and etoposide x4 cycles q3weeks. RT started w/ cycle #1
Lung.results: Median OS 23 vs. 19 mos 2-yr OS 47% vs. 41% (NS) 5-yr OS 26% vs. 16%, p=0.04 LF 36% vs 52% p=0.06 More grade 3 esophagitis 27% vs. 11%
Lung.interpretation: BID RT to 45 Gy with concurrent chemo results in improved OS compared to QD RT to 45 Gy and chemo.
Lung.commentary: Criticisms: 45 Gy QD fx and 45 Gy BID fx are not BED equivalent. Treatment field: Ipsilateral hilum and bilateral mediastinum. Inferior border is 5 cm below carina or at inferior hilum, whichever is lower. Include SCV if involved
Lung.notes: -Early trials show an OS benefit with RT, and this is confirmed on meta-analysis -INT 0096 establishes 45 Gy/ 30 fx BID as standard -Over 15 years later, CONVERT and CALGB compare 45 Gy BID regimen to 60-66 Gy and did not show superiority. Trends in results support 45 Gy BID
Lung.references: 1. Turrisi AT, Kim K, Blum R, et al. Twice-Daily Compared with Once-Daily Thoracic Radiotherapy in Limited Small-Cell Lung Cancer Treated Concurrently with Cisplatin and Etoposide. N Engl J Med 1999;340:265-71. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199901283400403

Lung.methods: Phase II ➜ 54 Gy BID to GTV with SIB 45 Gy to PTV/ 30 fx vs. ➜ past phase II cohort of 45 Gy BID/30 fx cisplatin/etoposide x4-6 cycles
Lung.results: median PFS 13.3 vs. 11.1 mos, p=0.08 2-yr OS 66% vs. 39% grade 2 esophagitis worse with SIB, but grade ≥3 no different grade ≥2 pneumonitis not different
Lung.interpretation: In this phase II dose escalation study, dose escalation to 54 Gy BID in 30 fx had improved OS with similar adverse effects. Findings require validation in phase III.
Lung.commentary: Although the CONVERT and CALGB studies were negative for dose escalation, the THORA and Sun-Yat Sen University studies provide some evidence for benefit of dose-escalation with BID or hyperfx approaches. In this study, given that these were two separate phase II trials that are being compared, there is a possibility of imbalances. The THORA authors make the point that doses without phase III evidence have a history of being employed in SCLC (e.g. 60-66 Gy daily, 70 Gy daily before their Phase III studies, etc). The data for RT for oligometastasis in NSCLC is phase II and widely implemented. SC…
Lung.references: 1. Peng F, Yu TT, Hu X, et al. Comparison of Simultaneous Integrated Boost IMRT for Dose Escalation（54 Gy）and Conventional IMRT（45 Gy）Twice Daily Combined With Concurrent Chemotherapy for Limited-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer: Analysis of Two Prospective Trials. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2022. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2022.07.442

Lung.citation: Grønberg et al, Lancet Oncol, 2021 Grønberg et al, J Thorac Oncol, 2025
Lung.methods: Phase II ➜ 45 Gy BID/30 fx vs. ➜ 60 Gy BID/ 40 fx (54 Gy allowed if constraints exceeded, or lower if still not met) platinum/etoposide x4 cycles PCI 25 Gy/10 or 30 Gy/15 allowed
Lung.results: 2-yr OS 48% vs. 74%, p=0.0005 5-yr OS 28% vs. 39% Median OS 22.5 vs. 43.5 mos Median PFS 15.0 vs. 18.6 mos, p=0.13 Disease progression 63-64%, not different LC 23% vs. 37%, p=0.037 DM ~45%, not different Brain mets 12% vs. 20%, p=0.17 grade 3-4 esophagitis 18-21% grade 1-2 esophagitis 37-44% n=2 esophgeal strictures, only with 60 Gy grade 3-4 pneumonitis 0% vs. 3%
Lung.interpretation: In this phase II dose escalation study, 60 Gy BID had improved OS with similar adverse effects. Randomized trials are warranted.
Lung.commentary: Although the CONVERT and CALGB studies were negative for dose escalation with daily dosing, the THORA and Sun-Yat Sen University studies provide some evidence for benefit of dose-escalation with BID. In THORA four patients in the 60 Gy arm received 45 Gy when constraints were not achieved, and one by mistake. Per protocol outcomes were similar. Information is not provided regarding how many had 54 Gy. Stratification was by T and N staging, reducing imbalance by tumor volume. 85% had PCI. It is unusual to see an OS benefit without a PFS benefit, but not an impossibility. PFS did have a trend to…
Lung.references: 1. Grønberg BH, Killingberg KT, Fløtten Ø, et al. High-dose versus standard-dose twice-daily thoracic radiotherapy for patients with limited stage small-cell lung cancer: an open-label, randomised, phase 2 trial. Lancet Oncol 2021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30742-7 2. Grønberg BH, Killingberg KT, Fløtten Ø, et al. High-Dose Versus Standard-Dose Twice-Daily Thoracic Radiotherapy in Limited-Stage SCLC: Final Survival Data, Long-Term Toxicity, and Relapse Patterns in a Randomized, Open-Label, Phase II Trial. J Thorac Oncol. Published online April 19, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtho.2025.04.007

Lung.methods: Phase II: ➜ 45 Gy BID/30 fx (BED 52) vs. ➜ hypofx 65 Gy QD/26 fx (BED 66.4) cis/etoposide x4-6 cycles 1⁰ Endpoint: PFS IMRT RT starts with cycle 1-3 of chemo No ENI PCI for good response
Lung.results: Median OS 33.6 vs. 39.3 mos, p=0.137 (OS data still maturing) Median PFS 13.4 vs. 17.2 mos, p=0.031 2-yr PFS 28% vs. 42% Median LPFS 23.9 mos vs. not reached 2-yr LPFS 50% vs. 69%, p=0.017 - More grade 3+ acute lymphopenia with QD RT 40% vs. 72% - No differences on grade 3-4 esophagitis, pneumonitis, or death Treatment compliance similar between both groups
Lung.interpretation: Moderately hypofractionated QD RT to 65 Gy/26 fx showed improved PFS & LPFS with similar toxicities compared with SOC standard 45 Gy BID RT in LS-SCLC. This regimen should be evaluated in a phase III study.
Lung.commentary: Although the CONVERT and CALGB studies were negative for dose escalation with daily dosing, the THORA and Sun-Yat Sen University studies provide some evidence for benefit of dose-escalation with BID. This study was designed to test slightly higher BED than CONVERT 70 Gy arm (BED of 66.4 Gy here vs. 64.6), but within shorter period of overall treatment time based on data showing worse outcomes in those with prolonged treatment time. The authors also cite preclinical data suggesting that hypofx RT may improve the efficacy of immunotherapy. As always, phase III trials are required to fully confir…
Lung.references: 1. Qiu B, Li Q, Liu J, et al. Moderately Hypofractionated Once-Daily Compared With Twice-Daily Thoracic Radiation Therapy Concurrently With Etoposide and Cisplatin in Limited-Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Multicenter, Phase II, Randomized Trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2021;111:424-435. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2021.05.003

Lung.patients: Trials of chemo vs. chemoRT for limited-stage
Lung.methods: Meta-analysis 13 randomized trials evaluating chemoRT vs. chemo
Lung.results: Absolute 5% benefit in 3-yr OS Addition of RT to chemo gave 14% reduction in mortality
Lung.interpretation: RT improves OS when combined with chemo in limited-stage SCLC
Lung.commentary: Note chemotherapy used was not cisplatin etoposide
Lung.references: 1. Pignon J-P, Arriagada R, Ihde DC, et al. A Meta-Analysis of Thoracic Radiotherapy for Small-Cell Lung Cancer. N Engl J Med 1992;327:1618-24. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199212033272302

Lung.patients: Trials of chemo vs. chemoRT for limited-stage
Lung.methods: Meta-analysis of 11 randomized trials evaluating chemoRT vs. chemo
Lung.results: Absolute 5% benefit in OS Absolute 25% LC benefit to RT (48% vs. 23%)
Lung.interpretation: RT improves OS in SCLC in this meta-analysis.
Lung.references: 1. Warde P, Payne D. Does thoracic irradiation improve survival and local control in limited-stage small-cell carcinoma of the lung? A meta-analysis. J Clin Oncol 1992;10:890-5. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1992.10.6.890

Lung.methods: Prospective. RT to 45 Gy/ 1.5 Gy BID with carbo/etoposide. RT directed only to PET avid disease
Lung.results: overall LR 65%, 3% isolated regional node failure Median OS 19 mos, median PFS 14 mos
Lung.interpretation: In this prospective study there is low regional failure with omission of ENI in SCLC.
Lung.references: 1. van Loon J, De Ruysscher D, Wanders R, et al. Selective Nodal Irradiation on Basis of 18FDG-PET Scans in Limited-Disease Small-Cell Lung Cancer: A Prospective Study. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2010;77:329-36. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2009.04.075

Lung.methods: Meta-analysis of 7 trials evaluating early vs late RT
Lung.results: Clear benefit to early RT, starting at cycle 1 or 2, less than 9 weeks after start of chemo. 2 yr OS RR 1.17
Lung.interpretation: There is OS benefit to starting RT and cycle 1 or 2.
Lung.commentary: One example here. Similar results in many other meta-analyses
Lung.references: 1. Fried DB, Morris DE, Poole C, et al. Systematic Review Evaluating the Timing of Thoracic Radiation Therapy in Combined Modality Therapy for Limited-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2004;22:4837-45. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2004.01.178

Lung.methods: ➜ 25 Gy/10 fx vs. ➜ 25 Gy/10 fx with hippocampal avoidance
Lung.results: 12-mos intracranial recurrence ~15%, noninferior No difference in HVLT-R DR Hippocampal avoidance led to less neurocognitive failure
Lung.interpretation: Although the primary endpoint of HVLT-R DR was not met, hippocampal avoidance did lead to less neurocognitive failure.
Lung.commentary: Protocol dose constraints pdf, p27: https://mtcancer.org/wp-content/uploads/2015/12/CC003-05.pdf#page=27 hippocampus D100 ≤7.5 Gy (VA ≤8.5 Gy) hippocampus max ≤13.5 Gy (VA ≤15 Gy) optics ≤25 Gy (VA ≤36 Gy)
Lung.references: 1. Gondi V, Pugh SL, Mehta MP, et al. Hippocampal Avoidance During Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation for Patients With Small Cell Lung Cancer: Randomized Phase II/III Trial NRG-CC003. J Clin Oncol. Published online August 11, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-25-00221 Study protocol: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/instance/12342643/bin/NIHMS2097990-supplement-PV_Protocol.pdf#page=27

Lung.methods: Meta-analysis of 7 trials of SCLC with CR after chemo comparing ➜ PCI vs. ➜ no PCI
Lung.results: PCI improved 3-yr OS 20.7% vs. 15.3% 3-yr brain mets: 33% vs. 59% Dose increase led to less mets but no change in survival Earlier PCI resulted in less brain mets
Lung.interpretation: PCI improves OS for SCLC in those with CR.
Lung.commentary: Differing doses used. This study is prior to MRI era and some mets may have not been detected (but also recall that WBRT for limited brain mets has questionable OS benefit, Patchell 1998). Also confirmed by Brussels meta-analysis of 12 trials (Meert 2001)
Lung.references: 1. Aupérin A, Arriagada R, Pignon J-P, et al. Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation for Patients with Small-Cell Lung Cancer in Complete Remission. N Engl J Med 1999;341:476-84. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199908123410703
Lung.commentary_refs: Meert AP, Paesmans M, Berghmans T, et al. Prophylactic cranial irradiation in small cell lung cancer: a systematic review of the literature with meta-analysis. BMC Cancer. 2001;1:5. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2407-1-5

Lung.citation: Le Péchoux et al, Lancet Oncol, 2009 Le Péchoux et al, Ann Onc 2011
Lung.patients: Limited-stage SCLC in CR after chemo and RT
Lung.methods: ➜ 25 Gy/10 daily fx vs. ➜ 36 Gy/18 QD or 36 Gy/24 BID
Lung.results: 2-yr OS trend to worse with high dose 42% vs. 37%, p=0.05 2-yr brain mets 29% standard vs. 23% high dose, p=0.18 More fatigue, headache, and nausea with high dose. No difference in neurocognitive function However looking at RTOG 0212 alone showed worse chronic neuroadverse effects in high dose arm (Wolfson 2011)
Lung.interpretation: Although there was some trend to benefit in LC with high dose PCI, OS was worse, compared to lower dose PCI. High dose PCI increased adverse effects.
Lung.references: 1. Le Péchoux C, Dunant A, Senan S, et al. Standard-dose versus higher-dose prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) in patients with limited-stage small-cell lung cancer in complete remission after chemotherapy and thoracic radiotherapy (PCI 99-01, EORTC 22003-08004, RTOG 0212, and IFCT 99-01): a randomised clinical trial. Lancet Oncol 2009;10:467-74. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(09)70101-9 2. Le Péchoux C, Laplanche A, Faivre-Finn C, et al. Clinical neurological outcome and quality of life among patients with limited small-cell cancer treated with two different doses of prophylactic cranial irradiation in the intergroup phase III trial (PCI99-01, EORTC 22003-08004, RTOG 0212 and IFCT 99-01). Annals of oncology : official journal of the European Society for Medical Oncology 2011;22:1154-63. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/annonc/mdq576
Lung.commentary_refs: Wolfson AH, Bae K, Komaki R, et al. Primary analysis of a phase II randomized trial Radiation Therapy Oncology Group (RTOG) 0212: impact of different total doses and schedules of prophylactic cranial irradiation on chronic neurotoxicity and quality of life for patients with limited-disease small-cell lung cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2011;81:77–84. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2010.05.013

Lung.citation: Rodriguez de Dios et al, JCO, 2021
Lung.methods: ➜ PCI 25 Gy/10 fx vs. ➜ IMRT PCI 25 Gy/10 fx with hippocampal avoidance
Lung.results: Better free delayed recall with hippocampal sparing RT (33% HA-PCI vs. 11%) with persistent benefit seen at 6 mos, 1 year, and 2 years 1-yr rate of brain mets ~20% 4 pts developed brain mets in area of HA No difference in QoL, OS, or incidence of brain mets with HA-PCI
Lung.interpretation: Hippocampal avoidance preserves short term memory in PCI in SCLC.
Lung.references: 1. Rodríguez de Dios N, Couñago F, Murcia-Mejía M, et al. Randomized Phase III Trial of Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation With or Without Hippocampal Avoidance for Small-Cell Lung Cancer (PREMER): A GICOR-GOECP-SEOR Study [published online ahead of print, 2021 Aug 11]. J Clin Oncol 2021;JCO2100639. doi: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.21.00639

Lung.citation: Gregor et al, Eur J Cancer, 1997
Lung.methods: If CR to chemo→ ➜ PCI (36 Gy/18 fx, 24/12, 30/10, or 8/1) vs. ➜ obs Dose options changed throughout trial
Lung.results: There was neurological impairment in both groups prior to PCI Further impairment equal in both groups at 6 mos and 1 year 60% had 20 Gy/5 fx and still had benefit
Lung.interpretation: PCI results in minimal neurocognitive adverse effects and QOL changes compared to obs.
Lung.notes: -There was previously a debate as to whether PCI caused cognitive decline -Early trials were mixed on whether PCI causes neuroadverse effects (INT and UKCCR/EORTC) -SRS trials looking at addition of WBRT (EORTC, MDACC, Alliance NCI), or WBRT vs. SRS (RTOG 1270) showed improved QOL and cognitive function without WBRT. -See also the PCI study in NSCLC, NVALT-11/DLCRG-02, that does show cognitive impairment with PCI but similar QOL later. -Hippocampus sparing with IMRT in 30 Gy WBRT has shown improvement in cognitive function and seems translatable to PCI. -PREMER and NRG CC003 show some benefit …
Lung.references: 1. Gregor A, Cull A, Stephens RJ, et al. Prophylactic cranial irradiation is indicated following complete response to induction therapy in small cell lung cancer: Results of a multicentre randomised trial. Eur J Cancer 1997;33:1752-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/s0959-8049(97)00135-4

Lung.methods: Retrospective analysis of 76 lesions in 74 patients from 24 institutions who underwent SABR for inoperable early SCLC
Lung.results: 1-yr LC 97%, 3-yr LC 96% Median DFS 49.7 mos 1-yr DFS 58%, 3-yr DFS 53% Median DSS 52 mos 1-yr DSS 85%, 3-yr 64% Median OS 17.8 mos 1-yr OS 70%, 3-yr OS 34% Chemo led to increase in OS and DFS, effect also on multivariate analysis Failures: 46% distant, 25% nodal, 21% elsewhere lung Median dose 50 Gy/5 fx SABR
Lung.interpretation: SABR for early stage inoperable SCLC results in favorable outcomes in this retrospective series. SABR should be considered an alternative treatment option.
Lung.references: 1. Verma V, Simone CB, Allen PK, et al. Multi-Institutional Experience of Stereotactic Ablative Radiation Therapy for Stage I Small Cell Lung Cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2017;97:362-371. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2016.10.041

Lung.patients: Extensive stage SCLC. Brain mets were allowed (28%)
Lung.methods: ➜ 30 Gy/ 10 fx to gross disease at day 21-28 after first cycle vs. ➜ 🏆 no RT → total 4 cycles of carboplatin, etoposide, durvalumab followed by durvalumab
Lung.results: OS 10-11 mos, not different PFS 5.1 mos, not different Deaths other causes 15% vs. 4%, but not more when excluding deaths before RT started Adverse effects 88% vs. 70% Grade 3-4 esophagitis 10% with RT
Lung.interpretation: Consoildative thoracic RT did not improve OS.
Lung.commentary: Increase in deaths other than RT, which generally occurred before RT started, suggests that there may have been an OS benefit to RT if the trial had higher power. It's possible as well that outcomes might have been improved if radiation was given at the end of chemotherapy as in the CREST study.
Lung.references: 1. Gronberg BH, Dumoulin DW, Oselin K, et al. Concurrent thoracic radiotherapy (TRT), platinum/etoposide chemotherapy, and durvalumab immunotherapy in extensive-stage (ES) small cell lung cancer (SCLC): A phase III trial. J Clin Oncol 44;2026:s17.LBA8005. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2026.44.17_suppl.LBA8005

Lung.citation: Slotman et al, Lancet, 2015 Slotman et al, Lancet, 2015
Lung.patients: Extensive stage SCLC, no brain mets, who responded to 4-6 cycles chemo, ECOG 0-2
Lung.methods: ➜ 🏆 30 Gy thoracic RT + PCI vs. ➜ PCI Primary endpoint: 1-yr OS
Lung.results: ⬆️ 2-yr OS 13% vs. 3%, p=0.004 ↗️ 1-yr OS 33% vs. 28%, p=0.066 ⬆️ 6-mo PFS 24% vs. 7% ⬇️ Thoracic failure 44% vs. 80% CR only 5% in both arms Post-hoc analysis in author's reply: No benefit in CR. Benefit was only in those with residual disease. 95% had residual disease -OS and PFS in residual disease very similar to above. Stats not provided for those with CR
Lung.interpretation: Consolidative thoracic RT improves OS in extensive stage SCLC that responded to chemo. However the primary endpoint of 1-yr OS was not met.
Lung.commentary: This trial confirmed the survival benefit of consolidative RT in extensive SCLC, a condition with poor prognosis and few innovations over years other than immunotherapy. For some the role of RT is controversial - so much so that even the author Slotman published an accompanying editorial doubting his own results! The role of radiotherapy could be in priming an immune response, cytoreduction, or LC reducing future pulmonary complications. The trial reproduced the results of a smaller Yugoslavia trial six years prior. Opponents to consolidative thoracic RT might respond that CR to chemotherapy d…
Lung.references: 1. Slotman BJ, van Tinteren H, Praag JO, et al. Use of thoracic radiotherapy for extensive stage small-cell lung cancer: a phase 3 randomised controlled trial. Lancet 2015;385:36-42. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(14)61085-0 2. Slotman BJ, van Tinteren H, Praag JO, et al. Radiotherapy for extensive stage small-cell lung cancer – Authors' reply. Lancet 2015;385:1292-3. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(15)60679-1
Lung.commentary_refs: 1. Melosky BL, Leighl NB, Dawe D, et al. Canadian Consensus Recommendations on the Management of Extensive-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer. Curr Oncol 2023;3:6289-6315. DOI: https://doi.org/10.3390/curroncol30070465 2. NICE guideline. Lung cancer: diagnosis and management. Last updated March 8, 2024. Available from: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng122/resources/lung-cancer-diagnosis-and-management-pdf-66141655525573 Accessed May 14, 2024.

Lung.patients: Extensive stage SCLC with PR or stable disease by RECIST after 4-6 cycles chemo plus atezolizumab
Lung.methods: ➜ RT to up to 5 sites, 5 fx simultaneously on days 1-5 only Thorax or liver: 45 or 30 Gy Extrathoracic RT: 30 or 20 Gy followed by atezolizumab vs. ➜ atezolizumab
Lung.results: ongoing study Primary endpoints: PFS (phase II), and OS (phase III)
Lung.commentary: The CREST study showed benefit with RT to thorax. On an exploratory analysis the benefit was only in residual disease, but that analysis was likely under powered (see CREST commentary). In CASPIAN and Impower133, only 3% in the immunotherapy arms had CR, which may indicate a continuing role for consolidative RT with immunotherapy. PET may overestimate CR compared to RECIST. The immunotherapy trials withheld standard of care therapy with thoracic RT. The RAPTOR trial would benefit from a dose constraint for blood pool, or separation of radiation courses, as 5 courses of radiation or even 3 is a…
Lung.references: 1. NRG Oncology. Testing the Addition of Radiation Therapy to the Usual Immune Therapy Treatment (Atezolizumab) for Extensive Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer, The RAPTOR Trial. Accessed May 18, 2023. Available at: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04402788 NLM identifier: NCT04402788.

Lung.patients: 1-4 metastatic lesions and no brain metastases after CR/PR from 4-6 cycles platinum chemo
Lung.methods: Phase II: ➜ PCI 25 Gy only vs. ➜ PCI and consolidative RT to thorax and original sites of mets Rec dose to chest and mets of 45 Gy in 15 fx. 30-40 Gy was also allowed if needed for dose contraints
Lung.results: Closed early due to futility 1-yr OS unchanged 60% vs. 51% consolidatived RT, p=0.21 3-mo progression 53% vs. 15% 12-mo progression 80% vs. 75% TTP favored PCI+RT, HR 0.53, p=0.01 One patient in each arm had grade 4 adverse effects One patient in PCI+RT had grade 5 respiratory failure but had exceeded V20 and mean dose contraints
Lung.interpretation: Consolidative RT to thorax and extracranial metastases delayed progression but did not improve 1 year OS.
Lung.commentary: 69% given >45Gy RT to chest 94% given 25Gy PCI More patients over 65 years old in PCI+RT
Lung.references: 1. Gore EM, Hu C, Sun A, et al. NRG Oncology/RTOG 0937: Randomized Phase 2 Study Comparing Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation (PCI) Alone to PCI and Consolidative Extracranial Irradiation for Extensive Disease Small Cell Lung Cancer (ED-SCLC). Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2016;94:5. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2015.10.039

Lung.methods: Cis/etop x3 then if distant CR and local CR/PR: ➜ thoracic RT 54 Gy/36 fx BID + cis/etop x2 vs. ➜ cis/etoposode x4 Followed by PCI
Lung.results: Improved OS and LRR with thoracic RT 5-yr OS 9% vs. 4% 5-yr LRC 20% thoracic RT vs. 8%
Lung.interpretation: Consolidative thoracic hyperfractionated RT improves OS in extensive stage SCLC that responded to chemo.
Lung.commentary: Although this trial was the first to confirm the benefit of consolidative RT to chest after response to chemotherapy, the particular regimen here never seemed to reach much application, even with its favorable 5-yr OS. Six years passed before results were reproduced in CREST.
Lung.references: 1. Jeremic B, Shibamoto Y, Nikolic N, et al. Role of Radiation Therapy in the Combined-Modality Treatment of Patients With Extensive Disease Small-Cell Lung Cancer: A Randomized Study. J Clin Oncol 1999;17:2092-. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1999.17.7.2092

Lung.methods: ➜ 25 Gy/10 fx vs. ➜ 25 Gy/10 fx with hippocampal avoidance
Lung.results: 12-mos intracranial recurrence ~15%, noninferior No difference in HVLT-R DR Hippocampal avoidance led to less neurocognitive failure
Lung.interpretation: Although the primary endpoint of HVLT-R DR was not met, hippocampal avoidance did lead to less neurocognitive failure.
Lung.commentary: Protocol dose constraints pdf, p27: https://mtcancer.org/wp-content/uploads/2015/12/CC003-05.pdf#page=27 hippocampus D100 ≤7.5 Gy (VA ≤8.5 Gy) hippocampus max ≤13.5 Gy (VA ≤15 Gy) optics ≤25 Gy (VA ≤36 Gy)
Lung.references: 1. Gondi V, Pugh S, Mehta MP, et al. Primary Endpoint Results of NRG CC003: Phase IIR/III Trial of Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation (PCI) with or without Hippocampal Avoidance (HA) for Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC). Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2023. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2023.08.028

Lung.trial: National Kyushu Cancer Center, Fukuoka, Japan
Lung.patients: Extensive stage SCLC who responded to platinum chemotherapy. No brain metastases on MRI
Lung.methods: ➜ PCI 25 Gy/10 fx vs. ➜ observation
Lung.results: No OS benefit with PCI. BM improved 1-yr OS 54% vs. 48% (NS, p=0.094) 2-yr OS 15% vs. 19% Median OS 11.6 mos PCI vs. 13.7 mos 1-yr brain mets 33% vs. 59% Overall brain mets 48% vs. 69%
Lung.interpretation: PCI did not improve OS in extensive stage SCLC staged with MRI. The incidence of future brain metastases was reduced.
Lung.commentary: This is the first trial to require MRI for eligibility. PCI did reduce the incidence of new brain mets. The study at hand may be used to support close observation of patients with MRI followed by WBRT when brain metastases present, or potentially even SRS. Some might choose to offer PCI based on improvement in LC. adverse effects should also be considered: some studies stress that there are few to no cognitive changes with PCI UKCCR/EORTC, Gregor 1987), while those in support of hippocampal sparing (PREMER) or SRS (NRG CC009) caution of its potential hazards. Compare to the EORTC study (Slotma…
Lung.references: 1. Takahashi T, Yamanaka T, Seto T, et al. Prophylactic cranial irradiation versus observation in patients with extensive-disease small-cell lung cancer: a multicentre, randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2017;18:663-71. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30230-9

Lung.methods: 4-6 cycles of chemo, wait 4-6 weeks, then if any response to therap→ ➜ PCI 20-30 Gy vs. ➜ observation
Lung.results: 1-yr OS 27% vs. 13% median OS 5.4 mos vs 6.7 mos (after randomization) 1-yr symptom detected BM 15% vs. 40% median DFS 12.0 weeks vs 14.7 weeks
Lung.interpretation: PCI reduces symptomatic brain mets and improves DFS and OS. This trial was performed prior to the MRI era.
Lung.commentary: In the trial's era, MRI was not required for workup prior to PCI. "Response to therapy" was not defined in the protocol. SEER analysis (Patel 2009) also shows survival benefit with PCI. The benefit persists with multivariate analysis.
Lung.references: 1. Slotman B, Faivre-Finn C, Kramer G, et al. Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation in Extensive Small-Cell Lung Cancer. N Engl J Med 2007;357:664-72. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa071780
Lung.commentary_refs: Patel S, Macdonald OK, Suntharalingam M. Evaluation of the use of prophylactic cranial irradiation in small cell lung cancer. Cancer 2009;115:842-50. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.24105

Lung.methods: ➜ SRS vs. ➜ WBRT
Lung.results: No difference in OS Worse neurocognitive decline with WBRT
Lung.interpretation: SRS for 1-10 brain metastasis in SCLC leads to similar OS as WBRT.
Lung.commentary: Small study. Information regarding LC or salvage WBRT was not provided.
Lung.references: 1. Bernhardt D, El Shafie R, Thomas M, et al. Stereotactic Radiotherapy vs. Whole Brain Radiation Therapy for Patients with 1-10 Brain Metastases from Small Cell Lung Cancer: Results of the Randomized ENCEPHALON (ARO 2018-9) Trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2023. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2023.08.031 Full text pdf: https://www.astro.org/ASTRO/media/ASTRO/Meetings%20and%20Education/PDFs/AM23/2023_LBAs_corrected.pdf

Lung.methods: Retrospective. 28 centers SRS compared to WBRT
Lung.results: On propensity score analysis and MVA, WBRT was associated with improved CNS-TTP over SRS but not OS
Lung.interpretation: In this large retrospective study, WBRT led to better CNS-TTP than SRS but no change in OS.
Lung.commentary: A retrospective study with propensity score analysis is not a replacement for a randomized trial. Nonetheless some practitioners might choose to practice SRS in SCLC brain mets based on no change in OS with PCI in the Kyushu study or for patients who are opposed to whole brain PCI. The authors acknowledge that randomized trials are warranted. The thought process behind treating brain mets with SRS in SCLC is very similar to that of using INRT/ISRT in lymphoma.
Lung.references: 1. Rusthoven CG, Yamamoto M, Bernhardt D, et al. Evaluation of First-line Radiosurgery vs Whole-Brain Radiotherapy for Small Cell Lung Cancer Brain Metastases: The FIRE-SCLC Cohort Study [published online ahead of print, 2020 Jun 4] [published correction appears in doi: 10.1001/jamaoncol.2020.3404]. JAMA Oncol 2020;6:1028-1037. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2020.1271

Lung.methods: Phase III ➜ SRS vs. ➜ 30 Gy hippocampal avoidance IMRT SRS doses 24 Gy for <1 cm 22 Gy for 1-2 cm 18 Gy for 2-3 cm
Lung.results: Ongoing trial Primary endpoint: time to cognitive failure Secondary endpoint: PROs, salvage therapy, time to LR, OS, time to neuro death, adverse effects, necrosis
Lung.commentary: Time to cognitive failure is the primary endpoint, but the degree of LR in the SRS alone arm and neurologic death will also be very interesting. Brainstem lesion peripheral doses to PTVs: 18-20 Gy for 1 cc, 16-18 Gy for 1-4 cc, 14-16 Gy for 4-10 cc SRS Dose contraints Branstem D0.03 cc < 12 Gy (12-14 Gy VA. Or if brainstem lesion, will not be met) Optics <8 Gy (8-10 Gy VA) Hippocampi D100% <9 (variation acceptable <10) Hippocampi max <16 (VA 17) Optics max <30 Gy (VA <37.5)
Lung.references: 1. NRG Oncology. Phase III Trial of Stereotactic Radiosurgery versus Hippocampal Avoidant WBRT for Small Cell Lung Cancer Brain Metastases. Available from: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04804644. NLM identifier: NCT04804644. Accessed February 2, 2022.

Lung.patients: Extensive stage SCLC with no prior therapy Ineligible: prior chest consolidative RT or palliative radiation
Lung.methods: atezolizumab (anti PD-L1) + carbo/etop x4 → ➜ atezolizumab vs. ➜ 🏆atezolizumab plus lurbinectedin PCI permitted as discretion, but not chest or palliative radiation
Lung.results: ⬆️ 1-yr IRF PFS 12% vs. 21% ⬆️ 1-yr OS 44% vs. 56%, p=0.017 Grade 3-4 adverse effects 38% vs. 22% Lurbinectedin had more cytopenias
Lung.interpretation: Lurbinectedin added to maintenance atezolizumab in extensive SCLC improves IRF and OS.
Lung.commentary: Lurbinectedin is an alkylating drug that binds guanine residues in the minor groove of DNA. Those with brain metastasis were excluded. 27% were not randomized after four cycles of chemo. After years of stalled progress, lurbinectedin and immunotherapy finally managed to improve OS in extensive SCLC, and CREST showed an OS benefit with consolidative chest radiation. Since lurbinectedin was already available as second line treatment at the time of the study, the trial perhaps should have been crossover design. Some advocate or choose to employ lurbinectedin after failure of atezolizumab (Choudhu…
Lung.references: 1. Paz-Ares L, Borghaei H, Liu SV, et al. Efficacy and safety of first-line maintenance therapy with lurbinectedin plus atezolizumab in extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (IMforte): a randomised, multicentre, open-label, phase 3 trial. Lancet 2025;40:2129-2143. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(25)01011-6 Study protocol: available in Lancet supplement
Lung.commentary_refs: 1. Choudhury NJ, Garassino MC. Lurbinectedin with atezolizumab maintenance therapy in extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer. Lancet 2025;405:2104-2106. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(25)01145-6

Lung.patients: Extensive stage SCLC with no prior therapy
Lung.methods: ➜ durvalumab + tremelimumab + plat/etop vs. ➜ 🏆durvalumab + plat/etop vs. ➜ plat/etop PCI allowed but not chest RT
Lung.results: Median OS 10.4 vs. 12.9 vs. 10.5 mos CR 3% vs. 3% vs. 1% Serious adverse effects 45% vs. 36% vs. 32% Treatment deaths 5% vs. 2% vs. 1%
Lung.interpretation: Durvalumab + platinum/etoposide improves OS in extensive stage SCLC compared to chemo alone. The addition of tremelimumab to durvalumab + chemo did not improve outcomes.
Lung.commentary: Patients did not have access to standard of care consolidative chest radiation. PCI was optional in the control arm but not permitted in the immunotherapy arm. Immunotherapy was given with each cycle of chemo and maintenance every 4 weeks. In CASPIAN and IMpower133, only 3% in the immunotherapy arms had CR, which may indicate a continuing role for consolidative RT with immunotherapy. The CREST study showed benefit with RT in those with residual disease.
Lung.references: 1. Goldman JW, Dvorkin M, Chen Y, et al. Durvalumab, with or without tremelimumab, plus platinum-etoposide versus platinum-etoposide alone in first-line treatment of extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (CASPIAN): updated results from a randomised, controlled, open-label, phase 3 trial [published online ahead of print, 2020 Dec 4]. Lancet Oncol 2020;S1470-2045(20)30539-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30539-8

Lung.citation: Horn et al, NEJM, 2018 Lui et al, JCO, 2021
Lung.patients: Extensive stage SCLC with no prior therapy
Lung.methods: ➜ 🏆 atezolizumab (anti PD-L1) + carbo/etop vs. ➜ placebo + carbo/etop PCI allowed but not chest RT
Lung.results: ⬆️ Median OS 12.3 mos vs. 10.3 mos ⬆️ 1.5-yr OS 34% vs. 21% Median PFS 5.2 mos vs. 4.3 mos CR 3% vs. 1% Benefit in any PDL1 status Time to intracranial progression 20.2 vs. 10.5 mos If no PCI, intracranial progression 16.7 vs. 9.8 mos Progression at initial sites 59% vs. 56% Progression at new sites 53% vs. 48%
Lung.interpretation: First line atezolizumab in extensive stage SCLC improves OS and PFS, and delays time to intracranial progression.
Lung.commentary: Patients in this trial did not have access to standard of care of consolidative chest radiation. In CASPIAN and IMpower133, only 3% in the immunotherapy arms had CR, which may indicate a continuing role for consolidative RT with immunotherapy. The CREST study showed benefit with RT in those with residual disease.
Lung.references: 1. Horn L, Mansfield AS, Szczęsna A, et al. First-Line Atezolizumab plus Chemotherapy in Extensive-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer. N Engl J Med 2018;379:2220-9.1. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1809064 Protocol pdf: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa1809064/suppl_file/nejmoa1809064_protocol.pdf 2. Liu SV, Reck M, Mansfield AS, et al. Updated Overall Survival and PD-L1 Subgroup Analysis of Patients With Extensive-Stage Small-Cell Lung Cancer Treated With Atezolizumab, Carboplatin, and Etoposide (IMpower133). J Clin Oncol 2021;39:619-630. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.20.01055

Lung.trial: National Cancer Institute of Aviano, Italy
Lung.patients: Malignant pleural mesothelioma, any histology, M0
Lung.methods: Phase III lung sparing surgery and platinum+pemetrexed→ ➜ hemithoracic Tomotherapy 50 Gy to pleural cavity + 60 Gy SIB to gross disease vs. ➜ palliative radiation to surgical scar and/or gross residual
Lung.results: 2-yr OS 58% vs. 28% Median OS 25.6 vs. 12.4 mos 2-yr LRC 83% vs. 27% DM, no change With hemithoracic RT: acute grade 3 adverse effects in n=11 late grade 3-4 toxicty in n=17 grade ≥2 pneumonitis in n=9 grade 5 pneumonitis in n=1
Lung.interpretation: Hemothoracic RT improves OS compared to palliative RT to scar or gross residual. There is notable adverse effects.
Lung.commentary: contralateral lung V5 <17% mean lung <24 Gy kidney V25 <40%, V10 <10% liver V30 <40% heart: no constraint listed Palliative regimens ranged from 21 Gy/ 3 fx and 20-35 Gy in 5-10 fractions
Lung.references: 1. Trovo M, Revelant A, Polesel J, et al. Radical Hemithoracic Radiotherapy vs. Palliative Radiotherapy in Non-metastatic Malignant Pleural Mesothelioma: Results from a Phase III, Randomized Clinical Trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2020. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2020.11.057

Lung.patients: epithelioid or biphasic malignant pleural mesothelioma Stage I-IIIA
Lung.methods: pleurodectomy/decortication and pemetrexed + platinum (order optional)→ ➜ no RT vs. ➜ hemithoracic pleural IMRT 45-50.4 Gy
Lung.results: ongoing protocol Primary endpoints: OS Secondary endpoints: LF, DM, PFS, adverse effects, EORTC QOL
Lung.interpretation: ongoing protocol
Lung.commentary: Lungs V20 <37%, Mean <20.5 Gy Ipsilateral: each lung V40<67% Contralateral: Lung R V5%<50%, Lung L Mean <8 Heart (right): V40 <35%, mean <30 Gy Heart (left): V40 <35%, V30 <50%, mean <30 Gy
Lung.references: 1. NRG Oncology. Testing the Addition of Targeted Radiation Therapy to Surgery and the Usual Chemotherapy Treatment (Pemetrexed and Cisplatin [or Carboplatin]) for Stage I-IIIA Malignant Pleural Mesothelioma. Available at: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04158141. NLM identifier: NCT04158141. Accessed August 21, 2020.

Lung.patients: Malignant pleural mesothelioma, resectable, stages T1-3, N0-2, M0
Lung.methods: Phase II randomized. 3 cycles of cis/pemetrexed → EPP → If macroscopic resection → ➜ RT vs. ➜ no RT
Lung.results: 34% received an objective response Of those who had surgery, complete macroscopic resection in 64% For the RT randomization: Median total RT dose 55.9 Gy Median LR-RFS 7.6 mos vs. 9.4 mos with RT RT grade ≥3 AE N&V 11%, esophagitis 7%, pneumonitis 7%
Lung.interpretation: Findings did not support the use of hemithoracic radiotherapy after neoadjuvant chemotherapy and EPP.
Lung.commentary: RT delivered to hemithorax, thoracomtomy channel, mediastinal node stations. Boost given to areas at high risk of relapse
Lung.references: 1. Stahel RA, Riesterer O, Xyrafas A, et al. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy and extrapleural pneumonectomy of malignant pleural mesothelioma with or without hemithoracic radiotherapy (SAKK 17/04): a randomised, international, multicentre phase 2 trial. Lancet Oncol 2015;16:1651-1658. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(15)00208-9

Lung.patients: Mesothelioma patients who underwent large bore pleural interventions
Lung.methods: immediate vs. deferred RT (for later mets) to tracts. 21 Gy/ 3 fx
Lung.results: Incidence of procedure tract metastasis: 9% vs. 16%, p=0.14
Lung.interpretation: There is a trend to benefit of immediate RT to procedure tracks in mesothelioma, but the results were nonsignificant and do not support routine use.
Lung.commentary: Subgroup analysis showed possible benefit for epithelioid (p=0.057) and no chemo (p=0.021)
Lung.references: 1. Clive AO, Taylor H, Dobson L, et al. Prophylactic radiotherapy for the prevention of procedure-tract metastases after surgical and large-bore pleural procedures in malignant pleural mesothelioma (SMART): a multicentre, open-label, phase 3, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Oncol 2016;17:1094-104. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(16)30095-X

Lung.trial: Japanese Association for Research on the Thymus Database Study
Lung.patients: Masaoka Stage II-III thymoma and thymic carcinoma
Lung.methods: Retrospective database review
Lung.results: Stage II-III thymic carcinoma: RT was associated with improved RFS for but not OS Stage II-III thymoma: RT was not associated with benefit in RFS or OS. No change on subgroup analysis
Lung.interpretation: RT improved RFS for stage II-III thymic carcinoma. No improvement in outcomes in Stage II-III thymoma.
Lung.references: 1. Omasa M, Date H, Sozu T, et al. Postoperative radiotherapy is effective for thymic carcinoma but not for thymoma in stage II and III thymic epithelial tumors: The Japanese Association for Research on the Thymus Database Study. Cancer 2015;121:1008-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.29166

Lung.citation: Kondo et al, Ann Thorac Surg, 2003
Lung.methods: Retrospective review
Lung.results: Stage III-IV thymoma 5-yr OS 93% for total resection, 64% STR, 36% inoperable Thymic carcinoma 5-yr OS 67% GTR, 30% STR, 24% inoperable No benefit to RT in Stage III-IV thymoma or thymic carcinoma, or in GTR stage II-III thymoma
Lung.interpretation: Outcomes are demonstrated. RT does not appear to have benefit in GTR of thymoma.
Lung.commentary: Possible benefit with RT in incompletely resected thymoma
Lung.references: 1. Kondo K, Monden Y. Therapy for thymic epithelial tumors: a clinical study of 1,320 patients from Japan. Ann Thorac Surg 2003;76:878-84. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/s0003-4975(03)00555-1

Lung.methods: Review of patients treated with or without radiation
Lung.results: Masaoka stage and extent of resection were correlated with recurrence. Stage I, R0: no recurrences Stage II-III, R0: mediastinal recurrence 53% vs. 0% with RT Stage II-III, R1/R2: recurrence 21% with RT
Lung.interpretation: R0 alone appears to be inadequate therapy in higher stage thymoma.
Lung.references: 1. Curran WJ Jr, Kornstein MJ, Brooks JJ, Turrisi AT 3rd. Invasive thymoma: the role of mediastinal irradiation following complete or incomplete surgical resection. J Clin Oncol 1988;6:1722-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1988.6.11.1722

Lymphoma.subtopic: Fav/Unfav: RT in PET negative disease
Lymphoma.citation: Raemaekers et al, JCO, 2014 André et al, JCO, 2017 Federico et al, JCO, 2023
Lymphoma.patients: Stage I-II HL, above diaphragm
Lymphoma.methods: ABVD x2 → PET → ➜🏆 Standard, non-PET directed, both RER and SER: → ABVD (x1 for F, x2 for U) + 30-36 Gy INRT vs. ➜ PET2 directed INRT: → PET2 negative → additional ABVD (total x6 for U, total x4 for F) → SER PET2 positive → BEACOPP x2 + 30-36 Gy INRT Primary endpoint: noninferiority of PFS, delta of 10%. Superiority stats also included Positive PET was defined as Deauville 3-5
Lymphoma.results: -In favorable 86% achieved negative PET2. -In unfavorable 75% had negative PET2 -PET negative arm stopped early at 1-yr analysis 📌 Fav PET negatives 3ABVD + INRT vs. 4ABVD: 5-yr PFS 99% vs. 87% ⬆️ 10-yr PFS 99% vs. 85%, superior 10-yr OS 100% vs. 98%, not different 10-yr 2nd cancer 4.0% vs. 3.8% 📌 Unfav PET negatives 4ADVD + INRT vs. 6ABVD: 5-yr PFS 92% vs. 90% 5-yr OS 100% in both arms ✅ 10-yr PFS 91% vs. 86%, not noninferior 10-yr OS 95%, not different 10-yr 2nd cancer 6.8% vs. 6.5% -With ABVD alone, 73% failures were soley at original sites No difference in 10-yr adverse effects or 2nd canc…
Lymphoma.interpretation: Observation is not noninferior to INRT in early stage HL, both favorable and unfavorable, with negative PET2. The PET negative arms were closed early due to futility. PFS with INRT was superior in favorable PET negative. Additional cycles of chemotherapy do not replace INRT in this setting. There is no difference in 10-yr adverse effects or second cancer. There is no OS benefit to INRT in CR, but INRT spares some from salvage therapy. In SER PET2 positive, BEACOPPesc improves PFS over ABVD. Chemo and INRT shows good results and allows some to be spared transplant.
Lymphoma.commentary: Since this study, SWOG 1826 showed 3-yr PFS of 98% in Stage III HL in peds treated with N+AVD x6. Thus there may no longer be a role for routine ISRT for PET2+ in Stage I-III HL treated with this regimen, but there is still a role for ISRT for refactory PET6+. Two to three cycles of chemo followed by ISRT may be useful for those who want less chemotherarpy. This trial's 10 year follow-up confirms long term PFS benefit with INRT in PET2 negative, without detriment to OS or increase in secondary cancer. The study also shows that additional cycles of chemo cannot replace INRT. There is no benefit…

Lymphoma.subtopic: Fav/Unfav: RT in PET negative disease
Lymphoma.citation: Radford et al, NEJM, 2015 Barrington et al, Radiother Oncol, 2019
Lymphoma.patients: Stage IA-IIA (about 1/3 unfavorable) Bulky disease excluded
Lymphoma.methods: ABVD x3 → PET → PET3 negative: ➜🏆 30 Gy IFRT vs. ➜obs PET3 positive: ➜🏆 ABVD x1 + 30 Gy IFRT Primary endpoint: noninferiority of PFS, delta of 7% in CI Positive PET was defined as Deauville 3-5
Lymphoma.results: PET3 negative in 75% 📌 PET3 negative (IFRT vs. obs): 3-yr PFS favors RT, 95% vs. 91% (CI exceeded noninferiority boundary of 7) 3-yr OS 97% vs. 99% (p=-.21) Per protocol analysis (12% in RT arm "declined" RT) 3-yr PFS 97% vs. 90% 📌 PET3 positive PFS 88% Only 5.5% of PET positive had transplant PET score of 5 was the best predictor of prognosis. PET score of 1-4, EORTC or GHSH favorable vs. unfavorable not predicitive
Lymphoma.interpretation: Observation is not noninferior to IFRT in early stage HL with negative PET after chemo, and PFS favored IFRT. Favorable or unfavorable was not predictive. There were protocol violations in the RT arm. When analyzed per protocol, RT has superior PFS. In those PET positive, ABVDx4 plus IFRT allowed 88% to avoid transplant or death.
Lymphoma.commentary: The noninferiority margin was initially 10 then amended to 7. The trial still did not meet noninferiority, and 12% in the RT arm "declined" radiation, but the authors' conclusion was that prognosis was very good in both arms. There were protocol violations in the RT arm: per protocol analysis shows benefit with RT; however, per protocol evaluations are no longer randomized. Long term results of EORTC H10 show that INRT is superior in CR in favorable, and in unfavorable omission is not noninferior. Since this study, SWOG 1826 showed 3-yr PFS of 98% in Stage III HL in peds treated with N+AVD x6.…
Lymphoma.notes: Hodgkin lymphoma summary: -HL initially treated with RT alone with large fields (STNI) -ABVD chemo later developed, and eventual NCIC ECOG HD6 showed chemo may have better OS than RT. But there are criticisms to the study -As a result, RT use decreases over time some regions -Multiple trials (H7, H8, HD8) show that RT field can be safely reduced to IFRT when chemo given -HD10 for favorable lymphoma establishes ABVD + 20 Gy IFRT -HD11 for unfavorable lymphoma establishes ABVD + 30 Gy IFRT -RAPID and EORTC trials show RT still gives improvement in PFS for PET negative after chemo. EORTC H10 has …

Lymphoma.subtopic: Fav: RT in PET negative disease
Lymphoma.citation: Fuchs et al, JCO, 2019 Baues et al, IJROBP, 2021 Eichenauer et al, Blood, 2023
Lymphoma.patients: Stage I-II HL, favorable NLPHL allowed
Lymphoma.methods: ABVD x2 → PET → ➜ Standard, non-PET directed: 🏆 20 Gy IFRT vs. PET2 directed IFRT: ➜ SER PET2 positive:🏆 20 Gy IFRT ➜ PET2 negative: no RT Primary endpoint: noninferiority of PFS Positive PET was defined as Deauville 3-5
Lymphoma.results: PET2 negative in 66% 📌 PET 2negatives (standard vs. directed): 5-yr PFS 93% IFRT vs. 86% no RT -Infield recurrence 2% vs. 11% -No difference in outfield recurrence, 4-7% both arms 5-yr OS 98% both arms 📌 PET2 positive /PR/ SER using Deauville 4 ADVD+IFRT 5-yr PFS in PET negative 93% ADVD+IFRT 5-yr PFS in PET positive 80% 📌 HD16 + HD17 NLPHL: PET2 negative NLPHL, no RT compared to RT 5-yr PFS 83% without RT, 100% with RT 5-yr OS 100% in both
Lymphoma.interpretation: Observation is not noninferior to IFRT in favorable HL with negative PET2 after chemo. PFS favors RT. In SER PET positive, ABVDx2 plus IFRT allowed 80% to avoid relapse or death. RT is recommended for NLPHL in those PET negative after initial chemotherapy.
Lymphoma.commentary: An inferiority margin was prespecified in the paper but not met with goal of HR of ≥3.01 with 5-yr PFS of at least 10%. Thus observation is not noninferior. Some guidelines endorse 36-40 Gy for refractory (PR), but only 20 Gy was given for PET+ here (ILROG, Constine 2018). Since this study, SWOG 1826 showed 3-yr PFS of 98% in Stage III HL in peds treated with N+AVD x6. Thus there may no longer be a role for routine ISRT for PET2+ in Stage I-III HL treated with this regimen, but there is still a role for ISRT for refactory PET6+. Two to three cycles of chemo followed by ISRT may be useful for t…

Lymphoma.subtopic: Fav: RT dose, omission of RT
Lymphoma.patients: favorable I-IIB HL
Lymphoma.methods: After 6 cycles EBVP and CRu→ ➜ 🏆 36 Gy IFRT vs. ➜ 🏆 20 Gy vs. ➜ no RT No RT arm stopped early due to high recurrences >20%
Lymphoma.results: Worse RFS with obs. 20 Gy and 36 Gy noninferior 5-yr RFS 89% vs. 84% vs. 70% 5-yr OS same at 97-100%
Lymphoma.interpretation: After CRu in favorable HL, RT results in superior RFS compared to observation.
Lymphoma.commentary: Trial was concluded in 2004, but not reported until 2017. Prior to PET era. EBVD is no longer standard chemo. This was later reproduced in EORTC H10.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Thomas J, Fermé C, Noordijk EM, et al. Comparison of 36 Gy, 20 Gy, or No Radiation Therapy After 6 Cycles of EBVP Chemotherapy and Complete Remission in Early-Stage Hodgkin Lymphoma Without Risk Factors: Results of the EORT-GELA H9-F Intergroup Randomized Trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2018;1;100(5):1133-1145. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2017.10.015

Lymphoma.citation: Bröckelmann et al, JAMA Oncol, 2020 Bröckelmann et al, JCO, 2023
Lymphoma.patients: unfavorable I-IIB HL
Lymphoma.methods: Phase II ➜ N+AVD x4 → 30 Gy ISRT vs. ➜ N x4 → N+AVDx2 → AVD x2 → 30 Gy ISRT
Lymphoma.results: Interim CR after N+AVD x2 or Nx4 87% vs. 51% CR after completion of treatment 90% vs. 94% 1-yr PFS 100% vs. 98% Hypothyroid requiring long term meds in 15% LV EF wnl 95% No 2nd malignancies in either arm at median f/u 41 mos
Lymphoma.interpretation: Both approaches show excellent results. Single agent nivolumab upfront may warrant further investigation for de-escalation of therapy. There is a 15% chance of hypothyroidism.
Lymphoma.commentary: Some treat early stage HL with N+AVD without ISRT based on this study and SWOG 1826. SWOG did achieve 3-yr PFS of 98% in Stage III peds with N+AVD with only 1% receiving radiotherapy, which could justify its omission in Stages I-II. The NIVAHL regimen could be useful for those who wish to receive less chemotherapy. ISRT was required in both arms in NIVAHL. Less cycles are used here than in SWOG 1826, which used 6. EORTC H10 showed PFS benefit with ISRT after CR in early stage unfavorable treated with ABVD. In SWOG 1826 hypothyroidism was 7% and hyperthyroidism 3%. Commentary last updated June …

Lymphoma.subtopic: Fav/Unfav: ABVD, Removal of RT in RER, brentuximab & nivolumab
Lymphoma.patients: Pediatric and adult HL Stage I-II favorable or unfavorable per adult staging, bulky allowed, age 5-60
Lymphoma.methods: ABVD x2 → PET → RER Favorable/Unfav ➜ ABVDx2 for F, AVDx4 for U vs. ➜ Brentuximab + nivolumab x4 SER Favorable/Unfav ➜ escBEACOPPx2 + 30 Gy ISRT IMRT or protons vs. ➜ Brentuximab + nivolumab x4 + 30 Gy ISRT IMRT or protons
Lymphoma.results: Ongoing protocol Primary endpoint: PFS Secondary endpoints: noninferiority of 12-yr OS, 12-yr EFS, adverse effects, QOL
Lymphoma.commentary: Background. Both pediatric and adult patients are eligible. In order to preserve fertility and reduce late effects, adult HL regimens are now being trialed in the pediatric population. SER. As in essentially all other clinical trials for early stage HL, RT remains mandated for those with SER, anticipated in 15%. The investigators write "Therefore, there is no data to support omitting radiotherapy among patients with SER for patients with Stage I/II disease." RT for SER shows good results in studies, and there is no evidence that response adapted chemo for something that already seems chemo res…
Lymphoma.references: 1. NCI. A Study to Compare Standard Therapy to Treat Hodgkin Lymphoma to the Use of Two Drugs, Brentuximab Vedotin and Nivolumab. NLM identifier: NCT05675410. Accessed March 2, 2024. Available from: https://www.clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05675410

Lymphoma.subtopic: Unfav: RT in PET negative disease IFRT vs. INRT
Lymphoma.citation: Borchmann et al, Lancet Oncol, 2021 Eichenauer et al, Blood, 2023 Rosenbrock et al, Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2024
Lymphoma.patients: Stage I-II HL, unfavorable NLPHL allowed (excluded: stage IIB bulky or Stage IIBE)
Lymphoma.methods: escBEACOPP x2 + ABVD x2 → PET → ➜ Standard, non-PET directed: 30 Gy IFRT vs. ➜ PET4 directed INRT: PET4 positive refractory: 30 Gy INRT PET4 negative: no RT Noninferiority
Lymphoma.results: Results noninferior 5-yr PFS 97% vs. 95% 5-yr OS 98% both arms None of the failures with INRT would have been prevented by IFRT 📌 HD16 + HD17 NLPHL: PET negative NLPHL, no RT compared to RT 5-yr PFS 83% without RT, 100% with RT 5-yr OS 100% in both
Lymphoma.interpretation: INRT can be safely omitted in negative PET after treatment with escBEACOPP + ABVD. PET-guided INRT is noninferior to routine IFRT in PFS and OS. None of the failures with INRT would have been prevented by IFRT. RT is recommended for NLPHL in those PET negative after initial chemotherapy.
Lymphoma.commentary: Observe that the PFS rates here with escBEACOPP+ABVD are better than EORTC H10, which used ABVD for unfavorable, and also even better than favorable HL trials HD10 and HD16. After the publication of this study, long term f/u of EORTC H10 showed that omission of RT was not noninferior in unfavorable early stage PET negative HL treated with ABVD. From the two studies it seems that BEACOPP must be used to support omission of RT. Another difference of this study from other PET directed studies is that PET is performed at cycle 4 here, not cycle 2 or 3. EscBEACOPP with ABVD was established in HD14,…

Lymphoma.patients: Stage IA or IIA, non-bulky
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ ABVD x4-6 vs. ➜ 35 Gy STLI (for unfavorable risk, ABVD x2 added before STLI) Prior to PET era
Lymphoma.results: Improved OS and EFS with chemotherapy, and improved FFP with RT: 12-yr OS 94% vs. 87%, p=0.04 12-yr FFP 87% vs. 92%, p=0.05 12-yr EFS 85% vs. 80% Death due to second cancers: n=4 vs. 10 Other causes (not 2nd cancer): n=2 vs. 10 Cardiac events: n=16 vs. 26 On subanalysis, the difference is in unfavorable risk but not in favorable
Lymphoma.interpretation: Treatment with chemo alone had superior OS compared to RT with STLI in early stage HL. With STLI 20 died of other causes (6 had chemo), leading to inferior OS.
Lymphoma.commentary: This trial was used by some in the past to support omission of RT in Hodgkin lymphoma for years. Radiation techniques have since modernized to much smaller fields than STLI, and the trial is now historical since the publications of UK RAPID, EORTC H10, and HD16. Those trials showed that IFRT/INRT improves PFS in PET negative HL. EORTC H10 has ten year f/u. In HL there is a careful balance between cure, adverse effects, and secondary malignancy, with a focus for some on de-escalation. There is admittedly excellent OS for the ABVD alone arm in NCIC ECOG HD6. Differences in OS were partly due to …

Lymphoma.patients: Stage IA-IIB with at least one risk factor
Lymphoma.methods: ➜🏆 escBEACOPPx2 + ABVDx2 + IFRT 30 Gy vs. ➜ ABVD x4 + IFRT 30 Gy
Lymphoma.results: FFTF and PFS improved with BEACOPP/ABVD: 5-yr FFTF 95% escBEACOPP/ABVD vs. 88% ABVD on subanalysis, benefit only in bulky or B sx 5-yr OS 97% in both arms relapses 3% vs. 8% acute adverse effects increased, late adverse effects not different
Lymphoma.interpretation: BEACOPP/ABVD + IFRT improves FFTF over ABVD + IFRT in unfavorable HL.
Lymphoma.commentary: BEACOPP is not commonly applied in some regions due to adverse effects and added complexity, and PFS benefit without OS benefit. On subanalysis, those with bulky masses or elevated ESR tended to have better outcomes with escBEACOPP but the trial was not powered for this analysis. Some practicioners instead elect to use 6-8 cycles ABVD in bulky HL. But an advantage of the 2+2 regimen is that there are fewer cycles. The trial did not confirm long term adverse effects with escBEACOPP. And even if that's the case, BEACOPP does spare patients from long term adverse effects of salvage chemo and BMT.…
Lymphoma.references: 1. von Tresckow B, Plütschow A, Fuchs M, et al. Dose-Intensification in Early Unfavorable Hodgkin's Lymphoma: Final Analysis of the German Hodgkin Study Group HD14 Trial. J Clin Oncol 2012;30:907-13. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2011.38.5807

Lymphoma.citation: Fermé et al, Eur J Cancer, 2007 Fermé et al, Eur J Cancer, 2017
Lymphoma.patients: Unfavorable stage I-II HL (age ≥50, 4-5 sites, bulky, ESR ≥50 no B-symptoms, ESR ≥30 and B-symptoms)
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ ABVD x6 + IFRT vs. ABVD x4 + IFRT vs. ➜ BEACOPPbaseline x4 + IFRT RT to 30 Gy ±boost
Lymphoma.results: 5-yr EFS noninferior: 85.9% vs. 88.8% vs. 89.9% 5-yr OS 93-94% (NS) BEACOPP regimens result in worse adverse effects
Lymphoma.interpretation: Outcomes are noninferior. BEACOPP has more adverse effects. ABVD x4 + IFRT is the preferred regimen.
Lymphoma.commentary: Trial was performed before the era of PET imaging.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Fermé C, Eghbali H, Meerwaldt JH, et al. Chemotherapy plus Involved-Field Radiation in Early-Stage Hodgkin's Disease. N Engl J Med 2007;357:1916-27. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa064601 2. Fermé C, Thomas J, Brice P, et al. ABVD or BEACOPPbaseline along with involved-field radiotherapy in early-stage Hodgkin Lymphoma with risk factors: Results of the European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer (EORTC)–Groupe d'Étude des Lymphomes de l'Adulte (GELA) H9-U intergroup randomised trial. Eur J Cancer 2017;81:45-55. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejca.2017.05.005

Lymphoma.subtopic: Unfav: BEACOPP or ABVD, IFRT dose
Lymphoma.citation: Eich et al, JCO, 2010 Sasse et al, JCO, 2017
Lymphoma.patients: Unfavorable HL, Stage I or IIA, with ≥1 risk factor: large MS mass, extranodal disease, increase ESR, ≥3 nodal regions. Or Stage IIB with increased ESR or ≥3 nodes
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ escBEACOPP x4 vs. ➜ 🏆 ABVD x4 ➜ 🏆 IFRT 30 Gy vs. ➜ 20 Gy Noninferiority [prior to PET era]
Lymphoma.results: ABVD 30 Gy, BEACOPP 20 Gy, and BEACOPP 30 Gy are noninferior. 5-yr FFTF ~86%, 5-yr OS ~95%, 5-yr PFS ~88% ABVD 20 Gy is not noninferior. 10-yr PFS 76% ABVD 20 Gy vs. 84% in other arms BEACOPP gave increased adverse effects over ABVD: grade ≥3, 74% vs. 52% 30 Gy gave increased adverse effects over 20 Gy: grade ≥3, 12% vs. 6% No differences in secondary malignancy at 10 years
Lymphoma.interpretation: ABVD + 30 Gy and BEACOPP + 20 Gy are preferred treatment options for unfavorable HL. Among the arms found noninferior, these have the least adverse effects. Increased adverse effects with BEACOPP compared to ABVD. Increased adverse effects with 30 Gy compared to 20 Gy IFRT.
Lymphoma.commentary: Although some studies have shown late adverse effects with BEACOPP, those trials used more cycles than the 4 in HD11 (Carde 2016; HD15, Engert 2012). Still, some have concerns with BEACOPP and prefer ABVD (EORTC H10). A later GHSG study, HD14, showed benefit with escBEACOPPx2 + ABVDx2 + IFRT 30 Gy over ABVDx4 + IFRT.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Eich HT, Diehl V, Görgen H, et al. Intensified Chemotherapy and Dose-Reduced Involved-Field Radiotherapy in Patients With Early Unfavorable Hodgkin's Lymphoma: Final Analysis of the German Hodgkin Study Group HD11 Trial. J Clin Oncol 2010;28:4199-206. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2010.29.8018 2. Sasse S, Bröckelmann PJ, Goergen H, et al. Long-Term Follow-Up of Contemporary Treatment in Early-Stage Hodgkin Lymphoma: Updated Analyses of the German Hodgkin Study Group HD7, HD8, HD10, and HD11 Trials. J Clin Oncol 2017;35:1999-2007. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2016.70.9410

Lymphoma.citation: Engert et al, NEJM, 2010 Sasse et al, JCO, 2017
Lymphoma.patients: Early-stage favorable HL (7.5% of patients had ESR<30 with B symptoms)
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ 🏆 ABVD x2 vs. ➜ ABVD x4 then ➜ 🏆 IFRT 20 Gy vs. ➜ 30 Gy Noninferiority [prior to PET era]
Lymphoma.results: No significant differences in outcomes 5-yr FFTF ~92% 10-yr OS 94% 10-yr PFS 87% No differences in secondary malignancy at 10 years
Lymphoma.interpretation: 20 Gy with 2 cycles ABVD is preferred in favorable HL. All regimens were noninferior, thus the most de-escalated option is preferred.
Lymphoma.commentary: Only 7.5% had B symptoms with ESR<30. Based on this, some may question whether B symptoms should be able to be classified as favorable. PET not used in this study. See HD16 as well as RAPID and EORTC trials for RT in PET negative (Fuchs 2018, Radford 2015, Raemaekers 2014).
Lymphoma.references: 1. Engert A, Plütschow A, Eich HT, et al. Reduced Treatment Intensity in Patients with Early-Stage Hodgkin's Lymphoma. N Engl J Med 2010;363:640-52. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1000067 2. Sasse S, Bröckelmann PJ, Goergen H, et al. Long-Term Follow-Up of Contemporary Treatment in Early-Stage Hodgkin Lymphoma: Updated Analyses of the German Hodgkin Study Group HD7, HD8, HD10, and HD11 Trials. J Clin Oncol 2017;35:1999-2007. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2016.70.9410

Lymphoma.subtopic: Fav: RT dose, omission of RT
Lymphoma.patients: favorable I-IIB HL
Lymphoma.methods: After 6 cycles EBVP and CRu: ➜ 36 Gy IFRT vs. 20 Gy vs. ➜ no RT No RT arm stopped early due to high recurrences >20%
Lymphoma.results: Worse RFS with obs. 20 Gy and 36 Gy noninferior 5-yr RFS 89% vs. 84% vs. 70% 5-yr OS same at 97-100%
Lymphoma.interpretation: After CRu in favorable HL, RT results in superior RFS compared to observation.
Lymphoma.commentary: This trial was concluded in 2004, but not reported until 2017. Prior to PET era. EBVD is no longer standard chemo. This was later reproduced in EORTC H10, UK RAPID, and HD16 with ABVD and PET.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Thomas J, Fermé C, Noordijk EM, et al. Comparison of 36 Gy, 20 Gy, or No Radiation Therapy After 6 Cycles of EBVP Chemotherapy and Complete Remission in Early-Stage Hodgkin Lymphoma Without Risk Factors: Results of the EORT-GELA H9-F Intergroup Randomized Trial. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2018;1;100(5):1133-1145. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2017.10.015

Lymphoma.patients: Hodgkin lymphoma Stage I-II
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ 40 Gy EFRT + 40 Gy IFRT vs. ➜ 30 Gy EFRT + 40 Gy IFRT
Lymphoma.results: No difference in RFS or OS. RFS 78% vs. 83% (p=0.093), OS 91% vs. 96% (p=0.16)
Lymphoma.interpretation: De-escalation to 30 Gy from 40 Gy shows favorable outcomes.
Lymphoma.commentary: Historical trial that shows the evolution of HL dosing. Interesting trend to worse outcomes with higher dose
Lymphoma.references: 1. Dühmke E, Franklin J, Pfreundschuh M, et al. Low-Dose Radiation Is Sufficient for the Noninvolved Extended-Field Treatment in Favorable Early-Stage Hodgkin’s Disease: Long-Term Results of a Randomized Trial of Radiotherapy Alone. J Clin Oncol 2001;19:2905-14. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2001.19.11.2905

Lymphoma.patients: Stage I-II supradiaphragmatic, unfavorable HD
Lymphoma.methods: Fav: ➜ MOPP/ABV x3 + IFRT vs. ➜ STNI alone Unfav: ➜ MOPP-ABV x6 + IFRT vs. ➜ MOPP-ABVx4 + IFRT vs. MOPP-ABVx4 + STNI
Lymphoma.interpretation: F: IFRT with MOPP/ABV has superior EFS and OS over STNI alone. MOPP/ABV is superior to EBVP chemotherapy. U:IFRT with chemo and STNI with chemo had similar EFS and OS. IFRT is preferred.
Lymphoma.commentary: No differences in second cancers: 4 or 5 in each arm. Dyspnea increased with more bleomycin; unrelated to RT field size.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Fermé C1, Eghbali H, Meerwaldt JH, et al. Chemotherapy plus involved-field radiation in early-stage Hodgkin's disease. N Engl J Med. 2007;357(19):1916-27. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa064601

Lymphoma.patients: F: prognostic score 1-5 U: prognostic score >8
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ EBVP x6 + IFRT 36-4 Gy vs. ➜ Fav: STNI 40 Gy ➜ Ufav: MOPP/ABV x6 + IFRT
Lymphoma.interpretation: F: IFRT with EBVP has superior EFS over STNI alone. There is no OS benefit when EBVP is utilized on H7F (compare to H8F). U: MOPP/ABV improves EFS and OS compared to EBVP.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Noordijk EM, Carde P, Dupouy N, et al. Combined-Modality Therapy for Clinical Stage I or II Hodgkin's Lymphoma: Long-Term Results of the European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer H7 Randomized Controlled Trials. J Clin Oncol 2006;24:3128-35. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2005.05.2746

Lymphoma.citation: Engert et al, JCO, 2003 Klimm et al, Ann Oncol, 2007 Sasse et al, JCO, 2017
Lymphoma.patients: Early-stage unfavorable HL
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ COPP/ABVD x2 + IFRT 30-40 Gy vs. ➜ COPP/ABVD x2 + EFRT 30-40 Gy
Lymphoma.results: No difference in 10-yr FFTF, PFS, OS, or secondary malignancy. Worse OS with EFRT in age >60
Lymphoma.interpretation: IFRT has similar PFS and OS to EFRT. IFRT is preferred.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Engert A, Schiller P, Josting A, et al. Involved-Field Radiotherapy Is Equally Effective and Less Toxic Compared With Extended-Field Radiotherapy After Four Cycles of Chemotherapy in Patients With Early-Stage Unfavorable Hodgkin’s Lymphoma: Results of the HD8 Trial of the German Hodgkin’s Lymphoma Study Group. J Clin Oncol 2003;21:3601-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2003.03.023 2. Klimm B, Eich H, Haverkamp H, et al. Poorer outcome of elderly patients treated with extended-field radiotherapy compared with involved-field radiotherapy after chemotherapy for Hodgkin's lymphoma: an analysis from the German Hodgkin Study Group. Ann Oncol 2006;18:357-63. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/annonc/mdl379 3. Sasse S, Bröckelmann PJ, Goergen H, et al. Long-Term Follow-Up of Contemporary Treatment in Early-Stage Hodgkin Lymphoma: Updated Analyses of the German Hodgkin Study Group HD7, HD8, HD10, and HD11 Trials. J Clin Oncol 2017;35:1999-2007. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2016.70.9410

Lymphoma.citation: Borchmann et al, JCO, 2011 Tresckow et al, Lancet Haematol, 2018
Lymphoma.patients: newly diagnosed advanced stage HL
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ BEACOPPesc x8 vs. ➜ BEACOPPesc x4 + BEACOPPbase x4 Refractory or bulky → ➜ 🏆 30 Gy RT vs. ➜ no RT 1580 were randomized to radiation or no radiation
Lymphoma.results: 5-yr FFTF ~85% in BEACOPP arms, not different 5-yr FFTF 90.4% with RT vs. 87%, p=0.08 ⭐⬆️ On subanalysis of PRs there was 6% FFTF benefit with RT, and no benefit in bulky disease with CR ASCT 4% vs. 5% without RT 10-yr 2nd cancer 10% BEACOPPx8+RT 9% BEACOPPx8 6% BEACOP 4+4 + RT 6% BEACOPP 4+4 ⭐ 10-yr f/u of HD9 and HD12: RT shows PFS and OS benefit in those with residual disease 10-yr PFS 90% vs. 83%, p=0.027 10-yr OS 94% vs. 88%, p=0.025 ⭐⭐ Late causes of death from 2nd cancer plus adverse effects were about double that of deaths from Hodgkin lymphoma ⭐ Long term data after 5 years did not su…
Lymphoma.interpretation: RT improves FFTF in advanced HL with refractory compared to no further treatment. These were able to avoid transplant. BEACOPPesc seems preferred to BEACOPPbase. A combined analysis of HD9 and HD12 at ten years shows PFS and OS benefit with radiation for residual disease.
Lymphoma.commentary: HD15 and onward incorporate PET guided RT as standard based on HD12. A long term combined analysis of HD12+9 showed PFS & OS benefit with radiation. The HD9 portion was not randomized, which could lead to imbalances in the analysis. The mechanism of radiation might be cytoreductive and could also stimulate an immune response. The use of nivolumab in lymphoma shows that lymphoma can be immunogenic (Ansell 2023). Dose & field. Although some guidelines endorse 36-40 Gy for PR (ILROG, Constine 2018), 30 Gy is what was established in HD12 and incorporated into HD15. In HD9 IFRT was given for bulky …
Lymphoma.notes: Advanced HL summary -HD12 showed FFTF with RT in PET+ refractory. EORTC 20884 also showed that RT maintained outcomes in PET+ -There seems to be no benefit thus far with RT after CR per EORTC 20884 -RATHL established use of AVD -ECHELON showed an OS benefit with Br+AVD over ABVD. Few studies have shown an OS benefit to any intervention -HD21 showed a PFS benefit with BrECADD over escBEACOPP. RT was used in PET+ refractory -SWOG1826 showed a PFS benefit with N+AVD over Br+AVD. SWOG's protocol had very strict criteria for the use of RT, thus it was used only in 1% in both arms -Some now use N+AV…

Lymphoma.citation: Gallamini et al, JCO, 2018 Gallamini et al, JCO, 2020
Lymphoma.patients: Advanced stage with LN size ≥5 cm, s/p
Lymphoma.methods: Prospective, with an unpowered RT randomization ABVD x 2→ →PET2 negative Deauville 1-3→ ABVD x 6 total→ →CR PET2&6 negative and bulky size ≥5 cm: ➜ IFRT 30.6 Gy vs. ➜ obs →SER PET2 positive Deauville 4-5→ escBEACOPP (no RT) Primary endpoint: 3-yr PFS of ≥85% for entire cohort
Lymphoma.results: •RT vs. no RT in bulky, PET CR: 6-yr PFS 91-95%, not different Trial was not specifically powered for this randomization No difference in subgroups size 5 cm or 10 cm Median RT dose 30.6 Gy 3-yr PFS in positive PET of 60% 3-yrs PFS in negative PET of 87%
Lymphoma.interpretation: There was no observable difference between no RT and RT in those CR with bulky disease in advanced Hodgkin lymphoma, but this randomization was underpowered.
Lymphoma.commentary: EORTC 20884 is likely underpowered. GITIL/FIL HD0607 delivered IFRT to bulky only CR, but was intentionally not powered to test the role of RT, since according to the authors, lack of published reports were available. In EORTC IFRT to multiple areas may have been too large of a field. EORTC H10, the trial that showed PFS benefit with INRT after CR in early stage HL, had higher powerer wth n=1137, while EORTC 20884 had n=421. Out of concern for high late adverse effects many are hesitant to escalate. In HD12+9 late causes of death from 2nd malignancy and adverse effects were about double that o…

J Clin Oncol 2018;36:454-462. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2017.75.2543 2. Gallamini A, Rossi A, Patti C, et al.
