J Clin Oncol. Published online September 26, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-25-02137

Lymphoma.patients: Stage III-IV HL with CR or PR after MOPP-ABV
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ 🏆 obs vs. ➜ 16-24 Gy IFRT Refractory →🏆 18-30 Gy IFRT
Lymphoma.results: CR in 60% No change in 5-yr EFS or OS 5-yr EFS 77% vs. 73%, p=0.35 5-yr OS 85% vs. 78%, p=0.07 RT in refractory yielded similar outcomes to those with CR 5-yr EFS 79%, 5-yr OS 87% 5-yr 2nd malignancy 5% vs. 8% with RT, p=0.05
Lymphoma.interpretation: IFRT did not improve EFS or OS in advanced HL with CR. RT in refractory led to OS matching those with CR.
Lymphoma.commentary: EORTC 20884 is likely underpowered. GITIL/FIL HD0607 delivered IFRT to bulky only CR, but was intentionally not powered to test the role of RT, since according to the authors, lack of published reports were available. In EORTC IFRT to multiple areas may have been too large of a field. EORTC H10, the trial that showed PFS benefit with INRT after CR in early stage HL, had higher powerer wth n=1137, while EORTC 20884 had n=421. Out of concern for high late adverse effects many are hesitant to escalate. In HD12+9 late causes of death from 2nd malignancy and adverse effects were about double that o…
Lymphoma.references: 1. Aleman BMP, Raemaekers JMM, Tirelli U, et al. Involved-Field Radiotherapy for Advanced Hodgkin's Lymphoma. N Engl J Med 2003;348:2396-406. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa022628

Lymphoma.trial: Hodgkin's Disease Overview Study Group, Leipzig, Germany
Lymphoma.patients: advanced Hodgkin lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: Meta-analysis of 14 trials with 16 comparison to evaluated the benefit of RT with the same chemotherapy or a different chemotherapy than in control arm
Lymphoma.results: -Adding RT to chemo shows 11% benefit in LC. No OS difference -There was worse OS in CR when RT was given -However in comparing the addition of RT to a second type of chemo, there is no benefit to RT, and 8% improvement in OS without RT
Lymphoma.interpretation: RT added to chemo leads to inferior survival if chemotherapy is given appropriately. The use of RT is limited to specific indications.
Lymphoma.commentary: 6 of 14 trials were abstract only. There is the possibility that IFRT fields were too large. Evaluation of limited INRT fields may be warranted.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Loeffler M, Brosteanu O, Hasenclever D, et al. Meta-analysis of chemotherapy versus combined modality treatment trials in Hodgkin's disease. International Database on Hodgkin's Disease Overview Study Group. J Clin Oncol 1998;16:818-29. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1998.16.3.818

Lymphoma.citation: Herrera et al, NEJM, 2024 Castellino et al, JCO, 2026
Lymphoma.patients: age ≥12, Stage III-IV Hodgkin lymphoma (24% were pediatric, age 12-17)
Lymphoma.methods: Phase III ➜ Br+AVD x6 vs. ➜ 🏆 Nivolumab + AVD x6 Refractory at PET6 → RT 30-36 Gy allowed to 1-2 sites at physician discretion if specific criteria were met (see commentary for more details) No specific RT type, such as ISRT or IFRT, was required.
Lymphoma.results: • Entire cohort ⬆️ 2-yr PFS 83% vs. 92% ⬆️ 2-yr EFS 81% vs. 90% Deauville 4-5 in 24% vs. 12% of patients PFS for PR not yet reported Received RT in 0.8% vs. 0.7% Protocol specified RT in 0.6% vs. 0.8% Non protocol RT prior to progression 0.6% vs. 1.0% Grade ≥3 adverse events are in Table S8, but weren't totaled. Selected adverse effects: Grade ≥3 neutropenia 26% vs. 48% Grade ≥3 peripheral neuropathy 8% (Br) vs. 1% Hypothyroidism 7% and hyperthyroid 3% with N+AVD. Approx 0 thyroid disorders with Br+AVD • Pediatric cohort, ages 12-17 3-yr PFS 82% vs. 93% ⭐Stage III 3-yr PFS 91% vs. 98% Stage IV…
Lymphoma.interpretation: Nivolumab + AVD improves PFS compared to Br+AVD. Similar results of pediatric cohort to the entire cohort shows that HL trials and regimens can include both adult and pediatric patients. Consolidative radiation was only used in ≤1%, though the criteria for allowing radiation in the study were extremely strict.
Lymphoma.commentary: SWOG 1826 and HD21 are groundbreaking clinical trials in advanced HL, showing 2 and 4-yr PFS of ≥90%. Adolescents and adults were eligible in SWOG 1826, ages ≥12. 68% had IPI 0-3, and 32% had IPI 4-7. Stage III had an outstanding 3-yr PFS of 98% in pediatrics! This does seem to validate omission of RT in this group and also in Stage I-II treated with this regimen. The use of RT was low in the study. 3-yr PFS in Deauville 4-5 in peds was 43%. In the overall population 12% in the N arm were still Deauville 4-5. This could have been improved with broader use of RT, including less strict criteria …
Lymphoma.references: Study protocol available at: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-25-00203 1. Herrera AF, LeBlanc M, Castellino SM, et al. Nivolumab+AVD in Advanced-Stage Classic Hodgkin's Lymphoma. N Engl J Med. 2024;391(15):1379-1389. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2405888 2. Cheson BD, Ansell S, Schwartz L, et al. Refinement of the Lugano Classification lymphoma response criteria in the era of immunomodulatory therapy. Blood 2016;128:2489-2496. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2016-05-718528 3. Published online January 9, 2026. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-25-00203

Lymphoma.citation: Borchmann et al, Lancet, 2024 Ferdinandus et al, Lancet Oncol, 2025
Lymphoma.patients: adults age ≤60, newly diagnosed stage HL, Stage IIB, IIE, III, or IV (no pediatric patients were enrolled)
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ escBEACOPP x4 (x6 if PET2+) vs. ➜ 🏆 BrECADD x4 (x6 if PET2+) (Brentuximab, etoposide, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, dacarbazine, dexamethasone) Refractory at PET4 or PET6 → 🏆 30 Gy RT
Lymphoma.results: 4-yr OS 98.2% vs. 98.6% ⬆️ 4-yr PFS 91% vs. 94%, p=0.035 ⬆️ PET2 negative 4-yr PFS 93% vs. 97% SER PET2+ 4-yr PFS 88% vs. 90% All three treatment related deaths were in BEACOPP group 2nd malignancy 2% vs. 3% Received RT 15% vs. 14% PFS for RT for PR not reported -Treatment morbidity 59% vs. 42% -Heme morbidity 52% vs. 31% -grade 3-4 organ adverse effects 17% vs. 19% -Organ toxicities resolved fully or to grade 1 in 96% 4-yr gonadal recovery women 73% vs. 95% 4-yr gonadal recovery men 86% vs. 40% 5-yr parenthood men 9% vs. 3% 5-yr parenthod women 19% vs. 17% 1-yr grade 2 peripheral neuropathy 2…
Lymphoma.interpretation: BrECADD has better efficacy than escBEACOPP and less adverse effects. RT was required for refractory. Results in PET+ were also excellent. This approach with radiation for refractory and PET4+ allowed many to avoid transplant.
Lymphoma.commentary: SWOG 1826 and HD21 are groundbreaking clinical trials in advanced HL, showing 2 and 4-yr PFS of ≥90%, an achievement unthinkable not so many years ago. 41% were PET2+ and had six cycles. 30 Gy RT was required in refractory (in 15%) and the high PFS shows that many were able to avoid transplant with this approach. BrECADD plus RT for PR in HD21 has higher PFS than Br+AVD and optional RT in ECHELON - again even those PET positive in HD21 had higher PFS than Br+AVD! In HD21, 54% were IPS 0-2, 47% were ≥3. In ECHELON 21% had IPS 0-1, 53% 2-3, and 26% had ≥4. In SWOG 1826, 68% had IPI 0-3, and 32% …

Lymphoma.citation: Connors et al, NEJM, 2018 Ansell et al, NEJM, 2022
Lymphoma.patients: Stage III-IV Hodgkin lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ ABVD x6 vs. ➜ 🏆 Br+AVD (Br=brentuximab vedotin) x6 Refractory and ≥2.5 cm → 30 Gy optional (counted as PFS event)
Lymphoma.results: ⬆️ 6-yr OS 89% vs. 94% ⬆️ 2-yr PFS 77% vs. 82% ⬆️ 6-yr PFS 75% vs. 82% 2nd cancer 4.9% vs. 3.5% 8-9% received RT Br+AVD had more peripheral neuropathy (4.7% vs. 2.4%), less pulmonary adverse effects (1% vs. 3%), and more febrile neutropenia
Lymphoma.interpretation: Br+AVD had superior OS compared to ABVD. RT was optional for refractory.
Lymphoma.commentary: Br+AVD was also known as A+AVD in the study. Radiation was 30 Gy and given in 8-9% as subsequent therapy. The number of cycles given was not PET adapted in this trial. BrECADD plus RT for PR in HD21 has higher PFS than Br+AVD and optional RT in ECHELON - even those PET positive in HD21 had higher PFS than Br+AVD. In HD21, 54% were ISP 0-2, 47% were ≥3, and in ECHELON 21% had IPS 0-1, 53% 2-3, and 26% had ≥4.
Lymphoma.references: Study protocol: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa2206125/suppl_file/nejmoa2206125_protocol.pdf 1. Connors JM, Jurczak W, Straus DJ, et al. Brentuximab Vedotin with Chemotherapy for Stage III or IV Hodgkin’s Lymphoma. N Engl J Med 2018;378:331-44. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1708984 2. Ansell SM, Radford J, Connors JM, et al. Overall Survival with Brentuximab Vedotin in Stage III or IV Hodgkin's Lymphoma. N Engl J Med. 2022;387(4):310-320. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2206125

Lymphoma.patients: Advanced Hodgkin Lymphoma Stage IIB-IV or Stage IIA with bulky or ≥3 sites
Lymphoma.methods: Noninferiority (PFS within 5% margin) ABVDx2 → if negative PET2, randomized: ➜ ABVD x4 vs. ➜ 🏆 AVD x4 SER PET2+ → escBEACOPPx4 or BEACOPP14x6 Refractory PET6+ → 30-36 Gy RT optional
Lymphoma.results: Negative PET: ABVD vs. AVD 3-yr PFS 85.5% vs. 84.3%, noninferior 7-yr PFS 81.0% vs. 79.2% 7-yr 2nd malignancy 5.1% vs. 5.8% 6.6% received RT 2nd cancer was not increased with RT Positive PET, BEACOPP: 7-yr PFS 66% 2nd malignancy 2.5%
Lymphoma.interpretation: AVD is noninferior to ABVD for interim PET negative advanced Hodgkin's lymphoma. RT was optional for refractory.
Lymphoma.commentary: On the initial publication, 3-yr PFS was not noninferior, but with additional follow-up the 3-yr outcomes eventually became noninferior. RT was 30-36 Gy IFRT and given in 6.6%. Only 9% of those that received radiation relapsed, while the 7-yr PFS of the entire PET+ group was 66% (AHOD2131 protocol, 2023). Interestingly, BEACOPP for those PET+ had less secondary malignancy in this study. The regimen has been adapted by some for use in early stage Hodgkin Lymphoma. However since long term results of EORTC H10 showed that radiotherapy should not be omitted, the regimen is less appropriate. In EOR…

Lymphoma.citation: Engert et al, Lancet, 2012 Engert et al, Hemasphere, 2017
Lymphoma.patients: newly diagnosed advanced stage HL
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ BEACOPPesc x8 vs. ➜🏆 BEACOPPesc x6 vs. ➜ BEACOPP(14) x8 Refractory and size ≥2.5 cm → 🏆 30 Gy
Lymphoma.results: 5-yr FFTF 84% vs 89% vs 85% 10-yr PFS 80%. vs. 85% vs. 84% (5-yr noninferior in 6xBesc and 8xB(14) compared to 8xBesc) 5-yr OS 92% vs 95% vs 95% 10-yr OS 87% vs. 92% vs. 92% (improved with 6xBesc over 8xBesc) 8xBesc had higher deaths due to toxicty 10-yr est 2nd malignancy 10% vs. 7%. vs. 7% 10-yr est 2nd malignancy 8% RT vs. 7% no RT 12-mo NPV with PET 94%. 11% received RT
Lymphoma.interpretation: BEACOPPesc x6 followed by PET guided RT for refractory disease led to improved FFTF and is preferred. PET can guide the use of RT. This allows some to avoid transplant.
Lymphoma.commentary: HD15 maintains RT as standard for PET PR refractory disease based on PFS benefit in HD12, and PFS and OS benefit in HD9 + 12 combined analysis.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Engert A, Haverkamp H, Kobe C, et al. Lymphoma.citation: Engert et al, JCO, 2009 Tresckow et al, Lancet Haematol, 2018
Lymphoma.patients: Advanced Hodgkin lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ COPP/ABVDx8 vs. ➜ baseline BEACOPPx8 vs. ➜ escBEACOPPx8 Refractory → 40 Gy Bulky → 30 Gy
Lymphoma.results: 10-yr FFTF 64% vs. 70% vs. 82% 10-yr OS 75% vs. 80% vs. 86% 15-yr 2nd malignancy 7% vs. 13% vs. 11% ASCT 11% vs. 6% vs. 4% ⭐ 10-yr f/u of HD9 and HD12: RT shows PFS and OS benefit in those with residual disease 10-yr PFS 90% vs. 83%, p=0.027 10-yr OS 94% vs. 88%, p=0.025 ⭐⭐ Late causes of death from 2nd cancer plus adverse effects were about double that of deaths from Hodgkin lymphoma
Lymphoma.interpretation: escBEACOPP yields superior OS and FFTF compared to baseline BEACOPP and COPP/ABVD. A combined analysis of HD9 and HD12 at ten years shows PFS and OS benefit with radiation for residual disease.
Lymphoma.commentary: A later trial HD12 showed benefit with radiation in PR. A combined analysis of HD12 and HD9 showed PFS and OS benefit with radiation for PR.

Lymphoma.patients: advanced Hodgkin lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: Data from patients treated in 25 centers used to created prognostic score
Lymphoma.results: Stage, Hb <10.5, Age >45, Albumin <4, WBC >15, male, Lymphocytes <6 or <8%
Lymphoma.interpretation: 0 points FFP 84%, 1 point FFP 77%, 2 points FFP 67%, 3 points FFP 60%, 4 points FFP 51%, ≥5 points FFP 42%

Lymphoma.citation: Borchmann et al, JCO, 2011 Tresckow et al, Lancet Haematol, 2018
Lymphoma.patients: newly diagnosed advanced stage HL
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ BEACOPPesc x8 vs. ➜ BEACOPPesc x4 + BEACOPPbase x4 Refractory or bulky → ➜ 🏆 30 Gy RT vs. ➜ no RT 1580 were randomized to radiation or no radiation
Lymphoma.results: 5-yr FFTF ~85% in BEACOPP arms, not different 5-yr FFTF 90.4% with RT vs. 87%, p=0.08 ⭐⬆️ On subanalysis of PRs there was 6% FFTF benefit with RT, and no benefit in bulky disease with CR ASCT 4% vs. 5% without RT 10-yr 2nd cancer 10% BEACOPPx8+RT 9% BEACOPPx8 6% BEACOP 4+4 + RT 6% BEACOPP 4+4 ⭐ 10-yr f/u of HD9 and HD12: RT shows PFS and OS benefit in those with residual disease 10-yr PFS 90% vs. 83%, p=0.027 10-yr OS 94% vs. 88%, p=0.025 ⭐⭐ Late causes of death from 2nd cancer plus adverse effects were about double that of deaths from Hodgkin lymphoma ⭐ Long term data after 5 years did not su…
Lymphoma.interpretation: RT improves FFTF in advanced HL with refractory compared to no further treatment. These were able to avoid transplant. BEACOPPesc seems preferred to BEACOPPbase. A combined analysis of HD9 and HD12 at ten years shows PFS and OS benefit with radiation for residual disease.
Lymphoma.commentary: HD15 and onward incorporate PET guided RT as standard based on HD12. A long term combined analysis of HD12+9 showed PFS & OS benefit with radiation. The HD9 portion was not randomized, which could lead to imbalances in the analysis. The mechanism of radiation might be cytoreductive and could also stimulate an immune response. The use of nivolumab in lymphoma shows that lymphoma can be immunogenic (Ansell 2023). Dose & field. Although some guidelines endorse 36-40 Gy for PR (ILROG, Constine 2018), 30 Gy is what was established in HD12 and incorporated into HD15. In HD9 IFRT was given for bulky …
Lymphoma.notes: Refractory HL summary -HD12 showed FFTF with RT in PET+ refractory. EORTC 20884 also showed that RT maintained outcomes in PET+ -Two randomized studies for ASCT, HD-R1 and BLNI, showed EFS and PFS with ASCT. There is NO OS benefit with ASCT in either study -As of this writing, randomized studies evaluating CAR-T in HL have not been completed

Lymphoma.patients: Biopsy proven relapsed or refractory HL
Lymphoma.methods: Phase I/II: Salvage chemo → TLI + IFRT to 150 cGy BID → HD CIV chemo and ASCT TLI was given in AM to 1500 cGy total and IFRT in PM to 3000-3400 cGy total
Lymphoma.results: Most failures were within RT field 5- and 10-yr LC 78% 14/33 infield failures were at sites PET positive after salvage chemo CR to chemo associated with improved OS and PFS 5/10/15-yr OS 73/68/58% 5/10/15-yr PFS 73/69/59% 5/10/15-yr EFS 72/68/58% Secondary malignancy in 9% (5 heme and 3 solid)
Lymphoma.interpretation: TLI has favorable OS in refractory/relapsed HL, especially with CR to chemo. However most failures were in field, and PFS continues to fall long term. New approaches should continue to be investigated.
Lymphoma.commentary: This study is an eye opening evaluation concerning what occurs when radiation is given to biopsy proven relapsed or refractory disease. Given that this was TLI, it makes sense that most failures would be infield, but this is an indication that radiation is not eradicating the relapsed disease. This may mean that some dose escalation would be of benefit, that radiation should be TBI, or that some HL is resistant to radiation. The study is not showing that there is no benefit to radiation - as it is not randomized - but does illustrate that even TLI may not be a cure. HD12 and the HD12+9 analysi…
Lymphoma.references: 1. Paudel N, Schulze D, Gentzler RD, et al. Patterns of Failure and Survival Outcomes after Total Lymphoid Irradiation and High-Dose Chemotherapy with Autologous Stem Cell Transplantation for Relapsed or Refractory Classical Hodgkin Lymphoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2019;104:436-46. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2019.02.007

Lymphoma.patients: Relapsed Hodgkin lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: Dexa-BEAMx2→ ➜ Dexa-BEAMx4 vs. ➜ 🏆 HD-BEAM and ASCT
Lymphoma.results: 3-yr FFTM 55% vs. 34% No change in OS. 3-yr OS 78%
Lymphoma.interpretation: ASCT with HD-BEAM improves FFTF in relapsed Hodgkin lymphoma.
Lymphoma.commentary: BEAM = carmustine, etoposide, cytarabine, and melphalan Both the GHSG and BLNI trials showed benefit with ASCT in EFS and PFS, but neither showed an OS benefit. These randomized trials are the reason why many practioners use ASCT. What is the role of radiation in refractory or recurrent disease? HD12 randomized radiation in advanced HL, and found -5.8% FFTF benefit with RT in those with PR. A combined 10 year analysis of HD12 + HD9 showed PFS and OS with radiation for residual disease. Many clinical trials mandate RT for those PET positive after chemotherapy, including UK RAPID, EORTC H10, HD1…

Lymphoma.methods: ➜ mini BEAM vs. ➜ 🏆 HD-BEAM and ASCT
Lymphoma.results: EFS and PFS were improved with ASCT No change in OS No difference in EFS whether or not radiation was given, but this was not randomized The trial closed prematurely because patients declined to be randomized
Lymphoma.interpretation: ASCT improves EFS and PFS in relapsed Hodgkin lymphoma.

These randomized trials are the reason why many practioners use ASCT. What is the role of radiation in refractory or recurrent disease? HD12 randomized radiation in advanced HL, and found -5.8% FFTF benefit with RT in those with PR. A combined 10 year analysis of HD12 + HD9 showed PFS and OS with radiation for residual disease. Many clinical trials mandate RT for those PET positive after chemotherapy, including UK RAPID, EORTC H10, HD1…

Lymphoma.subtopic: Bridge RT with CAR T cell
Lymphoma.patients: Relapsed Hodgkin lymphoma to receive CAR T cell
Lymphoma.methods: Retrospective CAR T cell harvest → RT → CAR T Most received 20 Gy/ 5 fractions
Lymphoma.results: n=14 had bridging RT outcomes maintained, not different 1-yr OS 67% bRT vs. 63% 1-yr PFS 47% bRT vs. 42% In the RT group, 71% of progression was out of field, and 29% in field adverse effects not different 50% had CR, 35% PR (no difference with RT)
Lymphoma.interpretation: Radiation can be safely administered as a bridge to CAR T cell. Progression is mainly out of field.
Lymphoma.commentary: Randomized trials for CAR-T cell have only been performed in aggressive NHL as of this writing (ZUMA, TRANSFORM, BELINDA). The use of CAR-T in HL is more investigational.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Saifi O, Breen W, Lester SC, et al. Radiation Therapy as Bridging Treatment to CAR T Cell Therapy in Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2021;111:s132. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2021.07.299

Lymphoma.subtopic: Bridge RT with CAR T cell
Lymphoma.patients: Relapsed Hodgkin lymphoma to receive CAR T cell 6/12 were double hit and 6/12 were bulky ≥10 cm
Lymphoma.methods: Retrospective CAR T cell harvest → RT → axi cell Most received 30 Gy/10 fractions or 20 Gy/ 5 fractions 7 had concurrent chemo
Lymphoma.results: 11/12 proceeded to axi-cell infusion 2/12 had out of field progression during bridging 27% had severe cytokine release or neuroadverse effects ORR 82% CR in 45% at last f/u
Lymphoma.interpretation: Radiation can be safely administered as a bridge to CAR T cell. Progression is mainly out of field.
Lymphoma.commentary: Randomized trials for CAR-T cell have only been performed in aggressive NHL as of this writing (ZUMA, TRANSFORM, BELINDA). The use of CAR-T in HL is more investigational.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Sim AJ, Jain MD, Figura NB, et al. Radiation Therapy as a Bridging Strategy for CAR T Cell Therapy With Axicabtagene Ciloleucel in Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2019;105:1012-1021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2019.05.065

Lymphoma.patients: Stage I-II HL, favorable or unfavorable NLPHL were allowed - this study is a subanalysis of these patients (excluded: stage IIB bulky or Stage IIBE)
Lymphoma.methods: 📌 HD16 ABVD x2 → PET → ➜ Standard, non-PET directed: 20 Gy IFRT vs. ➜ PET directed IFRT: if PET positive: 20 Gy IFRT PET negative: no RT 📌 HD17 escBEACOPP x2 + ABVD x2 → PET → ➜ Standard, non-PET directed: 🏆 30 Gy IFRT vs. ➜ PET directed INRT: PET positive: 30 Gy INRT PET negative: no RT
Lymphoma.results: Post-hoc subanalysis of PET negative NLPHLs (from all arms of both trials; nonrandomized) those PET negative who had RT vs observation: 5-yr PFS 100% vs. 83% 5-yr OS 100% in both
Lymphoma.interpretation: RT is recommended for NLPHL in those PET negative after initial chemotherapy.
Lymphoma.commentary: The original randomization was Standard non-PET directed therapy vs. PET directed radiation. This comparison evaluates those who were PET negative who had radiation vs those who did not - this differs from the original study design and is more similar to that of the UK RAPID study.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Eichenauer DA, Bühnen I, Baues C, et al. Interim PET-guided treatment for early-stage NLPHL: a subgroup analysis of the randomized GHSG HD16 and HD17 studies. Blood 2023;142(6):553-560. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1182/blood.2023019939

Lymphoma.patients: NLPHL
Lymphoma.methods: Regression analysis of risk factors to create IPS
Lymphoma.results: IPS factors: age ≥45, stage III-IV, hemoglobin <10.5, and splenic involvement Inceasing score associated with worse PFS, OS, and CSM
Lymphoma.interpretation: IPS score prognositicates NLPHL.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Binkley MS, Flerlage JE, Savage KJ, et al. International Prognostic Score for Nodular Lymphocyte-Predominant Hodgkin Lymphoma. J Clin Oncol 2024. Published online March 26, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.23.01655

Lymphoma.patients: Stage I-II NLPHL
Lymphoma.methods: Multi-institutional retrospective review of outcomes by modality
Lymphoma.results: 5-yr PFS: 91% with RT, 91% chemo + RT, 78% chemo, 73% obs, 81% R+RT, 39% R 5-yr OS excellent in all cohorts -In the RT cohort, worse outcomes with >2 sites and variant immunoarchitectural pattern -Large cell transformation in 4%
Lymphoma.interpretation: If treatment is recommended, RT alone seems preferred in Stage I-II NLPHL. Observation also results in favorable PFS. OS is excellent with all approaches.
Lymphoma.commentary: Low numbers of patients were treated with chemo alone, obs, R+RT, or R alone. Most received RT or chemo + RT. Propensity matched analysis was not performed.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Binkley MS, Rauf MS, Milgrom SA, et al. Stage I-II nodular lymphocyte-predominant Hodgkin lymphoma: a multi-institutional study of adult patients by ILROG. Blood 2020;135:2365-2374. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1182/blood.2019003877

Lymphoma.patients: Stage IA NLPHL
Lymphoma.methods: Retrospective analysis of patients receiving chemo+RT, EFRT, IFRT or R alone
Lymphoma.results: 8-yr PFS and OS CMT: 89% and 99% EFRT: 84% and 96% IFRT: 92% and 99% R 4-yr PFS 81% and 4-yr OS 100% Second malignancy in 6.6%
Lymphoma.interpretation: Chemo+RT, EFRT and IFRT had similar outcomes, therefore IFRT is preferred. Treatment with R alone led to more recurrence.
Lymphoma.commentary: These patients were treated prior to the ISRT/INRT era.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Eichenauer DA, Plütschow A, Fuchs M, et al. Long-Term Course of Patients With Stage IA Nodular Lymphocyte-Predominant Hodgkin Lymphoma: A Report From the German Hodgkin Study Group. J Clin Oncol 2015;33:2857-62. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2014.60.4363

Lymphoma.commentary: NHL summary -Prior to rituxan, three studies looked at benefit of RT. SWOG showed OS benefit, ECOG showed PFS benefit, and GELA found no benefit with RT -With rituxan, RT use has declined -Lysa/Goelams finds no benefit with RT when used with R. But these were very good prognosis -RICOVER shows PFS with RT in extranodal in ages >60. -UNFOLDER found EFS benefit with RT in bulky and extranodal in ages 18-60 and in PMBCL. RT seemed to maintain outcomes when being used for PR -GHSG meta-analysis shows benefit with RT in bony involvement in R era -OPTIMAL>60 investigatesd benefit for RT in age>60 if…

Lymphoma.citation: Pfreundschuh, et al, ASCO, 2017 Pfreundschuh et al, ASH, 2017
Lymphoma.patients: Any stage and any IPI aggressive B cell NHL, age 61-80
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ RCHOP vs. ➜ RCHOP liposomal vincristine x6 cycles for bulky, and x4 for nonbulky PET positive refractory ➜🏆 39.6 Gy RT Compare to RICOVER cohorts, in which RT was required for bulky vs. not required after amendment (not randomized)
Lymphoma.results: 48% were PET positive after chemo When compared to RICOVER-60 cohort, results were noninferior. Interim analysis 50-mos PFS (within OPTIMAL study): PET positive no RT 35% vs. PET positive with RT 70% vs. PET negative no RT 80%
Lymphoma.interpretation: Administering RT only for PET positive refractory disease, and omitting for PET negative, maintains outcomes compared to RICOVER-60. This approach allows some to be spared CAR T.
Lymphoma.commentary: Since this study, German guidelines now recommend radiation only for all refractory (PET positive, PRs) in DLBCL, and no RT for CR, regardless of bulky, extranodal status or age (Oertel 2023). The rationale is further justified by the British Colombia retrospective study (Freeman 2021). And in LYSA/ GOELAMS those with refractory disease on PET treated with RT had similar EFS and OS to those who had CR. This allows patients to be spared CAR T. The study is a cohort comparison and not randomized. Patient characteristics were worse in OPTIMAL, with more IPI 3-5. Despite this results were maintain…

Lymphoma.subtopic: RT to bulky or extranodal, IPI 0-1
Lymphoma.citation: Thurner et al, Hemasphere, 2023 Held et al, Hemasphere, 2023
Lymphoma.patients: IPI 0-1, aggressive B-cell NHL, age 18-60. Of these, only bulky and extranodal were randomized to RT vs. no RT
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ RCHOP-q21d x6 vs. ➜ RCHOP-q21d x6 +39.6 Gy IFRT vs. ➜ RCHOP-q14d x6 G-CSF vs. ➜ RCHOP-q14d x6 G-CSF +39.6 Gy IFRT Only bulky (>7.5 cm) or extranodal sites entered the RT randomization Primary endpoint: EFS
Lymphoma.results: •RT randomization Closed early due to excess failures without RT ⬆️ 3-yr EFS 68% vs. 84% RT (mainly due to lower PR rate in RT arm) PR 11% vs. 2% CR 79% vs. 90% 3-yr PFS 81% vs. 89%, p=0.22 3-yr OS 93% both arms -Results consistent in extranodal -No benefit with RT in CR/CRu on subanalysis ✅ PRs in obs arm who received RT had identical survival. RT may compensate for PR -Grade 3-4 acute adverse effects 1-3% with RT, mostly mucositis and esophagitis 💡 Second cancer 7% vs. 3% (worse without RT) •PMBCL subset (Held 2023) ⬆️ 3-yr EFS 78% vs. 94% (mostly due to PR triggering RT in no RT arm) 3-yr P…
Lymphoma.interpretation: In early bulky or extranodal NHL in ages 18-60, RT improves EFS, but not PFS or OS. RT seemed to compenstate for refractory: these had identical survival to CRs and were able to avoid salvage therapy. There was no benefit with RT in CRs. This study was performed prior to PET era. In PMBCL RT improved EFS but not PFS or OS. Results were consistent in extranodal. There is no difference in RCHOP q14 days or q21 days.
Lymphoma.commentary: DSHNHL: Deutsche (German) Study Group for NHL This trial is the largest to assess the benefit of RT in extranodal and bulky disease in ages 18-60 in NHL in R era. It was conducted prior to PET era. About 70-75% had PET. Whether PET was obtained or not, response was assessed by CT. Secondary malignancy was increased in the obs arm. Salvage therapies could have increased the risk of 2nd cancer. CAR T cell has reported secondary malignancies of 4-16% (Cappell & Kochenderfer 2023). Information is not provided on what salvage therapies were given. The authors write that RT may have helped maintain …
Lymphoma.references: Study protocol [German language]: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10325769/bin/hs9-7-e904-s001.pdf 1. Thurner L, Ziepert M, Berdel C, et al.
