Lymphoma.citation: Pfreundschuh et al, Lancet, 2008 Held et al, JCO, 2014
Lymphoma.patients: Any stage and any IPI aggressive B cell NHL, age 61-80 (Stage I-II=50%, Stage III-IV=50%)
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ CHOP-q14d x6 vs. ➜ CHOP-q14d x8 vs. ➜ R-CHOP-q14d x6 vs. ➜ R-CHOP-q14d x8. ➜ Comparison of 36 Gy IFRT to bulky >7.5 cm and no RT cohorts IFRT was initially required for bulky disease. A later amendment allowed RT to be omitted, and the two cohorts were compared.
Lymphoma.results: In MVA of ITT and adjusting IPI and age >70 with bulky disease, EFS omitting RT is inferior (p=0.005), and there is trend to inferior PFS (p=0.058) and OS (p=0.127). On per protocol analysis, there is benefit to RT in EFS, PFS, and OS, HR 4.3, p=0.002. No benefit to 8 cycles Addition of R to CHOP-14 x6 improved EFS, PFS, and OS. 3-yr EFS 66%, OS 88%
Lymphoma.interpretation: RT to bulky sites improves EFS. On per protocol analysis, there is benefit in OS or PFS. In early NHL in ages 61-80, R-CHOP is superior to CHOP. There is no benefit to more than 6 cycles. PET was not required in this study.
Lymphoma.commentary: The study is a cohort comparison and not randomized. RT is sometimes preferred in this population in particular because patients age 61-80 are less likely to tolerate ASCT and late effects of RT are less likely to be of relevance (Kahl 2014).
Lymphoma.notes: NHL summary -Prior to rituxan, three studies looked at benefit of RT. SWOG showed OS benefit, ECOG showed PFS benefit, and GELA found no benefit with RT -With rituxan, RT use has declined -Lysa/Goelams finds no benefit with RT when used with R. But these were very good prognosis -RICOVER shows PFS with RT in extranodal in ages >60. -UNFOLDER found EFS benefit with RT in bulky and extranodal in ages 18-60 and in PMBCL. RT seemed to maintain outcomes when being used for PR -GHSG meta-analysis shows benefit with RT in bony involvement in R era -OPTIMAL>60 investigatesd benefit for RT in age>60 if…
Lymphoma.references: 1. Pfreundschuh M, Schubert J, Ziepert M, et al. Six versus eight cycles of bi-weekly CHOP-14 with or without rituximab in elderly patients with aggressive CD20+ B-cell lymphomas: a randomised controlled trial (RICOVER-60). Lancet Oncol 2008;9:105-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(08)70002-0 2. Held G, Murawski N, Ziepert M, et al. Role of Radiotherapy to Bulky Disease in Elderly Patients With Aggressive B-Cell Lymphoma. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:1112-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2013.51.4505
Lymphoma.commentary_refs: Kahl BS. Bulky aggressive B-cell lymphoma: to radiate or not to radiate--that is the question. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:1097–1098. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2013.54.1730

Lymphoma.patients: Limited stage non bulky (<7 cm) DLBCL (~95% were IPI 0-1)
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ R-CHOP-14 x4 or 6 cycles + 40 Gy IFRT vs. ➜ R-CHOP-14 x4 or 6 cycles PET used to assess CR/refractory Refractory ➜ 40 Gy IFRT 4 cycles chemo for 0 prognostic factors and 6 cycles for ≥1 factors.
Lymphoma.results: Arms are noninferior 5-yr EFS 89% vs. 92% RT (p=0.18) 5-yr OS 92% vs. 96% RT (p=0.28) OS and EFS of refractory was not different from those with CR 13 relapses in no RT arm, 5 at initial site 10 relapses in RT arm, none in field
Lymphoma.interpretation: R-CHOP alone is noninferior to R-CHOP plus RT in nonbulky limited stage DLBCL. RT for chemo refractory disease led to similar EFS and DFS to those with CR. This approach may spare some from CAR T therapy.
Lymphoma.commentary: This trial tests the benefit of RT in early DLBCL in the rituxan era. The study population was very favorable: 56% IPI 0, 38% IPI 1, 64% age<60. It's already known that low risk patients do very well. Perksy (2018) speculates that higher risk patients might better benefit from RT. RT was used for all patients with refractory disease and led to similar outcomes. RT may have compensated for PR, allowing patients to avoid CAR T.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Lamy T, Damaj G, Soubeyran P, et al. R-CHOP 14 with or without radiotherapy in nonbulky limited-stage diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. Blood 2018;131:174‐181. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2017-07-793984
Lymphoma.commentary_refs: Persky DO. Limited-stage DLBCL: it’s patient selection. Blood 2018;131:155. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2017-11-813915

Lymphoma.patients: Stage I-II DLBCL. 46% Stage II, 42% extranodal, 58% >60
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ NCDB analysis of patients treated from 1998-2012 chemo + RT vs. ➜ chemo alone
Lymphoma.results: OS improved with use of RT Estimated 5-yr OS 82% chemoRT vs. 75% chemo 10-yr OS 64% vs. 55% OS benefit for RT remained after adjustment for immortal times and indication bias (HR 0.66) Use of RT declined from 47% in 2000 to 32% in 2012
Lymphoma.interpretation: Chemo alone was associated with worse OS compared to when RT was given with chemo. Randomized trials are required for confirmation as adjustments for imbalances in database studies cannot remove all confounding variables.
Lymphoma.commentary: NCDB provides OS data only and no info on EFS, FFP, etc. Although an intriguing study, there is no information here regarding CR, PR, or bulky disease. The eras are hand selected and must guestimate was treatments were employed. Caution using large population observational studies: A review found that the conclusions of these were not better than chance at matching randomized trials, and no factors were found to improve conclusions (Soni 2019). This does not necessarily mean the reports worthless - they still contain more data than a single insitution retrospective report for example - but tha…
Lymphoma.references: 1. Vargo JA, Gill BS, Balasubramani GK, Beriwal S. Treatment Selection and Survival Outcomes in Early-Stage Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma: Do We Still Need Consolidative Radiotherapy? J Clin Oncol 2015;33:3710-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.61.7654
Lymphoma.commentary_refs: Soni PD, Hartman HE, Dess RT, et al. Comparison of Population-Based Observational Studies With Randomized Trials in Oncology. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1209-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.01074

Lymphoma.subtopic: RT and R in bony disease
Lymphoma.patients: 9 GHSG trials
Lymphoma.methods: retrospective meta-analysis
Lymphoma.results: RT gave benefit in EFS and trend in OS in patients with bony disease. Benefit retained even in Stage III/IV, ECOG>1, age >60, bulky, and extranodal >1. Rituximab did not give benefit.
Lymphoma.interpretation: RT to lymphomy with bony involvement benefits EFS and OS. PET was not required in this study.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Held G, Zeynalova S, Murawski N, et al. Impact of Rituximab and Radiotherapy on Outcome of Patients With Aggressive B-Cell Lymphoma and Skeletal Involvement. J Clin Oncol 2013;31:4115-22. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2012.48.0467

Lymphoma.patients: DLBCL patients in rituxan era
Lymphoma.methods: Statistical analyses of patients from 7 NCCN centers to create prognostic scoring system treated in rituxan era
Lymphoma.results: Age >40 to ≤60 - 1 point Age >60 to ≤75 - 2 point Age >75 - 3 points LDH 1 to ≤3 - 1 point LDH >3 - 2 points Ann Arbor Stage III-IV - 1 point Extranodal - 1 point Performance status ≥2 - 1 point Validated in an independent cohort from the British Columbia Cancer Agency
Lymphoma.interpretation: This prognositic system is more effective than IPI. It was validated in an external cohort. Low (0-1 pts), 5-yr OS 96%, PFS 91% Low int (2-3 pts) 5-yr OS 82%, PFS 74% High int (4-5 pts) 5-yr OS 64%, PFS 51% High (≥6) 5-yr OS 33%, PFS 30%
Lymphoma.commentary: Better at predicting outcome than prior IPI score, but difficult to memorize. Note that age is the best predictor followed by LDH.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Zhou Z, Sehn LH, Rademaker AW, et al. An enhanced International Prognostic Index (NCCN-IPI) for patients with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma treated in the rituximab era. Blood 2014;123:837. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2013-09-524108

Lymphoma.patients: aggressive B cell lymphoma, age 18-60, stage I-II, normal LDH, ECOG 0-1, non bulky (<7.5 cm)
Lymphoma.methods: Noninferiority of PFS ➜ RCHOP x6 vs. ➜ RCHOP x4 + Rx2 No RT in either arm. Chemo q21d
Lymphoma.results: Results were noninferior 3-yr PFS 93% vs. 96% in RCHOP x4 + R x2 Less heme and non-heme adverse effects in R-CHOP x4
Lymphoma.interpretation: RCHOP x4 + R x2 may be implemented in favorable risk aggressive B cell lymphoma. The regimen is noninferior to RCHOP x6 and adverse effects is reduced.
Lymphoma.commentary: As an exception, RT was allowed for testicular lymphoma. Note that the eligibility criteria for the study is for particularly favorable types.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Poeschel V, Held G, Ziepert M, et al. Four versus six cycles of CHOP chemotherapy in combination with six applications of rituximab in patients with aggressive B-cell lymphoma with favourable prognosis (FLYER): a randomised, phase 3, non-inferiority trial. Lancet 2020;394:2271-2281. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(19)33008-9

Lymphoma.patients: Any stage receiving local therapy for definitive or curative treatment (82% DLBCL, 67% Stage I-II, 10% received R)
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ Aggressive NHL 30 Gy/ 15 fx Indolent NHL 24 Gy/12 fx vs. ➜ 40-45 Gy/ 20-23 fx for all
Lymphoma.results: No difference in overall response rate, LF, PFS, or OS. adverse effects trended to worse in the high dose arms Aggressive NHL: 5-yr FFLP 82% vs. 84% (NS) 5-yr OS 64% vs. 68% (NS)
Lymphoma.interpretation: 30 Gy is appropriate dose for aggressive NHL, and 24 Gy for indolent NHL.
Lymphoma.commentary: Some patients had salvage or palliative RT. No data on chemotherapy given, few with R. No PET imaging
Lymphoma.references: 1. Lowry L, Smith P, Qian W, et al. Reduced dose radiotherapy for local control in non-Hodgkin lymphoma: A randomised phase III trial. Radiother Oncol 2011;100:86-92. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2011.05.013

Lymphoma.patients: age >60 with Stage I-II aggressive lymphoma and IPI=0
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ CHOP x4 + 40 Gy IFRT vs. ➜ CHOP x4
Lymphoma.results: 5-yr EFS 64% vs. 61% (NS) 5-yr OS 72% vs. 68% (NS) Isolated LR 21% vs. 47% Any LR: 34% vs. 63% Distant recurrence: 79% vs. 53%
Lymphoma.interpretation: Adding RT to chemo did not improve EFS or OS in elderly patients with NHL.
Lymphoma.commentary: Prior to rituxan and PET era. PFS might be a surrogate for OS (Shi 2018). A low risk group: eldery with IPI of 0 and normal LDH. In field recurrence was 34%, indicating possible poor quality RT: 12% did not receive RT. Only half of patients started RT before week 7. Terminated early due to presumed futility and R release.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Bonnet C, Fillet G, Mounier N, et al. CHOP Alone Compared With CHOP Plus Radiotherapy for Localized Aggressive Lymphoma in Elderly Patients: A Study by the Groupe d’Etude des Lymphomes de l’Adulte. J Clin Oncol 2007;25:787-92. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2006.07.0722
Lymphoma.commentary_refs: Shi Q, Schmitz N, Ou F-S, et al. Progression-Free Survival as a Surrogate End Point for Overall Survival in First-Line Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma: An Individual Patient–Level Analysis of Multiple Randomized Trials (SEAL). J Clin Oncol 2018;36:2593-602. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2018.77.9124

Lymphoma.patients: Risky Stage I, or IE-IIE aggressive lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ CHOP x8 + 30 Gy IFRT vs. ➜ CHOP x8 Refractory ➜ 40 Gy IFRT
Lymphoma.results: In CR pts, DFS improved with IFRT 6-yr DFS 73% IFRT vs. 56%, p=0.05 FFS and TTP favored RT, p=0.06 in both Any LR 17% vs. 48% Distant relapse 82% vs. 52% 6-yr FFS 63% in PRs
Lymphoma.interpretation: Adding RT to chemo improves LC compared to chemo alone in NHL, with trend to benefit in FFS and TTP. Giving RT to all PR results in favorable outcomes.
Lymphoma.commentary: Prior to rituxan and PET era. RT arm had more bulky disease: 26% vs. 7%. The LYSA study later repeated the question in the PET and R era and found no benefitt with IFRT. IFRT for all PRs did seem to maintain outcomes. See LYSA commentary: some have commented that the population could have been to favorable to derive a benefit. LYSA and OPTIMAL >60 supports RT in those with PR.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Horning SJ, Weller E, Kim K, et al. Chemotherapy With or Without Radiotherapy in Limited-Stage Diffuse Aggressive Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma: Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group Study 1484. J Clin Oncol 2004;22:3032-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2004.06.088

Lymphoma.citation: Miller et al, NEJM, 1998 Miller et al, Blood, 2001 Spier et al, ASH, 2004, Stephens et al, JCO, 2016
Lymphoma.patients: Intermediate-grade stage I-IIE NHL, including bulky (≥10 cm) stage I
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ CHOP x3 + 40-55 Gy IFRT (most had 40-50 Gy) vs. ➜ CHOP x8
Lymphoma.results: 5-yr PFS 77% IFRT vs. 64% 5-yr OS 82% vs. 72% 10-yr PFS and OS not different At long term f/u PFS never plateaus No infield relapses Late failures occurred out of field in the CHOPx3+IFRT arm
Lymphoma.interpretation: Adding IFRT to chemo improves PFS and OS compared to chemo alone in NHL at 5 years. However the benefit is lost on long-term f/u.
Lymphoma.commentary: Prior to rituxan era. At about 7 years, the curves cross and become NS. PFS never plateaus. Most failures were outside RT field. This study was prior to PET and R era. It offers some support for ISRT in both CR and PR, but the LYSA study later repeated the question in the PET and R era and found no benefitt with IFRT. IFRT for all PRs did seem to maintain outcomes. See LYSA commentary: some have commented that the population could have been to favorable to derive a benefit. LYSA and OPTIMAL >60 supports RT in those with PR.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Miller TP, Dahlberg S, Cassady JR, et al. Chemotherapy Alone Compared with Chemotherapy plus Radiotherapy for Localized Intermediate- and High-Grade Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma. N Engl J Med 1998;339:21-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199807023390104 2. Miller TP. CHOP Alone Compared to CHOP Plus Radiotherapy for Early Stage Aggressive Non-Hodgkin's Lymphomas: Update of the Southwest Oncology Group (SWOG) Randomized Trial. Blood 2001;98:724A:3024. Abstract only available in print. 3. Spier CM, LeBlanc M, Chase EM, et al. Histologic Subtypes Do Not Confer Unique Outcomes in Early-Stage Lymphoma: Long-Term Follow-Up of SWOG 8736. Blood 2004;104:3263. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1182/blood.V104.11.3263.3263 4. Stephens DM, Li H, LeBlanc ML, et al. Continued Risk of Relapse Independent of Treatment Modality in Limited-Stage Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma: Final and Long-Term Analysis of Southwest Oncology Group Study S8736. J Clin Oncol. 2016;34(25):2997–3004. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.65.4582

Lymphoma.patients: Follicular or marginal lymphoma, definitive or palliative intent
Lymphoma.methods: Noninferiority ➜ 24 Gy vs. ➜ 4 Gy
Lymphoma.results: Results not non-inferior 2-yr local PFS 94% vs. 80% 5-yr local PFS 90% vs. 70%
Lymphoma.interpretation: 24 Gy leads to better LC than 4 Gy for follicular or marginal zone lymphoma.
Lymphoma.commentary: Due to slow accrual, the trial opened eligibility to both definitive and palliative patients. Is there still a use for the "boom boom" regimen? Since there is no difference in OS, and 2x2 allows for repeat treatments, some may still opt for the regimen. But the wide improvement in LC, and still short course of 12 fractions, strongly supports 24 Gy for many.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Hoskin PJ, Popova B, Schofield O, et al. 4 Gy versus 24 Gy radiotherapy for follicular and marginal zone lymphoma (FoRT): long-term follow-up of a multicentre, randomised, phase 3, non-inferiority trial. Lancet Oncol 2021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30686-0
Lymphoma.commentary_refs: 1. @DrLesterColl. Lester-Coll, N. Twitter. Available at: https://twitter.com/DrLesterColl/status/1357832722966851585?s=20 Accessed Feb 5, 2021

Lymphoma.patients: Follicular or marginal lymphoma, definitive or palliative intent
Lymphoma.methods: Noninferiority →24 Gy vs. →4 Gy
Lymphoma.results: Results not non-inferior 2-yr local PFS 94% vs. 80% 5-yr local PFS 90% vs. 70%
Lymphoma.interpretation: 24 Gy leads to better LC than 4 Gy for follicular or marginal zone lymphoma.
Lymphoma.commentary: Due to slow accrual, the trial opened eligibility to both definitive and palliative patients. Is there still a use for the "boom boom" regimen? Since there is no difference in OS, and 2x2 allows for repeat treatments, some may still opt for the regimen. But the wide improvement in LC, and still short course of 12 fractions, strongly supports 24 Gy for many.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Hoskin PJ, Popova B, Schofield O, et al. 4 Gy versus 24 Gy radiotherapy for follicular and marginal zone lymphoma (FoRT): long-term follow-up of a multicentre, randomised, phase 3, non-inferiority trial. Lancet Oncol 2021. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30686-0
Lymphoma.commentary_refs: 1. @DrLesterColl. Lester-Coll, N. Twitter. Available at: https://twitter.com/DrLesterColl/status/1357832722966851585?s=20 Accessed Feb 5, 2021

Lymphoma.patients: Stage III-IV follicular lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: Phase II ➜ RT to 2 Gy x 2 or 4 Gy x 1
Lymphoma.results: ORR 92%, CR 61%, PR 31%, SD 6% Median TTLP 25 months
Lymphoma.interpretation: Short course RT for palliation of Stage III-IV follicular lymphoma has favorable outcomes.
Lymphoma.commentary: FORT showed that 24 Gy is better than 4 Gy (Hoskin 2014); however, 4 Gy may still be appropriate in select patients.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Haas RLM, Poortmans P, Jong Dd, et al. High Response Rates and Lasting Remissions After Low-Dose Involved Field Radiotherapy in Indolent Lymphomas. J Clin Oncol 2003;21:2474-80. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2003.09.542

Lymphoma.patients: Follicular lymphoma Stage I-II, low grade. PET not required
Lymphoma.methods: 30 Gy IFRT vs. 30 Gy IFRT + R-CVP
Lymphoma.results: 10-yr PFS 41% vs. 59% 10-yr OS not different, ~90%
Lymphoma.interpretation: Adding R-CVP to IFRT improves PFS. OS is unchanged.
Lymphoma.commentary: Note that IFRT is correctly standard of care in both arms.
Lymphoma.references: 1. MacManus M, Fisher R, Roos D, et al. Randomized Trial of Systemic Therapy After Involved-Field Radiotherapy in Patients With Early-Stage Follicular Lymphoma: TROG 99.03. J Clin Oncol 2018;36:2918-25. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2018.77.9892

Lymphoma.patients: Follicular lymphoma Stage I-II, grade 1-2, era 1998-2012
Lymphoma.methods: NCDB retrospective analysis
Lymphoma.results: OS improved with use of RT 5-yr OS 86% with RT vs. 74% with no RT 10-yr OS 68% vs. 54% RT remained significant on MVA On Kaplan-Meier OS, RT gives better benefit than chemo. Adding chemo to RT provides little benefit
Lymphoma.interpretation: Use of RT in follicular lymphoma is associated with improved OS. Randomized trials are required for confirmation as adjustments for imbalances in database studies cannot remove all confounding variables.
Lymphoma.commentary: NCDB provides OS data only and no info on EFS, FFP, etc. Caution using large population observational studies: A review found that the conclusions of these were not better than chance at matching randomized trials, and no factors were found to improve conclusions (Soni et al, JCO, 2019). This does not necessarily mean the reports worthless - they still contain more data than a single insitution retrospective report for example - but that the eras and parameters should be carefully selected, that the results should be considered something in the realm of hypothesis generating and exploratory, a…
Lymphoma.references: 1. Vargo JA, Gill BS, Balasubramani GK, Beriwal S. What is the optimal management of early-stage low-grade follicular lymphoma in the modern era? Cancer 2015;121:3325-34. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.29491
Lymphoma.commentary_refs: Soni PD, Hartman HE, Dess RT, et al. Comparison of Population-Based Observational Studies With Randomized Trials in Oncology. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1209-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.01074

Lymphoma.patients: newly diagnosed follicular lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: Creation of prognostic scoring system
Lymphoma.results: Risk factors: B2 microglobin elevated, +BM, Hb<12, LN >6 cm size, age >60 0 points 3/5-yr PFS 91/80%, 1-2 points 3/5-yr PFS 69/51%, 3-5 points PFS 51/19% 0 points 3-yr OS 99%, 1-2 points 3-yr OS 96%, 3-5 points 3-yr OS 84%
Lymphoma.interpretation: FLIPI-2 effectively prognositicates follicular lymphoma in the era of immunotherapy.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Federico M, Bellei M, Marcheselli L, et al. Follicular Lymphoma International Prognostic Index 2: A New Prognostic Index for Follicular Lymphoma Developed by the International Follicular Lymphoma Prognostic Factor Project. J Clin Oncol 2009;27:4555-62. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2008.21.3991

Lymphoma.patients: DLBCL patients treated with R-CHOP
Lymphoma.methods: Retrospective analysis of patients treated at British Colombia
Lymphoma.results: Uses same IPI factors: age>60, ECOG PS≥2, LDH>1.5x nl, extranodal dx >1 site, stage III/IV 0 risk factors: 4-yr OS 94%, PFS 94% 1-2 factors: 4-yr OS 79%, PFS 80% 3-5 factors: 4-yr OS 55%, PFS 53%
Lymphoma.commentary: This nomogram is from a single institution and is not externallhy validated. The new NCCN-IPI is validated and was created in the rituxan era (Zhou 2014).
Lymphoma.references: 1. Sehn LH, Berry B, Chhanabhai M, et al. The revised International Prognostic Index (R-IPI) is a better predictor of outcome than the standard IPI for patients with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma treated with R-CHOP. Blood 2007;109:1857. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2006-08-038257

Lymphoma.patients: Follicular lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: Univariate and multivariate analyses to create prognostic index.
Lymphoma.results: FLIPI: age (> 60 years vs ≤ 60 years), Ann Arbor stage (III-IV vs I-II), hemoglobin level (< 120 g/L vs ≥ 120 g/L), number of nodal areas (> 4 vs ≤ 4), and serum LDH level (above normal vs normal or below) 5yr OS 91% if 0-1 factors, 78% if 2, 50% if 3
Lymphoma.commentary: A useful historical reference, created prior to rituxan. The rituxan era FLIPI-2 should now be used (Frederico 2009).
Lymphoma.references: 1. Solal-Céligny P, Roy P, Colombat P, et al. Follicular Lymphoma International Prognostic Index. Blood 2004 104;1258-1265. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1182/blood-2003-12-4434

Lymphoma.citation: International NHL Prognostic Factors Project, NEJM, 1993
Lymphoma.patients: Aggressive NHL
Lymphoma.methods: Regression analysis of risk factors to create IPI
Lymphoma.results: IPI factors: age>60, ECOG PS≥2, LDH>1.5x nl, extranodal dx >1 site, stage III/IV 5-yr OS: Low (0-1 pts) 73%, Low int (2 pts) 51%, High int (3 pts) 43%, High (4-5 pts) 26%
Lymphoma.interpretation: The IPI and age adjusted IPI effectively prognositicates follicular lymphoma.
Lymphoma.commentary: Mnemonic: "APLES" A useful historical reference, created prior to rituxan. The rituxan era IPI should now be used. This study is prior to the R era. To obtain R outcomes, add about 20% to each 5-yr OS value.
Lymphoma.references: 1. A Predictive Model for Aggressive Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma. N Engl J Med 1993;329:987-94. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199309303291402

Lymphoma.citation: International NHL Prognostic Factors Project, NEJM, 1993
Lymphoma.patients: Nodal NHL, Stage I-II
Lymphoma.methods: Regression analyses of factors to create age adjusted IPI for those age <60
Lymphoma.results: Age adjusted IPI for those age <60 Uses just 3 factors from IPI: ECOG PS≥2, LDH>1.5x nl, stage III/IV (not age or extranodal) Low (0 pts) - 5-year OS 83%, Low-int (1 point) - 5-yr OS 69%, High-int (2 pts) - 5-yr OS 46%, High (3 pts) - 5-yr OS 32%
Lymphoma.interpretation: The IPI and age adjusted IPI effectively prognositicates follicular lymphoma.
Lymphoma.references: 1. A Predictive Model for Aggressive Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma. N Engl J Med 1993;329:987-94. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199309303291402

Lymphoma.subtopic: RT to PR bulky in elderly
Lymphoma.patients: Any stage and any IPI aggressive B cell NHL, age 61-80
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ RCHOP vs. ➜ RCHOP liposomal vincristine x6 cycles for bulky, and x4 for nonbulky PET positive refractory ➜🏆 39.6 Gy RT Compare to RICOVER cohorts, in which RT was required for bulky vs. not required after amendment (not randomized)
Lymphoma.results: 48% were PET positive after chemo When compared to RICOVER-60 cohort, results were noninferior. Interim analysis 50-mos PFS (within OPTIMAL study): PET positive no RT 35% vs. PET positive with RT 70% vs. PET negative no RT 80%
Lymphoma.interpretation: Administering RT only for PET positive refractory disease, and omitting for PET negative, maintains outcomes compared to RICOVER-60. This approach allows some to be spared CAR T.
Lymphoma.commentary: Since this study, German guidelines now recommend radiation only for all refractory (PET positive, PRs) in DLBCL, and no RT for CR, regardless of bulky, extranodal status or age (Oertel 2023). The rationale is further justified by the British Colombia retrospective study (Freeman 2021). And in LYSA/ GOELAMS those with refractory disease on PET treated with RT had similar EFS and OS to those who had CR. This allows patients to be spared CAR T. The study is a cohort comparison and not randomized. Patient characteristics were worse in OPTIMAL, with more IPI 3-5. Despite this results were maintain…
Lymphoma.notes: Refractory NHL summary -OPTIMAL>60 found RT maintained outcomes in PET+ refractory PR. RT is recommended for PET positive. -PARMA showed OS benefit with ASCT -ZUMA and TRANSFORM found EFS benefit with CAR T cell -Some reports outline techniques to use RT as a bridging tool while awaiting CAR T cell infusion
Lymphoma.references: 1. Pfreundschuh M, Christofyllakis K, Altmann B, et al. Radiotherapy to bulky disease PET-negative after immunochemotherapy in elderly DLBCL patients: Results of a planned interim analysis of the first 187 patients with bulky disease treated in the OPTIMAL>60 study of the DSHNHL. J Clin Oncol 2017;35:7506-. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2017.35.15_suppl.7506 Press release: https://lymphomahub.com/medical-information/asco-2017-dlbcl-interim-results-of-the-phase-iii-optimalgreater60-trial-and-identification-of-clinically-relevant-copy-number-alterations-in-patient-plasma-samples
Lymphoma.commentary_refs: 1. Oertel M, Berdel C, Held G, et al. The new German evidence-based guideline on diffuse large B-cell lymphoma-key aspects for radiation oncologists. Strahlenther Onkol. 2023;199(2):115-120. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/s00066-022-02035-9 2. Leitlinienprogramm Onkologie (Deutsche Krebsgesellschaft, Deutsche Krebshilfe, AWMF) (2022) Diagnostik, Therapie und Nachsorge für erwachsene Patient*innen mit einem diffusen großzelligen B‑Zell-Lymphom und verwandten Entitäten (AWMF-Registernummer: 018/038OL). Available from [German language]: https://register.awmf.org/assets/guidelines/018-038OLl_Diagnostik-Therapie-Nachsorge-erwachsene-PatientIinnen-diffusen-grosszelligen-B-Zell-Lymphom-verwandten-Entitaeten-DLBC-2022-10.pdf Accessed May 2, 2024.

Lymphoma.patients: Relapsed NHL
Lymphoma.methods: Phase III →chemo x2 cycles→ ➜ DHAP x4 ± IFRT 35 Gy/20 fx to bulky vs. ➜ ± 26 Gy IFRT for bulky >5cm or extranodal T3/4 →🏆HD BEAC + ASCT
Lymphoma.results: 5-yr OS 32% vs. 53% 5-yr EFS 12% vs. 44% ORR 44% vs. 84% Grade 5 adverse effects n=0 vs. 3 Relapsed infield ASCT group: 18% IFRT vs. 36% no IFRT non ASCT group: 42% vs. 62%
Lymphoma.interpretation: ASCT improves OS in relapsed NHL.
Lymphoma.commentary: This trial established the role of ASCT in relapsed and refractory NHL. In the ASCT arm, IFRT was given to 26 Gy in 1.3 Gy fractions BID with at least 4 hours between treatments. One might think the PARMA trial was performed in Italy, but it was performed at Lyon, France.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Philip T, Guglielmi C, Hagenbeek A, et al. Autologous bone marrow transplantation as compared with salvage chemotherapy in relapses of chemotherapy-sensitive non-Hodgkin's lymphoma. N Engl J Med 1995;333:1540-1545. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM199512073332305

Lymphoma.subtopic: Bridge RT with CAR T cell
Lymphoma.patients: Relapsed/refractory B-cell lymphomas DLBCL (88%), PMBCL (10%), and others 50% had bulky disease
Lymphoma.methods: Retrospective Bridging RT → leukapheresis → CAR-T cell infusion
Lymphoma.results: Most common regimen was 30 Gy/10 fractions. 35% had comprehensive bridging RT to all sites. This was associated with superior OS 2-yr OS 49% 2-yr PFS 35%
Lymphoma.interpretation: Bridging radiation before CAR-T cell seems to produce favorable outcomes. Comprehensive RT to all sites was associated with improved OS.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Yegya-Raman N, Wright CM, Ladbury CJ, et al. Bridging Radiotherapy Prior to Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy for B-Cell Lymphomas: An ILROG Multi-Institutional Study. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2023;117:s50-51. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2023.06.333

Lymphoma.subtopic: Bridge RT with CAR T cell
Lymphoma.patients: Relapsed/refractory B-cell lymphomas
Lymphoma.methods: Retrospective Bridging RT or salvage RT with CAR-T cell
Lymphoma.results: Most common bridge RT dose 20 Gy/5 fx Most common salvage RT dose 20 Gy/5 fx, or 36 Gy/12 fx for comprehensive RT Bridge RT vs. salvage RT after CAR-T cell 1-yr infield failure 13% vs. 32% 1-yr LC 84% vs. 62% CR 70% vs. 32% Bridge RT ORR 97% on 30-day PET Among salvage RT with limited sites, better outcomes with comprehensive RT vs. focal RT 1-yr OS 51% vs. 12% 1-yr FFP 31% vs. 0% 1-yr EFS 19% vs. 0%
Lymphoma.interpretation: Bridging RT seems to result in better outcomes compared to salvage RT, despite lower dose and more bulky disease. Salvage RT in limited sites is also effective when RT is given to all sites.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Saifi O, Breen WG, Lester SC, et al. Don't Put the CART Before the Horse: The Role of Radiation Therapy in Peri-CAR T-cell Therapy for Aggressive B-cell Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2023;116:999-1007. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2022.12.017
Lymphoma.commentary_refs: 1. Parikh RR, Milgrom SA, Campbell BA. Pushing the CART to the Finish Line: Integrating Radiation Therapy Into Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy Programs to Improve Outcomes for Patients With Relapsed/Refractory Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2024;118:1152-1158. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2023.03.076

Lymphoma.subtopic: Bridge RT with CAR T cell
Lymphoma.patients: relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma 55% had bridging therapy. Those who received bridging had worse performance and disease
Lymphoma.methods: Retrospective Bridging RT, bridging chemo, or no bridge → leukapheresis → axi-cell CAR-T cell infusion
Lymphoma.results: Median RT dose 35 Gy, median dose per fx 2.5 Gy comprehensive RT vs. focal RT 1-yr PFS 57% vs. 17% After excluding those who did not receive axi-cell, there was no difference in bridge vs no bidge outcomes, but favored bridging 1-yr PFS 29% vs. 44%, p=0.05 CR 82% bridge RT only vs. 38% chemo bridge 100% who had bridgeRT underwent axi-cell With chemo bridge alone, 74% had axi-cell 1-yr PFS 40% no RT and 21% bridge RT 1-yr OS 65% and 48%
Lymphoma.interpretation: Bridging radiation before CAR-T cell seems to produce favorable outcomes. Comprehensive RT to all sites was associated with improved PFS.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Pinnix CC, Gunther JR, Dabaja BS, et al. Bridging therapy prior to axicabtagene ciloleucel for relapsed/refractory large B-cell lymphoma. Blood Adv 2020;4:2871-2883. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1182/bloodadvances.2020001837
Lymphoma.commentary_refs: 1. Parikh RR, Milgrom SA, Campbell BA. Pushing the CART to the Finish Line: Integrating Radiation Therapy Into Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy Programs to Improve Outcomes for Patients With Relapsed/Refractory Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2024;118:1152-1158. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2023.03.076

Lymphoma.patients: relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ CAR T Lisocabtagene maraleucel vs. ➜ standard of care RDHAP, R-ICE, or R-GDP then HD chemo and ASCT
Lymphoma.results: Median EFS 10.1 vs. 2.3 mos Neutropenia 80% vs. 51% Prolonged cytopenia 43% vs. 3% Grade 3 cyctokine release 1% Grade 3 neuroadverse effects 4%
Lymphoma.interpretation: CAR T liso cell markedly improves EFS compared to SOC.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Kamdar M, Solomon SR, Arnason J, et al. Lisocabtagene maraleucel versus standard of care with salvage chemotherapy followed by autologous stem cell transplantation as second-line treatment in patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma (TRANSFORM): results from an interim analysis of an open-label, randomised, phase 3 trial [published correction appears in Lancet. 2022 Jul 16;400(10347):160]. Lancet 2022;399:2294-2308. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(22)00662-6

Lymphoma.citation: Locke et al, NEJM, 2022 Westin et al, NEJM, 2023
Lymphoma.patients: relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ CAR T axicabtagene ciloleucel vs. ➜ 2nd line chemo then HD chemo and ASCT
Lymphoma.results: Median OS not reached vs. 31.1 mos est 4-yr OS 55% vs. 46% Median PFS 14.7 vs. 3.7 mos est 4-yr PFS 42% vs. 24% CR 65% vs. 32% Grade ≥3 adverse effects 91% vs. 83% Grade ≥3 cytokine release 3% Grade ≥3 neuroadverse effects 21%
Lymphoma.interpretation: CAR T axi cell markedly improves EFS compared to SOC. There are high adverse effects.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Locke FL, Miklos DB, Jacobson CA, et al. Axicabtagene Ciloleucel as Second-Line Therapy for Large B-Cell Lymphoma. N Engl J Med 2022;386:640-654. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2116133 2. Westin JR, Oluwole OO, Kersten MJ, et al. Survival with Axicabtagene Ciloleucel in Large B-Cell Lymphoma. N Engl J Med 2023;389:148-157. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2301665

Lymphoma.patients: relapsed or refractory aggressive B-cell lymphoma
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ CAR T tisagenlecleucel vs. ➜ 2nd line chemo then HD chemo and ASCT
Lymphoma.results: Median EFS 3.0 mos in both arms, not different Response 46% vs. 42%
Lymphoma.interpretation: CAR T tisagenlecleucel was not superior to standard salvage therapy.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Bishop MR, Dickinson M, Purtill D, et al. Second-Line Tisagenlecleucel or Standard Care in Aggressive B-Cell Lymphoma. N Engl J Med 2022;386:629-639. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2116596

Lymphoma.patients: Gastric MALT lymphoma Stage IE, H pylori+
Lymphoma.methods: Prospective. After H pylori eradication with amoxicillin and omeprazole (2nd line omeprazole, metronidazole, and clarithromycin if needed), patients get EGD and PCR
Lymphoma.results: CR in 80%. Of those who achieved CR, EFS 98% (events: relapse, residual disease, death. 3/116 with relapse). Of 16 with residual disease, 100% with CR after 2nd line antibiotics. 7/120 deaths.
Lymphoma.interpretation: Antibiotics in H pylori+ gastric MALT lead to excellent control.
Lymphoma.commentary: Risk factors: t(11;18), RR 3 ongoing monoclonality, RR 6 Deep invasion of gastric wall Perigastric nodes
Lymphoma.references: 1. Wündisch T, Thiede C, Morgner A, et al. Long-Term Follow-Up of Gastric MALT Lymphoma After Helicobacter Pylori Eradication. J Clin Oncol 2005;23:8018-24. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2005.02.3903

Lymphoma.patients: nasal NKT cell lymphoma, 72% localized.
Lymphoma.methods: Retrospective review, Japan, multi-institutional
Lymphoma.results: 5-yr OS localized 68%, 5-yr OS advanced 24% RT-DeVIC 5-yr OS 72% and 5-yr PFS 61% IL-2 is prognostic factor for worse OS and PFS RT-DeVIC most commonly used in localized, in 66%. L-asparaginase most commonly used for adv (in 30%)
Lymphoma.interpretation: RT concurrent with DeVIC leads to favorable outcomes.
Lymphoma.commentary: Current largest resource for NKT cell lymphoma outcomes
Lymphoma.references: 1. Yamaguchi M, Suzuki R, Oguchi M, et al. Treatments and Outcomes of Patients With Extranodal Natural Killer/T-Cell Lymphoma Diagnosed Between 2000 and 2013: A Cooperative Study in Japan. J Clin Oncol 2017;35:32-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2016.68.1619

Lymphoma.citation: Yamaguchi et al, JCO, 2009, Yamaguchi et al, JCO, 2012
Lymphoma.patients: nasal NKT cell lymphoma Stage I-IIE
Lymphoma.methods: 50 Gy/25 fx (to lesion, nasal cavity, and paranasal sinuses, with LN if involved) + DeVIC chemo (dex, etoposide, ifosphamide, carboplatin x3)
Lymphoma.results: 2-yr OS 78% (compared to historical control of RT alone 45%) 5-yr OS 70% (compared to historical control of RT alone 40%) 77% CR 5y PTV control rate 94% (Only 2 in field failures).
Lymphoma.interpretation: RT concurrent with DeVIC has excellent local control and low adverse effects.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Yamaguchi M, Tobinai K, Oguchi M, et al. Phase I/II Study of Concurrent Chemoradiotherapy for Localized Nasal Natural Killer/T-Cell Lymphoma: Japan Clinical Oncology Group Study JCOG0211. J Clin Oncol 2009;27:5594-600. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2009.23.8295 2. Yamaguchi M, Tobinai K, Oguchi M, et al. Concurrent Chemoradiotherapy for Localized Nasal Natural Killer/T-Cell Lymphoma: An Updated Analysis of the Japan Clinical Oncology Group Study JCOG0211. J Clin Oncol 2012;30:(32)4044-4046. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2012.45.6541

Lymphoma.patients: nasal NKT cell lymphoma Stage I-IIE
Lymphoma.methods: Phase II: 40 Gy/20 fx (to mass plus margin, nodes optional) + concurrent VIPD (cisplatin 30 mg/m2 + adjuvant etoposide, ifosphamide, cisplatin, and dex x3)
Lymphoma.results: CR 80% Overall response 100% 3-yr OS 86%, 3-yr PFS 85%
Lymphoma.interpretation: RT concurrent with VIPD has favorable outcomes.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Kim SJ, Kim K, Kim BS, et al. Phase II Trial of Concurrent Radiation and Weekly Cisplatin Followed by VIPD Chemotherapy in Newly Diagnosed, Stage IE to IIE, Nasal, Extranodal NK/T-Cell Lymphoma: Consortium for Improving Survival of Lymphoma Study. J Clin Oncol 2009;27:6027-32. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2009.23.8592

Lymphoma.patients: PMBCL (randomized if Deauville 1-3)
Lymphoma.methods: Noninferiority R+ anthracycline chemo→ if Deauville 1-3 (seen in 51%): ➜ 30 Gy vs. ➜ no RT Deauville 4-5 were treated according to investigator's choice
Lymphoma.results: 30-mos PFS 99% vs. 96% (lower events than expected; noninferiority not reached) 5-yr OS 99% both arms Deauville 4 5-yr PFS 96%, OS 98% Deauville 5 5-yr PFS 60%, OS 75% In Deauville 4, 86% required RT only, and 57% in Deauville 5
Lymphoma.interpretation: Lower events occurred than expected and noninferiority was not reached. Results are excellent in both arms, which may support omission of radiation for those who achieve Deauville 1-3 CR. Longer f/u is awaited. In Deauville 4, 86% were treated RT only, and the overall results of Deauville 4 were similar to those with CR, suggesting radiation leads to excellent outcomes in D4.
Lymphoma.commentary: Although noninferiority was not reached, long term f/u is awaited. PFS favors radiation. Recall that similar findings were reported in early stage PET negative lymphoma at initial follow-up in EORTC H10, and then later reporting showed a PFS benefit with radiation therapy. RT for refractory disease in Deauville 4 seems to maintain outcomes similar to those with CR in the study. 49% did not achieve CR. Various chemotherapy regimens were allowed: CHOP14/21, DA-EPOCH, Mega-CHOP, ACVBP, VACOP-B or MACOP-B.
Lymphoma.references: Study protocol: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO-24-01373/suppl_file/protocol_JCO-24-01373.pdf 1.Martelli M, Ceriani L, Ciccone G, et al. Omission of Radiotherapy in Primary Mediastinal B-Cell Lymphoma: IELSG37 Trial Results. J Clin Oncol. Published online August 19, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-24-01373

Lymphoma.patients: Patiets enrolled on IESLG-26 who had PET results available after consolidation RT
Lymphoma.methods: A secondary analysis of RT effect in IESLG-26 based on Deauville score ➜ R and anthracyline based chemo → PET → Consolidation RT optional (in 82%) CR defined as Deauville Score ≤3
Lymphoma.results: CR (i.e. Deauville 1-3) improved from 74% without RT to 89% with RT After RT, 11% had persistent Deauville score 4-5 At median f/u of 60 mos, no relapses in CR or Deauvile 4 after RT All Deauville score 5 patients had progression in field and died
Lymphoma.interpretation: All patients with Deauville 1-3 after RT had no progression at 5 years in PMBCL. There were also no relapses in Deauville 4 after RT and anthracycline based chemo, suggesting additional therapy is not needed.
Lymphoma.commentary: RT may give benefit in PMBCL patients treated with R-CHOP who have Deauville 4-5.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Ceriani L, Martelli M, Gospodarowicz MK, et al. Positron Emission Tomography/Computed Tomography Assessment After Immunochemotherapy and Irradiation Using the Lugano Classification Criteria in the IELSG-26 Study of Primary Mediastinal B-Cell Lymphoma. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2017;97:42-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2016.09.031

Lymphoma.patients: PMBCL
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ R and anthracyline based chemo → PET → Consolidation RT optional (in 82%)
Lymphoma.results: CR (Deauville 1-2) in 47%, Deauville 3 in 23% Deauville 4 in 21%, and 5 in 9% Deauville 1-2 predicted 5-yr PFS (98% vs 92%) and 5-yr OS (100% vs. 91%) Deauville 3 had equally good outcomes Cutoff of liver uptake (i.e. Deauville 4-5) differentiated outcomes: 5-yr PFS 99% vs 68%, 5-yr OS 100% vs. 83%
Lymphoma.interpretation: Despite low CR rate, 90% expected to be alive and progression free at 5 years.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Martelli M, Ceriani L, Zucca E, et al. [18F]Fluorodeoxyglucose Positron Emission Tomography Predicts Survival After Chemoimmunotherapy for Primary Mediastinal Large B-Cell Lymphoma: Results of the International Extranodal Lymphoma Study Group IELSG-26 Study. J Clin Oncol 2014;32:1769-75. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2013.51.7524

Lymphoma.patients: PMBCL
Lymphoma.methods: Phase II: DA-R-EPOCH (dose-adjusted etoposide, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, vincristine, prednisone, rituximab) and filgastrim without RT
Lymphoma.results: 5-yr EFS 93%, 5-yr OS 97% At 10 mo -14 years follow-up, 2 patients (4%) relapsed and were disease free with RT salvage
Lymphoma.interpretation: DA-EPOCH-R led to excellent outcomes in PMBCL, suggesting routine RT is not needed.
Lymphoma.commentary: Note phase II and small number of patients. This study establishes outcomes for PMBCL treated with R-CHOP and epidemiologic facts. "Dose adjusted" means to adjust doses of chemo frequently per protocol based on changes in CBC.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Dunleavy K, Pittaluga S, Maeda LS, et al. Dose-Adjusted EPOCH-Rituximab Therapy in Primary Mediastinal B-Cell Lymphoma. N Engl J Med 2013;368:1408-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1214561

Lymphoma.patients: PMBCL and DLBCL
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ RCHOP x6 vs. ➜ CHOP x6
Lymphoma.results: In PMBCL patients (n=87): CRu 80% with R vs. 54% PD 2.4% vs. 25% 3-yr EFS 78% vs. 52% 3-yr OS 89% vs. 78%
Lymphoma.interpretation: Adding R to CHOP improves EFS.
Lymphoma.commentary: What is MinT? MabThera International Trial Group What is MabThera? A brand of rituximab
Lymphoma.references: 1. Rieger M, Österborg A, Pettengell R, et al. Primary mediastinal B-cell lymphoma treated with CHOP-like chemotherapy with or without rituximab: results of the Mabthera International Trial Group study. Ann Oncol 2010;22:664-70. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/annonc/mdq418

Lymphoma.patients: Mycosis Fungoides
Lymphoma.methods: Prospective. 12 Gy/ 8 fx in 2 weeks Standford regimen
Lymphoma.results: CR 18%, PR 69%, stable 8%, progression 5% After CR, median time to replapse 7.3 mos Median PFS 13.2 mos (26.5 for IB, 10-11 for Stave IIB-III)
Lymphoma.interpretation: The Standford regimen results in favorable outcomes and low adverse effects.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Morris S, Scarisbrick J, Frew J, et al. The Results of Low-Dose Total Skin Electron Beam Radiation Therapy (TSEB) in Patients With Mycosis Fungoides From the UK Cutaneous Lymphoma Group. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2017;99:627-33. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2017.05.052

Lymphoma.patients: Newly-diagnosed PCNSL, age ≤60
Lymphoma.methods: MTX, BCNU, VP16, R, and prednisone → ➜ WBRT 40 Gy vs. ➜ thiotepa-busulfan-cyclophosphamide ASCT
Lymphoma.results: 8-yr EFS 39% vs. 67% 8-yr OS 65% vs. 69%, not different Balance and neurocognition better with ASCT
Lymphoma.interpretation: ACST improves EFS and neurolocognitive function compared to WBRT in first line treatment for PCNSL.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Houillier C, Taillandier L, Dureau S, et al. Radiotherapy or Autologous Stem-Cell Transplantation for Primary CNS Lymphoma in Patients 60 Years of Age and Younger: Results of the Intergroup ANOCEF-GOELAMS Randomized Phase II PRECIS Study. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:823-833. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.00306

Lymphoma.patients: Newly-diagnosed, HIV negative PCNSL
Lymphoma.methods: MC vs. R-MC vs. R-MTC → ➜ 36 Gy WBRT (+9 Gy for PR) vs. ➜ carmustine thiopeta ASCT
Lymphoma.results: Results for WBRT vs. ASCT: 2-yr PFS 80% vs. 69% (p=0.17) With ASCT, more heme adverse effects and 2 infectious deaths
Lymphoma.interpretation: WBRT and ACST are both effective. PFS and adverse effects outcomes seem to favor WBRT.
Lymphoma.commentary: Lower dose RT might provide more favorable outcomes. Although results were not significant in this study, there was a trend toward benefit with RT. 46% had 45 Gy.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Ferreri AJM, Cwynarski K, Pulczynski E, et al. Whole-brain radiotherapy or autologous stem-cell transplantation as consolidation strategies after high-dose methotrexate-based chemoimmunotherapy in patients with primary CNS lymphoma: results of the second randomisation of the International Extranodal Lymphoma Study Group-32 phase 2 trial. Lancet Haematol 2017;4:e510-e23. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S2352-3026(17)30174-6

Lymphoma.patients: Newly-diagnosed, immunocompetent PCNSL
Lymphoma.methods: Phase I and II. Phase II portion: R/ MTX/ LV/ TMZ → WBRT 36 Gy/1.2 Gy BID → TMZ x36 wks
Lymphoma.results: 2-yr OS 80.8% and 2-yr PFS 63.6% ORR 85.7%
Lymphoma.interpretation: This regimen has improved outcomes compared to RTOG 9310.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Glass J, Won M, Schultz CJ, et al. Phase I and II Study of Induction Chemotherapy With Methotrexate, Rituximab, and Temozolomide, Followed By Whole-Brain Radiotherapy and Postirradiation Temozolomide for Primary CNS Lymphoma: NRG Oncology RTOG 0227. J Clin Oncol 2016;34:1620-5. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.64.8634

Lymphoma.patients: Newly-diagnosed, immunocompetent PCNSL
Lymphoma.methods: MTX+ifosphamide → ➜ WBRT 45 Gy vs. ➜ no RT (Cytarabine given for PR with no RT)
Lymphoma.results: WBRT vs. no RT Results not noninferior Per protocol PFS from last MTX 25.5 vs. 12 mos (SS) Per protocol PFS 18.2 vs. 11.9 mos (NS) ITT PFS from last MTX 19.9 vs. 11.9 mos ITT PFS 15.4 vs. 9.9 mos Per protocol median OS ~ 36 mos (NS)
Lymphoma.interpretation: Omission of RT was not noninferior to WBRT. Per protocol PFS outcomes seemed to favor WBRT. OS was similar.
Lymphoma.commentary: Results were not noninferior. Note that PFS was improved. 45 Gy was given for CR and PR. Lower dose RT might provide better outcomes.
Lymphoma.references: 1. Korfel A, Thiel E, Martus P, et al. Randomized phase III study of whole-brain radiotherapy for primary CNS lymphoma. Neurology 2015;84:1242. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1212/WNL.0000000000001395

Lymphoma.patients: Newly-diagnosed, immunocompetent PCNSL
Lymphoma.methods: Phase II: MPV (MTX, procarbazine, vincristine, IT MTX) x5 → WBRT 45 Gy → Ara-C
Lymphoma.results: CR 58%, median PFS 24 mos, median OS 37 mos. Severe delayed neuroadverse effects in 15%. Increased risk in age >60
Lymphoma.interpretation: Chemo with WBRT has favorable outcomes but risk of severe neuroadverse effects.
Lymphoma.commentary: There can be high neuro adverse effects with RT, especially in elderly, and as a result use of radiation has declined over time (Kasenda 2015, Fallah 2016).
Lymphoma.references: 1. DeAngelis LM, Seiferheld W, Schold SC, Fisher B, Schultz CJ. Combination Chemotherapy and Radiotherapy for Primary Central Nervous System Lymphoma: Radiation Therapy Oncology Group Study 93-10. J Clin Oncol 2002;20:4643-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2002.11.013
Lymphoma.commentary_refs: Fallah J, Qunaj L, Olszewski AJ. Therapy and outcomes of primary central nervous system lymphoma in the United States: analysis of the National Cancer Database. Blood Adv 2016;1:112-21. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1182/bloodadvances.2016000927 Kasenda B, Ferreri AJ, Marturano E, et al. First-line treatment and outcome of elderly patients with primary central nervous system lymphoma (PCNSL)--a systematic review and individual patient data meta-analysis. Ann Oncol 2015;26(7):1305–1313. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/annonc/mdv076

Lymphoma.patients: ALL age ≤18 with CR to induction
Lymphoma.methods: ➜ 🏆 12 Gy TBI with etoposide vs. ➜ fludarabine, thiotepa, and either busulfan or treosulfan Noninferiority design, with pre-specified stopping rules for inferiority
Lymphoma.results: Trial ended early due to worse outcomes with chemo 2-yr OS 91% vs. 75% 3-yr OS 90% vs. 71% 3-yr EFS 81% vs. 59% 2-yr treatment related mortality 2% vs. 9% 2-yr relapse 12% vs. 33%
Lymphoma.interpretation: Myeloablative conditioning with TBI leads to profound OS benefit in peds ALL with CR compared to chemo alone.
Lymphoma.commentary: This was a worldwide multi-institutional study performed in the six main continents. As of this writing this is the only randomized trial evaluating TBI in ASCT. Results appear overwhelmingly positive. The incredible survival benefit in this study insists upon implementation and study in other leukemias and lymphomas as well. In pediatrics there is concern regarding late effects of growth impairment, gonadal and cognitive function, cataracts, and secondary malignancies. The authors write that any difference in 2nd malignancy would unlikely washout this marked survival benefit.
Lymphoma.references: Study protocol [radiation planning p51]: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.20.02529/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.20.02529.pdf#page=51 1. Peters C, Dalle JH, Locatelli F, et al. Total Body Irradiation or Chemotherapy Conditioning in Childhood ALL: A Multinational, Randomized, Noninferiority Phase III Study [published online ahead of print, 2020 Dec 17]. J Clin Oncol 2020;JCO2002529. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.20.02529

Palliation.citation: Chow et al, Clin Oncol, 2012 Chow et al, Lancet Oncol, 2014 Chow et al, Radiother Oncol, 2019
Palliation.patients: Bone metastases
Palliation.methods: Meta-analysis of 29 trials in most recent publication
Palliation.results: ↔️ Equivalent efficacy for ORR, CR, pathologic fracture, and cord compression ✅ Multifraction has lower re-treatment rate •Results stable across meta-analyses Single fraction vs multifraction (meta-analysis of 25 trials) ↔️ ORR 60% single vs 61% mult (NS) ↔️ CR 23% vs 24% (NS) ↔️ No difference in pathologic fractures ⛔ Retreatment 2.6x higher with single fraction, p<0.00001 Overall pain relief with re-tx 45% with 8 Gy/1 and 51% with 20 Gy/5 fx ↔️ No significant differences in acute adverse effects
Palliation.interpretation: There is equivalent ORR, CR, pathologic fractions and cord compression efficacy between regimens. Multifraction has lower re-treatment rate. Authors rec no additional funding to be spent on trials.
Palliation.references: 1. Chow E, Zeng L, Salvo N, et al. Update on the Systematic Review of Palliative Radiotherapy Trials for Bone Metastases. Clin Oncol 2012;24:112-24. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clon.2011.11.004 2. Chow E, van der Linden YM, Roos D, et al. Single versus multiple fractions of repeat radiation for painful bone metastases: a randomised, controlled, non-inferiority trial. Lancet Oncol 2014;15:164-71. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(13)70556-4 3. Chow R, Hoskin P, Schild SE, et al. Single vs multiple fraction palliative radiation therapy for bone metastases: Cumulative meta-analysis. Radiother Oncol 2019;141:56–61. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2019.06.037

Palliation.patients: ≤3 painful bone mets from solid tumor, uncomplicated, pain score ≥2/10
Palliation.methods: Phase III ➜ SBRT 20 Gy single fx vs. ➜ EBRT 8 Gy single fx
Palliation.results: 1-mo CR 25% vs. 37% 3-mos CR 24% vs. 33% 3-mos CR and treated per protocol 54% vs. 31%
Palliation.interpretation: SBRT did not improve CR rates at the primary endpoint of 1 month, but did improve CR rates at 3 months for those treated per protocol.
Palliation.references: 1. Mercier C, Billiet C, Ost P, et al. Dose-Escalated Stereotactic Versus Conventional Radiotherapy for Painful Bone Metastases (ROBOMET): A Multicenter, Patient-Blinded Randomized Clinical Trial. J Clin Oncol 2025;43:2164-2172. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO-24-01447

Palliation.patients: 1-3 spine metastasis, localized, NRPS score of ≥5
Palliation.methods: Phase III ➜ SBRT 16 or 18 Gy single fx (as low as 80% coverage permitted) vs. ➜ EBRT 8 Gy single fx
Palliation.results: Pain response seemed better with EBRT, 41% vs. 61%, p=0.99 (one-side p test), and p=0.01 for 2-sided p No difference in adverse effects
Palliation.interpretation: Single fraction SBRT and EBRT to spine show no difference in pain response or adverse effects.
Palliation.commentary: Why was SBRT worse? Possibly due to undercoverage: as low as 80% coverage was allowed with SBRT, and 16 Gy might be too low of a dose. This trial was also created prior to the development of the SINS scoring system for spinal pain, which is more detailed than other scoring systems. EBRT covers the entire bone plus 1 superior and inferior. Use of narcotics was not reported; only pain response. Alternatively there is a possibility that EBRT and fractionation is superior. Dose constraints 10 Gy <10% to 6 mm above and below 10 Gy <0.35 cc to any cord 14 Gy < 0.03 cc max point dose
Palliation.references: 1. Ryu S, Deshmukh S, Timmerman RD, et al. Stereotactic Radiosurgery vs Conventional Radiotherapy for Localized Vertebral Metastases of the Spine: Phase 3 Results of NRG Oncology/RTOG 0631 Randomized Clinical Trial [published online ahead of print, 2023 Apr 20]. JAMA Oncol 2023;e230356. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2023.0356

Palliation.citation: Sahgal et al, Lancet Oncol, 2021 Zeng et al, IJROBP, 2022
Palliation.patients: Painful spine metastasis, ≤3 consecutive vertebra (oligo not required)
Palliation.methods: Phase III ➜ 🏆 SBRT 24 Gy/ 2 fx vs. ➜ conventional 20 Gy/ 5 fx
Palliation.results: ✅ 3-mo pain CR 35% vs. 14% ✅ 2-yr LF 15% vs. 36% Iatrogenic vertebral fractures n=8/12 vs. 4/12 All grade 3 vertebral fractions were with SBRT (n=5/12)
Palliation.interpretation: SBRT to 24 Gy in 2 fractions leads to better CR in pain response than conventional RT in 5 fractions.
Palliation.commentary: Why was this study positive when RTOG 0631 was not? Possibly due to undercoverage allowed in RTOG 0631, or a radiobiological process involving single vs double fraction SBRT
Palliation.references: 1. Sahgal A, Myrehaug SD, Siva S, et al. Stereotactic body radiotherapy versus conventional external beam radiotherapy in patients with painful spinal metastases: an open-label, multicentre, randomised, controlled, phase 2/3 trial [published online ahead of print, 2021 Jun 11]. Lancet Oncol 2021;S1470-2045(21)00196-0. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(21)00196-0 2. Zeng KL, Myrehaug S, Soliman H, et al. Mature Local Control and Reirradiation Rates Comparing Spine Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy to Conventional Palliative External Beam Radiotherapy [published online ahead of print, 2022 Jun 5]. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys. 2022;S0360-3016(22)00550-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2022.05.043

Palliation.patients: Bone metastases
Palliation.methods: Phase II noinferiority ➜ single fx SBRT vs. ➜ 30 Gy/ 10 fx (12 Gy for ≥4 cm, 16 Gy for <4 cm)
Palliation.results: Better pain response and LC with SBRT On subanalysis, only the 16 Gy group had significant response 2-wk CR/PR 62% vs. 36% 3-mos CR/PR 72% vs. 49% 9-mos CR/PR 77% vs. 46% Similar adverse effects and QOL
Palliation.interpretation: In this phase II study, single fx SBRT to bone metastases is noinferior to 30 Gy/10 fx by the prespecified study design. The differences in results seem to suggest SBRT is superior.
Palliation.commentary: The trial is a Phase II noninferiority design. To show superiority, a phase III trial would be required. A later trial, TROG 17.0 (Sahgal 2020) does show superiority in spine lesions but RTOG 0631 did not. The SBRT group in this study all received dexamethasone at time of treatment to reduce pain flair. Pain response was defined as both increased pain scores and increased use of narcotics. Histology was balanced between arms.
Palliation.references: 1. Nguyen Q-N, Chun SG, Chow E, et al. Single-Fraction Stereotactic vs Conventional Multifraction Radiotherapy for Pain Relief in Patients With Predominantly Nonspine Bone Metastases: A Randomized Phase 2 Trial. JAMA Oncol 2019;5:872-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2019.0192

Palliation.patients: Breast or prostate bone metastasis, 1-3 sites, moderate to severe pain
Palliation.methods: ➜ 8 Gy vs. ➜ 30 Gy/10 fx
Palliation.results: CR 15% vs. 18%, NS PR 50% vs .48%, NS At 3 mos, 33% no longer needed narcotics Re-treatment 18% vs 9% in single males Grade 2-4 adverse effects 10% vs 17%
Palliation.interpretation: Single fx and 10 fx RT were equivalent at 3 months with few adverse effects. There were retreatments with single fx arm but less adverse effects.
Palliation.references: 1. Hartsell WF, Scott CB, Bruner DW, et al. Randomized Trial of Short- Versus Long-Course Radiotherapy for Palliation of Painful Bone Metastases. J Natl Cancer Inst 2005;97:798-804. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/jnci/dji139

Palliation.patients: Painful bony mets, no prior RT to site. Stratified single mets and multiple mets (with one painful site)
Palliation.methods: Single met: 40.5 Gy/15 fx vs. 20/5 Multiple mets: 30/10 vs. 15/5 vs. 20/5 vs. 25/5.
Palliation.results: 90% achieved pain relief, 54% complete relief. Fractionation did not affect the frequency of relief. Of those reporting pain relief, in 96% this occurred within 4 weeks
Palliation.interpretation: Low-dose short-course RT is equivalent to long-course for pain relief.
Palliation.references: 1. Tong D, Gillick L, Hendrickson FR. The palliation of symptomatic osseous metastases: final results of the Study by the Radiation Therapy Oncology Group. Cancer 1982;50:893-9. PUBMED: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/6178497/

Palliation.patients: high risk bone lesion: bulky size ≥2 cm, junctional spine or posterior element, hip or sacroiliac joint, long bone with 1/3-2/3 cortical thickness
Palliation.methods: ➜ 🏆 prophylactic RT vs. ➜ standard of care Endpoint: SRE (skeletal related event: fracture, cord compression, surgery, or later pain requiring RT)
Palliation.results: ✅ 1-yr SRE 2% vs. 29% 1-yr SRE (excluding RT) 0% vs. 11% Hospitalizations for SRE n=0 vs 4 Pain also reduced with RT Median OS 1.7 years vs. 1.1 years Most events in junctional spine or bulky disease
Palliation.interpretation: Prophylactic radiation to high risk bony lesions reduced skeletal related events. In this phase II study, OS showed improvement.
Palliation.references: 1. Gillespie EF, Yang JC, Mathis NJ, et al. Prophylactic Radiation Therapy Versus Standard of Care for Patients With High-Risk Asymptomatic Bone Metastases: A Multicenter, Randomized Phase II Clinical Trial [published online ahead of print, 2023 Sep 25]. J Clin Oncol 2023;JCO2300753. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.23.00753 Protocol pdf: https://ascopubs.org/doi/suppl/10.1200/JCO.23.00753/suppl_file/protocol_JCO.23.00753.pdf

Palliation.trial: University Medical Center Utrecht, the Netherlands
Palliation.patients: Bone metastases treated with repeat RT, most with single fx, after initial dose of 4-8 Gy x 1
Palliation.methods: Meta-analysis of 7 trials
Palliation.results: Pain response with reirradiation 58% Subtantial heterogeneity between studies
Palliation.interpretation: Reirradiation is effective for pain relief but ~40% do not benefit.
Palliation.references: 1. Huisman M, van den Bosch MAAJ, Wijlemans JW, et al. Effectiveness of Reirradiation for Painful Bone Metastases: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2012;84:8-14. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2011.10.080

Palliation.citation: van der Linden et al, IJROBP, 2020
Palliation.patients: bone metastasis with pain
Palliation.methods: ➜ 8 mg dex prior to RT, then daily x3 vs. ➜ 8 mg dex prior to RT, then placebo x3 vs. ➜ placebo x4
Palliation.results: pain flair 38% vs. 27% vs. 39%, p=0.07 But flairs in group B did not return to baseline pain flair duration 2.7 vs. 4.5 vs. 3.3 days, p=0.567 pain flair delay by 2-5 days 52% vs. 73% vs. 99% Arm 1 had low scores on days 2-5
Palliation.interpretation: Dexamethasone did not reduce the incidence of pain flairs significantly. The power of the study may be too low for three arms. Dexamethasone did lead to delayed pain flare and lower scores on day 2-5.
Palliation.commentary: The trial had low power: the investigators lowered power when they found that trial accrual was slow. Results differ from the Toronto study, where benefit was found with 4 days of dex (Chow 2015). There was a trend to reduction with dex over 4 days, but also a trend of increased pain flair duration with 4 day dex.
Palliation.references: 1. van der Linden YM, Westhoff PG, Stellato RK, et al. Dexamethasone for the prevention of a pain flare after palliative radiotherapy for painful bone metastases: the multicenter double-blind placebo-controlled three-armed randomized Dutch DEXA study. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2020. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2020.05.007

Palliation.patients: bone metastasis with pain
Palliation.methods: 8 mg dexamethasone at least 1 hour before start of RT (dose single 8 Gy in all) then every day for 4 days after RT vs. placebo
Palliation.results: pain flares improved with dexamethasone: 26% vs. 35%, p=0.05
Palliation.interpretation: Dexamethasone reduces radiation induced pain flare.
Palliation.commentary: Note that even with dexamethasone, 1/4 will experience pain flare with single fraction.
Palliation.references: 1. Chow E, Meyer RM, Ding K, et al. Dexamethasone in the prophylaxis of radiation-induced pain flare after palliative radiotherapy for bone metastases: a double-blind, randomised placebo-controlled, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2015;16:1463-72. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(15)00199-0

Palliation.patients: 1-5 oligometastases
Palliation.methods: ➜ 24 Gy single fx SBRT vs. ➜ 27 Gy/3 fx SBRT
Palliation.interpretation: Single fraction SBRT to 24 Gy for oligometastasis provides better local control than 27 Gy in 3 fractions.
Palliation.commentary: The BED of the single dose is higher than that of the multifraction dose and may have led to the improvement. 88% were bony metastasis. This trial has varying histologies: results may differ by specific tumor types. The trend to improved DM is intriguing, but the trial may not be powered for this endpoint. The patient characteristics, including tumor type, seemed well balanced.
Palliation.references: 1. Zelefsky MJ, Yamada Y, Greco C, et al. Phase III Multi-Center, Prospective, Randomized Trial Comparing Single Dose 24Gy Radiotherapy to a 3-Fraction SBRT Regimen in the Treatment of Oligometastatic Cancer. Int J Radiat Oncol Bio Phys 2020. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2021.01.004

Palliation.patients: Spinal cord compression at one site caused by metastatic solid tumor. Cannot have been paraplegic for >48 hours. Expected survival of 3 months
Palliation.methods: ➜ Surgery with 30 Gy RT vs. ➜ 30 Gy RT alone Endpoint: ability to walk 4 steps Most surgery was anterior approach
Palliation.results: •Surgery improves ability to walk •Ability to walk 84% vs. 57%, 122 days vs. 13 days •If able to walk at entry, 94% vs. 74% ambulating, 153 vs. 54 days •If unable to walk at entry, 62% vs. 19% regained ability to walk, 59 vs. 0 days •Surgical group had lower need for coricosteroids and narcotics
Palliation.interpretation: Laminectomy plus RT improves ability to walk over RT alone.
Palliation.commentary: "Ability to walk" is defined as taking 4 steps. Despite the criticisms of the endpoint, anecdotally it does seem clear that surgery provides at the very least more immediate relief over RT. Anterior approach surgery, performed in most in this trial, is a superior approach to typical posterior laminectomy.
Palliation.references: 1. Patchell RA, Tibbs PA, Regine WF, et al. Direct decompressive surgical resection in the treatment of spinal cord compression caused by metastatic cancer: a randomised trial. Lancet 2005;366:643-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(05)66954-1

Palliation.patients: Metastatic spinal cord compression
Palliation.methods: Nomogram developed to predict ambulatory function after RT and validated in a prospective trial.
Palliation.results: Refer to publication for useful nomogram to predict ability to walk and survival. For outcomes use scoring system that is validated in 2011 publication. There was a benefit to laminectomy plus stabilization followed by RT with all groups combined. Addition of laminectomy did not provide benefit compared to RT alone. Important factors: primary tumor type, interval between tumor diagnosis and MSCC, visceral metastases, motor function before RT, and time developing motor deficits before RT
Palliation.interpretation: This nomogram helps guide treatment opions including best supportive care, surgery, and radiation
Palliation.references: 1. Rades D, Douglas S, Huttenlocher S, et al. Validation of a Score Predicting Post-Treatment Ambulatory Status After Radiotherapy for Metastatic Spinal Cord Compression. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2011;79:1503-6. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2010.01.024

Palliation.subtopic: Single fraction vs. Short course RT
Palliation.patients: Metastatic epidural cord compression
Palliation.methods: ➜ Single fraction 8 Gy x 1 vs. ➜ Short course 4 Gy x 5 Primary endpoint ambulatory status (AS) at week 8 Secondary endpoints: weeks 1, 4, and 12
Palliation.results: •8-week AS 69% vs. 73% (did not meet predefined noninferiority in CI) •Secondary AS at weeks 1, 4, and 12 were noninferior •12-wk OS 50-55%, not different
Palliation.interpretation: Single fraction vs. short course RT for cord compression was not found to be noninferior at the primary endpoint of 8 weeks. However secondary endpoints at week 1, 4, and 12 were noninferior. Altogether the results are similar and suggest effectiveness of the single fraction regimen.
Palliation.references: 1. Hoskin P, Hopkins K, Misra V, et al. Effect of Single-Fraction vs Multifraction Radiotherapy on Ambulatory Status Among Patients With Spinal Canal Compression From Metastatic Cancer. JAMA, 2019;322:2084-2094. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2019.17913

Palliation.citation: Rades et al, JCO, 2016 Rades et al, IJROBP, 2019
Palliation.patients: Metastatic epidural cord compression
Palliation.methods: ➜ Short course 4 Gy x 5 vs. ➜ Long course 3 Gy x 10
Palliation.results: •Patient reported pain relief and distress were noninferior at 1, 3, and 6 months •ORR not different, 87% short vs 90% (NS) •No difference in ambulatory rates, improvement, no further progression, or deterioration 6-mo OS 42% vs 38% (NS) 6-mo local PFS 75% vs. 82% (NS)
Palliation.interpretation: Short course 5 fx RT to 20 Gy is noninferior to long course 10 fx to 30 Gy in metastatic cord compression in pain relief, distress, ambulatory rates, improvement, no further progression, or deterioration.
Palliation.references: 1. Rades D, Šegedin B, Conde-Moreno AJ, et al. Radiotherapy With 4 Gy × 5 Versus 3 Gy × 10 for Metastatic Epidural Spinal Cord Compression: Final Results of the SCORE-2 Trial (ARO 2009/01). J Clin Oncol 2016;34:597-602. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.64.0862 2. Rades D, Šegedin B, Conde-Moreno AJ, et al. Patient-Reported Outcomes-Secondary Analysis of the SCORE-2 Trial Comparing 4 Gy × 5 to 3 Gy × 10 for Metastatic Epidural Spinal Cord Compression. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2019;105:760-764. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2019.08.002

Palliation.patients: Metastatic spinal cord compression
Palliation.methods: ➜ Short course 8 Gy x 1 or 4 Gy x 5 vs. ➜ Long course 3 Gy x 10 or 2.5 Gy x 15 or 2Gy x 20
Palliation.results: 1-yr LC 61% vs 81% 1-yr OS 23% vs 30%, p=0.28 motor function improvement 37-39% (NS)
Palliation.interpretation: Long course regimens lead to improved LC for cord compression compared to short course. Good prognosis patients should be considered for long course RT.
Palliation.references: 1. Rades D, Lange M, Veninga T, et al. Final Results of a Prospective Study Comparing the Local Control of Short-Course and Long-Course Radiotherapy for Metastatic Spinal Cord Compression. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2011;79:524-30. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2009.10.073

Palliation.patients: 1-3 spine metastasis, localized, NRPS score of ≥5
Palliation.methods: Phase III ➜ SBRT 16 or 18 Gy single fx (as low as 80% coverage permitted) vs. ➜ EBRT 8 Gy single fx
Palliation.results: Pain response seemed better with EBRT, 41% vs. 61%, p=0.99 (one-side p test), and p=0.01 for 2-sided p No difference in adverse effects
Palliation.interpretation: Single fraction SBRT and EBRT to spine show no difference in pain response or adverse effects.
Palliation.commentary: Why was SBRT worse? Possibly due to undercoverage: as low as 80% coverage was allowed with SBRT, and 16 Gy might be too low of a dose. This trial was also created prior to the development of the SINS scoring system for spinal pain, which is more detailed than other scoring systems. EBRT covers the entire bone plus 1 superior and inferior. Use of narcotics was not reported; only pain response. Alternatively there is a possibility that EBRT and fractionation is superior. Dose constraints 10 Gy <10% to 6 mm above and below 10 Gy <0.35 cc to any cord 14 Gy < 0.03 cc max point dose
Palliation.references: 1. Ryu S, Deshmukh S, Timmerman RD, et al.
