JAMA Oncol 2023;e230356. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2023.0356

Palliation.citation: Sahgal et al, Lancet Oncol, 2021 Zeng et al, IJROBP, 2022
Palliation.patients: Painful spine metastasis, ≤3 consecutive vertebra (oligo not required)
Palliation.methods: Phase III ➜ 🏆 SBRT 24 Gy/ 2 fx vs. ➜ conventional 20 Gy/ 5 fx
Palliation.results: ✅ 3-mo pain CR 35% vs. 14% ✅ 2-yr LF 15% vs. 36% Iatrogenic vertebral fractures n=8/12 vs. 4/12 All grade 3 vertebral fractions were with SBRT (n=5/12)
Palliation.interpretation: SBRT to 24 Gy in 2 fractions leads to better CR in pain response than conventional RT in 5 fractions.
Palliation.commentary: Why was this study positive when RTOG 0631 was not? Possibly due to undercoverage allowed in RTOG 0631, or a radiobiological process involving single vs double fraction SBRT
Palliation.references: 1. Sahgal A, Myrehaug SD, Siva S, et al. Stereotactic body radiotherapy versus conventional external beam radiotherapy in patients with painful spinal metastases: an open-label, multicentre, randomised, controlled, phase 2/3 trial [published online ahead of print, 2021 Jun 11]. Lancet Oncol 2021;S1470-2045(21)00196-0. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(21)00196-0 2. Zeng KL, Myrehaug S, Soliman H, et al. Mature Local Control and Reirradiation Rates Comparing Spine Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy to Conventional Palliative External Beam Radiotherapy [published online ahead of print, 2022 Jun 5]. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys. 2022;S0360-3016(22)00550-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2022.05.043

Palliation.patients: refractory HCC or liver metastasis, ECOG 0-3, life expectancy ≥3 mos, pain score 4-10/10 and stable
Palliation.methods: ➜ best supportive care vs. ➜ 8 Gy single fraction with zofran and dexamethasone
Palliation.results: 1-mo improvement of hepatic pain by 2 points in 22% vs. 67% 1-mo grade 3-4 adverse effects abdominal pain 3% vs. 9% ascities 3% vs. 6%
Palliation.interpretation: Single fraction palliative radiation therapy to liver improves pain scores.
Palliation.commentary: Although tempting to treat with 5 or 10 fractions, liver patients often are already ill and prone to experience nausea, thus treating single fraction is often a prudent choice.
Palliation.references: 1. Dawson LA, Ringash J, Fairchild A, et al. Palliative radiotherapy versus best supportive care in patients with painful hepatic cancer (CCTG HE1): a multicentre, open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 3 study. Lancet Oncol 2024. Published online September 5, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(24)00438-8

Palliation.patients: pulmonary oligometastasis (133 lesions)
Palliation.methods: ➜ 28 Gy single fx SBRT vs. ➜ 48 Gy/ 4 fx SBRT
Palliation.results: 3-yr FFLF 64% vs. 80%, p=0.13 Higher LF in colorectral tumors 3-yr OS 67-81%%, not different 3-yr FFDM 37% vs. 32%, not different more immune activation with single fx
Palliation.interpretation: In pulmonary metastasis, single fraction radiation is favored. This method leads to no difference in LC, OS, or DFS, and additionally activates the immune system better then multi-fraction RT.
Palliation.commentary: 47% colorectal, ~11% lung, 11% kidney, ~5% melanoma, ~5% sarcoma, 16% other
Palliation.references: 1. Siva S, Bressel M, Mai T, et al. Single-Fraction vs Multifraction Stereotactic Ablative Body Radiotherapy for Pulmonary Oligometastases (SAFRON II): The Trans Tasman Radiation Oncology Group 13.01 Phase 2 Randomized Clinical Trial [published online ahead of print, 2021 Aug 29]. JAMA Oncol 2021;10.1001/jamaoncol.2021.2939. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2021.2939 Protocol pdf in supplements: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2021.2939

Palliation.citation: Jeremic et al, ASTRO, 2017 Rathod et al, ASTRO, 2017
Palliation.patients: Advanced NSCLC stage wet IIIB and IV
Palliation.methods: ➜ platinum chemo x3 vs. ➜ low fraction RT (10 Gy/ 1 fx or 16 Gy/ 2 fx with one wk split) followed by same chemo
Palliation.results: •RT improved Global HRQoL, role and social functioning, pain, dyspnea, N/V, and lung specific symptoms •QOL improved at 2 weeks and was maintained With ITT, no difference in OS Median OS 6.5 vs. 5.8 mos 1-yr OS 33% vs. 19 % 2-yr OS 16% vs. 6% (p=0.19) 1-yr LRPFS 20% vs. 12% 2-yr LFPRFS 9% vs. 3% (p=0.52) Acute grade >3 adverse effects similar and infrequent
Palliation.interpretation: Short course thoracic RT prior to chemo improves QOL. Effect is sustained over time. No impact on palliative RT in outcomes or adverse effects.
Palliation.commentary: Although presented in abstract form in 2017, the peer-reviewed article has not been published.
Palliation.references: 1. Jeremic B, Ghosh S, Fidarova E, et al. The International Atomic Energy Agency Randomized Trial on Chemotherapy With or Without Radiation Therapy in Advanced Non-small Cell Lung Cancer (NCT00864331). Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2017;99:S103-S4. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2017.06.247 2. Rathod S, Jeremic B, Fidarova E, et al. Quality of Life Outcomes in a Phase 3 Randomized Trial of Optimization of Treatment of Advanced Non-small Cell Lung Cancer Using Radiation Therapy and Chemotherapy: IAEA Multicentric Randomized Phase 3 Study (NCT00864331). Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2017;99:S103. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2017.06.246

Palliation.trial: Cross Cancer Institute, Edmonton, Alberta, Canada
Palliation.patients: 13 randomized trials comparing two or more dose fractionation schemes
Palliation.methods: 13 randomized trials comparing two or more dose fractionation schemes. Random effects model used
Palliation.results: High dose RT associated with improved OS and symptom control. Total symptom control 65% low dose vs 77% high dose RT For individual symptoms, results comparable Symptom control improved with BED10 >35Gy 1-yr OS 22% low dose vs 27% high dose Low dose with 1.2-fold chance of repeat RT Dysphagia greated with high dose RT
Palliation.interpretation: Higher dose RT was associated with better OS. Doses of BED10 >35Gy may be warranted but should weighed against adverse effects and time.
Palliation.commentary: There may be some selection bias here that would lead patients with better performance status and prognosis to enroll in trials with longer fractionation schemes.
Palliation.references: 1. Fairchild A, Harris K, Barnes E, et al. Palliative Thoracic Radiotherapy for Lung Cancer: A Systematic Review. J Clin Oncol 2008;26:4001-11. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2007.15.3312

Palliation.citation: Strøm et al, Br J Cancer, 2013
Palliation.patients: Stage III NSCLC with poor prognostic factors and otherwise curative
Palliation.methods: ➜ carbo/vinorelbin x4 vs. ➜ same with 42 Gy/15 starting at 2nd cycle
Palliation.results: OS improved with chemoRT Median OS 9.7 mos vs. 12.6 chemoRT 1-yr OS 53% vs. 34% HRQOL declined during treatment with chemoRT but then remained unchanged
Palliation.interpretation: ChemoRT improved OS compared to chemo alone in Stage III patients not eligible for curative therapy.
Palliation.references: 1. Strøm HH, Bremnes RM, Sundstrøm SH, et al. Concurrent palliative chemoradiation leads to survival and quality of life benefits in poor prognosis stage III non-small-cell lung cancer: a randomised trial by the Norwegian Lung Cancer Study Group. Br J Cancer 2013;109:1467-75. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1038/bjc.2013.466

Palliation.trial: University of Warmia and Mazury, Poland
Palliation.citation: Nawrocki et al, J Thorac Oncol, 2010
Palliation.patients: NSCLC Stage IIIA and IIIB not eligible for radical treatment: tumor >8cm and/or FEV≤40%, ECOG 0-2, tumor related chest symptoms
Palliation.methods: ➜ 30 Gy/10 fx alone vs. ➜ cis/vinorelbine x2 then same RT with concurrent chemo x1 cycle
Palliation.results: OS and PFS improved with chemoRT Median OS 9.0 mos RT vs. 12.9 chemoRT No deaths during tx with RT alone, six with chemoRT PFS 4.7 mos vs. 7.3 mos Response rate 27% vs. 53% (NS)
Palliation.interpretation: ChemoRT improved OS and PFS compared to RT alone in Stage III patients not eligible for curative therapy.
Palliation.references: 1. Nawrocki S, Krzakowski M, Wasilewska-Tesluk E, et al. Concurrent Chemotherapy and Short Course Radiotherapy in Patients with Stage IIIA to IIIB Non-small Cell Lung Cancer Not Eligible for Radical Treatment: Results of a Randomized Phase II Study. J Thorac Oncol 2010;5:1255-62. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/JTO.0b013e3181e15d33

Palliation.patients: locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC not suitable for curative chemoRT
Palliation.methods: ➜ 36 Gy/12 fx alone vs. ➜ 40 Gy/20 fx + cis/vinorelbine
Palliation.results: No difference in Intrathoracic Symptom Burden Index, QOL, or OS
Palliation.interpretation: Chemotherapy added to palliative RT does not improve, or worsen, outcomes.
Palliation.commentary: Perhaps there is no difference in outcomes because chemotherapy might often be started after RT nonetheless.
Palliation.references: 1. Lehman M, Bernard A, See A, King M, Michael M. A Randomised Phase III Trial of Palliative Radiotherapy (PRT) versus Concurrent Chemotherapy and PRT (C-PRT) in Patients with Good Performance Status, Locally Advanced or Metastatic NSCLC with symptoms due to intrathoracic disease who are not suitable for radical Chemo-radiotherapy: Results of the Trans-Tasman Radiation Oncology Group (TROG) 11.03 Trial [published online ahead of print, 2020 Nov 30]. Pract Radiat Oncol 2020;S1879-8500(20)30298-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.prro.2020.11.009

Palliation.citation: Adamson et al, Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol, 2021
Palliation.patients: incurable esophageal cancer s/p stent
Palliation.methods: stent then ➜ 20 Gy/5 fx or 30 Gy/10 fx vs. ➜ no RT
Palliation.results: No difference in dysphagia deterioration, stent complication, re-intervention, or OS Median time to bleeding 49 wks RT vs. 66 wks
Palliation.interpretation: There was no benefit with palliative RT in QOL or OS. There may be benefit with RT to reduce bleeding.
Palliation.references: 1. Adamson D, Byrne A, Porter C, et al. Palliative radiotherapy after oesophageal cancer stenting (ROCS): a multicentre, open-label, phase 3 randomised controlled trial [published online ahead of print, 2021 Feb 18]. Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol 2021;S2468-1253(21)00004-2. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S2468-1253(21)00004-2

Palliation.trial: Sichuan Cancer Hospital & Institute, Chengdu, China
Palliation.patients: Stage IV esphageal cancer
Palliation.methods: ➜ 50-60 Gy/ 25-30 fx with cisplatin and docetaxel vs. ➜ cis/docetaxel alone
Palliation.results: Improved response, PFS, and OS with RT Overall response 83% vs. 47% PFS 9.3 mos vs. 4.7 mos Median OS 18 mos vs. 12 mos
Palliation.interpretation: ChemoRT is associated with improved PFS and OS over chemo alone. Multicenter randomized trial needed.
Palliation.commentary: Long course RT may be an attractive option for local control, but many medical oncologists will prefer short course in order to move toward systemic therapy. The concurrent chemo dosing given with RT is lower than systemic doses. It is also acknowledged that this is a small study published abstract only.
Palliation.references: 1. Li T, Lv J, Li F, et al. Prospective Randomized Phase 2 Study of Concurrent Chemoradiation Therapy (CCRT) Versus Chemotherapy Alone in Stage IV Esophageal Squamous Cell Carcinoma (ESCC). Palliation.citation: Spanos et al, IJROBP, 1989 Spanos et al, IJROBP, 1993 Spanos et al, IJROBP, 1994
Palliation.patients: Advanced pelvic malignancies (Gyn, GU, colorectal, other)
Palliation.methods: Quad shot: Phase II: 3.7 Gy BID x 2 days, repeat q4 wks to total 44.4 Gy in 3 courses Phase III: 2 week vs. 4 week interval
Palliation.results: For patients completing 3 courses: CR 14%, PR 31%, no change 40%, progression 7%, unknown 8% Rest interval of 2 weeks led to more compliance over 4 weeks. No benefit in LC or adverse effects. estimated 1.5-yr late adverse effects 7% [vs. 49% with 30 Gy/10 historical control]
Palliation.interpretation: The "quad shot" regimen leads to favorable outcomes and low adverse effects.

Peds.trial: Chemo regimen? VDC+IE LF worse with size ≥200 mL and axial site
Peds.n: Leavey et al, JCO, 2021 Ahmed et al, IJROBP, 2026
Peds.methods: Localized Ewing sarcoma
Peds.results: ➜ VTC+VDC+IE vs. ➜ VDC+IE Choice of surgery or RT for local therapy Sites that may be more appropriate for radiation: skull, facial bones, vertebrae, acetabuluar pelvis, especially if bulky or would impact appearance, or poor response to chemo 📌 Radiation details Start at week 13 Definitive 45 + 10.8 Gy = 55.8 Gy Pre-op 36 Gy, and if R1 boost to 14.4 Gy postop, if R2 boost to 19.8 Gy Vertebra 50.4 Gy R1 post-op 50.4 Gy R2 post-op 55.8 Gy Resected LNs 50.4 Gy Unresected LNs 45 + 10.8 Gy
Peds.interpretation: No difference between chemo regimens 5-yr EFS 78% 5-yr OS 87% Surgery 5-yr LF 5%, RT 8%, S+RT 6%, p=0.47 For size ≥200 mL vs. <200 mL, LF 11% vs. 4%, p=0.09 On MVA worse LF associated with maximal dimension, axial site, and age Lowest risk of LF in extremity site
Peds.commentary: LF was similar after surgery, radiation, or both. LF was more common with size ≥200 mL and axial site. Regarding the randomization, there was no difference between chemotherapy regimens. Outcomes are improved from prior studies.
Peds.notes: AEWS2431 will evaluate dose escalation to 64.8 Gy for tumors ≥200 mL. A Tata Memorial randomized trial also showed LC benefit with dose escalation for unresectable Ewing sarcoma (Laskar 2022). Note that the results of this subanalysis differ from the 2017 meta-analysis (Ahmed). In the discussion the authors point out that this study was the first to require PET, and that the CTV was decreased to 1.0 cm from 1.5 cm in prior COG trials. Tumor size was poorly documented in prior trials, and thus this data in 1031 allows for a comprehensive analysis of LF by size. Over time there have been advance…

Peds.trial: Low risk Omit INRT after CR? unclear
Peds.methods: Low risk HL: Stage IA, IB, or IIA, age ≤18
Peds.results: Phase II →OEPAx2→ ➜ if PET PR, INRT to 19.8 Gy plus 10 Gy boost to CR or bulky ➜ if PET CR, omit INRT PR was defined as Deauville 1-2 OEPA = vincristine, etoposide, prednisone, doxorubcin RT goal endpoint: 5-yr EFS of 90% in the CR and PR groups
Peds.interpretation: CR, no RT 5-yr EFS 86.5% (less than 90% goal) IFRT for PR 5-yr EFS 88.6% (95% CI was within 90% goal) On subanalysis, the EFS goal was not reached for those with ESR >30 or bulky disease in either CR and PR
Peds.commentary: The trial did not meet its endpoint for the CR portion. Those with ESR >30 or bulky disease in either CR and PR had worse outcomes.
Peds.notes: The EFS for those who had PR and received radiation was numerically higher than those who had CR with omission of RT, which should give caution to the findings. The authors' conclusion was that radiation could be omitted in CR, but the trial did not meet its endpoint for the CR portion. RT is still recommended for PR. PR was defined strictly at the time, as Deauville 1-2. Both EuroNet-PHL-C1 and AHOD0431 are nonrandomized trials regarding the radiation analysis. On 10-yr f/u of AHOD0431 for low risk pediatrics, there was benefit with IFRT in RER and SER at PET1 after AVPC. A prior COG randomiz…

Peds.results: ABVD x2 → PET → RER Favorable/Unfav ➜ ABVDx2 for F, AVDx4 for U vs. ➜ Brentuximab + nivolumab x4 SER Favorable/Unfav ➜ escBEACOPPx2 + 30 Gy ISRT IMRT or protons vs. ➜ Brentuximab + nivolumab x4 + 30 Gy ISRT IMRT or protons

SER. As in essentially all other clinical trials for early stage HL, RT remains mandated for those with SER, anticipated in 15%. The investigators write "Therefore, there is no data to support omitting radiotherapy among patients with SER for patients with Stage I/II disease." RT for SER shows good results in studies, and there is no evidence that response adapted chemo for something that already seems chemo res…

Peds.subtopic: Low risk: Removal of RT in CR
Peds.trial: Low risk Omit IFRT after RER or SER? no
Peds.n: Keller et al, Cancer 2018 Parekh et al, Blood, 2022 Hoppe et al, JAMA Oncol, 2024
Peds.methods: Low risk HL: nonbulky Stage IA-IIA
Peds.results: Phase II AVPC x3 (doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone, cyclophosphamide) [a minimalist regimen] ➜ PET or gallium scan & CT at cycle 1 and 3. If CR/PR on PET3→ ➜ CR: observe and assess for relapse ➜ PR: 🏆 21 Gy IFRT then off protocol ➜ Low risk relapse after CR: 🏆 IV/DECA + 21 Gy IFRT ➜ High risk relapse after CR: off protocol ⭐Post-hoc analysis of SERs and RERs at PET1: ➜ 🏆 those who had IFRT compared to ➜ those who had no RT
Peds.interpretation: 4 year outcomes 49% had minimal chemo and no RT 89% in remission without HD chemo, ASCT, or IFRT 4-yr OS 99.6% 4-yr EFS 78% in CR and 83% in PR/SD ⭐ Low risk relapse after CR: IV/DECA + 21 Gy IFRT 84% avoided SCT 2-yr EFS 84%, 5-yr EFS 81% 10-yr OS 100% no 2nd cancers ⭐Post-hoc analysis by RERs and SERs: RERs (negative PET1): 4-yr PFS 97% IFRT vs. 83% no RT 10-yr PFS 97% vs. 84% SERs (positive PET1): 4-yr PFS 75% vs. 67%, not different 10-yr PFS 81% vs. 64%, p=0.03 In RERs without IFRT, all 14/14 relapses were at initial site, and in SERs without IFRT, 14/16 relapses were at initial site. In S…
Peds.commentary: IFRT improves PFS in both those with rapid early response and slow early response in continued 10 year f/u. In those who did not receive IFRT, almost all relapses were at initial sites. Because some SERs treated with IFRT relapsed in field, dose-escalation for SERs seems warranted. This strategy will be implemented in the next AHOD protocol for low and intermediate risk HL. In those with low risk relapse after CR, IV/DECA + 21 Gy IFRT allowed a large majority of patients to avoid transplant.
Peds.notes: When ISRT was given for low risk relapse after CR, 84% avoided transplant. ISRT is required for SER in open protocol AHOD2131, which evaluates brentuximab + nivolumab. The second publication is a post-hoc analysis of outcomes of those with SERs and RERs at PET1 that did or did not receive IFRT. The planned protocol was based on response at PET3. The radiation portion was not randomized. Such an analysis can create a problem of imbalanced arms, but this is less of a concern in pediatric trials, where populations typically do not have variable comorbidities as adults do and are thought to be mor…

Peds.trial: Advanced stage (adult staging) Nivolumab? yes Less RT? yes
Peds.methods: age ≥12, Stage III-IV Hodgkin lymphoma (24% were pediatric, age 12-17)
Peds.results: Phase III ➜ Br+AVD x6 vs. ➜ 🏆 Nivolumab + AVD x6 Refractory at PET6 → RT 30-36 Gy allowed to 1-2 sites at physician discretion if specific criteria were met (see commentary for more details) No specific RT type, such as ISRT or IFRT, was required.
Peds.interpretation: • Entire cohort ⬆️ 2-yr PFS 83% vs. 92% ⬆️ 2-yr EFS 81% vs. 90% Deauville 4-5 in 24% vs. 12% of patients PFS for PR not yet reported Received RT in 0.8% vs. 0.7% Protocol specified RT in 0.6% vs. 0.8% Non protocol RT prior to progression 0.6% vs. 1.0% Grade ≥3 adverse events are in Table S8, but weren't totaled. Selected adverse effects: Grade ≥3 neutropenia 26% vs. 48% Grade ≥3 peripheral neuropathy 8% (Br) vs. 1% Hypothyroidism 7% and hyperthyroid 3% with N+AVD. Approx 0 thyroid disorders with Br+AVD • Pediatric cohort, ages 12-17 3-yr PFS 82% vs. 93% ⭐Stage III 3-yr PFS 91% vs. 98% Stage IV…
Peds.commentary: Nivolumab + AVD improves PFS compared to Br+AVD. Similar results of pediatric cohort to the entire cohort shows that HL trials and regimens can include both adult and pediatric patients. Consolidative radiation was only used in ≤1%, though the criteria for allowing radiation in the study were extremely strict.
Peds.notes: SWOG 1826 and HD21 are groundbreaking clinical trials in advanced HL, showing 2 and 4-yr PFS of ≥90%. Adolescents and adults were eligible in SWOG 1826, ages ≥12. 68% had IPI 0-3, and 32% had IPI 4-7. Stage III had an outstanding 3-yr PFS of 98% in pediatrics! This does seem to validate omission of RT in this group and also in Stage I-II treated with this regimen. The use of RT was low in the study. 3-yr PFS in Deauville 4-5 in peds was 43%. In the overall population 12% in the N arm were still Deauville 4-5. This could have been improved with broader use of RT, including less strict criteria …

Peds.subtopic: Int & high risk: Removal of RT in CR, COPP vs COPDAC
Peds.trial: Omit RT in CR? yes COPP vs. COPDAC? risk vs benefit
Peds.methods: Intermediate risk: Stage IAE, IBE, IIAE, IIB, IIIA High risk: Stage IIBE, IIIA/B E, IIIB, IVA/B
Peds.results: →OEPAx2 → ➜ COPP (x2 for int, x4 for high) vs. ➜ COPDAC (x2 for int, x4 for high) ➜ if PET PR, INRT to 19.8 Gy plus 10 Gy boost to CR or bulky ➜ if PET CR, omit INRT RT goal endpoint: 5-yr EFS of 90% in those with CR
Peds.interpretation: CR, no INRT 5-yr EFS 90.1% 5-yr EFS 90% COPP vs. 86% COPDAC
Peds.commentary: Results support omission of INRT in those with CR. COPDAC was not as effective as COPP, but has less adverse effects.
Peds.notes: AHOD0031, a randomized study in intermediate risk pediatric HL, also supports omission of radiation in CR. INRT is still recommended in those with PR. Although called IFRT in the protocol, the method is more similar to AP/PA INRT, as I have labeled it here. The protocol has detailed instructions for radiation planning.

Peds.subtopic: Intermediate risk: Removal of RT in CR
Peds.trial: Intermediate risk Omission of RT after rapid early response and PET CR? yes
Peds.n: Friedman et al, JCO, 2014 Dharmarajan et al, IJROBP, 2015 Charpentier et al, IJROBP, 2016
Peds.methods: Intermediate risk HL: [=anything other than Stage IA-IIA nonbulky and Stage IIIB, IVB] Stage IA-IIA with bulky disease or stage IB, IAE, IIB, IIAE, IIIA, IVA with or without bulky disease. Bulky: ≥6cm nodal aggregate or >1/3 ratio for mediastinum
Peds.results: ABVE-PC x2 (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vincristine, etoposide, cyclophosphamide, prednisone) → CT at cycle 2 to assess for rapid early response (RER) of CR or PR, or slow early response (SER). 🔀 RERs: ABVE-PC +2 then CT or PET eval 🔀 CR after RER: ➜ 21 Gy/ 14 fx IFRT vs. ➜ 🏆 obs 🔀 <CR after RER: IFRT in all 🔀 SERs: ➜ 🏆 DECAx2 + ABVE-PCx2 + 21 Gy IFRT vs. ➜ ABVE-PCx2 + 21 Gy IFRT
Peds.interpretation: •RERs with CR: No benefit in EFS with IFRT 4-yr EFS 88% IFRT vs. 84%, p=.11 -For PET defined CRs, EFS 87% both arms -OS 99% in both arms (primary endpoint is EFS) -Those with both anemia and bulky Stage I/II disease benefited from RT, 4-yr EFS 89% vs. 78% •Amongst any responders: First relapse is usually at initial nodal sites, bulky or nonbulky, and rarely out of field •SERs: No SS EFS benefit with DECA (79% DECA vs. 75%, p=0.11) In those PET positive at cycle 2, marginal improvement with DECA (71% vs. 55%, p=0.05)
Peds.commentary: Early response assessment supports omitting RT in RERs with CR and adding DECA to chemo in SERs. However p=0.11 and longer follow-up may be warranted. RERs with CR with initial bulky Stage I-II disease and anemia had improved EFS with RT.
Peds.notes: Longer follow-up is warranted. EFS does trend to favor RT. In a similar study in adults, EORTC H10, 10-yr PFS eventually favored INRT for favorable and was not noninferior for unfavorable. IFRT was 21 Gy in 1.5 Gy per fx, AP/PA IFRT is still required in all SERs and RERs with <CR! How was RER (rapid early response) assessed? RER was sum of perpendicular diameters of <60% of all volumes after cycle 2 CR: ≥80% improvement in sum of perpendicular diameters, or a return to normal size, plus no residual MS mass >2.0 cm, and negative gallium or PET. RER/SER was determined by CT. CR could be determin…

Peds.trial: Advanced stage (adult staging) Nivolumab? yes Less RT? yes
Peds.methods: age ≥12, Stage III-IV Hodgkin lymphoma (24% were pediatric, age 12-17)
Peds.results: Phase III ➜ Br+AVD x6 vs. ➜ 🏆 Nivolumab + AVD x6 Refractory at PET6 → RT 30-36 Gy allowed to 1-2 sites at physician discretion if specific criteria were met (see commentary for more details) No specific RT type, such as ISRT or IFRT, was required.
Peds.interpretation: • Entire cohort ⬆️ 2-yr PFS 83% vs. 92% ⬆️ 2-yr EFS 81% vs. 90% Deauville 4-5 in 24% vs. 12% of patients PFS for PR not yet reported Received RT in 0.8% vs. 0.7% Protocol specified RT in 0.6% vs. 0.8% Non protocol RT prior to progression 0.6% vs. 1.0% Grade ≥3 adverse events are in Table S8, but weren't totaled. Selected adverse effects: Grade ≥3 neutropenia 26% vs. 48% Grade ≥3 peripheral neuropathy 8% (Br) vs. 1% Hypothyroidism 7% and hyperthyroid 3% with N+AVD. Approx 0 thyroid disorders with Br+AVD • Pediatric cohort, ages 12-17 3-yr PFS 82% vs. 93% ⭐Stage III 3-yr PFS 91% vs. 98% Stage IV…
Peds.commentary: Nivolumab + AVD improves PFS compared to Br+AVD. Similar results of pediatric cohort to the entire cohort shows that HL trials and regimens can include both adult and pediatric patients. Consolidative radiation was only used in ≤1%, though the criteria for allowing radiation in the study were extremely strict.
Peds.notes: SWOG 1826 and HD21 are groundbreaking clinical trials in advanced HL, showing 2 and 4-yr PFS of ≥90%. Adolescents and adults were eligible in SWOG 1826, ages ≥12. 68% had IPI 0-3, and 32% had IPI 4-7. Stage III had an outstanding 3-yr PFS of 98% in pediatrics! This does seem to validate omission of RT in this group and also in Stage I-II treated with this regimen. The use of RT was low in the study. 3-yr PFS in Deauville 4-5 in peds was 43%. In the overall population 12% in the N arm were still Deauville 4-5. This could have been improved with broader use of RT, including less strict criteria …

Peds.trial: High risk Remove bleo and add brentuximab?

Peds.trial: Relapsed/refractory Nivolumab plus brentuximab vedotin? yes in this phase II
Peds.n: Harker-Murray et al, Blood, 2023 Daw et al, JAMA Oncol, 2025
Peds.methods: ages 5-30 (children and young adults), relapsed/refractory classic Hodgkin lymphoma with Deauville 4-5
Peds.results: Phase II 📌 Low risk cohort 1L anthracycline-based ≤3 cycles ➜ induction nivolumab plus brentuximab vedotin x4 →if CR, ISRT →if no CR, BV plus bendamustine x2 intensification 📌 Standard risk cohort ➜ induction nivolumab plus brentuximab vedotin x4 →if CR, auto SCT →if no CR, BV plus bendamustine intensification
Peds.interpretation: 📌 Low risk - Transplant avoidance approach CR 82% after nivolumab plus BV x4 CR 93% any time before consolidation ISRT 3-yr EFS 87% 3-yr PFS 95% 📌 Standard risk CR 59% after nivolumab plus BV x4 CR 94% any time before consolidation (nivolumab plus BV ± BV plus bendamustine) 1-yr PFS 91%
Peds.commentary: High response rates were observed with nivolumab plus BV in relapsed or refractory Hodgkin lymphoma. Excellent responses in the low risk group allowed a large proportion of patients to avoid transplant.
Peds.notes: Although phase II thus far, some are interested in this approach to omit radiation. Radiation for low risk relapsed and refractory disease allowed 84% to avoid SCT in AHOD0431, and ISRT is still required for SER in open protocol AHOD2131. The Checkmate 744 approach does not allow those with standard risk and good response to avoid transplant. Response can be difficult to assess after nivolumab. The LYRIC criteria propose instructions for assessing indeterminate response from immunotherapy (see Cheson 2016 - the criteria are too lengthy to outline here). Clinicians however should use caution in…

Peds.trial: TBI vs. chemo? TBI
Peds.n: Peters et al, JCO, 2020 Locatelli et al, ASH, 2022
Peds.methods: ALL age ≤18 with CR to induction
Peds.results: ➜ 🏆 12 Gy TBI with etoposide vs. ➜ fludarabine, thiotepa, and either busulfan or treosulfan Noninferiority design, with pre-specified stopping rules for inferiority
Peds.interpretation: Trial ended early due to worse outcomes with chemo 2-yr OS 91% vs. 75% 3-yr OS 90% vs. 71% 3-yr EFS 81% vs. 59% 2-yr treatment related mortality 2% vs. 9% 2-yr relapse 12% vs. 33%
Peds.commentary: Myeloablative conditioning with TBI leads to profound OS benefit in peds ALL with CR compared to chemo alone.
Peds.notes: This was a worldwide multi-institutional study performed in the six main continents. As of this writing this is the only randomized trial evaluating TBI in ASCT. Results appear overwhelmingly positive. The incredible survival benefit in this study insists upon implementation and study in other leukemias and lymphomas as well. In pediatrics there is concern regarding late effects of growth impairment, gonadal and cognitive function, cataracts, and secondary malignancies. The authors write that any difference in 2nd malignancy would unlikely washout this marked survival benefit.

Peds.trial: Tandem BMT vs. single CEM BMT? Tandem Benefit to RT boost? no
Peds.n: Park et al, JAMA, 2019 Liu et al, JCO, 2020
Peds.methods: Neuroblastoma, high risk
Peds.results: ➜ 🏆 tandem consolidation of thiopeta/cyclophosphamide followed by modified carboplatin/etoposide/melphalan (CEM) vs. ➜ single CEM consolidation 21.6 Gy to primary tumor + 14.4 Gy boost to residual of >1 cc. Compare to 🏆 historical control without boost
Peds.interpretation: 3-yr EFS 62% tandem vs. 48% single 3-yr OS not different nearly half not randomized for various reasons 5-yr LF 11% overall In gross residual disease: LF 16% with boost vs. 11% no boost in historical A3973, not different
Peds.commentary: Tandem transplant results in superior EFS over single. There is no LC benefit with boost in gross residual disease.
Peds.notes: One of few neuroblastoma studies that investigated a radiation question. Since LF was actually worse in this study that used boost, the authors stated that a trial even with larger sample size would be unlikely to show a difference and is thus not warranted.

Peds.n: Matthay et al, NEJM, 1999 Haas-Kogan et al, IJROBP, 2003 Matthay et al, JCO, 2009
Peds.methods: Neuroblastoma, high risk
Peds.results: Induction chemotherapy → surgery (+10 Gy for gross residual) → ➜ myeloablative chemo + 10 Gy TBI + 🏆 ABMT vs. ➜ continuation chemo x3 cycles If no progression ➜ 🏆 6-mos cis-retinoic acid vs. ➜ obs
Peds.interpretation: 5-yr EFS 19% vs. 30% 5-yr OS 30% vs. 39% 5-yr LRR 51% vs. 33% Cis-retinoic acid improved 5-yr OS, 42% vs. 50% No improvement in RFS with cis-RA For patients who received 10 Gy TBI, LR improved compared to continuation chemo, LR 22% vs 52% RT did not increase acute adverse effects except TPN use

Peds.subtopic: Low risk and very low risk
Peds.trial: More chemo for MYOD1 or TP53 mutated?
Peds.methods: • Very low risk, FOXO1 fusion negative: Stage 1 Group I • Low risk, FOXO1 fusion negative: Stage 1 Group II Stage 1 Group III orbit Stage 2 Group I/II Ineligible: FOXO1 fusion negative and age <10 years old
Peds.results: ➜ Very low risk Regimen VA VCR/DACT x 24 weeks -If MYOD1 or TP53 mutation, VCR/DACT/CPM x 36 weeks (Regimen M) at cycle 2 or 3 ➜ Low risk Regimen VAC/VA VCR/DACT/CPM x 12 weeks then VCR/DACT x 12 weeks -Radiation at week 13 if indicated -If MYOD1 or TP53 mutation, VCR/DACT/CPM x 36 weeks (Regimen M) at cycle 2 or 3 📌 Radiation details: -RT at week 13 if indicated in low risk -Dose for R0 is now 36 Gy -Include regional nodes if N+ -If PR, RT replanning allowed at 36 Gy -Delayed primary excision allowed after week 12 (debulking not allowed) -For size >5 cm, boost to 59.4 Gy
Peds.interpretation: Ongoing study Primary endpoint: FFS Secondary endpoint: OS
Peds.notes: Note that very low risk and low risk are now redefined. Prior Subset 2 "low risk" from 0331 is now within eligibility criteria for intermediate risk trial ARST 1431. ARST 0331's prior Subset 1 "very low risk" group is now split into new very low risk and low risk in this trial. The radiation dose for R0 is now 36 Gy. 45 Gy is no longer recommended for orbit. RT dosing R0 resection (Group I): 36 Gy R1 resection or resected nodes (II): 41.4 Gy R2 or biopsy only (III): 50.4 Gy R2 or biospy only size >5 cm (III): 59.4 Gy

Peds.trial: Low risk For subsets 1&2, lower dose of C to preserve fertility? subset 1 yes, subset 2 no For subset 1, shorter duration chemo? yes Avoid RT for group III vaginal tumors with CR? Not with low dose C; return to high dose C
Peds.n: Walterhouse et al, JCO, 2014 Walterhouse et al, Cancer, 2017 Ermoian et al, Pediatr Blood Cancer, 2017 Lautz et al, Pediatr Blood Cancer, 2020
Peds.methods: Surgery first if operable, except orbit and gyn • Subset 1: Stage 1 Group I/II Stage 1 Group III orbit Stage 2 Group I/II • Subset 2: Stage 1 Group III Stage 3 Group I/II age<50
Peds.results: ➜ Subset 1: Shorter duration chemo with VAC (VCR/DACT/🏆reduced dose CPM) x4 then VA x 🏆22 wks reduced duration [compare to 45 wks and higher dose CPM in D9602] then RT as indicated ➜ Subset 2: VAC x 4 with dose reduced CPM then VA x46 wks total [compare to 🏆standard dose CPM from D9602] then RT as indicated 📌Chemo details: CPM is reduced to 4.8 g/m2 for fertility preservation 📌Radiation details: - RT for microscopic or gross residual disease. Dose details in commentary. - RT was eliminated for Group III vaginal tumors who achieved CR to chemo, with or without delayed surgery. Compare to trials…
Peds.interpretation: ➡️ Subset 1: Outcomes maintained 3-yr FFS 89% [comparable to D9602 subgroup A, 90%] 3-yr OS 98% 3-yr LF 7.6%, RF 1.5%, DM 3.4% Orbit CR at week 12: 5-yr FFS 100% Orbit PR/SD at week 12: 5-yr FFS 84% ➡️ Subset 2: More failures than expected 20 failures vs. expected 10.5 3-yr FFS 70%, 3-yr OS 92% For girls GU embryonal, 3-yr FFS 57% Higher LR at vagina site if CR and no RT For all other subset 2: 3-yr FFS 77% [worse than D9602 5-yr FFS 85%, though some lower risk were moved to lower subset]
Peds.commentary: • Subset 1: Shorter duration chemo with lower dose C is preferred. FFS was maintained. COG now recommends a dose of >45 Gy for orbit. There were failures in those with <CR to chemo, followed by 45 Gy. • Subset 2: Higher dose C is preferred. Reduced dose C led to worse FFS. COG recommends to use high dose C in gyn patients and omit RT for CR, even though FFS was worse with omission of RT, to spare organ function. Subset 2 is hereafter integrated into intermediate risk in trial ARST 1431.
Peds.notes: Inclusion critera changed from D9602: All Group II other than Stage 3 Group II were moved from subgroup B to this trial subset 1. RT dosing R0 resection (Group I): no RT R1 resection (IIA): 36 Gy resected nodes (IIB/C): 41.4 Gy RT week 13 for all patients Group IIA LF 8.1% and Group III LF 11.5%, compared to 11.7% and 11.9% in D9602 A later guideline clarified the approach to gyn tumors (Lautz 2020). Subset 2 is subsequently categorized as intermediate risk in ARST 1431 (NCT02567435).

Peds.methods: Surgery first if operable, except orbit and gyn •Subgroup A: Stage 1 Group I/IIA Stage 1 Group III orbit Stage 2 Group I •Subgroup B: Stage 1 Group IIB/IIC Stage 1 Group III Stage 2 Group II Stage 3 Group I/II
Peds.results: ➜ Subgroup A (very low risk): VA x45 wks, with RT for Stage 1 group IIA [⛔ C removed and 🏆RT doses reduced from IRS-IV; compare to IRS-IV and IRS-III] ➜ Subgroup B (low risk): VAC x45 wks plus RT [🏆RT dose reduced from IRS-IV] 📌 Radiation details: -🏆36 Gy RT for microscopic residual, and 45 Gy for Group III orbit [Reduced dose. Compare to IRS-III/IV. IRS-IV doses were 41.4 Gy for microscopic and 50.4 Gy or 59 Gy for orbit.] -🏆For Group II/III vaginal, RT is delayed. If N0 with repeat negative biopsies, RT was avoided.
Peds.interpretation: ➡️ Subgroup A: 5-yr FFS 89% Group III orbit 5-yr FFS 86% Stage I Group IIA 5-yr FFS 86% ➡️ Subgroup B: 5-yr FFS 85%
Peds.commentary: FFS and OS were similar to IRS-III, including those receiving reduced RT, but lower than IRS-IV patients receiving VAC. No evidence that reduced dose RT contributed to worse outcomes when comparing to IRS-III and IV. 5-yr FFS rates similar for subsets A and B. 45 Gy to orbit gives good FFS.

Peds.trial: Intermediate risk Add temsirolimus to VAC/VI? pending
Peds.n: Gupta et al, Lancet Oncol, 2024 Jackson et al, IJROBP, 2025
Peds.methods: Surgery first if operable Intermediate risk RMS Embryonal: Stage I Group III non orbit Stage 3 Group I/II Stage 2/3 Group III Stage 4 Group IV, age <10 Alveolar Stage 1-3 Group I-III
Peds.results: ➜ 🏆 VAC/VI [from ARST 0531] + RT vs. ➜ VAC/VI/temsirolimus + RT 📌 Alveolar FOXO1 fusion negative, Stage 1 Group I-II and Group III orbit, and Stage 2 Group I-II: single arm VAC/VA and RT 📌 Radiation details: -RT at week 13 for all arms -If CR to chemo at week 9 PET, dose reduced to 36 Gy -If PR, RT replanning allowed at 36 Gy -Delayed primary excision allowed at week 12 (debulking strongly discouraged) -For size >5 cm, boost to 59.4 Gy
Peds.interpretation: 3-yr EFS ~65%, not different •Boost to 59.4 for size >5 cm showed no LC benefit - Only 66% received the protocol specified boost, for various reasons such as concern for adverse effects - A post-hoc comparison was performed
Peds.commentary: Temsirolimus added to VAC/VI did not improve EFS in intermediate risk RMS. A post-hoc analysis showed no improvement in LC with boost to 59.4 Gy for size >5 cm.
Peds.notes: Although a negative trial, it does still share COG's radiation therapy protocol. Note that the intermediate risk criteria are expanded in this study. Subset 2 from low risk ARST 0331 is now intermediate risk, as well as Stage 4 group IV patients age <10. In this trial RT starts at week 13 (like low risk and int risk trial D9803) based on no difference in timing in RT between IRS-IV, D9803, and ARST 0531. Week 13 chosen to allow for assessment of feasibility of temsirolimus, complex RT planning, and possible delayed primary excision. The post-hoc analysis of dose escalation is obviously limited…

Peds.trial: Intermediate risk Add VI to VAC? yes RT at week 4? week 4-13 ok
Peds.n: NCI, ARST 1431, clinicaltrials Hawkins et al, JCO, 2018 Casey et al, Cancer, 2019
Peds.methods: Surgery first if operable Intermediate risk RMS Embryonal, botyroid, spindle cell RMS, or ectomesenchymoma: Stage 2/3 Group III Alveolar: Stage 1-3 Group I-III
Peds.results: ➜ VAC vs. ➜ 🏆 VAC/VI alternating (low dose C in 2nd arm) 📌 Radiation details: -RT at week 4 [compare to week 10 in IRS-IV and week 13 in D9803] After 36 Gy, RT volume is modified to post chemo disease plus any infiltration
Peds.interpretation: No change in OS or EFS. 4-yr EFS ~60% Embryonal 2-yr EFS ~68% Alveolar 2-yr EFS ~58% LF 22%, not different 4-yr OS ~73% VAC/VI has less neutropenia, anemia, and thrombocytopenia. Also potential for less infertility. No difference in outcomes with week 4 RT vs. IRS-IV's week 10. •0531's VAC/VI vs. D9803's VAC 5-yr LF 28% (0531) vs. 19% (D9803) EFS and OS also worse in 0531 -LF worsening maintained after excluding those who did not receive RT. Also no differences with protons -LF worse with size >5 cm
Peds.commentary: VAC/VI chemotherapy has less adverse effects than VAC and maintains EFS and OS. LF with VAC/VI was unexplainably worse in ARST 0531 than in D9803; this may be due to confounders. In the subsequent trial ARST 1431, VAC/VI is the chemo regimen. Boost is allowed for size >5 cm. Week 13 is chosen for RT to allow for assessment of feasibility of temsirolimus, complex RT planning, and possible delayed primary excision.
Peds.notes: Even though LF was worse in 0531 than D9803, VAC/VI chemo is incorporated into new protocol ARST 1431. The authors are uncertain as to why LF was worse than in D9803. There was no change in LF in the 0531 or D9803 randomizations themselves. This trial illustrates the problem with historical controls. This method of analysis is reasonable in pediatrics because pediatric patients tend to be more uniform and without other comorbidites, whereas randomized trials are preferred in adults due to more confounding variables. The authors admit it can be problematic to compare outcomes from two different…
Peds.commentary_refs: 1. National Cancer Institute (NCI). Combination Chemotherapy With or Without Temsirolimus in Treating Patients With Intermediate Risk Rhabdomyosarcoma. Peds.subtopic: Intermediate risk: Chemo, delayed primary excision
Peds.trial: Intermediate risk Benefit of VAC/VTC alt over VAC? no Benefit to delayed primary excision? LC same but allows lower RT dose
Peds.n: Arndt et al, JCO, 2009 Skapek et al, Pediatr Blood Cancer, 2013 Rodeberg et al, Int J Cancer, 2015 Wolden et al, IJROBP, 2015
Peds.methods: Surgery first if operable Group III embryonal or alvelolar RMS and Group I/II alveolar RMS
Peds.results: ➜ 🏆 VAC [from IRS-IV] x39 wks vs. ➜ VAC/VTC alternating 📌Radiation details: -RT at week 13. Immediate RT for parameningeal RMS with intracranial extension -Group III: Local therapy with to 50.4 Gy with or without delayed primary excision ("second look surgery") at 36 Gy, week 12. Adjustment of dose allowed for complete (36 Gy) or microscopic (41.4 Gy) resection -Group I/II: 36-41.4 Gy Evaluation of PAX-FOXO1 fusion outcomes
Peds.interpretation: No benefit in FFS 4-yr FFS 73% VAC vs 68% VAC/VTC, p=.3 CR in 84% Group I/II 5-yr EFS 69%, 5-yr LF 10% Group III 5-yr EFS 70%, 5-yr LF 19% 45% of Group III underwent delayed primary excision. Removal of gross disease in 84%. 5-yr LF rate was similar from IRS-IV PAX-FOXO1 negative alveolar have similar outcomes to embryonal.
Peds.commentary: VAC/VTC does not improve EFS compared to VAC. There is no LC benefit with delayed primary excision, though adverse effects may be reduced by allowing lower dose RT in that setting. LC was excellent for Group I/II alveolar RMS, 90%. More LF with size ≥5 cm, which were mostly RP sites. LF still poor, 63%, in group III. PAX-FOXO1 negative alveolar have similar outcomes to embryonal.
Peds.notes: Delayed primary excision may be used by some because it does allow for lower RT dose, and thus potentially less adverse effects in the future. Since LF was worse in size >5 cm, protocol ARST 1431 boosts tumors >5 cm to 59.4 Gy (NCT02567435). VAC=vincristione, actinomycinD, cyclophosphamide

Peds.trial: High risk Whole lung RT in pulm mets? yes
Peds.citation: COG WLI review of D9802, D9803, ARST08P1, ARST0431
Peds.methods: RMS with pulmonary metastasis
Peds.results: Retrospective review of whole lung irradiation (WLI) benefit in those with pulmonary metastasis in D9802, D9803, ARST08P1, ARST0431. All trials mandated WLI for pulmonary metastasis. ➜ WLI vs. ➜ no WLI -WLI was 15 Gy/10 fx -Delivered simulateously with RT to primary
Peds.interpretation: 5-yr EFS 38% vs. 25% 5-yr OS 46% vs. 32%, p=0.08 In subgroup analysis the benefit is in age ≥10 , but otherwise present across all factors including histology, favorable vs unfavorable, and number of metastatic sites 55% did not receive WLI despite protocol requirements
Peds.commentary: In this retrospective review, WLI improves OS and EFS in those with pulmonary metastasis.
Peds.notes: The EpSSG FAR-RMS trial is an open randomized trial evaluating this question. The study here offers fair support for WLI in those with pulmonary metastasis in the meantime. Retrospective studies tend to be more reliable in pediatrics since children have less comorbidities than adults, though there is still the potential for selection bias. Those with better performance status and prognosis may have been more likely to be given WLI. WLI was delivered at week 12 in D9803, week 15 in D9802, and week 20 in ARST0431 and ARST08P1.
Peds.commentary_refs: 1. Luo LY, Xue W, Qumseya A, et al. Whole Lung Irradiation in Rhabdomyosarcoma With Lung Metastases: A Report From the Soft Tissue Sarcoma Committee of the Children's Oncology Group. J Clin Oncol 2024. Peds.trial: Omit lung RT after CR from chemo? slightly worse EFS Benefit of adding cyclo/etop in LOH? yes
Peds.n: Dix et al, JCO, 2018 DIx et al, JCO, 2019 Dix et al, JCO, 2025
Peds.methods: Stage IV favorable histology with lung metastasis
Peds.results: 6 weeks DD4A (DACT/VCR/DOXO) → •CR and no LOH 1p16q: ➜ no whole lung RT [vs. lung RT historical controls] •incomplete response or LOH 1p16q: ➜ 🏆 CPM/ETOP x4 ("regimen M") + whole lung RT [vs. historical controls] Additional analysis of regimen M in +LOH 1p16q
Peds.interpretation: •CR and no LOH, whole lung RT omitted: 4-yr EFS 80% [vs. 85% in historical control] 4-yr OS 96% Expected vs. observed events 15% vs. 20%, p=0.052 •Incomplete response plus whole lung RT: 4-yr EFS 89%, 4-yr OS 95% Expected vs. observed events 25% vs. 12% •benefit of regimen M (CPM/ETOP) in +LOH: 4-yr EFS 90% (AREN0533/0552) vs. 61% (historical NWST-5) •1q molecular analysis 1q gain is a prognostic indicator of EFS and OS.
Peds.commentary: In Wilms with lung metastasis there is excellent OS with omission of lung RT after lung nodule CR to chemo but more events than expected compared to historical control. Escalating chemo with Regimen M (addition of cisplatin and etoposide to chemo) in LOH tumors improves EFS compared to historical control. 1q gain is a prognostic indicator of EFS and OS.
Peds.notes: Regimen M = DD4A plus CE.

Peds.methods: Wilms, clear cell sarcoma of kidney, or malignant rhabdoid tumor of kidney
Peds.results: Stage and histology specific therapy. Tumors assayed for LOH of 1p and 16q Stage I-II: DACT/VCR Stage III-IV: RT then DD4A (DACT/VCR/DOXO)
Peds.interpretation: Stage I-II: RFS and OS worse for LOH of 1p only, LOH of 16q only, and LOH of both Stage III-IV: RFS and OS worse only for LOH on both Any LOH in FH 29% Any LOH in anaplasia 29% Any LOH in CCCSK 6% No LOHs detected in RTK
Peds.commentary: LOH for 1p and 16q identifies a subset of FH Wilms tumors who have increased risk of relapse and death. More intensive treatment in stage III-IV may overcome effect of LOH
Peds.notes: Outcomes if no LOH: Stage I age<24 mos, wt <550g: 4-yr RFS 96%, 4-yr OS 100% Stage I age≥24 mos, ≥550g: 4-yr RFS 94%, 4-yr OS 98% Stage II FH: 4-yr RFS 86%, OS 98% Stage III FH: 4-yr RFS 87%, OS 94% Stage IV FH: 4-yr RFS 76%, OS 86% Stage V FH: 4-yr RFS 67%, OS 91%
Peds.commentary_refs: 1. Grundy PE, Breslow NE, Li S, et al. Loss of Heterozygosity for Chromosomes 1p and 16q Is an Adverse Prognostic Factor in Favorable-Histology Wilms Tumor: A Report From the National Wilms Tumor Study Group. J Clin Oncol 2005;23:7312-21. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2005.01.2799

Peds.trial: Is RT needed in Stage II? no Can RT dose be lowered in Stage III? yes
Peds.methods: Stage I-IV favorable and unfavorable histology
Peds.results: High dose RT, low dose RT and no dose RT were compared based on stage 2x2 randomizations ➜ Stage I FH: No RT. Vinc/ActinoD x 10 weeks vs 6 mos ➜ Stage II FH: No RT vs. 20 Gy. DD4A (DACT/VCR/DOXO) vs. intensive DACT/VCR ➜ Stage III FH: RT 10 Gy vs. 20 Gy. DD4A (DACT/VCR/DOXO) vs. intensive DACT/VCR ➜ Stage IV any and all UH: RT per NWST 2 then DD4A (DACT/VCR/DOXO) vs. same plus CPM
Peds.interpretation: No difference any arms; therefore, the de-escalated regimens are favored.
Peds.commentary: Less intense therapy is appropriate for low risk patients. Cyclophosphamide does not benefit high risk. Stage II does not benefit from RT. In Stage III, RT to 10 Gy is sufficient with Actino/VCR.
Peds.commentary_refs: 1. D'Angio GJ, Breslow N, Beckwith JB, et al. Treatment of Wilms' tumor. Results of the Third National Wilms' Tumor Study. Cancer 1989;64:349-60. PMID: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/2544249/

Peds.trial: Induction chemo with ASCT? Yes
Peds.methods: ATRT
Peds.results: Surgery → ➜ induction VCR/CDDP/CTX/VP16/MTX → if no PD, consolidation thiotepa/carboplatin/ASCT → IFRT to 50.4 or 54 Gy plus Optional CSI to 23.4 or 36 Gy
Peds.interpretation: 4-yr EFS 37%, improved from historical control 4-yr OS 43% Best EFS in SHH-NOTCH (4-yr 50%) -Failures rare after 2 years (9%) -Treatment deaths 6% -Due to early pulm toxicities, RT timing was then changed to after consolidation. Adverse effects improved
Peds.commentary: This intensified regimen led to markedly improved EFS and OS compared to historical controls, and provides a basis for future trials. SHH-NOTCH tumors have improved outcomes. Based on observed adverse effects, RT is recommended to be sequenced after consolidation.
Peds.commentary_refs: 1. Reddy AT, Strother DR, Judkins AR, et al. Peds.trial: RT? Yes, standard. Outcomes defined Immediate RT vs chemo then delayed RT in younger age? Immediate RT
Peds.methods: Ependymoma patients undergoing resection, age 1-21 Eligible: differentiated WHO II, anaplastic WHO III, clear cell, papillary Ineligible: spinal, myxopapillary, subependymoma
Peds.results: Phase II ➜ Stratum 1: GTR of grade I-II, supratentorial → observation ➜ Stratum 2: STR of any grade or location → chemo, optional 2nd surgery, RT ➜ Stratum 3: near GTR (residual <5 mm) of any grade or location → immediate RT ➜ Stratum 4: GTR of grade III supratentorial, or any grade infratentorial → immediate RT RT to 59.4 Gy (54 Gy for age ≤1.5 yrs), CTV 1.0 cm
Peds.interpretation: 📌 Stratum 1 GTR (observation): 5-yr EFS 61% 📌 Stratum 2 STR (chemo, 2nd surg, RT): 37% 📌 Stratum 3 nearGTR (immediate RT): 67% 📌 anaplastic EFS 61% 📌 classic EFS 75% 5-yr EFS (of 63%) for age <3 years who had immediate RT was higher than historical controls of those who would have had chemo then delayed RT Age and supra/infratentorial location did not effect EFS. EFS was higher in infratentorial without 1q gain that with gain. EFS was worse with increasing grade.
Peds.commentary: Outcomes are demonstrated. EFS for age <3 who received immediate RT was similar to older patients, implying chemo first is not needed. Location had no effect on EFS. Radiation should remain standard.
Peds.notes: EFS after observation for GTR of grade I-II is only 61%. Would these patients also benefit from radiation or other therapy?
Peds.commentary_refs: 1. Merchant TE, Bendel AE, Sabin ND, et al. Peds.subtopic: NGGCT: WVI vs. CSI germinoma: response guided RT
Peds.trial: NGGCT: whole ventricle RT instead of CSI? no; arm closed Germ cell: induction chemo and reduced dose WV RT? not noninferior
Peds.n: Fangusaro et al, JCO, 2019 Bartels et al, Neuro Oncol, 2021
Peds.methods: NGGCT and Germinomas Germinomas may be diagnosed by labs and imaging; bx not required
Peds.results: Phase II: ➜ Stratum 1 (NGGCT): cis/etoposide alt cis/ifosphamide x6 → if CR or PR → 30.6 Gy/1.8 fx whole ventricle RT [compare to 🏆 36 Gy CSI in ACNS0112] then IFRT boost to 54 Gy ➜ Stratum 2 (Germinomas): [Can RT dose be lowered with induction chemo and good response?] Cis/etoposide x4 → ➜ If CR/CCR → 18 Gy WV RT → IFRT boost to 30 Gy ➜ If PR and <1.5 cm on 2nd look surgery → 24 Gy WV RT → IFRT boost to 36 Gy
Peds.interpretation: 📌 Stratum 1 (NGGCT): Closed early due to failures. All failures in spine 3-yr PFS 88% [vs. 92% in ACNS012] 3-yr OS 92% [vs. 94% in ACNS0112] Serum AFP and BHCG did not predict failure 📌 Stratum 2 (GCT): 3-yr PFS 95% with 18 Gy & 94% with 24 Gy (not noninferior by prespecified analysis)
Peds.commentary: 📌 Stratum 1 (NGGCT): De-escalation of RT led to increased failures in spine. The 36 Gy CSI regimen from ACNS0112 remains standard. 📌 Stratum 2 (GCT): Results were favorable with lower dose WV RT after CR to induction chemo, although not techically not noninferior by prespecified design.

Peds.methods: Germinoma
Peds.results: ➜ Localized: CSI 24 Gy + boost 16 Gy vs. induction carbo/etoposide alt etoposide/ifos then 40 Gy IFRT ➜ Metastatic: optional induction chemo then reduced dose CSI 24 Gy and boost to primary and metastasis to 16 Gy
Peds.interpretation: Improved PFS with CSI in localized germinoma 5-yr PFS 97% vs. 88% (relapses in ventricles) 5-yr EFS ~90% and OS ~95%, not different Metastatic: EFS 98% and OS 98%
Peds.commentary: Localized germinoma can be treated with reduced dose CSI or induction chemo followed by focal RT, though PFS does favor CSI. Reduced dose CSI is effective in metastatic disease.

Peds.methods: Medulloblastoma average risk
Peds.results: ➜ 23.4 Gy 🏆 CSI plus 30.6 Gy posterior fossa boost to 54 Gy with cis/CPM vs. ➜ same but with ⛔18 Gy CSI in ages 3-7 and 🏆 IFRT in ages 2-21
Peds.interpretation: EFS and OS not different for IFRT vs. PFRT 5-yr EFS ~81% 5-yr OS 85% 5-yr cumulative LF 10% IFRT vs. 8% PFRT, p=0.21 Low dose CSI worse than standard dose 5-yr EFS 83% vs. 71% 5-yr OS 86% vs. 78%, p=0.049 5-yr isolated neuraxis failure 12.5 % vs. 8.7% Isolated DF 8.2% vs. 12.8% •Molecular subgroups Group 3 had worst EFS WNT had best EFS SHH had better EFS with IFRT over PFRT Group 4 had worse EFS with LD-CSI Low dose had more cognitive AEs Only 18.5% had an isolated PF failure. Prostate.patients: PSA detected prostate cancer (any Gleason score) Age 50-69 (Characteristics: Median age 62 77% Gleason 6, 20% Gleason 7, 2% Gleason 8-10, 76% with T1c)
Prostate.methods: ➜ Active surveillance (55% had treatment) vs. ➜ 🏆 Radical prostatectomy vs. ➜ 🏆 RT 74 Gy/ 37 fx (or brachy) + 3-6 mos ADT 3DCRT 74 Gy/37 fx LDR brachytherapy allowed (11%) Most surgeries were nerve-sparing Adjuvant and salvage RT were allowed
Prostate.results: -At 15 years, with RT vs. surgery, there is no difference in PCM, DM, disease progression, ADT salvage, or all deaths. -AS had worse DM, disease progression, and ADT compared to RP and RT. However 15-yr PCM and all cause mortality were similar. Surveillance vs. RP vs. RT: 10-yr PCM 1.85% vs. 0.67% vs. 0.73% 15-yr PCM 2.2% vs. 1.5% vs. 2.1% 10-yr DM 5.6% vs. 2.4% vs. 2.7% 15-yr DM 7.1% vs. 3.5% vs. 3.7% 10-yr ADT salvage 8.7% vs. 4.8% vs. 5.6% 15-yr ADT salvage 9.4% vs. 5.3% vs. 5.6% 10-yr clinical progression 20% vs. 5.9% vs. 6.6% 15-yr clinical progression 21% vs. 8.0% vs. 8.4% 10-yr all deat…
Prostate.interpretation: RT+ADT and radical prostatectomy show no difference in prostate cancer mortality or DM. Active surveillance had worse DM and ADT salvage, but this did not lead to worse PCM at 15 years. RT+ADT and surgery have differing adverse effect profiles. Surgery has worse incontinence and sexual dysfunction. RT+ADT also leads to some ED at mid term f/u, then ED eventually converges. RT has slightly worse long term loose stool, blood in stool, and GI incontinence compared to AS. Late nocturia is similar between RT and AS. 12-yr adverse effects results were similar.
Prostate.commentary: Patient characteristics & effects. There was no difference on subanalysis by Gleason grade group, showing that the results apply to both low and intermediate risk. Most of the patients fit low risk criteria. 77% were Gleason 6, 20% had Gleason 7, 2% had Gleason 8-10. <1% were black. Active surveillance. There was selection bias favoring the AS group: it featured more favorable tumors. But despite the better characteristics handicap, plus the crossovers in the group to treatment, the AS group still performed worse. In AS, 55% eventually received treatment. Of these, 49% had surgery, 33% had pro…

Prostate.methods: ➜ 6 mos apalutamide and ADT → surgery → 6 mos apalutamide and ADT vs. ➜ peri-op 6 mos ADT plus placebo Biochemical failure defined as two consecutive rises to ≥0.2 Imaging q6 mos after BF
Prostate.results: pCR or MRD 9% vs. 1% 5-yr MFS 78% vs. 74% 5-yr EFS 49% vs. 40% 4-yr freedom from disease 22% vs 18% Adverse effects during treatment Grade 3-4 adverse effects 40% vs. 31% Urinary incontinence grade 1-2 in about 50% both arms Urinary incontinence grade 3 in 1-2% both arms Median time to testosterone recovery from end of 12 mos ADT 8.1 vs. 6.6 mos Any subsequent therapy 42% vs. 57% Post op radiation 11% vs. 21% (of all patients) (Calculated from Table s4) Post op radiation 27% vs. 37% (of those who had any additional treatment)
Prostate.interpretation: Peri-op apalutamide added to ADT improved response rates, MFS, and EFS.
Prostate.commentary: There was no standard of care arm without any ADT. Pre or peri-op ADT has not shown benefit over surgery only in a randomized trial. Note the eligibility criteria - not all high risk were eligible. The need for adjuvant or salvage radiation was reduced, but radiation therapy was at discretion. The primary endpoints are of more interest. There were no RT planning instructions of any kind in the protocol or reporting of any RT details in the manuscript. ADT may delay recurrence and not cure or eradicate disease. A malignant cell may simply be converted to a benign appearing imposter cell, which …

Prostate.citation: Wilt et al, NEJM, 2012 Wilt et al, NEJM, 2017
Prostate.patients: T1-T2Nx, PSA<50, Age<75, negative bone scan, life expectancy >10 years 40% low risk, 33% medium risk, 22% high risk Mean age 67 median PSA 7.8 10 year f/u
Prostate.methods: ➜ Watchful waiting vs. ➜ Radical prostatectomy
Prostate.results: On 12 yr f/u, radical prostatectomy resulted in reduced all cause mortality for PSA >10. Those with intermediate or high risk were close to a difference (p 0.07). With RP 5.8% died due to cancer or treatment, and with obs 8.4% died, p=0.09. At 19.5 years follow-up: death in 67% obs vs. 61% surgery, p=0.06 death from cancer or treatment 7.4% vs. 11.4%, p=0.06 Surgery may have led to improved mortality in int risk disease (difference 14.5%) Surgery did not improve mortality in low or high risk disease Urinary dysfunction and erectile and sexual function worse with surgery
Prostate.interpretation: There is a trend to benefit in OS with surgery over observation, especially in those with intermediate risk disease. Surgery leads to notable adverse effects.
Prostate.commentary: Surgical complications occurred in 21% during first 30 days, erectile dysfunction 81% vs 44%, urinary incontinence 17% vs 6.3%, bowel dysfunction 12% vs 11% By ten years, almost half participants had died, indicating that life expectancies were overestimated. Some say the trial may be underpowered.
Prostate.references: 1. Wilt TJ, Brawer MK, Jones KM, et al. Radical Prostatectomy versus Observation for Localized Prostate Cancer. N Engl J Med 2012;367:203-13. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1113162 2. Wilt TJ, Jones KM, Barry MJ, et al. Follow-up of Prostatectomy versus Observation for Early Prostate Cancer. N Engl J Med 2017;377:132-42. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1615869

Prostate.subtopic: Active Surveillance in very low risk
Prostate.citation: Tosoian et al, JCO, 2011 Tosoian et al, JCO, 2015
Prostate.patients: Very low risk and low risk: T1c, PSA density <0.15, GS ≤6 with ≤2 cores, <50% of any core
Prostate.methods: Phase II active surveillance. Repeat biopsy every year. PSA and DRE q6mos. Treatment if enrollment criteria no longer met
Prostate.results: 15-yr OS 69%, 15-yr CSS 99.9%, 15-yr MFS 99.4% Grade reclassification: 10-yr 26%, 15-yr 31% Incidence of curative intervention: 10-yr 50%, 15-yr 57% Median treatment free survival 8.5 yrs
Prostate.interpretation: Long term active surveillance in very low and low risk prostate cancer seems acceptable, leading to high survival and low DM.
Prostate.references: 1. Tosoian JJ, Trock BJ, Landis P, et al. Active Surveillance Program for Prostate Cancer: An Update of the Johns Hopkins Experience. J Clin Oncol 2011;29:2185-90. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2010.32.8112 2. Tosoian JJ, Mamawala M, Epstein JI, et al. Intermediate and Longer-Term Outcomes From a Prospective Active-Surveillance Program for Favorable-Risk Prostate Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2015;33:3379-85. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2015.62.5764

Prostate.citation: Klotz et al, JCO, 2010 Klotz et al, JCO, 2015
Prostate.patients: Low or intermediate risk patients. The study later only enrolled low risk patients mean age 67.8 74% T1c 84% Gleason ≤6 13% Gleason 3+4=7 87% PSA <10 11% PSA 10-15 25% intermediate risk
Prostate.methods: Phase II active surveillance. PSA q3mos for 2 years, then q6mos. Biopsy q12 months, then q3-4 yrs. Biopsies to initial site and anterior and anterolateral regions Treated is triggered for PSA DT <3 yrs, biopsy progression, or positive biopsy.
Prostate.results: 1.5% deaths from prostate cancer 2.8% with metastatic disease (1.3 % alive) 15-yr OS 62% 10-yr CSS 98.1%, 15-yr CSS 94.3% Those untreated and still on surveillance: 5-yr 76%, 10-yr 64%, 15-yr 55% HR for nonprostate to prostate mortality: 9.2:1 BF after treatment with RP vs. RT: 25% and 24%
Prostate.interpretation: Long term active surveillance in low risk prostate cancer seems acceptable, leading to high survival and low DM.
Prostate.commentary: The report does not reveal how many had Gleason Score <6. Some would not be classified as having cancer by modern metrics.
Prostate.references: 1. Klotz L, Zhang L, Lam A, Nam R, Mamedov A, Loblaw A. Clinical Results of Long-Term Follow-Up of a Large, Active Surveillance Cohort With Localized Prostate Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2010;28:126-31. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2009.24.2180 2. Klotz L, Vesprini D, Sethukavalan P, et al. Long-Term Follow-Up of a Large Active Surveillance Cohort of Patients With Prostate Cancer. J Clin Oncol 2015;33:272-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2014.55.1192

Prostate.citation: Bill-Axelson et al, NEJM, 2005 Bill-Axelson et al, NEJM, 2014 Bill-Axelson et al, NEJM, 2018
Prostate.patients: age <75, T1-T2, operable, life expectancy >10yrs, PSA<50, negative bone scan Characteristics: mean age 65 13% gleason 2-4, 48% gleason 5-6, 22% 7, 4% 8-9 Mean PSA 13. 29% PSA 10-20, 20% PSA >20
Prostate.methods: ➜ Watchful waiting vs. ➜ Radical prostatectomy ADT started for symptomatic local progression or distant disease
Prostate.results: 29-yr f/u -NNT to prevent one death: 8.3 -Mean OS absolute benefit of 2.9 yrs -ECE had 5x risk of death, Gleason 7 had 10x risk death 23-yr Death due to prostate cancer 31% WW vs. 20% RP 23-yr Any mortality 84% vs. 72% -In age <65, 34% vs. 18% deaths from prostate cancer, and less difference if age 65 or older. -Deaths from prostate cancer in intermediate risk group 39% vs. 15%. No difference in low or high risk group OS benefit in low risk age <65 23-yr DM 43% vs. 27%. Benefit in both age groups, low and int risk ADT used in 67% vs. 43%. Benefit in both age groups, and all risk groups
Prostate.interpretation: At 29 year follow-up, OS benefit of 2.9 years with RP. ECE and Gleason 7 highly increase risk of death. Reduced mortality with surgery with 23 year follow-up. The benefit is largest in men <65 years of age.
Prostate.commentary: All Gleason scores were included, including those too low to be cancer. The mixed scores somewhat limit interpretation of the trial in the modern era of risk stratification. But this trial laid an important foundation. 4% were Gleason 8-9 and 20% had PSA >20.
Prostate.references: 1. Bill-Axelson A, Holmberg L, Ruutu M, et al. Radical Prostatectomy versus Watchful Waiting in Early Prostate Cancer. N Engl J Med 2005;352:1977-84. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1311593 3. Bill-Axelson A, Holmberg L, Garmo H, et al. Radical Prostatectomy or Watchful Waiting in Prostate Cancer — 29-Year Follow-up. N Engl J Med 2018;379:2319-29. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1807801

Prostate.patients: CAP, ERSPC, PLCO, Quebec, Stockholm
Prostate.methods: Meta-analysis of five PSA screening trials
Prostate.results: PCSM beyond 12 years resulted in a relative 9% reduction in PCSM, p<0.001 PCSM beyond 8 yrs per 10,000 108 vs. 98 patients
Prostate.interpretation: PSA screening reduces PCSM.
Prostate.commentary: There was crossover within the trials, diluting the results. This meta-analysis clarifies the controversy. The authors explain that one reason this analysis differed from others was that CAP finally became positive after 15 year follow-up.
Prostate.references: 1. Nguyen DD, Tadayon Najafabadi B, Lin L, et al. Effect of Prostate Cancer Screening with Prostate-specific Antigen Testing on Long-term Prostate Cancer-specific Mortality: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials. Prostate.citation: Tsodikov et al, Ann Intern Med, 2017
Prostate.patients: ERSPC: ages 55-69 PLCO: ages 55-74
Prostate.methods: Re-analysis of PLCO and ERSPC trials taking in to account those who were actually screened vs. not and lead time bias. Cox regression analysis
Prostate.results: Screening estimated to give 7-9% reduction in PCSM per year of mean lead time 11-year follow-up ERSPC: 25-31% relative benefit in PCSM PLCO: 27-32% relative benefit in PCSM
Prostate.interpretation: The ERSPC and PLCO screening studies show potential benefit screening for prostate cancer.
Prostate.references: 1. Tsodikov A, Gulati R, Heijnsdijk EAM, et al. Prostate.citation: Andrioloe et al, NEJM, 2009 Crawford et al, JCO, 2011
Prostate.methods: ➜ PSA screening q6 yrs and DRE q4 yrs vs. ➜ usual care
Prostate.results: Screening reduced number of deaths, 22 vs. 38. 10-yr NNT 5 to prevent one death if no comorbidity. If at least 1 comorbidity, no benefit
Prostate.interpretation: Prostate cancer screening in men with few comorbidities seems to reduce death from prostate cancer.
Prostate.commentary: Contamination in control group: 40-52% PSA screened and 41-46% DRE screened. In screening arm, 85% were screened. Baseline PSA screening for both arms 44%.

Prostate.results: 8-yr NNT: 48 13-yr NNT to prevent PC death: 742 16-yr NNT to prevent PC death: 570 NN to screen: 1410 13-yr NN to screen: 27 13-yr PCM 0.14%, 16-yr 0.18% Metastatic prostate cancer detected in 0.67% vs 0.87% per 1000 men, a relative 30% reduction. Relative decrease of 21% in PCSM, or absolute change of 0.71 out of 1000 men. No change in overall mortality 23-yr PCM 1.4% vs. 1.6%, RR 0.87 23-yr ARR 0.22% 23-yr NNdiagnose 12 vs. 18 NNinvite 456 vs. 628
Prostate.interpretation: Prostate cancer screening reduces death from prostate cancer.

Prostate.citation: Lippman et al, JAMA, 2009 Klein et al, JAMA, 2011
Prostate.patients: age 55 or older (if black, age ≥ 50), PSA≤4, negative DRE
Prostate.methods: ➜ Selenium + placebo vs. ➜ vitamin E + placebo vs. ➜ selenium + vitamin E vs. ➜ two placebos
Prostate.results: No difference in rate of prostate cancer. Vitamin E alone rates trend slightly higher (4.9% vs. ~4.5% other arms, p=0.06).
Prostate.interpretation: Vitamin E seemed to increase the incidence of prostate cancer. Selenium showed no differences.

Prostate.commentary: Dose escalation summary -A number of single and multi institutional studies show improvement in BPFS with dose escalation (MDACC, GETUG, Dutch CKVO, MRC, Ontario, Fox Chase) -RTOG designed a multi-institutional protocol somewhat later: RTOG 0126. The protocol allowed IMRT in an amendment. Some of its publications focus on the effects of IMRT -Further increase in dose esclation is sought out with brachytherapy boost. Again, there is a conflict between increase in adverse effects, improved BPFS, and no confirmed OS benefit. (ASCENDE-RT, RTOG 0232, McMaster University, Mount Vernon) -SBRT is also…

Prostate.patients: High risk prostate cancer: PSA ≥20, Gleason score ≥8, or T3-T4
Prostate.methods: ➜ 70 Gy/ 35 fx vs. ➜ 🏆 80 Gy/ 40 fx ADT 3 years in all
Prostate.results: 5-yr OS 89% vs. 83% 10-yr OS 77% vs. 66% 5-yr CSS 99% vs. 97% 10-yr CSS 96% vs. 90% 5-yr PFS 91% vs. 88% 10-yr PFS 84% vs. 72% Late adverse effects not different
Prostate.interpretation: Dose escalation to 80 Gy improves OS, CSS, and PFS.
Prostate.references: 1. Hennequin C et al. Long-term results of dose escalation (80 vs 70 Gy) combined with long-term androgen deprivation therapy in high risk prostate cancers: GETUG-AGU 18 randomized trial. J Clin Oncol 2024;42:s4. Prostate.citation: Kerkmeijer et al, JCO 2021 Guricová et al, JCO, 2025
Prostate.patients: Intermediate and high risk (84% high risk)
Prostate.methods: ➜ 77 Gy/ 35 fx in 2.2 Gy vs. ➜ 🏆 Above + SIB focal boost up to 95 Gy to MRI nodules as feasible with priority to OARs (95 Gy achieved in 20%)
Prostate.results: ⬆️ 5-yr bDFS 85% vs. 92% 10-yr bDFS 71% vs. 86% 10-yr OS and DMFS not different Late grade ≥2 GU 23% vs. 28%, p=0.19 Late grade ≥3 GU 3.5% vs. 5.6%, p=0.22 Late grade ≥2 GI 12% vs. 13%, not different
Prostate.interpretation: Focal boost to tumor on MRI improves bDFS. Late GU adverse effects may be slightly higher. There was no benefit in DMFS or OS.
Prostate.commentary: Only 20% actually reached 95 Gy. There was no CTV or PTV margin from the GTV nodule for the high dose SIB. Sparing of OARs was prioritized over covering the nodule with high dose. Dose constraints were by institution and are located in the supplemental appendix. None employed a urethral constraint. IMRT or VMAT was used in all. Because of this, there might be some question regarding the reproducibility or future consistency of the results. Nonetheless it would seem that very good results should still be achieved if one maintains adequate constraints in planning, though there could be some unce…

Prostate.citation: Michalski et al, IJROBP, 2013 Michalski et al, JAMA Oncol, 2018 Michalski et al, IJROBP, 2023
Prostate.patients: Intermediate risk: T1b-T2b, GS 2-6, PSA ≥10 and <20 or GS 7 with PSA<15 M0
Prostate.methods: ➜ 70.2 vs. ➜ 🏆 79.2 Gy Primary endpoint: OS. Secondary endpoints: BF, DM, QOL, adverse effects Trial originally required 3DCRT, then amended to allow IMRT. Compared 3DCRT vs. IMRT adverse effects.
Prostate.results: Volumes to bladder and rectum lower with IMRT With IMRT, decrease in G2+ acute collective GI/GU 26% reduction in G2+ late GI trend (p=0.099) V70 > 15% associated with G2+ rectal 10-yr OS ~67% both arms 10-yr DM 7% vs. 4% (p=0.008) 15-yr DM 11% vs. 6% 10-yr Phoenix BC 61% vs. 78% 15-yr Phoenix BC 55% vs. 71% 10-yr grade 2+ GI 16% vs. 22% 10-yr yr grade 2+ GU 9% vs. 14% Patient reported QOL was not different. 15-yr grade 2+ GI 17% vs. 23% 15-yr grade 2+ GU 11% vs. 17% No difference in late grade 3+
Prostate.interpretation: Dose escalation improves DM and BF. Late grade 2 adverse effects are increased. Patient reported QOL did not differ. The primary endpoint of OS was not improved. IMRT reduced acute grade 2+ GI and GU adverse effects. There is a trend for decrease in late grade 2+ GI adverse effects.
Prostate.commentary: A primary endpoint of OS was too ambitious and negative in this study. DM did show improvement. The MDACC dose escalation study showed improvement in PCSM and DM, and GETUG 18 showed improved OS and CSS. Prostate.citation: Pollack et al, IJROBP, 2002 Kuban et al, IJROBP, 2008 Kuban et al, IJROBP, 2011 Pasalic et al, IJROBP, 2018
Prostate.patients: T1-T3, N0, PSA ≤30 low risk 20% intermediate risk 46% high risk 34%
Prostate.methods: ➜ 70 Gy vs. ➜ 🏆 78 Gy Dose to isocenter. 46 Gy box to prostate plus boost. Prior to ADT era. Long term failure was defined by Phoenix criteria
Prostate.results: 10-yr FFF 50% vs. 73% 15-yr FFF 81% vs. 88% 20-yr FFF 81% vs. 88% 20-yr BC 88% vs. 93% Later f/u showed no differences in FFF/BC when stratifying by NCCN risk group (not powered for this). On MVA, higher Gleason score, T3, and PSA were significant. 15-yr DM 3% vs. 1% 20-yr DM 4% vs. 1% No difference in OS. Improved PCSM 15-yr PCSM 6% vs. 3% 20-yr PCSM 10% vs. 5% 20-yr other deaths 65% vs. 75% (p=0.061) 15-yr salvage therapy 39% vs. 22% 2nd malignancy 2% in field in both arms 10-yr adverse effects: GI Grade 3: 1% vs. 7%, GI Grade 2: 13% vs. 26% GU Grade 3: 4% vs. 5% (NS), GU Grade 2: 8% vs. 13%…
Prostate.interpretation: Dose escalation improves BF, DM, and PCSM at long term f/u.
Prostate.commentary: Conventional dose escalation studies show benefit in DM, PCSM, and OS in various studies. The MDACC trial showed benefit in late 15- and 20-yr DM and PSCM. RTOG 0126 showed benefit in 10- and 15-yr DM. GETUG 18 showed benefit in 10-yr CSS and OS. Radiation planning details: The initial 4 field phase consisted of 11 x 11 cm A/P beams and 11 x 9 cm laterals. In the 70 Gy arm, the boost was a 9 x 9 cm box. In the 78 Gy arm, the boost was 3D CRT with CTV to block edge margin of 1.25-1.5 cm ant/inf and 0.75-1.0 cm post/sup.

Prostate.patients: T1b-T3a, PSA<50
Prostate.methods: neoadj ADT 3-6mos → ➜ 64 Gy vs. ➜ 74 Gy 3DCRT, concurrent ADT
Prostate.results: Dose escalation improves 5-yr bPFS, 60% vs. 71%. Significant for all risk groups. Also trend towards better clinical PFS, metastases-free survival, use of salvage HT. 10-yr BPFS 43% vs. 55%
Prostate.interpretation: Dose escalation improves bPFS and increases adverse effects.
Prostate.commentary: Grade 3 GI adverse effects 10% vs. 6% (SS) Grade 3 GU 4% vs. 2% (NS)
Prostate.references: 1. Dearnaley DP, Sydes MR, Graham JD, et al. Prostate.results: 5-yr FFF 64% vs. 54%. On subanalysis, benefit only in intermediate risk patients
Prostate.interpretation: Dose escalation improves FFF.
Prostate.commentary: No significant increase in late GI or GU adverse effects

Prostate.interpretation: Dose escalation improves bPFS.

Prostate.patients: intermediate and high risk prostate cancer 57% high risk, 43% unfavorable intermediate risk
Prostate.methods: 46 Gy WPRT ➜ ➜ LDR 110 Gy boost vs. ➜ 🏆 HDR 15 Gy boost Primary endpoint: QOL adverse effects data was collected retrospectively
Prostate.results: GU QOL eventually was similar at 18 months. HDR GU QOL recovered by 6 months, and LDR QOL took longer, 18 monhts Bowel QOL was improved in HDR after 5 years No difference in sexual HRQOL GU grade 3 in n=1 in both arms GU grade 2 in 2% LDR vs. 7% HDR GI grade 3 in n=1 in LDR, none in HDR GI grade 2 in 11% vs. 5% Median time to nadir PSA 14.1 vs. 13.8 mos 4-yr ≥0.2 PSA 83% vs. 81% 5-yr bFFS 90% vs. 94% 8-yr bFFS 85% vs. 86%
Prostate.interpretation: HDR boost has improved bowel QOL over LDR. GU QOL is similar at 18 months, but HDR recovers sooner.
Prostate.commentary: The study is the first randomized trial to compare LDR vs HDR boost. adverse effects data was collected retrospectively, which can lead to some bias. The hope was that HDR would show less long-term GU adverse effects than LDR, but late GU QOL was not different and GU adverse effects was slightly worse with HDR. Bowel QOL is improved with HDR. HDR has not yet been compared to IMRT or SBRT. SpaceOAR can further decrease bowel adverse effects in external beam. In PACE B with SBRT there was zero grade 3-5 bowel adverse effects, and without use of SpaceOAR. In the ASCENDE trial, LDR boost markedly …
Prostate.references: 1. Crook J, Moideen N, Arbour G, et al. A Randomized Trial Comparing Quality of Life After LDR or HDR Prostate Brachytherapy Boost with Pelvic External Beam Radiation Therapy. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2024.02.064

Prostate.subtopic: EBRT + LDR boost vs. LDR alone
Prostate.patients: intermediate risk prostate cancer: T1c-T2b and GS 2-6/PSA 10-20 or GS7/PSA<10
Prostate.methods: ➜ 45 Gy partial pelvis EBRT/IMRT+LDR vs. ➜ LDR alone
Prostate.results: 5-yr FFF-ASTRO no change 86% vs. 83% 5-yr FFF-Phoenix 88% vs. 86% 5-yr late GU & GI grade ≥2 adverse effects 43% vs. 26% 5-yr late GU & GI grade ≥3 adverse effects 8% vs. 4%
Prostate.interpretation: There was no difference in PFS with LDR boost with EBRT vs LDR alone. LDR boost with EBRT worsened adverse effects.

Prostate.subtopic: EBRT + LDR boost short vs. long ADT
Prostate.patients: T2c-T3a, PSA >20, or Gleason score ≥8
Prostate.methods: 6 mos ADT + LDR boost to 110 Gy I-125 then EBRT 45 Gy→ Superiority →no further ADT (total 6 mos) vs. →24 mos ADT (total 30 mos)
Prostate.results: Median f/u 9 years Cumulative BF 8-9%, not different No difference in secondary endpoints
Prostate.interpretation: 30 mos ADT did not show superiority to 6 mos ADT. The authors write that a noninferiority trial would be required to show noninferiority.
Prostate.commentary: Some might be tempted to quote the trial to promote the idea that brachytherapy can be used in place of some ADT. The authors do not endorse this view in their conclusion. Results should perhaps be interpreted caution given that in the ASCENDE trial, brachy boost did not improve long term MFS, CSS, or OS.

Prostate.results: BF not yet reported Primary endpoint: late adverse effects From the abstact the f/u length is unclear Late grade 1 GI 39% vs. 54% Late grade 2 GI 7% vs. 13% No difference in grade 1-2 GU Few grade 3 in both arms, no grade 4

Prostate.subtopic: EBRT + LDR boost vs. EBRT alone
Prostate.citation: Morris et al, IJROBP, 2017 Rodda et al, IJROBP, 2017 Oh et al, IJROBP, 2022
Prostate.patients: intermediate and high risk prostate cancer (66% high risk and 33% int risk) Excluded: T3b, T4, PSA>40, prior TURP
Prostate.methods: 12 mos ADT + 46 Gy WPRT then: ➜ LDR boost (115 Gy I-125) vs. ➜ EBRT boost (78 Gy total)
Prostate.results: No difference in OS, CSS, or DM BPFS improved 10-yr MFS 88% vs. 86%, p=0.56 10-yr CSS 94% vs. 91%, p=0.26 10-yr OS ~75%, not different 15-yr bPFS 85% vs. 67% Worse adverse effects with LDR 5-yr cumulative grade 3 GU adverse effects 18% vs. 5% (SS), prevalence of grade 3 GU adverse effects 8.6% vs. 2.2% (p=.58), 5-yr any pad usage 16% vs. 3% (SS), 5-yr late catheterization 12% vs. 3% (SS) cumulative grade 3 GI adverse effects 8.1% vs. 3.2% (p=.124), prevalence grade 3 GI 1.0% vs. 2.2% Similar erectile function 45% vs. 37% (p=.30)
Prostate.interpretation: LDR boost with EBRT substantially improved BC compared to EBRT alone. However there was no improvement in long term MFS, CSS, or OS. LDR led to a marked increase in GU adverse effects and trended towards worse GI adverse effects.
Prostate.commentary: BF was improved and there was some trend to benefit in CSS, p=0.26. Adverse effects were significantly increased. ASCENDE did use dose escalated EBRT in the control arm, unlike the McMaster and Mt Vernon trials. There is no benefit in 15-yr DM. Even if there is no benefit in DM, OS, or PCS long term, improved BF may still be useful to avoid or delay more ADT for some. Another question concerns whether intermediate risk really need an escalation in dose or WPRT. In PACE B 70% of patients were unfavorable intermediate risk, excluding Gleason 4+3=7, and had excellent bPFS without any ADT, suggest…

Similar result in NCI Rome with 62 Gy in 3.1 Gy per fx but this was superiority trial and dose did not show superiority -Possibly worse adverse effects: RTOG 70 Gy/28 and Fox Chase 70.2 Gy/26. MDACC's 72 Gy/30 fx in 2.4 Gy per fx (BED 85 Gy in 1.8 fx) also had worse adverse effects, but not when dose to rectum was reduced, but also had improved BF. Fox Chase's regimen trends to worse DM at 10 years. -Swiss meta-analysis reinfor…

Prostate.patients: Low or intermediate risk prostate cancer
Prostate.methods: ➜ IMRT vs. ➜ Proton beam therapy 79.2 Gy or 70 Gy/28 fx allowed
Prostate.results: No difference in QOL or PFS at any timepoint
Prostate.interpretation: Proton beam radiation did not improve QOL.
Prostate.commentary: adverse effects, dosimetry and use to techniques such as pencil beam and VMAT have not yet been reported. Results were stratified by rectal spacer use. Thus far there is no meaningful clinical improvement with protons, but some may continue to use protons based on a "just so" philosophy - it's better just because it is. In an environment in which cost doesn't matter, protons show better dosimetry on paper, just as IMRT did when it was implemented prior to randomized trials.
Prostate.references: 1. Efstathiou JA, Yeap BY, Michalski JM, et al.
