Prostate.patients: Meta-analysis of ten phase III trials Patient characteristics were balanced, though ADT use differed
Prostate.methods: Meta-analysis of phase III trials ➜ conventional RT (some dose-escalated, some not) vs. ➜ hypofractionated RT (various BEDs)
Prostate.results: No differences in BF, BCF, OS, PCSM, acute GU, or late GU or late GI F/u of 5 years for most studies There was heterogeneity in acute and late GI adverse effects. No heterogeneity in BF, BCF, OS, PCSM, or acute or late GU adverse effects. Acute GI adverse effects 9.1% less with conventional RT Acute GI adverse effects was worse with use of ADT or inclusion of full seminal vesicles
Prostate.interpretation: Hypofx RT leads to similar outcomes as conventional RT in this meta-analysis. However acute GI adverse effects are increased with ADT or inclusion of SV.
Prostate.commentary: Caution in the applicability of these results. Regimen designs differed among individual trials. Some trials used non-dose-escalated RT as the standard arm, others used dose escalated. The hypofractionated regimens were designed with various purposes: either to be isoeffective to late tissue effects, to non-dose escalated RT, or to dose escalated RT. There is heterogeneity in GI acute and late effects. There was no heterogeneity in BCF or OS. GI late effects may be able to be mitigated with IMRT and rectal spacer, though the urethra and bladder neck cannot be avoided. Adverse effects may be un…
Prostate.notes: Brenner and Hall (IJROBP, 2017) observe that moderate hypofx regimens with higher BED tend to have higher adverse effects, specifically BED1.5 of >183 and BED 2.5 of >136. Dose, BED 1.5/ 2.5 Gy CHHiP 57/3 Gy in 20 fx, 171, 125 PROFIT CHHiP 60/3 Gy in 20 fx, 180, 132 HYPRO 64.6/3.4 Gy in 19 fx, 211, 152 NCI Rome 62/3.1 Gy in 20 fx, 190, 139 Fox Chase 70.2/2.7 Gy in 26 fx, 197, 146 RTOG 70/2.5 Gy in 28 fx, 186, 140 MDACC 72/2.4 Gy in 30 fx, 187, 141 (Low esc conv 73.8/1.8 Gy) (162, 126) (Conventional 79.2/1.8 Gy) (174, 136) (Conventional 78/2 Gy) (182, 140) (MSKCC dose esc 86/1.8 Gy) (189, 148) …

Prostate.patients: high risk prostate cancer
Prostate.methods: Noninferiority ➜ 76 Gy/ 38 fx + 46 Gy WPRT vs. ➜ 68 Gy/2.72 in 25 fx + 45 Gy SIB WPRT All received 28 mos ADT Primary endpoints: acute and late adverse effects
Prostate.results: ✅ adverse effects similar (Due to low events, the prespecified noninferiority analysis was not done) grade 1+ late GI n=49 vs. 55 grade 2+ late GI n=12 vs. 15 grade 3+ late GI n= 0 vs. 3 grade 4-5 late GI n=0 grade 1+ late GU n=35 vs. 34 grade 2+ late GU n=11 vs. 3 (p=0.035) grade 3+ late GU n=3 vs. 1 grade 4-5 late GU n=0 10-yr adverse effects not different ✅ outcomes similar (secondary endpoints) 7-yr [BC] 86% 7-yr DM [8%] 7-yr [OS] 82% 7-yr [PCSS] 95% 10-yr results not different
Prostate.interpretation: adverse effects rates at 2 years are low in both hypofractionated RT with SIB to pelvis vs. conventional RT in high risk prostate cancer. Efficacy is similar. Due to low events in both arms, the prespecified noninferiority analysis was not able to be performed.
Prostate.commentary: In PCS 5 acute and late adverse effects were the primary endpoints; efficacy outcomes were secondary endpoints. After low adverse effects events in both arms, the noninferiority analysis was determined to be underpowered and dropped. Indeed, the trial seems as though it could be underpowered at 329 patients (FLAME had n=571, PACE 874, PROFIT 1200, and CHHIP 3200). RTOG 0415 used similar dosing to prostate only, 70 Gy/ 28 fx, and had slight increases in late adverse effects. Still, outcomes seem favorable and may be satisfactory enough to offer this hypofractionated regimen for some, though a f…

Prostate.patients: Intermediate risk prostate cancer
Prostate.methods: ➜ 78 Gy/39 fx in 2 Gy vs. ➜ ✅60 Gy/20 fx in 3 Gy No ADT Noninferiority
Prostate.results: 5-yr BPFS 85% in both arms No significant differences in late grade ≥3 GI or GU adverse effects
Prostate.interpretation: Hypofx 60 Gy/20 fx in 3 Gy per fx was noninferior to conventional dose escalated 78 Gy RT with no increase late in adverse effects.
Prostate.commentary: Out of the hypofractionation trials for prostate cancer, PROFIT has the best results: the control arm was dose escalated, it was a noninferiority trial rather than superiority, BPFS was noninferior, and adverse effects was not increased. No other hypofx trial holds these criteria. However it does not yet have the 10 year f/u results that some studies share. The MDACC, GETUG 18, and RTOG 0126 studies showed benefit in OS, PSCM, and DM with conventional dose escalation vs non-dose escalated conventional radiation. Prostate.methods: ➜ 73.8 Gy/41 fx in 1.8 Gy vs. ➜ 70 Gy/28 fx in 2.5 Gy Noninferiority ~80% IMRT
Prostate.results: 5-yr DFS 85% vs. 86%, HR 0.85 (noninferior) 12-yr DFS 56% vs. 62% 12-yr BC 83% vs. 90% No difference in patient assessed QOL in EPIC GI, GU, sexual domains or Hopkins Symptom anxiety or depression Late grade ≥2 GI 15.4% vs. 23.8% Late grade ≥2 GU 26.8% vs. 33.4% Late grade ≥3 GI 3.2% vs. 4.4% Late grade ≥3 GU 3.4% vs. 4.4%
Prostate.interpretation: Hypofx 70 Gy/28 fx is noninferior to conventional non-dose escalated RT per the study design. Efficacy outcomes tended to favor hypofx. There is no difference in patient assessed QOL. However there is increased late grade 2 GI and GU adverse effects with hypofx, and possibly slight increase grade 3 adverse effects.
Prostate.commentary: Late adverse effects was slightly increased in the hypofractionated arm. Some effects may be able to be mitigated with rectal spacer and IMRT, though the urethra and bladder neck will not be able to be avoided. The standard arm in this trial was not dose escalated, though BED of the hypofx arm is similar to conventional dose escalation. Conventional dose escalation studies show benefit in DM, PCSM, and OS in various studies (RTOG 0126, MDACC, GETUG 17). Hypofractionated schedules found to be equivalent or noninferior to non-dose-escalated conventional RT should be carefully considered. Dose co…

Prostate.citation: Incrocci et al, Lancet Oncol, 2016 Wortel et al, IJROBP, 2018 de Vries et al, IJROBP, 2019 Sinzabakira et al, IJROBP, 2021
Prostate.patients: T1b-T4Nx-0Mx-0, PSA 60 or less
Prostate.methods: ➜ 78 Gy/39 fx in 2.0 Gy vs. ➜ 64.6 Gy/19 fx in 3.4 Gy, 3 fx per week Superiority. Primary endpoint: RFS (67% received ADT)
Prostate.results: 7-yr RFS 68-72%, not different 7-yr OS 81% vs. 78%, p=0.17 On PROs, hypofrac had worse painful defecation, mucous, rectal bleeding, stool frequency, and urinary straining 3-yr Grade ≥2 GU 39% in standard vs. 41.3% in hypofx. HR 1.16 3-yr Grade ≥2 GI 17.7% vs. 21.9%. HR 1.19 Grade ≥3 GU late 12.9% vs. 19% Grade ≥3 GI late 2.6% vs. 3.3%
Prostate.interpretation: Hypofractionation was not superior to conventional fractionation RFS. Cannot confirm that hypofx 64.6 Gy/19 fx (3.4 Gy per fx, 3 fx per week) was not noninferior for late adverse effects. Late GU 3+ adverse effects and GI/GU PROs were worse in hypofrac arm. Cannot confirm that hypofx was not noninferior in GI and GU QOL.
Prostate.commentary: This hypofx regimen has highest BED of any prostate hypofx trial. GI late effects may be able to be mitigated with IMRT and rectal spacer, though the urethra and bladder neck cannot be avoided.

Prostate.citation: Arcanjeli et al, IJROBP, 2012 Arcanjeli et al, JCO, 2017
Prostate.patients: High risk prostate
Prostate.methods: ➜ 80 Gy/40 fx in 2.0 Gy vs. ➜ 62 Gy/20 fx in 3.1 Gy 9 months ADT Superiority, endpoint late adverse effects
Prostate.results: Overall 5-yr FFBF 79% vs. 85%, p=.065 PSA<20 5-yr FFBF 95% vs. 83%, p=.02 PSA>20 5-yr FFBF 73% vs. 72% (NS) Overall 5-yr FFDF 86% vs. 90% (NS) PSA<20 5-yr FFDF 98% vs. 87%, p=0.04 If PSA 20 or less, hypofx significantly better in bPFS, LC, and DMFS. 10-yr FFBF 65% vs. 72% (NS) 10-yr OS 64% vs. 75% (NS) 10-yr CSS 88% vs. 95%, p=0.066 No difference in late grade ≥2 GI or GU On MVA, hypofx, pretreatment PSA, Gleason score, and stage impacted FFBF and hypofx and Gleason score impacted CSS
Prostate.interpretation: At long term f/u, hypofx did not maintain outcomes and did not reduce late adverse effects. MVA showed that hypofx 62 Gy/20 fx in 3.1 Gy per fx did improve FFBF and CSS compared to dose escalated 78 Gy, but this analysis is nonrandomized.
Prostate.commentary: The trial is possibly underpowered. Noninferiority would have been a better design. In PROFIT, hypofx 60 Gy/20 fx in 3 Gy per fx - a regimen with lower dose than Regina Elina's 62 Gy/20 fx - was noninferior to conventional dose escalated 78 Gy RT with no increase late in adverse effects.

Prostate.methods: Noninferiority ➜ 78 Gy/39 fx or 62 Gy/20 fx to prostate vs. ➜ 🏆 SBRT 36.25 Gy/5 fx to PTV, 40 Gy to CTV SIB to prostate Primary endpoint: noninferiority of BPFS No ADT SpaceOAR not allowed CyberKnife (41%) or VMAT SBRT were allowed. Fiducials were not required. 5 fx daily or alternating days permitted
Prostate.results: 5-yr BPFS 94.6% vs. 95.8% 5-yr DM 1% both arms Cumulative RTOG (text & Fig S10) 5-yr Late GU grade ≥2, 18% vs. 27% 5-yr Late GI grade ≥2, 10.2% vs. 10.7% Cumulative CTCAE (Fig S10) 5-yr Late GU grade ≥2, 29% vs. 42% 5-yr Late GI grade ≥2, 19% both arms Late effects were nearly all grade 1-2 In SBRT there were 1.4% grade 3 GU RTOG events, and 1.7% CTCAE events There were no grade 3 GI events, and no grade 4-5 events of any kind Worst CTCAE ED (text and Fig S9) 5-yr CTCAE grade ≥2 ED 29.1% vs. 26.4% 5-yr CTCAE grade 3 ED 6.1% vs. 4.4% No grade 4 ED Acute GI or GU adverse effects Grade 2+ acute G…
Prostate.interpretation: SBRT is noninferior to conventional fractionation in BPFS. There is slight increased risk of GU adverse events with five fraction radiation. Overall adverse effects with both SBRT and conventionally fractionated radiotherapy is low. There were no grade 3-5 GI adverse effects, and SpaceOAR was not permitted. ADT was also not permitted, showing that good results can be achieved in unfavorable intermediate risk prostate cancer without it. Of note Gleason 4+3=7 was excluded. Acute GI & GU adverse effects were predictive of late adverse effects.
Prostate.commentary: This randomized trial finally established SBRT as a standard treatment option. Surprisingly extra dose escalation doesn't offer an additional benefit in bFPS, but could at later follow-up. There is some increased risk of acute and late adverse effects with 5 fractions. Conventional fractionation can be useful for patients who want the lowest risk of adverse effects possible, but the increased risk is not enough to only offer conventional. Clinicians should also be mindful of deciding for the patient that grade 1-2 adverse effects don't matter: sleep is one of the most important functions the b…

Prostate.patients: Non-metastatic prostate cancer
Prostate.methods: Meta-analysis of 11 trials evaluating addition of ADT to RT
Prostate.results: •Addition of any ADT 10-yr OS benefit of +7.7%, HR 0.86 10-yr MFS benefit of +8.6%, HR 0.83 10-yr BR benefit of +14.4%, HR 0.61 •Addition of LT adjuvant ADT 10-yr OS benefit +5.6%, HR 0.85 10-yr MFS benefit +7.7%, HR 0.84 10-yr BF benefit of 16.0%, HR 0.56 MFS benefit in high risk HR 0.85 MFS benefit in int risk HR 0.81 •adjuvant LT-ADT NNT to prevent 1 DM intermediate risk NNT 16 high risk NNT 10 •prolonged neoadjuvant ADT >2 months No benefit in MFS, OS, or BF No benefit in MFS any risk group on subanalysis On subanalysis, no effect of RT dose on benefit of ADT
Prostate.interpretation: ADT improves OS and MFS in prostate cancer. The benefit of ADT remains regardless of RT dose. Use of ADT may also be individualized, keeping in mind the risk of adverse effects and QOL. Prolongation of neoadjuvant ADT >2 months provides no benefit compared to 2 mos ADT neoadjuvant immediately and should not be prescribed.
Prostate.commentary: The authors point out that due to stage and grade migration, today's intermediate risk may be less advanced than those in some of the studies in this analysis. LT-ADT here includes a mix of durations including 18, 28, and 36 months. Refer to the respective trials for specific numbers for each duration, but some trials may not include analyses for modern risk criteria. Although there was no benefit to prolongation of neoadjuvant ADT, this does not necessarily rule out benefit to prolongation of neoadjuvant ADT in order to delay therapy; note that the randomization for shorter neoadjuvant ADT wa…

Prostate.patients: Prostate cancer receiving neoadjuvant, concurrent, or adjuvant ADT
Prostate.methods: Meta-analysis of RTOG 9413 and Ottawa 101 ➜ neoadjuvant or concurrent ADT vs. ➜ 🏆adjuvant ADT
Prostate.results: 15-yr PFS 29% vs. 36% favoring adjuvant DM, MFS, and BF all improved with adjuvant No difference in late ≥3 GI or GU adverse effects
Prostate.interpretation: Sequencing ADT adjuvantly results in better DM, PFS, and BF than neoadjuvant or concurrent treatment.
Prostate.commentary: This combined reanalysis of two trials delivers a result that challenges conventional wisdom, that ADT should be administered after radiation. Such a method may cause practitioners anxiety, that start to treatment is being delayed while fiducials and radiation planning are underway, or while healing takes place for adjuvant radiation when PSA is perhaps even as low as 0.05. Should the results be doubted because the combined analysis wasn't preplanned? Cancer cells don't know if an analysis was planned or not. "P hacking" is an artifact of human imagination, a term for inevitable type 1 and 2 e…

Prostate.subtopic: Whole pelvis RT, ADT neoadjuvant or adjuvant
Prostate.citation: Roach et al, JCO, 2003 Roach et al, IJROBP, 2006 Lawton et al, IJROBP, 2007 Roach et al, Lancet Oncol, 2018
Prostate.patients: PSA <100 and risk of LN involvement >15% risk per Roach formula T2c-T4 with Gleason 6 eligible regardless of Roach calculation
Prostate.methods: ➜ Neoadj conc ADT vs. ➜ adj ADT ➜ Prostate only RT vs. ➜ WPRT Powered to compare each randomization, not all four arms WPRT 50.4 Gy, prostate boost 70.2 Gy, ADT 4 months total. Some patients had "mini-pelvis" RT and some had whole pelvis
Prostate.results: •4-yr PFS PO RT 47% vs. WPRT 54%, p=0.022. This effect was lost on the update at 6.6 yr follow-up. •4-yr PFS NC ADT vs. adj ADT ~50%, not different •No change in OS. •Exploratory comparison of all 4 arms: 4-yr PFS improved with WPRT + NC ADT over other 3 arms, 60% vs. 44-50% (but not powered for 4 arm comparison) •At median 6.6 yr f/u, no difference in PFS with NC ADT vs. adj ADT, or with WPRT vs. PO RT (the prespecified analysis). •But when comparing 4 arms, PFS benefit with WPRT + NC ADT over other arms. •On 4 arm comparison, OS worse in WPRT + adj ADT, p=0.027 •No difference in DM •Late Gra…
Prostate.interpretation: On the prespecified analysis, WPRT improved 4-yr PFS over PO RT, but the effect was lost at later follow-up. ADT can be delivered neoadj-concurrent or adjuvant. There were no differences in PFS between delivery times in the prespecified analysis. There was no benefit in DM at any follow-up point. Post-hoc analyses to compare the four arms together at long term f/u resulted in unexpected interactions. WPRT+adj ADT led to worse PFS and OS. At 10 years the best PFS was seen in PO RT + adj ADT and WPRT + NC ADT. The trial was not powered or designed for this comparison.
Prostate.commentary: This trial has a history of controversy due to its statistical approaches. An important finding of 9413 that is often overlooked was that ADT can be administered either before or after radiation without differences. A later combined analysis of 9413 and an Ottawa trial reported that outcomes are superior when ADT is delivered after radiation (Spratt 2020). The WPRT randomization was later repeated in RTOG 0924 with stricter eligibility criteria and reproduced no difference. PSMA PET was not used in the study. POP-RT had higher risk patients than 9413 and used PSMA PET staging. The trial found …

Prostate.subtopic: Adjuvant LT ADT vs. int length ADT
Prostate.patients: High risk: T3-4, PSA>20, or GS>7 (~40% had Gleason 8, 18% Gleason 9, and 1-2% Gleason 10. 27% had Gleason 7 and 13% Gleason 6. PSA >20 in 45%.) Modern high risk
Prostate.methods: Superiority ➜ ADT 18 mos starting 4 mos neoadj vs. ➜ ADT 36 mos starting 4 mos neoadj RT to 70 Gy with WPRT 44 Gy (4.5 Gy per week hypofractionation).
Prostate.results: 10-yr OS 62%, not different 5-yr OS 86% vs. 91%, p=0.07 DM 3%, not different (calc from table 3) 10-yr BC 69% vs. 75%, SS 5-yr BC 84% vs. 88%, p=0.12 10-yr DFS 39% vs. 45%, p=0.055 5-yr DFS 69% vs. 77%, SS 10-yr DSS 92%, 5–yr 98%, not different QOL favored 18 mos ADT Testosterone recovery 3.6 vs. 6.6 yrs. With 18 mos ADT about 75% recover at about 5 years
Prostate.interpretation: 36 months of ADT is not superior to 18 months in high risk prostate cancer.
Prostate.commentary: The compliance rate of 36 mos ADT was only 53%, further reinforcing the realistically low feasibility of the approach. The trial might have been better designed as noninferiority. Some have interpreted the trial in such a way, and consider 18 mos ADT for high risk prostate cancer as a risk vs adverse effects decision. Quadshot editors comment that if the trial was designed as noninferiority, it does not appear that the delta range would have been reached at the five year timepoint. ADT leads to a change in QOL with significant adverse effects, and recovery is markedly prolonged with 36 mos ADT…

Prostate.citation: Zapatero et al, Lancet Oncol, 2015 Zapatero et al, IJROBP, 2016 Zapatero et al, Lancet Oncol, 2022
Prostate.patients: Prostate T1-T3, Gleason >6, PSA <100 53% high risk, 47% int risk
Prostate.methods: ➜ 28 mos ADT + 76-82 Gy RT (includes 4 mos ADT neoadjuvant) vs. ➜ 4 mos NCADT + 76-82 Gy RT WPRT not permitted
Prostate.results: Median RT dose 78 Gy 5-yr bDFS improved with LTADT: 90% vs. 81% 10-yr bDFS 70% vs. 62% (CI crosses 1) 5-yr OS benefit: 95% vs. 86% 10-yr OS 78% vs. 73% (CI crosses 1) 5-yr DM 6% vs. 17% 10-yr MFS 76% vs. 71% On subanalysis, benefit in OS and DM only seen in high risk group (BF was close, p=0.054) High risk 5-yr bDFS 88% vs. 76% (p=0.054) 10-yr bDFS 67% vs. 54%, p=0.03 5-yr OS 96% vs. 82% 10-yr OS 79% vs. 67% (p=0.056) 5-yr DM 6% vs. 21% 10-yr MFS 77% vs. 65%, p=0.069 Int risk 5-yr bDFS 92% vs. 88% (p=0.174) 5-yr OS 94% vs. 91% (p=0.318) 5-yr DM 6% vs. 11%(p=0.124) No difference in early or lat…
Prostate.interpretation: There is benefit in OS and DM when adding LT-ADT to high risk patients with dose escalated RT over adding ST-ADT. Dose escalation does not negate the benefit of ADT.
Prostate.commentary: This trial is one of few trials that evaluated dose escalated RT with ADT. This study reports modern outcomes for BF. Note the long term data. There are some that unfortunately eventually will fail later. Could this be remedied by focal dose escalation (FLAME), SBRT, HDR, or other kinds of ADT? Even though there was not a statistically significant benefit to LT-ADT in int risk, the p values are suggestive. This study has relatively more modern inclusion criteria than others though very high PSA was allowed.

Prostate.patients: Intermediate risk prostate cancer
Prostate.methods: Meta-analysis of 7 randomized trials ➜ RT alone vs. ➜ 🏆 RT with ≤6 mos ADT
Prostate.results: 5-yr OS 87% vs. 90% 10-yr OS 64% vs. 69% 15-yr OS 42% vs. 44% OS HR 0.88, BF HR 0.50, BF+bPFS HR 0.54 On subgroup analysis, ADT duration, RT dose, and Gleason score were associated with improved PSA response, but no factor was associated with OS
Prostate.interpretation: Adding short term ADT to radiation for intermediate risk prostate cancer improves OS, PFS, and BF.
Prostate.commentary: This meta analysis provides very good evidence for an OS benefit for ADT in intermediate risk prostate cancer, shortly after RTOG 0815 showed a 5-yr DM benefit but no benefit in 5-yr OS. All trials were prior to the widespread implementation of PSMA PET, therefore some tumors could be understaged and show more than expected benefit. Similarly in all prostate trials and meta-analyses innovations will advance faster than the trials, and more next line therapies are now available since the publication of the individual trials, lengthening OS in both arms. It's unclear if the results apply to all …

Prostate.subtopic: Neoadj ST ADT intermediate risk w dose escalation
Prostate.patients: intermediate risk T2b-T2c, Gleason 7, and/or PSA 10-20 (33% had 2-3 risk factors, and 27% had Gleason 4+3=7) Ineligible: all three risk factors or ≥50% cores positive
Prostate.methods: ➜ 79.2 Gy vs. ➜ 79.2 Gy + 6 mos ADT 45 Gy + brachy boost also allowed (12%)
Prostate.results: 5-yr BF 21% vs. 10% 5-yr DM 4% vs. 1% 5-yr OS 79-84%, not different 5-yr PCM n=10 vs. 1 On MVA there was DM benefit with both one risk factor and 2-3 risk factors. There was no benefit in Gleason 2-6. There was no OS benefit in any subgroup.
Prostate.interpretation: Short term ADT added to dose escalated radiation improves PCM and DM, but not OS in favorable and unfavorable intermediate risk prostate cancer. There was no benefit in those with Gleason score less than 7.
Prostate.commentary: The improvement at 5 years seems small, but risk of DM without ADT may continue to increase and curves continue to separate with longer follow-up. Intermediate risk patients are often expected to live long, and in that setting the case for ADT may become stronger. Because so many next line therapies are available, an OS benefit may not show until later, or may not be present at all. Note that some unfavorable intermediate risk were eligible and there were DM improvements in both (if Gleason score was 7) but not OS. Those excluded had all three risk factors or ≥50% cores positive. 33% had 2-3 r…

Prostate.interpretation: Short term ADT improves 10 year OS, CSS, BFS, DM, and pathologic tumor clearance in prostate cancer T1b-T2b, PSA≤20. At 18 years the OS benefit is lost but BF and DM benefits persist. Risk stratification and staging have since changed since this trial. Low, intermediate, and high risk were all included. On exploratory analysis, the benefit with ADT seems to only be in unfavorable intermediate risk. The data supports subcategorization of intermediate risk.
Prostate.commentary: RT dose was low, 66.6 Gy. Still, note the low disease specific mortality. 20% positive biopsies at 2 years with combined treatment. Radiation may leave some indolent and less aggressive disease present after treatment. This trial used more modern risk stratification.

Prostate.results: •4-yr PFS PO RT 47% vs. WPRT 54%, p=0.022. This effect was lost on the update at 6.6 yr follow-up. •4-yr PFS NC ADT vs. adj ADT ~50%, not different •No change in OS. •Exploratory comparison of all 4 arms: 4-yr PFS improved with WPRT + NC ADT over other 3 arms, 60% vs. 44-50% (but not powered for 4 arm comparison) •At median 6.6 yr f/u, no difference in PFS with NC ADT vs. adj ADT, or with WPRT vs. PO RT (the prespecified analysis). •But when comparing 4 arms, PFS benefit with WPRT + NC ADT over other arms. •On 4 arm comparison, OS worse in WPRT + adj ADT, p=0.027 •No difference in DM •Late Gra…
Prostate.interpretation: On the prespecified analysis, WPRT improved 4-yr PFS over PO RT, but the effect was lost at later follow-up. ADT can be delivered neoadj-concurrent or adjuvant. There were no differences in PFS between delivery times in the prespecified analysis. There was no benefit in DM at any follow-up point. Post-hoc analyses to compare the four arms together at long term f/u resulted in unexpected interactions. WPRT+adj ADT led to worse PFS and OS. At 10 years the best PFS was seen in PO RT + adj ADT and WPRT + NC ADT. The trial was not powered or designed for this comparison.
Prostate.commentary: This trial has a history of controversy due to its statistical approaches. An important finding of 9413 that is often overlooked was that ADT can be administered either before or after radiation without differences. A later combined analysis of 9413 and an Ottawa trial reported that outcomes are superior when ADT is delivered after radiation (Spratt 2020). The WPRT randomization was later repeated in RTOG 0924 with stricter eligibility criteria and reproduced no difference. PSMA PET was not used in the study. POP-RT had higher risk patients than 9413 and used PSMA PET staging. The trial found …

Urologists believed a positive margin or EPE could be benign, and rad oncs worried the worst cells could be growing for months. A meta-analysis of timing of salvage radiation had shown worse prognosis with rising PSA, particularly above 0.2 (King 2012). Prostate.methods: ➜ adjuvant RT vs. ➜ 🏆 salvage RT at PSA failure (defined as PSA >0.1 & rising, or any PSA rising x3) ➜ ADT none vs. 6 mos vs. ➜ 🏆 24 mos -RT 66 Gy/ 33 fx or 52.5 Gy/ 20 fx. WPRT 46 Gy optional
Prostate.results: 🔀 Adjuvant vs. salvage 10-yr DM 7% vs. 10%, p=0.095 10-yr PFS ~75%, not different No change in OS Adjuvant started at median 4.9 mos (IQR 4.1-5.6) Salvage started at median 1.5 years worse GI and GU incontinence with adjuvant urethral stricture 13% vs. 6% 🔀 no ADT vs. 6 mos: no improvement in MFS 🔀 6 vs. 24 mos ADT: 10-yr MFS 72% vs. 78% On subanalysis no interaction by PSA level, Gleason score, margins, RT fractionation, adjuvant vs salvage, or type of ADT No benefit in OS 🔀 66 Gy/33 fx vs. 52.5 Gy/20 fx (not randomized) grade 1-2 cystitis at 1-2 years 30% vs. 20%, p=0.04 grade 1-2 cystitis a…
Prostate.interpretation: Early salvage RT is preferred. There is no difference in BPFS or DM between adjuvant RT and early salvage RT. Adjuvant RT leads to worse adverse effects. 24 months ADT shows improved MFS over 6 mos ADT. There is no benefit in MFS with 6 mos ADT over none. 52.5 Gy has less adverse effects than 66 Gy in this nonrandomized comparison. Both regimens were well tolerated.
Prostate.commentary: There is actually trend to benefit in DM with adjuvant radiation, but results are statistically insignificant, and there is no benefit in 10-yr PFS. Does ADT improve outcomes in salvage RT? Results are mixed but seem to favor 24 mos ADT. In RADICALS, the largest trial, 24 mos ADT vs. none improved MFS, but 6 mos ADT vs. none showed no improvement. There was no difference in benefit based on subanalysis of PSA, Gleason score, or margins. There was no benefit in OS. In RTOG 9601, 24 mos bicalutamide improved DM and OS. In RTOG SPORT, 6 mos ADT + prostate bed RT vs. prostate bed RT did not improv…

Prostate.patients: T2-3 with or without positive margin Excludes T2 with negative margin and PSA <0.1. Excludes nadir ≥0.2 and Gleason ≥7
Prostate.methods: ➜ 66.6 Gy/ 37 fx (EQD2 = 63) vs. ➜ 62.5 Gy/25 fx in 2.5 per fx (EQD2 = 71) ADT of ≤6 months allowed WPRT not permitted
Prostate.results: Mean GU change scores not different after RT and at 6, 12, and 24 months Mean GI changes scores worse with 62.5 Gy/ 25 fx after RT then not different after RT and at 6, 12, and 24 months Grade ≥3 adverse effects 6% vs. 9% 2.1-yr BF 8% vs. 12%, p=0.28
Prostate.interpretation: Hypofractionated RT is noninferior to conventional RT for patient reported late adverse effects at 2-years. However some institutions have reported severe late toxicty at long term follow-up (see commentary). There were increased GI adverse effects at the end of RT, but scores returned to baseline at 6 months.
Prostate.commentary: Bladder minus CTV V65 <50%, V40 <70% (Var Acc V40<77 and V65<55) Rectum V40 <55%, V65 <35% It's unclear why a hypofrac regimen with higher EQD2 was chosen. One of the institutions that originally investigated the regimen reported their 13-year follow-up showing 34% grade 3-5 toxiciy (Ranta IJROBP 2025). Two years is a short follow-up period. Recall also that PACE B showed that acute GI & GU adverse effects were predictive of late adverse effects.

Prostate.patients: PSA ≥0.1 and <2.0, and T2-3N0/Nx, with or without positive margin, Gleason ≤9
Prostate.methods: ➜ prostate bed RT 64.8-70.2 Gy vs. ➜ PB RT + 4-6 mos ADT vs. ➜ 🏆 WPRT 45 Gy + PB RT + 4-6 mos ADT Primary endpoint: FFP. BF defined per Phoenix definition p<0.0125 required for significance Protocol dose constraints pdf, p29: https://classic.clinicaltrials.gov/ProvidedDocs/80/NCT00567580/Prot_SAP_000.pdf#page=29
Prostate.results: 5-yr FFP 71% vs. 81% vs. 87% (group 2 & 3 > group 1) 5-yr DM 9% vs. 6% vs. 5% (group 3 > group 1) 5-yr BF (PSA ≥0.4) 31% vs. 21% vs. 13% 5-yr BF (Phoenix) 20% vs. 14% vs. 8% (cumulative, group 3 & 2 > group 1) (Phoenix definition is now outdated due to PSMA PET) 5-yr OS 94-96%, not different 5-yr castrate resistance 3% vs. 2% vs. 1% (cumulative, group 3 > groups 1 & 2) 5-yr regional failure 5% vs. 2% vs. 1% (cumulative, groups 3 & 2 > group 1) Late grade 2+ adverse effects (>3 mos) not different, except for worse BM adverse effects with WPRT
Prostate.interpretation: Addition of ADT and WPRT results in improved FFP compared to prostate bed RT alone without ADT. GI and GU adverse effects are not worsened. Secondary endpoints of DM, BF, castrate resistance, and regional failure are also improved.
Prostate.commentary: The authors chose the Phoenix definition to define BF, and also report results with 0.4. Which one is better? Good luck explaining to your patients that 2 above the nadir is failure when they've been meticulously monitoring it up to 0.2, which is what they were told was failure before starting. Failure rates are increased when using a more conservative definition of ≥0.4: BF with prostate only RT was 30%! See also in the figures that failures continue to rise. PSMA PET has changed our definition of failure. New oligometastasis could be potentially detected with PSMA PET at PSA of 0.5 (or even …

Prostate.patients: salvage prostate
Prostate.methods: Meta-analysis OS and MFS in RADICALS, RTOG 9601, GETUG/AFU 16, GETUG/AFU 22, SPPORT ➜ no ADT vs. ➜ 6 mos ADT vs. ➜ 24 mos ADT
Prostate.results: ADT improved MFS and PCSS No duration of ADT improved OS. There may be some OS benefit in higher PSAs and higher CAPRA-S scores
Prostate.interpretation: ADT improves MFS and PCSS in salvage prostate, but not OS. There may be some OS benefit in those with higher risk factors. Risk and benefit should be discussed with the patient.
Prostate.commentary: One of the studies within this meta analyses, RTOG 9601, did show an OS benefit with 24 mos ADT at long term follow-up. The CAPRA S score is a prognostic system to assess recurrence risk in salvage prostate. It combines PSA, Gleason score, margin, EPE, SVI, and node involvement (Cooperberg 2011).

Prostate.patients: 2 rises in PSA and PSA >0.1 or any PSA after 3 rises, s/p RP with nadir of ≤0.4. Current PSA ≤2 and no gross disease (45% had positive margins)
Prostate.methods: ➜ 🏆 64 Gy/32 fx vs. ➜ 70 Gy/35 fx Prostate bed RT only. No WPRT allowed No ADT in this trial 3DCRT or IMRT
Prostate.results: 6-yr bPFS 61-62%, not different No difference in PFS, OS, or time to ADT Late grade 2 GU 21% vs. 26% Late grade 3 GU 8% vs. 4% (p=0.81) Late grade 2 GI 7% vs. 20% (p=0.009) Late grade 3 GI 4% vs. 2%
Prostate.interpretation: Salvage prostate RT to 70 Gy leads to no benefit in bPFS compared to 64 Gy, and results in worse GI adverse effects.
Prostate.commentary: There was no subanalysis of factors such as positive margin or grade. There is a possibility that the trial is underpowered for some characteristics. 45% had positive margins. 56-57% had IMRT. Gleason 6 in 15%, Gleason 7 in about 66%, and Gleason ≥8 in ~18%. The median time from surgery to RT start was 26-30 months! The radiation was directed to prostate bed only. WPRT and ADT were not allowed. rectal wall V70<20%, V60<50% bladder wall V65<50%

Prostate.patients: pT3N0 or T2N0 with positive margins, with elevated PSA 0.2-4.0 after RP PSA failure defined as >0.5 if PSA was 0, or >0.3 above nadir
Prostate.methods: ➜ 🏆 RT with 24 mos bicalutamide conc and adj vs. ➜ RT 64.8 Gy to prostate fossa
Prostate.results: 10-yr FFP: improved 46% vs. 30% BPFS improvement in GS<7, GS 7, and GS 8-10 12-yr OS improved 76% vs. 71% 18-yr OS 53% vs. 43% 12-yr DM 14.% vs. 23.0% 18-yr DM 22% vs. 31% No OS or DM benefit in PSA ≤0.6 (Dess 2020) 12-yr PC death 6% vs. 13% 18-yr PC death 18% vs. 28% On subgroup analysis, 18-yr DM benefit in PSA >1.5, Gleason 8-10, and positive margin OS benefit in PSA <0.7 with high Decipher score Late grade 3-4 GI adverse effects similar at 7% Late grade 3-4 GU adverse effects similar, 2.7 vs 1.6% Gynecomastia 70% vs. 11%
Prostate.interpretation: Adding bicalutamide to RT improves FFP, OS, CSS, and DM. On subgroup analysis, 18-yr DM benefit in PSA >1.5, Gleason 8-10, and positive margin.
Prostate.commentary: Some of the PSA values in this study were considerably higher than in modern salvage, where many treat at PSA of 0.2. In this study 85% had PSA<1.6, and 15% had PSA of 1.6-4. A secondary publication showed no OS benefit with 2 years bicalutamide in PSA ≤0.6 with PSA as a continuous variable. There is a possibility that a higher powered study might show OS differences with PSA by Gleason scores. PSA failure was defined as a PSA rise of ≥0.5. In RADICALS there was no change in the MFS benefit of 24 mos ADT by PSA score on subanalysis. There was no OS benefit. There was also no OS benefit with 6 …

Prostate.citation: Carrie et al, Lancet, 2016 Carrie et al, Lancet Oncol, 2019
Prostate.patients: pT2-T4a (bladder neck only) with rising PSA of 0.2-2.0
Prostate.methods: ➜ 66 Gy RT alone vs. ➜ 🏆 RT with 6 mos ADT 46 Gy WPRT given if Partin score ≥10% before RP and no LND Endpoints: PFS, OS
Prostate.results: ADT reduced DM, but did not improve OS 5, 10-yr PFS 62%/49% vs. 80%/64% ADT 10-yr DM 31% vs. 25% ADT No change in OS Grade 3 GU 8% vs. 7% On exploratory subanalysis, no clear difference in any subgroup with all HRs crossing 1, but general trend favors ADT in all groups. There was better benefit in PSA >0.5 in initial publication
Prostate.interpretation: Adding ADT to salvage radiation improves DM and PFS.
Prostate.commentary: Does ADT improve outcomes in salvage RT? Results are mixed but seem to favor 24 mos ADT. In RADICALS, the largest trial, 24 mos ADT vs. none improved MFS, but 6 mos ADT vs. none showed no improvement. There was no difference in benefit based on subanalysis of PSA, Gleason score, or margins. There was no benefit in OS. In RTOG 9601, 24 mos bicalutamide improved DM and OS. In RTOG SPORT, 6 mos ADT + prostate bed RT vs. prostate bed RT did not improve DM, but 6 mos ADT + WPRT did. In GETUG 16, 6 mos ADT improved DM but not OS.

Prostate.subtopic: Docetaxel for high risk, after RT
Prostate.citation: Rosenthal et al, JCO, 2019 Sandler et al, ASCO, 2020
Prostate.patients: High risk 1) GS 7-8, any T, PSA>20 2) GS 9-10, any T, any PSA All had PSA<150
Prostate.methods: ➜ IMRT 72-75.6 Gy + 24 mos ADT + docetaxel x6 adjuvant vs. ➜ RT+ADT without chemo
Prostate.results: Chemo improves OS and DM at 4 years, but not different at 10 years 4-yr OS 93% vs. 89% 6-yr DM 9% vs. 14% 6-yr DFS 65% vs. 55% 6-yr BC 79% vs. 75% 10-yr RMST difference 0.42, p=0.048 10-yr DM 20% vs. 22%, p=0.29 10-yr deaths n=62 vs. 50, p=0.16, HR crosses 1
Prostate.interpretation: Chemotherapy improves OS and DM in high risk prostate cancer. However at 10 year follow-up the benefit appears to be lost.
Prostate.commentary: 53% were Gleason score 9-10. No subanalyses were performed to explore whether benefits are present in some groups more than others. Effect seems lost at 10-yr f/u. There is not even clear benefit of DM at 10 years. The endpoint of RMST (restricted mean survival time) was not pre-specified. Do 4-yr OS and DM matter if there is no difference at 10 years?

Prostate.interpretation: ADT added to RT improved BC and OS in locally advanced prostate cancer, including node positive.

Prostate.results: 10-yr PCM 24% ADT vs. 12% RT+ADT 10-yr OM 39% vs. 30% 10-yr BF 75% vs. 26%
Prostate.interpretation: RT added to ADT improves OS and CSS in high risk prostate cancer.

Prostate.citation: James et al, JAMA Oncol, 2016 Ali et al, JAMA Oncol, 2021
Prostate.patients: Must have 2/3: stage T3/T4, PSA >40, GS 8-10, with intention to treat with RT OR newly diagnosed node postitive disease or mets High met burden: visceral mets and/or ≥4 bone mets with ≥1 outside the vertebra or pelvis Low met burden: anything less than high burden
Prostate.methods: A adaptive trial that opens new arms and closes accrued arms with time. Nodal arms only shown here. ADT in all plus one of the following: Nonregional nodal mets: ➜ 🏆 RT to prostate (mets not treated) vs. ➜ no RT Regional nodes: ➜ 🏆 RT to prostate plus WPRT vs. ➜ no RT (not randomized; a protocol amendment changed RT requirements) RT choice of 55 Gy/20 fx or 36 Gy/6 fx once weekly
Prostate.results: 🔀 nonregional nodal mets: RT to prostate vs. no RT 3-yr OS 80% vs. 73% 3-yr FFS 51% vs. 29% 🔀 regional nodes: RT to prostate plus WPRT vs. no RT (not randomized; a protocol amendment changed RT requirements): 2-yr FFS 89% vs. 64%
Prostate.interpretation: RT to prostate only in nonregional N+ seems to improve FFS. RT to prostate plus WPRT in regional N+ improves FFS.
Prostate.commentary: The accuracy of the findings is limited due to self reporting from the patient, no randomization for node positive, optional WPRT, no nodal boost specifications in the protocol, and many of those who were planned to receive RT offered no reporting. Still it provides some preliminary support for radiation in node positive. For node positive, receipt of RT and field were apparently self reported by the patient per my interpretation of the manuscript (I reached out to the authors but did not receive a response). The protocol states directly that it's not their objective to collect RT details. Abo…

Prostate.methods: ➜ Indefinite goserelin/orchiectomy vs. ➜ obs, with ADT to be given if symptomatic recurrence or DM
Prostate.results: ADT Improved OS, DSS, PFS, and BF Median OS 13.9 yrs vs. 11.3 yrs
Prostate.interpretation: Early adjuvant ADT improves OS and BC over observation with salvage ADT for clinical progression (not PSA progression) for node positive prostate cancer after surgery.

Prostate.results: Two groups had CSS benefit from RT 1) ≤2 positive nodes with Gleason score 7-10, pT3b-T4, or positive margins, p=0.002 2) 3-4 nodes positive regardless of other factors, p=0.02
Prostate.interpretation: RT improves CSS in prostate cancer with 1-2 positive nodes with Gleason score 7-10, pT3b-T4 ot positive margins, and those with 3-4 nodes positive regardless of other factors.

Prostate.citation: James et al, JAMA Oncol, 2016 James et al, Lancet, 2016 James et al, NEJM, 2017 Parker et al, Lancet, 2018 Ali et al, JAMA Oncol, 2021 Parker et al, PloS Med, 2022
Prostate.patients: Must have 2/3: stage T3/T4, PSA >40, GS 8-10, with intention to treat with RT OR newly diagnosed node positive disease or mets High met burden: visceral mets and/or ≥4 bone mets with ≥1 outside the vertebra or pelvis Low met burden: anything less than high burden
Prostate.methods: A adaptive trial that opens new arms and closes accrued arms with time ADT in all plus one of the following: ➜ prostate RT (RT required in non metastatic patients) ➜ zoledronic acid ➜ docetaxol ➜ ZA plus docetaxol ➜ celecoxib ➜ abiraterone ➜ metformin ➜ enzalutamide RT choice of 55 Gy/20 fx or 36 Gy/6 fx once weekly
Prostate.results: 🔀 ≤3 bone mets ±nonregional nodal mets (post-hoc analysis): RT to prostate vs. no RT 3-yr OS 85% vs. 75% 3-yr FFS 53% vs. 33% 🔀 "low met burden" (prespecified analysis): RT to prostate vs. no RT 3-yr OS 81% vs. 73% 5-yr OS 65% vs. 53% -Median OS 85.5 vs. 63.6 mos -No change in severe adverse effects -5% grade 3-4 during RT, 4% grade 3-4 after RT -No difference in SBRT vs. hypofx 🔀 nonregional nodal mets: RT to prostate only vs. no RT 3-yr OS 80% vs. 73% 3-yr FFS 51% vs. 29% 🔀 regional nodes: RT to prostate plus WPRT vs. no RT (not randomized; a protocol amendment changed RT requirements): 2-yr…
Prostate.interpretation: There is OS and FFS benefit with RT to prostate in those with ≤3 bone mets or nonregional LN metastasis. There is no OS or FFS benefit in those with ≥4 bone lesions. There is no benefit for visceral metastases. There was better FFS benefit with 55 Gy/20 fx over 36 Gy in weekly fractions. RT to prostate plus WPRT in regional N+ seems to improve FFS. RT to prostate only in nonregional N+ also improves FFS. Docetaxol in locally advanced, N+, and M+ improves OS. There is benefit in low and high burden metastatic disease. Abiraterone for high risk non-metastatic prostate cancer improves MFS, PCSS, …
Prostate.commentary: The analysis of "low met burden" was the planned analysis, while the others evaluating number of mets and nonregional nodes were exploratory. Observe that the definition of low metastatic burden actually allows for some with a greater number of mets, such as >5, to be included given these are within the axial skeleton. Both definitions may be used to guide treatment decisions. In the future we may learn more about who best qualifies for oligometastatic treatment, and if location, volume, and/or quantity matter most. There was better FFS benefit with 55 Gy/20 fx over 36 Gy in weekly fx on suban…

Prostate.patients: metastatic prostate cancer (including subanalysis of those with low volume)
Prostate.methods: ➜ standard arm: ADT + docetaxel (prior to an amendment, ADT alone was also allowed) vs. ➜ standard + abiraterone vs. ➜ standard + 74 Gy/ 37 fx vs. ➜ 🏆 standard + abiraterone + 74 Gy
Prostate.results: Low met burden & RT: 3-yr PFS 3.0 yrs SOC vs. 4.4 abi vs. 2.6 RT vs. 7.5 RT+abi (significant for RT abi vs. abi, p=0.019) Median OS 6.9 yrs no RT vs. 7.5 yrs RT, p=0.81 Median OS 7.1 SOC vs. not reached RT+abi+SOC Overall population Median PFS 2.2 yrs SOC vs. 4.5 abi Median CSS 5.8 yrs vs. not reached ADT with docetaxel population Median PFS 2.0 yrs vs. 4.5 yrs Median OS in low met burden not reached Median OS high mets 3.5 vs. 5.1 yrs Median PFS low mets 2.7 yrs vs. not reached Median PFS high mets 1.6 yrs vs. 4.1 yrs Castrate resisteance median 1.4 yrs vs. 3.2 yrs Median CSS 4.7 yrs vs. not …
Prostate.interpretation: Adding abiraterone to ADT and docetaxel improves PFS and OS. RT + abi improves PFS in low volume metastatic compaired to abi alone. OS seems to favor RT, especially when it is given with abiraterone.
Prostate.commentary: RT was given after docetaxel was completed. Only n=125 were randomized to Abi vs. Abi +RT. The trial may have been underpowered to show an OS benefit with RT in low volume mets. Or perhaps radiation needs to be delivered prior to chemotherapy. Much of this interpretation and data from the oral presentation is thanks to Daniel Spratt's excellent tweetorial (Available from: https://twitter.com/DrSpratticus/status/1665362426781478913?s=20)

Prostate.patients: Metastatic prostate cancer
Prostate.methods: Meta-analysis of M1 patients in STAMPEDE and HORRAD. ➜ 🏆 RT to prostate + ADT vs. ➜ ADT
Prostate.results: If <5 bone mets, RT to prostate improved OS. RT improved BF and FFS in all M+. For <5 bone mets, 3-yr OS 77% vs. 70% In all M+, RT improved 3-yr BF, 25% vs. 36%
Prostate.interpretation: RT to the primary prostate tumor in the setting of ≤5 bone mets improves OS in this meta-analysis.

Prostate.patients: prostate cancer, ≤5 oligometastatic lesions
Prostate.methods: Phase II 2 mos ADT ➜ ➜ 🏆 SBRT to metastatic lesions + continuing ADT vs. ➜ continuing ADT
Prostate.results: Median PFS not reached vs. 15.8 mos Time from eugonad testosterone to progresssion: not reached vs. 6.1 mos increased markers of T-cell activation, proliferation, and clonal expansion limited to the combined therapy arm
Prostate.interpretation: SBRT to all oligometastatic sites in prostate cancer improves PFS and eugonad PFS. SBRT may allow for prolongation of intermittent ADT in oligometastasis.
Prostate.commentary: The immunologic results show that the benefit of radiation may be more than simply local.

Prostate.methods: Phase II pooled analysis ➜ 🏆 SBRT or surgery to metastatic lesions vs. ➜ observation
Prostate.results: Median PFS 11.9 vs. 5.9 mos Median castrate resistance 18.3 vs. 17 mos, NS Median OS not reached in either arm

Prostate.patients: recurrent ADT-sensitive prostate cancer, ≤3 oligometastatic lesions s/p treatment to primary (RT or RP) no ADT in last 6 months
Prostate.methods: Phase II ➜ 🏆 SBRT to metastatic lesions vs. ➜ observation
Prostate.results: 6-mo progression 19% vs. 61% 6-mo PSMA avid new lesions 16% vs. 63% Median PFS not reached vs. 5.8 mo SBRT led to T cell clonal expansion, p=0.03 (OS is not an endpoint)
Prostate.interpretation: SBRT to all oligometastatic sites in prostate cancer reduced progression and new lesions. T cell proliferation was also seen.
Prostate.commentary: Arms were imbalanced: Gleason scores were higher in the observation arm. 83% of primary tumors were managed upfront with surgery, 17% with radiation. The T cell expansions cannot be confirmed to be toward prostate cancer. No other therapy, including ADT, was given in either arm. Phase III trials are in process. Trivia: What is the reasoning behind the title ORIOLE? Oriole --> Baltimore Orioles --> Johns Hopkins and Baltimore

Prostate.citation: Palma et al, Lancet, 2019 Palma et al, JCO, 2020
Prostate.patients: 1-5 oligometastases (93% were 1-3) any histology, controlled primary tumor from definitive treatment (such as chemoRT) (most were lung, breast, colorectal, prostate. Misc was about 1/3)
Prostate.methods: →controlled primary tumor from definitive treatment (such as chemoRT)→ Phase II ➜ SBRT and palliative standard of care (+salvage SABR allowed) vs. ➜ palliative standard of care Doses: SBRT to 30-60 Gy/ 3-8 fx or single fx of 16-24 Gy for brain, spine In this phase II trial, p<0.005 was prespecified as definitive. A p of <0.05 requires validation in phase III.
Prostate.results: Median OS 50 vs. 28 mos 5-yr OS 42% vs. 18%, p=0.006 8-yr OS 27% vs. 14% grade 5 toxicity 4.5% (n=3) vs. none Median PFS 12 vs. 6.0 mos 8-yr PFS 21% vs. 0, p=0.001 5-yr LC 63% vs. 46% 11/25 patients in the SABR arm alive beyond 5 years had no progression 5/25 had salvage SABR at some point after randomization Grade 4 adverse effects 0 On MVA, histology did not impact findings
Prostate.interpretation: SABR in oligometastatic disease of varying histologies improved PFS and OS as per phase II statistical design. Results are maintainted long term. Overall results, including power, applicability by # mets and reproducibility in individual histologies, are to be validated in Phase III trials.
Prostate.commentary: Note that PFS was definitive (p<0.005), and OS was nearly definitive per the statistical design. Results should eventually be evaluated in individual histologies. The median PFS of 6 mos and LC of 63% suggests that the improvement in OS was in part a result of continuing salvage SBRT and potentially reduced future spread due to lower tumor burden, and possible immune response. There were more prostate cancer patients in the SBRT arm (21% vs. 6%). A post-hoc sensitivity analysis of other histologies showed consistent results. Although there were perhaps more favorable histologies in the SBRT ar…

Prostate.patients: recurrent prostate cancer, ≤3 oligometastatic lesions as seen on choline PET, and testosterone >50
Prostate.methods: Phase II (p<0.20 significant) ➜ 🏆 SBRT or surgery to metastatic lesions (repeat SBRT allowed) vs. ➜ observation
Prostate.results: SBRT/surgery improved ADT free survival 3-yr ADT free survival 21 mos vs. 13 mos, p=0.11 In treatment arm, 11 had repeat SBRT QOL similar
Prostate.interpretation: SBRT to all oligometastatic sites in prostate cancer improves ADT free survival.
Prostate.commentary: The groups were not balanced. The SBRT/surgery arm had less mets. After post-hoc stratification, the results remained significant. Failure triggering ADT was defined as progression to >3 mets, local progression of known mets, or symptomatic progression No symptomatic or local progression occurred in the treatment arm.

Prostate.patients: newly diagnosed metastatic disease, PSA ≥5,
Prostate.methods: All get 7 mos ADT initially, then if PSA falls to ≤4, randomized to intermittent vs continuous ADT. Noninferiority study
Prostate.results: No difference in OS. Median OS 5.8 years in continuous vs. 5.1 years in intermittent (HR for intermittent 1.10, p=0.25). No difference with minimal vs. extensive disease.
Prostate.interpretation: Intermittent ADT could not be confirmed to be noninferior to continuous ADT. Nonetheless both regimens seemed to result in good outcomes.
Prostate.commentary: Despite the findings of the trial, intermittent ADT is sometimes used to lessen adverse effects of ADT if possible.

Prostate.citation: Morris et al, Lancet, 2024 Fizazi et al, Ann Oncol, 2025
Prostate.patients: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer that progressed on one prior ADT. Ineligible: prior taxane
Prostate.methods: no prior taxanes ➜ 🏆 Lu-177–PSMA-617 (7.4 GBq) q6weeks x6 + no change in ADT vs. ➜ change in ADT Crossover allowed if radiographic progression (60%)
Prostate.results: Median OS 24.5 vs. 23.1 mos by ITT, but high crossover Crossover adjusted HR 0.59 60% overall crossed over, or 75% that had radiographic progression Median radiographic PFS 11.6 vs. 5.6 mos Median time to BF 10.6 vs. 4.2 mos symptomatic skeletal event NR vs. 18.0 mos grade 3-5 adverse events 36% vs. 48% grade 5 2% in both arms
Prostate.interpretation: Lu-177–PSMA-617 improves PFS, BF, and time to skeletal events in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer treated with one prior ADT and no taxane history. There was no confirmed OS benefit, likely due to a high rate of crossover. Crossover adjusted analysis appears to show OS benefit.
Prostate.commentary: Just as on a PSMA PET scan, there is PSMA uptake in liver, salivary glands, kidney, bowel, and spleen normal tissue. Thus Pluvicto has side effects and dose limiting adverse effects related to these organs. Lu-177 emits beta and gamma. Maximum range 2 mm and half-life 6.7 days. Safety: outpatient shielded or separated, with separate bathroom, with significant radiation protection and contamination prevention. Peptides are infused for renal protection. Many rad onc clinics do not have the certifications to deliver radiopharmaceuticals; they are delivered in the nuclear medicine clinic in many h…

Lancet 2024. Published online September 13, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(24)01653-2 2. Fizazi K, Chi KN, Shore ND, et al. Final overall survival and safety analyses of the phase III PSMAfore trial of [177Lu]Lu-PSMA-617 versus change of androgen receptor pathway inhibitor in taxane-naive patients with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. Ann Oncol. Published online July 17, 2025. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.annonc.2025.07.003

Prostate.citation: Sartor et al, NEJM, 2021 Fizazi et al, Lancet Oncol, 2023
Prostate.patients: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
Prostate.methods: previous treatment with ADT and 1-2 taxanes ➜ 🏆 Lu-177–PSMA-617 (7.4 GBq) q6weeks x4-6 + SOC vs. ➜ standard of care
Prostate.results: Radiographic median PFS 8.7 vs. 3.4 mos Median OS 15.3 vs. 11.3 mos Median time to 1st skeletal event or death 11.5 vs. 6.8 mos QOL metrics improved on surveys Increased cytopenias with tx Grade 5 adverse effects 1% (n=5) vs. 0 (pancytopenia [n=2], BM failure [n=1], subdural haematoma [n=1], and intracranial haemorrhage [n=1]) Only median outcomes were reported. Looking at Fig 2, it seems that 2-yr OS and time to first symptomatic skeletal event remain different. PFS curves seem to merge at 2 years
Prostate.interpretation: Lu-177–PSMA-617 improves PFS and OS in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer treated with prior taxane. Time to skeletal events or death is prolonged.
Prostate.commentary: Just as on a PSMA PET scan, there is PSMA uptake in liver, salivary glands, kidney, bowel, and spleen normal tissue. Thus Pluvicto has side effects and dose limiting adverse effects related to these organs. Lu-177 emits beta and gamma. Maximum range 2 mm and half-life 6.7 days. Safety: outpatient shielded or separated, with separate bathroom, with significant radiation protection and contamination prevention. Peptides are infused for renal protection. Many rad onc clinics do not have the certifications to deliver radiopharmaceuticals; they are delivered in the nuclear medicine clinic in many h…
Prostate.references: 1. Sartor O, de Bono J, Chi KN, et al. Lutetium-177-PSMA-617 for Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer. N Engl J Med 2021;385:1091-1103. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2107322 Protocol pdf: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa2107322/suppl_file/nejmoa2107322_protocol.pdf 2. Fizazi K, Herrmann K, Krause BJ, et al. Health-related quality of life and pain outcomes with [177Lu]Lu-PSMA-617 plus standard of care versus standard of care in patients with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (VISION): a multicentre, open-label, randomised, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2023;24:597-610. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(23)00158-4

Prostate.patients: Castration resistant prostate cancer with ≥2 bone metastases Prior docetaxel allowed Exclusion: visceral metastasis
Prostate.methods: ➜ 🏆 radium-223 qmonthly x6 plus best standard of care vs. ➜ placebo and best standard of care (2:1 randomization) Eligibility: ANC ≥ 1500, Plt ≥ 100, Hgb ≥ 10 at first dose Subsequent doses: ANC ≥ 1000 and Plt ≥ 50
Prostate.results: Median OS 14.9 mos vs. 11.3 mos Median time to bony event 15.6 vs. 9.8 mos Median time to alk phos rise 7.4 vs. 3.8 mos PR of ≥30% reduction in alk phos 47% vs. 3% CR in alk phos 34% vs. 1% (total alk phos response rate 81%) Improved QOL with radium-223 Only median outcomes were provided. Looking at Figure 1, OS and time to first symptomatic skeletal event curves seem to merge at 2 years -The study was terminated early for efficacy at interim analysis -Adverse effects in radium-223 group were significantly lower than in placebo arm
Prostate.interpretation: Radium-223 improves OS in castrate resistant nonmetastatic prostate cancer with bone metastases.
Prostate.commentary: Why was there worse adverse effects in the standard arm? The adverse effects could be due to random chance, or perhaps therapies were escalated in the placebo arm when those patients performed worse without radium-223. Alk phos response and OS were still markedly superior with radium-223. In view of new docetaxel findings since this trial, some oncologists might advocate docetaxel over radium-223. But the favorable QOL with radium-223 is appealing. Dose of 50 kBq/kg given q4 weeks. Placebo used was isotonic saline. Safety: given in outpatient office. Only mild precautions required. 30 minutes …
Prostate.references: 1. Parker C, Nilsson S, Heinrich D, et al. Alpha Emitter Radium-223 and Survival in Metastatic Prostate Cancer. N Engl J Med 2013;369:213-23. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1213755 Protocol pdf: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa1213755/suppl_file/nejmoa1213755_protocol.pdf

Prostate.patients: nonmetastatic castrate resistant, PSA DT ≤10 mos
Prostate.methods: ➜ darolutamide indefinitely + ADT vs. ➜ ADT alone
Prostate.results: median DMFS 40 mos vs. 18 mos Also improved OS Grade ≥3 adverse effects and discontinuations similar
Prostate.interpretation: Darolutamide improves DMFS and OS with similar adverse effects to ADT alone in castrate resistant nonmetastatic prostate cancer.
Prostate.commentary: Note that the drug was used indefinitely until progression. Could the length of therapy be responsible for much of the benefit? Darolutamide and ADT discontinuation rates were similar; in SPARTEN and PROSPER, more adverse effects led to worse adherence.
Prostate.references: 1. Fizazi K, Shore N, Tammela TL, et al. Darolutamide in Nonmetastatic, Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer. N Engl J Med 2019;380:1235-46. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1815671

Prostate.patients: nonmetastatic castrate resistant, PSA DT ≤10 mos
Prostate.methods: ➜ apalutamide indefinitely + ADT vs. ➜ ADT alone
Prostate.results: median DMFS 41 mos vs. 16 mos More adverse effects and discontinuation with apalutamide
Prostate.interpretation: Apalutamide improves DMFS in castrate resistant nonmetastatic prostate cancer.
Prostate.references: 1. Smith MR, Saad F, Chowdhury S, et al. Apalutamide Treatment and Metastasis-free Survival in Prostate Cancer. N Engl J Med 2018;378:1408-18. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1715546

Prostate.patients: nonmetastatic castrate resistant, PSA DT ≤10 mos
Prostate.methods: ➜ enzalutamide indefinitely + ADT vs. ➜ ADT alone
Prostate.results: median DMFS 37 mos vs. 15 mos median BF 37 mos vs. 4 mos Interim analysis overall mortality 11% vs. 13% Grade ≥3 adverse effects 31% vs. 23%
Prostate.interpretation: Enzalutamide improves DMFS in castrate resistant nonmetastatic prostate cancer.
Prostate.references: 1. Hussain M, Fizazi K, Saad F, et al. Enzalutamide in Men with Nonmetastatic, Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer. N Engl J Med 2018;378:2465-74. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1800536

Prostate.patients: localized, postprostatectomy, nonmetastatic castration-resistant, and metastatic hormone-sensitive
Prostate.methods: Meta-analysis of Decipher in retrospective studies, prospective databases, and prospective and post hoc randomized trial analyses. 42 studies
Prostate.results: In 32 studies Decipher was independently prognostic of adverse pathology, BF, DM, CSS, and OS
Prostate.interpretation: Decipher improves prognostication, which can assist in decision making.
Prostate.references: 1. Jairath NK, Dal Pra A, Vince R Jr, et al. A Systematic Review of the Evidence for the Decipher Genomic Classifier in Prostate Cancer [published online ahead of print, 2020 Dec 5]. Eur Urol 2020;S0302-2838(20)30885-X. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eururo.2020.11.021

Prostate.patients: salvage RP, HIFU, cryo, SBRT, LDR, HDR
Prostate.methods: Meta-analysis of studies. 150 found for analysis. Evaluated RFS and adverse effects
Prostate.results: covariate adjusted RFS trended to improved with radiation, but with significant heterogeneity 5-yr RFS 50% cryo, 53% HIFU, 54% RP, 56% LDR, 60% HDR, 60% SBRT Severe GU adverse effects less with RT than with RP: 20% RP, 5.6% SBRT, 9.6% HDR, 9.1% LDR Severe GI adverse effects lower with HDR than RP: 0% vs. 1.8%
Prostate.interpretation: There is a range of RFS outcomes with the various modalities. GU and GI adverse effects favor salvage treatment with radiation over RP.
Prostate.references: 1. Valle LF, Lehrer EJ, Markovic D, et al. A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Local Salvage Therapies After Radiotherapy for Prostate Cancer (MASTER). Eur Urol 2021;80:280-292. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eururo.2020.11.010

Prostate.citation: Crook et al, IJROBP, 2019 Crook et al, IJROBP, 2021
Prostate.patients: Low or int risk, biopsy proven recurrence, PSA<10, failure >30 mos after EBRT
Prostate.methods: LDR I-125 to 140 Gy or Pd-103 to 120 Gy Goal grade 3 < 20%
Prostate.results: 10-yr BF 46% 10-yr LF 5%, DM 19% 5-yr DFS 61% 10-yr DFS 33% 14% late grade 3 adverse events Higher V100 predicted late events
Prostate.interpretation: Grade 3 adverse effects with salvage LDR did not exceed the predetermined threshhold.

Prostate.patients: High risk prostate cancer
Prostate.methods: NCDB analysis of OS in RP, EBRT+ADT, and EBRT+brachy±ADT. Inverse probability of treatment weighting used.
Prostate.results: No difference in OS in RP vs. EBRT+brachy±ADT EBRT+ADT had worse OS than RP EBRT+ADT≥7920 narrowed the difference but still with worse OS
Prostate.interpretation: After adjusting for imbalances, there was no difference in OS between RP vs. EBRT +brachy ± ADT. EBRT + ADT was associated with worse OS. Randomized trials are required for confirmation as adjustments for imbalances in database studies cannot remove all confounding variables.
Prostate.commentary: NCDB does not provide recurrence data and has limited data on receptor status or RT fields. The eras are arbitrary. Caution using large population observational studies: A review found that the conclusions of these were not better than chance at matching randomized trials, and no factors were found to improve conclusions (Soni 2019). This does not necessarily mean the reports worthless - they still contain more data than a single insitution retrospective report for example - but that the eras and parameters should be carefully selected, that the results should be considered something in the re…
Prostate.references: 1. Ennis RD, Hu L, Ryemon SN, Lin J. Survival after surgical or radiotherapeutic treatment for high-risk localized prostate cancer: A National Cancer Database analysis with comprehensive treatment group imbalances adjustments. J Clin Oncol 2018;36:55-. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2018.36.6_suppl.55
Prostate.commentary_refs: Soni PD, Hartman HE, Dess RT, et al. Comparison of Population-Based Observational Studies With Randomized Trials in Oncology. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1209-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.01074

Prostate.patients: High risk prostate patients treated with EBRT±ADT, LDR±ADT, RP±EBRT
Prostate.methods: Cohort study to assess BPFS, cRFS, and PCSM, and adverse effects in high risk prostate cancer patients RT to at least 78 Gy/2 Gy fx or 70 Gy/2.5 Gy fx, or LDR 144 Gy. Adjuvant/salvage median dose 70 Gy/2 Gy fx
Prostate.results: 5- and 10-yr BPFS: EBRT 74% and 53%, LDR 74% and 52%, RP 65% and 47%, p=0.0001 5- and 10-yr cRFS: EBRT 85% and 73%, LDR 90% and 76%, RP 89% and 75%, p=.121 5- and 10-yr PCSM: EBRT 5% and 11%, LDR 3% and 4%, RP 3% and 7%, p=0.0004 10-yr grade ≥3 GI adverse effects EBRT 5%, LDR 1%, RP 1%, p<0.0001 10-yr grade ≥3 GU adverse effects EBRT 8%, LDR 7.2%, RP 16%, p<.0001 Use of ADT did not improve PCSM with LDR
Prostate.interpretation: In high risk prostate cancer patients, better BPFS with LDR or EBRT compared to RP. Equivalent cRFS between modalities. Improved PCSM with LDR and RP over EBRT. Adverse effects are lowest for LDR. LDR for high risk prostate cancer is a reasonable treatment option to consider.
Prostate.commentary: Although limited by its nonrandomized nature, the study's cohort design does allow ordered tracking of outcomes. The EBRT group had higher PSA, more Gleason score 9 and 10, and more T3. If ADT given, most had ADT of 1-6 mos duration. PCSM outcomes are similar to Sooriakumaran study. Different definitions of biochemical failure were used over time. EBRT patients had longer follow-up (Sooriakumaran 2014).
Prostate.references: 1. Ciezki JP, Reddy CA, Weller MA, et al. The Effect of Androgen Deprivation Therapy on Prostate Cancer-Specific Mortality in Patients With High-Risk Prostate Cancer Treated with I-125 Brachytherapy as the Sole Radiation Therapeutic Modality. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2017;99:E223. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2017.06.1136

Prostate.patients: Prostate cancer gleason score 8-10, N0, from NCDB treated with RP or EBRT (83% ADT), age <60
Prostate.methods: NCDB analysis of OS in RT (EBRT or EBRT+brachy) vs. surgery in age <60 with Gleason score 8-10. Cox regression analysis
Prostate.results: HR 0.52 for OS favors RP Estimated 8-yr OS 85% RP and 75% RT RP patients had higher comodbidity scores Benefit remained with adjustment for prognostic factors Mean EBRT dose 77.4 Gy In RP group, 17% had post-op RT, 87% with 64.8 Gy
Prostate.interpretation: Surgery is associated with better OS compared to RT in this NCDB analysis. Results are maintained after adjustments for prognostic factors. Randomized trials are required for confirmation as adjustments for imbalances in database studies cannot remove all confounding variables.
Prostate.commentary: NCDB does not provide recurrence data and has limited data on receptor status or RT fields. The eras are arbitrary. Caution using large population observational studies: A review found that the conclusions of these were not better than chance at matching randomized trials, and no factors were found to improve conclusions (Soni 2019). This does not necessarily mean the reports worthless - they still contain more data than a single insitution retrospective report for example - but that the eras and parameters should be carefully selected, that the results should be considered something in the re…

Prostate.commentary_refs: Soni PD, Hartman HE, Dess RT, et al. Comparison of Population-Based Observational Studies With Randomized Trials in Oncology. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1209-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.01074

Prostate.patients: Meta-analysis
Prostate.methods: Meta-analysis of 19 studies to compare RT (EBRT or brachy) and surgery
Prostate.results: Overall mortality (HR 1.63, p<0.00001) and Prostate CSM (HR 2.08, p<0.00001) were worse with radiation therapy. Results same on subgroup analysis by risk group, radiation regimen, time period, and follow-up length.
Prostate.interpretation: Surgery is associated with better OS and PCSM compared to RT.
Prostate.commentary: Based on these sorts of studies, use of surgery has increased for intermediate and high risk prostate cancer. "Garbage in, garbage out."? These studies are confounded in that more advanced tumors are more likely to have been referred for radiation - no way to control for all variables. For example, all Stage III are not the same prognosis. Consider PROTECT trial. 11/19 studies give no info on dose."Before vs after 2005" not an adequate surrogate for dose. ADT often not adequate length. 80% of data is from registries. No info on salvage and adjuvant RT given. NOS scale does not adequately deter…
Prostate.references: 1. Wallis CJD, Saskin R, Choo R, et al. Surgery Versus Radiotherapy for Clinically-localized Prostate Cancer: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. Eur Urol 2016;70:21-30. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eururo.2015.11.010
