Prostate.patients: Received treatment with RT or surgery. Included low, int, high, and metastatic groups
Prostate.methods: Observational study of patients from multiple cancer registries in Sweden. Comparison of RT vs. surgery.
Prostate.results: 15 year f/u. In nonmetastatic patients, hazard ratio 1.76 for prostate cancer mortality favored surgery. On subanalysis, the benefit is greater with younger age and int and high risk disease.
Prostate.interpretation: Surgery is associated with better OS compared to RT.
Prostate.commentary: Data from large databases should be interpreted with caution. For example, NCDB does not provide recurrence data and has limited data on receptor status or RT fields. The eras are arbitrary. Caution using large population observational studies: A review found that the conclusions of these were not better than chance at matching randomized trials, and no factors were found to improve conclusions (Soni 2019). This does not necessarily mean the reports worthless - they still contain more data than a single insitution retrospective report for example - but that the eras and parameters should be ca…
Prostate.references: 1. Sooriakumaran P, Nyberg T, Akre O, et al. Comparative effectiveness of radical prostatectomy and radiotherapy in prostate cancer: observational study of mortality outcomes. Br Med J 2014;348:g1502. doi: https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.g1502
Prostate.commentary_refs: Soni PD, Hartman HE, Dess RT, et al. Comparison of Population-Based Observational Studies With Randomized Trials in Oncology. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1209-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.01074

Sarcoma.citation: Wang et al, JCO, 2015 Wang et al, JAMA Oncol, 2023
Sarcoma.patients: Extremity soft tissue sarcoma
Sarcoma.methods: ➜ Cohort A: Pre-op IGRT with chemotherapy (arm closed early and not reported) ➜ Cohort B: IGRT without chemotherapy IMRT or 3DCRT allowed. 50 Gy. IMRT used in 75% patients
Sarcoma.results: 2-yr Grade 2+ adverse effects 11% vs. 37% in historical NCIC trial. LF 6% pCR 19% LF with pCR 0% vs. 9% in <pCR 5-yr OS with pCR 100% vs. 56% if <pCR fibrosis 5% [vs. 32% in NCIC] stiffness 4% [vs. 18%] edema 5% [vs. 15%] wound complications 37% [same] lower extremity wound complications 42% [vs. 45%]
Sarcoma.interpretation: Pre-op RT with IGRT leads to less late adverse effects compared to historical control. pCR is associated with improved OS and reduced LF.
Sarcoma.commentary: Grade 2-3 and ≥8 cm (need both): CTV 3 cm sup/inf and 1.5 cm radially. Grade 1 or <8 cm: CTV 2 cm sup/inf and 1 cm radially. Edema was additionally included in CTV. Dose constraints skin/subcutaneous tissue V20<50% bone V50<50 joint V50<50% Avoid full rx to joint (do not extend CTV to joint)
Sarcoma.references: Study protocol: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/instance/10064284/bin/jamaoncol-e230042-s001.pdf#page=21 1. Wang D, Zhang Q, Eisenberg BL, et al. Significant Reduction of Late Toxicities in Patients With Extremity Sarcoma Treated With Image-Guided Radiation Therapy to a Reduced Target Volume: Results of Radiation Therapy Oncology Group RTOG-0630 Trial. J Clin Oncol 2015;33:2231-8. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2014.58.5828 2. Wang D, Harris J, Kraybill WG, et al. Pathologic Complete Response and Clinical Outcomes in Patients With Localized Soft Tissue Sarcoma Treated With Neoadjuvant Chemoradiotherapy or Radiotherapy: The NRG/RTOG 9514 and 0630 Nonrandomized Clinical Trials. JAMA Oncol 2023;9:646-655. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2023.0042

Sarcoma.patients: Extremity soft tissue sarcoma s/p limb sparing surgery
Sarcoma.methods: Retrospective ➜ LDR 45 Gy vs. ➜ IMRT pre-op or post-op
Sarcoma.results: 5-yr LC 81% vs. 92% IMRT group had more positive and close margins, larger tumors, and nerve involvement
Sarcoma.interpretation: In this retrospective analysis, IMRT results in improved LC compared to LDR brachytherapy.
Sarcoma.commentary: Generally if a retrospective study leads to results that are unexpected, such as in this one, then the results seem more convincing. Here LDR was thought to be superior in LC over IMRT, but was actually found to be worse even when tumor characteristics favored the LDR group.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Alektiar KM, Brennan MF, Singer S, et al. Local control comparison of adjuvant brachytherapy to intensity-modulated radiotherapy in primary high-grade sarcoma of the extremity. Cancer 2011;15;117:3229-34. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.25882

Sarcoma.patients: Extremity soft tissue sarcoma with planned surgery and post-op RT, without chemo
Sarcoma.methods: ➜ 50 Gy (5 cm sup/inf and 2 cm radially) + 16 Gy boost (2 cm sup/inf/radial) vs. ➜ 66 Gy (2 cm sup/inf/radial)
Sarcoma.results: No difference in 2-yr limb function or adverse effects (skin, subcutaneous, bone, or joint) Due to small events, not possible to assess LRFS
Sarcoma.interpretation: Events in both arms were low and statisitical analysis could not be be undertaken.
Sarcoma.commentary: This trial was reported abstract only at ASTRO 2016, but work on the publication is in process (Hughes A, email communication, Jan 2024). The conclusion of the study was that because of the small number of events, volume reduction cannot be recommended. The conclusion is interesting because other noninferiority studies sometimes conclude the opposite, that although noninferiority was not reached and events were low in both arms, either regimen is appropriate (UK NCI RAPID in lymphoma, AMAROS in breast). Sometimes if there are small differences, extra power is unnecessary. Information has not y…
Sarcoma.references: 1. Robinson MH, Gaunt P, Grimer R, et al. Vortex Trial: A Randomized Controlled Multicenter Phase 3 Trial of Volume of Postoperative Radiation Therapy Given to Adult Patients With Extremity Soft Tissue Sarcoma (STS). Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2016;96:S1. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2016.06.021 Protocol pdf: https://www.birmingham.ac.uk/Documents/college-mds/trials/crctu/vortex/pdfsindexvortex/VORTEXProtocolversion602082010.pdf#page=24

Sarcoma.citation: Davis et al, Lancet, 2002 O'Sullivan et al, JCO, 2004 Davis et al, Radiother Oncol, 2005
Sarcoma.patients: Non-metastatic STS of limbs
Sarcoma.methods: ➜ pre-op 50 Gy/25 fx vs. ➜ post-op 66 Gy/33fx. Primary endpoint: wound complications (not powered for survival or recurrence) Longitudinal borders 5 cm to block edge. Boost 2 cm around target.
Sarcoma.results: Pre-op had increased wound complications. Post-op trended to more fibrosis, edema, stiffness. 2-yr wound adverse effects 35% pre-op vs. 17% post-op Wound complications are primarily in lower extremity, not upper LC unchanged. OS improved early in pre-op but later not different. 5-yr LC ~93%, 5-yr DM ~68% (NS) 2.5-yr OS 85% vs. 72%, p=0.04 5-yr OS 73% vs. 67% (NS) Pre-op vs. post-op 2-yr adverse effects: wound complications 35% vs. 17% (45% wound for low ext) grade 2 fibrosis 32% vs. 48%, p=0.07 edema 15% vs. 23% joint stiffness 18% vs. 23% Higher total field size in this trial than in others.
Sarcoma.interpretation: Pre-op and post-op RT are equally effective in LC and OS. Because adverse effects differ, anatomic site should be considered in approach.
Sarcoma.references: 1. O'Sullivan B, Davis AM, Turcotte R, et al. Preoperative versus postoperative radiotherapy in soft-tissue sarcoma of the limbs: a randomised trial. Lancet 2002;359:2235-41. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(02)09292-9 2. O'Sullivan B, Davis A, Turcotte R, et al. Five-year results of a randomized phase III trial of pre-operative vs post-operative radiotherapy in extremity soft tissue sarcoma. J Clin Oncol 2004;22:9007-. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/jco.2004.22.90140.9007 3. Davis AM, O'Sullivan B, Turcotte R, et al. Late radiation morbidity following randomization to preoperative versus postoperative radiotherapy in extremity soft tissue sarcoma. Radiother Oncol 2005;75:48-53. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.radonc.2004.12.020

Sarcoma.citation: Yang et al, JCO, 1998 Beane et al, Ann Surg Oncol, 2014
Sarcoma.patients: Extremity sarcoma s/p WLE. Excluded gross residual tumor and widely positive margins Allowed desmoid and dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans
Sarcoma.methods: Low grade: ➜ 🏆 post-op RT 45 Gy, boost to 63 Gy vs. ➜ obs High grade: ➜ 🏆 post-op RT with doxo/cyclo vs. ➜ doxo/cyclo
Sarcoma.results: Low grade: LC 96% vs. 67% High grade: LC 100% vs. 81% Low grade excluding desmoid and dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans: LC 95% vs. 68%, p=0.067 (low power) No difference in OS or DMFS 10-yr OS 77% RT vs. 82% (p=.22, all grades) 20-yr OS 61% vs. 71%, not different EBRT had more wound complications, edema, and limb deficits
Sarcoma.interpretation: EBRT provides high rates of local control. When evaluating low risk tumors while excluding desmoid and dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, there is trend to benefit with radiation that is not signficant, however the analysis is likely underpowered.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Yang JC, Chang AE, Baker AR, et al. Randomized prospective study of the benefit of adjuvant radiation therapy in the treatment of soft tissue sarcomas of the extremity. J Clin Oncol 1998;16:197-203. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1998.16.1.197 2. Beane JD, Yang JC, White D, Steinberg SM, Rosenberg SA, Rudloff U. Efficacy of Adjuvant Radiation Therapy in the Treatment of Soft Tissue Sarcoma of the Extremity: 20-year Follow-Up of a Randomized Prospective Trial. Ann Surg Oncol 2014;21:2484-9. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1245/s10434-014-3732-4

Sarcoma.patients: Soft tissue sarcoma T1 (size <5 cm), trunk or extremity
Sarcoma.methods: Prospective Function sparing surgery→ ➜ If R1→RT ➜ If R0→obs
Sarcoma.results: R0, obs: 5-yr LR 8%, 10-yr LR 11% R1, radiation: LR 43% 58% were high grade. 11/12 recurrences were in high grade, and 8/12 recurrences were deep tumors
Sarcoma.interpretation: Findings suggest that surgery alone is acceptable for some patients such as those of size <5 cm with complete resection, grade 1-2.
Sarcoma.commentary: Radiation was 64 Gy in 32 fractions to a 5-7 cm field.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Pisters PW, Pollock RE, Lewis VO, et al. Long-term results of prospective trial of surgery alone with selective use of radiation for patients with T1 extremity and trunk soft tissue sarcomas. Ann Surg 2007;246:675-81; discussion 81-2. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/SLA.0b013e318155a9ae

Sarcoma.patients: Extremity and superficial trunk sarcoma s/p WLE with goal of complete resection (15% R1, 85% R0)
Sarcoma.methods: ➜ 🏆 brachy Ir-192 42-45 Gy+ 2cm margin vs. ➜ obs
Sarcoma.results: 5-yr LC improved with BT: 82% vs. 69% obs high grade 5-yr LC 89% vs. 66% low grade no difference in negative margins, 13% vs. 28% in positive margins, 33% vs. 36% No change in DM (17% vs. 24%) or DFS (84% vs. 81%) 15% were R1
Sarcoma.interpretation: Adjuvant brachy improves LC for high grade STS. No difference in DSS or DM.
Sarcoma.commentary: Established the use of brachytherapy for high grade, negative margins. Since the development of IMRT, brachytherapy has slowly declined in use due to higher technical and workflow requirements. A retrospective MSKCC study showed that IMRT had better outcomes.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Pisters PW, Harrison LB, Leung DH, Woodruff JM, Casper ES, Brennan MF. Long-term results of a prospective randomized trial of adjuvant brachytherapy in soft tissue sarcoma. J Clin Oncol 1996;14:859-68. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1996.14.3.859

Sarcoma.patients: High-grade extremity STS
Sarcoma.methods: ➜ WLE + post-op 50 Gy + 10-20 Gy boost vs. ➜ amputation adjuvant chemo in both arms
Sarcoma.results: LR 15% vs. 0% amputation No significant difference in 5-yr DFS (71% vs. 78%) or OS (83% vs. 88%)
Sarcoma.interpretation: Limb-sparing surgery with RT and chemo is an effective treatment strategy.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Rosenberg SA, Tepper J, Glatstein E, et al. The treatment of soft-tissue sarcomas of the extremities: prospective randomized evaluations of (1) limb-sparing surgery plus radiation therapy compared with amputation and (2) the role of adjuvant chemotherapy. Ann Surg 1982;196:305-15. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/00000658-198209000-00009

Sarcoma.patients: Extremity or trunk soft tissue sarcoma
Sarcoma.methods: Phase II ➜ Pre-op 42.75 Gy/ 15 fx daily
Sarcoma.results: 30-month LC 93% Major wound complications within 120 days 31% Grade ≥3 adverse effects in n=4
Sarcoma.interpretation: Hypofractionated radiation leads to favorable local control with acceptable adverse effects in this cohort.
Sarcoma.commentary: The study is single institution and non-randomized, but may be useful for those with limited schedules or finances. The median time to surgery was 5.7 weeks, IQR 4.6–6.4 weeks. Bone V35 Gy <65%, mean <30.5 Gy Joint V42.75Gy ≤ 50% Femur V42.75 Gy < 100% Max < 42.75 Gy if possible, V38 Gy ≤ 50%
Sarcoma.references: 1. Guadagnolo BA, Bassett RL, Mitra D, et al. Hypofractionated, 3-week, preoperative radiotherapy for patients with soft tissue sarcomas (HYPORT-STS): a single-centre, open-label, single-arm, phase 2 trial. Lancet Oncol 2022;23:1547-1557. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(22)00638-6 PubMed full text: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9817485/ Protocol pdf [radiation planning p14]: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9817485/bin/NIHMS1849648-supplement-1.docx

Sarcoma.citation: Lansu et al, JAMA Oncol, 2020 Lansu et al, JAMA Oncol, 2026
Sarcoma.patients: Localized myxoid liposarcoma, trunk or extremity
Sarcoma.methods: Prospective. ➜ Pre-op 36 Gy/ 18 fx daily Surgery at ≥4 weeks
Sarcoma.results: 91% had a ≥50% pathologic response LC 100% 5-yr LFRFS 97% 5-yr PFS 81% 5-yr DSS 90% 5-yr OS 89% Wound complicatoin 16% Grade 2 adverse effects 15% Grade 3 adverse effects 3%
Sarcoma.interpretation: This de-escalated regimen for myxoid sarcoma has excellent LC and pathologic response.
Sarcoma.commentary: The trial was performed at 9 institutions in Europe and US. The study is non-randomized, but may be useful for those with limited schedules or finances.
Sarcoma.references: Study protocol pdf: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7662477/bin/jamaoncol-e205865-s001.pdf 1. Lansu J, Bovée JVMG, Braam P, et al. Dose Reduction of Preoperative Radiotherapy in Myxoid Liposarcoma: A Nonrandomized Controlled Trial [published online ahead of print, 2020 Nov 12]. JAMA Oncol 2020;e205865. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2020.5865 2. Lansu J, Bovée JVMG, Braam PM, et al. Dose Reduction of Preoperative Radiotherapy in Myxoid Liposarcoma: The Phase 2 DOREMY Nonrandomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol. Published online May 16, 2026. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1001/jamaoncol.2026.1577

Sarcoma.trial: Maria Sklodowska- Curie Memorial Cancer Center, Warsaw, Poland
Sarcoma.citation: Koseła-Paterczyk et al, Eur J Surg Oncol, 2014
Sarcoma.patients: Extremity or trunk soft tissue sarcoma
Sarcoma.methods: Prospective. ➜ Pre-op 25 Gy/ 5 fx daily Immediate surgery
Sarcoma.results: 3-yr LR 19% 3-yr OS 72% 42% had adverse effects 7% surgery for complications 65% were grade 3, 42% with size >10 cm
Sarcoma.interpretation: Hypofractionated radiation leads to favorable local control with acceptable adverse effects in this cohort.
Sarcoma.commentary: The study is single institution and non-randomized, but may be useful for those with limited schedules or finances.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Koseła-Paterczyk H, Szacht M, Morysiński T, et al. Preoperative hypofractionated radiotherapy in the treatment of localized soft tissue sarcomas. Eur J Surg Oncol 2014;40:1641-1647. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejso.2014.05.016

Sarcoma.trial: Fondazione IRCCS Istituto Nazionale dei Tumori, Milan, Italy
Sarcoma.patients: Extremity soft tissue sarcoma
Sarcoma.methods: Development of nomogram Initial cohort formed at Milan and then externally validated at London, Toronto, and Gustave Roussy.
Sarcoma.results: Refer to publication for useful nomogram to predict OS and DM Overall 10-yr OS 79%, crude DM 25% Factors include age, size, grade, histology
Sarcoma.interpretation: Nomogram was developed to predict OS and DM in sarcoma of the extremity and externally validated.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Callegaro D, Miceli R, Bonvalot S, et al. Development and external validation of two nomograms to predict overall survival and occurrence of distant metastases in adults after surgical resection of localised soft-tissue sarcomas of the extremities: a retrospective analysis. Lancet Oncol 2016;17:671-80. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(16)00010-3

Sarcoma.patients: Extremity soft tissue sarcoma, nonmetastatic, prospective database 1982-2006, receiving no RT or chemo
Sarcoma.methods: Development of nomogram to predict LR. Internally validated using concordance index, 10-fold cross validation, and comparison to observed LR
Sarcoma.results: Refer to publication for useful nomogram to predict LR. Factors include age, size, margin, grade, and histology. Histology other than atypical lipoma or grade 1 has the greatest effect.
Sarcoma.interpretation: Nomogram was developed to predict LR in sarcoma of the extremity and validated internally.
Sarcoma.commentary: Based on this nomogram, a well differentiated sarcoma or any atypical lipoma has low LR risk and would not benefit much from RT
Sarcoma.references: 1. Cahlon O, Brennan MF, Jia X, Qin L-X, Singer S, Alektiar KM. A postoperative nomogram for local recurrence risk in extremity soft tissue sarcomas after limb-sparing surgery without adjuvant radiation. Ann Surg 2012;255:343-7. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/SLA.0b013e3182367aa7

Sarcoma.citation: Bonvalot et al, Lancet Oncol, 2020 Haas et al, Cancer, 2022
Sarcoma.patients: operable localized retroperitoneal soft tissue sarcoma Exclusion: expected R2 (subtype characteristics: 33% well differentiated LPS, 39% dedifferentiated LPS, 2% other LPS, 14% leiomyosarcoma, 11% other)
Sarcoma.methods: ➜ pre-op RT to 50.4 Gy then en bloc surgery in 4-8 weeks vs. ➜ en bloc surgery Primary endpoint: abdominal RFS (composite of LR or DM progression during or after RT based on CT 2 weeks after RT, 3 on ASA scale, R2, sarcomatosis, or LR following R0/R1 resection) Dose constraints and margins: https://acsjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/action/downloadSupplement?doi=10.1002%2Fcncr.34239&file=cncr34239-sup-0001-TableS1-S6.pdf
Sarcoma.results: No difference in abdominal RFS, OS, DM 3-yr abdominal RFS ~60% Median abd RFS 4.5-5.0 yrs 5-yr OS 80%, DMFS 68% both arms No change in operability, re-operations -various sensitivity analyses found no differences in abd RFS -in subanalysis of liposarcoma, CI crosses to 1.02 and p not provided, but curves appear to begin to separate. Trial not powered for subtypes Selected sub-endpoints of abd RFS: -LR following R0/R1: 13% vs. 29% -LR/DM progression during/after RT 14% (12% were LR, 2% DM) (supp p6-7, calculated by myself) -Serious adverse effects 24% vs. 10% -In RT group, lymphopenia in 77%, a…
Sarcoma.interpretation: Pre-op RT in RP sarcoma did not improve abdominal RFS. There may be benefit in the sub-endpoint of LR following R0/R1. The trial was not powered to evaluate specific subtypes. RT QA noncompliance was associated with worse OS.
Sarcoma.commentary: STRASS was an impressive undertaking for an uncommon malignancy. Concerns about the trial center around its choice of endpoints and radiation planning. The authors are commended for their extensive reporting and appendix, though a few more points of information could be useful. Abd RFS is a broad composite endpoint and is not necessarily validated - its components are enlightening. The sub-endpoint of LR/DM progression during/after RT of 14% seems to be the culprit that led to no difference in abd RFS (supp p6-7), and this was mostly due to LR "during" RT. Are these tumors actually truly recur…
Sarcoma.references: 1. Bonvalot S, Gronchi A, le Pechoux C, et al. Preoperative radiotherapy plus surgery versus surgery alone for patients with primary retroperitoneal sarcoma (EORTC-62092: STRASS): a multicentre, open-label, randomised, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol 2020. doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30446-0 Dose constraints and margins supplement: https://acsjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/action/downloadSupplement?doi=10.1002%2Fcncr.34239&file=cncr34239-sup-0001-TableS1-S6.pdf 2. Haas R, Stelmes JJ, Zaffaroni F, et al. Critical impact of radiotherapy protocol compliance and quality in the treatment of retroperitoneal sarcomas: Results from the EORTC 62092-22092 STRASS trial. Cancer. 2022;128(14):2796-2805. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.34239
Sarcoma.commentary_refs: 1. Baldini EH, Abrams RA, Bosch W, et al. Retroperitoneal Sarcoma Target Volume and Organ at Risk Contour Delineation Agreement Among NRG Sarcoma Radiation Oncologists. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2015;92:1053–1059. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2015.04.039 2. Baldini EH, Wang D, Haas RL. Treatment Guidelines for Preoperative Radiation Therapy for Retroperitoneal Sarcoma: Preliminary Consensus of an International Expert Panel. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2015 Jul 1;92(3):602-12. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2015.02.013 3. @ErinGillespieMD. Gillespie, E. Twitter. February 10, 2021. Available from: https://twitter.com/ErinGillespieMD/status/1359490977598214145?s=20 Accessed February 10, 2021. 4. @toddscarbrough. Scarbrough, T. Twitter. February 11, 2021. Available from: https://twitter.com/toddscarbrough/status/1359722986958426115?s=20 Accessed February 11, 2021. 5. @SprakerMDPhD. Spraker, M. Twitter. February, 11, 2021. Available from: https://twitter.com/SprakerMDPhD/status/1359735286603911168?s=20 Accessed February 11, 2021. 6. Chowdhary M, Spraker MB. Preoperative radiotherapy for retroperitoneal sarcoma. Lancet Oncol 2021;22:e2. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(20)30626-4

Sarcoma.patients: "STREXIT" cohort: Sarcoma patients at 10 STRASS recruiting centers that were not enrolled in STRASS, s/p curative intent surgery, with or without RT
Sarcoma.methods: Propensity matched analysis of STRASS and STREXIT cohorts ➜ pre-op RT to 50.4 Gy then en bloc surgery in 4-8 weeks vs. ➜ en bloc surgery Endpoint: abdominal RFS
Sarcoma.results: RT improved abdominal RFS in well-differentiated liposarcoma and grade 1-2 dedifferentiated liposarcoma No improvement in abdominal RFS with RT in grade 3 dedifferentiated liposarcoma or leiomyosarcoma
Sarcoma.interpretation: In this propensity matched analysis, pre-op RT was associated with improved abdominal RFS in well-differentiated liposarcoma and grade 1-2 dedifferentiated liposarcoma. A randomized trial focused on this subgroup may be warranted.
Sarcoma.commentary: In the STRASS trial, subanalysis hinted towards a benefit with pre-operative RT in liposarcoma, but the analysis was not powered. This STREXIT propensity analysis, published by many of the same authors, further investigates. Although a propensity analyses is not a replacement for a randomized trial (Soni 2019), the study at hand does provide a fair degree of evidence, especially considering that it sources data of those actually treated on protocol plus a non-enrolled cohort from STRASS credentialed protocol centers where high quality treatment was presumably provided.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Callegaro D, Raut CP, Ajayi T, et al. Preoperative Radiotherapy in Patients With Primary Retroperitoneal Sarcoma: EORTC-62092 Trial (STRASS) Versus Off-trial (STREXIT) Results [published online ahead of print, 2022 Jul 14]. Ann Surg 2022;10.1097/SLA.0000000000005492. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/SLA.0000000000005492
Sarcoma.commentary_refs: Soni PD, Hartman HE, Dess RT, et al. Comparison of Population-Based Observational Studies With Randomized Trials in Oncology. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1209-16. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.18.01074

Sarcoma.patients: operable localized retroperitoneal soft tissue sarcoma Exclusion: expected R2
Sarcoma.methods: ➜ pre-op chemo x3 then en bloc surgery in 3-6 weeks vs. ➜ en bloc surgery High grade LPS: doxo + ifos Leiomyosarcoma: doxo + dacarbazing
Sarcoma.results: ongoing study Primary endpoint: DFS Secondary endpoint: RFS, OS, LF, DM
Sarcoma.references: 1. European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer - EORTC. Surgery With Our Without Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy in High Risk RetroPeritoneal Sarcoma (STRASS2). Available at: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04031677. NLM identifier: NCT04031677. Accessed September 18, 2020.

Sarcoma.trial: Brigham and Women's Hospital, Harvard Medical School
Sarcoma.patients: RPS patients undergoing definitive resection
Sarcoma.methods: Nomogram developed and then subsequently validated at North American and European centers
Sarcoma.results: Refer to publication for useful nomogram 7-yr DFS 38%, 7-yr OS 58% overall 30% received RT Prognostic factors: age, tumor size, grade, histologic subtype, multifocality, and quality of surgery Liposarcoma was worse tumor type
Sarcoma.interpretation: Nomogram was developed to predict DFS and OS in RP sarcoma and then externally validated.
Sarcoma.commentary: The nomogram includes some who received RT. This nomogram consequentially should be used to estimate outcomes in the setting of typical therapy, not necessarily to guide the use of RT. Worse outcomes in liposarcoma may suggest that RT should be employed in this situation.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Raut CP, Miceli R, Strauss DC, et al. External validation of a multi-institutional retroperitoneal sarcoma nomogram. Cancer 2016;122:1417-24. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.29931

Sarcoma.trial: Fondazione IRCCS Istituto Nazionale dei Tumori, Milan, Italy
Sarcoma.patients: RPS at 6 European and two North American institutions
Sarcoma.methods: Retrospective review of outcomes, treatment modality, and factors for recurrence
Sarcoma.results: -Factors affecting outcomes: size, grade, subtype, multifocality, degree of resection -refer to figures for outcomes by subtype -RT impacted LR in liposarcoma but not leiomyosarcoma -Well differentiated liposarcoma: 8-yr LR 5% with RT + extended surgery, 35% with extended surgery + no RT, 42% limited surgery and no RT
Sarcoma.interpretation: Outcomes in RPS are outlined based on patient characteristics. There may be a benefit with radiation therapy in well differetiated liposarcoma.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Gronchi A, Strauss DC, Miceli R, et al. Variability in Patterns of Recurrence After Resection of Primary Retroperitoneal Sarcoma (RPS): A Report on 1007 Patients From the Multi-institutional Collaborative RPS Working Group. Ann Surg 2016;263:1002-1009. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1097/SLA.0000000000001447

Sarcoma.citation: DeLaney et al, J Surg Oncol, 2014
Sarcoma.patients: primary or locally recurrent T, L, S, or paraspinal chordomas or sarcomas
Sarcoma.methods: Phase II Dose escalation with photons or protons ≤72.0 GyRBE in n=25 76.6-77.4 GyRBE in n=25
Sarcoma.results: Primary tumors 5-yr LC 94%, 8-yr LC 85% All tumors 5-yr LC 81%, 8-yr LC 74% 8-yr grade 3-4 adverse effects 13% -No myelopathies. Sacral neuropathies in n=3 with 76.6-77.4 GyRBE
Sarcoma.interpretation: Dose escalation for spinal sarcomas results in favorable control and acceptable adverse effects.
Sarcoma.references: 1. DeLaney TF, Liebsch NJ, Pedlow FX, et al. Long-term results of Phase II study of high dose photon/proton radiotherapy in the management of spine chordomas, chondrosarcomas, and other sarcomas. J Surg Oncol 2014;110:115-122. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/jso.23617

Sarcoma.trial: Ludwig Maximilian University Hospital, Munich, Germany
Sarcoma.citation: Albertsmeier et al, Ann Surg Oncol, 2018
Sarcoma.patients: resectable soft tissue sarcomas, any site
Sarcoma.methods: Meta-analysis of studies evaluating EBRT vs. no EBRT (not all randomized)
Sarcoma.results: -RT reduced LR in RPS and other sites -RT improved OS in RPS but not others -Pre-op RT improved LR over post-op -Wound complications more in pre-op RT
Sarcoma.interpretation: There is LC benefit with adjuvant RT. There may be an OS benefit with RT in RP sarcoma.
Sarcoma.commentary: Authors acknowledge high risk of bias in some studies included
Sarcoma.references: 1. Albertsmeier M, Rauch A, Roeder F, et al. External Beam Radiation Therapy for Resectable Soft Tissue Sarcoma: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Ann Surg Oncol 2018;25:754-67. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1245/s10434-017-6081-2

Sarcoma.citation: DeLaney et al, J Surg Oncol, 2014
Sarcoma.patients: resected soft tissue sarcoma, RT optional
Sarcoma.methods: doxorubicin/ifosphamide vs. no chemo
Sarcoma.results: No OS or DM benefit with chemo
Sarcoma.interpretation: There is no OS or DM benefit with adjuvant doxo/ifosphamide.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Woll PJ, Reichardt P, Le Cesne A, et al. Adjuvant chemotherapy with doxorubicin, ifosfamide, and lenograstim for resected soft-tissue sarcoma (EORTC 62931): a multicentre randomised controlled trial. Lancet Oncol 2012;13:1045-54. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(12)70346-7

Sarcoma.patients: 18 trials of adj chemo for adults
Sarcoma.methods: Meta-analysis evaluating phase III adjuvant chemotherapy trials
Sarcoma.results: Absolute benefits for doxorubicin alone: LC 5%, DM 10%, RFS 10%, and OS NS There was OS benefit for doxo+ifosfamide
Sarcoma.interpretation: There is OS benefit with adjuvant doxo/ifosphamide in this meta-analysis.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Pervaiz N, Colterjohn N, Farrokhyar F, Tozer R, Figueredo A, Ghert M. A systematic meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials of adjuvant chemotherapy for localized resectable soft-tissue sarcoma. Cancer 2008;113:573-81. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.23592

Sarcoma.trial: IRCCS Humanitas Research Hospital, Rozzano, Milan, Italy.
Sarcoma.patients: ≤4 lung metastases, ≤5 cm, from sarcoma
Sarcoma.methods: Phase II peripheral ≤10mm: 30 Gy/ 1 fx peripheral 11-20mm: 60 Gy/ 3 fx peripheral >20mm: 48 Gy/ 4 fx central:60 Gy/ 8 fx
Sarcoma.interpretation: SBRT for sarcoma lung metastasis leads to excellent local control. Randomized trials are warranted to compare this approach to surgery. Long term PFS is still poor however, and investigation into additional therapy is warranted.
Sarcoma.references: 1. Navarria P, Baldaccini D, Clerici E, et al. Stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) for lung metastases from sarcoma in oligometastatic patients: a phase 2 study [published online ahead of print, 2022 Aug 17]. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2022;S0360-3016(22)03131-5. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2022.08.028

Benign.patients: neovascular age-related macular degeneration ≥ prior 3 ranibizumab or bevacizumab injections within one year
Benign.methods: ➜ 🏆 16 Gy SRS vs. ➜ 🏆 24 Gy SRS vs. ➜ sham SRS
Benign.results: Change in best-corrected visual acuity -10.0 vs. -7.5 vs. -6.7 letters Loss of <15 letters 68% vs. 75% vs. 79% microvascular abnormalities in 18 SRT eyes (that were attributed to radiation) and 6 control eyes, but only 2 possibly affected vision Best response when linear size ≤ 4 mm (the size of the treatment zone) and a macular volume >median (7.4 mm³)
Benign.interpretation: SRS reduced intravitreal injections. Some microvascular changes are caused, but these affected vision in only 1%.
Benign.references: 1. Jackson TL, Chakravarthy U, Slakter JS, et al. Stereotactic radiotherapy for neovascular age-related macular degeneration: year 2 results of the INTREPID study. Ophthalmology 2015;122:138-145. DOI: https: doi.org/10.1016/j.ophtha.2014.07.043

Benign.patients: pretreated chronic active neovascular age-related macular degeneration ≥3 prior anti-VEGF injections, including at least one in the last 4 months
Benign.methods: ➜ 🏆 16 Gy SRS vs. ➜ sham SRS intravitreal 0.5 mg ranibizumab given immediately after each treatment 0.5 mg ranibizumab given q4 weeks if CATT retreatment criteria met
Benign.results: 2-yr mean ranibizumab injections 10.7 vs. 13.3 best-corrected visual acuity change was noninferior Reading center-detected microvascular abnormalities 35% vs. 12%. Those with abnormalities had better best-corrected eyes.
Benign.interpretation: SRS reduces ranibizumab injections, and vision is noninferior.
Benign.commentary: The eye was tracked with SRS using a suction cup device attached to the lens with infrared reflectors. Ranibizumab blocks VEGF, similar to bevacizumab. 16 Gy was selected because it was effective in the INTREPID trial and has the potential for less adverse effects.
Benign.references: Study protocol: https://www.thelancet.com/cms/10.1016/S0140-6736(24)00687-1/attachment/bcfc19a7-6ff2-428a-8d82-96343c99e1ef/mmc1.pdf 1. Jackson TL, Desai R, Wafa HA, et al. Stereotactic radiotherapy for neovascular age-related macular degeneration (STAR): a pivotal, randomised, double-masked, sham-controlled device trial. Lancet 2024;404:44-54. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(24)00687-1

Benign.citation: Hall et al, Pract Radiat Oncol, 2022
Benign.methods: Review of retrospective studies
Benign.results: Radiation provides very good control and with low adverse effects Dose 20-45 Gy/ 10-25 fx
Benign.interpretation: Radiation provides very good control and with low adverse effects and is a reasonable alternative to surgery.
Benign.commentary: Treating amyloidosis is similar to the concept of treating plasmacytoma/multiple myeloma. The disease is caused by amyloid light chain secretion by monoclonal plasma cells. See paper for general treatment guidelines (Table 2). The authors suggest to consider RT for recurrent disease or when surgery would lead to morbidity. Similar to lymphoma, treat the "involved site."
Benign.references: 1. Hall J, Rubinstein S, Lilly A, Blumberg JM, Chera B. Treatment of Localized Amyloid Light Chain Amyloidosis With External Beam Radiation Therapy. Pract Radiat Oncol 2022;12:504-510. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.prro.2022.03.011

Benign.patients: Unruptured AVM (Characteristics: Spetzler-Martin grade I-III in ~30% each, and grade IV in ~10%)
Benign.methods: ➜ 🏆 medical management vs. ➜ medical management + intervention (can include SRS, embolization, neurosurgery, or combination)
Benign.results: -Mean f/u of 50 mos -Death or stroke 3.39 vs. 12.32 per 100 pt-years -Deaths: n=2 vs. 4 -Adverse events 59% vs. 79% •Neurosurgery in 21%. 95% of these had eradication, 41% had an event •SRS in 54%. 21% had eradication, 37% had an event •Embolization in 62%. 52% had eradication, 50% had an event
Benign.interpretation: Medical management alone results in less death, stroke, and adverse effects compared to interventional therapy (SRS, surgery, or embolization) in unruptured AVM.
Benign.commentary: This is the first randomized trial evaluating intervention in unruptured AVM. Follow-up may be too short - closure can occur later. It should still give some caution toward interventional therapy. SRS, a less invasive option, might possibly be preferred. The trial seems to show that events are fewer with SRS than other modalities. However, the rate of eradication is lower. Recognize that outcome data by modality is non-randomized and non-stratified. Deep and larger tumors would be more likely to undergo SRS. This would favor the surgical and embolization groups. Even so, SRS showed less advers…
Benign.references: 1. Mohr JP, Overbey JR, Hartmann A, et al. Medical management with interventional therapy versus medical management alone for unruptured brain arteriovenous malformations (ARUBA): final follow-up of a multicentre, non-blinded, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Neurol 2020. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1474-4422(20)30181-2

Benign.methods: Retrospective. AVM treated with gamma knife. Rate of hemorrhage assessed pre-surgery, during latency, and after obliteration
Benign.results: Risk of hemorrhage decreased 54% in latency period and 88% after obliteration 5-yr obliteration 91% Risk of reduction greater in those who presented with hemorrhage
Benign.interpretation: The risk of hemorrhage is reduced with GK in AVM. The effect begins before obliteration.
Benign.commentary: Mean dose 21 Gy
Benign.references: 1. Maruyama K, Kawahara N, Shin M, et al. The Risk of Hemorrhage after Radiosurgery for Cerebral Arteriovenous Malformations. N Engl J Med 2005;352:146-53. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa040907

Benign.methods: Retrospective cohort study SRS Gamma ventral capsulotomy for OCD Median dose 135 Gy (range 120-180 Gy) SRS with 1-2 isocenters
Benign.results: 45% response rate (40% of above still in remission at last f/u) (48% were stable) (8% had worsening of scores) Those treated with 2 isocenters were more likely to have improvement 25% mood disturbances 3% neurological complications
Benign.interpretation: SRS Gamma ventral capsulotomy thus far seems to be a reasonably effective treatment for refractory OCD per this limited retrospective data.
Benign.commentary: Simmons et al (2023) from Irving published a useful review and guidelines of SRS for functional disorders. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.prro.2023.05.003
Benign.references: 1. Gupta A, Shepard MJ, Xu Z, et al. An International Radiosurgery Research Foundation Multicenter Retrospective Study of Gamma Ventral Capsulotomy for Obsessive Compulsive Disorder. Neurosurgery 2019;85:808-816. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/neuros/nyy536
Benign.commentary_refs: 1. Simmons G, Gallitto M, Lee A, Baltuch G, Youngerman BE, Wang TJC. The Use of Stereotactic Radiosurgery to Treat Functional Disorders: A Topic Discussion. Pract Radiat Oncol. 2023;13(5):e395-e399. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.prro.2023.05.003

Benign.patients: Celiac plexus pain secondary to pancreatic or other upper GI malignancy
Benign.methods: Phase II ➜ 25 Gy SRS to celiac plexus
Benign.results: 53% had at least a partial pain response grade ≥3 adverse events in 9% Most common grade 3-4 events: abdominal pain 28% fatigue 18%
Benign.interpretation: SRS to celiac plexus is effective in reducing pain. Randomized trials comparing to conventional celiac block or neurolysis are warranted
Benign.commentary: Study was undertaken in eight hospitals in five countries: Israel, Poland, Canada, the USA, and Portugal.
Benign.references: 1. Lawrence YR, Miszczyk M, Dawson LA, et al. Celiac plexus radiosurgery for pain management in advanced cancer: a multicentre, single-arm, phase 2 trial. Published online ahead of print. Lancet Oncol 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(24)00223-7

Benign.methods: Phase III ➜ obs vs. ➜ 🏆 15 Gy/5 fx + 15 Gy/5 fx, each 1 week, separated over 4-12 weeks Endpoints: CTCAE v4.03, QuickDash, URAM and pain
Benign.results: (lower scores are better) 3-yr QuickDASH 17.8 v 10.9, p=0.013 3-yr URAM 4.8 v 3.1, p=0.056 3-yr pain 2.1 v 1.0, p=0.001
Benign.interpretation: Low dose RT for Dupuytren’s contracture improves QOL.
Benign.references: 1. Martin JM, Burgess T, McClelland B, et al. Toxicities and Quality of Life following Observation or Radiation Therapy for Dupuytren's disease: Three year secondary analysis data from the Prevention Group of the International DEPART Randomized Trial. Benign.methods: Choice of observation or RT dose randomization Phase III ➜ 🏆 15 Gy/5 fx + 15 Gy/5 fx, each 1 week, separated over 10-12 weeks vs. ➜ 21 Gy/ 7 fx in one series Orthovoltage 120 kV with blocking of uninvolved areas
Benign.results: At least 5 years f/u for all patients any progression in 62% obs vs. 20% vs. 24% surgery in 30% vs. 8% vs. 12% Tubiana stage progression in 52% vs. 16% vs. 22% symptomatic relief 8% vs. 26% vs. 21% remission of nodules, cords or stage in 17% stable in 53% progressed in 29% Grade 1 CTC adverse effects 27% Grade 2 CTC adverse effects 2.5% Late effects (mainly dry skin) 14% grade 1 No second cancers
Benign.interpretation: Radiation is an effective treatment for Dupuytren’s contracture.
Benign.commentary: DEGRO 2022 guidelines: Radiation should be performed only in the active early stage of “nodule formation” (stage N and N/I). Evidence level 2c, recommendation level B The trial did not randomize to observation or an alternative therapy. Still, radiation therapy seems to show impressive results. Yunes (2025) provides useful radiation planning instructions, patient selection criteria, and even tests to follow response in the appendix. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.prro.2024.11.010 Dupuytren’s contracture and Ledderhose disease are the same pathological process, which is why the same dose is use…

Benign.methods: ➜ 25 Gy single fx to arrhythmogenic scar regions defined by electrophysiology imaging Endpoint: improvement measured by indwelling implantable cardioverter defibrillators or 24-hour PVC burden
Benign.results: Median episodes reduced from n=119 to n=3 frequency of VT episodes or PVC burden reduced 75% in 89% of patients Use of dual antiarrhythmic medications reduced to 12% from 59% Adverse effects: 1 pericarditis and 1 gastropericardial fistula
Benign.interpretation: Electrophysiology guided cardiac radioablation is highly effective.

Benign.trial: Humanitas Research Hospital IRCCS, Rozzano, Italy
Benign.methods: SRS medial thalamotomy for intractable pain Systematic review of literature, all retrospective
Benign.results: Six studies were identified meaningful pain reduction in 55% (43-100%) persisted in 38% at last f/u Better pain relief was achieved when the target was more posterior
Benign.interpretation: SRS medial thalamotomy for intractable pain appears to provide pain relief. Prospective studies would be enlightening.
Benign.commentary: Simmons et al (2023) from Irving published a useful review and guidelines of SRS for functional disorders. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.prro.2023.05.003

Benign.methods: ➜ 🏆 15 Gy/5 fx + 15 Gy/5 fx, each 1 week, separated over 10 weeks vs. ➜ sham radiation
Benign.results: ⬆️1-yr pain relief 74% vs. 56% ⬆️QOL scores with RT RT had higher mean walking speed and step rate with barefoot speed walking
Benign.interpretation: Radiation therapy is an effective treatment for pain relief and QOL in Ledderhose disease.
Benign.commentary: Planning: mark all Ledderhose nodules and/or cords on the sole then add 2.5 cm sup/inf margin and 1.5 cm lateral margin 8 MeV electrons used for size ≤1 cm, and 10 MeV for size >1 cm. The authors wrote that the dose was chosen because it's the most common used. The study was conducted in the Netherlands. Other treatment options include cortisone injections or extracorporeal shock wave therapy. Surgery is also an option but not routinely performed. DEGRO 2022 guidelines: Radiation therapy can be performed in Ledderhose disease when symptoms are clearly increasing, both primarily and secondarily…

Benign.methods: Systematic review of SRS for tremors (essential, Parkinson's, or multiple sclerosis) SRS unilateral thalamotomy, ventral intermediate nucleus
Benign.results: 88% mean reduction in tremor Time to response mean 4.8 mos Most had short f/u, some with 6-7 years Risk/benefit ratio seems best with 130-150 Gy Adverse effects mean 17% (range 0-100%), usually not permanent 3 studies were prospective, 1 retrospective comparative, and 30 retrospective
Benign.interpretation: This review of cohorts in SRS for tremors shows effectiveness and safety. The procedure is endorsed by the International Stereotactic Radiosurgery Society.
Benign.commentary: Dose 130-150 Gy to ventral intermediate nucleus using 4 mm cone. "Certain centers modify the target so that the 20%–30% isodose does not exceed the interior edge of the internal capsule" A common atlas used by some centers is Schaltenbrand and Wahren (1977). Most use 1.5 T MRI (3 or 7 T can be blurry). Consider tractography MRI. Simmons et al (2023) from Irving published a useful review and guidelines of SRS for functional disorders. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.prro.2023.05.003
Benign.references: 1. Khattab MH, Cmelak AJ, Sherry AD, et al. Noninvasive Thalamotomy for Refractory Tremor by Frameless Radiosurgery. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2022;112:121-130. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijrobp.2021.08.021

Benign.methods: Systematic review. 65 studies
Benign.results: -Mean freedom from pain 80-87% -(no difference in GK, LINAC, or CK) -Mean time to relief 15-81 days -Max time to relief considered as 180 days -Hypoesthesia ranges 0-17% -Mean recurrence 25-32% -(For recurrence, LINAC was better than GK. No difference between GK and CK) 7-yr pain relief 22-60% 10-yr pain relief 30-45%
Benign.interpretation: SRS for trigmeminal neuralgia leads to very good pain control and is an effective treatment option. Over time efficacy can wane in some and repeat therapy may be beneficial.

Benign.commentary: On X Koneru writes that there are no randomized trials comparing total knee replacement vs placebo or sham. Benign.methods: Review of history and randomized, prospective, and retrospective studies
Benign.results: Pain relief 60-90% Minimal to no adverse effects 2 recent randomized trials were completed, but negative - limited by low power, possibly too high of radiation dose, too advanced disease, and only one course of radiation In Germany over 1/3 of RT use is for benign disease See review for suggestions for planning Dose: 3 Gy/ 6 fx QOD or BIW, rx to midpoint
Benign.interpretation: Low dose RT seems to be an effective treatment option for osteoarthritis. High quality randomized trials are warranted.
Benign.commentary: Yu (2025) also provides useful instructions for sim and field design. An atlas from HM Hospitales, Madrid gives useful anatomical and CT based instructions (Alvarez 2021). The DEGRO guidelines quote an additional risk of basal cell carcinoma 0.1% and of leukemia of 0.03% with 3 Gy dose Practice management. There are some concerns that an overwhelming influx of OA patients could hinder access to cancer care for some clinics. Clinics could schedule shorter visits with OA patients, and instead of a CT simulator, an xray simulator could be used.

Radiopharm.citation:
Sartor et al, NEJM, 2021 
Fizazi et al, Lancet Oncol, 2023
Radiopharm.methods:
previous treatment with ADT and 1-2 taxanes

➜ 🏆 Lu-177–PSMA-617 (7.4 GBq) q6weeks x4-6 + SOC
vs. 
➜ standard of care
Radiopharm.results:
Radiographic median PFS 8.7 vs. 3.4 mos
Median OS 15.3 vs. 11.3 mos

Median time to 1st skeletal event or death
11.5 vs. 6.8 mos
QOL metrics improved on surveys

Increased cytopenias with tx

Grade 5 adverse effects 1% (n=5) vs. 0
(pancytopenia [n=2], BM failure [n=1], subdural haematoma [n=1], and intracranial haemorrhage [n=1])

Only median outcomes were reported. Looking at Fig 2, it seems that 2-yr OS and time to first symptomatic skeletal event remain different. PFS curves seem to merge at 2 years
Radiopharm.interpretation: Lu-177–PSMA-617 improves PFS and OS in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer treated with prior taxane.  Time to skeletal events or death is prolonged.
Radiopharm.commentary:
Just as on a PSMA PET scan, there is PSMA uptake in liver, salivary glands, kidney, bowel, and spleen normal tissue. Thus Pluvicto has side effects and dose limiting adverse effects related to these organs. 

Lu-177 emits beta and gamma. Maximum range 2 mm and half-life 6.7 days. 

Safety: outpatient shielded or separated, with separate bathroom, with significant radiation protection and contamination prevention. Peptides are infused for renal protection. 

Many rad onc clinics do not have the certifications to deliver radiopharmaceuticals; they are delivered in the nuclear medicine clinic in many hospitals. In US this means that rad onc will not receive the diagnostic fee. A rad onc facility can deliver these with proper NRC certification.
Radiopharm.references:
1. Sartor O, de Bono J, Chi KN, et al. Lutetium-177-PSMA-617 for Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer. N Engl J Med 2021;385:1091-1103. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2107322 Protocol pdf: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa2107322/suppl_file/nejmoa2107322_protocol.pdf

Lancet Oncol 2023;24:597-610. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(23)00158-4

Radiopharm.methods:
no prior taxanes

➜ 🏆 Lu-177–PSMA-617 (7.4 GBq) q6weeks x6 + no change in ADT
vs. 
➜ change in ADT

Crossover allowed if radiographic progression (60%)
Radiopharm.results:
Median OS 24.5 vs. 23.1 mos by ITT, but high crossover
Crossover adjusted HR 0.59
60% overall crossed over, or 75% that had radiographic progression

Median radiographic PFS 11.6 vs. 5.6 mos
Median time to BF 10.6 vs. 4.2 mos
symptomatic skeletal event NR vs. 18.0 mos

grade 3-5 adverse events 36% vs. 48%
grade 5 2% in both arms
Radiopharm.interpretation:
Lu-177–PSMA-617 improves PFS, BF, and time to skeletal events in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer treated with one prior ADT and no taxane history.

There was no confirmed OS benefit, likely due to a high rate of crossover. Crossover adjusted analysis appears to show OS benefit.
Radiopharm.commentary:
Just as on a PSMA PET scan, there is PSMA uptake in liver, salivary glands, kidney, bowel, and spleen normal tissue. Thus Pluvicto has side effects and dose limiting adverse effects related to these organs. 

Lu-177 emits beta and gamma. Maximum range 2 mm and half-life 6.7 days. 

Safety: outpatient shielded or separated, with separate bathroom, with significant radiation protection and contamination prevention. Peptides are infused for renal protection. 

Many rad onc clinics do not have the certifications to deliver radiopharmaceuticals; they are delivered in the nuclear medicine clinic in many hospitals. In US this means that rad onc will not receive the diagnostic fee. A rad onc facility can deliver these with proper NRC certification.
Radiopharm.references: 1. Morris MJ, Castellano D, Herrmann K, et al. 177Lu-PSMA-617 versus a change of androgen receptor pathway inhibitor therapy for taxane-naive patients with progressive metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (PSMAfore): a phase 3, randomised, controlled trial. Lancet 2024. Published online September 13, 2024. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(24)01653-2

vs.

Radiopharm.results:
Median PFS 17.6 vs. 7.4 mos

Grade ≥3 lymphopenia 6.7% vs. 4.8%

Radiopharm.references: 1. Kishan AU, Valle LF, Wilhalme H, et al. 177Lu-Prostate-Specific Membrane Antigen Neoadjuvant to Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy for Oligorecurrent Prostate Cancer (LUNAR): An Open-Label, Randomized, Controlled, Phase II Study. Radiopharm.patients:
Castration resistant prostate cancer with ≥2 bone metastases

Prior docetaxel allowed

Exclusion: visceral metastasis
Radiopharm.methods:
➜ 🏆 radium-223 qmonthly x6 plus best standard of care
vs. 
➜ placebo and best standard of care (2:1 randomization)

Eligibility: ANC ≥ 1500, Plt ≥ 100, Hgb ≥ 10 at first dose
Subsequent doses: ANC ≥ 1000 and Plt ≥ 50
Radiopharm.results:
Median OS 14.9 mos vs. 11.3 mos
Median time to bony event 15.6 vs. 9.8 mos
Median time to alk phos rise 7.4 vs. 3.8 mos
PR of ≥30% reduction in alk phos 47% vs. 3%
CR in alk phos 34% vs. 1%
(total alk phos response rate 81%)
Improved QOL with radium-223

Only median outcomes were provided. Looking at Figure 1, OS and time to first symptomatic skeletal event curves seem to merge at 2 years

-The study was terminated early for efficacy at interim analysis
-Adverse effects in radium-223 group were significantly lower than in placebo arm
Radiopharm.interpretation: Radium-223 improves OS in castrate resistant nonmetastatic prostate cancer with bone metastases.
Radiopharm.commentary:
Why was there worse adverse effects in the standard arm? The adverse effects could be due to random chance, or perhaps therapies were escalated in the placebo arm when those patients performed worse without radium-223. Alk phos response and OS were still markedly superior with radium-223. 

In view of new docetaxel findings since this trial, some oncologists might advocate docetaxel over radium-223. But the favorable QOL with radium-223 is appealing. 

Dose of 50 kBq/kg given q4 weeks. Placebo used was isotonic saline. 

Safety: given in outpatient office. Only mild precautions required. 30 minutes per treatment. 

Many rad onc clinics do not have the certifications to deliver radiopharmaceuticals; they are delivered in the nuclear medicine clinic in many hospitals. In US this means that rad onc will not receive the diagnostic fee. A rad onc facility can deliver these with proper NRC certification.
Radiopharm.references:
1. Parker C, Nilsson S, Heinrich D, et al. Alpha Emitter Radium-223 and Survival in Metastatic Prostate Cancer. N Engl J Med 2013;369:213-23. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1213755 Protocol pdf: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa1213755/suppl_file/nejmoa1213755_protocol.pdf
Links: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1213755

Radiopharm.patients: metastatic or inoperable locally advanced and progressive midgut neuroendocrine tumors after first line treatment, somatostatin receptor+
Radiopharm.methods:
➜ 🏆 Lu-177-Dotatate q8wks x 4 + octreotide LAR
vs. 
➜ octreotide LAR alone


primary endpoint: 20-mos PFS
secondary endpoint: median OS
Radiopharm.results:
Median OS 48 vs. 36.3 mos, p=0.30
2-yr OS 76% vs. 63%
5-yr OS ~36% both arms
20-mo PFS 65% vs. 11%
Response rate 18% vs. 3%

2% had MDS
Grade 3-4 neutropenia 1%
thrombocytopenia 2%
lymphopenia 9%
Radiopharm.interpretation: Lu-177-Dotatate improves PFS in advanced progressive midgut neuroendocrine tumors. There was an improvement in OS that was nonsignificant.
Radiopharm.commentary:
Lu-177-Dotatate emits beta and gamma. Maximum range 2 mm and half-life 160 hrs. Dose 7.4 GBq q8wks x4 doses. 

Safety: outpatient shielded or separated, with separate bathroom, with intermediate radiation protection and contamination prevention. 1-3 hours per treatment.

Many rad onc clinics do not have the certifications to deliver radiopharmaceuticals; they are delivered in the nuclear medicine clinic in many hospitals. In US this means that rad onc will not receive the diagnostic fee. A rad onc facility can deliver these with proper NRC certification.
Radiopharm.references:
1. Strosberg J, El-Haddad G, Wolin E, et al. Phase 3 Trial of 177Lu-Dotatate for Midgut Neuroendocrine Tumors. N Engl J Med 2017;376:125-135. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1607427 Protocol pdf: https://www.nejm.org/doi/suppl/10.1056/NEJMoa1607427/suppl_file/nejmoa1607427_protocol.pdf
2. Strosberg JR, Caplin ME, Kunz PL, et al. 177Lu-Dotatate plus long-acting octreotide versus high‑dose long-acting octreotide in patients with midgut neuroendocrine tumours (NETTER-1): final overall survival and long-term safety results from an open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncol. 2021;22:1752-1763. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(21)00572-6
3. Hope TA, Abbott A, Colucci K, et al. NANETS/SNMMI Procedure Standard for Somatostatin Receptor-Based Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy with 177Lu-DOTATATE. J Nucl Med 2019;60:937-943. DOI: https://doi.org/10.2967/jnumed.118.230607
Links: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34793718/

5-yr RFS 96% vs. 98%, noninferior

On subanalysis, no benefit in any subgroup
